facts: Ascend Elements celebrated the grand opening of its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility in Covington, Georgia on March 30, 2023.
The $50 million Base 1 facility is North America's largest electric vehicle battery recycling facility.
The facility began partial operations in August 2022.
The facility now has an annual capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
The processing capacity equals 70,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
Ascend Elements recovers up to 98% of the critical battery metals in used electric vehicle batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap.
The company uses its patented Hydro-to-Cathode™ process to recycle used lithium-ion batteries and scrap.
The recycling process can reduce the carbon footprint of new electric vehicle battery cathode materials by up to 90%.
The facility is powered by renewable energy.
The facility features onsite wastewater recycling and treatment systems.
The facility currently employs about 100 team members.
Ascend Elements plans to increase headcount to 185 by 2024.
CEO Mike O'Kronley stated the facility is an important part of America's EV battery infrastructure and that this is just the start.
CEO Mike O'Kronley emphasized the industry needs to continue building a closed-loop battery materials supply chain to make electric vehicle batteries cleaner and more sustainable.
Ascend Elements is based in Westborough, Mass.
Ascend Elements produces lithium-ion battery precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM) from spent lithium-ion cells using its Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis technology.
The company's direct precursor synthesis technology results in improved economics and lowered GHG emissions compared to traditional methods.
source_title: Ascend Elements Opens North America's Largest Electric Vehicle Battery Recycling Facility in Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Mobis plans to invest $926 million in an Electric Vehicle (EV) Power Electric system plant in Bryan County.
The plant will eventually employ at least 1,500 people.
Governor Brian P. Kemp celebrated the groundbreaking of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
This announcement marks the second supplier to locate in that area in just two weeks.
The EV Power Electric system plant will expand Hyundai Mobis’ current footprint in Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing facility with almost 1,200 employees in West Point since 2009.
The existing West Point facility is the largest Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia.
The West Point plant supplies automotive manufacturers with complete cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies.
H.S. Oh serves as Vice President of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit.
The investment reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia’s auto industry.
Hyundai Mobis will be a major production player in the EV market, triggering more sector growth.
Once at full production, the new facility will have 1,200,000 square feet.

The facility will supply over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems annually to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Bryan County, Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA) in Montgomery, and the Kia Georgia plant.
The facility will also supply 450,000 Integrated Charging Control Units annually to those same locations.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin as early as January 2023.
Production at the new facility is expected to begin in 2024.
The facility is located at the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.
Jon Seagraves serves as Chairman of the Development Authority of Bryan County.
The site has close proximity to available housing in nearby Heartwood, a top-performing public school system, interstate access, and rail.
The Development Authority purchased the site about two years ago.
The project represents nearly $1 billion of investment and the creation of over 1,500 good-paying jobs.
Bryan County, the City of Richmond Hill, and Raydient Places provided support and partnership on the project.
Alyce Thornhill serves as Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team.
The GDEcD partnered with the Development Authority of Bryan County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power on this competitive project.
Hyundai Mobis and other suppliers are transitioning to jobs of the future and becoming part of Georgia’s growing EV ecosystem.
In May, officials predicted that non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia.
That prediction was blown past in about two weeks.
Partners like Georgia Quick Start and sister agencies are preparing Georgia’s workforce for the jobs being created now.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment to Georgia.
These projects have announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
Offsite suppliers connected to the HMGMA project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: Second Global Automotive Supplier for Hyundai Metaplant To Create 1,500 Jobs – Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority

facts: Georgia’s strategic location plugs positions automotive manufacturers and suppliers into the growing Southeast supply chain and easy access to key consumer markets.
Georgia has been leading the industry for over 100 years.
Georgia’s automotive industry thrives thanks to its business-friendly environment, extensive infrastructure, and a highly skilled workforce.

For more than a century, Georgia has fostered innovative automotive industry practices, encouraged collaboration, and established itself as a leader in emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility sectors.
Seven global automotive companies are headquartered in Georgia.
The Port of Brunswick is the top Ro/Ro port in the United States, according to Georgia Ports Authority in 2025.
Georgia’s robust automotive ecosystem supports every aspect of the manufacturing process (BusinessWise, 2025).
University System of Georgia and the Technical College System offer specialized programs across the state.
Located in the center of the southeastern United States, Georgia offers proximity to diverse manufacturers within a 300-mile radius.
Connected ports, railways, and interstates enable fast delivery to customers.
Georgia is quickly becoming a national leader in electric and hybrid vehicle manufacturing due to the well-established automotive industry.
Since 2018, advanced mobility investments have steadily increased, anchored by major projects from Kia, Hyundai Motor Group, and Rivian.
Georgia offers an end-to-end supply chain for vehicle assembly, advanced battery, and materials, supporting innovation, scalability, and long-term growth in electrification.
Collaboration and innovation are encouraged in Georgia through partnerships among businesses, nonprofits, universities, and government.
These partnerships have resulted in living laboratories – such as Curiosity Lab and The Ray – that develop, test, and advance next-generation technologies for autonomous and connected vehicles.
Strength in engineering, technology, and cybersecurity also make the state an ideal hub for innovation centers for a variety of industry leaders.
Leading automotive innovators in Georgia include Novelis, GM IT Innovation Center, Panasonic Automotive, Heliox, and Porsche Digital.
Georgia provides business support and industry development resources for companies already located in the state.
source_title: Automotive & EV | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Mobis plans to invest $926 million in an Electric Vehicle (EV) Power Electric system plant in Bryan County that will eventually employ at least 1,500 people.
The EV Power Electric system plant expands Hyundai Mobis’ current footprint in Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing facility with almost 1,200 employees in West Point since 2009 as the largest Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia.
The West Point plant supplies automotive manufacturers with complete cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies.
H.S. Oh, Vice President of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit, stated the investment reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia’s auto industry.
H.S. Oh noted that Hyundai Mobis will become a major production player in the EV market and trigger more growth within the sector.

Once it reaches full production, the 1,200,000-square-foot facility will be able to supply over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems and 450,000 Integrated Charging Control Units annually to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) factory in Bryan County, the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA) factory in Montgomery, and the Kia Georgia plant.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin as early as January 2023, and production is expected to begin in 2024.
The new facility is located at the GRAD Certified Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.
Jon Seagraves, Development Authority of Bryan County Chairman, welcomed Hyundai Mobis to the community.

Jon Seagraves stated the site was purchased about two years ago and highlighted its proximity to available housing in nearby Heartwood, a top-performing public school system, interstate access, and rail.
Jon Seagraves noted the project represents nearly $1 billion of investment and the creation of over 1,500 good-paying jobs.
Jon Seagraves expressed gratitude to Bryan County, the City of Richmond Hill, and Raydient Places for their support and partnership on the project.

Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Development Authority of Bryan County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that suppliers who have been part of Georgia’s automotive industry are transitioning to the jobs of the future and becoming part of Georgia’s growing EV ecosystem.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that in May, they predicted non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia, a prediction already blown past in about two weeks.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that with the support of partners like Georgia Quick Start and sister agencies, they are excited to prepare Georgia’s workforce of tomorrow for the jobs being created now.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Offsite suppliers connected to the HMGMA project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
source_title: Second Automotive Supplier for Hyundai Metaplant To Create 1,500 Jobs | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Daechang Seat Corp. USA, a supplier specializing in automotive seat frames, will invest $72.5 million in the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The facility is located in close proximity to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
The project will create over 500 jobs.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that suppliers for the HMGMA project alone reach at least nine counties.
Governor Brian Kemp highlighted that the news highlights the impact projects like the Hyundai Metaplant have beyond their initial investment.
Daechang Seat Corp. (DSC) was established in 1979 in Kyungju, South Korea.
DSC has become one of the largest seat frame producers in the world.
Globally, DSC employs more than 5,000 people in seven countries.
The company's production process includes fully automatic lines with arc welding and spot-welding machines.
Jinsuk Lee serves as the CEO of Daechang Seat Savannah Corp.
DSC takes pride in creating improved opportunities and employment prospects within Chatham County and the Savannah area.
DSC maintains a steadfast partnership with the City of Savannah.
DSC's primary objective is to drive growth through facility expansions, ultimately transforming into a global entity.
DSC currently holds a prominent partnership with Hyundai Transys and Hyundai Mobis at HMGMA.
DSC hopes to collaborate with even more projects in the future.
DSC’s new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The company will hire for assembly operators, equipment technicians, mechanical engineers, and electrical engineers.
Hiring will occur through local job fairs and website postings in the future.
DSC's location is only minutes from the Port of Savannah.
Griff Lynch, Georgia Ports Authority President and CEO, stated that DSC is ideally located to take advantage of Georgia’s world-class connectivity.
The Georgia Ports Authority is focused on providing the best global gateway for U.S. supply chains.
Trip Tollison is the Savannah Economic Development Authority President and CEO.
Daechang Seat Corp. USA will be the third Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America supplier to locate in the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The project will bring 500 new jobs and $72.5 million in capital investment to Chatham County.
Trip Tollison expressed excitement about the announcement.
Daechang Seat Corp. USA is recognized as a well-established global company.
The Savannah Economic Development Authority looks forward to working with DSC as it grows and becomes part of the community.
Alyce Thornhill is the Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy at the GDEcD's Global Commerce team.
The project was developed in partnership with the Savannah Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
Pat Wilson is the GDEcD Commissioner.
Countless partners are helping transform Georgia into a center of the automotive and mobility industry.
Pat Wilson expressed excitement about welcoming DSC in Savannah.
Savannah is an established global gateway.
Chatham County has leveraged its gateway advantage to create a bustling community for living and working.
As the market transitions to electric vehicles, Georgia has pursued its entire EV supply chain.
Georgia has created more than $25 billion in investments and 30,000 jobs since 2018.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in Georgia's automotive industry increased by 324 percent compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
DSC is recognized for its quality and technological expertise in the automotive seat sector.
DSC aims to be a top-tier company that respects humanity, leveraging its accumulated technology and customer trust.
DSC has recently ventured into the electric vehicle sector.
DSC continues to expand its scope.
DSC utilizes automated production lines in its processes.
DSC anticipates greater opportunities for growth with the completion of new manufacturing facilities, providing larger installation spaces.

links.context: Press Secretary, Office of Governor Brian P. Kemp
Deputy Press Secretary, Office of Governor Brian P. Kemp
Communications Manager, Georgia Department of Economic Development

source_title: Daechang Seat Corp. USA to Create 500 Jobs in Chatham County | Georgia

facts: June 22, 2023 – Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that automotive parts manufacturer NVH Korea will establish a new facility in Locust Grove.
The project will create over 160 new jobs and bring $72 million in investment to Henry County.
The announcement was made while Governor Kemp was on an economic development trip to the Paris Air Show in France.
This is the second economic development project Governor Kemp has announced that week.
NVH Korea is an automotive parts manufacturer based in the City of Ulsan, established in 1984.
The company specializes in automotive noise, vibration, and heat-control parts, currently produced in Korea, Europe, and India.
The company already operates a manufacturing facility in Georgia that produces floor mats, cargo mats, and cargo trays.
The new facility will supply battery parts for electric vehicles (EVs) in the United States for manufacturers such as Hyundai Mobis, Kia Georgia, and Hyundai Motor Group.
Ja Kyum Koo, Chairman and CEO of NVH Korea, stated that Georgia was chosen as the company’s first EV battery component manufacturing site.
The new facility will be located at Gardner Logistics Park on Colvin Drive in Locust Grove.
The facility will produce EV battery system components that protect, connect, and sense the performance of battery cells.
Operations are expected to begin in the second quarter of 2024.
Mayor Robert Price of the City of Locust Grove stated that NVH’s investment will increase manufacturing jobs and make the city part of Georgia's electric vehicle ecosystem.
Carlotta Harrell, Chair of the Henry County Board of Commissioners, stated that the announcement will add quality manufacturing jobs for citizens and diversify the local economy.

Project Manager Jacob Lee represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Henry County Development Authority, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that NVH’s investment will increase manufacturing jobs, welcome innovators establishing their first U.S. or e-mobility manufacturing presence in Georgia, and enrich the local industry mix.
From July 1 through December 31, 2022, the automotive industry was the second-largest source of new jobs and investment in Georgia.
Since 2020, Georgia has pursued job creation along the entire EV supply chain, resulting in more than $22.7 billion in investments and the creation of over 28,400 jobs.

links.context: Press Secretary for Governor Brian P. Kemp
Deputy Press Secretary for Governor Brian P. Kemp
Communications Manager for Georgia Department of Economic Development

source_title: NVH Korea to Create Over 160 Jobs, Invest $72M in Henry County | Georgia

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that lithium-ion battery recycler and raw materials provider SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, LLC., will locate its first U.S. recycling facility in Georgia.
SungEel Recycling Park Georgia is a subsidiary of the Korea-based global industry leader SungEel HiTech Co., Ltd.
The company will invest more than $37 million in the new facility.
The project will create 104 jobs in Stephens County.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia announced record-breaking numbers for fiscal year 2022 and that companies like SungEel are evidence of a rapidly growing electric mobility ecosystem generating new jobs across the state.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that Korea has been a key partner in Georgia’s growing sustainable technology industry and expressed excitement that SungEel chose Georgia for their first U.S. facility.
SungEel has more than 10 years of patented technology.
SungEel specializes in complete recycling technology using a 100 percent full circulatory system.
The recycling system processes end-of-life batteries and battery manufacturing scrap.
The system achieves a metal recovery rate of more than 95 percent.
Recovered metals include nickel, cobalt, and lithium.

Suk Jae Yim, Representative of SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, stated that SungEel HiTech's entry into Georgia is the last piece of the puzzle to build a sustainable ecosystem of Georgia’s electric vehicle supply chain.
SungEel Recycling Park Georgia will fulfill its responsibility to build a U.S. eco-friendly industrial ecosystem in line with the expectations of the State of Georgia and Stephens County.
The new facility will be located at the Hayestone Brady Business Park in Toccoa.
Hayestone Brady Business Park is a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site.
The GRAD Program is a proactive effort by Georgia’s economic development community to develop a portfolio of available sites ready for fast-track development.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in early 2024.
Over the next few years, the company will be hiring for technicians, operators, administrative, and sales roles.

Brian Akin, Chairman of the Stephens County Development Authority, stated that investments in infrastructure, workforce development, and education were significant factors in SungEel's selection process.
Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Stephens County Development Authority and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that SungEel is at the cutting edge of sustainable technology and that its proximity to other members of Georgia’s electric mobility ecosystem will lead to success.
Pat Wilson emphasized that preparing key industries, such as the auto industry, for the next technological revolution means creating jobs of the future and protecting opportunities of today.
GDEcD expressed gratitude to partners in Stephens County for their investment in speed to market solutions like site preparation.

Georgia’s prime location, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and business-friendly climate have made it an attractive location for diverse rapidly developing industries focused on creating a sustainable future.
Georgia is cultivating a vertically integrated supply chain that will help companies increase efficiencies by reducing reliance on imported materials.
EV-related projects have contributed more than $13 billion in investments to Georgia since 2020.
EV-related projects have contributed more than 18,100 new jobs to Georgia since 2020.
SungEel HiTech Co., Ltd. (KOSDAQ, KQ 365340) is headquartered in Korea and operates as a global leader in lithium ion battery recycling and raw material provision.
SungEel HiTech currently operates facilities in South Korea, China, Malaysia, India, and Hungary.
New SungEel HiTech facilities are underway in Poland and Germany.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Korean Lithium-Ion Battery Recycler SungEel HiTech to Build First U.S. Recycling Facility, SungEel Recycling Park Georgia | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: February 06, 2024: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. will invest more than $30 million in a new manufacturing facility in Metter.
The project will create 200 new jobs in Candler County.
This marks the second automotive supplier announced in Candler County in less than six months.
In fiscal year 2023 alone, 82 percent of new jobs created and more than $20 billion of investments went to communities outside the metro Atlanta area.
Doowon Group was established in Korea in 1974 and specializes in designing and manufacturing automotive climate control systems.
The company will be a key supplier for Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia (Kia Georgia) and Hyundai Motor Group.
Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. is making significant investments to establish itself as Doowon Group’s inaugural automotive air conditioning system production facility in the United States.
The new manufacturing facility will be located in the Metter-Candler County I-16 Industrial Park.
The site is a “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development” (GRAD) certified site.
The facility will primarily produce climate control systems for use in traditional and electric vehicles.
Operations are projected to begin in 2026.

Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. plans to hire employees in various fields such as injection molding technicians; assembly operators; quality, materials, and production specialists; engineers; and management.
Job opportunities will be posted in local newspapers and on recruitment websites as they become available.
The City of Metter is welcoming Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. as its newest corporate citizen.
Bringing 200 jobs and over $30 million in investment to the community advances growth in desirable manufacturing employment for citizens.
Lightning has now struck three times in the industrial growth of Candler County with this announcement.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Candler County Industrial Authority, Georgia Power, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Quick Start.
Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. marks the second automotive supplier announced in the City of Metter and Candler County in less than six months.
The City of Metter and Candler County’s intentional investment in site preparation positioned them for success.
Georgia has pursued the entire electric vehicle supply chain, creating more than $25.7 billion in investments and 30,200 jobs since 2018.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to FY21.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
Doowon Group is the ultimate parent entity of Doowon Climate Control America, Inc. and is an international corporation headquartered in South Korea.
Doowon Group manages four domestic production plants, a technology research institute, and thirteen overseas production facilities, including operations in the United States.
Doowon Group presently sustains a workforce exceeding 3,125 employees.
Doowon Group achieved sales revenue surpassing $1.9 billion in fiscal year 2023.

Doowon Group possesses advanced technological capabilities to develop and produce air conditioning systems for all types of vehicles, including electric cars, hybrids, and internal combustion engine vehicles.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Second Automotive Supplier Creating 200 New Jobs in Candler County | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp, in conjunction with the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), held the official launch of the Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA) at the Governor’s Mansion.

The EMIA launch included partners from the private and public sectors who will work on five committees to develop a multi-step approach setting state objectives for Supply Chain, Infrastructure, Workforce, Innovation, and Policy/Initiatives.
Stuart Countess, COO of Kia Georgia, was announced as the chair of the Supply Chain committee.
Jannine Miller, Director of Planning for the Georgia Department of Transportation, was announced as the chair of the Infrastructure committee.
Randall “Randy” Hatcher, President of MAU Workforce Solutions, was announced as the chair of the Workforce committee.
State Representative Bruce Williamson was announced as the chair of the Policy and Initiatives committee.
Cynthia Curry, Director of Smart Cities Ecosystem Expansion at the Metro Atlanta Chamber, was announced as the chair of the Innovation committee.
The Carl Vinson Institute of Government at the University of Georgia will host EMIA’s future committee meetings and integrated research forums.
Leaders from the Carl Vinson Institute were introduced at the launch event.
Governor Kemp stated that the Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance’s recommendations will help make strategic decisions that align with industry needs.
Governor Kemp stated that maintaining business-friendly policies will ensure Georgians have the greatest opportunities for the jobs of the future.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the alliance announcement is already garnering attention in the mobility industry.
Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the diverse group of experts leading EMIA and their knowledge, experience, and ideas will help position Georgia to further develop the electric mobility ecosystem.
Governor Kemp announced the formation of the Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance in July 2021.

EMIA will offer policy recommendations intended to further enhance the state’s attractiveness to the electric mobility industry and foster innovation in related fields, including drones, autonomous vehicles, connected vehicles, battery technology, and more.
In conjunction with the initiative, GDEcD launched new website assets for the program.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) serves as the state’s sales and marketing arm and the lead agency for attracting new business investment, encouraging industry expansion, locating new markets, promoting tourism, and planning/mobilizing state resources for economic development.

links.context: New website assets for the Electric Mobility and Innovation Initiative

source_title: Governor Kemp, Georgia Department of Economic Development Announce Leadership of Electric Mobility and Innovation Initiative | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Aspen Aerogels plans to construct an advanced manufacturing facility at Bulloch County’s Southern Gateway Commerce Park.
Aspen intends to invest a minimum of $325 million in the new site near Statesboro.
The project will create more than 250 advanced manufacturing jobs.
Production at the new Georgia site is expected to begin in late 2023.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the project and highlighted Georgia's growing electric mobility sector.
The planned facility, combined with Aspen’s original East Providence, Rhode Island plant, is designed to triple Aspen’s nameplate aerogel capacity.
The expansion supports Aspen’s thermal barrier expansion plans in the fast-growing electric vehicle market.
Aspen aims to capitalize on increased demand for its Aerogel Technology Platform™ as EV OEMs adopt PyroThin® aerogel thermal barrier solutions.
Don Young, President and CEO of Aspen, noted the company is pleased to be in Georgia for siting, development, and construction of the PyroThin materials plant.
The City of Statesboro and Bulloch County region offer access to efficient ports, a strong available workforce, and secure low-cost utilities and critical raw materials.
Aspen’s PyroThin materials provide thermal management, mechanical stability, and fire protection properties to EV battery systems.
Aspen plans to build a 500,000-square-foot advanced manufacturing facility situated on a 90-acre parcel in southeast Georgia.
Hiring for personnel will begin in the second half of 2022 across skilled manufacturing operators, advanced process engineers, quality and maintenance technicians, and professional support roles.
Billy Allen, Vice Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, welcomed Aspen to the Statesboro-Bulloch County community.
Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College are expected to provide critical workforce support to Aspen.
Assistant Director Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce division on this project.
The project was developed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson expressed excitement about welcoming Aspen’s innovative solutions for the electric vehicle and energy storage markets to Georgia.
Southern Gateway Commerce Park is a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified park.
Aspen’s carbon aerogel program seeks to increase the performance of lithium-ion battery cells to enable EV manufacturers to extend driving range and reduce costs.
Aspen manufactures its products at its East Providence, R.I. facilities and is headquartered in Northborough, MA.
source_title: Gov. Kemp: Aspen Aerogels to Open Advanced Manufacturing Facility in Bulloch County | Governor Brian P. Kemp Office of the Governor

facts: Georgia Quick Start is recognized as one of Georgia’s most effective discretionary incentives for attracting jobs to the state.
Georgia Quick Start holds the ranking of the No. 1 program of its type in the U.S.
Hyundai Mobis is the largest tier-one supplier to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, supplying three major modules.
Georgia Quick Start maintains a long-time training relationship with Hyundai Mobis.
Hanwha Qcells is the largest maker of solar modules in North America.
Hanwha Qcells has made an investment in Dalton and Cartersville, Georgia.
Hanwha Qcells' investment helps secure Georgia’s reputation as a clean-tech leader.
Georgia Quick Start assists Hanwha Qcells in developing a workforce of the future.
Georgia Quick Start pioneers customized training solutions drawing from fields such as electrical engineering, software design, robotics, and control systems.
Mechatronics originated in the 1970s when mechanical engineering blended with electronics.
To meet industry needs, Georgia Quick Start recently built a first-of-its-kind mechatronics trainer.
Georgia Quick Start designs, equips, and operates training centers to help Georgians learn skills needed for success.
The Georgia BioScience Training Center is the first facility featured in a new series showcasing Quick Start's training centers.
The Georgia BioScience Training Center building was named the best “Built Project” by the Georgia chapter of the American Institute of Architecture.
source_title: Home - Georgia Quick Start

facts: Quick Start is continuing its longtime partnership with HYUNDAI TRANSYS in a new location.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS makes seating systems and powertrains.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS is a major supplier of Kia Motors in West Point, Ga.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS will be a core affiliate of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America near Savannah, Ga.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is slated to start production by early 2025.
Quick Start has begun providing HYUNDAI TRANSYS with company-specific training to prepare for on-site work.
Training topics include seat assembly processes on various Hyundai EV models, electrical motor controls, and a manufacturing process overview.
HYUNDAI TRANSYS trainees are provided with safety and organizational development courses such as Lean, 5S, and workplace ethics.
Quick Start is using its main Advanced Manufacturing Training Center in Pooler, Georgia, to provide the training.
Two interim facilities are being used by Quick Start alongside the main center for training purposes.
The new Hyundai Mobility Training Center of Georgia is under construction adjacent to the Metaplant.
Eddie Mabe serves as the Senior Manager of Human Resources and Administration at HYUNDAI TRANSYS in West Point, Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai Transys - Georgia Quick Start

facts: Georgia currently leads the nation in committed investments and permanent jobs in EV and battery manufacturing, beating out Michigan and every other state.
Since November 2021, a whopping $22 billion and nearly 24,000 new jobs in private-sector EV and battery manufacturing have been announced for Georgia.

Blue Bird’s factory in Peach County has taken advantage of the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law’s Clean School Bus program to shift production from polluting diesel engines toward more efficient electric buses.
Blue Bird’s Fort Valley facility currently employs 2,000 workers in a town of only 9,000 residents.
Blue Bird has announced plans to ramp up EV bus production from two per day to 20 per day.
Blue Bird is developing a “registered apprentice” program to train workers in partnership with local colleges, high schools and trade schools.
Hyundai operates several EV and battery-related facilities in Georgia.

Hyundai entered into a memorandum of understanding with Savannah Technical College to provide prospective EV industry employees with training for shop operations, electrical principles, and servicing of hybrids and EVs.
Similar EV training programs now exist at Columbus and Augusta Technical Colleges.
Hyundai’s $7.6 billion assembly plant in Ellabell is projected to create upward of 2,000 construction jobs and 8,500 permanent jobs.
Hyundai’s Ellabell plant will eventually build 300,000 EVs annually.
Rivian plans to build its second production facility in Georgia for electric SUVs and pickup trucks.
Rivian's Georgia facility is anticipated to employ 7,500 workers by 2030.
The infusion of EV and battery-industry funding driving Georgia's growth is supported directly by the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and tax credits codified through the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act.
Norwegian battery company FREYR chose to build a $2.6 billion facility near Atlanta instead of in Norway because of incentives from the IRA.

Out of 130 options across 25 states, the Atlanta metro area was selected for FREYR due to its strong connections to air, sea and rail ports and a robust engineering workforce trained at schools like Georgia Tech.
EV policy has become a partisan lightning rod, with Democratic leaders often claiming to be pro-EV and Republicans against.
Georgia Republican Governor Brian Kemp recently visited the Kia plant in West Point to celebrate hundreds of new jobs accompanying production of the EV9.
The EV9 won recognition as the North American Utility Vehicle of the Year.

97 percent of the nearly 24,000 EV and battery manufacturing jobs announced for Georgia since the passage of the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law are located in congressional districts represented by Republicans.
These EV facilities are creating significant tax revenues for the state and cementing Georgia’s place as a major technology hub in the growing clean-energy economy.
Governor Brian Kemp stated after driving the first Georgia-built EV off the production line at West Point’s Kia factory: “We are working to become the e-mobility capital of the nation.”
source_title: The Emerging Leader in EV Production: Georgia, not Michigan

facts: Since December 2021, North Carolina has announced two major projects in the electric vehicle industry.
Toyota, a Japanese automotive company, will construct a battery manufacturing facility at the Greensboro-Randolph megasite.
VinFast, a Vietnamese electric vehicle company, will bring its first US manufacturing plant to Moncure in Chatham County.
Toyota announced an automotive battery plant on an 1,800-acre megasite in the Greensboro-Randolph area in December of 2021.
The $1.29 billion Toyota project is expected to be online by 2025.
The Toyota battery plant will create 1,750 jobs.
The battery plant will initially have four production lines in operation to produce 200,000 batteries per year.
The Toyota battery plant has the capability to expand to six production lines.
Within 20 years, economic growth from the Toyota plant is expected to increase by $9.5 billion in North Carolina.
The Toyota battery plant project has received around $439 million in state and local incentives for its development.
The Toyota battery plant location is just 30 miles southwest of Burlington.
The Toyota site offers easy access to the highway, railroad, airport, and seaports.
Quality schools to train employees in related manufacturing industries are located within Randolph County and its surrounding counties.
There is strong speculation that Toyota will soon announce an even larger expansion at the new battery plant.
The 2022-23 North Carolina Budget, passed by state lawmakers and signed into law in mid-July, contained an appropriation of $225 million to “the megasite industrial park in Randolph County.”
No company was specifically named for the $225 million megasite appropriation.
The state budget agreement would trigger if a mystery company added an additional 2,125 jobs with capital investment between $1.7 and $2.1 billion.
Most of the $225 million appropriation would be for site work, roadwork, and wetlands mitigation, with some available for job creation.
In March 2022, North Carolina welcomed its first automobile manufacturing facility within the state: VinFast.
VinFast chose Moncure in Chatham County to construct a new automobile manufacturing facility.
The VinFast project will bring 7,500 jobs to the area over the course of five years.
The 2,100-acre Triangle Innovation Point megasite will host the VinFast project and supplier industries.
The goal for the VinFast facility is to begin operation in July of 2024.
VinFast expects initial vehicle output capacity to be around 150,000 annually.
VinFast manufacturing will focus on two models of VinFast SUVs in addition to producing batteries for electric vehicles and electric buses.
The arrival of VinFast marks the largest North Carolina economic development project in history.
By 2026, VinFast plans to invest $4 billion in both the company and the county.
Within 30 years, the VinFast facility is expected to grow the North Carolina economy by $71.6 billion.
The North Carolina Department of Commerce has approved $854 million in state incentives for the VinFast project.
The VinFast project also received $400 million in local incentives.
Electric vehicles help to improve fuel economy, lower fuel costs, and reduce emissions.
Using a battery electric vehicle (BEV) or plug-in hybrid electric vehicle (PHEV) means energy use does not rely on petroleum consumption but rather electricity.
Electricity sources for EVs can extend to outputs such as solar energy, coal, nuclear energy, wind energy, and hydropower.
The manufacturing work from these projects will bring in higher paying and higher skilled jobs to the area.
Higher paying jobs increase average spending within the community, which can support local businesses and supply additional funding for community events.

Although the EV projects are locating in Chatham County and Randolph County, surrounding communities like Burlington are likely to experience economic benefits from related manufacturing industries moving close to the automotive plants.
This will introduce indirect job creation to surrounding areas, producing a much wider economic impact than just the EV projects themselves.
VinFast does not have an established presence in the United States.
North Carolina has never had an automobile facility before this project.
Both the state and VinFast have something to prove and are hoping to demonstrate success.
Electric vehicles are relatively new within the automotive industry, so the newness of the technology presents risks.
There is concern with existing supply chain issues exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic and other contributors, which could lead to stutters in the workforce needed and the supply chain at hand.
The introduction of such a novel industry is high risk but can lead to high reward.

North Carolina is taking a high-risk, high-reward perspective on VinFast and Toyota after seeing the success of automotive manufacturing in other states and the increasing need for alternative forms of energy consumption.
The location of these two manufacturing facilities is just south of Burlington.
Alamance County shares county lines with Chatham County, where VinFast will relocate.
Alamance County also shares a border with Randolph County, the location of the Toyota plant.

Projects on the scale of VinFast and Toyota, with new job creation estimates nearing 10,000, will most certainly have significant regional impacts in future supplier site development and the regional workforce.
There is a significant opportunity for the relocation of not only people who will work for VinFast and Toyota, but also the relocation of suppliers, which can create numerous jobs within Burlington.
Many comparisons are drawn to the BMW facility in South Carolina.
BMW pledged to employ 2,000 people within 8 years of its announcement in 1992.
Thirty years later, BMW employs more than 11,000 people and has invested $12 billion in the facility.
Many manufacturing operations of suppliers flocked to South Carolina after the establishment of BMW.
For every one BMW employee, around 2.5 to 3.5 indirect jobs were created by suppliers.
There was a noticeable increase of international companies to South Carolina following the BMW facility.
Before the BMW facility was built, there were 34 international companies present in South Carolina.
Twenty years later, there are more than 130 international companies present in South Carolina.
Should VinFast and Toyota grow successfully in the next few years, North Carolina could see stark changes in its industrial scenes at the heart of the state.
An essential aspect of manufacturing work is the presence of accessible education and training.
Alamance Community College offers programs in fields such as mechatronics, industrial systems, welding, and more.
Elon University will be a promising asset, especially given its construction of the Innovation Quad to house the Engineering and Physics programs.
Once VinFast and Toyota begin the hiring process, they will look for people in these educational programs.
Burlington is a growing, attractive area for workers to reside in with an easy commute to work at those locations.
Burlington offers quality education, available areas for manufacturing, and a thriving community to encourage workers to want to live in.
North Carolina has not had a strong presence in the automotive industry until recently but is eager to start onto a road of success.
source_title: Introducing North Carolina's Electric Vehicle Industry  | Burlington, NC - Official Website

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) hosted its Grand Opening celebration in Ellabell, Ga., on March 26, 2025.
The ceremony marks the completion of the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history, occurring just two and a half years after breaking ground.
HMGMA is located in Bryan County, Georgia, approximately 20 miles outside of historic Savannah on a 2,906-acre Megasite.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated the plant represents advanced manufacturing capabilities, innovation, and investment in relationships with partners and communities in Georgia.

The event was attended by Governor Brian P. Kemp, U.S. Representative Buddy Carter, Vice Chair Jaehoon Chang, Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO José Muñoz, Kia Corporation President and CEO Ho Sung Song, company officials, state and local leaders, HMGMA Meta Pro employees, and other distinguished guests.

Governor Brian P. Kemp highlighted the plant as a once-in-a-generation opportunity enabled by partnerships with job creators and Georgia’s universities and technical colleges, including Georgia Quick Start’s on-site training facility.

Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) entered into a landmark agreement with the State of Georgia in May 2022 to establish an electric and hybrid vehicle production and battery manufacturing facility in Ellabell, Georgia.
HMGMA was officially formed by June 2022, with groundbreaking occurring within four months of formation.
On October 3, 2024, less than two years after breaking ground, the first vehicle—a Hyundai IONIQ 5—rolled off the assembly line at HMGMA.
The Hyundai IONIQ 9 three-row electric SUV is now in production at HMGMA and will be joined in 2026 by the first Kia model built at the facility.
In addition to electric vehicles for Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia, the plant is equipped to produce hybrid vehicles.
HMGMA initially has a capacity to produce 300,000 vehicles annually.
The Group plans to expand HMGMA's annual production capacity to a total of 500,000 units, an increase of 200,000 units from the original plan.

Hyundai Motor Group’s investment in Georgia related to HMGMA and joint battery ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK On is $12.6 billion, representing the largest single investment in the state's history.
The $12.6 billion investment includes a total of 8,500 jobs at HMGMA by 2031.

The Group’s total investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs and generate $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year, according to the Center for Automotive Research.

As of July 2024, more than $2.5 billion in capital investment and approximately 6,900 jobs have been announced by HMGMA suppliers across 12 counties in Georgia, more than doubling the originally anticipated $1 billion supplier investment.
To date, Hyundai Motor Group has invested more than $20.5 billion in the U.S., creating or supporting over 570,000 jobs nationwide.
Earlier this week (relative to March 26, 2025), the Group announced a significant investment of $21 billion in the U.S. from 2025 to 2028.
Of the $21 billion commitment, $9 billion will be invested to establish an annual production capacity in the U.S. of 1.2 million vehicles.
The Group’s existing U.S. production sites—Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Autoland Georgia—will also see improvements in production facilities.

A total of $6 billion will be allocated to enhance the Group’s parts and logistics business and establish an Electric Arc Furnace-based integrated steel mill in the U.S. through affiliate Hyundai Steel, with an annual steel production capacity of 2.7 million tons.
The new steel mill will supply high-quality automotive steel plates to HMGMA when commercial steel production begins in 2029.

An additional $6 billion will be invested to drive innovation and expand strategic partnerships with U.S. companies in autonomous driving, robotics, artificial intelligence (AI), and advanced air mobility (AAM).
Through the $21 billion commitment, the Group anticipates creating 14,000 new direct full-time jobs in the U.S. by 2028.
The overall economic impact of the $21 billion commitment is expected to generate more than 100,000 direct and indirect job opportunities across related industries.
HMGMA’s architecture blends advanced technology with design elements reflecting Hyundai’s brand identity and the natural beauty of the Savannah area.
The facility has integrated proof-of-concept manufacturing technologies and systems from Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center Singapore (HMGICS), scaled for larger production volumes.
All HMGMA processes—including order collection, procurement, logistics, and production—are optimized using AI and real-time data.
Cutting-edge robotics and vision systems ensure high-quality vehicle production at the plant.
The innovative manufacturing system creates a human-centered work environment where robots assist human workers to improve efficiency.

HMGMA expands its mixed-model production capability and integrates various robot processes, including automated guided vehicles for logistics, Boston Dynamic’s Spot for exterior quality inspection in the weld shop, and parking robots in the assembly shop.
Automation in HMGMA’s assembly shop is nearly double that of the Group’s existing facilities.

Oscar Kwon, CEO of HMGMA, stated that creating a comfortable and welcoming work environment for employees (Meta Pros) is as important as building an energy-efficient and technologically advanced plant.
All work zones from office to production areas feature open layouts with ample daylight, indoor landscaping, and team centers with skylights for employee breaks and meetings.

The 3,050-foot-long front of the HMGMA building’s exterior is lit in two parallel lines inspired by a stream, outlining an area where interstate travelers can view vehicles on conveyors from paint to general assembly.
A solar parking lot at the front of the site will generate 5.2 megawatts of energy, accounting for roughly five percent of the Metaplant’s total power consumption.
The solar panels are installed above 1,878 parking spaces, providing shade while producing electricity.
A 41-acre ecological park featuring trails, running tracks, sports fields, and picnic areas is under construction in front of the Metaplant for employees to use before or after work.
HMGMA has deployed Hyundai Motor XCIENT heavy-duty hydrogen fuel-cell electric trucks for clean logistics operations.

A total of 21 XCIENT trucks are currently in operation, representing more than a third of the Glovis America truck fleet at HMGMA that transports vehicle parts from suppliers across the region to the Megasite daily.
Since 2022, Hyundai and Genesis have donated nearly $4.5 million to local organizations in Georgia through social responsibility initiatives and nonprofit partnerships.

Kia's Autoland Georgia factory in West Point has provided over $22 million in support of Georgia communities since 2009, including more than $6 million for educational programs such as the THINC College & Career Academy and SAE’s “A World in Motion.”

links.url: Newsroom: Media Hub by Hyundai , Kia Global Newsroom , Genesis Global Newsroom

links.context: Contact email for Dain Kang, Global PR Strategy & Planning

source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America Celebrates Grand Opening, Powering U.S. Economic Growth

facts: Over the last three years, electric transportation companies continue to choose Georgia for their new locations, while current companies expand to meet demand.
Rivian is establishing its East Coast headquarters in Atlanta.
RJ Scaringe, Founder and CEO of Rivian, stated that Atlanta embodies top talent, exceptional creativity, and a desire to always be moving forward.
The Rivian East Coast head office will feature a 500-person office space.
The Rivian office space is intended to complement a new, state-of-the-art manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Rivian's headquarters announcement was sourced from a Rivian Press Release dated 7.72025.
South Korea-based Doowon Climate Control is creating 200 jobs in Metter, Georgia.
Doowon Climate Control's new manufacturing facility is located in the Metter-Candler County I-16 Industrial Park.
The Doowon facility will primarily produce climate control systems for use in traditional and electric vehicles.

Ki Chun Sung, CEO of Doowon Climate Control America, Inc., stated that the company is making significant investments to establish its inaugural automotive air conditioning system production facility in the United States.
Doowon Climate Control America expressed optimism that its technological expertise and industry experience will play a vital role in the growth of the local community.
The Doowon Climate Control announcement was published by Area Development News Desk on 2.7.2024.
source_title: Georgia - A Hub for Electric Transportation | Select Georgia

facts: OSHA cited U.S. Battery Manufacturing Co. for exposing employees to lead, unguarded machinery, and other safety hazards at its facility in Augusta, Georgia.
The company faces proposed penalties of $115,594.
OSHA cited the manufacturer for exposing employees to lead exceeding the permissible exposure limit.
OSHA cited the manufacturer for exposing employees to arsenic above the action level.
The company failed to record on OSHA's 300 log that it medically removed employees from exposure.
The company failed to conduct required training.
The company failed to implement a medical surveillance program.
The company failed to properly guard equipment.
Elevated lead levels can cause debilitating and permanent health issues, according to OSHA Atlanta-East Area Director William Fulcher.
OSHA's lead standard requires employers to minimize workers' exposure by using measures including engineering controls, safe work practices, and providing protective clothing and equipment.
The investigation was initiated under OSHA's Regional Emphasis Program on Lead.

The company has 15 business days from receipt of the citations and proposed penalties to comply, request an informal conference with OSHA's area director, or contest the findings before the independent Occupational Safety and Health Review Commission.
Under the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970, employers are responsible for providing safe and healthful workplaces for their employees.
source_title: U.S. Department of Labor Cites Georgia Battery Manufacturer For Lead and Other Safety Hazards; Proposes $115,594 in Penalties | U.S. Department of Labor

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America LLC
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America LLC
facts: Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung, Governor Brian P. Kemp, and company officials, along with state and local leaders, officially broke ground on the new manufacturing plant at the Bryan County Mega site in Bryan County, Ga., on Oct. 25, 2022.

The celebration included a morning groundbreaking followed by a future technology expo at Enmarket Arena in Savannah highlighting advanced technology and human achievement for hundreds of community attendees.
Hyundai Motor Group announced a $5.54 billion investment for the project five months ago.
The facility plans to produce a diverse range of innovative Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia electric vehicles (EVs) alongside a new battery manufacturing facility.
HMGMA will create more than 8,100 jobs over the next few years.
The company aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV batteries and other EV components in the U.S. market through this project.
HMGMA’s new smart factory is expected to begin commercial production in the first half of 2025.

Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated that Hyundai and Genesis EVs are recognized as best in class and expressed determination to become the global leader in electrification, safety, quality, and sustainability through this partnership.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp highlighted record jobs and investment coming to all parts of the state, citing award-winning workforce development programs and infrastructure as drivers of economic growth alongside the Hyundai partnership.
Approximately $1 billion is anticipated to be invested by non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project in other locations.
Hyundai Motor Group plans to sell 3.23 million full electric vehicles annually globally by 2030 as part of its accelerated electrification efforts.
The group intends to establish a global EV production network to ensure a stable supply of EVs worldwide and maintain its position as one of the top three EV providers in the U.S.

The investment aligns with the U.S. government’s roadmap to accelerate electrification, following a federal initiative requiring zero-emission vehicles to account for at least half of OEM new vehicle sales by 2030.
The Georgia facility will utilize a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem to realize customer value.
All production processes, including order collection, procurement, logistics, and production, will be optimized using AI and data.
The innovative manufacturing system will create a human-centered work environment where robots assist human workers.
The plant will primarily rely on renewable energy sources to power operations and will use emission-reduction technologies to meet RE100 requirements.
Hyundai Motor Group has made total donations of $1.54 million as part of its corporate social responsibility initiatives for the project.
Hyundai and Genesis, along with various social responsibility initiatives and nonprofit organizations, donated $940,000 to local organizations.

This includes programs through Hyundai Hope and Genesis Gives, which have previously collectively committed $120,000 to nearly ten organizations supporting local communities in and around Bryan County.
Kia’s Accelerate the Good corporate social responsibility program will donate $600,000 to help local youth in Bryan County realize their hopes and dreams.
Kia’s initiatives include purchasing homes for homeless families that recently lost their homes.
Kia’s initiatives establish after-school programs where low-income children can eat and receive tutoring.
Kia’s initiatives pay travel expenses for pediatric cancer care at St. Jude in Memphis, Tennessee.
Kia’s initiatives support low-income children with technology and resources to facilitate learning.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Breaks Ground on Metaplant America Dedicated EV and Battery Plant

canonical_name: Solvay Battery-Grade Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF) Manufacturing Facility
facts: National Energy Technology Laboratory (NETL) prepared a Draft EA (DOE/EA-2237) for the Solvay Battery-Grade Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF) Manufacturing Facility project in Richmond County, Georgia.
The Draft EA evaluates the potential environmental consequences of DOE providing financial assistance to Solvay Specialty Polymers USA, LLC.
Solvay Specialty Polymers USA, LLC is a member of the Syensqo Group.
The project involves the construction of a chemical manufacturing facility for the production of PVDF (a highly non-reactive thermoplastic) for use in electric vehicle batteries.
At maximum capacity, the facility would support the production of more than 5 million EV batteries per year.
There are no public comment opportunities at this time.
source_title: DOE/EA-2237: Solvay Battery-Grade PVDF Manufacturing Facility; Richmond County, Georgia | Department of Energy

facts: Freyr Battery is a Norway-based clean-tech company named after a Norse god.
Freyr Battery will invest $2.57 billion in Georgia by building a plant on a 368-acre site in Coweta County.
The facility will produce Lithium-Ion battery cells for stationary energy storage, electric mobility, and additional applications.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp touted Freyr as the latest investment in Georgia’s fast-growing electric battery industry.
Georgia’s electric battery industry is bolstered by forthcoming electric vehicle factories and parts suppliers.
Tom Einar Jenson serves as Freyr’s co-founder and CEO.
Jenson stated the Coweta factory represents a large step in enhancing the U.S. domestic supply chain for Lithium-Ion batteries, a technology currently dominated by China.
Jensen said Freyr is deeply committed to decarbonizing the transportation and energy sectors with U.S. partners.
Freyr expects to make meaningful investments to spur job creation and develop localized, decarbonized supply chains in the U.S. to enhance energy security and economic activity.
The factory will be named Giga America.
Giga America is expected to create 723 jobs over the next seven years.
Georgia and Coweta County are collectively providing strong financial incentives for the Giga America project, though specific amounts were not disclosed.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development declined to provide further details on the incentives, stating the project is still active.
The facility will be located at the Bridgeport Industrial Park site.
Bridgeport Industrial Park is an industrial megasite off I-85 near Newnan.
Coweta County Development Authority Chair John Daviston welcomed the venture, citing the addition of quality and highly-skilled jobs as a significant stimulus to the local economy.
source_title: Georgia Lands New Gigantic $2.6B Electric Battery Plant

facts: Sales of electric vehicles in Georgia are down about 80% in the last year and a half, the result of lawmakers slashing the state’s generous tax incentive—and replacing it with a new registration fee.
The state’s $5,000 tax credit for the purchase of an electric vehicle was eliminated in July 2015, and a $200 registration fee took its place.
EV sales fell from more than 1,400 before the credit expired to less than 250.
The decline has remained consistent in the last year and a half.
Georgia has the second-most electric vehicles on the road, but it’s likely to lose that distinction as sales have stalled, the predictable result of a large incentive coming off the table.
It was one of the largest incentives around the country, before lawmakers repealed it.
Atlanta Electric Vehicle Development Coalition founder Jeff Cohen told the Journal-Constitution that the state should have about 40,000 EVs on the roads.
Jeff Cohen said, “We’re not growing.”
Georgia currently has about 25,000 electric vehicles registered in the state.
California, by comparison, has over 100,000 electric vehicles.
The tax credit helped Georgia develop a reputation for a growing EV market, but some lawmakers believed the state was spending too much.
A Georgia State University Center report found the tax credit cost the state over $1 million in 2012 and over $14 million in 2013.
Georgia Power is building a network of public charging stations.
Georgia Power has developed about three dozen locations in Atlanta and other cities through its “Get Current. Drive Electric” initiative.
The “Get Current. Drive Electric” initiative ran its course through the end of the year.
There are about 520,000 plug-in electric vehicles in the United States, according to Plug-in America.
There are more than 15,000 public charging stations and almost 40,000 charging outlets in the United States, according to the U.S. Department of Energy.
source_title: Georgia electric vehicle sales shrink 80% in wake of tax credit repeal | Utility Dive

facts: Automotive parts manufacturer NVH Korea will invest $72 million to build a new assembly plant in Locust Grove, GA.
The factory is expected to create more than 160 jobs.
NVH Korea specializes in door trim, thermal management systems, and components for reducing noise and vibration.
The company already has a factory in Columbus, GA that produces floor mats, cargo mats and cargo trays.
The new facility will produce battery parts for electric vehicles for Hyundai Mobis, Kia Georgia, and Hyundai Motor Group.
The factory is scheduled to begin production in the second quarter of 2024.
source_title: EV Supplier to Create 160 Manufacturing Jobs in Georgia | ASSEMBLY

entity_name: LG Energy Solution-Hyundai Georgia Battery Plant
canonical_name: LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group Joint Venture Battery Plant
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced they will build a $4.3 billion electric battery plant as part of Hyundai’s new electric vehicle assembly plant in southeast Georgia.
The companies will split the investment for the project.
Production at the plant is slated to start as soon as late 2025.
Hyundai Motor Co. CEO Jaehoon Chang said the battery plant would “create a strong foundation to lead the global EV transition.”
Chang explained the company wants to speed up efforts to produce electrified Hyundai and Kia vehicles in North America.
Chang stated, “Hyundai Motor Group is focusing on its electrification efforts to secure a leadership position in the global auto industry.”
In 2022, Hyundai said it would invest $5.5 billion to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, just west of Savannah.
The Ellabell site is supposed to have 8,100 employees.
The Ellabell site is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Garrison Douglas, a spokesperson for Republican Gov. Brian Kemp, said the 3,000-job battery plant would be part of the 8,100 overall jobs.
Douglas said the $4.3 billion investment would be part of the previously announced $5.5 billion total.
The Hyundai/LG plant is supposed to be able to supply batteries for 300,000 electric vehicles per year.
This capacity matches the initial projected production of the adjoining vehicle assembly plant.
Hyundai has said the Georgia plant could later expand to build 500,000 vehicles annually.

Gov. Brian Kemp said, “This is exactly what we envisioned when Georgia landed the Hyundai Metaplant in May of last year, and this project is the latest milestone in Georgia’s path to becoming the EV capital of the nation.”
Auto parts suppliers have pledged to invest more than $2 billion in the region around the Hyundai site.
Those auto parts suppliers have pledged to hire 4,800 people in the region around the Hyundai site.
The announcements are part of an electric vehicle and battery land rush across the United States.
Under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be assembled in North America to qualify for a full $7,500 EV tax credit.
A certain percentage of battery parts and minerals must come from North America or a U.S. free trade partner to qualify for the full $7,500 EV tax credit under the IRA.
Currently, no Hyundai or Kia vehicles are eligible for the tax credit unless they are leased.
Hyundai opposed having foreign-made vehicles excluded from the tax credit, in part because it is building American factories.
Gov. Brian Kemp has supported that position on the tax credit exclusion.
Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff said Hyundai should wait until it is producing vehicles domestically, using American-made batteries.

Sen. Jon Ossoff told reporters in an online news conference Friday morning that as soon as those vehicles are produced in Georgia, they can be eligible for the credits through the incentives in the IRA.

Ossoff stated, “These manufacturing incentives are attracting and accelerating billions of dollars of investments in jobs and advanced energy and electric vehicle production capacity here in the state of Georgia.”
LG said this would be its seventh battery plant in operation or under construction in the U.S.
LG is concentrating efforts to expand production in the country, citing federal incentives as a lure for manufacturers.
This is the second huge electric battery plant that Hyundai is partnering to build in Georgia.
Hyundai and SK On, a unit of South Korea’s SK Group, announced in December they would jointly invest $4 billion to $5 billion to build a new plant northwest of Atlanta.
The Hyundai/SK On plant will supply electric batteries for Hyundai and Kia electric vehicles assembled in the U.S.
That plant, in Cartersville, is planned to begin production in 2025.
The Cartersville plant is projected to employ a projected 3,500 people.
Hyundai will need batteries for more than just vehicles made in Ellabell.
The company is already assembling electric vehicles at its plant in Montgomery, Alabama.
In April, Hyundai announced it would start assembling its electric Kia EV9 large SUV at the Kia plant in West Point, Georgia.
Partnering with LG and SK also will diversify Hyundai’s supplier base.
The partnerships give the automaker more than one battery manufacturer from which to buy.
Because the battery plant is part of the overall Hyundai complex, no additional incentives would be offered for it.
The state of Georgia and local governments already have pledged $1.8 billion in tax breaks and other incentives.
Greg LeRoy, executive director of Good Jobs First, said the $1.8 billion is the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised an automotive plant.
Good Jobs First is described as a group skeptical of subsidies to private companies.
source_title: Hyundai and LG announce $4.3 billion plant in Georgia to build batteries for electric vehicles | AP News

facts: Conducts research on lithium-ion batteries, including developing new electrode materials for higher energy density and lower cost.
Performs modeling of reaction and degradation mechanisms.
Develops low-temperature chemistries.
Studies interfaces in battery systems.
Researches solid-state batteries, focusing on solid electrolyte development.
Performs cell engineering for high energy and improved safety.
Develops high-capacity anodes.
Conducts chemo-mechanical modeling.
Explores flow batteries for renewable energy storage.
Investigates sodium-ion batteries.
Investigates aqueous zinc batteries.
Researches supercapacitors.
Characterizes battery materials and cells using X-ray spectroscopy.
Characterizes battery materials and cells using electron microscopy.
Characterizes battery materials and cells using X-ray imaging and tomography.
Characterizes battery materials and cells using Raman spectroscopy.
Characterizes battery materials and cells using X-ray scattering.
Develops new processes for Li-ion battery recycling.
Implements AI-enabled manufacturing.
Sources sustainable materials for batteries.
source_title: Research – Georgia Tech Advanced Battery Center

entity_name: Toyo Tire North America Manufacturing Inc.
canonical_name: Toyo Tire North America Manufacturing Inc.
facts: Georgia Department of Economic Development named Toyo Tire North America Manufacturing Inc. the first-ever winner of its Automotive Supplier of the Year Award.

The Georgia Automotive Awards recognize companies and individuals throughout the state for their contributions to build the state’s automotive ecosystem in five categories: Company of the Year, Leader of the Year, Innovation Award, Supplier of the Year and Community Impact Award.
Jim Hawk, Toyo Tires’ chairman, stated that Georgia is a key market for Toyo Tires and its work there is paramount to the organization's overall success.
Jim Hawk cited Georgia’s robust automotive industry and network, business-friendly tax incentives, and low cost of doing business as factors making it an ideal location for Toyo Tires' growth path.
Toyo Tire North America Manufacturing Inc. was awarded the Automotive Supplier of the Year Award for its growth and contributions to the state’s economic development efforts.
Toyo has more than doubled the size of its facility in White, Ga., since its groundbreaking in 2004.
The facility increased its staff from 81 employees to 1,180.
The facility’s fourth expansion is scheduled to be complete in 2017.
The expansion will bring the manufacturing space to 3 million square feet.
The expansion will increase annual production to 9 million tires.
Bartow County Commissioner Steve Taylor stated that the expansion will boost Toyo’s local investment to more than $1 billion.
source_title: Toyo Honored with Georgia Automotive Supplier Award - Tire Review Magazine

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced two years ago that South Korea’s Hyundai Motor Group was going to build an electric vehicle plant near Savannah.
The deal promised nearly $8 billion and more than 8,000 well-paying jobs for hard-working Georgians.
A federal raid occurred last week at the Hyundai Metaplant and the EV battery facility that is a joint venture between Hyundai and Korean-company LG Energy Solution.
Federal officials detained approximately 475 foreign workers during an ongoing investigation into illegal labor practices.
Approximately 300 of the detained workers were Korean.
The Current GA has published numerous stories about misleading wage claims and workplace safety problems at the sprawling, 2,300-acre site in Bryan County.
Hyundai assembled a complete supply chain of companies on the site that are separate legal entities but all have a role in meeting employment and wage targets for Georgia.
Georgia provided $2 billion in state tax credits and abatements to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, the company that oversees production at the metaplant.
Prior to the raid, HMGMA said it was not responsible for the actions of affiliates, suppliers, and subcontractors allegedly violating federal workplace safety laws.
Hyundai announced on Friday that it was bringing a senior executive to supervise the firms amid the ongoing federal investigation at the battery plant.
As of Monday, no arrests have been made regarding the detained workers.
It is still unclear who employed those detained.
The search warrant authorizing the raid cited the battery plant as a target, along with five other companies that appear to have served as subcontractors for the plant.
Operations at the Hyundai site are intertwined, meaning a worker in one company may have been recruited by a different company or subcontractor.
To build EVs, Hyundai organized its whole supply chain at the site, including affiliates Hyundai Material, Hyundai Mobis, Hyundai Glovis, Hyundai Steel, and Hyundai Transys.
The supply chain also includes the battery factory with LG Energy Solution.
Together, those companies must invest $5.545 billion and hire at least 8,500 workers by 2031 making an average of $58,105 in order to get tax credits and tax deferments offered by the state.
As of mid-March, Hyundai told The Current that approximately 1,200 people were employed across the site, not counting Koreans.
More than a dozen suppliers have set up shop in surrounding counties to make components such as brakes and bumpers.
The Current has published investigative stories revealing alleged violations of labor and workplace safety laws at the plant.
source_title: Explainer: What Hyundai companies are part of the Savannah-area car plant? - The Current

facts: Rivian Automotive Inc. is an electric truck startup valued at more than $100 billion.
Rivian is planning to announce that it will build a vehicle-assembly and battery plant in Georgia.
source_title: Rivian to announce plan for Georgia EV factory, report says - Automotive News

facts: Kia unveiled a new electric vehicle Wednesday, which the automaker says it will manufacture at its existing Georgia factory in West Point.
The EV9 will be the Korean automaker’s first three-row electric SUV, mirroring the company’s gas-powered West Point-built Telluride model.
The new EV was shown off Wednesday for the first time in North America at the 2023 New York International Auto Show.
Kia says the EV9 will go on sale in the U.S. by the end of this year, and the model will be built in Georgia starting in 2024.

Sean Yoon, president and CEO, Kia North America and Kia America, stated: “With the launch of the EV9, we are incorporating our experience in producing brilliantly designed, best-in-class SUVs with our deep expertise in developing the industry’s leading EVs.”
Sean Yoon described the EV9 as a credible, high-value SUV that encapsulates Kia’s approach to bringing innovative, consumer-focused products to market with a serious commitment to electrification.
Kia announced a $5.5 billion investment in Georgia for its Kia Motors Innovation Hub and a partnership with Savannah State University.
The EV9 model is expected to create between 3 million to 5 million jobs.
Kia announced a $12 billion investment for its Georgia EV plant construction on December 5, 2022.
A California-based EV charging network operator is part of the plant's supply chain for electric vehicle components.
The West Point plant will manufacture both the gas-powered Telluride and the new EV9.
Kia is exploring facility expansion and maximizing local manufacturing talent for career opportunities in the region.
source_title: Georgia at center of Kia’s EV campaign with new electric SUV

facts: Georgia is among the top ten largest markets in the nation for Electric Vehicle (EV) charging.
By 2030, it is predicted that 50% of all new car sales will be electric vehicles.
Georgia Power is committed to connecting communities with charging infrastructure at home and in public spaces so drivers can explore the state with range confidence.
As the number of electric vehicles on the road increases, Georgia Power is committed to increasing the availability of electric vehicle charging stations.
Georgia Power customers may be eligible to receive up to a $150 rebate for installing a Level 2 Charger in their home.
The rebate is available while funds last and the amount is subject to change.
Shopping for Level 2 Chargers on the Georgia Power Marketplace provides an instant rebate of $150.
Electric vehicles provide quiet, clean, and efficient transportation while offering savings on fuel and maintenance costs and reducing environmental emissions.
All electric and plug-in hybrid cars purchased in or after 2010 may be eligible for a federal tax credit.
