Charging an electric vehicle can cost as little as $0 when using a standard 120-volt circuit available in every home.
Georgia Power recommends using a dedicated 120-volt outlet to avoid overloading the circuit.
Higher-powered chargers are available for faster charging but may require a permit for home installation due to higher voltage requirements.
source_title: EV Charging & Rebates | Georgia Power

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on 11.07.2022 that Joon Georgia, Inc. will create 630 new jobs and invest $317 million in Bulloch County.
The company’s new manufacturing facility marks the first confirmed supplier for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that less than two weeks after the groundbreaking of Hyundai Motor Group’s innovative facility, they are already seeing regional impact and Joon Georgia will be the first of many companies drawn to Georgia to support the Metaplant.
Joon Georgia is part of Ajin USA, a current supplier for multiple Hyundai Motor Group facilities.
The company produces a variety of automotive body parts and electronics for original equipment manufacturers.
Jung Ho Sea, CEO of Ajin USA, stated that Joon Georgia will create approximately 630 new jobs with competitive benefits in Bulloch County and the State of Georgia.

The company aims to be the best parts supplier for automotive and EV companies like Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, and the best employer and corporate citizen giving back to the community of Bulloch County and the City of Statesboro.
Joon Georgia’s new facility will be located at Bruce Yawn Commerce Park, previously known as Southern Gateway Commerce Park, near Statesboro.
The site is in close proximity to the Port of Savannah, allowing both Hyundai and Joon Georgia to access global markets quickly and efficiently.
Operations at the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park are expected to begin in mid-2024.
The company will begin hiring mid-2023.

Billy Allen, Chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, noted that local education resources, including Georgia Southern University and Ogeechee Technical College, will provide critical workforce support for Joon Georgia.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project was executed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Quick Start, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia EMC.
Workforce development for the new facility will be supported by Quick Start, described as the top-ranked workforce training program in the nation.

By partnering with the Technical College System of Georgia, the program helps assess workers, train new employees on unique processes, and develop customized job-specific training using the most current techniques and media.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Hyundai Motor Group, its suppliers, and the Savannah area JDA are creating generational opportunities for communities in Georgia.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Non-affiliated suppliers connected to the Hyundai project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: First Hyundai Motor Group Supplier to Build Facility in Bulloch County | Development Authority of Bulloch County

entity_name: Georgia Tech / Hailong Chen Research Team
facts: A multi-institutional research team led by Georgia Tech’s Hailong Chen has developed a new, low-cost cathode material for lithium-ion batteries.
The revolutionary cathode material is iron chloride (FeCl3).
FeCl3 costs a mere 1-2% of typical cathode materials while storing the same amount of electricity.
Cathode materials play a major role in a battery’s capacity, energy, efficiency, lifespan, and affordability.
The team published their findings on the new cathode in Nature Sustainability.
“For a long time, people have been looking for a lower-cost, more sustainable alternative to existing cathode materials. I think we’ve got one,” said Chen.
Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) were first commercialized by Sony in the early 1990s.
Batteries are currently responsible for about 50% of an EV’s total cost.
Chen’s FeCl3 cathode would be the fifth type successfully commercialized for LIBs.
The development represents a significant step forward toward all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries.
Conventional LIBs use liquid electrolytes that have hard limits on energy storage and can leak or catch fire.
All-solid-state LIBs use solid electrolytes to dramatically boost efficiency, reliability, safety, and energy capacity.
With the FeCl3 cathode, a solid electrolyte, and a lithium metal anode, the cost of the whole battery system is 30-40% of current LIBs.
“Our cathode can be a game-changer,” said Chen.
The technology may be less than five years from commercial viability in EVs.
Currently popular EV cathodes are oxides that require a gigantic amount of costly nickel and cobalt.
FeCl3 contains only iron (Fe) and chlorine (Cl), which are abundant, affordable elements found in steel and table salt.
In initial tests, FeCl3 performed as well as or better than other, much more expensive cathodes.
FeCl3 has a higher operational voltage than the popularly used LiFePO4 (lithium iron phosphate) cathode.

“This could not only make EVs much cheaper than internal combustion cars, but it provides a new and promising form of large-scale energy storage, enhancing the resilience of the electrical grid,” said Chen.
The research was led by Chen and postdoc Zhantao Liu, who is the lead author of the study.
Collaborators included Ting Zhu and Yuanzhi Tang from Georgia Tech, Jue Liu from Oak Ridge National Laboratory, and Shuo Chen from the University of Houston.
Funding for the project came from the National Science Foundation under Grant Nos. 1706723 and 2108688.
Chen holds appointments in Georgia Tech’s George W. Woodruff School of Mechanical Engineering and the School of Materials Science and Engineering.
The team's lab began researching solid electrolyte materials in 2019.
Initial attempts starting in 2019 to make solid-state batteries using chloride-based solid electrolytes with traditional oxide-based cathodes failed because the materials did not get along.
Researchers hypothesized that a chloride-based cathode would provide a better pairing with the chloride electrolyte for improved battery performance.
The team is open to opportunities to scale up the technology and push it toward commercial applications.
source_title: New Battery Cathode Material Could Revolutionize EV Market and Energy Storage | News Center

facts: Anovion Technologies is a leading U.S.-based manufacturer of synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries.
The company was founded by industry experts with over 140 years of combined experience in materials science.
Anovion Technologies specializes in producing high-quality battery components essential for electric vehicles, energy storage systems, and various electronic applications.
It is North America’s first commercial producer of synthetic graphite anode materials.
The company contributes significantly to establishing a secure domestic battery supply chain.
Its operations help reduce dependence on international suppliers.
Anovion Technologies is headquartered in Chicago, United States.
source_title: Anovion Technologies - Battery-Tech Network

facts: A multi-institutional research team led by Georgia Tech’s Hailong Chen has developed a new, low-cost cathode for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs).
The new cathode material could radically improve LIBs and potentially transform the electric vehicle (EV) market and large-scale energy storage systems.
Hailong Chen stated that researchers have long sought a lower-cost, more sustainable alternative to existing cathode materials and believe they have found one.
Chen is an associate professor with appointments in Georgia Tech’s George W. Woodruff School of Mechanical Engineering and the School of Materials Science and Engineering.
The revolutionary material developed by the team is iron chloride (FeCl3).
FeCl3 costs a mere 1-2% of typical cathode materials while storing the same amount of electricity.
Cathode materials significantly affect battery capacity, energy, efficiency, performance, lifespan, and affordability.
Chen described the cathode as a potential game-changer that would greatly improve the EV market and the whole lithium-ion battery market.
The team's work detailing this invention was published in Nature Sustainability.
Lithium-ion batteries were first commercialized by Sony in the early 1990s, initially sparking growth in personal electronics before advancing to electric vehicles.
Batteries currently account for about 50% of an EV’s total cost, making clean-energy cars more expensive than internal combustion vehicles.
The Chen team's invention has the potential to change the high cost of LIBs relative to EVs.
Compared to alkaline and lead-acid batteries, LIBs store more energy in a smaller package and power devices longer between charges.
Conventional LIBs contain expensive metals including semiprecious elements like cobalt and nickel, along with high manufacturing costs.
Only four types of cathodes had been successfully commercialized for LIBs prior to this development; Chen’s would be the fifth type.
The invention represents a significant step forward in battery technology through the development of an all-solid-state LIB.
Conventional LIBs use liquid electrolytes to transport lithium ions, which impose hard limits on energy storage and pose risks of leaking and catching fire.
All-solid-state LIBs use solid electrolytes, which dramatically boost efficiency and reliability while making batteries safer and capable of holding more energy.
All-solid-state technology is currently in the development and testing phase globally.
Using the FeCl3 cathode paired with a solid electrolyte and a lithium metal anode reduces the cost of the entire battery system to 30-40% of current LIBs.

Chen noted this advancement could make EVs much cheaper than internal combustion cars, provide new large-scale energy storage options enhancing grid resilience, and improve sustainability and supply chain stability for the EV market.
The team's research into FeCl3 originated in 2019 when they attempted to create solid-state batteries using chloride-based solid electrolytes with traditional oxide-based cathodes.
Initial attempts failed because the traditional cathode and electrolyte materials were incompatible.
Researchers hypothesized that a chloride-based cathode would pair better with the chloride electrolyte for improved battery performance.
Chen explained that FeCl3 was chosen because its crystal structure is potentially suitable for storing and transporting ions, and it functioned as expected.
The most popularly used EV cathodes are oxides requiring large amounts of costly nickel and cobalt, which are heavy, toxic elements posing environmental challenges.
In contrast, the Chen team’s cathode contains only iron and chlorine, which are abundant, affordable, and widely used elements found in steel and table salt.
Initial tests showed FeCl3 performed as well as or better than other, much more expensive cathodes.
FeCl3 demonstrated a higher operational voltage than the popularly used LiFePO4 (lithium iron phosphate) cathode.
The technology may be less than five years away from commercial viability in EVs.
The team will continue investigating FeCl3 and related materials moving forward.
The research was led by Chen and postdoc Zhantao Liu, who is the lead author of the study.

Collaborators on the project included Ting Zhu (Georgia Tech Woodruff School), Yuanzhi Tang (Georgia Tech School of Earth and Atmospheric Sciences), Jue Liu (Oak Ridge National Laboratory), and Shuo Chen (University of Houston).
Chen stated the team aims to perfect the materials in the lab, understand underlying mechanisms, and remains open to scaling up the technology for commercial applications.
The work was funded by the National Science Foundation under Grant Nos. 1706723 and 2108688.
source_title: New Battery Cathode Material Could Revolutionize EV Market and Energy Storage | Research

facts: Lotte Energy Materials Corp., one of South Korea’s leading battery materials makers, plans to build a copper foil plant in the US to meet growing demand for battery components from electric vehicle makers in North America.
Lotte, formerly Iljin Materials Co., last week filed an application with the Delaware state government to establish its US subsidiary.
The application review usually takes two weeks and Lotte is set to start the process of building the copper foil plant as soon as it gains approval.
The company is considering Michigan, Kentucky, Tennessee and Georgia, where US and Korean EV makers operate, as potential locations for the factory site.
Lotte is already in talks with several US state governments to discuss tax benefits for the construction of the battery material plant.
If all goes to plan, Lotte aims to pick a location for the factory by the end of this year and break ground early next year.
Initial investments in the new plant will likely be between 700 billion won and 800 billion won ($526 million-$601 million).
Lotte’s plan to build a copper foil plant in the US is a response to the US Inflation Reduction Act, which favors EV and battery makers operating in North America.
A Lotte official earlier said its clients have been requesting the Korean company to build a plant in the US for closer business cooperation there.
A copper foil, also known as elecfoil, is a thin foil that surrounds the anode, the negative end of a lithium-ion battery.
High-strength copper foils are essential to making safer, high-density rechargeable batteries.
A thin copper foil less than 10 micrometers thick made through electrolysis of a copper sulfate solution, elecfoil is also used to make cathode collectors in rechargeable lithium batteries.
It is widely used in EVs and energy storage systems.
The growth of the EV market is generating robust growth in the elecfoil market.
According to market tracker SNE Research, the US high-end copper foil market is forecast to grow from 10,000 tons in 2022 to 100,000 tons by 2026 and 340,000 tons by 2030.
Lotte currently runs factories in two locations globally: one in Korea with an annual production capacity of 20,000 tons and the other in Malaysia with a capacity of 40,000 tons.

The company, the world’s fourth-largest copper foil maker, is aggressively expanding its production capacity with a plan to raise its Malaysian factory capacity to 60,000 tons annually and build a 30,000-ton plant in Spain with a 560 billion won investment.
The overall copper foil market is expected to rise to 750,000 tons by 2025, according to an industry estimate.
Lotte Energy’s clients include China’s BYD, Korea’s LG Energy Solution Ltd. and Samsung SDI Co.
Lotte’s first US copper foil plant is expected to have an initial production capacity of 30,000 tons, similar to that of its factory in Spain, and later expand it in line with demand.
The US plant is expected to focus on 6-micrometer products, more expensive and thus more profitable.
Lotte Chief Executive Kim Yeon-seop said in July the company plans to expand its annual high-end copper foil production capacity to 240,000 tons by 2028 from the current 60,000 tons.
Lotte Chemical Corp., a unit of Lotte Group, bought a controlling stake in Iljin Materials for 2.7 trillion won in 2022 and renamed it Lotte Energy Materials.
“The US market is critical to our global expansion plan,” said Lotte Energy CEO Kim in July.
source_title: Lotte Energy Materials to build copper foil plant in US for EV makers - KED Global

facts: SEDA held its annual meeting discussing how to secure enough industrial workers to meet employer demand in the Savannah area.
Boosting the area’s workforce is a top priority for SEDA officials in 2024.

SEDA President and CEO Trip Tollison stated that recruiting and working with schools and other institutions is necessary to increase the workforce because there are a lot of jobs to hire in a short period of time.
Tollison spoke about the Authority’s accomplishments and future goals to a crowd at the Savannah Convention Center.
The Savannah Harbor - Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority released a study projecting a deficit of more than 1,400 area industrial workers in 2025.
Manufacturers like Hyundai Motor Group and supplier companies plan to hire thousands of employees locally in the coming years.
SEDA announced the creation of a new organization tasked with boosting regional workforce numbers through six key areas.
Tollison touted the work of economic officials in 2023 despite the projected deficit.
Approximately 3,700 jobs were created with $2.4 billion in regional investment in 2023.
SEDA's focus will shift to securing more manufacturing businesses in the area through new incentive programs in the coming year.
Tollison stated that 2023 was a remarkable year for not just Savannah, but the whole region.
Several manufacturing facilities are currently under construction, including Hyundai’s Bryan County electric vehicle and battery processing plant which is expected to start production in early 2025.
source_title: Savannah economic leaders discuss planning for workforce demand

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung announced at a signing ceremony that Hyundai Motor Group will open its first fully dedicated electric vehicle (EV) and battery manufacturing facility in the State of Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group will invest $5.54 billion in opening a state-of-the-art U.S. smart factory at the Bryan County Megasite.
Non-affiliated Hyundai Motor Group suppliers will invest approximately another $1 billion in the project.
The project will deliver approximately 8,100 new jobs to Georgia’s coastal region.

Governor Kemp was joined by Jay Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, and José Muñoz, President and COO of Hyundai Motor Company, along with Hyundai Motor Group executives and state and local economic development leaders at the premier 2,923-acre site.
The megasite was purchased jointly by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA) and the State less than a year ago.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that welcoming Hyundai Motor Group to Georgia is part of forging an innovative future together, citing initial conversations on his economic development mission to Korea and Georgia’s investment in the Bryan County Megasite as long-term preparations for this opportunity.

Governor Kemp described the announcement as the largest economic development project in Georgia's history and a commitment to making the state a premier destination for quality companies creating jobs.

Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated that the U.S. holds an important place in the Group’s global strategy and expressed excitement about partnering with Georgia to achieve shared goals of electrified mobility and sustainability.
Hyundai Motor Group expects to begin construction on the new facility in January 2023.
Full production is expected in the first half of 2025.
The facility will have an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Wages throughout the project will be well-paying advanced manufacturing positions competitive in the local market.
Jaehoon (Jay) Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that America embraces change and drives innovation, making it the location for their first dedicated EV plant in the U.S.
Chang noted the new EV plant is the future of their business and will help meet growing demands of U.S. customers for leading edge design, safe, zero-emissions vehicles.
Hyundai Motor Group cited speed-to-market, workforce, and the state’s ability to meet the company’s carbon neutrality standards as reasons for choosing Georgia.
Georgia is home to an existing network of Hyundai subsidiaries and suppliers.
The Bryan County Megasite was developed by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA over the last several years in partnership with the state.
The megasite offers the needed speed-to-market requirements that reduce barriers to speedy operations.
Industrial utilities are adjacent to the site, and extensive due diligence reports have been completed.

José Muñoz, President and COO of Hyundai Motor Company, stated they partnered with Governor Kemp and the Savannah Joint Economic Development Authority to bring a flexible EV smart factory to Bryan County.
Muñoz said the plant will position them well to win in a competitive market with consumers demanding smart, sustainable mobility solutions.
Hyundai Motor Group is headquartered in Seoul, Korea, and employs 250,000 people worldwide.
The Group’s vehicles are sold in 193 countries through 5,000 dealerships and showrooms.
At the new Georgia highly connected, automated, and flexible EV smart factory, Hyundai Motor Group plans to produce a diverse range of innovative full electric vehicles.
Details of production models will be shared at later dates.
Through the battery manufacturing facility, the Group aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV battery and other EV components in the U.S. market.
When it nears planned 2025 start of operations, Hyundai Motor Group will seek local vendors and suppliers.

Carter Infinger, Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority Board of Directors Chairman, stated the announcement is transformative and that the jobs being created and the investment being made by the Hyundai Motor Group will reverberate for years and generations to come.
Infinger noted this is a great day for Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham and Effingham counties as well as the entire region and the State of Georgia.
Bryan County is the fastest-growing county in the state.
The strategic purchase of the Bryan County Megasite in 2021 was the largest in state history.
The site is adjacent to Interstate 16 with immediate access from I-95 and I-16 to 250 major metro areas.
It is less than 30 miles from the Port of Savannah, the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S.
The site has two Class I rail facilities on-site provided by Georgia Central Railway.

Russell McMurry, Georgia Department of Transportation Commissioner, stated Hyundai's announcement is evidence that Georgia’s collective focus on supporting the electric mobility future is working and exemplifies the power of partnership and preparation.
McMurry noted Georgia’s planning ensures logistics networks are safe, sustainable, innovative, and ready to support economic growth.

Griff Lynch, Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director, stated Hyundai's new location provides stellar access to global and domestic markets for both assembly components and finished vehicle marketing.
Lynch said the decision to expand in Georgia is a clear endorsement of the Peach State as a dynamic center for cutting-edge manufacturing.
Building on existing automotive assets and developing workforce, Georgia is consistently welcoming increased investment in the electric mobility ecosystem and supply chain across the state.
Georgia is the Southeastern leader for EV registrations per 1,000 registered automobiles.
Georgia offers more EV charging outlets per capita than anywhere else in the Southeast.

The Georgia Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance is a public-private-nonprofit initiative focused on growing the electric mobility ecosystem in the state and strengthening Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) was represented by Project Manager Taylor Walden in partnership with the Department’s Georgia-based Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim.

Representatives also included the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA, which includes Bryan County, Bulloch County, Chatham County, and Effingham County; the Georgia Department of Transportation; the Georgia Ports Authority; Georgia Quick Start; and Georgia Power.
Pat Wilson, Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner, stated under Governor Kemp’s leadership they have focused on putting Georgia at the forefront of electric mobility.
Wilson said the announcement solidifies Georgia's spot at the vanguard of the EV transition and welcomes the Hyundai Group to celebrate the incredible investment.
Wilson noted the state-of-the-art facility will create exciting new possibilities for all Georgians and transform an entire region.
At the new facility, Hyundai Motor Group plans to implement many of its advanced intelligent manufacturing technologies that are currently under test at the Group’s innovation hub.
Technologies include intelligent A.I.-equipped manufacturing and data-driven processes to best meet demands of customers.
The plant will help support Hyundai Motor Group’s efforts to achieve its carbon neutrality target through the active use of renewable energy for the facility.
Since 2020 alone, Georgia has announced more than 20 EV-related projects that will invest more than $13.54 billion and create nearly 18,000 jobs in the state.

links.context: Project RFIs, RFPs listing, and supply chain outreach

source_title: Hyundai Motor Group to Invest $5.54B in Georgia at First Fully Dedicated Electric Vehicle & Battery Manufacturing Facility - Savannah CEO

entity_name: Hyundai EV Plant in Bryan County
canonical_name: Hyundai EV Plant in Bryan County
facts: Construction of the incoming Hyundai EV plant is underway as of Jan. 9, with the clearing portion completed.
Clearing of the nearly 3,000-acre track of land in Bryan County began last August.
The $5.5 billion manufacturing facility near Savannah was announced last year.
Plans include employing 8,100 workers between an electric vehicle assembly plant and an adjacent EV battery factory.
It is the largest single economic development project in Georgia history.
Initial plans called for manufacturing around 300,000 vehicles a year once the assembly facility comes online around January 2025.
The facility will have the capacity to manufacture 500,000 vehicles, nearly doubling the initial estimate.
Hyundai's plant in Montgomery, Ala., has the capacity to produce nearly 400,000 vehicles per year.
Hyundai has operated a 3.4-million square-foot manufacturing plant in Montgomery, Alabama, since 2005.
The Montgomery facility cost $1.8 billion and was Hyundai's first manufacturing plant in the U.S. prior to the Bryan County announcement.
The Montgomery plant resides on 1,744 acres of land.
The Bryan County facility will be three times the size of the Montgomery plant, with the estimate only containing the assembly plant and not the battery plant.
The adjacent EV battery factory will start being constructed in the spring.
An announcement on a battery partner is expected in the coming months.
Former state senator and one-time gubernatorial candidate Eric Johnson will join the Savannah JDA as project director.
Eric Johnson is looking into doing a housing study for South Korean families moving to the area.
New Hampstead will be ground zero for future housing developments, with more need and opportunities expected.
Pooler is popular with South Korean families who are already here.
Around 100 South Korean families have come so far, and another 100 are expected to take up positions at the Hyundai plant.
A site for the Quick Start Center has been selected on Highway 280.
The Quick Start Center facility will cost $70 million and will be the home for training the local workforce for the incoming EV plant.
Suppliers have already begun to move into the region as the megasite is built out.
Projected supplier jobs total more than 3,400 between the four suppliers currently in the area.
The Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center played a big role in getting suppliers for Hyundai to come to the area.
Igneo Tech, which resides at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center, has 150 new jobs coming to the region.
source_title: Hyundai EV plant in Bryan County to be 3x size of Montgomery and other updates from SEDA

facts: Georgia reached a historic milestone in EV manufacturing with the grand opening of the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) in Bryan County.
The facility was completed just two and a half years after breaking ground.
HMGMA stands as the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
In 2022, Hyundai Motor Group announced a landmark $5.5 billion investment to establish its first U.S.-based EV mass production facility on a 3,000+ acre site in Bryan County, Georgia.
The total project investment has grown to over $8 billion, encompassing HMGMA and its network of Tier 1 suppliers.
The initiative is projected to create more than 12,000 direct, long-term jobs across coastal Georgia.
Commercial production at the facility officially began in March 2025.
The Hyundai Metaplant integrates cutting-edge smart factory technology and sustainability initiatives.
The project was delivered through a Construction Manager at Risk (CMAR) approach to ensure feasibility, efficiency, and cost-effectiveness from concept to completion.
Hussey Gay Bell served as the architecture, engineering, and surveying partner for the HMGMA project.
Hussey Gay Bell acted as both prime consultant and subconsultant across multiple Tier 1 supplier projects related to the initiative.
Hussey Gay Bell provided comprehensive professional services to facilitate seamless development and integration within Hyundai’s advanced EV network.
Hussey Gay Bell played a vital role in the design and development of on-site facilities for the project.
Hussey Gay Bell contributed to several off-site Tier 1 supplier developments designed and constructed in parallel with the Hyundai Metaplant.
Each facility was engineered to align with Hyundai’s next-generation EV production standards to ensure efficiency, sustainability, and long-term operational success.
The project's success resulted from a public–private partnership among Hyundai Motor Group, the State of Georgia, the Georgia Department of Economic Development, and regional development authorities.
Georgia has held the title of America’s #1 State for Business for over a decade.
source_title: Shaping The Region: Hussey Gay Bell Helps Bring the Hyundai Metaplant to Life - Hussey Gay Bell

facts: A massive immigration raid occurred at a Hyundai–LG Energy joint venture battery plant in Georgia.
Federal agents arrested about 450 workers during the operation.
More than 30 South Korean nationals were among those arrested.
The raid took place just weeks after Seoul pledged a $350 billion U.S. investment.
Federal agents with ICE and Homeland Security Investigations executed the raid on Thursday at the construction site in Ellabell, Georgia.
Officials said the operation was tied to an investigation into “unlawful employment practices and other serious federal crimes.”
The ATF’s Atlanta office said those arrested were “unlawful aliens.”
NBC News verified video showing agents entering the site with a search warrant and ordering construction halted.
Workers said they were questioned about their citizenship during the raid.
Additional footage showed employees lined up and their bags inspected.
South Korea’s foreign ministry confirmed that more than 30 of its nationals were detained and expressed “concern and regret” to the U.S. Embassy.
A spokesperson stated that “the economic activities of our companies investing in the U.S. and the rights and interests of our nationals must not be unfairly violated.”
Seoul dispatched consular officials to Washington and Atlanta and set up an on-site task force to monitor the situation.
LG Energy confirmed that some of its employees on business travel were detained and said it was working with the South Korean government to secure their release.
A spokesperson for the HL-GA Battery Company said the company has paused work at the site while cooperating with investigators.
The Ellabell complex is billed as one of the largest manufacturing projects in Georgia’s history.
The project represents a $7.6 billion investment by Hyundai and LG Energy.
Once complete, the plant will supply batteries to Hyundai’s nearby electric vehicle plant.
The plant is projected to create 8,500 jobs by 2031.
Just last month, Hyundai announced that it would boost its U.S. investment to $26 billion through 2028.
The raid comes only weeks after Seoul pledged a $350 billion investment in the U.S. as part of talks that ended with a 15% tariff rate on its imports.
Analysts warned that repeated large-scale enforcement operations could disrupt cooperation between allies.

Hur Jung, a professor at Sogang University in Seoul and president of the Korean Association of Trade and Industry Studies, said it would damage trust and hurt industrial cooperation between the U.S. and South Korea across various industries, with negative repercussions for local communities as well.
source_title: 450 arrested in Georgia immigration raid, testing US-South Korea ties

facts: Seoyon E Hwa Mfg. Savannah is a Tier 1 automotive supplier building a state-of-the-art manufacturing facility in Savannah, Georgia.
The facility focuses on producing advanced interior parts for leading global automakers.
The company is ramping up operations to meet rising demand for electric vehicle components and advanced automotive interiors.
Production at the Savannah facility was scheduled to launch in October 2024.
Seoyon E Hwa Mfg. Savannah specializes in developing and producing automotive interior components such as door trims, headliners, and tailgate trims.
The facility is positioned at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The site provides proximity to the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and the Port of Savannah for efficient logistics.
The company made an investment of nearly $76 million in the plant.
The plant is expected to create 740 new jobs.
The company emphasizes ESG management, quality innovation, and advanced manufacturing technologies.
Seoyon E Hwa Mfg. Savannah was founded in 2023.
Seoyon E Hwa Mfg. Savannah is a private company.
The company is implementing strategic efforts to expand market presence and reach new customer segments.
The company is implementing advanced technologies to improve service delivery and customer experience.
source_title: Seoyon E Hwa Mfg. Savannah | Automotive Interior Parts Su...

facts: Hyundai's electric vehicle and EV battery plant in Savannah does not open until early 2025.
Hyundai is recruiting competitively for experienced engineers and leaning on young talent from colleges and universities to operate the region's first smart factory.

Brent Stubbs, senior manager of learning and development for the Hyundai Metaplant, stated that operating the plant will require computer scientists and software engineers, necessitating relationship building for automation talent.
Of the 8,100 employees Hyundai is looking to hire overall, the EV manufacturing plant will hire 2,600.
Approximately 15% of the plant's hires will be professional engineers, according to Hyundai spokesperson Bianca Johnson.

According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, job growth for industrial engineers (manufacturing engineers) will grow 10% between 2021 and 2031, and job growth in computing and information research will grow 21% for the same period.
Savannah is home to few advanced manufacturing facilities, and talent is already in demand at existing manufacturers such as Gulfstream and JCB.

Daniel Park, chief administration officer for the Hyundai Metaplant, noted there was no automotive industry in the area previously, prompting Hyundai to create opportunities and develop programs with technical colleges.
Recruiting engineers from outside the region is more expensive due to relocation and other incentives, making a homegrown workforce a priority.
Park stated Hyundai's priority is hiring from Savannah, Bulloch, Bryan, and Effingham counties, prioritizing good local candidates even if they must eventually hire from outside.
Savannah-based engineering firm Thomas and Hutton has found success recruiting recent engineering school graduates to fill openings.
Georgia Southern University is partnering with Hyundai to supply a pipeline of talented engineers and business professionals.
Talks between Georgia Southern and Hyundai began in October, followed by an information session that same month to familiarize students with Hyundai's brand and introduce job opportunities.
As of May, Hyundai has made nearly a dozen offers to Georgia Southern students.
Glenn Gibney, associate vice president for career readiness at Georgia Southern, stated the goal is to make Hyundai's job easy.
Georgia Southern recently announced two new engineering doctorates: a Ph.D. in applied computing and a Ph.D. in engineering.
The proposed engineering Ph.D. will have concentrations in civil, electrical, advanced manufacturing, and mechanical engineering.
The Ph.D. in applied computing will enable students to solve advanced technical problems in computing foundations, cybersecurity, and machine learning.
Mohammad Davoud, a professor at Georgia Southern's Allen E. Paulson College, said faculty and staff planned these new Ph.D. programs for a decade anticipating the region's economic development needs.
Hyundai is recruiting from other local universities including Mercer, Savannah State, University of Georgia, Georgia Tech, Georgia State, and Kennesaw State.
Hyundai is also looking across the Savannah River at talent from Clemson and the University of South Carolina.
Savannah-Chatham School Board Member Michael Johnson emphasized that workforce engagement needs to begin as early as pre-k to teach robotics and art deeply.

Angie Lewis, executive director of the Office of College and Career Readiness for the Savannah school district, noted existing career pathways are preparing the workforce for high-level engineering jobs.
Lewis stated computer science is huge because the cars being built are computerized, and the district offers computer science programs at each high school and middle school.
In 2019, the Georgia General Assembly passed a law mandating a gradual increase in computer science offerings in middle and high schools.
By the 2024-2025 school year, all high schools must offer a computer science course; middle and elementary schools are directed to offer instruction.
The legislation aims to increase the number of computer science graduates across Georgia.

In 2019, the Georgia Department of Education stated there were close to 20,000 open computing jobs in the state, but only 1,200 students graduated from Georgia colleges each year with computer science degrees.
Savannah high schools offer different concentrations in computer science, including general computer science, cybersecurity, programming and networking, as well as gaming.
At elementary schools, skills are taught at a much simpler level using an approach called "unplugged coding" that teaches basic coding concepts through games and puzzles.
Stubbs noted the plant has a responsibility to partner with K-12 to plant seeds and build relationships, giving students opportunities to see and tour the facility.
As programs become available at universities and colleges, that will impact recruiting at high schools and what young people aspire to.
source_title: Engineers needed: Hyundai searching for tech muscle for Savannah EV 'smart factory' - AOL

facts: On May 20, 2026, Bojangles announced the launch of its first EV charging station at its location on Ogeechee Road in Savannah, Georgia.
The installation introduces a new “charge-and-dine” experience for customers.
The move positions the fast-growing chain as one of the latest quick-service restaurant brands investing in EV infrastructure to accommodate drivers seeking convenient recharge during daily travel.
Richard Del Valle, Chief Information Officer at Bojangles, stated that driving trends and expectations are changing, and EV charging turns a stop into an opportunity to relax and refuel.
The Savannah site was developed in partnership with XLR8 America and Energy and Environmental Design Services.
This installation marks the beginning of a broader national rollout planned for Bojangles locations across key U.S. markets.
The charging stations will include both Level 2 and Level 3 fast chargers featuring more than 97% uptime.
The hardware installed is XCHARGE North America’s C7 Ultra Fast Charger, which offers output up to 400kW in a standalone, compact design.

Frank O’Connor, CEO of XLR8 America, stated that the partnership aligns with their philosophy to “charge where you park, not park where you charge,” aiming to build a national network making EV charging effortless.
Bojangles was founded in Charlotte, North Carolina in 1977 and has grown into a chain with more than 870 company-owned and franchised locations across 23 states.

XLR8 America’s business model allows restaurants, retailers, hospitality brands, and commercial property operators to deploy turnkey EV charging solutions with no upfront capital cost to the host site.
XLR8 America promotes integrating charging infrastructure into locations consumers already visit regularly rather than dedicated charging destinations.
XLR8 America reports its active project portfolio now exceeds $250 million in value as demand for EV charging infrastructure continues expanding nationwide.
EVinfo.net commentary notes that restaurant owners who have not installed EV charging are losing money by missing the opportunity to attract EV drivers spending at nearby businesses.
Bojangles is attracting an ever-growing number of EV drivers who spend money at or close to where they charge their cost-saving, clean electric vehicles.
Competitors of Bojangles that have not yet installed charging are missing this opportunity and losing revenue as a result.
EVinfo.net spoke with Devin and Ryan Grandis of Energy and Environmental Design Services regarding the project.
source_title: Iconic Southern Restaurant Chain Bojangles Installs First EV Charging Station - EVinfo.net

facts: Hanon Systems is a global supplier of thermal and energy management solutions for electric and conventional vehicles.
Hanon’s products include components used in the heating and/or cooling operations of battery boxes and vehicle interiors.

In an effort to increase Hanon’s presence within the US electric vehicle (EV) market, Hanon is expanding its operations to rural Statesboro, Georgia, by building a manufacturing facility to serve a nearby EV manufacturing plant.
The facility will total approximately 112,000 square feet and will have the opportunity to expand to 330,000 square feet should Hanon need additional space.
Total project cost is $17,600,000.
MuniStrategies Allocation is $9,500,000.
Bulloch County is a Persistent Poverty County with a poverty rate of 22.6%.
The median household income in Statesboro in 2022 was $39,306, compared to $72,837 for the state overall.
Bulloch County has an unemployment rate of approximately 1.5x the national average.
This project will create 91 new permanent jobs, 75% of which are accessible to the low-income community and those without a college education.
The project will also create 90 temporary construction jobs.
The average salary is 240% above the living wage for Bulloch County.

The project will support the area’s growing electric vehicle ecosystem and supply chain by allowing Hanon to serve as a supplier of automotive thermal solutions to electric vehicle manufacturers in the area.

By contributing to Georgia’s electric vehicle ecosystem, Hanon’s new facility can attract additional automotive suppliers to the area, allowing Georgia’s automotive and e-mobility industries to continue to grow and creating additional jobs and spurring economic activity in Bulloch County.

The project is partnering with the Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG), Quick Start (a division of TCSG), and the Development Authority of Bulloch County (DABC) for recruitment, training, and development of its workforce.
source_title: Hanon Systems - MuniStrategies

facts: Bryan and Bulloch counties are seeking permits to withdraw 6.625 million gallons of water a day from the Floridan aquifer to support the Hyundai EV manufacturing plant.
Fundamental questions remain about how the Korean company calculated its water usage needs or how the water will be used.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is the legal entity contracted with the state of Georgia to receive $2.1 billion in tax deferments, subsidized construction costs, and other perks.

For the last six months, The Current has been trying to find data that could explain the water needs of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and associated suppliers building cars in Coastal Georgia.
Earlier this year, consultants hired to secure permits described the water usage data as a commercial secret.
Last month, those consultants revealed to The Current that they have no reliable figures or detailed explanations from the Korean manufacturer regarding water usage needs.
source_title: Hyundai’s water use: What does it take to build an EV in Coastal Georgia? – WABE

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced on January 5, 2023 that Ecoplastic Corporation will create 456 new jobs and invest approximately $205 million in a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
Ecoplastic is the second Hyundai supplier to announce plans to build a plant in Bulloch County.
On November 7, 2022, Joon Georgia announced plans to build a $317 million plant in the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park.
Joon plans to employ 630 workers at its new facility.
In February 2022, Aspen Aerogels announced plans to build a $325 million manufacturing facility in Bulloch County.
Construction on the Aspen Aerogels plant is visible from Highway 301 and I-16 in the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Ecoplastic will serve the Hyundai Metaplant well from Statesboro due to its proximity to Georgia's ports system.
Ecoplastic Corporation was established in 1984 as a Korea-based company manufacturing plastic automobile parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and other suppliers.
Ecoplastic is a Tier-1 supplier for Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) and Kia.
Ecoplastic engages in the manufacturing of automotive interior and exterior plastic parts, including bumpers, consoles, trims, and plastic molds.
Han Shang, President and CEO of Ecoplastic, stated the company is honored to establish a production facility for electric vehicle parts in Bulloch County, Georgia.
Ecoplastic aims to contribute to the economic development of Georgia and Bulloch County by increasing local jobs and providing a stable supply of high-quality products to client HMGMA.
Ecoplastic appreciates the support of the State and County governments for the project.
Ecoplastic's new facility will be located at 4822 Highway 301 South, just over one mile from I-16.
The site is on additional land acquired by the Development Authority of Bulloch County and recently rezoned to light industrial by the Bulloch County Commission.
The new plant will be capable of injection molding and painting.
The facility will produce exterior parts, bumpers, garnishes, interior parts, consoles, and trims for electric vehicles.
Ecoplastic expects to begin operations at the new facility by October 2024.
The company will be hiring for HR, finance, parts development, quality control, facility maintenance, injection, and paint production positions.
Development Authority of Bulloch County Chair Billy Allen expressed pleasure that this important Hyundai supplier chose their community and confidence in the location's workforce and quality of life.

Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on this project in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that Ecoplastic has decades of experience as a supplier for Kia and Hyundai.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry, building on assets that make the automotive industry successful.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and over 26,300 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in neighboring Bryan County.
Offsite suppliers connected to the Hyundai project were predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
Projects announced to date have already exceeded the predicted $1 billion investment from offsite suppliers.
Ecoplastic Corporation was originally established in June 1984 as Apollo Ind Co., Ltd.
Ecoplastic established its first Technical Research Center in 1987.
Ecoplastic was publicly listed on the Korean Stock Exchange in March 2000.
Ecoplastic currently employs more than 770 employees and recorded sales revenue of more than $700 million in 2021.
source_title: Ecoplastic building plant in Bulloch bringing additional 456 new jobs  - Grice Connect

facts: Groundbreaking for a $4 billion EV manufacturing plant occurred Friday morning at Triangle Innovation Point in Moncure, Chatham County.
The site is being called the “crown jewel of VinFast’s global expansion”.

Attendees included Madam Le Thi Thu Thuy (CEO of VinFast Auto), Vietnam ambassador to the U.S. Nguyen Quoc Dzung, NC Gov. Roy Cooper, Rep. Robert Reives (D-Chatham), Sen. Natalie Murdock (D-Durham/Chatham County), Sen. Jim Burgin (R-Harnett), NC Commerce Secretary Machelle Baker Sanders, NCDOT Secretary J. Eric Boyette, Chatham County officials, and members of Merry Oaks Baptist Church.
The Chatham County site is the first facility VinFast is building outside Vietnam.
The plant is slated to create 7,500 jobs.
CEO Thuy stated they are proud to create thousands of jobs in North Carolina and that the recognition reflects mutual ambition for the new facility.
Thuy noted thanks to support from their parent company, VinFast is well positioned to continue global expansion with a stronger presence in North America.
The U.S. secretary of the Securities and Exchange Commission cleared the way for VinFast’s “business combination” on the day of the groundbreaking.
This brings the EV maker one step closer to listing VinFast in the U.S., with an official announcement expected very soon.
Black Spade Acquisition Company and VinFast issued a joint press release later in the day about the merger.
On July 13, shareholders of Black Spade Acquisition Company voted to extend the company’s lifespan by another year.
The vote gave the SPAC until July 20, 2024, to merge with VinFast.
The merger would allow VinFast to go public through a stock offering in the U.S., valuing the company at approximately $27 billion with an equity value of $23 billion.
After the transaction, existing shareholders of VinFast would hold about 99% shares of the combined company.
In connection with the extension, shareholders redeemed approximately $147 million, or over 80% of their shares, leaving $28.56 million in the trust account.
The company has not updated any information on the change.
Vietnam ambassador Dzung stated the event coincides with the 10th anniversary of the U.S.-Vietnam comprehensive partnership launched by former Presidents Obama and Sang in 2013.
Dzung said VinFast’s success is encouraging other Vietnamese businesses to explore investment opportunities in the U.S. and North Carolina.
NC Gov. Cooper stated the state has been working for decades to bring an automobile assembly plant to North Carolina.
Cooper credited VinFast for leading an army of clean-energy companies coming to the state.
Cooper said this influx has helped set records for job growth and economic expansion in the state over the last few years.
Cooper noted that automakers and truck makers are moving away from gas and diesel into the EV market at breakneck speed.
Cooper emphasized it is important to pull together to harness the economic windfall for North Carolinians due to fierce competition.
Cooper said they are working to ensure infrastructure will be ready for EVs by making sure there will be charging stations all over the state.
Rep. Reives stated Chatham County has been made the example for every other county and southern state about how to get it done.
The company donated three acres of land on their site to Merry Oaks Baptist Church.
The company recognized two members of the church in attendance.
The company is using eminent domain to complete the project.
The project will displace the 135-year-old church to make way for a new highway needed for site access.
VinFast first announced plans to build the 1,800-acre site over a year ago.
The plant was originally scheduled to open in 2024 but has been pushed back to a 2025 opening.
Plans include a 2.85 million square foot complex comprising eight buildings.
The complex includes a press shop, which is the tallest building at 75.2 feet.
The complex includes a general assembly building, which is the largest at 995,900 square feet.
Other planned buildings include a body shop, central energy plant, paint shop, guard house, pump house, and waste building.
VinFast’s factory is designed to reach a capacity of 150,000 vehicles per year in Phase 1.
The factory will consist of two main areas: electric vehicle production and assembly.
The complex will also house supplementary supplier businesses.
To attract the company, the state and Chatham County committed to spending nearly $1.2 billion in incentives over the next 32 years.

Using the state’s Transformative Job Development and Investment Grant program (JDIG), VinFast could get up to $316.1 million in reimbursement from the state over three decades if hiring goals are met.
The total state appropriation is estimated at $766 million.
Chatham County is giving VinFast another $400 million incentive package.
The company reported last month that it lost $598 million, or 49%, in the first quarter of this year.

According to Reuters, the company attributed a quarterly revenue decline of almost $84 million to lower selling prices and its phase-out of internal combustion vehicles as it pivoted to become a dedicated EV maker.
The company has been losing money for some time, having lost $2.1 billion in 2022 and $1.3 billion the year prior.
A recall of all 999 VF8 2023 vehicles sent to the U.S. was issued in May due to safety concerns.
Reviews of the vehicle used words like abysmal, “very, very bad,” “yikes,” “simply unacceptable,” and “return to sender.”
MotorTrend said VinFast had the right idea, but the VF8 is nowhere near ready for customer deliveries that are already taking place.
source_title: VinFast holds groundbreaking on $4 billion Chatham County EV plant

facts: Four years after North Carolina leaders celebrated VinFast’s promise to build the state’s first automobile assembly plant in Chatham County, the state is now suing the Vietnamese electric-vehicle maker, alleging it failed to meet key construction and job-creation deadlines tied to one of the largest incentive packages in state history.
The lawsuit seeks to reclaim the Moncure megasite for future economic development.
Attorney General Jeff Jackson filed suit on behalf of the North Carolina Department of Commerce on May 21, 2026.
State officials allege VinFast breached agreements related to its planned electric-vehicle and battery manufacturing facility in Chatham County.
Through the lawsuit, North Carolina says it is exercising its contractual right to acquire the property and preserve the site for another manufacturing project.
Attorney General Jeff Jackson stated: “VinFast agreed to build a factory and create jobs for North Carolinians — it didn’t do either.”
Gov. Josh Stein said the action was aimed at “protecting taxpayers” and getting the Chatham County megasite back on the market for future manufacturing jobs.
In 2022, state officials announced that VinFast would create 7,500 jobs and invest billions of dollars at Triangle Innovation Point, a large industrial site near Moncure.
The project was promoted as North Carolina’s first car manufacturing plant and, at the time, the largest economic development announcement in state history.
According to the Department of Justice, VinFast was required to meet construction benchmarks, have the facility operational by July 2026 and create 1,750 jobs by the end of 2026.
State officials say the company cleared and graded the site in 2023, with costs reimbursed by the state, but failed to meet key obligations afterward.

The original announcement came on March 29, 2022, when then-Gov. Roy Cooper and VinFast executives unveiled the company’s plan for its first North American automotive assembly and battery manufacturing plant.
The company intended to invest up to $2 billion in the first phase at the Triangle Innovation Point megasite in Chatham County.
Production was expected to begin in July 2024.
The first phase was projected to have capacity of 150,000 vehicles per year, including the VF 8 midsize electric SUV and VF 9 three-row electric SUV.

North Carolina’s Economic Investment Committee approved a Transformative Job Development Investment Grant (JDIG), authorizing potential reimbursements of up to $316.1 million over 32 years if VinFast met hiring and investment targets.
The project was estimated to grow North Carolina’s economy by at least $71.59 billion over that period, according to the governor’s office.
VinFast later described the total public incentive package as roughly $1.2 billion.
The incentive package included a $450 million state appropriation for site preparation, transportation improvements and water and sewer infrastructure.
It also included $38 million in community-college training.
It included a $50 million Golden LEAF Foundation grant.
It included $400 million in incentives from Chatham County.

VinFast executives agreed in November 2022 to a land-buyback arrangement allowing the state to buy back the property if the company failed to begin vertical construction by Jan. 1, 2024, or failed to commence operations by July 1, 2026.
The state notified VinFast in January 2026 that it had defaulted and that North Carolina intended to protect its investment by exercising its right to acquire the site.
VinFast disputed that conclusion, arguing it had met construction deadlines and still planned to open on a delayed 2028 timeline.
VinFast told state officials it had built footers, wind columns and vertical walls for water basins, which the company argued should satisfy the vertical construction requirement.
Attorney General Jeff Jackson declined to define "vertical construction" publicly, saying the state would leave that argument for court.
State officials acknowledge VinFast cleared and graded the property in 2023 but allege the company did not move meaningfully beyond site preparation.
The lawsuit alleges VinFast did not begin vertical construction by the Jan. 1, 2024 deadline and never actually received a building permit.
Chatham County was prepared to issue a building permit in May 2024 but required VinFast to identify contractors working on the site; the company allegedly did not respond to that request.
North Carolina wants to be repaid $80 million allocated to VinFast for site preparation and wants to exercise its option to purchase the land for a future manufacturer.
VinFast told Reuters it had not yet received official documentation when the suit became public and said contracts with contractors had been signed, with construction expected to begin shortly.
VinFast said recent changes in U.S. policies related to the EV industry had affected the timeline and required more time to evaluate conditions.
In 2022, the original expectation was production in July 2024.
By 2024, VinFast had pushed the North Carolina plant’s opening to 2028, citing market uncertainty and the need to manage capital.
In July 2024, VinFast delayed the $4 billion U.S. factory from 2025 to 2028 and reduced its 2024 vehicle delivery target from 100,000 to 80,000 amid global EV market uncertainty.
VinFast reported 196,919 global EV deliveries in 2025, more than double its 2024 total.
VinFast reported revenue of about $3.6 billion in 2025, but its gross margin remained negative for the full year.
Reuters reported in March 2026 that VinFast’s fourth-quarter loss widened and that the company planned to resume building the North Carolina plant with a soft launch targeted for 2028.

The Chatham Economic Development Corporation said the project was expected to bring jobs and investment to Chatham and the surrounding areas, with the company planning 7,500 jobs at an average salary of $51,096.
VinFast entered the U.S. market with the VF 8, a two-row electric SUV aimed at one of the most competitive segments in the industry.

Car and Driver gave the 2025 VF 8 a 6 out of 10 rating and said the vehicle had brisk acceleration, competitive range and a strong warranty, but criticized its bouncy ride, safety-tech warnings and pricing.

Edmunds listed positives such as ample standard features, a clean interior design and a long transferable warranty for the 2025 VF 8, but listed “iffy build quality,” unimpressive range relative to competitors and too many unanswered questions among the cons.

MotorTrend noted that the 2025 VF 8 followed a “rocky start” and said earlier issues with controls and electronic features had reportedly been addressed through software updates, but warned that chassis and drivetrain refinement may require more than software fixes, while criticizing uncomfortable seating and low-quality interior materials for a vehicle at that price point.

InsideEVs, in a 2025 review of the 2024 VF 8, offered a nuanced assessment: the vehicle had improved in build quality, ride comfort, software design and driver-assistance tuning, but the reviewer still described the software as glitchy and inconsistent, with repeated error codes and problems with the interface.

In May 2023, VinFast recalled all 999 vehicles from its first U.S.-bound VF 8 batch after a dashboard software issue could prevent critical safety information from being displayed, increasing crash risk, according to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration.
VinFast said at the time it was not aware of incidents and issued the recall out of caution.

In September 2024, NHTSA opened a preliminary investigation into about 3,118 VinFast vehicles over reports that the Lane Keep Assist system had difficulty detecting lanes, provided improper steering inputs and was difficult for drivers to override.
VinFast said it would cooperate fully and took safety concerns seriously regarding the NHTSA investigation.
NHTSA had previously opened an investigation into an April 2024 crash involving a VinFast VF 8 in Pleasanton, California, in which four people died.
A complaint filed with NHTSA said steering may have been an issue in the Pleasanton crash, though that did not establish a defect or causation.
The company advertises a 10-year/125,000-mile new vehicle limited warranty and a 10-year/unlimited-mile battery warranty under standard use.
In 2024 the company delayed the plant and reduced delivery targets because of global market uncertainty and slowing EV demand.

In May 2026, the company planned to sell its Vietnamese manufacturing business for about $506 million to investors who would assume roughly $6.9 billion in debt, a move intended to make VinFast more asset-light.

State officials noted in their announcement that North Carolina announced more than $24 billion in new capital investment and more than 35,000 new jobs in 2025, and that the state has the largest manufacturing workforce in the Southeast.
source_title: North Carolina hits the brakes on VinFast: How a Chatham County EV dream became a court fight - Chatham Journal Newspaper

facts: VinFast is rumored to take a 1,000 acre plot in Research Triangle Park.
VinFast is expected to receive a generous incentives package.
VinFast began building gasoline-powered cars in 2019.
VinFast is committed to become a produce of only electric-powered vehicles within the next couple of years.
source_title: Vietnamese EV manufacturer to open North Carolina factory - SCANALYZER / The Happening World - Scanalyst

entity_name: Chatham County EV Charging Infrastructure Project
canonical_name: Chatham County Public EV Charging Station Initiative
facts: Chatham County is planning to install public electric vehicle (EV) charging stations in unincorporated areas outside Savannah.
The project aims to serve all residents fairly and equitably, addressing EV adoption barriers for lower-income residents.
Nine potential locations were identified based on criteria including accessibility, flood risk, and service to low-income populations.
The project was funded last year by a $21 million grant from the Southeast Crescent Regional Commission for infrastructure and economic development projects across the Southeastern United States.
Site recommendations followed three stakeholder meetings and two public engagement forums.
Consultants visited 50 spots throughout the county and announced nine locations on Mar. 24 for Georgetown, Garden City, Thunderbolt, and the islands.
Proposed locations had to be accessible along evacuation routes, unlikely to flood, serve low-income populations, and feel safe.
Chatham County Resilience Program Administrator Jackie Jackson stated the goal is to look at areas where residents cannot afford to install chargers at home.
As of Mar. 19 records from the Georgia Department of Revenue, Chatham County has over 3,000 active EV registrations and over 8,000 hybrid registrations out of more than 245,000 total registrations.
The Savannah Morning News reported just over 2,000 EV registrations in Chatham County in 2024.
Market research firm Luth Research found EVs are commonly used by high-income households attracted by government rebate incentives who live in cities with ample charging ports.
Jackie Jackson noted that some residents cannot get an EV because there is nowhere to charge, or their apartment complexes lack charging systems.
The county aims to build a foundation of charging infrastructure that is fair and equitable to all residents regardless of income.
Building many stations will require third-party buy-ins due to the difficult business case of building without existing customers.
Chris White from Kittelson and Associates highlighted the project is addressing interesting public-private partnerships.
A pilot project possibility involves lower-income qualified housing developments where residents can rent EVs in addition to using chargers.
Federal rollbacks to incentives make lower-income buyers less likely to purchase their own cars.
Consultants used Google Maps, Georgia Power energy grid maps, and evacuation routes to ground survey nearly 50 locations across unincorporated Chatham County.
Locations were required to be out of the floodplain and along evacuation routes, with chargers also capable of powering phones and household items during emergencies.
The county aims to build Level 2 and Level 3 chargers, while noting Level 1 takes about 12 hours and Level 3 (DC Fast Charging) takes about an hour.
Garden City Library will receive Level 2 chargers with fees based on kilowatt-hours.
Charles Brooks Park Lot #2 will receive Level 2 chargers.
Islands Library and High School will receive Level 2 chargers for employees, students, visitors, and nearby police station vehicles, with hourly fees.
Lake Mayer Park will host a DC Fast charging hub on three major evacuation routes, sized for larger vans and CAT buses.
Thunderbolt Town Hall will have Level 2 charging for condo residents and visitors, potentially for electric bikes and watercraft, with hourly pay.
Island Town Center will host a DC Fast Charging Hub owned and operated by iONNA, co-owned by Hyundai, at a junction of major evacuation routes.
Thrift Store Plaza will host a DC Fast Charging Hub.
A former paint store near the intersection of Ogeechee Road and Chatham Parkway will host a DC Fast charging hub.
A retail plaza under development near the intersection of Highways 204 and 17 will host a DC Fast Charging Hub.
Areas of high-flood risk, including Tybee Island, were avoided for site selection.

Resident Azania Heyward-James raised safety concerns at the potential site near Highways 204 and 17 intersections, noting residents may not leave vehicles for hours and need adequate lighting for students, elderly people, and women.
Chris White responded that resident safety is paramount but charging areas could transform scary spaces into exciting ones, using stations as space activators.
The City of Savannah was the first to participate in Georgia’s “Make Ready” program, a Georgia Power initiative covering installation costs.
In 2021, Savannah partnered with Georgia Power to install four EV stations, each equipped with eight chargers.
Jackie Jackson stated the county is building on what Savannah already has.
Chris White noted the county must find infrastructure funding and may need to build roads and stores to attract third-party owners and operators.
Sidewalks and road widenings might be required to compel third-party owners and operators for some stations.
Changes to codes and ordinances may be necessary, as lot sizes may not currently allow fire department trucks to enter in case of an electric fire.
source_title: Chatham County plans new EV charging stations

facts: A visit to Mercedes-Benz and Porsche plants in Stuttgart, Germany, served as a wake-up call for Georgia regarding the electric vehicle manufacturing race.

Pat Wilson, Commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), stated that the EV revolution is not theoretical and Georgia decided it needs to participate or risk getting left behind.
Governor Brian P. Kemp and Pat Wilson recognized that approximately 55,000 Georgian jobs are on the line due to industry disruption.
A statewide initiative was launched to strengthen Georgia’s position as a national leader in the electric mobility industry.
The Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance (EMIA) is driven by the GDEcD and includes partners from government, industry, electric utilities, education, nonprofits, and other stakeholders.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated at the launch that Georgia has a proven record of investing early in resources and infrastructure to connect to the world and develop future jobs.
EMIA's scope covers the entire EV industry transition, from rare earth metals to vehicle assembly, aiming to create jobs across the breadth of the industry.
Work resumed on a nearly US$2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, following an agreement between two rival Korean companies.
The Commerce battery plant will supply batteries for electric vehicles to Ford and Volkswagen.
Pat Wilson noted that the United States currently produces around 10% of its lithium-ion batteries while China produces 70% or more.
The presence of a battery plant attracts additional supply chain opportunities, with five suppliers already announcing plans to establish plants around the Commerce facility.

Investors in the Commerce battery plant include Dutch e-mobility charging systems leader Heliox, Turkish EV-parts manufacturer TEKLAS, German-owned lightweight automotive-body parts manufacturer GEDIA, and SK-supplier EnChem of Korea.
School bus manufacturer Blue Bird introduced a range of all-electric buses made in Georgia in 2018.
Blue Bird recently reported its 500th delivery of an electric bus.

Non-profits focused on automotive innovation within EMIA include The Ray, a non-profit public-private partnership and living highway testbed located on Georgia’s I-85 between LaGrange and the Alabama state line.
The Ray is involved in Peachtree Corners, Georgia, which is home to Curiosity Lab, a 5G-enabled autonomous vehicle and smart city living laboratory.
Curiosity Lab features a 1.5-mile test and demo track as its centerpiece for exploring emerging technologies in a real-world environment.
Additional infrastructure at Curiosity Lab includes a network operations center, smart poles, DSRC units, dedicated fiber, and a 25,000 square foot tech incubator.

EMIA stakeholders face the challenge of making policy and investment recommendations to ensure Georgia can deliver workforce, business policies, infrastructure, and supply chain elements needed for industry growth.
EMIA has been divided into different work streams to focus efforts and generate actionable suggestions for the state government's transition to electric vehicles.
A key focus area is ensuring the workforce is fully equipped through school-level education and retraining of currently employed automotive sector workers.
Pat Wilson emphasized that success in the transition requires a pipeline of the right skills.
source_title: Georgia partnership to steer through electric vehicle disruption - AI Online -

facts: SK Innovation will likely be able to move ahead with plans for large-scale U.S. battery production following a favorable decision by the U.S. International Trade Commission (ITC).
The ITC ruled that SK Innovation does not infringe on LG Chem patents.
The favorable decision came after pressure from Ford and other companies.
SK Innovation is engaged in a legal battle with rival LG Chem over allegations of misappropriating trade secrets during the hiring of former employees for factory ramp-ups in the U.S. and Hungary.
In February, the ITC initially voted to effectively ban SK Innovation from the U.S. market for 10 years.
The initial ban would have allowed SK Innovation to supply batteries for the Ford F-150 Electric pickup truck for four years and Volkswagen's Tennessee-built electric cars for two years.
SK Innovation currently supplies battery cells for several Kia models, including the Niro EV, which are sourced from South Korea.
SK Innovation is constructing a battery factory in Commerce, Georgia.
The Biden administration's EV plans emphasize both large production volumes and domestic production of components.
SK Innovation stated that its Georgia factory will be capable of making batteries for 300,000 EVs annually after the second phase is completed.
SK Innovation stated it will create 2,600 jobs in Georgia by 2024.
Georgia state lawmakers expressed concern over potentially losing those jobs if the ban were upheld.
LG Chem is investing $4.5 billion in U.S. battery production over the next four years.
LG Chem stated it could fulfill demand if SK Innovation's Georgia factory is scrapped.
The legal dispute between suppliers has complicated battery supply for the North American market.
Ford and Volkswagen had planned to use cells from SK Innovation's Georgia factory in upcoming EVs.
Volkswagen ID.4 crossovers were planned to be built at its Chattanooga, Tennessee, factory beginning in 2022 using those cells.
source_title: Battery supplier SK Innovation might not be banned in the US after all

facts: The Biden administration is betting big that renewing the American industrial machine will speed progress toward decarbonizing the U.S. electric grid by 2035.
The White House wants to use clean electricity to power a mass transition to electric vehicles to tackle greenhouse gases from transportation.
Spurred by incentives in the Inflation Reduction Act, a new Battery Belt is emerging in Michigan, Indiana, Ohio, Kentucky, Tennessee, Georgia, and the Carolinas.
Georgia leads the country in post-IRA clean energy factory investments with over $17 billion, according to a Canary Media analysis.
South Carolina comes in second with $9.3 billion in clean energy factory investments, followed by Michigan with $8.1 billion.
The Southeastern Battery Belt is a quickly growing cluster of factories spanning lithium processing, battery fabrication, auto assembly, and battery recycling.

Kings Mountain mine and nearby operations historically produced more hard-rock lithium than almost anywhere else in the world before shutting down and shipping mining jobs overseas at the close of the 20th century.
The Foote Mineral Company opened the Kings Mountain Mine in 1938, tapping the Carolina Tin-Spodumene Belt accessible across Cleveland and Gaston counties.
Lithium from the Kings Mountain mine was initially used for ceramics glazing and bipolar disorder pharmaceuticals before World War II.
During World War II, scientists used white metal from the Carolina Tin-Spodumene Belt for thermonuclear weapons research as part of the Manhattan Project.
Cold War arms race demands sustained the Kings Mountain mining operations for about half a century.
The Kings Mountain lithium mine shut down in 1988.
Livent’s Hallman-Beam Mine in Bessemer City shuttered in 1996.
Albemarle, which now owns the Kings Mountain mine, has been conducting economic-feasibility studies to determine whether to revive the dormant mine.

Newcomer Piedmont Lithium hopes to open a brand-new mine in the area, but its initial public outreach campaign failed due to neighbor concerns about mining explosions and potential destabilization of nearby water wells.
Energy Secretary Jennifer Granholm stated that new mining operations need reforms to ensure better environmental practices and respect for local residents, especially tribal communities.
South Korea’s SK Corporation opened a $2.6 billion battery factory in Commerce, Georgia, in early 2022.
The SK Georgia factory ships batteries to Volkswagen’s electric ID.4 production site in Chattanooga, Tennessee, and supplies Ford’s F-150 Lightning.
SK makes enough batteries at the Georgia facility to power 300,000 EVs annually, according to company statements.

Republican Governor Brian Kemp’s office praised SK in January for hitting employment targets two years ahead of schedule, with 2,600 people already working there and expectations to hit 3,000 by year’s end.
The SK Georgia facility is one of the largest single-site employers in the state.

Norwegian company Freyr opted to fast-track development of a second factory in Coweta County, southwest of Atlanta, to take advantage of Inflation Reduction Act benefits for U.S.-based battery production.
Freyr is making a $2.6 billion investment in Georgia in partnership with Koch Industries.
Freyr expects to make 2.5 gigawatt-hours’ worth of batteries in 2025, ramping to 38 gigawatt-hours shortly thereafter.
The Freyr facility should employ more than 700 people.
Freyr licensed battery technology developed by MIT spinout 24M, which advertises a streamlined production technique that lowers factory construction costs.
SK will team up with Hyundai to build a $5 billion factory in northern Georgia.
Georgia appointed an economic development lead specifically dedicated to attracting business from South Korea years ago.
Yoonie Kim started the South Korea-focused economic development job in 2006 and helped facilitate $11.5 billion of Korean investment in Georgia in 2022 alone, according to Site Selection magazine.
The Kemp administration uses tax incentives alongside Georgia’s Quick Start worker training program to prepare workers for jobs at new factories.
