Volkswagen planted a sapling at its Chattanooga plant in July 2022 to commemorate the start of electric vehicle production there.

The ID.4, Volkswagen’s electric SUV flagship, is largely sourced from within the U.S., with steel from Alabama and Ohio, interior parts from Indiana and South Carolina, electronics from Kentucky and North Carolina, and batteries from SK’s Georgia base.
When VW ramped up ID.4 assembly last year, it hired 1,000 new employees and added a third shift at its Chattanooga plant.
Another 500 hires are coming this year at the Volkswagen facility.
Tennessee is home to some 900 auto parts suppliers.
Roughly half the U.S. population lives within 650 miles of Nashville, per the city’s Chamber of Commerce.
Memphis serves as a logistics hub halfway up the Mississippi River, functioning as a railroad, trucking, and global air freight hub thanks to FedEx.
GM makes electric Cadillacs at a facility in Spring Hill, Tennessee.
BMW has added EV production to its plant in Spartanburg, South Carolina.
Hyundai and Kia are building new sites in Georgia.
Electric truck startup Rivian looked to be setting up shop outside Atlanta, but the state’s incentive package has been tied up in court due to a case brought by residents opposing the project.
Central North Carolina has attracted new factories from Toyota and Vietnamese firm VinFast.

The Southeastern EV sector region is solidly right to work, meaning workers do not have to pay union dues even if represented by a union, a policy used to weaken union power and give automakers leverage.
Tennessee just passed a constitutional amendment to perpetuate right-to-work status, with voters approving it by a nearly 40-point margin.
United Auto Workers President Shawn Fain has spoken about the need to ensure union representation at new EV plants popping up in the region.
Ascend Elements opened this spring in Covington, Georgia, as the first operational battery recycler in the region.
The Ascend facility uses a two-story-tall wet shredder to process used battery modules that still contain flammable liquid electrolyte.
To prevent sparking from high-speed blades grinding through metal, Ascend drops batteries through an airlock into an oxygen-purged space.
Chopped battery bits run through a room-sized rotating kiln to cook off the liquid electrolyte, which is condensed, cleaned, and sold back to battery makers.
Remnants are separated by density and particle size into aluminum chunks, copper, magnetic bits, and black mass powder containing electrode metals.
Ascend churns out black mass by the 1,000-kilogram sack.
A parallel processing line takes in scrap materials from nearby battery factories, including SK’s, to produce the same powdery output.
Scrap currently drives most inputs at Ascend’s plant, but the balance is expected to flip as more batteries reach retirement age.
Ascend sells its black mass back to battery manufacturers for proprietary processing.
Ascend is breaking ground on a $1 billion factory in southern Kentucky that will use a hydro-to-cathode technique to pull exact element ratios for each battery maker.
Ascend just announced a $1 billion deal to supply an unnamed U.S. battery manufacturer.
Redwood Materials, launched by Tesla co-founder JB Straubel, is building a $3.5 billion recycling plant near Charleston.
The Redwood plant will receive materials from Battery Belt factories and import metals through the Port of Charleston.
By 2025, Redwood plans to produce 100 gigawatt-hours per year of cathode active materials and anode foil annually across its U.S. operations, supplying approximately 1 million electric vehicles.
Nearly all anode and cathode production supplying U.S. battery cell manufacturers currently occurs overseas in Asia.

Former Tennessee Governor Phil Bredesen’s economic development lead Matt Kisber stated that the Southeast is very knowledgeable and productive around advanced manufacturing, with a legacy that battery storage combines.

Lithium markets expert Joe Lowry stated that regional mines could complement hard-rock mining in Canada and extraction from brines and clay formations elsewhere to create a diversified North American supply chain.

Former Charlotte Mayor Jennifer Roberts noted that state regulators often lack resources to police industrial polluters but emphasized that community engagement can make mining operations work responsibly.
Representative Sean Casten stated he wants high-value-add manufacturing in America because that is where the competitive advantage lies.
source_title: The South is building the most vibrant EV and battery… | Canary Media

facts: Engineering and manufacturing company Yancey Engineered Solutions plans to establish operations in Cordele, Georgia.
The $5.7 million project is expected to create 300 jobs in Crisp County.
source_title: Georgia Manufacturing / Industrial Project Announcements | corporate real estate news, economic development news, expansion announcements - Area Development

facts: A Korean automotive supplier will build a manufacturing facility in West Point to serve the nearby Kia plant and other automakers, announced by Gov. Brian Kemp on Tuesday.
Daesol Ausys will invest $72 million in the project.
The project will create more than 140 jobs.
Gov. Brian Kemp stated that West Point has become an automotive capital in its own right and is excited that Daesol Ausys will join other innovators in Harris County.
Georgia’s automotive industry currently employs over 50,000 Georgians, according to Gov. Kemp.
Gov. Kemp noted that the state’s automotive industry will continue to grow as e-mobility suppliers choose locations across the state for future jobs.
Daesol Ausys was established in 2017.
Daesol Ausys specializes in designing and manufacturing electric vehicle motor and interior components and accessories.
Daesol Ausys serves as a key supplier for Kia Georgia, Hyundai Motor Group, and General Motors.
Hyundai Motor Group is building a huge EV manufacturing plant west of Savannah.
The new Daesol Ausys plant will produce luggage boards and luggage covers.
Operations at the new Daesol Ausys plant are due to begin late next year.
Min Ho Kwon, CEO of Daesol Ausys, stated the company is committed to delivering world-class services that exceed client requirements and expectations.
Min Ho Kwon expressed excitement about the project in the Northwest Harris Business Park and look forward to continuing partnerships with the state of Georgia and Harris County.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team worked on the project in partnership with the Harris County Commission, Harris County Development Authority, city of West Point, Georgia Power, and the Technical College System of Georgia’s Quick Start program.
source_title: Korean automotive supplier coming to West Point |

facts: Hyundai Mobis will provide EV powertrains to the new Hyundai Meta Plant in Bryan County.
Hyundai Mobis is placing a $926 million, 1.2 million-square-foot facility nearby that will hire more than 1,500 people.
At full production, the facility will churn out more than 900,000 battery power systems and 450,000 per year once it hits its stride.
These production figures were cited in a news release from Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp’s office.
Hyundai Mobis is the latest tier-one supplier expected to flock to the area around the new plant and will likely be one of the largest.
Joon Georgia committed to build a $317 million body components factory in Statesboro.
Combined with Joon Georgia's commitment, supplier investment has already reached $1.2 billion.
This $1.2 billion surpasses the $1.1 billion officials projected when Hyundai broke ground on its $5.54 billion plant.
Hyundai Mobis has worked with Kia Motors across Georgia in West Point for more than a decade.
Since setting up in West Point, Mobis has supplied cockpit modules, chassis modules and bumper assemblies to Kia and other automakers around the Southeast.
Hyundai Mobis currently employs 1,200 people in Georgia.
Its global system of plants includes sites in Alabama, Ohio and Michigan, totaling 44 plants worldwide.
The new Bryan County plant will be located in the Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.
The Bryan County plant is set to provide components destined for Kia’s plant in Georgia and Hyundai’s in Montgomery, Ala.
Hyundai Mobis won the right to produce the electric powertrain for the e-GMP platform on which Kia and Hyundai are building their electric future.
Executives announced plans for a new U.S. site in September without disclosing the location at the time.
The company expects its compound annual growth rate to average 36 percent through 2030.
Order volumes stood at $660 million in North America in 2020, $1.4 billion in 2021 and $1.7 billion in the first half of 2022 alone.
H.S. Oh, vice president of Hyundai Mobis’s Electric Powertrain Business Unit, said EV investment is moving faster than expected.
H.S. Oh stated, “We’re going to be a major production player in the EV market, and that’s going to trigger more growth within the sector.”
Construction on the new facility could begin as early as January.
Production is most likely to commence in early 2024.
In October, about a month before this announcement, Mobis said it would spend $200 million on a new battery module assembly facility in Montgomery, Ala.
The Montgomery plant operates on an 83-acre site that Mobis has occupied for 20 years.
That plant will supply Hyundai’s Montgomery facility, which is investing $300 million into a production line to make electrified Genesis G70 SUVs.
source_title: Second Hyundai Supplier Announces $926M for EV Powertrain Plant  - Global Atlanta

facts: Apple is in talks with Hyundai-Kia to produce a vehicle in Georgia.
The Silicon Valley giant could partner with Kia on its first 'Apple Car.'
source_title: Apple, Hyundai-Kia in talks to produce vehicle in Georgia - Atlanta Business Chronicle

entity_name: SK On-Hyundai Motor Group Electric Vehicle Battery Plant
facts: The $5 billion SK On-Hyundai Motor Group electric vehicle battery plant is anchoring economic development along the 411 corridor between Rome and Cartersville.
The massive battery plant operates as a partnership between Hyundai Motor Group and SK On.
The development site is located east of the Floyd/Bartow county line off the north side of U.S. 411 near Bartow Centre.
The project is expected to add $40 million a year in tax revenue.
The plant will rival the SK plant located off I-85 in the Commerce area on the other side of the state.
First-tier supplier businesses to the SK plant need to be within a certain distance radius from the facility, according to Rome Floyd Development Authority Chair Missy Kendrick.
Second-tier suppliers to the SK plant need to be within about 50 miles of the facility, according to Rome Floyd Development Authority Chair Missy Kendrick.

The region will continue to see huge amounts of economic interest regardless of the exact distance suppliers need to be from the SK plant, according to Rome Floyd Development Authority Chair Missy Kendrick.
The Rome Floyd Development Authority purchased property for the Enterprise Corner development on the Bartow County border, which will soon have water and sewer access.
A $7.9 million allocation for water and sewer access for the Enterprise Corner Development was approved as part of a special purpose local option sales tax referendum passed by voters in 2023.

Interest in the Enterprise Corner Development saw a significant jump once the SPLOST package passed, as the lack of infrastructure previously inhibited economic development in that area, according to Rome Floyd Development Authority Chair Missy Kendrick.

West Cartersville is expected to become a huge growth area for the next several years, experiencing both industrial and significant residential growth, according to Bartow County Commissioner Steve Taylor.
Bartow County is building a 2 million gallon per day wastewater treatment plant on the Etowah River south of Neel’s Landing.
Plans are already in place to expand the Bartow County wastewater treatment plant to 4 million gallons per day.
The Bartow County wastewater treatment plant project costs between $70 and $80 million, according to Bartow County Commissioner Steve Taylor.

There are hardly any large plots of land available near I-75 all the way up to Chattanooga, which is driving interest in the corridor between Cartersville and Rome, according to Bartow County Commissioner Steve Taylor.

It is difficult to find 100-acre plots of land anywhere near I-75 until reaching Tennessee, making the 411 corridor highly attractive for development, according to Bartow County Commissioner Steve Taylor.
Floyd, Bartow, and Polk Counties have agreed to join forces to create a joint development authority to work together on large business development opportunities.
A multi-county development authority functions as an independent government agency designed to promote economic and industrial development across county lines.
The Gordon-Floyd Joint Development Authority previously served as an example of a multi-county entity responsible for recruiting Synthica Energy to locate in its jurisdiction.

Rome Floyd Development Authority Chair Jimmy Byars stated late last year that the joint development authority is a great initiative because Bartow and Floyd counties are growing toward each other and the 411 corridor is highly active.
source_title: 411 Corridor: This Is Only The Beginning |  | northwestgeorgianews.com

entity_name: Hyundai-SK On Georgia Battery Cell Plant
canonical_name: Hyundai-SK On Georgia Battery Cell Plant
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On plan to build an EV battery cell production plant in Bartow County, Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On each will have a 50% stake in the new battery cell plant.
The new battery cell plant is estimated to cost $5 billion to build.
The battery cell plant is expected to become operational in the second half of 2025.
Once operational, the battery cell plant is expected to have an annual production capacity of 35 GWh.
The 35 GWh annual capacity is sufficient to support the production of 300,000 EVs.
Battery cells built at the new plant will be shipped to Hyundai Mobis in Montgomery for assembly into battery modules.
Assembled battery modules will be shipped to Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA), Kia Georgia, and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant Atlanta.
Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama started producing the EV Genesis earlier this year.
The Electrified GV70 is Hyundai's third all-electric model.
The start of production at HMMA was made possible by a $300 million infrastructure expansion and upgrade at Hyundai’s Montgomery plant in 2022.
Hyundai Mobis announced in 2022 that it would invest $205 million to develop an EV battery module plant.
The Hyundai Mobis EV battery module plant is intended to support both Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Georgia.
Hyundai Mobis currently employs about 1,100 workers in Montgomery making front and rear chassis and cockpit modules for HMMA.
Hyundai Mobis will add about 400 employees to make the new battery modules.

H.S. Oh, vice president of electric powertrain business of Korean-based parent company Hyundai Mobis, stated that having a strong production capacity will be key in allowing Hyundai Mobis to see continued growth in the market as the EV market continues to grow.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group and SK On to build battery cell plant in Georgia

facts: EV Blocks operates a production location in Cartersville.
The facility manufactures Stock-24 Pole Base foundations for electric vehicle charging infrastructure.
Jeff Davis is listed as the contact person for the Cartersville site.
Technical information and resource libraries are provided for the company's product range.
Installation guides are available to assist with setting up EV Block charging stations.
source_title: Cartersville Supplier | EV Charger Foundations | EV Blocks

facts: Workers at a recycling facility in Covington, Georgia grind up dead batteries into a fine, dark powder known as black mass.
The Covington facility previously shipped black mass overseas to refineries for metal extraction but now processes it on site to produce lithium carbonate.
Lithium carbonate is a critical ingredient for making new batteries to power electric vehicles and store grid energy.
The Covington facility is owned by Massachusetts-based Ascend Elements.
It is the first new lithium carbonate producer in the nation in years and the only source of recycled lithium carbonate in North America.
The company is finalizing upgrades to its Covington facility that will allow production of up to 3,000 metric tons of lithium carbonate per year beginning later this month.
The only other domestic source of lithium carbonate is a small mine in Silver Peak, Nevada.
Since January, President Donald Trump has frozen grants and loans for clean energy efforts, hollowed out key agencies, and stalled renewable energy projects via executive action.
Trump cited economic and national security reasons to advance domestic production of critical minerals like lithium.
U.S. battery recyclers face uncertainty due to fast-changing tariff policies, potential repeal of Biden-era tax credits by Congress, and signs that the clean energy manufacturing boom is fading.
Battery recyclers are in a "limbo moment" and waiting for next steps, according to Beatrice Browning, a recycling expert at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.

Recycling end-of-life batteries from EVs, e-bikes, and cell phones can provide metals while reducing destructive mining needs, a process already scaling in China where most lithium-ion battery manufacturing occurs.
Under Biden, U.S. battery recyclers announced major expansion plans propelled by government financing and incentives.
The DOE launched R&D initiatives to support battery recycling and awarded hundreds of millions of dollars in funding to expanding firms.
The DOE Loan Programs Office offered to lend nearly $2.5 billion to two battery recycling companies.

The industry benefited from tax credits established or enhanced by the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act, particularly the 45X advanced manufacturing production credit which subsidizes domestic production of critical minerals including those from recycled materials.
Roger Lin, VP of government affairs at Ascend Elements, stated the 45X incentive has a direct bottom-line impact for recyclers.
The DOE did not respond to requests for comment on Biden-era grants and loans status, but recyclers report at least some federal support is continuing under Trump.
In 2022, Ascend Elements was awarded a $316 million DOE grant to construct a second battery recycling plant in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
The Kentucky grant funds capacity to make battery cathode precursor materials from recycled metals and is still active with minimal impact from the administration change, according to Lin.
Ascend Elements expects the Kentucky plant to come online in late 2026.

American Battery Technology Company, a Reno, Nevada-based firm, finalized a $144 million DOE contract in December for its second battery recycling facility to extract and refine battery-grade metals from scrap and end-of-life batteries.
CEO Ryan Melsert stated the American Battery Technology Company grant remains active with no changes since Trump's inauguration.
Cirba Solutions learned that a $200 million DOE grant to construct a new battery recycling plant in Columbia, South Carolina is moving forward.
At full capacity, the Columbia facility will produce enough battery-grade metals to supply half a million EVs a year.
Cirba Solutions is still spending funds from two earlier DOE grants, including a $75 million grant to expand a battery processing plant in Lancaster, Ohio.
Danielle Spalding, VP of communications and public affairs at Cirba Solutions, stated the company aligned very much to the administration's priorities.

Administration priorities include establishing the U.S. as the leading producer and processor of non-fuel minerals and facilitating domestic mineral production, per Trump executive orders in January and March.
The Trump administration believes America's national security depends on controlling critical mineral supply chains due to their use in military weapons.
Ajay Kochhar, CEO of Li-Cycle, wrote that resource recovery and recycling companies will continue playing an important role in providing domestic sources of critical minerals.
Li-Cycle closed a $475 million loan with the DOE Loan Programs Office in November but is now facing possible bankruptcy.
Trump pursued onshoring goals using tariffs and fast-tracking new mines, while economists criticized his indiscriminate and unpredictable application of tariffs.

Some battery recyclers are cautiously optimistic they will benefit from increased trade restrictions amid an escalating trade war with China that includes recent limits on exports of various critical minerals to the U.S.
China is the world's leading producer of most key battery metals.
Beatrice Browning stated limiting imports from China could strengthen mineral production in other regions, including the U.S.
Trade restrictions between the U.S. and partners outside China could be more harmful as U.S. recyclers often sell black mass to refiners in South Korea who pay a premium to secure material abroad.
Trump imposed 25 percent tariffs on Korean imports in April before placing them on a 90-day pause, which could cut off a key revenue stream if South Korea implements retaliatory tariffs.
Recycling companies noted there are currently no export bans or tariffs affecting their black mass and emphasized plans to build up local refining capacity.
Danielle Spalding stated the company sees tariffs as an opportunity to focus on domestic manufacturing.
Battery recyclers' interests diverge from Trump's on energy policy because without a clean energy manufacturing boom in the U.S., there would be far less need for battery recycling.
Nearly 40 percent of material available to U.S. battery recyclers is production scrap from battery gigafactories, according to Benchmark data.
Another 15 percent consists of used EV batteries at end-of-life or recalled, while grid storage and micromobility batteries account for 14 percent.
The remaining third of material available for processing is portable batteries like those in consumer electronics.
As more EVs reach end-of-life, an even greater fraction of battery scrap will come from the clean energy sector.
If planned battery and EV manufacturing facilities are canceled due to IRA tax incentive repeal, loss of federal funding, or rising costs, the recycling industry may have to scale back its ambitions.
A budget bill passed the House in May would undo key IRA provisions, eliminating some clean energy tax credits like the consumer EV tax credit at the end of this year.
The legislation scheduled the 45X manufacturing credit to end in 2031 rather than 2032, but could face significant changes in the Senate before heading to Trump's desk, possibly by July 4.

Despite IRA uncertainty, Trump's actions have put a damper on U.S. manufacturing as firms abandoned or delayed plans for $14 billion worth of U.S. clean energy projects since January, according to E2.
As Ascend Elements ramps up lithium production in Georgia, it has lined up at least one buyer outside the battery supply chain.
The battery industry accounts for nearly 90 percent of lithium demand globally, but the metal is also used in industrial applications like ceramics and glass making.
Integrating into the EV battery supply chain remains the ultimate goal for Ascend Elements, but the company is looking at other plans to ensure economic viability continues.
Ozburn Electrical Contractors, Inc. is a licensed, bonded, and insured electrical contracting company serving residential, commercial, and industrial clients since 1984.
The company's corporate office is located at 8316 Hazelbrand Rd NE, Covington, GA 30014.
Ozburn Electrical provides EV charger setups as part of its residential solutions for homeowners across Covington and east metro Atlanta.
The company offers specialized services including EV charger networks for apartments or businesses.
Ozburn Electrical handles smart electric vehicle (EV) charging station installations, site design, and installation services for businesses, apartment complexes, or retail locations.
The company assists clients in managing driver sign-ups while the EV charger manufacturer provides the network, reporting, and management support.
Ozburn Electric offers upgrades to Level 2 EV Chargers or Level 3 (Fast DC) EV Chargers.
The company is a member of IEC – Independent Electrical Contractors of Georgia, Better Business Bureau, Avetta (formerly PICS Auditing), ISN (Collect, Verify, Connect), and SAP Ariba.
Ozburn Electrical provides 24/7 emergency electrical response to minimize downtime for facilities anywhere in its service area.
The company's commercial and industrial teams manage large-scale automation, energy management systems, PLCs, VFDs, custom panel building, lighting retrofits, and generator setup.
Service areas include Covington, GA, and throughout metro Atlanta, GA, including neighborhoods like Sandy Springs, Snellville, Lawrenceville, Conyers, Loganville, Social Circle and beyond.
Ozburn Electrical Contractors offers a 1-year warranty on all services and a 100% satisfaction guarantee.
Technicians are fully screened, background checked, drug tested, uniformed, and equipped with modern diagnostic tools.
The company employs certified and LEED-Accredited contractors with high ethical standards.
source_title: Electrician Near Me Covington & Atlanta | Contractors

facts: Rivian has gained approval to start construction on a new manufacturing plant outside of Atlanta, Georgia.
The final approval for the project was granted by the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDECD) and the Joint Development Authority (JDA) of Jasper, Morgan, Newton and Walton counties.
State officials announced the approval on Thursday via WSB-TV.
The approval grants Rivian long-term rights to construct an automotive plant in the Stanton Springs industrial area east of Atlanta.
Plans for the facility were first set in motion in 2021.
The JDA and GDECD described closing and issuing bonds for the project as a generational opportunity for residents of Jasper, Morgan, Newton, and Walton counties.
The factory is expected to manufacture Rivian’s R2 next-generation platform for future vehicles.
The site is located near the I-20 corridor to Atlanta’s east, near the historic Walton County community Social Circle and touching on Morgan County.
This facility will be Rivian’s second manufacturing plant, following its first factory in Normal, Illinois, which produces the R1T pickup and the R1S SUV.
Rivian previously gained approval for its Economic Development Agreement (EDA) for the factory earlier this year.
The new approval officially allows the automaker to begin construction.
Shortly after Rivian’s initial 2021 commitment for the Stanton Springs North site, Georgia approved a $43 million EV battery recycling plant nearby.
The Rivian location encompasses just under 2,000 acres at a site previously known as the East Atlanta Megasite.
The facility is expected to create up to 7,500 jobs in the area.
Rivian will invest $5 billion into building the facility.
Rivian is targeting 2026 for initial production at the plant.
The company eventually expects to reach an annual volume production of around 400,000 vehicles.
Rivian is expected to accelerate charging station deployment in nearby shopping centers, state parks and near its Ponce City Market showroom.
The production facility has prompted Georgia lawmakers to consider changes to direct sales laws that currently ban automobile sales outside of dealerships.
Tesla holds an exemption from the direct sales ban but is limited to five statewide sales sites as of last year.
Rivian is introducing a 24-month apprenticeship program selecting local students to support the Stanton Springs North facility.
Georgia HOPE Grants will provide tuition for the apprenticeship, while Rivian plans to fund additional expenses including textbooks and other learning materials.
source_title: Rivian approved for $5 billion manufacturing plant in Georgia

facts: American automaker Rivian selected Chicago-based construction firm Clayco to build its $5 billion EV manufacturing plant in Stanton Springs, Georgia.
The 1,800-acre complex will sit about 40 minutes from Atlanta.
The plant will produce about 400,000 vehicles per year when fully complete.
Clayco expects the plant to begin vehicle production in 2026.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced the $5 billion campus in December 2021.
The announcement was cited as a sign of success in Georgia's push to recruit the entire electric mobility ecosystem to the state.
Other EV plant projects in Georgia include LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group’s $4.3 billion battery cell plant in Savannah, Georgia.
Auto parts manufacturer Sewon America is building a $300 million facility in Rincon, Georgia.
The state initiative Georgia Quick Start will establish and operate a manufacturing training center to meet workforce needs.
The training center will offer Rivian a tailored training program aligned with the company’s startup requirements.
The Georgia Department of Labor will collaborate with Rivian in identifying and recruiting skilled workers.
The project marks Clayco’s continued involvement in EV manufacturing construction.
In August, Clayco kicked off phase one of a $4 billion VinFast EV manufacturing campus in North Carolina.
The VinFast campus spans 3 million square feet and about 1,800 acres.
The VinFast plant consists of five main production areas: a body shop, general assembly, press shop, paint shop, and an energy center.
Clayco expects the VinFast factory to start production in 2025.
The VinFast factory will have a production capacity of 150,000 vehicles per year.
source_title: Clayco nabs $5B Rivian EV factory project in Georgia | Construction Dive

facts: French auto supplier OPmobility will be the first company to set up shop in Rivian’s new supplier park in Normal, Illinois.
The supplier park is where the electric-vehicle maker is preparing to build its next-generation R2 model.
The announcement comes just days after Rivian broke ground on its $5 billion factory in Georgia.
The Georgia factory is slated to open in 2028.
Rivian is trying to strengthen its supply chain ahead of the R2’s 2026 launch.
Illinois Governor JB Pritzker said OPmobility will assemble bumpers for Rivian’s upcoming R2 SUV.
OPmobility’s first Illinois facility will create 81 jobs with support from the state’s Reimagining Energy and Vehicles program.
In May, Rivian disclosed it was receiving $16 million in state incentives to help fund a new supplier park adjacent to its manufacturing plant.
Founded in France, OPmobility specializes in exterior and lighting systems, energy storage and hydrogen solutions.
OPmobility operates 150 industrial sites and 40 R&D centers globally.
Rivian founder and CEO RJ Scaringe said the new park “will provide improved material supply logistics as we increase production in 2026 to build R2, in addition to R1 and our commercial vans.”
Illinois has been investing heavily to keep Rivian’s manufacturing hub competitive.
In 2024, the state announced $1.5 billion in incentives to expand production of the R2 in Normal.
The state announced another $120 million package earlier this year to support the 1.2 million-square-foot supplier park.
The program includes $634 million in tax credits tied to job creation.
OPmobility follows Adient, a Michigan-based seat maker, which in July said it would open a nearby plant to assemble seating systems for Rivian, adding 75 jobs.
Rivian’s VP of manufacturing operations Carlo Materazzo said locating suppliers close to its factory “reduces costs and improves manufacturing efficiency.”
The Irvine, California-based automaker is scaling up capacity in Illinois as it prepares to sell the R2 SUV for about $45,000.
Orders for the R2 are expected to open in early 2026.
Rivian Founder and CEO RJ Scaringe said: “We appreciate the State of Illinois’s partnership and support in helping attract key suppliers to the Normal area.”
The new office will open in late 2025 and expand further in 2026 alongside construction progress at Rivian’s planned factory in Social Circle, east of Atlanta.
Earlier this year, Rivian said it would establish a new East Coast headquarters in Atlanta, Georgia, as part of its broader investment in the state.
The electric vehicle maker is building its second manufacturing plant in Georgia.
source_title: Rivian Names First Partner for Illinois Supplier Park Ahead of R2 Launch | EV

entity_name: Georgia EV and Battery Supply Chain
canonical_name: Georgia EV and Battery Supply Chain
facts: Georgia occupies a crucial position in the Southeast battery supply chain.
As the region’s leader in electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing investment, the state is a key market for batteries produced locally.
Since 2018, Georgia has announced over $27.3 billion in investments to manufacture electric mobility products.
Kia’s West Point manufacturing facility is cited as part of this growth.
Hyundai’s Bryan County Metaplant is cited as part of this growth.
Rivian is developing an upcoming Atlanta East Coast headquarters and a Social Circle, GA manufacturing facility.
Rapid manufacturing growth in Georgia has attracted suppliers to the state and sent a clear demand signal for the region that the industry is here.

Georgia’s success in attracting EV companies to build large scale final assembly plants is creating opportunity for other states in the region to benefit from the development of an end-to-end battery supply chain.

States across the region are leaning into their individual strengths to take advantage of new opportunities afforded from different parts of the value chain, including mineral sourcing, mid-stream manufacturing, and battery recycling.
This growth across Georgia and the broader region’s economy is fundamental to supporting the long-term success of the robust EV ecosystem in the state.
Each state in the region has a role to play in establishing a cluster of new technology development and industry growth and stands to gain significant job creation and economic benefits.

On November 5, C2ES cohosted a roundtable with the Georgia Tech Strategic Energy Institute to highlight the current industry landscape in Georgia and explore actions to secure the domestic battery and critical materials supply chain in the region.
The event brought together over 45 stakeholders from the Georgia and across the Southeast to discuss supportive strategies and next steps for an industry at an inflection point in its development.
Workforce development is a linchpin for the long-term growth potential of the industry.
There was broad agreement that Georgia must build on its existing workforce development programs to broaden access to training for emerging sectors.
The battery industry provides new career pathways in advanced manufacturing and innovation in a sector that may be unfamiliar to jobseekers.

Participants suggested that the industry should engage with technical colleges to identify more direct opportunities to support training, and the need for educational campaigns to increase young students’ awareness of opportunities in the battery sector.
Commercialization pathways must be clearer for the new technological innovations emerging from Southeast research institutions.
Currently, gaps exist in the translation of new technologies from concepts developed in a lab to real-world companies with commercial offerings.
This is due in part to a lack of early-stage investors for the battery industry in the Southeast.
Further challenges exist in the funding landscape for nascent companies, who are challenged to secure capital to prove the scalability of their technologies.

Roundtable participants called for more integrated partnerships between industry and research institutions, and more education of investors on the Southeast battery landscape to facilitate the commercialization of newly developed technologies.
New battery and material manufacturing facilities in the Southeast need near-term policy support to be more competitive on global price today.

To develop an American battery industry that is competitive in the long term with other global producers, Southeast states must invest today in building out an industrial base for battery material manufacturing.

This includes near-term policy support and industry actions to enable producers to compete on price in the global market currently dominated by mature actors that benefit from low input costs and economies of scale.

Participants identified several ways to lower costs for manufacturers, including providing access to low-cost renewable energy, offering preferential funding terms for new manufacturing lines, and supporting increased high-volume scale automated manufacturing processes.
Over the past year, C2ES has hosted four roundtables related to the battery and critical material industry across the Southeast.
Throughout the series, participants collaboratively developed a set of near-term, actionable recommendations to drive the industry forward.
This includes conversations hosted in the following states, each of which includes takeaways and recommendations from the conversation:
In the coming days, C2ES will compile and publish the takeaways from the roundtable Atlanta, Georgia.

Next year, participants from each event and other stakeholders will reconvene for a regional summit to turn ideas into action and build a path forward for a secure, resilient Southeast U.S. battery materials supply chain.
source_title: Georgia Manufacturing Charges Regional Battery Industry Growth - Center for Climate and Energy Solutions Regional RoundtablesCenter for Climate and Energy Solutions

facts: Installs EV chargers in garages, driveways, or carports to cut charging times in half.
Provides upfront pricing and exceptional customer service for EV charger installation in Social Circle, Georgia.
Offers a wide range of electrical services including lighting repair and installations, emergency support, outlet and smart home upgrades, and safety checks.
Prioritizes safety with thorough home electrical safety checks and whole-home surge protection to keep the electrical system secure.
Provides 24/7 local electrician customer support and dispatches service professionals 365 days a year for emergency needs.

Serves Social Circle, Georgia, and surrounding communities including Buckhead, Conyers, Covington, Good Hope, Hillsboro, Jenkinsburg, Loganville, Lovejoy, Madison, Mansfield, Monroe, Monticello, Newborn, Oxford, Porterdale, Rutledge, Shady Dale, and Sunny Side.
Specializes in installing a full range of EV chargers, including hardwired high-amperage units, plug-in models, and smart Wi-Fi options with load sharing.
Recommends 32-40A chargers as a great option for daily commutes under 40 miles.
Recommends 48-60A chargers to speed up the charging process for longer trips or households with multiple EVs.
Notes that renters often prefer plug-in chargers with NEMA 14-50 outlets for greater flexibility.
States homeowners generally opt for hardwired EV charger installations in Social Circle, Georgia, for enhanced speed, permanence, and reliability.
Most electric vehicle chargers require a dedicated 240V circuit to avoid tripping circuit breakers or interfering with other devices.
Level 1 chargers can usually be plugged into a standard outlet but charge much slower.
Panel upgrades are only needed if the electrical panel is full or the service cannot handle the added load.
Suggests alternatives like load management devices, smart chargers that throttle during peaks, or repurposing an existing 240V circuit to avoid a full upgrade.
Recommends a 200A service upgrade to future-proof homes, simplify permitting, and accommodate additional high-demand appliances.
Installing an EV charger at home typically requires an electrical permit and a final inspection from the local building department.
Installers submit load calculations and plans, and inspectors verify dedicated circuit, breaker size, and code compliance before providing approval.
Residents of condos, apartments, or HOA communities likely need written permission from the association or property manager, along with a municipal permit.
Utility companies and insurers may require proof of a permitted installation to qualify for rebates or maintain coverage.
Upgrades like panel changes, new circuits, or increased service capacity may necessitate extra permits or coordination with utility providers.
Safe EV charger installation begins with a code-compliant electrical plan including accurate load calculations and detailed reviews of the electrical panel.
Installers apply manufacturer's torque specifications on every connection, complete proper bonding throughout the system, and integrate whole-home surge protection.
Uses only UL-listed components and provides required GFCI protection for chargers located in garages or outdoors.

Outdoor EV charger installations receive additional protections including weather-rated enclosures (NEMA 3R/4), sealed conduit with drip loops, proper mounting height, and clearances that promote ventilation and easy accessibility.
Before energizing the system, professionals label the breaker, verify panel capacity, and test the circuit.
Can diagnose issues with existing EV charging stations and replace worn or faulty components like cables, connectors, holsters, and breakers.
Services most charger brands and models with code-compliant electrical repair.
Addresses code-related repairs including GFCI protection, proper breaker sizing, and detailed load calculations.
Handles necessary permitting and verifies performance with calibrated testing to ensure every repair or replacement meets code requirements.
Offers ongoing maintenance and upgrades including replacing worn components, installing surge protection, or increasing circuits to support higher charging speeds.
Actively hiring electricians, offering opportunities for growth and success in the electrical service space.

facts: Launch 3 Telecom announced it has secured a new facility in Lakeland, Florida.
The new Lakeland facility is located at 848 Kensinger Road, Lakeland, FL 33815.
The facility spans approximately 25,000 square feet.
Launch 3 expects to complete its move into the facility in the next two to three months from June 15, 2026.
The expansion enables Launch 3 to store, manage, and distribute a wider range of telecom, electrical, and related products.
CEO David Zoldan stated the company is investing heavily in logistics and new product categories as the traditional wireless market matures.
Launch 3 is strategically pivoting into logistics and third-party logistics (3PL) services.
Launch 3 is expanding into electrical products and complementary technologies.
Launch 3 is forming new distribution partnerships with manufacturers outside its core wireless business.
The Lakeland expansion supports Launch 3's goal to become a go-to logistics and distribution partner for manufacturers seeking reach, reliability, and scalable fulfillment in North America.
Launch 3 is actively seeking new partnerships with manufacturers in electrical and related sectors looking for distribution and logistics support.
To support its expansion, Launch 3 is actively hiring high-level, senior professionals across key functions.
Hiring focuses on top-tier, leadership-level talent for roles supporting logistics, operations, business development, and strategic growth.
Launch 3 Telecom is a nationwide distributor and logistics partner specializing in wireless infrastructure and electrical products and solutions.
The company generates approximately $25 million in annual revenue.
Launch 3 provides materials from leading manufacturers alongside value-added services including third-party logistics (3PL), site kitting, and operational support.
The company is headquartered in Fairfield, New Jersey.
Launch 3 serves the telecom, DAS, EV charging, data center, and electrical industries with scalable solutions designed to streamline project execution nationwide.
source_title: Launch 3 Telecom Secures New Lakeland Facility

facts: Freyr Battery is a Norwegian battery company that could receive more than $358 million in grants, tax breaks, and other inducements from state and local governments.
The planned Coweta County factory will produce giant energy storage systems for utilities and other clients.
Details of the incentives were obtained by The Atlanta Journal-Constitution through the Georgia Open Records Act.
Freyr announced plans in November for a $2.57 billion factory in Coweta County that will employ 724 workers making energy storage systems and lithium-ion batteries.
Freyr is among many companies moving to Georgia to produce electrified products, which is a priority for state leaders.
Freyr cited Georgia's education system and ability to recruit clean-tech workers as deciding factors for choosing Georgia over more than two dozen states for its first North American factory.
The package of incentives offered by Georgia and Coweta leaders helped tip the scales for Freyr's location decision.

Greg LeRoy, executive director of the incentives watchdog Good Jobs First, expressed concern that Coweta County residents may have to cover local government costs for Freyr's new factory until the tax abatement ends.
LeRoy stated that the county will need to hire more teachers, handle more trash collection, widen roads, and manage more 911 calls due to the factory.

Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, stated that Freyr represents a new niche in the electric battery and vehicle ecosystem and helps diversify Georgia's sector.
Wilson noted that Freyr's technology is vital to the transition to electrification and fills a gap in both Georgia's and the U.S.'s battery sectors.
Freyr CEO Tom Einar Jensen stated that Georgia's increasing talent pool cemented Coweta County as the company's final location choice.
Freyr stated that talent was the most important factor for selecting its first U.S. plant location.
The company acknowledged that the total package of financial incentives for its "Giga America" project played an important role in the decision.
The largest component of Freyr's incentives is approximately $228 million in local property tax breaks spread over 20 years.
Coweta County offered Freyr a $20 million grant.
Georgia will provide nearly $90 million in sales tax exemptions for qualified machinery and construction materials for the factory.
Freyr will receive nearly $11 million in state tax credits for new jobs created.
Georgia will provide a $7 million state grant to offset land costs.
The total incentive package could increase in value over time based on future expenditures.
No specific value was assigned to potential tax breaks for clean room equipment, manufacturing energy use, and other incentives based on future expenditures.
The planned factory, named Giga America, will be located near Amazon and Shopify distribution centers off I-85 near Newnan.
Sarah Jacobs, president of the Coweta County Development Authority, stated that Freyr will add industrial diversity to an area dominated by logistics and shipping companies.
As part of the incentive package, Freyr is required to employ 724 workers at the new factory with an average salary exceeding $60,000.
This average salary represents 127% of the average wage in Coweta County.
Coweta County and Arizona were the final two location options considered by Freyr.
Freyr's incentive package is smaller than those offered to Hyundai Motor Group ($1.8 billion) and Rivian ($1.5 billion) for their EV factories.
Greg LeRoy questioned the rationale for financially incentivizing job creation in Georgia while unemployment remains near record lows.
LeRoy stated that most new hires will likely come from outside the area or enter the labor market for the first time.
LeRoy calculated that the incentives average roughly $490,000 per job and questioned whether each employee could generate enough local spending and tax revenue to justify it.
Freyr was founded in 2018 and is simultaneously constructing a plant in Norway.
Freyr's energy storage systems utilize lithium-ion batteries to store electricity from renewable sources like wind and solar.
Freyr, which has been publicly traded since last year, reported a $93.9 million net loss in the third quarter.
Koch Industries supports Freyr and its Georgia project.
Koch Industries has become a major player in battery technology, and the project qualifies for incentives under President Joe Biden's Inflation Reduction Act.
The 870,000-square-foot facility is scheduled to open in 2026.
Phase one of the project will focus on initial battery cell production.
A second phase of cell production is expected to be operational by 2029.
Pat Wilson stated that Freyr's presence will help attract suppliers of battery components like cathodes and anthodes.
Qcells confirmed a $2.5 billion expansion of its Georgia production on Wednesday.
Officials described this Qcells expansion as the largest-ever investment in U.S. clean energy manufacturing.
The article's coverage is supported by a partnership with the 1Earth Fund, the Kendeda Fund, and Journalism Funding Partners.
The total value of the incentive package is $358.5 million.
The package includes a $7 million grant from the Regional Economic Business Assistance (REBA) program.
The package includes $3.3 million from the Georgia Quick Start program.
The incentive values listed are rounded.

links.context: Freyr Battery Incentive Package document by Zachary Hansen

source_title: In bid for big battery plant, Georgia anted up $358M in incentives

facts: Freyr Battery is inaugurating lithium-ion battery production on its demonstration line in Mo i Rana, Norway in March this year.
The battery maker will start sample production at its Customer Qualification Plant (CQP) based on the cell technology it licences from US-based 24M Technologies.
The demonstration line will produce batteries based on 24M’s SemiSolid lithium-ion battery platform.
The company is holding a Chapter One opening event on March 28.
Freyr is launching demonstration production at the 13,000 sq.m CQP to make sample sales for its customers.
It is also running test production on different materials and to train its operatives.
Taken together the company expects to iron out any potential problems ahead of the official start of production at its GigaArtic battery plant, also in Mo i Rana, which is set for 2024.
According to Freyr, the 24M battery production process differs from conventional battery cell production by only having steps five steps in the production process, compared to usual 15-17 steps.

Speaking last December at an event at the Royal Society of Arts, Manufactures and Commerce in London, UK, Freyr Battery’s co-founder and CEO, Tom Einar Jensen, said the modularised footprint his company was putting down in Mo i Rana, in northern Norway, will bring a step change to the low-emission, industrial-scale production of lithium batteries.
So far, the company has agreements to supply energy storage devices for customers including Impact Clean Power Technology.
It has also set up a joint venture with Nidec for the production of an integrated battery energy storage, called Nidec Energy.
Localised production for the automotive industry is in high demand in Europe.
In its fourth quarter update for 2022, Freyr said it is targeting the commercial and passenger vehicle markets to complement supply to the energy storage system market.
Last November Freyr also announced the purchase of a site for its Giga America battery manufacturing project in Coweta County, Georgia.
The plant will begin production with 34 GWh capacity and an investment of $1.7 billion.
source_title: Freyr Battery to launch test production in Norway | News | Automotive Logistics

facts: FREYR Battery, a developer of clean, next-generation battery cell production capacity, announced the selection and purchase of a site in Coweta County, Georgia for its planned Giga America battery plant.
The company plans to create 723 new jobs and invest more than $2.57 billion in Coweta County through 2029.
The new flagship manufacturing plant will be located at the Bridgeport Industrial Park near Moreland.
FREYR is a company based in Luxembourg committed to assisting the local supply chain for renewable energy sources.
The company’s battery cells have a variety of uses, including stationary energy storage (ESS) and electric mobility.
To allow for an expedited start to construction, FREYR is creating a strategy to carry out the project in a phased approach.
The new project execution plan takes into account the soaring incremental customer demand for clean, next-generation batteries and the recently passed U.S. Inflation Reduction Act.
FREYR’s Co-Founder and Chief Executive Officer Tom Einar Jensen commented that expanding into the U.S. has been a foundational aspect of the company's long-term strategy from inception.
The project is expected to be developed in multiple phases beginning with an initial battery cell production module of approximately 34 GWh.
The entire investment in Coweta County could be well over $2.57 billion, including planned, value-enhancing upstream and downstream modules.
The SemiSolidTM technology of FREYR’s American licensing partner, 24M Technologies, will serve as the foundation for the project.
Lithium-ion battery manufacture can be done at scale with a high level of capital efficiency thanks to the simplified SemiSolidTM platform.
With local utility providers involvement, FREYR is currently reviewing clean power supply options for the project.
The long-term goal of the company is to adopt the same approach used at Giga Arctic to create some of the cleanest next-generation batteries.

Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman Bob Blackburn welcomed FREYR Battery to the community, noting it is quickly becoming a premier destination for top-tier manufacturing industries that support the U.S. economy.
The site will be in the Bridgeport Industrial Park, where a mix of heavy and light industrial has been permitted since 2014.
FREYR chose Coweta County after an extensive and competitive site selection process, citing the county’s workforce, location, and business-friendly climate.
Coweta County has a strong manufacturing workforce with the ability to attract talent from universities and technical colleges.
The robust transportation infrastructure of highway and rail provides easy access to Atlanta and the Port of Savannah.
Coweta County Development Authority Chairman John Daviston stated that collaboration was integral to the success of the project and credited teamwork among local and statewide stakeholders.
FREYR Battery is looking to fill openings for administrative, technical, and managerial positions.
FREYR aims to provide industrial scale clean battery solutions to reduce global emissions and is listed on the New York Stock Exchange.
FREYR has commenced building the first of its planned factories in Mo i Rana, Norway.
The company announced potential development of industrial scale battery cell production in Vaasa, Finland, and the United States.
FREYR intends to install 50 GWh of battery cell capacity by 2025, 100 GWh annual capacity by 2028, and 200 GWh of annual capacity by 2030.
source_title: Battery Manufacturer to invest $2.57 Billion in Coweta County – Winters Media

facts: Anovion Technologies is an electric vehicle (EV) battery materials manufacturer.
Anovion announced it will invest $800 million in Decatur County, Georgia.
The investment will create 400 jobs.
A new 1.5 million-square-foot manufacturing facility is being built for the project.
The facility is located at the county-owned Downrange Industrial Park.
The facility will produce high-quality synthetic graphite anode, a material critical in the EV battery supply chain.
Rick McCaskill serves as head of the Development Authority of Bainbridge and Decatur County.
The Development Authority invested $50 million into the park’s infrastructure for the project.

Anovion selected the Southwest Georgia location due to its proximity to existing and planned low- and carbon-free energy sources, short supply chains, access to existing rail infrastructure, a skilled workforce, and a business-friendly environment.
Decatur County is a member of Locate South Georgia, a regional economic development entity comprised of 22 counties in Southwest Georgia.
Locate South Georgia conducts an annual bus tour covering more than 10,000 square miles to showcase sites and buildings to project managers.
In November 2022, the Locate South Georgia tour included Decatur County.
A Georgia Department of Economic Development project manager identified the site during the November 2022 tour as a potential fit for Anovion.
The request for proposal (RFP) was quickly sent and accepted following the site visit.
Prior to the Anovion announcement, Locate South Georgia reported $324.5 million in new investment and 575 jobs created in 2022 across its member counties.
Grant Buckley serves as executive director of the Cordele-Crisp County Industrial Development Council and chair of Locate South Georgia.
Grant Buckley reported hearing inquiries from Tier 2 or Tier 3 EV suppliers potentially relocating to the region due to the Anovion project.
Rick McCaskill stated that regional marketing efforts by Locate South Georgia have been a significant benefit for attracting large economic development projects.
U.S. Sen. Jon Ossoff and Gov. Brian Kemp attended Anovion Technologies’ groundbreaking ceremony.
source_title: Press - Development Authority of Bainbridge and Decatur County

On 25 April 2024, Syensqo broke ground on its future battery-grade polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) facility in Augusta, Georgia.

entity_name: Golf Car Manufacturers in the USA
canonical_name: Top 10 Golf Car Companies Based in the United States of America
facts: The golf car industry in the United States has seen remarkable growth, driven by advancements in electric mobility, sustainable energy solutions, and expanding usage beyond golf courses.
Golf carts are widely used in resorts, gated communities, airports, industrial facilities, and recreational areas.
Club Car is located in Augusta, Georgia, USA, established in 1958 as a manufacturer of electric and gas-powered golf cars, utility vehicles, and fleet golf cars.
Club Car is known for advanced technology including connected fleet management systems and lithium-ion batteries, with customizable options for personal and commercial use.
EZ-Go is located in Augusta, Georgia, USA, established in 1954 as a manufacturer of golf cars, personal transport vehicles, and utility vehicles.
EZ-Go is a subsidiary of Textron Inc., a global leader in aerospace and industrial technologies.
EZ-Go introduced the ELiTE lithium-powered golf carts, which deliver faster charging, longer battery life, and increased efficiency.
Yamaha Golf Car Company is located in Kennesaw, Georgia, USA, established in 1979 as a division of Yamaha Motor Corporation offering electric and gas-powered golf cars.
Yamaha models feature advanced suspension systems, fuel-efficient engines, and innovative power management technologies for smooth performance and long-term reliability.
Cushman is located in Augusta, Georgia, USA, established in 1901 as a manufacturer now part of Textron Inc., specializing in rugged golf cars and industrial transport vehicles.
Cushman vehicles include gas, electric, and diesel-powered options catering to golf courses, hospitality businesses, and commercial industries.
Polaris Industries Inc. is located in Medina, Minnesota, USA, established in 1954, producing golf cars under the GEM (Global Electric Motorcars) brand alongside ATVs and UTVs.

Polaris’ GEM golf cars are street-legal low-speed vehicles (LSVs) designed for residential, commercial, and recreational use, featuring safety certifications, modern styling, and long-lasting battery performance.

MMC Golf Carts is located in Bradenton, Florida, USA, operating as a distributor and custom dealer specializing in refurbished, street-legal, and customized golf carts with aftermarket modifications like high-performance batteries.

Star EV is located in Greenville, South Carolina, USA, established in 2003 and owned by JH Global Services, focusing on high-performance eco-friendly electric vehicles including lithium-ion-powered golf carts with faster charging times and zero emissions.

Tomberlin is located in Leesburg, Florida, USA, established in 2006 as a brand under the Columbia Vehicle Group manufacturing street-legal electric golf cars and neighborhood electric vehicles (NEVs) with standard features like seat belts, turn signals, LED lighting, and power steering.

Garia Luxury Golf Car is headquartered in Houston, Texas, USA (originally founded in Denmark in 2005), specializing in luxury electric golf carts featuring smart connectivity, ergonomic seating, and eco-friendly lithium battery systems.

Ruike is located in China & USA, established in 2009, manufacturing affordable high-performance electric golf carts and low-speed vehicles that integrate advanced battery technology and high-quality motors.
The top 10 companies offer a variety of models including electric, gas-powered, and solar-assisted vehicles designed for performance, comfort, and durability.
A well-maintained golf cart can last 10-15 years, with battery replacement needed every 4-6 years.
Top brands like Club Car, Yamaha, and EZ-Go offer warranties ranging from 2 to 5 years depending on the model.
Many golf car manufacturers and dealers offer financing plans, leasing options, and installment payments for purchasing carts.
Many companies offer fleet packages for resorts, golf courses, gated communities, and corporate campuses.
Brands like Garia, Ruike, Polaris GEM, and Star EV focus on energy-efficient lithium-ion batteries and sustainable production.

facts: Q CELLS shipped roughly 20 gigawatts of solar panels globally in 2024, making it one of the ten largest module manufacturers in the world.
The brand is most commonly quoted by US installers looking to split the difference between budget Chinese imports and premium European glass.
Q CELLS was founded in 1999 in Bitterfeld-Wolfen and built a strong European reputation before being acquired by South Korean conglomerate Hanwha Group in 2012.
Manufacturing is currently split across South Korea, Malaysia, and a facility in Dalton, Georgia.
The Dalton, Georgia facility produces Q.PEAK DUO series panels, which qualify for the full 30% federal Investment Tax Credit under the Inflation Reduction Act’s domestic content adder.
For most homeowners, Q CELLS represents a reliable tier-one option priced between $0.28 and $0.38 per watt at the module level, before installation labour.
The flagship residential line in 2026 is the Q.PEAK DUO BLK ML-G10+, a monocrystalline PERC panel available from 395W to 430W.
Panel efficiency for the Q.PEAK DUO BLK ML-G10+ sits between 20.4% and 21.6% depending on the specific wattage bin.
The Q.PEAK DUO L-G10.3 (non-black-frame version) tops out at 425W with a cell efficiency of 21.4%.
Temperature coefficient for power (Pmax) is -0.34%/°C on the G10+ series, which is better than the industry average of roughly -0.38%/°C.

Q CELLS markets low-light performance citing its proprietary Q.ANTUM cell technology, which uses a passivated rear surface and fine-line front metallisation to improve current collection under diffuse irradiation.

NREL testing has broadly validated that PERC rear-passivation improves output at low angles and under cloud cover compared to standard BSF cells, with gains typically 2–4% relative to conventional mono cells.
The 400W Q.PEAK DUO BLK ML-G10+ measures 1,755 × 1,038 × 32 mm and weighs 20.9 kg (46 lb).
Wind load rating is 2,400 Pa front and 2,400 Pa rear, and snow load is 5,400 Pa.
The panel carries UL 61730 and IEC 61215 certifications, both required by most US utilities and local authorities before interconnection approval.
Module-only pricing for Q.PEAK DUO BLK ML-G10+ panels in Q1 2026 sits at approximately $0.30–$0.36 per watt through US distributors.

For a typical 8 kW residential system (20 panels), module cost lands between $2,400 and $2,900, representing about 18–22% of the full installed system cost, which averages $24,000–$28,000 before incentives nationally according to SEIA data.
After the 30% federal Investment Tax Credit, an 8 kW system falls to roughly $16,800–$19,600.

Homeowners whose installers use domestically manufactured Q CELLS panels from the Dalton, Georgia facility may also qualify for an additional 10% domestic content adder under IRA rules, bringing the effective credit to 40% and reducing net cost further to around $14,400–$16,800.
California installers typically quote Q CELLS systems at $2.70–$3.20 per watt all-in.
Texas installers often come in at $2.40–$2.90 per watt due to a more competitive and lower-labour-cost market.
Massachusetts homeowners tend to pay a premium of $3.00–$3.50 per watt but benefit from the state’s SMART programme, which can add meaningful ongoing income on top of net metering.
Payback periods for a Q CELLS system typically run 7–10 years in high-electricity-cost states and 9–13 years in lower-rate markets, based on current NREL modelling.
Q CELLS offers a 25-year product warranty covering manufacturing defects and a 25-year linear performance warranty on the Q.PEAK DUO series.
The performance warranty guarantees no more than 2% degradation in year one and no more than 0.54% per year thereafter, with a guaranteed output of at least 86% of rated power at year 25.

The 0.54% annual degradation figure is slightly better than the industry standard of 0.55–0.60% per year that NREL cites as typical for monocrystalline PERC panels in its Long-Term PV Degradation Study.
Over 25 years, a 400W panel should still produce a minimum of 344W — or about 860 kWh annually on a south-facing roof in a mid-sun location.
The product warranty is honoured by Hanwha Q CELLS USA Inc., a US legal entity, which reduces insolvency risk compared to manufacturers backed by entities without US assets.
The company explicitly directs warranty claims to their US operations and maintains a dedicated US claims portal.

At the premium end, SunPower Maxeon 6 panels deliver 22.8% efficiency with a 40-year performance warranty, but module pricing of $0.55–$0.70 per watt makes a typical 8 kW system cost $4,400–$5,600 more at the panel level alone before installation labour.
Canadian Solar’s HiKu6 series is priced at $0.26–$0.30 per watt with efficiency up to 20.9%, slightly below Q CELLS on both metrics.
Panasonic EverVolt HK Black panels achieve 22.2% efficiency at $0.42–$0.48 per watt and carry an industry-leading temperature coefficient of -0.26%/°C.

Jinko Solar’s Tiger Neo series offers strong efficiency at the lowest price point — around $0.24–$0.28 per watt — but lacks US-based warranty support and domestic manufacturing, which can complicate ITC domestic content claims and long-term recourse.

Independent performance monitoring data from projects on the PVOutput community database and EnergySage’s installer network shows systems using Q.PEAK DUO panels in North Carolina and across the Sun Belt generally perform within 3–5% of their modelled PVWatts output.

Q CELLS rates its Q.PEAK DUO series as PID-resistant per IEC 62804 testing, which is increasingly important as string inverter system voltages exceed 1,000V in larger residential and commercial installations.
Panels without PID resistance can lose 5–30% output over time when installed in high-voltage strings, particularly in humid climates.

Field failure rate data from EnergySage’s 2024 installer survey places Q CELLS in the lower-failure-rate tier among leading brands, with reported defect rates under 0.1% over 5-year installation windows.
This aligns with Q CELLS’ Bloomberg NEF Tier 1 classification, held continuously since 2013.
Tier 1 status measures bankability and manufacturing quality control, not raw panel performance, but it correlates with consistent module quality over time.
source_title: Q CELLS Solar Panels Review 2026: Quality at Mid-Range Prices | Green Energy Calculators

facts: An automobile parts supplier plans to build a new factory in Henry County to supply Georgia’s electric vehicle industry, namely cars made by Hyundai Motor Group.
NVH Korea will invest $72 million in the new plant in Locust Grove, south of Atlanta.
The plant is expected to employ 160 workers.
The investment and employment details were announced in a Thursday news release.
NVH Korea produces a variety of noise, vibration, and heat-control parts.
The new facility will focus on supplying battery parts to Hyundai and its subsidiaries, including Kia and parts supplier Hyundai Mobis.
Gov. Brian Kemp made the announcement while in France for the Paris Air Show.
During his trip to France, Gov. Kemp is touting Georgia for aerospace industry expansion.
The NVH Korea auto parts plant is the second large jobs recruitment announcement to come during his trip to France.
Pratt & Whitney’s $206 million commitment to expand its Columbus operation and add 400 jobs preceded the NVH Korea announcement on this trip.
source_title: EV parts supplier announces plans for $72M metro Atlanta factory

facts: The client is a confidential, rapidly growing electric vehicle supplier with its name withheld at the client’s request.
The company was expanding capacity to support major new contracts with premier automakers across North America.
The enterprise required an entirely new layer of specialized production and launch management to hit critical delivery windows and successfully bring new production plants online on time.

Key operational pressures included a limited internal leadership bench equipped to manage the complexities of greenfield or brownfield plant launches, significant market difficulty recruiting highly experienced technically fluent automotive production executives, intense operational pressure to meet uncompromising automaker and OEM launch deadlines, an immediate organizational need for advanced-manufacturing and industrial automation expertise, the challenge of rapidly scaling a specialized workforce across multiple new manufacturing facilities, and pronounced operational-consistency and quality control concerns between separate geographic plants.

The company’s board and CEO determined that mitigating these launch risks required a synchronized executive team spanning a VP Production, VP Manufacturing Engineering, Launch Managers, Plant Managers, Director of Automation, and Director of Operational Excellence.
JRG Partners executed a highly confidential, multi-role North American retained search strategy to equip the supplier with critical manufacturing, launch, and automation leadership.

The engagement involved comprehensive candidate mapping across primary EV and automotive markets in North America, including Detroit, Columbus, Nashville, Austin, Phoenix, Atlanta, Toronto, and Monterrey.
JRG Partners evaluated 500+ elite EV production and advanced manufacturing executive profiles across North America.
The cross-functional plant launch and operational excellence core was successfully placed within a 10-month timeline.

Phase 1 of the search strategy involved partnering directly with the CEO, Board, and executive team to systematically map existing organizational gaps, identify hidden launch risks, clarify capacity objectives, and define exact leadership competencies required for long-term growth tied to the company’s multi-year plant launch strategy.

Phase 2 executed an exhaustive market-mapping initiative targeting high-performing, highly automated environments across North America, focusing on talent pools across electric vehicle manufacturers, specialized automotive suppliers, battery manufacturers, advanced manufacturing organizations, industrial automation companies, precision manufacturing businesses, and high-growth production environments.

Phase 3 utilized a research-driven, discreet outreach framework to directly contact and evaluate high-achieving executives with verified records of success in new plant launches, workforce scaling, and industrial robotics integration.

Phase 4 involved rigorous assessment of finalist candidates on strategic and functional capabilities, factory floor automation agility, team-building and leadership philosophies, cross-functional communication skills, cultural alignment with rapid scaling, and long-term retention potential.

Within ten months, JRG Partners successfully filled the entire multi-role mandate, providing the electric vehicle supplier with a synchronized manufacturing, engineering, and plant launch core equipped to expand capacity.
Newly placed plant directors and launch managers successfully brought new production facilities online on schedule, mitigating costly activation delays.
Placed production leaders successfully scaled plant output, allowing the organization to comfortably fulfill volume requirements for its new automotive contracts.
Strategic automation hires successfully integrated advanced manufacturing systems, noticeably lifting line productivity and facility throughput.
On-time facility launches and stable production scale-ups strengthened crucial OEM relationships and built long-term commercial trust.
The search reinforced Board and investor confidence in the long-term expansion plan by resolving immediate execution gaps and eliminating multi-site launch risks.
500+ targeted electric vehicle, advanced manufacturing, and industrial automation executive profiles were systematically mapped across key North American automotive corridors.
A complete, multi-member leadership framework spanning the VP level, launch management, plant leadership, and advanced automation was successfully placed and integrated within a 10-month timeline.
Separate regional facilities were unified under a single, cohesive operational excellence framework by installing proven transformation executives.
Line calibration and workforce scaling across multiple plants were accelerated by placing forward-thinking manufacturing engineering directors.
Advanced manufacturing bottlenecks were resolved and production yield improved through the strategic placement of specialized automation leaders.
Crucial OEM commercial contracts, launch milestones, and sensitive capital investments were protected during a high-stakes transition phase through strict search confidentiality.
The Board of Directors and CEO were provided with a high-performance management framework explicitly equipped to eliminate operational launch risks and drive profitable EV infrastructure innovation.

facts: Located at 500 Interstate N Pkwy, Atlanta, GA 30339.
Offers battery service and replacement to increase vehicle battery life and prevent potential breakdowns.
Staff includes industry-certified technicians with years of experience in maintaining, diagnosing, and installing vehicle batteries and electrical systems for many makes and models.
Technicians service electric, hybrid, and other technologically-advanced cars on the road today.
Certified technicians are required to recertify on an ongoing basis to keep up with new equipment and vehicle makes and models.
Only uses quality OEM parts when replacing batteries or other electrical components.
OEM parts are sourced from an original manufacturer in the vehicle's certified supply chain rather than unknown companies.

OEM parts are guaranteed to be identical and have the same or better quality as when the part was new, possessing the same functionality required to maintain the engine's precise operational parameters.
