facts: Hyundai supplier Seoyon E-Hwa is investing almost $76 million toward its second manufacturing facility in Georgia.
The investment details were shared in a Feb. 1 press release.
This will be the second Georgia location for the South Korea-based supplier.
Seoyon’s new facility will be located at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The new manufacturing facility will produce automotive interior parts such as door trims, headlining, and tailgate trims.
The facility will also supply electric vehicle parts.
Seoyon E-Hwa Chairman Ryu Yang-Seok announced the plans in the press release.
The facility is slated to create 740 new jobs.
Production at the new facility is slated to begin in October 2024.

Savannah Economic Development Authority President and CEO Trip Tollison stated that the facility’s location at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center “ideally positions” it close to Hyundai Motor Group’s EV campus and the Port of Savannah.
Last year, Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp called an incoming $5.5 billion plant the “largest economic development project in state history.”
Gov. Kemp estimated that the incoming plant would bring 8,100 jobs to Georgia’s coastal region with more to follow.
Seoyon E-Hwa is the fourth Hyundai supplier to add a manufacturing plant in the region.
In November, Joon Georgia became the first supplier to announce operations, with plans to invest $317 million in a production facility in Bulloch County.
Later that month, Kemp announced Hyundai Mobis as the second supplier.

Last month, Ecoplastic Corporation announced it was investing approximately $205 million in a new manufacturing facility in southeast Georgia to supply components to the incoming electric vehicle campus.

Wilson stated that “That level of growth gives our industries the groundwork for future success, and we’re excited to work with our many partners across the state and near Savannah to create the jobs of the future.”
Seoyon E-Hwa has another facility in LaGrange, Georgia.
The LaGrange facility supports 630 full-time jobs.
source_title: Hyundai supplier plans second Georgia manufacturing site | Manufacturing Dive

facts: A 3MW solar photovoltaic (PV) project has been finished and marketed by United Renewable Energy at Kimberly-Clark's center in LaGrange, Georgia.

The project is a partnership between Kimberly-Clark's center, United Renewable Energy LLC as the project developer and home builder, and NextEra Energy Resources, LLC (a subsidiary) as the long-term project owner.
All output from the new solar center, along with all renewable energy credit reports (RECs), will be sold to Georgia Power as part of Georgia Power's Renewable Energy Development Initiative.
The linked RECs will be retired on behalf of all of Georgia Power's consumers.
The 3MW solar project includes more than 8,600 photovoltaic panels.
Along with renting the land for the project, Kimberly-Clark has participated in a separate arrangement to acquire RECs from NextEra Energy Resources to counter its greenhouse gas emissions.
Jeremy Cannady, mill supervisor at Kimberly-Clark's LaGrange center, stated that the growth of the project and acquisition of RECs demonstrate their dedication to renewable energy.
The project provides satisfaction for LaGrange workers, and buying RECs from NextEra Energy Resources helps achieve a global objective of a 40% offset in greenhouse gas emissions by 2022.

William Silva, CEO of United Renewable Energy, expressed pride in being part of the quality group including Kimberly-Clark and NextEra Energy Resources for the benefit of the LaGrange plant and community.

Matt Ulman, vice president of distributed generation for NextEra Energy Resources, noted that collaborations with commercial customers focus on helping them achieve business objectives like saving on utility costs or meeting GHG emission goals, and that distributed generation properties allow commercial services to make an impact at their centers where they appear to stakeholders and staff.
source_title: Kimberly-Clark backs neighborhood 3MW solar ranch in LaGrange, Georgia

The facility also operates as a convenience store where customers can purchase coffee, frozen yogurt, and hot food items.

facts: Trojan Battery Company is the leading brand of flooded lead acid, AES AGM, standard AGM, and lithium-ion batteries.
Trojan products are the standard for motive applications including golf carts, LSVs, floor machines, boats, RVs, work platforms, industrial equipment, solar and renewable energy, and more.
Trojan has manufactured high-performing, innovative deep cycle batteries since 1925.
The company was founded by George Godber and Carl Speer.
In 1952, Trojan developed the first golf cart battery for the Autoette vehicle, pioneering deep cycle battery technology for the golf cart industry.
Trojan is headquartered in Santa Fe Springs, California.
Trojan operates three ISO 9001:2015 certified manufacturing plants located in California and Georgia.
Trojan operates two advanced research and development centers dedicated exclusively to deep cycle battery technologies.
Trojan maintains international offices across the Americas, Europe, Africa, Middle East, and Asia.
Trojan maintains a global worldwide network of master distributors.
Trojan is a proud member of the Battery Council International (BCI) and the Advanced Lead Acid Battery Consortium.
Trojan applies rigorous industry battery testing procedures that adhere to BCI and International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) test standards.
In 2018, Trojan Battery Company was acquired by C&D Technologies.
The acquisition merged two iconic brands, both leading specialty battery manufacturers with over 100 years of market leadership.
The partnership between Trojan and C&D Technologies features global distribution and has created one of the world's largest energy storage providers.
Trojan offers a wide range of deep cycle flooded, AGM, gel, and lithium-ion battery solutions.
Quality Golf Carts in South Carolina uses the Trojan OnePack for lithium battery golf cart conversions.
Over nearly 100 years, Trojan batteries have earned awards for innovation, efficiency, technology, performance, reliability, sustainability, safety, and value.
Trojan is committed to continuous process improvement, reliability, world-class service and support for dealers, distributors, OEMs, and consumers.
The Maroon Standard has set the pace since the first electric golf cart was turned on, ensuring Trojan products are built to never falter as technologies change.

Browse our most recent achievements and accolades

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is building a $7.6 billion manufacturing complex in Ellabell, Georgia.
The facility is named Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
HMGMA is a software-defined factory integrating AI and robotics automation for electric and hybrid vehicle assembly.
The plant currently produces the Hyundai Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 EVs, and the Kia Sportage hybrid.
At full capacity, the facility targets an annual production of 500,000 EV and hybrid vehicles.
Production will cover a mix across 10 different models for the Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia brands.
HMGMA officially opened in March last year.
Euisun Chung, executive chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated at the opening that the facility showcases the carmaker's advanced manufacturing capabilities and commitment to innovation.
The plant uses cutting-edge robotics and vision systems designed to ensure high-quality and flexible vehicle production.
Robots assist human workers to improve efficiency at the facility.
Human workforce at HMGMA is projected to number 8,500 by 2031.
An on-site battery manufacturing facility operates as a joint venture between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution called HLBMA (formerly HL-GA Company).
The HLBMA joint venture represents a $4.3 billion investment in Georgia.
Located on the HMGMA complex in Ellabell, the facility produces battery cells with an annual capacity of 40 GWh.
HLBMA currently employs more than 500 people, mostly local hires.
A spokesperson for HLBMA stated the facility's focus remains on safe, high-quality operations supported by comprehensive training and dedicated on-site emergency response capabilities.
Hyundai committed to spending $6 billion to localize parts and strengthen logistics.
The commitment includes investing in US steel production.
Hyundai will use digital technology to connect and optimize processes from order collection, procurement, logistics, and production.
Hyundai aims to strengthen its group logistics to ensure robust supply chains in the US.
The investment comes despite a volatile market for battery electric vehicles (BEVs), notably following the removal of incentives by the Trump administration in September last year.
HMGMA's flexible assembly line allows Hyundai to accommodate hybrid production with little interruption.
Hyundai states its core strategy has not changed despite the removal of US EV tax incentives.
The company is adjusting the mix of EVs, hybrids, and internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles to align with market demand and policy conditions.
Hyundai maintains its long-term electrification investment outlook remains unaltered.
HMGMA serves as the innovation flagship for Hyundai Motor Group.
The plant scaled up advanced manufacturing technologies developed and validated at the Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center Singapore (HMGICS) for mass production.
HMGMA integrates AI, robotics, and data technologies into an automated production system connecting all processes from order collection to assembly.
Employees at the plant are known as 'Meta Pros'.
Automation and technology at HMGMA are used to boost efficiency, replace repetitive and hazardous tasks, enhance productivity, reduce physical injury, and improve the work environment.
Automated vehicle assembly applications target operations requiring high precision or that are difficult for humans.
Jerald Roach, head of department for general assembly at HMGMA, stated that robotics and vision systems lend themselves well to applications requiring vision data to precisely position components.
Robotics and vision can perform repetitive tasks beyond human physical limitations, allowing HMGMA to integrate a high number of successful technologies in volume production.
Hyundai is not sharing details of assembly equipment providers at HMGMA, noting many are group affiliates.
Boston Dynamic’s Atlas humanoid robots are scheduled for deployment in January 2028.
The Atlas humanoid has transitioned from prototype to production-ready industrial robot and is being built at Boston Dynamic's headquarters.
At HMGMA, Atlas will initially be used in the external scheduling area for unloading and sorting inbound parts, focusing on packaging boxes with large or heavy components.
In October last year, Hyundai tested the Atlas robot by having it sort roof racks for the assembly line.

During testing, an engineer wearing a virtual reality headset took direct control of Atlas to guide its hands and arms until the task was learned, generating data to train the robot's AI models for autonomous operation.
The Atlas robot features an externally positioned battery that allows it to replace its own battery, supported by fast-charging technology for a factory fleet.
Atlas' camera technology and flexible head enable it to see its environment, its body parts, and its spatial position to comply with safety priorities when working alongside humans.
EV battery cells are supplied from the on-site HLBMA facility.
Battery cells are delivered to Mobis Battery System Assembly, which packages them onto skateboard chassis before moving to the HMGMA assembly area via conveyor.
A second on-site Mobis facility, Mobis Module, produces the front end module for the vehicle.
Jerald Roach stated that feeding and marrying battery sub-assemblies at HMGMA is done via a highly automated process ensuring repeatability and precision.
Automation ensures critical components like front suspension, drive system, rear suspension, and battery subassembly are precisely positioned to the underbody with controlled fastening torque.
Hyundai maintains common locating schemes for major components so automation can be easily modified to accept new models across ICE, BEV, or hybrid powertrains.
HMGMA was specifically designed and built to optimize the assembly of BEVs, differing from traditional automotive assembly lines.
Hyundai emphasizes that marrying a large battery to the underbody must be considered in both factory design and vehicle design upfront.
Plants designed for ICE vehicles require significant structural modifications or compromises to address fundamental BEV differences.
Hyundai's modular vehicle design enables the flexibility required to build various powertrain combinations in a single plant.
Hyundai uses automated material delivery systems, including automated guided vehicles (AGVs) and automated trailer unloading systems, to avoid safety concerns associated with tuggers and forklifts.
An aggressive kitting strategy distributes sequenced parts to the point of use, increasing flexibility for multiple models and reducing decisions required from Meta Pros.
The lack of high numbers of parts displayed lineside improves overall visual management of assembly operations.
AGVs move vehicles along the assembly line on programmed paths guided by QR codes on the factory floor instead of traditional rigid conveyors.

HMGMA's sustainability measures include renewable energy sourcing, solar generation, energy-efficient facility design, reduced emissions through electrified production, and an advanced wastewater treatment system.
Water conservation measures include reclaimed water for landscaping and stormwater collection with controlled release.
Hyundai Motor Group signed a 15-year power purchase agreement with Matrix Renewables in 2024.
Hyundai purchased 147 MW of generation capacity from the Stillhouse Solar Project in Texas via this agreement.
Hyundai expects to secure approximately 378 GWh of electricity annually through the agreement.
The company anticipates an annual reduction of 140,000 tons of carbon emissions across its US facilities.
Jerald Roach stated that Hyundai remains confident in the future of BEV manufacturing in the US and will continue investing in local production capacity.
The company views electrification as a long-term growth driver supported by localization, scale, and consumer choice, even as near-term policy dynamics evolve.
source_title: Hyundai Metaplant America reshapes EV and hybrid production with smart factory automation

facts: Hyundai Motor Group officially unlocked its $7.6 billion metaplant in Georgia Wednesday.
The facility opening saved the company from tariff threats as U.S. President Donald Trump formalized 25 percent levies on all imported cars.
The metaplant is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The metaplant covers 11.76 million-square-meter (126.58 million-square-foot).
The plant will help Hyundai boost its U.S. production capacity to roughly 1.2 million units.
This capacity represents around 70 percent of the company's total U.S. sales as of last year.
The facility includes an automobile plant focusing on EVs and hybrids.
The facility includes a joint battery plant with LG Energy Solution.
The facility includes an auto parts factory from Hyundai Mobis.
The auto plant's current capacity stands at 300,000 units.
The goal is to increase the auto plant's capacity up to 500,000 units in the future.
This beats the Hyundai Alabama plant's 360,000 units.
This beats Kia's Georgia plant's 340,000 units.
Hyundai's Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 are being manufactured at the plant as of now.
Euisun Chung, executive chair of Hyundai Motor Group, spoke during the plant's opening ceremony.
The opening ceremony was attended by Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp, Hyundai Motor Group Vice Chairman Chang Jae-hoon, and CEO José Muñoz.

Euisun Chung stated: "Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) not only represents the group's advanced manufacturing capabilities and commitment to innovation, but also our investment in relationships with our partners and communities right here in Georgia."

Euisun Chung also stated: "With the rich history of craftsmanship and manufacturing in this community, together with the talented workforce at HMGMA, we are building the future of mobility with America, in America."
Trump declared 25 percent tariffs on all imported cars to the U.S. market on Wednesday.
The tariffs will take effect on April 2.
A correction was issued on March 27 noting an earlier version misstated the location and size of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
source_title: Hyundai's $8 billion Metaplant opens in Georgia as Trump announces auto tariffs

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is now open and represents the key pillar of the Group’s $12.6 billion investment in Georgia, marking the largest economic development project in the state’s history.
The vehicle assembly and battery plant will produce up to 500,000 electric and hybrid vehicles annually for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis brands.
Hyundai Motor Group commits to an additional $21 billion investment from 2025 to 2028 to drive U.S. manufacturing growth.
Since entering the U.S., Hyundai Motor Group has invested $20.5 billion in the U.S., creating directly and indirectly more than 570,000 American jobs.
The Grand Opening celebration was hosted on March 26, 2025, in Ellabell, Ga., marking the completion of the project just two and a half years after breaking ground.

Euisun Chung, Executive Chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated that HMGMA represents the Group’s advanced manufacturing capabilities, commitment to innovation, and investment in relationships with partners and communities in Georgia.

The event was attended by Governor Brian P. Kemp, U.S. Representative Buddy Carter, Vice Chair Jaehoon Chang, Hyundai Motor Company President and CEO José Muñoz, Kia Corporation President and CEO Ho Sung Song, company officials, state and local leaders, HMGMA Meta Pro employees, and other distinguished guests.

Governor Kemp highlighted collaborations between HMGMA and Georgia’s universities and technical colleges, including Georgia Quick Start’s on-site training facility, as well as award-winning infrastructure like ports connecting companies to markets.

HMG entered a landmark agreement with the State of Georgia in May 2022 to establish an electric and hybrid vehicle production and battery manufacturing facility in Ellabell, Georgia, located 20 miles outside historic Savannah.
By June 2022, HMGMA was officially formed, and within four months, the company broke ground on the project.
On October 3, 2024, less than two years after breaking ground, the first vehicle, a Hyundai IONIQ 5, rolled off the assembly line.
The Hyundai IONIQ 9 three-row electric SUV is now in production at HMGMA and will be joined in 2026 by the first Kia model built at the facility.
The plant is equipped to produce hybrid vehicles and initially has a capacity of 300,000 vehicles annually.
The Group's investment in Georgia related to HMGMA and joint battery ventures with LG Energy Solution and SK On totals $12.6 billion, including 8,500 jobs at HMGMA by 2031.
Total investments in Georgia are expected to create nearly 40,000 direct and indirect jobs and $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year, according to the Center for Automotive Research.

As of July 2024, more than $2.5 billion in capital investment and approximately 6,900 jobs have been announced by HMGMA suppliers across 12 counties in Georgia, doubling the originally anticipated $1 billion supplier investment.
The Group plans to expand HMGMA's annual production capacity to a total of 500,000 units, an increase of 200,000 units from the original plan.
Existing U.S. production sites, Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Autoland Georgia, will also see improvements in production facilities.

A total of $6 billion will be allocated to enhance the Group’s parts and logistics business and establish an Electric Arc Furnace-based integrated steel mill in the U.S. through affiliate Hyundai Steel, with an annual capacity of 2.7 million tons.
Commercial steel production at the new mill is scheduled to begin in 2029, supplying high-quality automotive steel plates to HMGMA.

The Group will invest an additional $6 billion to drive innovation and expand strategic partnerships with U.S. companies in autonomous driving, robotics, artificial intelligence, and advanced air mobility.

Through the $21 billion commitment, the Group anticipates creating 14,000 new direct full-time jobs in the U.S. by 2028, generating more than 100,000 direct and indirect job opportunities across related industries.
HMGMA has integrated proof-of-concept manufacturing technologies and systems from Hyundai Motor Group Innovation Center Singapore (HMGICS), scaled to accommodate larger production volumes.
All HMGMA processes, including order collection, procurement, logistics, and production, are optimized using AI and real-time data.
Cutting-edge robotics and vision systems ensure high-quality vehicle production, creating a human-centered work environment where robots assist human workers to improve efficiency.

Automation in HMGMA’s assembly shop is nearly double that of the Group’s existing facilities, featuring automated guided vehicles for logistics, Boston Dynamic’s Spot for exterior quality inspection in the weld shop, and parking robots in the assembly shop.
Oscar Kwon, CEO of HMGMA, emphasized creating a comfortable and welcoming work environment for employees (Meta Pros) alongside an energy-efficient and technologically advanced plant.
Work zones from office to production areas feature open layouts with ample daylight, indoor landscaping, and team centers with skylights in the assembly building for employee breaks and meetings.

The 3,050-foot-long front of the HMGMA building’s exterior is lit in two parallel lines inspired by a stream, outlining an area where interstate travelers can view vehicles moving from paint to general assembly.

A solar parking lot at the front of the site will generate 5.2 megawatts of energy, accounting for roughly five percent of the Metaplant’s total power consumption, installed above 1,878 parking spaces.
Construction is ongoing on a 41-acre ecological park featuring trails, running tracks, sports fields, and picnic areas in front of the Metaplant for employees.

HMGMA has deployed 21 Hyundai Motor XCIENT heavy-duty hydrogen fuel-cell electric trucks for clean logistics operations, representing more than a third of the Glovis America truck fleet at the facility.
These hydrogen trucks transport vehicle parts from suppliers across the region to the 2,906-acre Megasite where HMGMA is located on a daily basis.
Since 2022, Hyundai and Genesis have donated nearly $4.5 million to local organizations in Georgia through social responsibility initiatives and nonprofits.

Kia's Autoland Georgia factory in West Point has provided over $22 million in support of Georgia communities since 2009, including more than $6 million for educational programs like the THINC College & Career Academy and SAE’s “A World in Motion.”
HMGMA is Hyundai Motor Group’s first dedicated electrified vehicle mass-production plant located in Bryan County, GA, featuring a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system.

Hyundai Motor Group operates with about 250,000 employees worldwide and includes mobility brands Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis, alongside value chains in steel, construction, logistics, finance, IT, and service.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America Celebrates  Grand Opening, Powering U.S. Economic Growth

facts: Hyundai Motor Group celebrated the opening of its new electric vehicle (EV) factory in Georgia on Wednesday (U.S. time).
HMGMA will serve as a strategic production base to drive the company's global growth and play a key role in realizing the future of mobility.
The facility will act as a hub to strengthen economic cooperation between Korea and the United States.
Key company officials at the opening ceremony included Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chairman Euisun Chung and Hyundai Motor Co. Chief Executive Officer (CEO) Jose Munoz.
Local government officials Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp and Rep. Buddy Carter attended the opening ceremony.

Executive Chairman Euisun Chung stated, "Standing here today, I can say I have never been more confident about building the future of mobility with America, in America," and added that Hyundai has come to Georgia "to stay, to invest and to grow."
HMGMA is located on an 11.83 million-square-meter site in Ellabell, Georgia.
The plant has an annual capacity of 300,000 cars, with plans to increase production to 500,000 units per year.
HMGMA is Hyundai Motor Group's third production facility in the U.S., following Hyundai Motor's Alabama plant and Kia Corp.'s Georgia factory.
With the completion of HMGMA, Hyundai Motor Group now has a total production capacity of 1 million cars in the U.S. per year.
Last year, Hyundai and Kia sold a combined 1.7 million vehicles in the U.S.
Before the official opening, the plant had been in partial operation since late last year.
During partial operation, the plant produced two EV models: the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9.
Some Kia models will be produced at HMGMA next year.
HMGMA is designed as a software-driven factory (SDF) that utilizes data from the entire production process.
The facility incorporates the latest automation and artificial intelligence (AI) technologies.
AI analyzes production big data to detect abnormalities in advance at the plant.
Advanced robots handle high-weight, high-risk processes and inspections at HMGMA.
The opening of HMGMA came a few days after Chung announced a US$21 billion investment plan in the United States over the next three years at the White House with President Donald Trump.
The investment plan also includes a $5.8 billion steel mill in Louisiana to produce auto parts for Hyundai Motor Group's assembly plants in Georgia and Alabama.
President Trump announced a 25 percent tariff on all imported cars starting next Wednesday.
The U.S. government is set to impose country-by-country reciprocal tariffs on the same day.
Chung called for concerted efforts by the South Korean government and carmakers to seek favorable deals in upcoming tariff negotiations with Washington.

Chung told reporters, "After the announcement, the government will lead tariff negotiations, and companies also have to be engaged. That's the beginning," and added, "I know our investment plan can hardly affect Washington's tariff policies. We are just a company. It's a country-to-country issue."
source_title: Hyundai Motor opens 3rd U.S. production plant in Georgia | Yonhap News Agency

facts: Hyundai is investing $12.6 billion in Georgia for its Metaplant America facility.
The HMGMA campus spans nearly 3,000 acres, comprises 11 buildings, and covers 7.5 million square feet.
The plant is located in Savannah, Georgia, with a new hydrogen refueling station under development in nearby Pooler, Georgia.
HMGMA currently employs more than 1,600 Hyundai team members, referred to as Meta Pros.
When fully staffed, including affiliate and supplier personnel, the facility will employ 8,500 workers.
Hyundai utilizes 21 XCIENT heavy-duty hydrogen fuel-cell electric trucks for zero-emission logistics operations at the site.
A new HTWO Energy Savannah hydrogen refueling station for the XCIENT trucks is in development in Pooler, Georgia.
The plant's buildings feature rounded corners to harmonize with the surrounding natural environment.
HMGMA is highly automated and uses robots, including Boston Dynamics Spot robot dogs, to scan vehicles on the assembly line for proper part installation.
Robotic devices at the facility both assemble cars and transport parts throughout the factory.
The sound generated by the plant's machines is pumped underground, allowing workers to converse at normal volume without hearing protection.
The plant currently manufactures the IONIQ 5 and IONIQ 9 electric vehicles.
Hyundai plans to expand HMGMA production to a total of 14 models, which will include Kia and Genesis vehicles.
HMGMA initially targeted an annual output of up to 300,000 vehicles.
Hyundai recently updated the plant's target to build as many as 500,000 vehicles per year when fully operational.
Hyundai aims to build 80 percent of the electric vehicles it sells in America domestically at this facility.
Electrified vehicles now account for one-third of all Hyundai sales globally.
In 2021, Hyundai ranked fifth in overall EV sales in the United States.
In 2025, Hyundai surpassed Ford and Volkswagen to move into third place behind Tesla and GM in U.S. pure EV market share.
Hyundai is developing extended range electric vehicles (EREVs) that combine high-performance battery and motor technology with less than half the battery capacity of standard EVs.
The EREV technology promises a driving range of more than 600 miles and lower vehicle costs due to reduced battery capacity.
Every vehicle built at HMGMA undergoes testing on a roughly one-and-a-half-mile test track before shipping.
The HMGMA test course subjects each car to 16 different evaluations, including rough-road, emergency-braking, turning-radius, and acceleration tests.
Professional drivers at the facility test between 30 and 40 cars daily on the test track.
Hyundai rented a private farm near HMGMA to set up an off-road rallycross course for testing the IONIQ 5 XRT model.
source_title: I visited Hyundai's $12.6 Billion Metaplant and now I understand why Tesla should be worried

facts: Rivian Automotive is a California-based electric vehicle maker.
The company is currently money-losing.
Rivian leaders express confidence as the company starts long-delayed work on a $5 billion facility in Georgia.
Groundbreaking for the new plant occurred Tuesday, September 16, 2025, east of Atlanta.
President Donald Trump successfully pushed to roll back electric vehicle tax credits.
Starting September 30, buyers will no longer qualify for savings of up to $7,500 per car.
source_title: It’s ‘do or die’ for electric vehicle maker Rivian as it breaks ground on $5B Georgia plant – WABE

canonical_name: Rivian Manufacturing Facility (Stanton Springs North, Georgia)
facts: The move underscores Rivian’s long-term growth ambitions as it prepares to launch higher-volume vehicles and expand domestic manufacturing.
Rivian is expanding the planned capacity of its Georgia factory to 300,000 vehicles annually, a 50% increase aimed at lowering costs and scaling U.S. EV production.
Rivian confirmed that its future facility in Stanton Springs North, Georgia will now target 300,000 units annually in its first phase, up from the originally planned 200,000.
CEO RJ Scaringe said the increased capacity will improve cost efficiency and support the company’s expansion as it ramps production of next-generation models like the Rivian R2.
The company is working with the U.S. Department of Energy to update a loan package of up to $4.5 billion, designed to support construction and manufacturing.
Rivian expects to begin drawing on the loan in early 2027, with vehicle production scheduled to start in late 2028.
Construction is set to accelerate in 2026, with key infrastructure such as stamping operations already in preparation as the site transitions into vertical build-out.
The Georgia plant is expected to create thousands of jobs and play a central role in Rivian’s broader strategy to scale production while maintaining cost discipline.
Increasing initial capacity allows the company to better position itself for higher demand and future expansion phases.
Through its partnership with Uber Technologies, the automaker plans to produce up to 50,000 robotaxis at the Georgia site starting in 2028.
The expanded plant capacity reflects a shift toward higher-volume manufacturing and long-term competitiveness in the global EV market.
source_title: Rivian Boosts Georgia EV Plant Capacity to 300,000 Units | EV.com

facts: Groundbreaking for the Hyundai Metaplant site occurred in October 2022.
The project is expected to bring 8,500 direct jobs to the area along with a variety of associated businesses.
The Liberty County Development Authority is considering an alternate wastewater discharge site.
Nearly 1,000 members from Liberty and Bryan Counties have opposed a proposed state-of-the-art water reclamation facility that would discharge treated wastewater near a sandbar.
Hyundai’s wastewater now flows through a new treatment facility into the Ogeechee River.
The Ogeechee Riverkeeper continues monitoring the river that it began three years prior.
Leaders in Southeast Georgia broke ground on a $146 million project to expand a surface water treatment plant in Port Wentworth.
The expanded plant will provide up to 90 million gallons of water per day to the region’s 350,000 residents and help protect the Floridan aquifer.
Georgia’s footprint in the EV industry is expected to grow in 2026.
Hyundai and Rivian have both made significant investments in Georgia’s electric vehicle industry.
Hyundai has opened a new assembly plant in Georgia, while Rivian broke ground on a multi-billion dollar facility.
The investments include providing training and educational opportunities for veterans, technical college students, and engineering students.
Three companies, including Hyundai’s battery plant in Bryan County, were fined a total of $27,000 by OSHA for safety violations that contributed to the death of a Korean worker in March.
A federal ICE raid at the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America site in Bryan County caused financial hardship for surrounding small businesses that relied on detained Korean workers.
International Paper’s closure of its Riceboro and Savannah paper mills will free up 22 million gallons of Floridan aquifer water per day.
The Southeast continues to invest in electric vehicle manufacturing despite recent setbacks.
Georgia leads the region in new EV factory development but lags behind other states in EV adoption and funding.
Federal officials detained 475 foreign workers at Hyundai’s electric vehicle plant near Savannah during an investigation into illegal labor practices.
Over 475 people were detained in a federal operation at the Hyundai electric vehicle plant in Georgia as part of an investigation into unlawful hiring practices.
Hyundai’s annual corporate sustainability report highlights water-saving efforts, including decreased total water usage and plans to use reclaimed water for irrigation.
The sustainability report does not provide specific data on water usage at the new Metaplant in Bryan County.
Coastal Georgia high schools are beginning to offer life skills courses as students increasingly choose trade careers over college.
The Georgia Department of Transportation (DOT) is planning to widen Interstate 16 west of Savannah from two lanes to three in both directions.
The widening project will extend from the I-16/I-95 interchange west to Georgia 67.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America was fined $30,000 by Georgia regulators for improper wastewater disposal at its Bryan County EV manufacturing site.
The potential fine for the wastewater violations could have reached up to $7.15 million.
Georgia’s U.S. senators are opposing proposed cuts to the Inflation Reduction Act that threaten the state’s EV manufacturing sector.
The proposed budget cuts could lead to the loss of 42,000 jobs in Georgia’s clean energy industry.
Georgia’s clean energy industry has created 43,000 new jobs since August 2022.
A report by Coltura found that 72,000 high-mileage drivers in Georgia’s 1st Congressional District could save almost $4,000 annually by switching to an electric vehicle.
The Georgia state legislature approved a $501 million water infrastructure package for Effingham County.
The package includes a new surface water treatment plant, which will be only the second in Coastal Georgia.
Hyundai’s top executives and Georgia’s top Republicans celebrated the opening of the manufacturing plant in Ellabell.
The celebration overlooked a looming tariff threat that could postpone electric vehicle production at the site.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America held its grand opening with 16 million square feet of factory space.
At the time of the grand opening, the plant employed 1,232 people out of a prospective workforce of 8,500.
The company announced a further financial commitment by Hyundai totaling $21 billion.
Hyundai’s Metaplant America has struggled with wastewater processing and disposal since September 2024.
Permit violations forced the company to stop treating wastewater at two Coastal Georgia communities.
The company began trucking industrial wastewater to sites in Jacksonville and Goose Creek, South Carolina, among others.
Courts have temporarily protected clean energy policies and Georgia EV jobs from federal rollback efforts.
President Donald Trump’s second term has reversed environmental regulations in favor of economic growth, potentially impacting Georgia’s clean energy goals and investments.
A legal settlement will bring increased monitoring and accelerate the end of Hyundai’s use of Floridan aquifer water.
A company spokesman stated that the Inflation Reduction Act has not directly benefited the Bryan County Hyundai Metaplant yet.
The Ogeechee Riverkeeper is urging the state to reconsider permitting the use of Floridan aquifer water for industrial purposes.
Georgia has gained strength in EV manufacturing while electric vehicle sales within the state have plummeted.
State regulators issued final water withdrawal permits for the Hyundai site and its associated development.
Construction at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) began in January 2023.
HMGMA and six other affiliate plants were built quickly, resulting in workplace injuries.
Hyundai has disclosed its total water requirements for EV production but has not specified how it will utilize this public resource.
The Republican-controlled Georgia General Assembly recently voted to allow private utilities to provide water in areas lacking public service within 18 months.
OSHA has investigated 12 workplace injuries at the Hyundai site near Savannah since January 2023.
The investigations include an incident involving the Korean company’s U.S. affiliate.
source_title: Hyundai Metaplant site news archive - The Current

facts: Rivian is accelerating production at its upcoming plant in Georgia.
Production will see a 20% increase during the first year of operations to 'build out its supply chain and manufacturing footprint,' according to Rivian.
Rivian is aiming to reach 150,000 vehicles annually by bringing down the cost of its next generation of $25,000 and $35,000 electric vehicles.
Rivian is increasing its production capacity to 150,000 vehicles annually for its full-size manufacturing facility in Georgia.
The 150,000 vehicle annual capacity represents a 20% increase over the initially planned capacity of 125,000 units.
The increased capacity is intended to facilitate a lower cost vehicle in later years.
Rivian and Gov. Brian Kemp are accelerating plans for a new plant at the Chattahoochee Hills site between Columbus and Atlanta.
Gov. Brian Kemp said they are excited to work with the Georgia Department of Economic Development to grow their manufacturing footprint in Georgia.
Kemp stated the project directly expands workforce opportunities and will create thousands of jobs in addition to Rivian’s growth as a global leader in innovation and technology.
Rivian anticipates construction to begin next year after the agreement was first announced by Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe in 2024.
The State of Georgia brokered the deal for Rivian to build a $15 billion plant in the Chattahoochee Hills, which included more than 50,000 jobs in two phases.
source_title: Rivian Announces Big Production Boost of EVs At Coming Georgia Plant | News | morgancountycitizen.com

facts: The fifth annual Electric Mobility Summit was held on April 14 and 15 in Athens at the University of Georgia Center for Continuing Education & Hotel.
More than 250 representatives from industry, government, and academia attended the fifth annual Electric Mobility Summit.
Fort Valley State University hosted a statewide electric vehicle awareness conference in partnership with the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility as a key workforce development initiative.
The Fort Valley State University electric vehicle awareness conference took place on September 25, 2025, in Fort Valley, GA.

More than 200 participants attended the Fort Valley State University electric vehicle awareness conference, including students, local officials, educators, industry leaders, and middle school students.
The inaugural Battery Belt Charging Forward Forum was held on Tuesday, August 26, in Atlanta, GA.
The Georgia Network for Electric Mobility (GNEM) hosted the inaugural Battery Belt Charging Forward Forum at the University of Georgia.
Guest experts from the American EV Jobs Alliance, Kia Georgia, MAGNET, and the Salata Institute for Climate and Sustainability attended the Battery Belt Charging Forward Forum.
Area governors, power company executives, and university presidents established the Mississippi-Alabama-Georgia Network for Evolving Transportation (MAGNET).
MAGNET is a regional economic development initiative aimed at leveraging the Southeastern U.S. automotive vehicle and battery industry to stimulate economic development and job growth.
The University of Georgia and Kia Georgia formed a partnership to advance Georgia's workforce development initiatives and overall economic growth.
The Georgia Automotive Manufacturers Association (GAMA) joined the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility (GNEM) as its first non-profit/start-up partner and member.
UGA has a corporate partnership with Georgia Power, which was highlighted in a spotlight video.
Kia committed a $1 million gift to the UGA College of Engineering and the Georgia Network for Electric Mobility to advance electric mobility in Georgia.

Cox Automotive provided a significant gift to the University of Georgia to help spearhead efforts to make electric mobility accessible to all Georgians and establish the state as a global leader in electric mobility innovation.
Georgia Power Company committed a $5 million gift to the UGA College of Engineering to help the university aim to be a leader in e-mobility.
source_title: Blog - Georgia Network for Electric Mobility

facts: The Kia Georgia assembly plant celebrated a 'triple milestone' marking the rollout of the first 2027 Kia Telluride.
The rolled-out vehicle is the 5 millionth vehicle produced at the West Point site.
The rollout marks the official start of production for the redesigned 2027 model year.
The 2027 Telluride is the first hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) ever assembled in the state of Georgia.
The introduction of the 2027 Telluride HEV marks a major shift in Kia’s domestic manufacturing strategy from internal combustion engine production to its first U.S.-assembled hybrid.

Sean Yoon, President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America, stated that the plant is the only one in the world that builds the Telluride and highlighted Kia's commitment to building the future of mobility in Georgia.
Reaching the 5-million-unit mark while debuting the state’s first HEV reflects Kia’s trajectory toward a diversified, electrified lineup.
Since its inception in 2009, Kia Georgia has grown into a cornerstone of the Southeastern manufacturing economy.
The Telluride has been exclusively manufactured at the West Point site since 2019.
Governor Brian P. Kemp attended the milestone ceremony and highlighted the public-private partnership sustaining growth between Kia Georgia, the local community, and the state.
Stuart Countess, President and CEO of Kia Georgia, noted that the plant’s ability to pivot between gasoline-powered vehicles, EVs, and HEVs is its greatest competitive advantage.
The West Point facility is one of the largest employers in the Southeast automotive sector.
Total production at the site reached 5,000,000 units from 2009 to present.
The 2027 Telluride will begin its rollout to dealerships across North America.
source_title: Kia Georgia Hits Triple Milestone: 5 Millionth Vehicle and First GA-Made Hybrid

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Mobis plans to invest $926 million in an Electric Vehicle (EV) Power Electric system plant in Bryan County.
The new plant will eventually employ at least 1,500 people.
Hyundai Mobis currently operates a manufacturing facility with almost 1,200 employees in West Point since 2009.
The existing West Point facility is the largest Tier 1 supplier to Kia Georgia.
The existing facility supplies automotive manufacturers with complete cockpit modules, chassis modules, and bumper assemblies.
H.S. Oh, Vice President of Hyundai Mobis Electric Powertrain Business Unit, stated the investment reflects an acceleration in the development of the EV supply chain in Georgia’s auto industry.
The new facility will be 1,200,000 square feet.

At full production, the facility will supply over 900,000 EV Power Electric systems and 450,000 Integrated Charging Control Units annually to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County, Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama in Montgomery, and the Kia Georgia plant.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin as early as January 2023.
Production at the new facility is expected to begin in 2024.
The facility will be located at the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.
Jon Seagraves, Chairman of the Development Authority of Bryan County, noted the site's proximity to available housing in Heartwood, a top-performing public school system, interstate access, and rail.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project is being developed in partnership with the Development Authority of Bryan County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Power.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that in May they predicted non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers would invest an additional $1 billion into Georgia, a prediction that was blown past in about two weeks.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have contributed over $13.3 billion in investment and announced more than 18,100 jobs in Georgia.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in Bryan County.
Offsite suppliers connected to the HMGMA project are predicted to invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around the state.
source_title: Second Global Automotive Supplier for Hyundai Metaplant To Create 1,500 Jobs | Development Authority of Bryan County

facts: Kia Motors Corp., South Korea's second-largest automaker, announced it will set up its first U.S. plant in the southern state of Georgia.
Production at the new facility will begin in 2009.
The factory represents a $1.2 billion investment and will employ 2,500 workers, according to Kia's press release.
Georgia Gov. Sonny Perdue and Kia President and CEO Euisun Chung made the formal announcement at a ceremony in Seoul.
Locations in Mississippi and Tennessee had also contended for the plant.
The facility will be located in West Point, Georgia, near the Alabama border.
The West Point plant is expected to produce 300,000 vehicles per year at maximum capacity.
Five to six auto suppliers are expected to set up nearby the new plant.
The nearby supplier presence is expected to create an additional 2,000 jobs.
Kia manufactures the Optima and Amanti midsize sedans, the small-size Picanto, and the Sorento midsize SUV.
Kia is an affiliate of South Korea's largest carmaker, Hyundai Motor Co., which operates a factory in Montgomery, Alabama, 120 kilometers (75 miles) away.
Proximity to the Hyundai site combined with a $258 million incentive package from the state helped sway Kia's decision, according to Georgia state officials.
Georgia has previously lost plants to Ford Motor Co. and General Motors Corp., largely due to increasing competition from Asian automakers.
Gov. Sonny Perdue stated that Kia's decision "is a testament to the tools, experience and know-how Georgia will deliver to one of the automotive industry's leading innovators."
Kia exported three-fourths of its South Korean production last year and is pursuing an aggressive overseas expansion.
Besides the U.S. plant, Kia is building a second plant in China.
Kia expects its first European factory to start up in Slovakia at the end of the year-end.
The company supplies materials to partners for vehicle assembly in Malaysia, Indonesia, Vietnam, Russia, and Iran.
Kia President and CEO Euisun Chung stated, "Kia Motors has entered an aggressive growth phase in the U.S."
Kia expects its sales in the United States and Canada to increase 15 percent to 350,000 vehicles in 2006.
Kia projects its U.S. and Canadian sales will grow further to 800,000 by 2010.
Kia Motors America posted record sales of 20,719 vehicles for February.
Kia's February sales pace was 3.2 percent ahead of its record-setting 2005 sales pace.
In January, Kia reported its 2005 net profit rose 2.9 percent to 680.9 billion won ($696.5 million).
Kia's 2005 revenue gained 4.6 percent to 16 trillion won ($16.4 billion).
Kia sold 1.27 million vehicles worldwide in 2005, which was 13.9 percent more than the previous year.
Kia has invested more than $300 million in the United States over the last four years.
Kia's U.S. facilities include a research and development center in Ann Arbor, Michigan.
Kia operates vehicle proving grounds in Mojave, California.
Kia shares what it calls the largest automotive design studio in California with Hyundai Motor America.
The company is currently constructing a $87 million U.S. corporate campus in Irvine, California.
The Irvine corporate campus is expected to open in December 2006 and will include a design center.
source_title: Kia Motors to set up first U.S. plant

facts: SK Battery America Inc. laid off 958 workers, representing roughly 37 percent of its workforce, at its manufacturing plant northeast of Atlanta on Friday, March 7, 2026.
The layoffs mark one of the most significant single-day job losses in Georgia's electric vehicle industry since the sector began its rapid expansion in the state.
Affected employees' last working day was Friday, and they will continue to receive pay through May 6.
Approximately 1,600 workers remain at the Commerce plant following the layoffs.
The plant opened in January 2022 following a $2.6 billion investment by SK Battery America Inc.
The facility served as a flagship example of SK Battery's U.S. ambitions and supplied lithium-ion battery cells for Ford's F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck.
SK Battery America cited automakers' shifting electrification timelines and uncertain consumer demand as the primary drivers behind the layoff decision.
Federal policy changes under the Trump administration eliminated the $7,500 federal EV tax credit and rolled back emissions standards.
These policy changes removed two of the most powerful market incentives that had previously fueled EV demand growth.

EV purchases in the United States accounted for roughly 8 percent of new vehicle sales in 2025, remaining flat compared to the prior year and falling short of projections used to justify multi-billion-dollar battery investments.
In December 2025, Ford announced it would cancel the fully electric version of the F-150 Lightning.
In the same month, SK Battery America Inc. and Ford ended their joint battery manufacturing venture, which had involved a combined pledge of $11.4 billion across multiple U.S. battery plants.
Ford's step back from the all-electric segment removed a core pillar of production rationale for the Georgia plant.

Law firm Strauss Borrelli announced it was investigating whether SK Battery violated the federal WARN Act, which mandates at least 60 days' written notification before major layoffs or plant closures.
SK's spokesperson maintained that the company's actions comply with WARN guidelines, though the legal investigation remains ongoing.
U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff placed direct blame on the White House, arguing that the Trump administration's stance on electric vehicles is damaging Georgia's economy.
Senator Raphael Warnock expressed concern for the affected workers and their families.
Governor Brian Kemp's office highlighted more than 219,700 new jobs announced in Georgia since 2019 as evidence of the state's overall economic resilience.

SK Battery America Inc. stated it remains committed to its Georgia operations and is actively pursuing customers in the Battery Energy Storage System market for data centers and power grid backup applications.
Demand in the Battery Energy Storage System sector continues to grow independently of passenger vehicle trends.
A second Georgia battery plant contracted to supply Hyundai Motor is scheduled to begin production in the first half of 2026.
A third SK facility located in Tennessee is expected to come online in 2028.
Both upcoming plants reflect SK's ongoing commitment to the long-term trajectory of EV adoption despite an uncertain near-term outlook.
Georgia benefited significantly from the Biden administration's Inflation Reduction Act, with green energy projects topping $20 billion in announced investments and pledging more than 25,000 jobs.
The Commerce plant was among the most prominent of these projects, symbolizing advocates' vision of Georgia emerging as a hub for clean energy manufacturing.
source_title: Nearly 1,000 Jobs Cut at SK Battery's Georgia Plant as EV Market Headwinds Intensify - Georgia Asian Times

facts: Bryan and Bulloch counties are seeking permits to withdraw 6.625 million gallons of water a day from the Floridan aquifer to support the Hyundai EV manufacturing plant.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is the legal entity contracted with the state of Georgia to receive $2.1 billion in tax deferments, subsidized construction costs, and other perks.
Consultants hired to secure permits described Hyundai's water usage data as a commercial secret for six months.

Thomas & Hutton Vice President Trent Thompson stated they are not in possession of the breakdown of needs for Hyundai’s various uses including fire protection, EV production, bathrooms, cafeteria, battery assembly, and landscaping.
HMGMA communications head Joe LaMuraglia stated that water use data is proprietary and impossible to calculate exactly by dividing usage by vehicles produced.
Local residents in Bryan and Bulloch County are mobilizing to oppose the water permits due to fears of adverse effects on farm wells.
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers has announced it is reevaluating the impact of the water permits.
Retired metallurgical engineer Ken Copi expressed concern about knowing what the water will be used for and requested mitigation or reduction methods.
An informational meeting regarding the water permits was held by the Georgia Environmental Protection Division at Southeastern Bulloch High School.
Construction of the Hyundai EV plant began in January 2023 on almost 3,000 acres of clear-cut land just south of I-16.
Hyundai officials aim to bring their first electric vehicle off the assembly line by the end of 2024 to qualify for Biden Administration’s 2024 Inflation Reduction Act buyer incentives.
Hyundai initially requested 4 million gallons a day for the Ellabell site in August 2023, which surfaced in public documents submitted to EPD.

Officials at the Georgia Department of Economic Development and the Savannah Harbor Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA) knew of the water request before finalizing the agreement in July 2022 but did not push back.
Savannah Economic Development Authority president and CEO Trip Tollison described his organization's role as a middleman between Bryan County and EPD regarding water volumes.
In June, Thomas & Hutton revised the justification of need for water usage, citing car manufacturer needs as "industry trade secrets."
The justification document submitted to EPD fails to connect requested water volume to Hyundai’s manufacturing processes at the Ellabell site.
The document states that without the wells, Bryan County would not meet commitments to provide water capacity for upcoming manufacturing facilities and regional growth would stall.
Four wells will be drilled in Bulloch County, just over the county line from Bryan County.

Bulloch County was chosen because it is the only one of four counties whose development authorities make up the JDA that is not under state-imposed water withdrawal restrictions to protect the aquifer and avoid a lawsuit with South Carolina regarding saltwater intrusion in Hilton Head wells.
Development authority officials explained that water needs were for the manufacturing plant plus anticipated industrial, commercial, and residential projects near the site.
Surface water from the Savannah River was rejected as not cost-effective compared to Bulloch well water.
Drilling wells in Effingham County was also rejected due to cost effectiveness.
Trip Tollison stated in July that a surface water alternative would have cost $362 million more than Bulloch well water and taken 10 years longer to implement.
Consultant Trent Thompson told the JDA in August that the cost for local counties for a surface water option would be upwards of $1 billion.

Tollison expressed confidence that Bulloch water wells would not negatively impact area residents or farmers, stating they would never pursue a project where groundwater or drinking water would be sacrificed.
Hyundai has not been required to explain why it needs the right to use the equivalent of six Olympic swimming pools full of water a day in public documents.
The nine-page justification document originally submitted in August 2023 and revised in June 2024 lists "N/A" for unit loadings for the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America/Battery JV.

Hyundai listed various water uses including concrete during construction, office domestic water, cafeteria operations, utility systems, boilers, chillers, HVAC systems, car body leak testing, coolant, paint shop equipment, fire suppression, plant cleaning, and general manufacturing operational use.
Hyundai's 2023 sustainability report disclosed an average water use of 714 gallons per vehicle across its global manufacturing lines.
Tesla reports using 655 gallons per vehicle for EV assembly.
Georgia Tech Professor Bert Bras stated that electric vehicle assembly generally uses less water than gas-powered cars due to less part cleaning required for engines and gearboxes.
Hyundai plans to produce 300,000 vehicles a year at the Ellabell site.
Based on Hyundai's global average, producing 300,000 cars would equate to approximately 0.587 million gallons per day.
The EPA’s Lean Water Toolkit estimates industrial workers use 10–25 gallons of water per shift, with higher estimates needed for facilities with toilets, showers, and kitchens.
The Hyundai site in Bryan County is projected to hire 8,500 workers.
Using the EPA's higher-end metric, the workforce alone would require an additional 0.213 million gallons a day.
LG Energy Solution will be making batteries for the Hyundai cars assembled in Ellabell but has not disclosed its water needs despite multiple requests.
LG Energy Solution stated they are cooperating with the county regarding water usage at their joint venture with Hyundai Motor Group.
LG's 2023 sustainability report states it evaluates water risks before establishing facilities and avoids using groundwater, obtaining processed water from approved suppliers instead.
All water expected to be piped to the Ellabell plant will come from groundwater in the Floridan aquifer.
Researchers estimate mining EV battery materials takes 500,000 to 2 million gallons of water to extract a ton of lithium.
The LG battery plant is projected to have an annual production capacity of 30 gigawatt hours, equal to about 450,000 vehicle batteries.
An SK Innovation battery site in Stanton, Tenn., is predicted to require 1.2 million gallons of water per day for a 45 GWh annual capacity.
Scaled to LG's Ellabell production, the SK Innovation rate would mean a 0.8 million gallon per day requirement at the Ellabell site.
SK Battery America in Commerce requested 3.6 million gallons per day for its EV battery factory with a 22 GWh capacity.
Scaling SK Battery's usage to LG's Ellabell production equates to 4.9 million gallons a day, which is more than Hyundai has requested for the whole site.
Irrigation at the megasite will require a portion of the 4 million gallons a day until the wastewater treatment plant is operational.
Trent Thompson told the JDA board in August that once operational, reclaimed water will be sent back to Hyundai for use.
source_title: Hyundai’s water use: What does it take to build an EV? - The Current

facts: A Dallas Fed blog post discussed a pivot by US battery manufacturers from EV batteries to power storage systems due to sagging EV sales in the US.
SK Battery America is laying off nearly 1,000 employees, or 37% of its workforce, at its plant outside of Atlanta.
SK Battery America stated it is pursuing a range of future customers, including the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
source_title: SK Battery Georgia Layoff

canonical_name: HL-GA Battery Facility (Joint Venture between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution)
facts: The Hyundai-LG joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution is developing a $7.6bn ($8.1bn) battery plant in Ellabell, rural Georgia.
On September 4, the site was targeted by the largest immigration enforcement operation in American history, resulting in 475 individuals being detained.
More than 100 of the 317 South Korean nationals detained had their B-1 visas reinstated without requiring reapplication by mid-November.
Approximately 50 detained South Korean engineers had re-entered the United States by mid-November to resume work at the facility.
Construction at the HL-GA Battery facility was indefinitely suspended following the raid but has now resumed in a phased manner as engineers return.
LG Energy Solution confirmed that construction and business travel have resumed, with plans to gradually expand business travel to HL-GA and other US production facilities.

When complete, the battery plant will supply cells for electric vehicles produced at Hyundai's adjacent Metaplant America assembly complex, which remains operational and unaffected by the enforcement action.

Nearly 200 of the detained South Korean nationals are preparing to file a lawsuit against US Immigration and Customs Enforcement alleging unlawful detention, racial profiling, human rights violations, and excessive force.

One engineer identified only as Mr Kim testified that workers' phones were confiscated and they were shackled around their wrists, ankles, and chest before being transported to a detention facility in Folkston, Georgia.
Some detained workers tripped and fell while being loaded onto buses due to the restraints, rendering them unable to walk properly.
The detained engineers spent seven days in detention before South Korea's government negotiated their release and arranged charter flights home.
Attorneys for the plaintiffs argue that the restoration of B-1 visas without reapplication demonstrates the workers were lawfully present, contradicting the basis for their detention.
LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Engineering have stated they are not involved in the lawsuit, describing it as being pursued on an individual basis by the workers.
The legal proceedings are likely to stretch well into 2026, with plaintiffs seeking undisclosed financial compensation for damages.
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung raised the incident directly with US President Donald Trump during bilateral meetings in October, describing it as causing severe trauma among workers.

President Lee warned reporters that some workers no longer wish to return to the United States and cautioned that without safety measures, factory construction projects across America could face disruptions.

President Trump acknowledged the complexity of the situation, noting foreign companies building advanced manufacturing facilities in the US often need to bring in experts during initial phases, and suggested his administration would work to resolve visa issues while maintaining enforcement priorities.
Ahead of the meeting with Trump, President Lee told Bloomberg News he expected a solution "in the not-too-distant future," though specifics remain vague.
According to reports from The Washington Post, several South Korean firms have pulled back or prolonged pauses on US investment projects following the Hyundai raid.
Three individuals representing South Korean and East Asian firms in America confirmed that companies remain cautious despite reassurances from the Trump administration.

The detained engineers were specifically brought to Georgia to install highly technical production equipment and train American personnel in battery cell manufacturing processes that remain scarce domestically.
Construction halted and training stopped following the raid, leaving US workers waiting to assume those roles sidelined.
Hyundai has since resumed limited international travel for technical teams, but a chilling effect on foreign expertise recruitment persists.
Federal immigration authorities insisted the enforcement action was lawful and necessary to protect American labour standards.

Steve Schrank, special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations for Georgia and Alabama, stated that individuals entering on short-term or recreational visas are prohibited from engaging in employment.
Governor Brian Kemp of Georgia called for changes to the visa system to prevent similar disruptions, arguing that clarity and consistency are essential to maintaining investor confidence.
White House border adviser Tom Homan promised more worksite enforcement operations following the Hyundai-LG raid.
Hyundai Motor Group has pledged $26bn in American investments through 2028, with much of that capital directed towards vehicle production, battery manufacturing, and supply chain localization.
The Hyundai-LG Georgia plant is now scheduled to become operational in the first half of 2026, a timeline that assumes continued access to foreign technical expertise during the ramp-up phase.
Battery cell production requires precision engineering, stringent contamination controls, and process knowledge accumulated over decades, much of it concentrated in South Korea, China, and Japan.
source_title: Hyundai-LG's Georgia battery plant: Workers return amid legal battles and diplomatic tensions

facts: ICE conducted an immigration raid on Hyundai’s Metaplant in Georgia on a Friday.
The raid is part of a series of high-profile federal government actions over the past few years regarding Hyundai's labor practices.
One of Hyundai's suppliers was involved in a child labor scandal in late 2022.
DHS's ICE carried out a large-scale raid on Hyundai’s new 2,900-acre Metaplant site in Ellabell, Georgia, just outside Savannah.
CNN notes a statement from the Homeland Security Investigations special agent in charge calling this action “the largest single-site enforcement operation” in the agency’s history.
Federal officials apprehended approximately 475 people during the sweep of the plant.
Most of the detained individuals are South Korean nationals.

ICE spokesperson Lindsay Williams said the raid largely focused on Hyundai’s new battery plant, which is under construction as part of a partnership with LG (officially called “HL-GA Battery Company”).
The agency states the raid occurred “part of an ongoing criminal investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices and other serious federal crimes.”

HSI agent Steven Shrank told media that today’s action was part of a “multi-month criminal investigation where we have developed evidence and conducted interviews, gathered documents and presented that evidence to the court in ode to obtain a judicial search warrant.”
Hyundai spokesperson Michael Stewart told media that the automaker is cooperating fully with agents, and that those detained were not direct employees of the company.
Direct quote from Stewart: “As of today, it is our understanding that none of those detained is directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company.”
Stewart added: “We prioritize the safety and well-being of everyone working at the site and comply with all laws and regulations wherever we operate.”
LG has not made an official comment on the raid.
ICE did not yet comment on whether the 475 individuals were contractors, subcontractors, or actually worked for either partner involved in the Metaplant’s battery site construction.
Hyundai stressed that the ICE operation did not impact its manufacturing plant’s production.
The Metaplant started building Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 SUVs in March, as part of Hyundai’s $7.6 billion manufacturing footprint in Georgia.
The South Korean government expressed “concern and regret” over the ICE operation affecting its nationals.
South Korea’s foreign minister, Lee Jaewoong, said it is dispatching Washington, DC-based diplomats at the embassy to Atlanta, with plans to form an on-site response team.
Lee Jaewoong stated: “The business activities of our investors and the rights of our nationals must not be unjustly infringed in the process of US law enforcement.”
HSI agent Shrank said most of the 475 detainees were taken to a detention center in Folkston, Georgia.
The Folkston detention center is close to the Florida state border and 2 hours south of Ellabell.
source_title: ICE Detains 475 Workers at Hyundai's Georgia Metaplant - TFLcar

facts: U.S. immigration enforcement authorities conducted a crackdown at the Hyundai Motor Group-LG Energy Solution joint battery plant (HL-GA Battery Company) in Georgia.
More than 300 people, including those from partner companies, were detained as a result of the crackdown.
Kim Gi-soo, executive director and chief human resources officer (CHO) of LG Energy Solution, departed for the United States on September 7.
Kim Gi-soo stated at Incheon International Airport that the top priority is the swift early release of all LG Energy Solution employees and employees of partner companies.
Kim noted that since the government is putting in all efforts to respond, LG Energy Solution will do its best for everyone's safe and quick return.
Kim refrained from mentioning details about U.S. response plans or the status of detained employees.

On September 4 (local time), U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and the Department of Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) launched a massive crackdown on undocumented immigrants at the construction site of the joint battery plant in Savannah, Georgia.
47 individuals affiliated with LG Energy Solution were detained, consisting of 46 South Korean nationals and 1 Indonesian national.
More than 250 personnel from partner companies related to the HL-GA Battery Company were detained.
Diplomatic authorities confirmed that over 300 South Koreans were detained.
It was determined that there were no detained employees from Hyundai.
LG Energy Solution stated in a press release that it is identifying regular medications for the detainees through an emergency contact network.
LG Energy Solution plans to request that necessary medications be delivered to the detainees.
LG Energy Solution is coordinating closely with the Korean government and relevant authorities to facilitate visits with the detainees.
LG Energy Solution is requesting that communication and contact be possible for the detainees.
source_title: LG Energy Solution HR Chief prioritizes swift release of detainees in Georgia - CHOSUNBIZ

facts: South Korean car parts manufacturer Hyundai Mobis has secured $940m in loans to build new battery system assembly and power electric system factories in the US.
The facilities will be built in four different locations, including in Alabama and Georgia, in response to the growing demand from the global electrification market.
The company stated that with this funding, it is anticipated that the electrification projects in North America will progress as planned.
Hyundai Mobis currently has production hubs in nine locations globally (six in South Korea and three overseas).
Last year, in October, Hyundai Mobis allocated $1.3bn to create new electrification production hubs in North America by the next decade.
US Congress passed the Inflation Reduction Act in August 2022, which has benefited several US states with multiple investment plans coming from abroad.

On the anniversary of the act, US President Joe Biden said the law had managed to create an estimated 170,000 clean energy jobs, with estimates that the number will reach approximately 1.5 million over the next decade.
The CEO of US Steel called the Inflation Reduction Act the “Manufacturing Renaissance Act” because it’s bringing jobs back to America, manufacturing here in America. Made in America.
On 1 November, Toyota said it would spend $8bn to expand its battery plant in North Carolina amid continuous US economic growth.
Nissan launched plans in June to invest $60m in a new 33,000m² facility in Bekasi, Indonesia to supply battery systems for flagship electric vehicle models.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis secures $940m in loans for US battery hub - Investment Monitor

facts: Hyundai Mobis in Montgomery is planning to build an EV Battery Module plant, creating 400 jobs.
According to Hyundai Mobis, the company plans to invest $205 million in a full-production, 450,000-square-foot facility.
The facility will be able to supply over 200,000 EV batteries annually to the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama factories in Montgomery and the Kia Georgia Plant.
The EV battery module plant will expand Hyundai Mobis’ already large presence in Montgomery.

Governor Kay Ivey stated that Alabama’s auto manufacturing sector is evolving rapidly to capitalize on the EV revolution that is sweeping the industry, and this new Hyundai Mobis battery plant represents another milestone in that transition.
Governor Kay Ivey expressed excitement about the company’s new investment and what it represents for the next chapter of automaking in Alabama.
Hyundai Mobis has operated a manufacturing facility on 83 acres in Montgomery since 2002 as the largest Tier 1 supplier to HMAA and Kia Georgia.
The existing plant supplies auto plants with complete cockpit modules, front and rear chassis modules, bumper assemblies, and instrument panels.
Montgomery Mayor Steven Reed stated that Montgomery and Hyundai Mobis have a powerful partnership.
Mayor Steven Reed expressed looking forward to continuing to strengthen this relationship in the coming years and working to support this tremendous investment.
Mayor Steven Reed noted that this is a continuation of the biggest year for economic development in the history of Montgomery.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin as early as December.
Production of EV battery assemblies is projected to begin in 2024.
source_title: Hyundai Mobis plans EV battery module plant in Montgomery, creating 400 jobs

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp announced Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution will invest an additional $2 billion in their battery cell manufacturing joint venture.
The additional investment brings the total investment to more than $4.3 billion.
The move will add another 400 jobs, bringing the total to 8,500 for the region.

José Muñoz, president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, stated the cars produced at the mega plant will be 100% electric vehicles.

Muñoz explained the decision to invest an additional $2 billion was driven by the need for more batteries and a next generation of batteries that offer more range, lower weight, and higher technology.
In May, Hyundai and LG Energy Solution announced a partnership in the $4.3 billion plant that will produce 300,000 batteries a year.
The facility is expected to open in late 2025.
The battery cell manufacturing facility opening is scheduled just months after Hyundai's EV assembly factory is expected to come online.

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp stated that in a single year they broke ground on the largest project in state history, landed multiple suppliers across the state for Hyundai's Metaplant, and welcomed LGES to Bryan County.
Kemp stated the goal is to continue making Georgia the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Kemp noted these major investments ultimately go to hardworking Georgians in the form of paychecks, improved schools and infrastructure and more.
The battery materials produced by the new facility will be assembled into battery packs by Hyundai Mobis.
Hyundai Mobis is building its own facility in Richmond Hill.
Hyundai Mobis will distribute the finished product to Hyundai Motor Group manufacturing facilities throughout the U.S. for Genesis EV models, Hyundai and Kia.
Carter Infinger, chairman of the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority, stated the announcement will bring well-paying jobs to those in the Savannah region.
Infinger noted the continued investment by Hyundai Motor Group and announced suppliers like LG Energy Solution is truly remarkable and will be transformative for the region.
Since 2018, Georgia's job creation has led to more than $25 billion in investments and more than 30,000 jobs.
The article originally appeared on Savannah Morning News.
source_title: 'Transformative': Another $2 billion investment in Hyundai metaplant to add 400 more jobs - AOL

entity_name: Hyundai MOBIS Alabama, LLC - Georgia Plant
canonical_name: Hyundai MOBIS Alabama, LLC - Georgia Plant
facts: Hyundai MOBIS is one of the largest leading auto parts specialist companies in the world.
Hyundai MOBIS forms the parts and service arm for South Korean automaker Kia Motors Georgia.
The Hyundai MOBIS Georgia Plant uses cutting-edge vehicle modularization technology to manufacture and supply chassis modules, cockpit modules, and front-end modules to the KIA Georgia factory.
Team members at the plant assemble front and rear bumpers in a just-in-sequence manufacturing environment.
Hyundai MOBIS is growing quickly and actively seeking professionals to join its team.
A Quality Control Specialist role was posted two weeks ago, reporting to the Quality Senior Manager.
The role supports day-to-day tactical objectives aligned with SQDCM initiatives covering safety, quality, delivery, cost, and morale.
The company maintains a culture committed to ethical practices, legal compliance, and a union-free environment.
Employees are expected to take initiative in training and development opportunities to prepare for current and future challenges.
Responsibilities include supporting the execution of quality management systems, procedures, and policies using metrology, statistical process control, and physical inspection.
Staff conduct application and analysis testing and inspection of parts and processes to ensure standards are met and use findings to develop, operate, and improve quality control systems.
Employees advise management on all aspects of quality control.
Workers identify process improvement opportunities within the MOBIS supply chain for chassis suppliers and support operations and engineering accordingly.
