Staff work with suppliers to develop corrective action plans to correct process failings and drive improvements in quality and productivity.
Employees assist in supplier qualification endeavors, including on-site audits and evaluations.
The role requires developing and implementing systems to ensure products meet or exceed customer requirements and expectations.
Staff participate in the change management process to assure products continue to meet customer requirements.
Employees participate on cross-functional teams with other departments, business units, and engineering disciplines.
Workers establish the principles of product quality evaluation and control for the system.
Staff provide independent oversight and review of deliverables throughout the lifecycle of each program.
Employees assist management with the development of quality programs and revisions.
Staff assist in defining and developing quality program status indicators, performance measurements, and reporting requirements.
Workers assist with implementing inspection plans including FTTQ, in-process inspection, final inspection, sampling, and inspection acceptance criteria.
Employees perform internal audits to assess compliance to standards and the internal quality system, including investigation, presentation of observations and findings, and reporting.
Staff apply problem-solving methodologies in identifying, prioritizing, communicating, and resolving all quality issues.
Workers assist customers in problem-solving exercises through recreation of issues and rework of parts and modules for chassis lines and engines.
Employees work with the customer on engine repairs and tabulation by recording incoming information and relaying it to applicable parties.
Staff work with the engine supplier and associated suppliers on routine visits to help catalogue, repair, and process engine stock.
Workers document ramifications from in-process quality issues such as downtime and OS&D and charge back costs to responsible parties.
The position requires a Bachelor’s Degree (BA/BS) in Mechanical Engineering, Electrical Engineering, or a related field.
Candidates need 0-3 years of experience, or an equivalent of 3+ years of experience in Quality within the automotive or manufacturing industry.
Intermediate communication and mathematical skills are required.
PC literacy including Microsoft Office products is required.
The seniority level is Entry level, employment type is Full-time, job function is Quality Assurance, and the industry is Motor Vehicle Manufacturing.
Referrals increase the chances of interviewing at Hyundai MOBIS Alabama, LLC - Georgia Plant by 2x.
Recruitment contact is handled by the Hyundai MOBIS Alabama, LLC - Georgia Plant Recruiting Team.
source_title: Quality Control Specialist in West Point at Hyundai MOBIS Alabama, LLC - Georgia Plant | Apply now! – StudySmarter – Talents

facts: Hyundai Glovis has opened a logistics center and an integrated warehouse in California and Georgia to sharpen its ability to navigate supply chain risks across North America.
The Los Angeles logistics center spans roughly 12,000 square meters (129,000 square feet) and sits about 25 minutes by car from both the Port of Long Beach and the airport.
Glovis plans to integrate transloading, airfreight, and warehousing and distribution services at the Los Angeles hub while strengthening its own inland transport capabilities.

Hyundai Glovis stated that the Los Angeles center will serve as a key hub for integrated logistics across the western United States to enhance responsiveness to urgent shipments and enable more flexible cost management amid market volatility.
Hyundai Glovis has set up a large-scale integrated warehouse in Savannah, Georgia, covering about 69,000 square meters.
The Savannah warehouse is aimed at handling rising volumes tied to Hyundai Motor Group’s Metaplant America, a massive EV plant, as well as demand from third-party clients in the region.
Savannah has emerged as a logistics nexus in the U.S. Southeastern areas thanks to its port and rapidly expanding manufacturing plants nearby.
Demand for logistics services continues to grow in Georgia as supply chains link raw material imports to finished goods exports.
Glovis stated that the close integration of port and inland transport networks positions Savannah as a strategic springboard for broader expansion across North America.
source_title: Hyundai Glovis opens LA logistics center, integrated warehouse in Georgia

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is located in Chatham County, Ga.
Many major companies around the city and world have partnered with Hyundai for the upcoming Metaplant.
St. Joseph’s/Candler’s St. Mary’s Community Center is stepping up to help fill the need for local hires.

Khailiah Robinson, Workforce Developer at St. Mary's Community Center, stated: “We’re strategically placed here for a reason... A lot of the members of our community don’t have a lot of transportation options. So, I thought it would be great to bring the employers to the community.”
The company had two departments that were interviewing candidates at the event.
Hyundai Glovis is a partner with Hyundai and is a logistics company looking to fill management positions.

Kyle Horton, Hyundai Glovis Transportation Department Manager, stated: “We’re providing job opportunities, a large number of job opportunities in the area... So, the benefit of us being here in the aspect of potential employment opportunities for qualified candidates and what we’re going to put back into the community and cities and the state as a whole.”
Robinson added that it’s important to bring large and small businesses, organizations, and companies to the area to hire locally.

Khailiah Robinson noted: “Especially with an event like this, with Hyundai, a lot of our clients don’t know how to start to apply or feel like they’re qualified... So, this way it brings them into the community that way people can come in, ask questions, meet them face to face, and that way they can get a good view of what the local workforce really looks like.”
With thousands of jobs created by the new Hyundai Meta Plant, the company’s staff say they plan to create numerous opportunities to develop strong employees.

Tony Sutton with Hyundai Transys human resources stated: “One thing that Hyundai Transys is doing... is becoming the preferred employer by developing within the region... It’s very important for us to hire and we have to do it now, we can’t wait.”
St. Joseph’s/Candler’s Hospital also announced a partnership with Hyundai to bring on-site medical centers to Hyundai’s Metaplant.
Event organizers say the job fair was a success with over 50 residents in Savannah and surrounding areas showing up.

Tony Sutton stated: "We want to give everyone in Savannah a seat at the table... We don’t want to leave anyone out when it comes down to these opportunities. So it’s important for us to come into these communities and engage with the local community."
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America continuing to host job fairs to hire locally

facts: Hyundai Motor Group has unveiled the product version of its Atlas Robot, developed with Boston Dynamics.
The robot will be used at Hyundai Motor’s EV metaplant in the US state of Georgia from 2028.
The robot has applications for material sequencing, assembly and machine tending.
From 2028, Hyundai will begin deploying Atlas robots throughout its business.

Hyundai said the humanoid robot laid the groundwork for robot commercialisation and a collaborative human-robot environment, in which robots took the physical burden and risk out of lineside assembly and logistics tasks.
The Atlas debut formed part of Hyundai’s wider AI robotics strategy focus at a technology trade show in the US, which it entitled Partnering Human Progress.
Atlas robots will be gradually deployed across different parts of the business through process-by-process validation.
Beginning in 2028, the robot will pick up tasks with proven safety and quality benefits, such as parts sequencing.
By 2030, applications will extend to component assembly.
source_title: Hyundai shows off Atlas humanoid as part of AI robotics strategy

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Investment
canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Georgia EV Battery Plant Partnership
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced a significant $2 billion investment in their battery manufacturing collaboration at Hyundai’s new EV plant in Georgia.
The partnership between Hyundai and LG Energy Solution was initiated just three months prior to the announcement.
The investment aims to enhance battery production capabilities in anticipation of Hyundai’s EV production start in 2025.
The additional investment is expected to create hundreds of jobs.
The initiative will bolster battery manufacturing facilities at the Metaplant in Bryan County.
LG Energy Solution has agreements with Toyota to supply lithium batteries.
LG Energy Solution announced a $3 billion investment in a Michigan plant for EV battery production.
Georgia’s favorable business environment, skilled workforce, and strategic location have made it an attractive destination for companies looking to capitalize on the EV boom.
The investments align with U.S. government objectives to accelerate the transition to electric vehicles, enhance energy storage solutions, and reduce carbon emissions.
These developments are aligned with initiatives like the Inflation Reduction Act.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution’s expansion in Georgia contributes to the creation of a sustainable and resilient EV supply chain in the U.S.
Strategic partnerships between Hyundai, LG Energy Solution, and other major players like Toyota signal a significant shift towards a robust EV ecosystem in the United States.

Automotive giants and battery manufacturers are increasing their footprint in the U.S., driven by growing demand for electric vehicles and the strategic imperative to localize supply chains for better resilience and efficiency.
Georgia is positioned at the forefront of the transformative wave toward electric mobility in the U.S.
source_title: Hyundai and LG’s $2B Investment Accelerates Georgia’s Role in the EV Revolution

entity_name: Hyundai-LG EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution announced a joint venture to build an EV battery plant in Georgia.
The joint venture represents a $4.3 billion investment.
The plant is set to be located in Bryan County near Savannah, Georgia.
The facility represents one of the largest foreign investments in the U.S. battery manufacturing sector.
This venture is Hyundai and LG's most ambitious project to date, building on their previous collaborations.
The construction of the plant is anticipated to create thousands of jobs, driving economic growth in the Savannah area and Georgia.
Senator Jon Ossoff highlighted that the project is a direct result of manufacturing incentives in the Inflation Reduction Act.
The plant is positioned as a step towards establishing Georgia as a key player in the green technology and renewable energy sectors.
The expansion of EV battery production capabilities accelerates the transition to sustainable transportation options and reduces reliance on fossil fuels.
The manufacturing process and sourcing of materials like lithium raise concerns about the environmental footprint of battery production.
The plant will have an annual production capacity of 30 GWh.
The facility's capacity is capable of supporting the production of 300,000 EVs.
The plant's location in Georgia is adjacent to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.

The proximity to Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America signifies a strategic move to integrate battery production with vehicle assembly, reducing logistics costs and minimizing transportation environmental impact.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution are breaking ground on the new venture.
source_title: Hyundai and LG Forge Ahead in the EV Race with a $4.3 Billion Battery Plant in Georgia

entity_name: LG Energy Solution-Hyundai Joint Battery Cell Plant
canonical_name: LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group joint battery cell plant
facts: LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group struck a joint venture deal to build a $4.3 billion battery cell plant.
The companies announced the joint venture on Friday.
The factory will be located in Savannah, Georgia.
Production at the new facility is slated to begin at the end of 2025.
The plant will have enough capacity to produce 300,000 EVs a year.
LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group are both South Korea-based companies.
Each company will take a 50% stake in the venture and facility.
Hyundai will assemble battery packs using cells from the plant to be sent to its U.S. manufacturing sites.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution have worked together in the EV battery sector since 2009.
In 2009, Hyundai introduced its Elantra Hybrid EV, for which LG Energy Solution provided batteries.
LG Energy Solution also provided batteries for Hyundai’s Kona Electric and IONIQ 6 EVs.
This joint venture is not the companies’ first partnership in this space.
In September 2021, the companies broke ground on a battery cell manufacturing plant in Karawang, Indonesia.
The $1.1 billion Indonesian plant is set to start production in the first half of 2024.
A new memorandum of agreement will strengthen Hyundai’s and LG Energy Solution’s ongoing EV battery partnership.
LG Energy Solution CEO Youngsoo Kwon stated: “Two strong leaders in the auto and battery industries have joined hands, and together we are ready to drive the EV transition in America.”

Youngsoo Kwon added: “By further advancing our product competitiveness and global operational expertise, LG Energy Solution will commit our best efforts to offering the ultimate sustainable energy solutions to our customers.”
Hyundai’s upcoming Georgia EV campus has been attracting other South Korea-based auto suppliers to the state.
Georgia announced its eighth Hyundai supplier to set up shop near its EV campus on Tuesday.
source_title: LG Energy Solution, Hyundai to build $4.3B joint battery cell plant in Georgia | Manufacturing Dive

facts: State and local officials announced Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solutions will invest another $2 billion in a battery manufacturing facility.
The battery manufacturing facility is located in Black Creek at the Metaplant site.
An agreement signing ceremony for the investment was held Thursday in Pooler.
The project announcement includes the creation of 400 jobs in Bryan County.
source_title: Hyundai,  LG announce to invest another $2 billion, 400 jobs in Bryan County - Bryan County News

facts: Georgia announced a $5 billion Rivian electric truck plant in December.
Hyundai Motor Group unveiled a $5.5 billion electric vehicle and battery facility in May.
Korean solar energy company Qcells announced a $171 million expansion of its photovoltaic module manufacturing operation in Dalton.
Construction on the Qcells expansion is due to start in the fall, bringing 470 jobs to Whitfield County when the plant opens in mid-2023.
Qcells currently employs 750 workers at a nearby plant it opened in 2019.
Together, Qcells' Dalton facilities will represent one-third of the solar module production capacity in the United States.
Qcells CEO Justin Lee stated that 'Georgia has become the clean energy manufacturing heart of America.'
German metal supplier Aurubis broke ground on June 17 on a $320 million recycling facility in Augusta (Richmond County).
The Aurubis Richmond facility will be the nation’s largest metal recycling facility and feature the first large secondary smelter for multimetal recycling.
When fully operational in mid-2024, the Aurubis plant will extract copper, tin, nickel, and other metals from 90,000 tons of materials such as circuit boards each year.
The Aurubis facility is expected to bring 120 jobs to Richmond County.
Georgia unveiled the 1,113-acre Middle Georgia Megasite near Fort Valley on June 16 as a partnership between the state and the Development Authority of Peach County.
The megasite project was funded by proceeds from selling a Chatham County site to Amazon last year.

Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the joint effort will create opportunities for the entire region and that projects attracted to megasites create a ripple effect of growth that crosses county lines.
Governor Brian Kemp announced on June 13 that Georgia maintained its top-level AAA bond rating from FitchRatings, Moody’s Investors Service, and S&P Global Ratings.
Georgia used its AAA rating to sell general obligation bonds due July 7 to finance $754 million in capital projects, including economic development.
source_title: Scoring Goals While Going Green - Atlanta Magazine

facts: The need for rare earth metals is growing and critical in making everything from fighter jets to power tools.

U.S. manufacturers have typically purchased essential metals and rare earths from foreign producers led by China, but unpredictable tariffs and trade policies, and geopolitics, have made these sources unreliable.
The U.S. government just announced a landmark equity stake in MP Materials and the country's only working rare earths mine, Mountain Pass.
A new generation of recyclers see electronic waste like old laptops, phones and TVs as another key to extracting critical elements in short supply globally.

As the U.S. and China vie for economic, technological and geopolitical supremacy, the critical elements and metals embedded in technology from consumer to industrial and military markets have become a pawn in the wider conflict, with nowhere more so the case than in China's leverage over the rare earth metals supply chain.
The Department of Defense took a large equity stake in MP Materials, the company running the only rare earths mining operation in the U.S.

Next-generation recyclers, a mix of legacy companies and startups, are innovating ways to gather and process the ever-growing mountains of electronic waste, or e-waste, which comprises end-of-life and discarded computers, smartphones, servers, TVs, appliances, medical devices, and other electronics and IT equipment.
Spent EV batteries, wind turbines and solar panels are fostering a burgeoning recycling niche.

The e-waste recycling opportunity isn't limited to rare earth elements; any electronics that can't be wholly refurbished and resold, or cannibalized for replacement parts, can be recycled to strip out gold, silver, copper, nickel, steel, aluminum, lithium, cobalt and other metals vital to manufacturers in various industries.
Recyclers are increasingly extracting rare-earth elements, such as neodymium, praseodymium, terbium and dysprosium, which are critical in making everything from fighter jets to power tools.

Kunal Sinha, global head of recycling at Swiss-based Glencore, stated that recycling of e-waste hasn't been taken too seriously until recently as a meaningful source of supply, and added that a lot of people are still sleeping at the wheel and don't realize how big this can be.

Traditionally, U.S. manufacturers purchase essential metals and rare earths from domestic and foreign producers—an inordinate number based in China—or through commodities traders, but with those supply chains now disrupted by unpredictable tariffs, trade policies and geopolitics, the market for recycled e-waste is gaining importance as a way to feed the insatiable electrification of everything.

John Mitchell, president and CEO of the Global Electronics Association, said the United States imports a lot of electronics that come with gold and aluminum and steel, creating a great opportunity for tariffs to act as an impetus for greater recycling in this country for goods bought from other countries.

Recycling contributes only around $200 million to Glencore's total EBITDA of nearly $14 billion, but the strategic attention and time the business gets from leadership is much more than that percentage.
Glencore believes that while a lot of mining is necessary to get to all the copper, gold and other metals needed, recycling is going to play a huge role.
Glencore has operated a huge copper smelter in Quebec, Canada for almost 20 years on a site that's nearly 100-years-old.

The Quebec facility processes mostly mined copper concentrates, though 15% of its feedstock is recyclable materials, such as e-waste that Glencore's global network of 100-plus suppliers collect and sort.
The smelter pioneered the process for recovering copper and precious metals from e-waste in the mid 1980s, making it one of the first and largest of its type in the world.

The smelted copper is refined into fresh slabs that are sold to manufacturers and traders, and the same facility also produces refined gold, silver, platinum and palladium recovered from recycling feeds.
The importance of copper to OEMs' supply chains was magnified in early July when prices hit an all-time high after President Trump said he would impose a 50% tariff on imports of the metal.
The U.S. imports just under half of its copper, and the tariff hike is intended to boost domestic production.
It takes around three decades for a new mine in the U.S. to move from discovery to production, which makes recycled copper look all the more attractive as demand keeps rising.
According to estimates by energy-data firm Wood Mackenzie, 45% of copper demand will be met with recycled copper by 2050, up from about a third today.
Foreign recycling companies have begun investing in U.S.-based facilities; in 2022, Germany's Wieland broke ground on a $100-million copper and copper alloy recycling plant in Shelbyville, Kentucky.
Last year, another German firm, Aurubis, started construction on an $800-million multi-metal recycling facility in Augusta, Georgia.

Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag stated that as the first major secondary smelter of its kind in the U.S., Aurubis Richmond will allow them to keep strategically important metals in the economy, making U.S. supply chains more independent.
The proliferation of e-waste can be traced back to the 1990s when the internet gave birth to the digital economy, spawning exponential growth in electronically enabled products.

This trend has been supercharged by the emergence of renewable energy, e-mobility, artificial intelligence and the build-out of data centers, translating to a constant turnover of devices and massive amounts of e-waste.

In 2022, a record 62 million metric tons of e-waste were produced globally, up 82% from 2010, according to the most recent estimates from the United Nations' International Telecommunications Union and research arm UNITAR.
That global e-waste number is projected to reach 82 million metric tons by 2030.
The U.S. produced just shy of 8 million tons of e-waste in 2022, yet only about 15-20% of it is properly recycled, illustrating the untapped market for e-waste retrievables.
The e-waste recycling industry generated $28.1 billion in revenue in 2024, according to IBISWorld, with a projected compound annual growth rate of 8%.

E-waste that stores data, especially smartphones, computers, servers and some medical devices, must be wiped of sensitive information to comply with cybersecurity and environmental regulations through a service referred to as IT asset disposition (ITAD).

ITAD is offered by conventional waste and recycling companies, including Waste Management, Republic Services and Clean Harbors, as well as specialists such as Sims Lifecycle Services, Electronic Recyclers International, All Green Electronics Recycling and Full Circle Electronics.
Full Circle Electronics CEO Dave Daily stated they are definitely seeing a bit of an influx of e-waste coming into their warehouses, which he thinks is due to some early refresh cycles.

This influx references businesses and consumers choosing to get ahead of the customary three-year time frame for purchasing new electronics and discarding old stuff in anticipation of tariff-related price increases.
Daily is also witnessing increased demand among downstream recyclers for e-waste Full Circle Electronics can't refurbish and sell at wholesale.
The company dismantles and separates e-waste into 40 or 50 different types of material, from keyboards and mice to circuit boards, wires and cables.
Recyclers harvest those items for metals and rare earths, which continue to go up in price on commodities markets, before reentering the supply chain as core raw materials.

Entrepreneurs have been launching e-waste recycling startups and developing technologies to process them for domestic OEMs even before the Trump administration's efforts to revitalize American manufacturing by reworking trade deals and recent changes in tax credits key to the industry.

Sinha noted that many regions of the world have been kind of lazy about processing e-waste, so a lot of it goes offshore, but there seems to be a trend of nationalizing e-waste because people suddenly realize they have the same metals they've been looking for from overseas sources.
People have been rethinking the global supply chain, realizing it's too long and needs to be more localized.
China commands roughly 90% of the global rare earth market in mining, processing and refining.
In the production of rare-earth magnets used in EVs, drones, consumer electronics, medical devices, wind turbines, military weapons and other products, China dominates the supply chain.

The lingering U.S.–China trade war has exacerbated the disparity; in April, China restricted exports of seven rare earths and related magnets in retaliation for U.S. tariffs, a move that forced Ford to shut down factories because of magnet shortages.
In mid-June, China issued temporary six-month licenses to certain major U.S. automaker suppliers and select firms; exports are flowing again but with delays and still well below peak levels.
Before the Trump administration deal, the Biden administration awarded $45 million in funding to MP Materials and the nation's lone rare earths mine in Mountain Pass, California.
Back in April, the Interior Department approved development activities at the Colosseum rare earths project located within California's Mojave National Preserve.
The Colosseum project is owned by Australia's Dateline Resources and will potentially become America's second rare earth mine after Mountain Pass.
Several recycling startups are extracting rare earths from e-waste; Illumynt has an advanced process for recovering them from decommissioned hard drives procured from data centers.

In April, hard drive manufacturer Western Digital announced a collaboration with Microsoft, Critical Materials Recycling and PedalPoint Recycling to pull rare earths, as well as copper, gold, aluminum and steel, from end-of-life drives.
Canadian-based Cyclic Materials invented a process that recovers rare-earths and other metals from EV motors, wind turbines, MRI machines and data-center e-scrap.
The company is investing more than $20 million to build its first U.S.-based facility in Mesa, Arizona.
Late last year, Glencore signed a multiyear agreement with Cyclic to provide recycled copper for its smelting and refining operations.
End-of-life lithium-ion batteries are a hot feedstock for e-waste recyclers, providing not only lithium but also copper, cobalt, nickel, manganese and aluminum.
Those materials are essential for manufacturing new EV batteries, which the Big Three automakers are heavily invested in.
Their projects are threatened by possible reductions in the Biden-era 45X production tax credit featured in the new federal spending bill.

It's too soon to know how that might impact battery recyclers including Ascend Elements, American Battery Technology, Cirba Solutions and Redwood Materials, who themselves qualify for the 45X and other tax credits.
They might actually be aided by other provisions in the budget bill that benefit a domestic supply chain of critical minerals as a way to undercut China's dominance of the global market.
Sinha warned that the looming uncertainty should be a warning sign for e-waste recyclers, cautioning them not to build a recycling company on the back of one tax credit because it can be short-lived.

Investing in recyclers can be precarious; while recycling is getting its due as a meaningful source of supply, Sinha cautions people to be careful when investing in this space because startups may have developed new technologies but lack good enough business fundamentals.
He advised not to invest on the hype but on the fundamentals.
Glencore has invested $327.5 million in convertible notes in battery recycler Li-Cycle to provide feedstock for its smelter.
The Toronto-based startup had broken ground on a new facility in Rochester, New York but ran into financial difficulties and filed for Chapter 15 bankruptcy protection in May.
This prompted Glencore to submit a stalking horse credit bid of at least $40 million for the stalled project and other assets.
Sinha stated that the current environment will lead to more startups and investments in e-waste recycling, adding that they are investing themselves.
source_title: In rare earth metals power struggle with China, old laptops, phones may get a new life – NBC 6 South Florida

facts: The page hosts current Aurubis press materials from Richmond including photos, videos, press releases and more.
A video titled 'First Melt Aurubis Richmond' is available for viewing via QR code.
Images documenting the 'First Melt' event are available for download.
Photographs feature U.S. Representative Rick W. Allen and Aurubis management.
A downloadable press release is available.
An Aurubis Richmond factsheet is available for download.
A document detailing the recycling process in Richmond is available for download.
The media library contains images of the Aurubis Richmond plant, including aerial views and material handling systems.
Additional imagery covers recycling materials and warehouse operations at the Aurubis Richmond facility.
David Schultheis serves as President & Managing Director of Aurubis Richmond.
Christoph Tesch is Head of Corporate Communications.
Meino Hauschildt is Manager Corporate Communications and Spokesperson.

facts: Duckyang, an electric mobility parts supplier of automotive battery modules and energy storage systems to SK Battery America (SKBA), will invest $10 million in opening its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Jackson County.
The new facility will create 285 jobs in Braselton, Georgia.
Duckyang’s new facility will be 230,000 square feet and located at 984 Broadway Avenue in Braselton.
The company will be hiring for positions in production, maintenance, logistics, and warehouse management.
Headquartered in Ulsan, Korea, Duckyang supplies cockpit modules to customers around the world including Hyundai, Kia, Renault Samsung Motors, Genesis, and Mobis.
Cockpit modules are integrated auto parts comprised of more than 130 subcomponents found near the driver’s seat of a vehicle.
By entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market with SKBA, Duckyang will cooperate with other automotive companies like Hyundai and Kia Motors that already have a manufacturing base in the U.S.

Duckyang expects business expansion opportunities to supply not only electric vehicle parts but also interior parts, including cockpit modules, door trim panel modules, and other flagship parts to global automakers based in the United States.
Governor Kemp stated that Duckyang is creating high-paying, advanced automotive manufacturing jobs for Georgians in Jackson County.
Georgia has remained the number one state for business for the past eight years.
Town Manager and Clerk of Braselton Jennifer Scott welcomed Duckyang to the community as a new manufacturing facility.

John Scott, Director of Economic Development for the Jackson County Area Chamber of Commerce, stated that companies like Duckyang are helping Jackson County and Georgia secure a place in the industry's future as the automotive industry moves toward electric.

Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce division on this project in partnership with the Jackson County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Georgia is focused on growing the entire electric mobility supply chain and uses relationships with SK Battery America and Duckyang to help companies secure local supply of needed parts.
The State of Georgia facilitated more than $1.3 billion in investment and more than 2,700 jobs from South Korean companies during 2020 alone.
Duckyang Industrial was established in July 1977 and was the first company to develop and produce automobile cockpit modules in Korea.
Duckyang solidified its position in the automobile business through a strategic alliance with Visteon, an American global automotive supplier spun off from Ford in 1999.

Duckyang is continuously promoting research and development on new technologies to play a role as a leader in the global automotive market and plans to expand production capabilities for interior automotive parts in addition to electronics in the future.
source_title: Korean Automotive Manufacturer Duckyang to Open Company's First U.S. Facility in Georgia - AllOnGeorgia

facts: Duckyang is investing USD 10 million for a plant in Georgia.
The Georgia plant will supply SK Battery America.
Duckyang is headquartered in Ulsan, Korea.
source_title: Duckyang invests USD 10 million for Georgia plant to supply SK Battery America - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: Seoyon E-HWA invests USD 76 million for a new plant in Georgia.
The new plant will supply Hyundai.
source_title: Seoyon E-HWA invests USD 76 million for new plant in Georgia to supply Hyundai - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: Sewon America Rincon Effingham (S.A.R.E.) is located in Rincon, GA.
Sewon America operates the largest automotive body parts manufacturing plant in North America.
The facility delivers high-quality products through cutting-edge automated manufacturing processes and efficient logistics.
In 2023, Sewon America broke ground on a new state-of-the-art automotive manufacturing plant in Rincon, Georgia.
The new Rincon facility is set to begin production in 2024.
This facility will be Sewon America's second plant in the United States.
Once fully operational, the Rincon plant will have created 740 new jobs.
The company is actively recruiting applicants to join its team in Rincon, GA.
The facility address is 1000 Sewon EV Road, Rincon, GA 31326.
The facility operates from 08:00 am to 05:00 pm.
source_title: Sewon America Inc. (S.A.R.E.)

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced that Sewon America will create 740 new jobs in Rincon.
Sewon America will invest more than $300 million in a new Effingham County manufacturing facility.
This project is the largest known private investment in the City of Rincon to date.
The announcement fulfills a commitment to create jobs and investment in all four Savannah Harbor Interstate-16 Joint Development Authority (JDA) counties.
This fulfillment results from Hyundai's Metaplant location in Bryan County, which is the largest economic development project in state history.
Suppliers for the Metaplant have now committed over $1.8 billion in investment.
Supplier investments surpass initial estimates by at least $800 million.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the announcement celebrates delivering on the promise to create jobs in all four JDA counties and highlights the growth of a valued partner.
On his first international trip as governor to Korea, Kemp congratulated Chairman Kim on the company’s then-recent expansion and discussed continuing collaboration.
Those discussions led to this announcement as Sewon America continues to grow its Georgia operations.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns, who represents part of Effingham County, played a role in securing the project and engaging Sewon America executives.
Speaker Jon Burns celebrated the creation of 740 new jobs for Effingham County and coastal Georgia.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones commended Governor Brian Kemp, Sewon America, and stakeholders on the historic economic development announcement for Rincon.
Burt Jones noted that investments by companies like Sewon build on Georgia’s commitment to remain the No. 1 state in which to do business while attracting a robust workforce.
The investment of more than $300 million in Effingham County will have a positive impact on the local community and state.
Sewon America operates one of the largest automobile body parts manufacturing plants in North America.
The company opened a manufacturing facility in LaGrange in 2008, which has since doubled in size.
Following completion of the Rincon facility, Sewon is expected to support 1,600 jobs in Georgia.

Vice Chairman Kim of Sewon America stated that Sewon America was founded 15 years ago in west Georgia and expressed excitement about building another facility in east Georgia as one family in two locations.
Kim credited the growth to the Sewon team, Governor Kemp, the Georgia Department of Economic Development, and Effingham County.
Sewon’s new facility will be located at the Grande View industrial park in Rincon.
The new manufacturing location will produce electric vehicle (EV) body parts for original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
OEM customers include Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Operations are expected to begin in 2025.

Rincon Mayor Ken Lee welcomed Sewon America and commended the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority for bringing the largest employment and private investment project in the city’s history.

Chairman of the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority (IDA) Troy Smith stated that selecting Grande View industrial park allows current and future generations to have fulfilling, well-paying careers without leaving Effingham County.
Smith thanked partners at the City of Rincon and the Effingham County Board of Commissioners for their help.
Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority Griff Lynch called the announcement terrific news for Effingham County and the State of Georgia.
Lynch noted that the Hyundai announcement continues to bring new economic opportunities to the Peach State.
Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
GDEcD partnered with the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Sewon has integrated into and lent its own industry experience to Georgia’s automotive community through local colleges and Georgia Quick Start.
Wilson mentioned Sewon's participation in statewide initiatives like Georgia Made.

Wilson noted that Sewon is reinvesting in Georgia as they support Hyundai Motor Group’s operations from Effingham County, which has the skilled workforce, infrastructure, and collaborative spirit to support growth.
On October 25, 2022, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America officially broke ground in neighboring Bryan County on its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility.
Georgia is positioned as a hub for the electric mobility industry.
Since 2020, more than 35 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment and 26,700 jobs in Georgia.
source_title: GA: Sewon America's New Manufacturing Facility to Create 740 Jobs in Effingham County | Trade and Industry Development

facts: Gov. Nathan Deal announced that SEWON America will create 100 jobs in LaGrange, GA.
SEWON America will invest $160 million in expanding its North American headquarters and manufacturing plant in LaGrange.
SEWON America is a leading supplier to the automotive industry.
Gov. Nathan Deal stated that Georgia is the No. 1 state for business.
Gov. Nathan Deal noted that Georgia maintains a competitive economic environment encouraging growth and innovation among companies already operating there.
SEWON America has a significant economic footprint in the Troup County area.
The company will continue to benefit from proximity to a growing family of automotive manufacturers and a strong workforce.
The expansion involves adding additional manufacturing lines to meet production requirements for new Kia and Hyundai models.
The new manufacturing lines will improve efficiency and flexibility compared to current technology, allowing for greater productivity.

Ken Horton, SEWON general manager of human resources, stated that with support from Gov. Nathan Deal, Troup County, and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), SEWON will remain operational and continue delivering parts to customers.
Ken Horton thanked the State of Georgia and the City of LaGrange for investing in SEWON America and its team members, affirming the company will continue creating jobs in LaGrange and Troup County.
source_title: SEWON America to create 100 jobs in LaGrange, GA - Location Georgia

facts: Hanon is a global supplier of thermal and energy management solutions for electric and conventional vehicles.
Hanon’s products include components used in the heating and/or cooling operations of battery boxes and vehicle interiors.
In an effort to increase Hanon’s presence within the US electric vehicle EV market, Hanon is expanding its operations to rural Statesboro, Georgia.
Hanon is building a manufacturing facility to serve a nearby EV manufacturing plant.
The facility will total approximately 112,000 sq. ft.
The facility will have the opportunity to expand to 330,000 square feet, should Hanon need additional space.
source_title: Hanon Systems - New Markets Tax Credit Coalition

facts: JLL Capital Markets led the sale and financing efforts for a mission-critical operation with potential for expansion.
Sale and acquisition financing was arranged for a Hanon Systems build-to-suit industrial property located at 8540 Zell Miller Road in Statesboro, Georgia.
The facility is described as a fully occupied Class A light manufacturing and distribution building.
Statesboro serves as an extension of the established Savannah, GA industrial market.
JLL represented the seller, which is a partnership between Graham Capital and Chesterfield.
Spaulding & Slye Investments acquired the property.
JLL arranged the acquisition financing with Seacoast Bank on behalf of the new owner.
The facility is strategically located 1.1 miles from Highway 301.
It is situated 7.2 miles from I-16 and 34.9 miles from the Hyundai Metaplant.
The location positions the facility to serve both the Hyundai Metaplant and the broader Savannah market.
The property spans 112,011 square feet.
The institutionally designed facility features a 32-foot clear height.
The building includes multi-sided loading, 11 dock-high doors, and two drive-in doors.
The facility contains 14,880 square feet of office space.
The property is 100% leased to Hanon Systems Alabama, Corp., a subsidiary of Hanon Systems.
Hanon Systems operates as a full-line thermal and energy management solutions provider.
The build-to-suit project was completed in April 2024.
The facility was designed for assembling HVAC products, radiators, condensers, heat exchangers, and battery heating and cooling products for automobiles.
The facility primarily serves the nearby Hyundai Metaplant.
The lease includes two expansion options for the tenant: a first option of 68,000 square feet and a second option of 145,600 square feet.
JLL Investment Sales and Advisory team was led by Directors Jim Freeman and Anthony Walters.
Senior Managing Directors on the JLL Investment Sales and Advisory team include Britton Burdette, Dennis Mitchell, and Matt Wirth.
Associate Bo Osgood served on the JLL Investment Sales and Advisory team.
JLL Debt Advisory team was spearheaded by Senior Director Bobby Norwood.
Senior Managing Director Mark Sixour also led the JLL Debt Advisory team.
Jim Freeman stated that Savannah's industrial market is experiencing exceptional growth driven by record-high cargo volumes at the Port and new operations entering to supply the Hyundai Metaplant.
The Savannah market has seen strong fundamentals and rental rate growth over the past two years.
The market boasts one of the highest levels of tracked tenant demand as a percentage of inventory in the country.
Graham Capital is a real estate investment company based in Birmingham, Alabama, focused on distribution centers in high-growth Southeastern industrial markets.
Chesterfield specializes in developing class A industrial, manufacturing, and office projects throughout the southeastern United States.
Spaulding & Slye Investments was founded in Boston in 1966 and manages approximately 4.5 million SF of development and operating assets.
Seacoast Banking Corporation of Florida is one of the largest community banks headquartered in Florida, with approximately $14.8 billion in assets and $12 billion in deposits as of March 31, 2024.
source_title: Sale and acquisition financing arranged for Hanon Systems Build-to-Suit industrial property

facts: Hanon Systems will invest close to $170 million in a new facility in Loudon, Tennessee.
The new facility will create 600 new jobs in the area.
The facility will be located in Huntingdon Park, according to the Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development (TNECD).
Hanon Systems is a global operation that supplies thermal solutions to automotive manufacturers.
The company's products include heating ventilation, air conditioning, powertrain cooling, compressors, fluid transport, electronics and fluid pressure.
This Tennessee location will supply North American automotive markets.
The company’s headquarters sits in South Korea.
Hanon Systems employs over 22,000 people in 21 countries.
Governor Bill Lee and Commissioner Stuart McWhorter announced the company's arrival on Tuesday.
Loudon County Mayor Buddy Bradshaw stated that Loudon County is very excited about an international company of Hanon Systems’ caliber locating there.
Mayor Bradshaw noted the number and quality of jobs will improve the job market landscape not just locally but regionally.
Mayor Bradshaw highlighted the nearly $170 million capital investment and pay scale will establish the company quickly in the community.
Mayor Bradshaw expressed excitement to see the partnership flourish and look forward to all Hanon Systems will bring to Loudon County.
source_title: Hanon Systems bringing 600 new jobs to Tennessee - WDEF

facts: An auto parts manufacturer plans to hire 630 workers at a new factory in southeast Georgia to supply Hyundai Motor Group’s first U.S. electric vehicle plant that is under construction nearby.
Joon Georgia will invest $317 million to produce parts in Bulloch County.
The supplier will open its facility roughly 30 miles west of the southeast Georgia site where Hyundai executives broke ground on the new EV plant two weeks ago.
The company is described as "the first of many" expected to come to Georgia to supply the $5.5 billion Hyundai plant in Bryan County.
Hyundai plans to open its Georgia plant in 2025, producing up to 300,000 electric vehicles per year.
Joon Georgia is a subsidiary of Ajin USA, which supplies parts to other Hyundai plants.
Ajin USA already operates a facility in Cusseta, Alabama, near the Georgia line that makes parts for Hyundai’s plant in Montgomery, Alabama, as well as for Kia’s auto plant in West Point, Georgia.
The Joon Georgia factory near the Hyundai EV plant is expected to open near Statesboro in mid-2024.
Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development, called Joon Georgia's announcement a "landmark moment as we drive Georgia’s automotive industry into the future."
State and local officials in Georgia lured Hyundai with tax breaks and incentives worth $1.8 billion, making it the state’s largest economic development deal.
Wilson and other Georgia officials have insisted that Hyundai's investment is worthwhile.
In addition to Hyundai hiring 8,100 workers, suppliers are expected to create thousands of additional jobs in the state.
source_title: Auto parts manufacturer to hire hundreds near Hyundai’s Georgia EV plant | FOX 5 Atlanta

facts: Ajin Georgia is located 30 miles west of the upcoming Hyundai EV plant in Bryan County.
According to a news release from Gov. Brian Kemp’s office, Joon plans to hire 630 workers for its future $317 million parts factory.
The strategic location marks a significant milestone in the region’s industrial landscape.
The venture aligns with a commitment to fostering economic growth and creating opportunities within the community.
Joon Georgia is shaping the future of manufacturing in the communities it serves.
source_title: Joon Georgia, Inc (Ajin Georgia) - Charge Forward Georgia

canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group EV and Battery Metaplant
facts: Hyundai Motor Group says its arrival will mean 8,100 new jobs at its site at Interstate 16 and Georgia 280 in Bryan County by the time the electric vehicle and battery plant are fully running.
Official announcements for the development include at least $5.5 billion in construction spending and suppliers that plan to hire thousands more employees.

The metaplant's staff count would put it among the biggest employers in the region, where only 11 private companies employ more than 1,000 people in Chatham, Bryan and Effingham counties according to Georgia Department of Labor’s 2022 data.
Gulfstream is the largest employer in the area with roughly 11,500 workers around Savannah and is looking for more staff.
The Savannah-Chatham Public School System has about 5,500 staff.
Some 3,500 civilians work at Fort Stewart, while 640 civilians are employed at Hunter Army Air Field.
Approximately 27,000 people work in the hospitality industry in Chatham, Bryan and Effingham counties, spread among some 1,600 relatively small businesses.
Hyundai and the next-door battery plant will pay at least $58,105 on average, plus benefits, for each job.
The average wage is required to qualify for all promised state and local grants, tax breaks and land, which amount to public funds worth about $228,000 per job.
Hyundai suppliers can cut separate pay, investment and subsidy deals with local governments.
Ecoplastic’s plant near Register needs to provide 456 jobs in eight years when fully functional making plastic parts for cars.
Ecoplastic workers need to be paid at least the county’s average wage of $21.65 an hour as of the February 2023 agreement with Bulloch County.
At 40 hours per week, that wage comes to about $43,300 per year for Ecoplastic workers.
In return for building in Bulloch County, Ecoplastic received a $2.3 million discount on the purchase price of its site and a discount on county property taxes for 15 years.
The Bulloch County tax discount does not apply to school and fire district taxes.
Ecoplastic may also receive a $1.6 million state grant.
Joon Georgia has a similar contract with Bulloch County and the state, promising an hourly rate matching the county average for 630 jobs it will create in its new facility.
In return, Joon Georgia received a $3.1 million discount on land near I-16, a $2.7 million state grant and a 10-year county property tax break.
The Joon Georgia county property tax break does not apply to school and fire rates.
The Hyundai Metaplant sits on more than a mile of Black Creek, about three miles before the tributary joins the Ogeechee River.
Hyundai and its suppliers are setting up near interstates and the waterways of the Ogeechee watershed.
A new wastewater treatment plant for the site and surrounding areas will discharge into the Ogeechee River.
The wastewater treatment plant across the interstate from Hyundai will treat the plant’s wastewater and north Bryan’s sewage before pouring it into the Ogeechee.
Environmentalists at the Ogeechee Riverkeeper have started tracking water quality in both the creek and the river due to distrust of state pollution monitoring.
Eight Hyundai suppliers will be spread out around Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham, Effingham and Liberty counties.
None of the supplier factories are on public transport lines, requiring a car for access.
Hyundai production is set to start in the first half of 2025.
The Metaplant site sits on the south side of I-16, about 22 miles northwest of Savannah.
Site clearance is already visible from satellite photos.
Satellite photos show the build-out of industrial sites in the area since 2005.
Daniel Defense and ORAFOL are located just across the interstate from the site.
The team that wooed Hyundai and suppliers was made up of state and county leaders as well as the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority.
Georgia competed against regional states for the development, meeting manufacturer expectations of being offered billions of dollars in subsidies.
Georgia’s Department of Economic Development led its offer letter to Hyundai with the Best State for Business honorific from a niche business publication.
The publication cites the area’s outstanding logistics, well-trained and educated workforce, low business costs, pro-business climate and high quality of life.
Coastal Georgia laborers can expect to benefit quickly from tens of millions of dollars that the state is investing in job-training specific to the needs of the new employers.
source_title: 5 things: Hyundai development | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Ecoplastic Corporation is an automobile parts manufacturer based in South Korea.
Ecoplastic Corporation is opening a factory in Georgia to support the Hyundai Motor Group electric vehicle plant.
The Hyundai Motor Group electric vehicle plant in Bryan County near Savannah has a total investment value of $5.54 billion.
Ecoplastic Corporation is building a $205 million plant at 4822 Highway 301 South Register near Statesboro in Bulloch County, northwest of Savannah.
Ecoplastic Corporation was established in 1984.
Ecoplastic Corporation is a tier-1 supplier for Hyundai and Kia.
Ecoplastic Corporation makes automotive interior and exterior plastic parts.
The new plant will have injection molding and printing capabilities.
The plant will produce automotive parts including bumpers, consoles and trim for electric vehicles produced by the Hyundai operation.
The company currently employs more than 770 employees.
The new plant will create another 456 new jobs.
The plant is expected to take a year-and-a-half to build.
The company expects to begin operations by October 2024.
The company will soon be hiring individuals for jobs in HR, finance, the parts development, quality control, maintenance, injection and paint production.

Billy Allen, chair of the Development Authority of Bulloch County, stated that they are pleased that this important Hyundai supplier has chosen their community and are confident that their location, workforce and quality of life will help them succeed here.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp said in a statement that the new plant is a welcome addition to the Peach State.

Georgia’s prime location, major ports system, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce and pro-business climate have been cited as factors that have positioned it as a hub for the electric transportation industry and made it a prime location for industries focused on creating a sustainable future.

Director of project implementation and supplier strategy Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) global commerce team in partnership with the Development Authority of Bulloch County, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Quick Start, and Georgia Electrical Membership Corporation in discussions with Ecoplastic.
GDEcD commissioner Pat Wilson said state officials are excited to welcome Ecoplastic to Georgia.
Since 2020, more than 30 EV-related projects have announced more than $21 billion in investment which will create more than 26,300 jobs in the state.
Last October, Hyundai broke ground in Bryan County for its first fully dedicated electric vehicle and battery manufacturing facility that will employ at least 8,100 workers.
In addition to Hyundai’s direct contributions to the region, offsite suppliers connected to the project are expected to invest about $1 billion in other locations around the state.
A series of projects have already been announced, exceeding those expectations.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On are partnering to build a new battery manufacturing plant for electric vehicles at a site in Bartow County, northwest of Atlanta.
The Hyundai-SK On battery plant is expected to cost $4 to $5 billion and create more than 3,500 jobs.
Hyundai Mobis is investing $926 million in an electric vehicle power system plant in Bryan County that will eventually employ at least 1,500 people.

Auto parts manufacturer Joon Georgia is investing $317 million in a new manufacturing facility at the Bruce Yawn Commerce Park near Statesboro in Bulloch County that will supply Hyundai and create 630 jobs.

Freyer Battery, a developer of clean, next-generation battery cell production capacity, announced it will invest $2.57 billion in a manufacturing facility in Cowetta County that will create more than 720 jobs.
source_title: Ecoplastic Corporation latest company to open $205M plant in Georgia

facts: Ecoplastic America Corp. is a leading manufacturer of automotive plastic parts, supplying eco-friendly bumpers, consoles, and trims to major OEMs like Hyundai and Kia from its advanced Georgia facility.
Ecoplastic America is likely focused on ramping up production at its new Georgia facility.
The company is integrating advanced eco-friendly technologies into its operations.
Ecoplastic America is expanding partnerships with major automotive OEMs.
The company completed and operationally launched a new manufacturing facility in Bulloch County, Georgia.
Ecoplastic America is making strategic efforts to expand market presence and reach new customer segments.
The company is implementing advanced technologies to improve service delivery and customer experience.
Ecoplastic America Corp. manufactures a wide range of plastic automobile parts, focusing on bumpers, consoles, trims, and molds for leading automotive brands.
The company is recognized for its commitment to innovation, research and development, and environmental sustainability.
With a new facility in Bulloch County, Georgia, Ecoplastic America is expanding its workforce and capabilities.
The expanded workforce and capabilities are intended to support the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America and other OEMs.
Their eco-friendly manufacturing processes and advanced technology position them as a key player in the automotive plastics sector.
Ecoplastic America Corp. was founded in 1984.
Ecoplastic America Corp. is a private company.
source_title: Ecoplastic America Corp. | Automotive Plastic Parts Manuf...

facts: Lithium-ion battery recycler Ascend Elements, Westborough, Massachusetts, recently celebrated the grand opening of its first commercial-scale recycling facility in Covington, Georgia.
The company says the $50 million Base 1 facility is the largest of its type in North America.
The 150,000-square-foot facility began partial operations in August 2022.
The facility has an annual capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap, equal to 70,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O’Kronley states that the facility is an important part of America’s EV battery infrastructure and 'just the start.'
CEO Mike O’Kronley emphasizes the industry's need to continue building a closed-loop battery materials supply chain to make electric vehicle batteries cleaner and more sustainable.
Ascend Elements recovers 98 percent of the critical battery metals in used electric vehicle batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap.
The company uses its patented Hydro-to-Cathode process to recycle used lithium-ion batteries and scrap.
This process can reduce the carbon footprint of new electric vehicle battery cathode materials by up to 90 percent.
The Hydro-to-Cathode direct precursor synthesis process produces sustainably engineered cathode precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).
The new facility is powered by renewable energy.
The facility features on-site wastewater recycling and treatment systems.
It currently employs about 100 team members.
The company plans to increase its headcount to 185 by 2024.
source_title: Ascend Elements opens Georgia battery recycling facility - Recycling Today

facts: Ascend Elements has opened its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility.
The facility is located in Covington, Georgia.
The facility cost $50 million to build.
The facility is named "Base 1".
Ascend Elements claims the Base 1 facility is North America's largest electric vehicle battery recycling facility.
The facility began partial operations in August 2022.
The facility has an annual capacity to process 30,000 metric tons of used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap.
This processing capacity is equal to 70,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
Mike O'Kronley serves as the CEO of Ascend Elements.

CEO Mike O'Kronley stated that the facility is an important part of America's EV battery infrastructure and emphasized the industry's need to continue building a closed-loop battery materials supply chain for cleaner and more sustainable EV batteries.
Ascend Elements claims to recover up to 98% of critical battery metals in used EV batteries and gigafactory manufacturing scrap.
The facility uses a patented Hydro-to-Cathode process that can reduce the carbon footprint of new EV battery cathode materials by up to 90%.
The facility is powered by renewable energy.
The facility features onsite wastewater recycling and treatment systems.
Ascend Elements is headquartered in Westborough, Massachusetts.
The company commercially produces lithium-ion battery precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).

The Hydro-to-Cathode direct precursor synthesis technology produces new CAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods, resulting in improved economics and lowered GHG emissions.
source_title: Ascend Elements Opens Largest EV Battery Recycling Facility of North America

facts: Thomas & Hutton assisted the Development Authority of Bryan County with planning and site selection for a 1,200,000-square-foot Hyundai Mobis electric vehicle power electric system manufacturing facility.
The Hyundai Mobis facility is located at the Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified Belfast Commerce Park in Richmond Hill.
The plant will supply essential production components, EV power electric systems, and integrated charging control units to nearby Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Thomas & Hutton worked alongside the Newton County Industrial Development Authority on site selection for Archer Aviation Inc.
Archer Aviation plans to locate its facility alongside the Covington Municipal Airport in Newton County.
Thomas & Hutton provided civil engineering services related to land analysis for the over 96-acre Archer Aviation site.
Thomas & Hutton oversaw coordination with local utilities and the City of Covington on water and sewer delivery and access road improvements.
The team consulted with Airport staff on access to taxiways and height restrictions related to preliminary engineering work.
Archer Aviation will produce state-of-the-art electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft (eVTOL) at the proposed facility.
Archer Aviation expects to create 1,000 jobs and invest $118 million over ten years in Newton County.
The Archer Aviation project location next to the Covington Municipal Airport is congruent with the long-term airport master plan, providing access to Georgia’s interstate highway system.
SJT Properties broke ground on the Georgia International Commerce Centre in Bryan County.
The Georgia International Commerce Centre distribution facility will be 6.5 million square feet and consist of at least eight buildings.
Proximity to I-16 and the Hyundai Metaplant America were cited as influential drivers for SJT Properties locating in the area.
Norma Precision Inc. is expanding in Bryan County, Georgia, by building a new 300,000-square-foot manufacturing and distribution facility at Interstate Centre III adjacent to U.S. 280 and I-16.
The Norma Precision site is Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) certified and was fast-tracked for development via advanced due diligence.
Norma Precision is already hiring for various positions at its Bryan County facility.
The Norma Precision project is expected to bring 600 jobs to Bryan County.

Fairfield County Economic Development expects that speed to market via completed due diligence and a pre-graded site will draw manufacturers to the I-77 International Megasite in Fairfield County, South Carolina.
Construction is underway on Marion Lake, a multifamily community in western Chatham County near Savannah.
T&H is on the Mariner Group team for the creation of the Marion Lake property just west of I-95 in Savannah.
The Marion Lake multifamily property will include 340 units on a 23-acre lakefront site.
Thomas & Hutton expanded its service footprint into Kentucky with Ascend Elements’ Apex II groundbreaking at Hopkinsville’s Commerce Park II.
The Ascend Elements Apex II facility will be 450,000 square feet and process used lithium-ion batteries into materials compatible with battery systems in electric vehicles.
The Ascend Elements operation is described as the most significant investment in southwestern Kentucky’s history.
Thomas & Hutton works with SSOE Group on the 140-acre site for the Ascend Elements facility.

links.url: office of Gov. Brian Kemp press release

source_title: T&H Projects In The News: November 2022 - Thomas & Hutton

facts: Ascend Elements and Call2Recycle formalized an agreement to offer customized electric vehicle (EV) battery management, logistics, and recycling services.
The program combines Ascend Elements' EV battery recycling infrastructure and advanced technologies with Call2Recycle's collection, logistics, and tracking capabilities.
The program offers online access to GreenTraxEV, an all-in-one cloud-based platform designed to seamlessly ship mid-life, end-of-life, and damaged-defective EV batteries.
Working together, the companies aim to collect and recycle the equivalent of over 300,000 EV battery packs by 2030.
Leo Raudys, CEO of Call2Recycle, stated that the commercial agreement enables a convenient collection process through the state-of-the-art GreenTraxEV logistics platform.
GreenTraxEV leverages Microsoft Dynamics and Microsoft Power Platform to streamline the scheduling, routing, and logistics of EV battery management.
Ascend Elements and Call2Recycle can tailor the GreenTraxEV workflow to meet the specific requirements of any customer.

Ian Braime, Chief Commercial Officer at Ascend Elements, stated that the program will help automotive dealerships, repair shops, and recyclers prepare for the expected wave of EV batteries in the coming years.

The program supports lithium-ion batteries from battery electric vehicles, plug-in hybrid vehicles, hybrid vehicles, electric buses, electric aircraft, stationary energy storage units, outdoor power equipment, e-bikes, e-scooters, hoverboards, and power tools.
Ascend Elements' battery recycling facility in Covington, Georgia has the capacity to process 30,000 metric tons per year.
The Covington, Georgia facility is one of the largest EV battery recycling facilities in North America.
Call2Recycle was founded in 1994 as a not-for-profit organization committed to safe and responsible end-of-life battery management across the U.S.

Ascend Elements was founded in 2015 as a leading provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery material solutions, including commercial-scale production of lithium-ion battery precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).

Ascend Elements uses Hydro-to-Cathode® direct precursor synthesis technology to produce new CAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods, resulting in improved economics and lowered GHG emissions.
source_title: Ascend Elements and Call2Recycle Formalize Agreement to Offer Customized EV Battery Management, Logistics and Recycling Services - EIN Presswire

facts: Anovion Technologies has selected Decatur County in Southwest Georgia as the location of its new manufacturing facility for the production of synthetic graphite anode materials.
The Decatur County facility is expected to produce 40,000 metric tonnes of synthetic graphite anode material for lithium-ion batteries per year.
The new facility will cost $800 million.
The project will create more than 400 new jobs.
In 2022, Anovion was awarded a grant totaling $117 million from the US Department of Energy (DOE) to scale production capacity to meet growing demand.
Anovion is commencing a multi-year expansion program of 150,000 metric tonnes of annual capacity for anode-grade synthetic graphite.
In addition to the new Georgia facility, the company is expanding its Anovion Center of Excellence.
The company is also expanding its hydro-powered, sister manufacturing facility near Niagara Falls, New York.

Eric Stopka, CEO of Anovion, stated that Anovion conducted a thorough search to identify a location that addressed their energy, transportation, logistics and human capital needs that would ensure they have the ecosystem in place to produce synthetic graphite anode material.
source_title: Charged EVs | Anovion to invest $800 million in new US graphite anode materials manufacturing facility - Charged EVs

facts: Anovion Technologies is a leading U.S.-based manufacturer of synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries.
The company was founded by industry experts with over 140 years of combined experience in materials science.
Anovion specializes in producing high-quality battery components essential for electric vehicles, energy storage systems, and various electronic applications.
Anovion Technologies is North America’s first commercial producer of synthetic graphite anode materials.
The company contributes significantly to establishing a secure domestic battery supply chain, reducing dependence on international suppliers.
Anovion Technologies is headquartered in Chicago, United States.
source_title: Anovion Technologies - Battery-Tech Network

facts: Anovion Technologies is a climate tech-driven advanced materials company headquartered in Chicago, Illinois.
Anovion is a U.S.-owned and U.S.-based business and currently America’s first commercial-scale manufacturer of premium, cleaner anode-grade synthetic graphite.
Anovion operates an existing footprint near Niagara Falls, New York, and Clarksburg, West Virginia.
Anovion began commercial production in early 2021.
Anovion is one of the first qualified U.S. suppliers of synthetic graphite anode materials for use in e-mobility applications.
Anovion is among a limited number of graphite anode producers in America to have successfully gained qualification for EV applications.
Graphite is the largest battery material used in electric vehicles by mass, exceeding copper, nickel, manganese, cobalt, and lithium.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Anovion will build a new manufacturing facility in Bainbridge, Georgia.
The new facility will be 1,500,000 square feet and feature Anovion’s proprietary furnace technologies.
The facility is located at Downrange Industrial Park in Bainbridge.
This facility will be the first of its size and scale in North America.
The project represents over $800 million in investment in Decatur County.
The project will create more than 400 jobs.
The facility will produce synthetic graphite for use in batteries that power EVs, electric storage systems, electronics, military applications, and other products.
Operations at the new Bainbridge facility are expected to begin in late 2025.
Anovion will be hiring for technical, production, engineering, administrative, and management roles.
CEO Eric Stopka stated that Georgia’s strong technical workforce and top-tier schools will help advance research partnerships and contribute to a highly skilled talent pipeline.
CEO Eric Stopka noted the region’s growing availability of clean energy will increase sustainability for Anovion’s manufacturing process.
CEO Eric Stopka expressed encouragement regarding support from partners in Georgia and the Federal government.
Governor Brian Kemp called Georgia 'fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation' and praised job creators like Anovion.
Bainbridge Mayor Edward Reynolds highlighted the $800 million investment and creation of over 400 high-paying, quality jobs as a huge win for the community.
Decatur County Board of Commissioners Chairman Pete Stephens noted the county’s long-term focus on attracting quality companies and diversifying its industry mix.
Project Manager Taylor Walden represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on the project.
The project was developed in partnership with the City of Bainbridge, Development Authority of Bainbridge, Decatur County, University System of Georgia, Georgia EMC, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that much of the critical e-mobility supply chain is currently sourced outside the U.S., and Anovion fills that need by delivering a critical component to Georgia.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted public-private partnerships working with utility partners, establishing infrastructure, and attracting top talent through the University System of Georgia.
Since 2020, 40 EV-related projects have announced more than $21.9 billion in investment and 28,000 jobs in Georgia.
More than 10 of these Georgia projects are battery manufacturers, recyclers, and suppliers, representing approximately $8.5 billion in investment and 6,000 jobs.
source_title: Anovion Technologies to Create Over 400 Jobs in Bainbridge, Invest $800M in Manufacturing Facility - Location Georgia

facts: Officials broke ground on April 25 in south Augusta on the largest electric vehicle battery material facility in North America.

Syensqo’s PVDF production facility will create 500 construction jobs, 100 highly skilled jobs, and the potential for more than 500 indirect jobs according to a news release from the office of Mayor Garnett Johnson.
The plant represents a more than $178 million investment by the Department of Energy and a supply chain investment of over $850 million.
Mayor Garnett Johnson stated that Syensqo chose Augusta-Richmond County to build North America’s biggest factory producing electric vehicle materials.
The project is part of the Biden-Harris Administration Workforce Hub City Initiative.
Syensqo was previously part of the Solvay Group and has been located in Augusta for more than two decades.
PVDF is a thermoplastic fluoropolymer that is used as a lithium-ion binder and separator coating in batteries, according to the Syensqo website.
The facility will produce Solef, Syensqo’s signature PVDF product, which enables EVs to go farther on each charge, extends battery life, and improves battery safety.
At full capacity, the new facility is forecast to produce enough PVDF for more than five million EV batteries per year.
The production volume represents 45% of expected PVDF demand required by 2030.
The facility will create a new and reliable supply for the rapidly growing U.S. EV battery market, as well as hundreds of jobs throughout the value chain.
Tom Perez, Senior Advisor and Assistant to the President and Director of the White House Office of Intergovernmental Affairs, joined Mayor Johnson and Syensqo officials at the groundbreaking.
The City of Augusta and the Augusta Economic Development Authority have worked to invest millions of dollars in tax savings on this project.
source_title: North America's largest EV battery material facility coming to Augusta - Augusta Good News

facts: Solvay has been selected for a $178.2 million U.S. Department of Energy grant to help its Augusta site contribute to shoring up the American electric-vehicle battery supply chain.
The grant is still subject to approval by the Solvay board of directors.
The grant would help the company construct a production line for polyvinylidene fluoride, or PVDF, a polymer used as a binder and coating for the separator in lithium-ion batteries.
PVDF is also used in semiconductors and other applications.
Solvay says most top producers of EV batteries already use the material.
The new plant could eventually produce enough to serve 5 million cars annually, which is about 10 times the number of pure EVs sold in the U.S. this year.
To meet increased demand globally, Solvay has already ramped up PVDF production in France and China.

Mike Finelli, president of Solvay Growth Platforms and Chief North American Officer, said the company is proud to receive the federal government’s support for manufacturing technologies that will provide the U.S. with critical raw materials for building an independent, sustainable EV value chain.
The quote was circulated by the office of U.S. Sen. Raphael Warnock, who lobbied Energy Secretary Jennifer Granholm for the grant.
DOE terms require the company to put down matching funds, meaning the total capital investment would be $356.4 million.
The project would create 500 temporary construction jobs and 100 permanent manufacturing jobs.
The DOE said if carried out, the project could lead to a further 500 indirect jobs created across the value chain.
Solvay aims to boost local STEM education among disadvantaged populations and prioritize these groups for recruitment at the Augusta site.
The grants were distributed to 21 projects around the country using $2.8 billion from the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law that passed in November 2021.
Solvay was the only project designated in Georgia, along with a few others in Tennessee and other Southern states.
This is the third major project in Augusta related to the electric-vehicle value chain within the last year.
Germany’s Aurubis is investing $340 million in a recycled copper smelter in Augusta.
Japan’s Denkai is putting $430 million into a copper foil facility in Augusta.
Solvay bought the Augusta facility from BP-Amoco in 2000 and has invested steadily in the community, including an $85 million expansion announced in 2015.
Global Atlanta visited the Augusta facility in 2019.
The Augusta plant is one of two major facilities run by Solvay Specialty Polymers USA LLC in Georgia.

During a historic trade mission to Atlanta in June, Princess Astrid of Belgium helped inaugurate the company’s new innovation lab and 3D printing line in Alpharetta during her first company visit on a packed two-day itinerary.
source_title: Solvay Gets $178M Grant to Make EV Battery Materials in Augusta  - Global Atlanta

facts: South Korea's Hanwha is one of the world's largest and most diversified manufacturing companies.
Hanwha made its name in the 1950s as an explosives manufacturer before expanding into sectors including aerospace, shipbuilding, machinery, and energy.
Clean energy is now one of Hanwha's biggest business focuses.
Qcells is a subsidiary of Hanwha and a globally renowned manufacturer of solar panels.
Qcells products are ranked as the most popular solar panels in the US by Wood Mackenzie.
Prior to the new facility, Qcells had no operational base in the US and shipped products from the Korean Peninsula.
