Qcells' newly opened production plant in Cartersville, Georgia, marks the company's first physical footprint in America.
Energy thinktank Ember reported on 10 June 2026 that solar surpassed coal in US power generation in May, marking the first time this occurred in a monthly reading.
Solar accounted for a record 12.8% of US electricity output in May 2026.
Coal's share slipped to 12.2% in May 2026, marking its fourth-lowest monthly share on record.
This shift occurred despite the Trump administration's active promotion of fossil fuel energy sources.
Qcells has been owned by South Korean conglomerate Hanwha since 2012.

The start of solar cell production at the Cartersville, Georgia facility marks a major step toward completing what Qcells describes as the only vertically integrated solar manufacturing plant in the US.
Hanwha is targeting rapid scale-up at the Cartersville site.
By Q3 2026, the Cartersville site is expected to produce 3.3GW each of ingots, wafers, and cells annually.
By Q3 2026, the Cartersville site is expected to produce 3.5GW of modules annually.
These production figures would make the Cartersville site the largest solar cell factory in US history.
Module assembly at the Cartersville site is already running at full capacity, producing 16,700 panels each day.
Andy Park, Global CEO of Qcells, stated that producing the first solar cells at Cartersville is a milestone for Qcells and American manufacturing.
Andy Park noted that as ingot, wafer, and cell lines reach full capacity, major solar panel components will be made in Georgia.
Andy Park emphasized that a dependable domestic supply chain creates thousands of good-paying jobs and gives customers greater certainty on price, supply, tariffs, and product trust.
Qcells has maintained a manufacturing presence in Georgia since opening its site in Dalton in 2019.
The Dalton campus tripled its module capacity to 5.1GW in late 2023.
Combined with the Cartersville plant, total module capacity across Qcells' Georgia operations is expected to reach 8.6GW per year.
This combined capacity is equivalent to 47,000 panels per day.
The combined output is sufficient to power approximately 1.3 million US homes annually.
The two Georgia sites are expected to support nearly 4,000 jobs total.
An estimated 3,800 of these roles will be direct positions.
The energy policy environment adds complexity to Qcells' position due to President Trump's open criticism of solar power and efforts to dismantle clean energy tax credits.
The Trump administration issued an executive order titled 'Ending Market Distorting Subsidies for Unreliable, Foreign Controlled Energy Sources'.
The executive order stated that the federal government has forced taxpayers to subsidize expensive and unreliable energy sources like wind and solar.
The executive order claimed reliance on green subsidies threatens national security by making the US dependent on supply chains controlled by foreign adversaries.
Qcells appears to have avoided the worst impacts of the Trump administration's subsidy reforms due to its fully domestic manufacturing footprint.
The company qualifies to claim the Section 45X Advanced Manufacturing Production Tax Credit at every stage of production, from ingots and wafers through to cells and modules.
This qualification position is currently unreplicated by any other US solar manufacturer.
Qcells states its integrated domestic model offers customers insulation from tariff volatility and international supply disruption by keeping major solar supply chain components within US borders.
source_title: What Qcells' First US Plant Says About Trump's View on Solar | Energy Digital

facts: Qcells has introduced its first domestically assembled residential battery energy storage system (BESS) to the US market.
The announcement for the BESS was made on 7 May.
The system is assembled at Jabil’s Auburn Hills facility in Michigan.
The BESS is a domestic content eligible version of Qcells’ third-generation residential ESS, known as ‘Q.HOME CORE G3’.
The system uses lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells and has a storage capacity ranging from 13.1kWh-26.2kWh.
Qcells was originally a German solar manufacturer.
Qcells was purchased out of bankruptcy in 2012 by South Korea’s Hanwha Group after Chinese PV cells flooded the market, effectively ending the European industry.

In addition to home energy and energy management solutions, Qcells develops, invests in, and delivers PV and energy storage projects through its engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) and development businesses in the US.
In February, LG ES Vertech announced a multi-year partnership with Qcells for utility-scale projects.
Under the partnership, LG ES Vertech will provide 5GWh of US-manufactured energy storage products and lifecycle services to Qcells to support its EPC projects from 2028-2030.
Qcells stated that while some sites may feature integrated Qcells solar PV equipment alongside LG ES Vertech battery storage, they won’t necessarily always integrate both on the same projects.
The announcement builds on an existing collaboration where Jabil assembles Qcells microinverters in Michigan for Qcells’ AC solar PV module products.
To date, Qcells and Hanwha claim to have invested a total of US$17 million in residential solar-plus-storage manufacturing in Michigan.
The collaboration reflects Qcells' commitment to onshoring its renewables supply chain.
In Georgia, Qcells has invested nearly US$3 billion in building a US solar supply chain.
Qcells is closing in on completing the construction of a factory in Cartersville, Georgia that will build critical solar components – ingots, wafers, cells and modules – under one roof.
Q.HOME CORE G3 pairs with Qcells’ AC module products (Q.PEAK DUO AC and Q.TRON AC), which are both assembled in Georgia, as well as integrated microinverters manufactured in Michigan.
The Biden administration moved to strengthen domestic supply chains through investment incentives and tariffs on Chinese batteries.
The Trump administration altered production tax credit (PTC) and investment tax credit (ITC) eligibility rules last year.

HR 1, the ‘One, Big, Beautiful Bill Act’ (OBBBA), imposed limits on material assistance from foreign entities of concern (FEOC), namely Chinese or Chinese-owned firms, for companies seeking PTC and ITC benefits.
This policy shift has created an opportunity for South Korean battery makers like LG ES, Samsung SDI, and SK On, which had already committed to US EV battery production facilities.
In March 2025, Nofar USA and Qcells signed an agreement to develop and construct two BESS projects in Texas totalling 350MW/700MWh.
source_title: Qcells partners Jabil in US for domestic content residential batteries - Energy-Storage.News

facts: Hanwha Advanced Materials Georgia (HAGA) will build a new advanced materials manufacturing facility in Bartow County, Georgia.
HAGA will supply Qcells with Encapsulant film, materials used to encapsulate solar cells and ensure long-term panel durability.
This $147 million investment is expected to create more than 160 new jobs.
The investment is critical to supporting Qcells’ plans to develop a fully integrated solar supply chain in America.
HAGA will be the only company in the United States manufacturing solar EVA.

Inhwan Kim, CEO of Hanwha Advanced Materials, stated that their products are an important piece of the clean energy supply chain puzzle and building cutting-edge advanced materials in Georgia will create new careers in solar and help bring more affordable, reliable clean energy to customers across the country.
Justin Lee, CEO of Qcells, stated that Qcells is doubling down on building a complete, domestic solar supply chain and this recent investment is critical to making that happen.
Justin Lee noted that working with Hanwha Advanced Materials will allow customers to confidently know that the solar they buy from Qcells was made right here in America.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia is leading the nation in attracting next generation jobs.
Governor Kemp noted that since welcoming Qcells to Georgia in 2018, the state has announced more than 4,000 related jobs for Georgians.
Governor Kemp expressed pride that Hanwha Advanced Materials is adding to that growing number as it becomes a valued member of the Bartow County community.
Senator Jon Ossoff stated that his solar manufacturing law continues to deliver jobs, investment, and wealth for Georgians.
Senator Ossoff called this announcement a continuation of the largest clean energy manufacturing investment in American history.

U.S. Senator Reverend Warnock stated that Georgians are reaping the economic benefits of the Inflation Reduction Act that has helped pave the way for advanced manufacturing companies like Qcells to invest and expand in Georgia.
Senator Warnock expressed looking forward to continuing to partner with Qcells as Georgia becomes the premier destination for 21st century jobs.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that Hanwha Advanced Materials provides key components for the solar supply chain in Georgia, supporting the state’s commitment to making energy solutions more affordable.
Commissioner Wilson noted that over the past five years, clean-tech jobs have been one of the fastest growing manufacturing sectors in Georgia.
Commissioner Wilson expressed excitement that Hanwha Advanced Materials will add another crucial part of the solar supply chain to Georgia’s robust ecosystem.
Cartersville Mayor Matt Santini stated that the community is thrilled to be adding another Hanwha affiliate to its growing family of industries.
Mayor Santini noted their investment brings quality jobs to the community and diversifies the local mix of industries in Cartersville and Bartow County.
Mayor Santini mentioned that recruiting world-class companies was a goal from the beginning, which this helps accomplish.
Mayor Santini noted it is important to highlight that this is another clean energy company coming to the community, illustrating the growth and importance of that market.

This announcement comes only two months after Qcells revealed plans to invest an historic $2.5 billion into building ingot, wafers, and cells as well as expanding solar panel manufacturing in Georgia.
Qcells recently announced plans to buy polysilicon from REC Silicon, which is using clean energy to power its facility in Moses Lake, Washington.
Soon, Qcells products, from polysilicon to panel, will be sustainably made in America.
This investment comes after years of partnership with leaders in Georgia and following the passage of the Solar Energy Manufacturing for America Act (SEMA) within the Inflation Reduction Act.
The demand for American-made solar panels is increasing rapidly driven by efforts to ensure energy independence, lower energy costs, and create new careers in energy.
Qcells’ domestic manufacturing expansion will fulfill the growing need for these clean energy solutions and strengthen Georgia’s position as a global leader in solar.
HAGA will hire more than 160 full time jobs such as engineers, line operators before starting production in June 2024.
source_title: Qcells Supplier Expands Supply Chain in Georgia - Qcells North America

facts: Qcells announced the successful completion of its Dalton, Georgia, factory expansion on Oct. 18, 2023.
Qcells added 2 gigawatts (GW) of solar capacity to Dalton, bringing the full factory’s output to more than 5.1 GW.
The Qcells Dalton factory is the largest manufacturing plant of its kind in the Western Hemisphere.
It is the first solar panel plant expansion since the passage of the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA).
This addition marks the first phase of Qcells’ historic $2.5 billion investment announced in January 2023.
The completed Dalton expansion created 510 additional solar factory jobs.
The expansion will assemble two new solar products: the Q.TRON G2 residential solar panel and a bifacial panel for the commercial and utility markets.
Qcells’ Dalton factory will manufacture nearly 30,000 solar panels per day.
Beyond Dalton, the investment includes the development of a fully-integrated solar supply chain factory in Cartersville, Georgia.
The Cartersville factory will manufacture solar ingots, wafers, cells and finished panels.
By 2024, between the Dalton and Cartersville facilities, Qcells anticipates its solar production capacity to reach 8.4 GW a year.
This capacity is enough to power 1.3 million homes annually with clean energy.
Justin Lee, CEO of Qcells, stated that completing this factory marks the third expansion in Dalton and the beginning of building a fully integrated solar supply chain in America.
Justin Lee credited the Inflation Reduction Act and Georgia’s economic development team for making these plans possible.
Governor Brian Kemp stated Qcells chose Georgia because of unrivaled assets and a competitive package put together in 2019.
Governor Brian Kemp noted the milestone is a testament to the Dalton community's hard work and the state-level relationship with Qcells.
U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff stated he wrote and passed the Solar Energy Manufacturing for America Act with this goal in mind.
U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff thanked Qcells for continued investment in Georgia.
U.S. Senator Jon Ossoff noted Georgia is emerging as the advanced energy capital of the nation due to federal infrastructure and manufacturing policies benefiting it more than any other state.
Senator Reverend Warnock stated Georgians are seeing real-time benefits of the IRA and its clean energy provisions through Qcells' expansion.
Senator Reverend Warnock noted over 500 solar manufacturing jobs were added in the process.
Senator Reverend Warnock expressed a desire to continue partnering with Qcells as Georgia becomes the premier destination for solar manufacturing.
Qcells opened its first factory in Georgia in 2019 and hired 750 people to manufacture 1.7 GW of solar.
This initial investment was made possible in part by Section 201 tariffs imposed on solar cells.
Last year, Qcells announced a plan to add 1.4 GW to its manufacturing output and hire 535 more people.
The completed third expansion and investments across the full solar value chain follow the passage of the Solar Energy Manufacturing for America Act within the IRA.
Both new products (Q.TRON G2 and bifacial panels) will likely achieve an ecolabel known as EPEAT to help customers identify sustainably made products.
Upon completed construction, Qcells’ production in Georgia could avoid more than 12 million metric tons of CO2 equivalents per year.
By 2024, the Dalton facility alone will employ nearly 1,800 people.
By 2024, combined Dalton and Cartersville facilities will employ nearly 4,000 people and produce nearly 46,000 solar panels per day.
Qcells is headquartered in Seoul, South Korea (Global Executive HQ) and Thalheim, Germany (Technology & Innovation HQ).
Qcells operates diverse international manufacturing facilities in the U.S., Malaysia, China, and South Korea.
Qcells provides renewable electricity retail services to utility, commercial, governmental, and residential markets globally.
source_title: Qcells North America Completes Dalton Factory Expansion - Qcells North America

facts: Freyr is based in Norway.
Freyr was one of the first companies to jump into the US market with big battery plans due to the Inflation Reduction Act passed by Democrats in 2022.
The author interviewed Freyr’s CEO, Tom Jensen, about the plans and the Inflation Reduction Act.
Freyr announced that it is pulling the plug on a giant battery factory it had planned to build in Georgia.
The canceled project was a $2.6 billion factory named “Giga America” (or prematurely named Giga America).
The plant cancellation occurred right after Trump took office and said he was going to pull the plug on all of the renewable energy and electric vehicle policies Biden had a hand in.

Jason Pearce, Freyr’s senior vice president of business development, stated that climbing interest rates, falling battery prices, and a change in leadership at the company all contributed to the decision.
The Newnan Times-Herald reported on Jason Pearce's statement regarding the cancellation reasons.
Higher interest rates were used to get inflation under control following factors related to the COVID-19 pandemic and the Russian invasion of Ukraine.

Falling battery prices have dropped significantly in the past few years, making profitability an extra difficult challenge for battery companies trying to get a factory off the ground and profitable in the United States.
Freyr was planning to produce batteries for stationary storage battery projects, not EV batteries, at this facility.
The company letter making the announcement came on January 21, basically right after Trump took office.
Freyr is going to return all grants and incentives linked to the project that the company received from Georgia.
While the “Giga America” battery project is canceled, Freyr is now moving forward with a solar panel production facility in Texas.
The Texas solar panel facility is nearly complete and Freyr has just acquired it.
The solar panel factory will provide “a fast path to cash” for the company.
The company prefers generating cash from the solar facility over burning cash for years on a battery factory that might never break even.
Freyr reported a net loss of $27.5 million in the 3rd quarter of 2024.
Freyr reported a net loss of $83 million through the first three quarters of 2024.
Freyr's cash reserves (with no debt) were down to $184.1 million on September 30, 2024, from $275.7 million on September 30, 2023.
source_title: Freyr Battery Factory in Georgia Bites the Dust - CleanTechnica

facts: Ogeechee Riverkeeper Damon Mullis is seeking evidence that the Hyundai plant in Ellabell is polluting the Ogeechee River.
The Coastal Georgia Regional Water Planning Council met in early December to chart water management practices for future needs at the site.
Some of the more than 300 South Korean workers detained in an immigration raid at Hyundai's Georgia manufacturing site have returned to their U.S. jobs two months later.
A September immigration raid halted construction of a plant to produce batteries for electric vehicles.
HL-GA Battery Co. announced in a Thursday statement that construction work has resumed at the battery plant.
Didi Caldwell, president and CEO of Global Location Strategies, discussed potential business fallout from the immigration raid at Hyundai's southeast Georgia manufacturing facility.
A plane carrying more than 300 detained South Korean workers departed Atlanta around noon on Thursday bound for South Korea after being held at a southeast Georgia detention center.
South Korean officials announced on Sunday that they would send a plane to repatriate the detained workers, with the Foreign Minister expressing deep concern over the arrests.

A South Korean foreign ministry spokesman stated that the business activities of investors and the rights of nationals must not be unjustly infringed following the detention of about 300 South Koreans.
U.S. immigration officials reported that approximately 475 people were detained during the raid at the sprawling Georgia site where Hyundai manufactures electric vehicles.
South Korea expressed "concern and regret" on Friday over the raid that detained a significant number of South Korean nationals at Hyundai's Georgia site.
U.S. ICE spokesman Lindsay Williams stated the Thursday operation focused on the construction site of a separate factory for making EV batteries.
Hyundai released its annual corporate sustainability report in late June.
The 145-page report covers Hyundai's 2024 worldwide operations and highlights its Ellabell Metaplant across five sections, including biodiversity impact and renewable energy usage.
Hyundai recently opened a high-tech auto plant in Georgia.
The Georgia plant was originally designed solely for EV production and is now expanding toward plug-in hybrid manufacturing.
source_title: News Articles | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: T1 Energy, formerly known as Freyr Battery, announced plans to establish a $850 million, 5-gigawatt solar cell facility in Rockdale, Texas.
The upcoming factory will employ up to 1,800 manufacturing jobs.
The site will house eight lines with a mix of 210RN and 210N silicon wafers for the solar cells.
T1 Energy aims to begin construction in Q2 or Q3.
Chairman and CEO Daniel Barcelo said production is anticipated to begin in Q4 2026.
The Rockdale plant advances T1 Energy’s new strategy to shift focus to solar energy, launched in November 2024 alongside the initial announcement of the facility.
Chairman and CEO Daniel Barcelo stated that demand for these solutions in the U.S. is expected to continue growing.

Barcelo noted that the emergence of power-intensive industries such as AI and cryptocurrency, and the electrification of the transportation sector will necessitate significant investment in the U.S. grid.
He added that solar and battery storage is emerging as a preferred solution to provide low-carbon reliable, cost-competitive power while enhancing grid reliability.
At the time of the strategy launch, the company operated as Freyr Battery and was mid-construction on a $2.57 billion battery cell manufacturing plant in Newnan, Georgia.
The company axed those plans last month and sold the land for net proceeds of $22.5 million.
Around the same time, the manufacturer underwent a full makeover with a new name, logo and ticker symbol as T1 Energy.
The changes include relocating the company’s headquarters, for the second time in just over a year.
The company was originally based in Luxembourg and moved its headquarters to Newnan, Georgia, in January 2024.
Now, the company has made Austin, Texas, its home, according to a Feb. 10 press release.
Barcelo stated that their corporate transformation positions them to build on their U.S. solar manufacturing position and leverage their team’s expertise in the battery market.

He added that solar and battery storage developments are among the fastest-growing resources of electricity supply in the U.S. based on declining costs and the speed with which customers can bring projects online.
Texas has been heavily involved in T1 Energy’s shifting focus on the U.S. solar and battery storage sector.
Barcelo said the Rockdale plant is expected to be T1 Energy’s earnings and cash flow engine.
He added that it’s “very important” that T1 Energy begins construction on the facility midway through the year to begin production by the end of 2026.
The site is the company’s “second piece” of its “American-made supply chain.”
The first piece is a 5-GW solar module manufacturing facility in Wilmer, Texas, which T1 Energy acquired last year from Trina Solar.
Barcelo stated that when they have domestic cells and modules, the combination will enable them to be a very important part of the U.S. supply chain for solar.

Jaime Gualy, EVP of corporate development, said the Wilmer plant gives T1 Energy an “American manufacturing platform” to maximize domestic production, with the Rockdale plant as a key step to achieving that goal.
While the Rockdale site is being built, the company plans to pursue U.S.-based component suppliers in an effort to help customers qualify for Inflation Reduction Act tax incentives.
The Wilmer plant’s output production is ahead of schedule.
For the first two months of 2025, the facility exceeded T1 Energy’s forecast by nearly 50%.

Barcelo stated that with four lines already on production and construction installation activities expected to be complete during the first half of 2025, they are on track to achieve their full year 2025 production target of 3.4 gigawatts at the Wilmer facility.
source_title: T1 Energy to establish $850M solar cell facility in Texas | Manufacturing Dive

facts: T1 Energy is building domestic solar & battery supply chains to invigorate America with scalable, reliable, and low cost energy.
America needs advanced manufacturing capacity to unlock its most scalable energy resources.
T1 delivers durable solar components that efficiently convert sunlight into electricity.
Solar modules are made of highly abundant materials such as silicon, glass and aluminum.
T1 Energy is building a domestic solar supply chain.
The company utilizes the latest generation of industrial scale PV tech designed to maximize efficiency and longevity.
Silicon nitride coating protects the cell from moisture and increases light harvesting efficiency.
Atomic-layer alumina front-passivation slashes surface recombination for ultra-high efficiency.
A thin boron doped polysilicon region is used to increase the harvesting efficiency of higher energy photons.
Ultra-pure single crystal silicon with excess negative charge from phosphorus delivers high lifetime and light induced degradation free power.
A nanometer scale SiOX barrier blocks recombination while letting electrons quantum tunnel.
A conductive layer is deposited on the tunnel oxide to selectively collect charge carriers.
Screen-printed Silver/Aluminum busbars gather current with minimal shading.
T1 Energy is an Austin-based manufacturer building the energy future.
source_title: T1 Energy | Advanced American Solar & Battery Manufacturing

entity_name: Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) / eVTOL Development in Georgia
facts: At least 20 states, including Georgia, are starting to explore Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) technology.
Current investment in AAM technology is more than $8 billion.
AAM technology investment is expected to grow to $45 billion by 2030.
Clement Solomon serves as the director of the Georgia Department of Transportation’s Intermodal Division.
The Federal Aviation Administration is expected to certify the first electric vertical take-off and landing vehicles (eVTOLs) late this year or early in 2025.
Georgia Commissioner of Transportation Russell McMurry noted that while the technology is futuristic, there has already been activity in Georgia with these types of AAMs.
California-based Archer Aviation is building a manufacturing plant adjacent to the Covington Municipal Airport in Newton County.
The Archer Aviation manufacturing plant will be 350,000 square feet.
The Archer Aviation manufacturing plant will produce up to 650 eVTOLs each year.
Augusta-based private air charter operator Bravo Air is working with Japan’s SkyDrive to begin flying passengers on short trips aboard eVTOLs.
SkyDrive has begun building the eVTOLs through a joint venture with Suzuki Motor Corp.
AAM technology focuses on transporting passengers and cargo on short trips at low altitudes.
eVTOLs fly at altitudes of 1,500 feet to 4,500 feet.
eVTOLs are capable of transporting four to six passengers up to 250 miles.
The current operational concept for AAM is described as an "Air Uber".
Freight potential for AAM technology includes carrying low-volume, high-value cargo such as computer chips, medical equipment, or human organs needed for transplant.
Because they are electric, eVTOLs are quieter and more environmentally friendly than traditional combustion engine aircraft.

State Transportation Board member Cathy Williams, representing Georgia’s 2nd Congressional District, noted that traditional airport operations in Columbus create a lot of noise and eVTOLs would not exacerbate that problem.
The Georgia DOT formed a working group last year to assess the potential for AAM technology in Georgia.
The Georgia DOT working group included representatives of airports, municipal planning organizations, and the academic and business communities.
A study by the working group found that nearly all of Georgia’s airports are compatible for eVTOLs and have the capacity to support AAM.
A network of eVTOL chargers needs to be built at a cost of $500,000 to $750,000 each.
Currently, the only eVTOL chargers in Georgia are located in Augusta and Valdosta.
The goals of the Georgia DOT study were to set the stage for rolling out AAM technology in the next three to five years.
eVTOLs are expected to begin commercial operations between 2026 and 2030.

Archer Aviation announced an agreement with the Abu Dhabi Investment Office last month to begin air taxi operations using its four-passenger Midnight eVTOL model in the United Arab Emirates as soon as next year.
Adam Goldstein is the founder and CEO of Archer Aviation.
SkyDrive’s agreement with Bravo Air calls for developing infrastructure to support a network of eVTOL air-taxi services that will include Augusta Regional Airport.
Bravo Air is buying five three-passenger eVTOL models from SkyDrive.
Bravo Air aims to compete for certification to begin operations during the Osaka World Expo next year.
Dann Standard serves as the vice president of operations at Bravo Air.
source_title: Electric flying vehicles about to become reality in Georgia - The Current

facts: Archer Aviation plans to invest $118 million to build a plant near Atlanta, Georgia, to manufacture small electric aircraft, potentially creating up to 1,000 jobs.
The California-based company will construct the plant near Atlanta, eventually hiring up to 1,000 people.
Archer Aviation is based in Santa Clara, California.
Archer plans to build its aircraft adjoining an airport in Covington, Georgia.
Archer's plan involves a battery-powered vertical takeoff and landing craft with six propellers, holding four passengers and a pilot.
The propellers would pivot allowing the aircraft to take off and land like a helicopter and fly like a plane.

United Airlines last week said it would fly the craft from downtown Manhattan to United's hub at Newark Liberty International Airport in New Jersey, cutting a trip that can take an hour by car in congested traffic to 10 minutes.
United earlier put down a $10 million deposit to buy 100 aircraft from Archer for $1 billion.
United later put down a $15 million deposit for 200 aircraft from another company.
After building a prototype weighing 3,330 pounds (1,510 kilograms), the company plans to unveil a production model dubbed "Midnight" on Wednesday, aiming for it to enter service in 2025.
The U.S. Air Force is also evaluating the company's aircraft for possible use, Archer has said.
Archer is still seeking Federal Aviation Administration certification, but says it could get it in 2024.
The company says it wants to produce 650 aircraft per year at the Georgia site, possibly scaling up to 2,300 per year.
Automotive firm Stellantis, owner of Dodge, Chrysler, Jeep and Ram, is providing manufacturing and engineering help.

Archer said it expects to get a roughly $40 million incentive package from Georgia and local governments, including the donation of 96 acres (39 hectares) of land at Covington Municipal Airport, a cash grant from the state, and other tax incentives.
Those incentives would often include a property tax break as well as a job tax credit that could be worth $15 million to Archer over five years, as long as employees make at least $31,300.
The company said it chose the Georgia location for locally available workers, the ability to conduct test flights, affordable construction costs and good highway, rail and air connections.

"Our eVTOL technology can transform how urban and rural communities live and commute and this factory can create pathways to highly skilled manufacturing jobs and other ladders of social and economic opportunity," Archer CEO Adam Goldstein said in a statement.
Archer said it expected to borrow money from Georgia-based Synovus Financial Corp. for the plant's construction, which is expected to begin and conclude in 2024.
The company went public last year, raising about $850 million.
It lost $91 million in the quarter ended Sept. 30, reporting $600 million in cash on hand.
Georgia economic development officials have been focusing on recruiting electric car companies, landing major plants from Rivian Automotive and Hyundai Motor Group last year.
Norwegian battery startup Freyr announced a $2.6 billion plant in Georgia on Friday, joining an already-constructed electric battery plant owned by South Korean firm SK Innovation.

Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson said economic recruiters focused on aerospace had worked with Archer, saying technology and innovation continue to drive change across strategic industries.
source_title: Electric air taxi company Archer plans $118M Georgia factory

facts: Archer Aviation Inc. is building a manufacturing site in Georgia.

Archer has a complex business deal involving a high-volume order for its electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) air taxis and a partnership with the buyer to establish air-mobility services globally.

Andrew Cummins, director of Business Development at Archer, stated that the new agreement grows their prospective order book to nearly $6 billion and opens up Archer’s global reach to premium international markets.
Future Flight Global (FFG) and Archer have a memorandum of understanding for FFG to purchase up to 116 Archer Midnight aircraft, which could be worth up to $580 million.
The buyer has made an initial deposit on the purchase, and Archer and FFG are finalizing a definitive aircraft purchase agreement.
FFG is a company formed by executives at Titan Aviation, a business-jet operator, and has a joint venture to operate advanced air mobility assets globally.
The plan includes FFG operating selected routes within Archer’s planned air-taxi service and providing service in other high-demand locations, including Southeast Asia, Europe, and the Middle East.
FFG will deploy Midnight eVTOLs to provide on-demand transit to high-value clients such as businesses, governments, or individuals.
Archer and FFG will collaborate to mature the eVTOL commercial market in areas including ecosystem development, infrastructure, regulatory engagement, and demand generation.

FFG founder and CEO Karan Singh stated that they are partnering with Archer to provide tailored, flexible aviation services to top clients, noting the Midnight’s versatility for diverse routes and operational efficiency attractive to corporate clients.
Archer is one of several companies planning to produce electric-powered aircraft capable of hovering, taking off, and landing vertically.

The Archer Midnight eVTOL is powered by lithium-ion battery packs that provide 142 KWh of energy and a maximum power of 1,300 KW, capable of carrying four passengers and/or cargo over a range of 100 100 miles.
Commercial-scale production of the Archer Midnight is expected to start soon at a new manufacturing plant in Covington, Georgia.
Stellantis, a major investor in Archer, agreed to finance approximately $370 million of manufacturing labor costs as production ramps up to 650 aircraft per year.
The Stellantis financing will be provided in exchange for a quarterly distribution of Archer shares based on the labor costs incurred.
Stellantis will also contribute up to $20 million for initial manufacturing costs.
source_title: Big Order Anchors Big Plan for eVTOL Developer | Archer Aviation | American Machinist

facts: Rockefeller Group recently completed two distribution buildings totaling 427,655 square feet on 39 acres in Braselton, Ga.
Braselton 85- Phase II is located on Jesse Cronic Road and Highway 124 along the I-85 North corridor in the North Atlanta submarket.
The project broke ground in 2023.
It includes two rear-load distribution buildings totaling 207,009 and 220,646 square feet respectively.
Both buildings have 32’ clear ceiling heights.
Both buildings have 52’ x 50’ column spacing.
The site includes ample auto and trailer parking.
Braselton’s location along I-85 provides potential tenants with a distribution channel from Atlanta to Charlotte, bypassing metro-Atlanta traffic.
The company sold the smaller of the two buildings first.
In December 2025, the company sold the 220,646 square foot distribution center.
The business park is Rockefeller Group’s second development in Braselton.
Rockefeller Group completed and sold Braselton 85 in 2021, a 222,618-square-foot distribution center located one mile from the new project.
Braselton 85 was leased to Duckyang America Inc., a Korean automotive equipment supplier.
Braselton 85 was sold to Cabot Properties.
source_title: Braselton 85 - Rockefeller Group

facts: The facility is located in Braselton, GA, USA.
The sector for the facility is EVs.
The activity at the facility is manufacturing.
The product manufactured at the facility is EV parts.
The project announcement date is 5/20/2021.
The operating status of the facility is Operating.
The target jobs for the project are 285.
The current jobs at the facility are 285.
The capital investment for the facility is $10 million.
The facility is located in Congressional District GA9.
We currently have no government subsidies tracked for this site.
An AP News article from 20 May 2021 reports that the Korean firm will invest $10M and hire 285 workers to make battery parts.

facts: Georgia Power joined Governor Brian Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development to welcome Rivian, an electric sport utility vehicle and pickup company, to Georgia.
Rivian was named the 2022 MotorTrend Truck of the Year and selected Georgia for a nearly 13 million square foot plant for vehicle and battery operations along Interstate 20 near Social Circle.
The Rivian project brings an estimated 7,500 jobs and a $5 billion investment to Georgia.
The announcement highlights the success of targeted statewide economic development efforts such as the Electric Mobility Innovation Alliance (EMIA), which was launched in July by Governor Kemp.

The EMIA aims to bring together public and private sector leaders, researchers, and utilities like Georgia Power to ensure state recruitment efforts and regulations welcome the growing electric transportation and battery charging industry and workforce development opportunities.

Chris Womack, president, chairman and CEO of Georgia Power, stated that the announcement is a tremendous victory for the state and an example of the longstanding partnership between state government and the business community on bringing new jobs and growing the economy.
Womack noted that Georgia has a network of nearly 1,000 public charging stations from multiple partners available across the state to support electric vehicle investments.
Original equipment manufacturers in Georgia and throughout the Southeast region are shifting their product lines to keep up with increasing electric vehicle production.
Georgia Power supports the EV needs of several OEMs including Kia, Mercedes-Benz, BMW, and Volkswagen.

Other EV industry leaders that have moved to or expanded in Georgia include battery producer SK Innovation, EnChem, modules and storage systems producer Duckyang, charging station leader Heliox, and all-electric bus manufacturer Blue Bird.
Georgia Power continues proactive efforts with the State of Georgia to attract multibillion-dollar companies involved in auto manufacturing production, recycling, and EV supply chain development.

Georgia Power partnered with the University of Georgia's College of Engineering to create an e-Mobility Certificate program covering engineering, public policy, public health, and business acumen to prepare the workforce for an electrified transportation future.
Georgia Power is working closely with design students at Georgia Tech to evaluate the environmental benefits and future workforce opportunities of transportation electrification.

Earlier in 2021, Georgia Power joined Southern Company and utilities across the South, Midwest, Gulf Coast, Central Plains, and mid-Atlantic to enable seamless EV driver travel through a coordinated charging network.
The Electric Highway Coalition will enable increased charging options along major highway routes from Texas to Washington, DC to provide easier access to EV options for drivers.

links.context: Charging capabilities and electric transportation benefits

source_title: Georgia Power welcomes Rivian to Georgia - Dec 16, 2021

facts: Korea-based automotive manufacturer Duckyang opened its first U.S. manufacturing facility in Braselton, Georgia.
The new facility will supply electric mobility parts like battery modules, cockpit modules and energy storage systems to the electric vehicle market.
Dong-in Son is CEO of Duckyang Ind. Co. Ltd.

Dong-in Son stated that by entering the U.S. electric vehicle battery market with SKBA, they will have the opportunity to cooperate with many other automotive companies, including Hyundai and Kia Motors, that already have a manufacturing base in the U.S.
The new facility will encompass 230,000 square feet.
Georgia attracted $1.3 billion in foreign investments in 2020.
source_title: New Manufacturing Plants Announced in May 2021 - IndustrySelect®

facts: Georgia is consistently recognized by Area Development magazine as the top state for business for over a decade.
The Port of Brunswick ranks No. 1 in the U.S. for roll-on/roll-off cargo.
The Port of Savannah operates as the fastest-growing single-terminal gateway.
Georgia is home to 16 Fortune 500 companies.
Tourism fuels the state economy through visitor spending that supports local businesses, generates tax revenue, and sustains jobs statewide.
Georgia provides business development resources across its 12 vibrant regions.
source_title: Georgia Economic Development | #1 State for Business | Georgia

canonical_name: U.S. Department of Energy Alternative Fueling Station Locator EV Charging Infrastructure Trends
facts: The U.S. Department of Energy's Alternative Fueling Station Locator contains information on public and private non-residential alternative fueling stations in the United States and Canada.
The locator currently tracks ethanol (E85), biodiesel, renewable diesel, compressed natural gas, electric vehicle (EV) charging, hydrogen, liquefied natural gas, and propane stations.
EV charging continues to experience rapidly changing technology and growing infrastructure among tracked fuels.
Quarterly reports on EV charging infrastructure trends from the Alternative Fueling Station Locator provide snapshots of the state of EV charging infrastructure in the United States.
These reports break down the growth of public and private non-residential charging infrastructure by charging level, network, and location.

Beginning in the First Quarter 2023, these reports measure progress towards meeting projected public charging needs cited in the 2030 National Charging Network: Estimating U.S. Light-Duty Demand for Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure report.
These reports help transportation planners, policymakers, researchers, infrastructure developers, and other transportation stakeholders understand the rapidly changing landscape for EV charging.
In Q2 of 2024, there was a 6.3% increase in the number of EV charging ports in the Station Locator.
The Q2 2024 increase included a 6.5% increase in public ports and a 4.4% increase in private ports.
DC fast charging ports increased by the greatest percentage (7.4%) in Q2 2024.
The Northeast region had the largest increase in public charging in Q2 2024 (13.2%).
California continues to lead the country in the number of available public EV charging ports as of Q2 2024.
In Q1 of 2024, there was a 4.6% increase in the number of EV charging ports in the Station Locator.
The Q1 2024 increase included a 4.8% increase in public ports and a 3.2% increase in private ports.
DC fast charging ports increased by the greatest percentage (8.2%) in Q1 2024.
The Northeast region had the largest increase in public charging in Q1 2024 (6.9%).
California continues to lead the country in the number of available public EV charging ports as of Q1 2024.
In Q4 of 2023, there was a 5.0% increase in the number of EV charging ports in the Station Locator.
The Q4 2023 increase included a 5.2% increase in public ports and a 3.5% increase in private ports.
DC fast charging ports increased by the greatest percentage (9.2%) in Q4 2023.
The Southeast region had the largest increase in public charging in Q4 2023 (7.8%).
California continues to lead the country in the number of available public EV charging ports as of Q4 2023.
In Q3 of 2023, there was a 7.7% increase in the number of EV charging ports in the Station Locator.
The Q3 2023 increase included an 8.4% increase in public ports and a 2.8% increase in private ports.
Level 1 EV charging ports increased by the greatest percentage (15.5%) in Q3 2023.
The Northwest region had the largest increase in public charging in Q3 2023 (13.0%).
California continues to lead the country in the number of available public EV charging ports as of Q3 2023.
source_title: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure Trends

facts: Tennessee Gov. Bill Lee, Department of Economic and Community Development Commissioner Stuart McWhorter and Enchem America Inc. officials announced that the company will invest $152.5 million to establish electrolyte manufacturing operations in Brownsville, Tennessee.
The new facility is located at the I-40 Advantage Industrial Park, a Select Tennessee Certified Site.
The project will create 190 new jobs in Tier 4, At-Risk Haywood County.
The Brownsville facility will operate similarly to Enchem’s U.S. headquarters in Commerce, Georgia.
The facility will manufacture electrolytes for electric vehicle (EV) batteries as well as other EV battery-related services.
Enchem America Inc. is a subsidiary of South Korea-based Enchem Co., Ltd.
The company specializes in the development and manufacturing of electrolytes and other additives for rechargeable batteries.
With the additional Tennessee operations, Enchem will be able to further support its EV partners across the Southeast.
Since 2019, TNECD has supported more than 50 economic development projects in the Southwest Tennessee region, resulting in nearly 9,300 job commitments and $7.2 billion in capital investment.

Gov. Bill Lee stated that Enchem’s decision to invest more than $150 million in Tennessee is a testament to the state's unmatched business climate, highly skilled workforce, and position as a leader in the automotive industry, and thanked the company for creating 190 new jobs in Haywood County.

TNECD Commissioner Stuart McWhorter noted that South Korea is one of Tennessee’s top 10 foreign direct investment partners and that Enchem is boosting the EV supply chain across Tennessee by investing in an At-Risk county.

Jung Kang Oh, CEO and founder of Enchem Co. Ltd., expressed gratitude to Haywood County, the City of Brownsville, and professionals who assisted with the expansion, noting the remarkable growth of the EV market over the coming decade and confidence in capitalizing on it.

Haywood County Mayor David Livingston welcomed Enchem, stating the economic opportunity will help transform Haywood County and West Tennessee with high-paying jobs, and thanked Governor Lee and TNECD for their partnership.

Brownsville Mayor Bill Rawls stated that years of work and investment in I-40 Advantage Industrial Park fulfilled the goal of bringing high-quality job opportunities to the city and expressed a look forward to building an advanced manufacturing workforce.

John Bradley, TVA senior vice president of economic development, congratulated Enchem America and noted TVA's pride in partnering with HTL Advantage, Brownsville-Haywood County Industrial Development Corporation, City of Brownsville, and TNECD.

Sen. Page Walley (R-Savannah) welcomed Enchem America to Haywood County, stating the investment will boost the regional economy and establish the community as a hub of innovation in the rapidly growing industry.

Rep. Johnny Shaw (D-Bolivar) cited the more than $152 million investment as an example of unprecedented growth in Brownsville, Haywood County, and West Tennessee, noting that BlueOval City lit the fuse for this economic explosion.

The Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development’s mission is to develop strategies to make Tennessee the No. 1 location in the Southeast for high-quality jobs by attracting new corporate investment and facilitating expansion.
source_title: Governor Lee, Commissioner McWhorter Announce Enchem America Inc. to Locate US Manufacturing Operations in Brownsville

facts: Groundbreaking for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America's new manufacturing plant occurred on October 25, 2022, at the Bryan County Megasite in Georgia.
The facility represents a $5.54 billion investment announced by Hyundai Motor Group and its affiliate suppliers in May 2022.
HMGMA will create more than 8,100 jobs over the next few years to establish a stable supply chain for EV batteries and associated components in the U.S. market.
The new smart factory is expected to begin commercial production in the first half of 2025.
The facility will produce a diverse range of innovative, fully electric vehicles (EV) and include a new battery manufacturing facility.
A morning groundbreaking ceremony was held on-site, followed by an expo and program at Enmarket Arena in Savannah for hundreds of attendees.
Hyundai Motor Group announced $1.54 million total in corporate social responsibility contributions to support local communities in and around Bryan County.
Through Hyundai Hope and Genesis Gives, the company committed $120,000 to roughly ten organizations supporting local communities in and around Bryan County.
Hyundai donated $120,000 to Georgia Tech for STEM education scholarships.
Hyundai Hope On Wheels presented $500,000 in Scholar Hope and Young Investigator Grants to Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta.
The Genesis Inspiration Foundation presented $200,000 in donations to Georgia-based nonprofits Drawchange and Paint Love.
Kia’s "Accelerate the Good" program donated $600,000 to Family Promise of the Coastal Empire, America’s Second Harvest of Coastal Georgia, St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital, and Inspiredu.
Governor Brian P. Kemp stated: "Our partnership with Hyundai Motor Group and the groundbreaking of this innovative facility exemplifies that unprecedented success."

Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated: "With the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, we will continue to evolve beyond an automaker to the world’s leading mobility solutions provider."

Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority Chairman Carter Infinger highlighted that the project signifies a long-term commitment to the Savannah region beyond its investment and job creation.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson noted the Bryan County facility will directly create jobs for the Savannah area and support opportunities in the EV revolution statewide.
The Bryan County Megasite was selected partly due to its close proximity to the Port of Savannah.
Hyundai will access global markets quickly via rail and highway connections from the site to the Ports of Savannah and Brunswick.
Non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers connected to the project will invest approximately $1 billion in other locations around Georgia.

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the fourth round of grants from the Rural Site Development Initiative (RSDI) within the OneGeorgia Authority on June 29, 2026.
The fourth round of RSDI grants totals $7.8 million for ten local projects across Georgia.
Since the program's launch in FY 2025, the State of Georgia has invested a total of $28 million into rural site development projects.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that over 64 percent of new jobs created since he took office have gone to areas outside the metro Atlanta region, with RSDI being part of this broader effort to bring opportunity to all parts of Georgia.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones commended Governor Kemp for leading the program, which has awarded $28 million for critical projects in rural communities across Georgia.

House Speaker Jon Burns highlighted that the grants support good-paying jobs in hardworking Georgians, with specific investments noted in Southeast Georgia navigating unprecedented changes in the forestry industry.

RSDI grants are awarded for site improvements or to assist with costs of seeking Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) certification on approved projects critical to local economic development.
Communities receiving grants must match state funding at various levels based on program criteria.
Georgia Department of Community Affairs Commissioner Christopher Nunn stated that GRAD sites are important to local strategies for attracting employers and investment in Georgia’s communities.
Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that GRAD sites have helped attract an estimated 10,000 jobs to communities across the state over the last several years.

Effingham County Development Authority was awarded $1.8 million for site readiness improvements, including a sewer pump station, at the GRAD-certified Savannah Portside International Park, with total project costs of $2.5 million.
Development Authority of Columbus was awarded $2 million for clearing and grading site improvement work at the GRAD-certified Muscogee Technology Park, with total project costs of $10 million.

McIntosh County Industrial Development Authority was awarded $1.5 million for road, water, and sewer infrastructure improvements at the GRAD-certified Tidewaters Industrial Park, with total project costs of $2.8 million.
This is the second site development award for McIntosh County, which previously received a GRAD-Certification Scholarship for this grant.

Thomaston-Upson County Industrial Development Authority was awarded $2 million to construct water, sewer, and transportation infrastructure at the GRAD-certified Central Georgia Business and Technology Park, with total project costs of $2.3 million.
Albany-Dougherty Economic Development Commission was awarded $135,000 to certify the Pecan Grove Industrial Park, with total project costs of $180,000.
Glynn County Development Authority was awarded $107,500 for surveys and research to prepare the Mallard Industrial Park for GRAD certification, with total project costs of $119,500.
Development Authority of Harris County was awarded $54,400 to certify the largest remaining tract of the Northwest Harris Business Park, with total project costs of $77,500.
Development Authority of Jeffersonville & Twiggs County was awarded $22,500 for surveys and studies required to certify the Hillandale Tract, with total project costs of $25,000.
Screven County Development Authority was awarded $121,500 for studies and surveys necessary for the Screven Industrial Park to receive GRAD certification, with total project costs of $135,000.
Seminole County Development Authority was awarded $47,250 for assessments and surveys to GRAD certify the East Industrial Park, with total project costs of $52,500.
Eligible activities for grant funds include site studies, land grading, and other essential community infrastructure efforts supporting site development.
Grantees must demonstrate substantive plans for industrial site development while meeting OneGeorgia Authority eligibility requirements and applicable state laws and regulations.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) serves as the state’s sales and marketing arm and lead agency for attracting new business investment, encouraging industry expansion, and promoting the state for economic development.

The Georgia Department of Community Affairs (DCA) partners with communities to build strong commercial and industrial areas through community and economic development planning and local government assistance.

facts: Rowley Spring & Stamping Corp. has, for many years, been a leading supplier of spring and stamping components used in hundreds of products in various applications.
Since its inception in 1954, Rowley Spring & Stamping Corp. has grown from a small manufacturer with 13 employees and a dozen customers to 125 employees and over 700 customers.
The company operates under NAICS code 332 for Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing.
Rowley Spring & Stamping made a pledge in April 2014 as part of the Better Plants Challenge.
The company is located in Bristol and participates in the Better Buildings & Better Plants Initiative.

canonical_name: Alternative Fuels Data Center: Electric Vehicle Charging Stations
facts: Tens of thousands of electric vehicle (EV) charging stations are available in the United States.
These charging stations are being installed in key areas throughout the country for public and workplace charging as a supplement to residential charging.
Most EV owners do the majority of their charging at home.
Consumers and fleets considering electric vehicles need access to charging stations, which typically starts with charging at home or at fleet facilities.
Charging stations at workplaces and public destinations may help bolster market acceptance by offering more flexible charging opportunities at commonly visited locations.

The EVI-X Toolbox offers resources to estimate the charging infrastructure necessary to support typical daily travel in a given state or city, charging infrastructure needs to support long-distance travel (100 miles or more) along highway corridors in a given state or county, and to determine how EV charging will impact electricity demand.
The Alternative Fueling Station Locator allows users to search for public and private charging stations.
Quarterly reports on EV charging station trends show the growth of public and private charging and assess the current state of charging infrastructure in the United States.
The charging infrastructure industry has aligned with a common standard called the Open Charge Point Interface (OCPI) protocol, which uses specific terminology to describe charging infrastructure.
A Station Location represents a physical place with one or more EV charging ports, such as a parking garage or lot.
An EV Charging Port provides power to charge only one vehicle at a time even though it may have multiple connectors.
A Connector is what is plugged into a vehicle to charge it, and multiple connector types can be available on one EV charging port.

AC Level 1 equipment provides charging through a 120 volt (V) AC plug and typically comes with a portable cordset featuring a standard NEMA connector on one end and an SAE J1772 standard connector on the other.
Level 1 charging is typically used when only a 120 V outlet is available, such as while charging at home.
Eight hours of charging at 120 V can replenish about 40 miles of electric range for a mid-size EV.
As of 2023, less than 1% of public EV charging ports in the United States were Level 1.
AC Level 2 equipment offers charging through 240 V (typical in residential applications) or 208 V (typical in commercial applications) electrical service.
Most residential Level 2 chargers operate at up to 30 Amps, delivering 7.2 kW of power and require a dedicated 40-Amp circuit to comply with National Electric Code requirements in Article 625.
As of 2023, nearly 80% of public EV charging ports in the United States were Level 2.
All commercially available EVs in the United States have the ability to charge using Level 1 and Level 2 charging equipment.
Vehicles with a J3400 connector can use the connector for all charging levels, including Tesla's Level 2 Destination Chargers and chargers for home.
DC fast charging equipment enables rapid charging along heavy traffic corridors at installed stations at power outputs up to 500 kW.
As of 2023, more than 20% of public EV charging ports in the United States were DC fast chargers.

The availability of DC fast charging is expected to increase as a result of federal funding to build a national EV charging network, such as the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Formula Program, the national Alternative Fuel Corridors grant program, and the Charging and Fueling Infrastructure Grants.
DC fast charging is projected to increase due to fleets adopting medium- and heavy-duty EVs, as well as the installation of fast charging hubs for transportation network companies.
There are three types of DC fast charging systems depending on the vehicle charge port: SAE Combined Charging System (CCS), CHAdeMO, and J3400.
The CCS connector lets drivers use the same charge port with AC Level 1, Level 2, and DC fast charging equipment and has two additional bottom pins compared to the standard connector.
Most EV models on the market can charge using the CCS connector.
The CHAdeMO connector is another common DC fast connector type among Japanese automakers.
SAE International is standardizing the J3400 connector based on Tesla’s design for the NACS connector, which works for all charging levels including Tesla's Superchargers.
Several vehicle manufacturers have announced adopting the J3400 connector as early as 2025 to allow non-Tesla EVs to charge at Tesla stations.
Tesla vehicles do not have CCS or CHAdeMO charge ports but come with a limited adapter that supports charging up to 19.2 kW, and Tesla sells full power adapters for both connector types.
Another standard (SAE J3068) was developed in 2018 for higher rates of AC charging using three-phase power, targeting power levels between 6 kW and 130 kW.

The Megawatt Charging System (MCS) is under development for DC charging up to 3.75 MW for short-dwell as well as lower power long-dwell overnight charging for medium- and heavy-duty vehicle applications.
A 2022 report looks at the requirements for charging stations that could support in-route charging for heavy-duty EVs.
The U.S. Department of Energy's Transportation Technologies Office is pursuing research to bridge technology gaps associated with implementing these networks in the United States.
Many EVs on the roads today are not capable of charging at rates higher than 200 kW, but vehicle technology is advancing and most new EV models will be able to charge at higher rates.
Inductive charging equipment uses an electromagnetic field to transfer electricity to an EV without a cord and has been introduced commercially for installation as an aftermarket add-on.
The U.S. Department of Energy is conducting research to investigate the feasibility of high-powered wireless charging.

facts: Over the last three years, automotive companies continue to choose Georgia for their new locations, while current companies expand to meet demand.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America celebrated its grand opening in Georgia.

Euisun Chung, Executive Chair of Hyundai Motor Group, stated that Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America represents the Group’s advanced manufacturing capabilities and commitment to innovation, as well as their investment in relationships with partners and communities right here in Georgia.
The grand opening was reported by Hyundai newsroom on 3.27.2025.
Rivian is establishing its East Coast headquarters in Atlanta.
RJ Scaringe, Founder and CEO of Rivian, stated that Atlanta embodies top talent, exceptional creativity, and a desire to always be moving forward.
The Rivian East Coast office will accommodate 500 people.
The Rivian headquarters is intended to complement a new, state-of-the-art manufacturing facility in Georgia.
The Rivian expansion was announced via a Rivian Press Release on 7.72025.
source_title: Automotive in Georgia | Select Georgia

canonical_name: State of Georgia / Georgia Department of Economic Development
facts: Verescence North America is the only specialized manufacturer of glass perfume bottles in the United States.
The French company has over 120 years of industry experience and was one of the first glassmakers to establish U.S. production facilities in 1996.
Verescence operates two North American production facilities in Georgia: a glass bottle manufacturing facility in Covington and a decorative/printing facility in Sparta, Georgia.
Verescence partnered with Georgia Quick Start to develop customized training materials for new employees to safely operate equipment and work with molten glass.
Takeda’s facility is located at the GRAD (Georgia Ready Accelerated Development) certified Stanton Springs industrial park east of Atlanta.
Takeda’s $1.7 billion, more than 1 million-square-foot facility manufactures two types of plasma-derived therapies used to combat over 400 primary immune deficiency diseases.
Takeda co-founded the global CoVIg-19 Plasma Alliance and manufactured its investigational hyperimmune globulin medicine at the Georgia facility.

Ethicon, a subsidiary of Johnson & Johnson, operates a medical device facility in Cornelia, Georgia, founded in 1946 as a J&J plant and co-located with Janssen on a campus that has since expanded to include an Athens location.
Johnson & Johnson attributed its decision to expand in Georgia to a business-friendly environment and an available, skilled workforce.
Communities around metro Atlanta are investing in workforce and talent solutions such as Georgia Quick Start’s Georgia BioScience Training Center and collaboration opportunities.
The University of Georgia (UGA) broke ground in April 2024 on its new independent School of Medicine to expand existing talent pipelines and research.

UGA President Jere W. Morehead stated the new School of Medicine will expand the pool of medical professionals, attract top-tier scientists and researchers to the state, and produce more physicians for underserved and rural Georgia communities.

Creature Comforts Brewing Co. was founded in Athens in 2014 and operates three production facilities in revitalized industrial spaces, including the original location at the old Snow Tire Building and a second facility at the historic Southern Mill property in Athens.
Starbucks located an additional production facility in Augusta to produce at-home ground blends, K-Cup pods, and cold brew concentrates closer to new markets.
Starbucks cited reliable partners, power and utilities, and a trained workforce as key factors for its Augusta location.
Georgia offers more rail miles than any other state in the Southeast at 5,000 miles.
Georgia has 20,000 miles of federal and state highways connecting companies to top-ranked logistics assets.
Georgia’s connectivity through Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport and two deepwater ports enabled the state to move more than $186 billion in U.S. trade in 2023.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development’s International Relations team organized a 2024 International VIP Tour that stopped in Athens, Augusta, Covington, Oconee, and Monroe to engage the Consular Corps with existing industries and local communities.
source_title: Five Georgia Manufacturers Share Secrets to Success | Georgia

facts: Dongwon Autopart Technology plans to invest $30 million in a new production facility in Emanuel County.
The automotive project is anticipated to create 200 new jobs over the next several years.
Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the investment on February 04, 2026.
Dongwon Autopart Technology was established in 1971.
The company supplies components such as doorframes, seat frames, and battery frames to automotive original equipment manufacturers (OEMs).
Dongwon Autopart Technology supports more than 500 direct jobs in the Southeast.
The company maintains operational facilities in Hogansville, Georgia.
Dongwon Autopart Technology has been in the United States since 2003.
Construction on the new manufacturing facility will be located at the Hwy 297 Industrial Park near Swainsboro.
The company will hire for positions including management, system leads, and production personnel.
Hiring is expected to begin in 2026.
Representative Butch Parrish, Chairman of the House Rules Committee, stated that Emanuel County’s work alongside state partners is delivering jobs to Georgians in the region.
Will Donaldson serves as Chairman of the Emanuel County Development Authority.
Ken Warnock is the CEO of the Emanuel County Development Authority and noted this will be the single largest new economic development project for Emanuel County in over 30 years.
Alyce Thornhill, Director of Project Implementation and Supplier Strategy, represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on the project.
The GDEcD partnered with the Emanuel County Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start for this competitive project.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that the automotive industry is expanding outward along the I-16 corridor as suppliers choose strategic locations with access to OEMs and Georgia’s ports.
Dongwon Autopart Technology’s second location in Georgia demonstrates the robust growth of Georgia’s automotive industry, which has been creating jobs across the state for more than 100 years.

entity_name: Oregon Alternative Fuels Laws and Incentives
canonical_name: Oregon Alternative Fuels Laws and Incentives
facts: Compressed natural gas motor fuel is subject to the Oregon state fuel excise tax at $0.34 per 123.57 cubic feet.
Liquefied petroleum gas motor fuel is subject to the Oregon state fuel excise tax at $0.34 per 1.353 gallons.
Hydrogen motor fuel is subject to the Oregon state fuel excise tax at $0.34 per kilogram.
Liquefied natural gas motor fuel is subject to the Oregon state fuel excise tax at $0.34 per 1.71 gallons.

Beginning January 1, 2025, all new light-duty state agency fleet vehicle purchases or leases must be all-electric, plug-in hybrid electric, or hydrogen fuel cell vehicles to the maximum extent possible.
If purchasing or leasing zero-emission or hydrogen vehicles is not feasible, Oregon state agencies may purchase or lease light-duty alternative fuel vehicles or qualifying low-emission vehicles.
Each Oregon state agency must develop and report a greenhouse gas reduction baseline and annual reduction targets to the Oregon Department of Administrative Services (DAS).

Reports to DAS must include the number of purchases or leases of zero-emission vehicles, alternative fuel vehicles, and alternative fuel vehicle conversions, plus the quantity of each type of alternative fuel used annually by state agency fleets.

The Oregon Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ) administers the Oregon Clean Fuels Program, which requires fuel producers and importers to register, keep records of, and report the volumes and carbon intensities of fuels provided in Oregon.
Phase 2 of the Oregon Clean Fuels Program, implemented in 2016, requires fuel suppliers to reduce the carbon content of transportation fuels.
A 2022 goal requires reducing the carbon content of transportation fuels by 20% below 2015 levels by 2030, and 37% below 2015 levels by 2035.
The DEQ continues to update rule-making to achieve the Clean Fuels Program carbon reduction goals.
All-electric vehicle owners in Oregon must pay an annual fee of $115 or a per-mile road use fee of $0.02 per mile through the OReGo program.
Hybrid electric vehicles and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles must pay annual fees based on MPG rating: $20 for 0-19 mpg, $25 for 20-39 mpg, and $35 for 40 mpg or greater.
These vehicle efficiency fees are in addition to standard registration fees.
Drivers with EVs or vehicles rated over 40 mpg are exempt from additional registration fees if they enroll in the OReGo program.

Oregon joined California, Colorado, Connecticut, District of Columbia, Hawaii, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, Nevada, New Jersey, New York, North Carolina, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, Vermont, Virginia, and Washington in signing a memorandum of understanding to support medium- and heavy-duty zero-emission vehicle deployment through a Multi-State ZEV Task Force.
In July 2022, the Multi-State ZEV Task Force published an action plan to support electrification of medium- and heavy-duty vehicles.
The multi-state action plan includes strategies to limit all new medium- and heavy-duty vehicle sales in signatory states to zero-emission vehicles by 2050.
The action plan aims to accelerate medium- and heavy-duty ZEV deployment and ensure deployment benefits disadvantaged communities.
Oregon has adopted California Advanced Clean Trucks requirements requiring manufacturers to meet zero tailpipe emission production and sales requirements.
Beginning with model year 2025, manufacturers will be required to sell zero-emission trucks as an increasing percentage of their annual sales for Class 2b through Class 8 vehicles in Oregon.
Zero-emission vehicles under the requirement include all-electric and fuel cell electric vehicles.
Oregon will pursue transportation electrification as part of greenhouse gas emissions reduction targets of at least 45% below 1990 levels by 2035, and at least 80% below 1990 levels by 2050.
Select Oregon state agencies must report actions to reduce emissions annually.
The Oregon Department of Transportation published a statewide transportation electrification infrastructure analysis report with updates in August 2022.

Oregon joined California, Connecticut, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Jersey, New York, Rhode Island, and Vermont in signing a memorandum of understanding to support zero-emission vehicle deployment through a ZEV Program Implementation Task Force.

In May 2014, the ZEV Program Implementation Task Force published an action plan identifying 11 priority actions to deploy at least 3.3 million zero-emission vehicles and adequate fueling infrastructure within signatory states by 2025.
The 2014 ZEV Action Plan includes a research agenda to inform future actions.

On an annual basis, each signatory state must report on the number of registered zero-emission vehicles, public electric vehicle chargers and hydrogen fueling stations, and workplace fueling information.
In June 2018, the Task Force published a new ZEV Action Plan for 2018-2021 building on the 2014 plan.

The 2018 ZEV Action Plan makes recommendations in five priority areas: raising consumer awareness of electric vehicle technology, building reliable residential/workplace/public charging and fueling infrastructure, continuing and improving access to purchase and non-financial incentives, expanding public and private sector fleet adoption, and supporting dealership efforts to increase zero-emission vehicle sales.
The Oregon Department of Energy administers the Small-Scale Local Energy Loan Program, which offers low-interest loans for qualifying alternative fuel projects.
Eligible alternative fuel projects for the loan program include fuel production facilities, dedicated feedstock production, fueling infrastructure, and fleet vehicles.
Loan recipients must complete a loan application and pay a loan application fee.

The Oregon Department of Environmental Quality uses funds from the Volkswagen Environmental Mitigation Trust deposited in the Clean Diesel Engine Fund to award grants for at least 450 diesel school buses.
Eligible school buses must have at least three years of remaining useful life.

Grants cover 30% (up to $50,000) for purchasing a new bus or up to 100% of the cost to retrofit a bus with emissions-reducing parts or technology that reduce diesel particulate matter by at least 85%.
Any unexpended Clean Diesel Engine Fund money will fund grants for diesel engine emission reduction as matching funds under the Diesel Emissions Reduction Act program.

The Clean Vehicle Rebate Program provides rebates to Oregon residents, businesses, non-profit organizations, and government agencies for purchasing or leasing new electric vehicles, plug-in hybrid electric vehicles, electric motorcycles, or fuel cell electric vehicles.
Electric vehicles and fuel cell electric vehicles purchased between May 22, 2025, and September 8, 2025, with a battery capacity greater than 10 kilowatt-hours are eligible for a $2,500 rebate.
Electric vehicles and fuel cell electric vehicles with a battery capacity of less than 10 kilowatt-hours are eligible for a $1,500 rebate.
Electric motorcycles are eligible for a $750 rebate under the Clean Vehicle Rebate Program.
Eligible electric vehicles may not have an MSRP over $50,000, and eligible fuel cell electric vehicles may not have an MSRP over $60,000.

The Oregon Department of Environmental Quality administers the Zero-Emission Fueling Infrastructure Grant Program to support charging and fueling infrastructure for medium- and heavy-duty zero-emission vehicles.
Eligible applicants for the Zero-Emission Fueling Infrastructure Grants include public agencies, businesses, and Tribal governments.
The grants reimburse a portion of eligible project costs for designing and installing direct-current fast chargers and Level 2 chargers.
Priority for Zero-Emission Fueling Infrastructure Grants is given to projects in locations disproportionately affected by poor air quality.
