facts: Ford has rehired more than 300 veteran quality inspectors after its artificial intelligence systems failed to meet operational expectations.
Executives admitted the automated tools lacked necessary expertise, prompting a swift return to human oversight.
The U.S. automaker initially adopted the automated technology across some parts of its operations to conduct quality checks, cut costs, and increase productivity.
Corporate executives acknowledged that the automated systems lacked the specialized training and expertise of experienced human technicians.
Charles Poon, vice president of vehicle hardware engineering, stated that Ford incorrectly assumed introducing AI and design requirements would automatically produce high-quality vehicles.
The company had previously deployed 900 AI-powered cameras across its manufacturing plants to identify quality issues at the source and mitigate supply disruptions.
Management eventually determined that the technology was only as effective as the information used to train it.
Many knowledgeable engineers departed the company before their expertise could be integrated into the automated systems.
Ford has now reintroduced these veteran workers to train the machine learning tools and mentor younger employees.
This operational shift marks a reversal in recent corporate messaging regarding automation.
In June, Ford Chief Executive Officer Jim Farley predicted that AI would displace white-collar workers.
Chief Operating Officer Kumar Galhotra told investors in October that the firm was deploying AI across its entire industrial system.
Following the rehiring initiative, Ford returned to the top position among mainstream automakers in the U.S. JD Power Initial Quality Study.
The firm had not held this industry benchmark ranking since 2010.
A corporate press release attributed the improvement to a significant talent refresh.
This restructuring involved replacing senior leaders in engineering, supply chain, and manufacturing operations, as well as hiring 300 veteran engineers to guide the production process.
source_title: Ford Rehires Human Engineers After AI Systems Fail Quality Checks

facts: The state of Georgia and local governments are on track to give $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai Motor Group.
South Korean automaker Hyundai announced last month it will invest an additional $2 billion at an electric vehicle complex it is building in Georgia.
Projected incentives will rise by more than $290 million from the $1.8 billion deal originally announced last year.
Only $2.75 million of the incentive increase represents additional cash from the state, while the rest comes from increases in tax breaks.
The deal calls for Hyundai and battery maker LG Energy Solution to invest $7.6 billion in the Georgia plant.
The deal requires hiring 8,500 workers by the end of 2031, up from the original job projection of 8,100.
The sprawling electric vehicle and battery complex is being built in Ellabell, west of Savannah, in Bryan County.
It is the largest economic development deal in Georgia history and comes with the largest incentive package.
Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that benefits to Georgia outweigh the incentives.
Hyundai is projected to have a direct payroll of $4.7 billion over the next 10 years.
The company has promised to pay workers a yearly average of $58,105 plus benefits.

Pat Wilson said in a statement on Friday when the state released an amended incentive agreement: “As we work together to deliver a state-of-the-art facility in Bryan County that will provide well-paying jobs to Georgians, we know Hyundai Motor Group will give back to the region, investing in our schools, families, and communities, and we are grateful for those planned investments.”
Local officials released property tax projections to the Associated Press on Tuesday.
The Hyundai package has been described as the largest subsidy package a U.S. state has ever promised a single automotive plant, according to Good Jobs First.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the assembly plant through 2048.
Local governments have agreed to abate property taxes on the battery plant through 2049.
During the tax abatement period, Hyundai is projected to pay $523 million in taxes while saving $669 million.
The state projects it will waive an additional $81.8 million in sales taxes on construction materials and machinery.
This brings Hyundai’s savings from sales tax exemptions to more than $478 million.
The company is projected to receive an additional $10.5 million in state income tax credits at $5,250 per job over five years due to the increase in jobs.
This brings Hyundai’s state income tax savings to $223 million.
If Hyundai did not owe that much state corporate income tax, Georgia would instead give the company personal income taxes collected from Hyundai workers.
The state will spend an additional $2.75 million to help fund construction, machinery and equipment, boosting that total to nearly $53 million.
Unchanged parts of the deal include state and local governments spending more than $112 million to buy and prepare 2,913 acres (1,179 hectares) for the plant.
The state will spend $175 million on water and sewer facilities.
The state will spend $210 million on road construction and improvements.
The state will spend more than $153 million to recruit and train workers.
The deal requires Hyundai to pay back a portion of the incentives if the company falls below 80% of promised investment or employment.
Hyundai plans to start EV production in 2025, initially making 300,000 vehicles per year.
Production may possibly expand to 500,000 vehicles a year.
Since the company announced its first U.S. plant solely dedicated to electric vehicles, suppliers have pledged to invest $2.2 billion and hire 5,300 people.
The announcements are part of an electric vehicle and battery land rush across the United States.
Under the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act, EVs must be assembled in North America to qualify for a full $7,500 EV tax credit.
A certain percentage of battery parts and minerals must come from North America or a U.S. free trade partner to qualify for the full tax credit.
source_title: Hyundai could get another $290m in government aid | Fortune

facts: Kia used its April 9 investor day to reset EV targets and announce that Boston Dynamics Atlas humanoid robots will reach Kia AutoLand Georgia in 2029.
Hyundai Motor Group's first deployment of Atlas at HMGMA in Savannah is scheduled for 2028.
The timeline marks a shift for Atlas from a single showcase program into a broader rollout across the company's manufacturing footprint.
Kia raised its five-year investment plan during the investor day, identifying robotics, autonomous driving, and software-defined vehicles as core growth engines.
HMGMA will function as the first operating proving ground for Atlas deployment.
Kia AutoLand Georgia will serve as the second site for Atlas deployment.
Both plants are located in Georgia, which lowers friction for support, service, training, and cross-site learning between the facilities.
Hyundai plans to field-test Atlas in Savannah, refine task libraries, integration layers, and operator playbooks before moving proven capabilities to Kia's existing Georgia operation.
Kia's investor-day materials treated robotics as a core strategy rather than a side experiment.
Boston Dynamics positions Atlas as an enterprise-grade humanoid robot designed for material handling, machine tending, order building, and sequencing work.
The current Atlas product specifications include 56 degrees of freedom, a 4-hour battery life, a 50-kilogram instant lift capacity, and a 30-kilogram sustained lift capacity.
Atlas supports autonomous battery swapping, barcode scanning, and integration with enterprise systems through Boston Dynamics' Orbit software platform.
Reuters reported that Kia plans to invest more than $500 million in physical AI capabilities and vision-language-action models.
Boston Dynamics and Google DeepMind announced a January partnership focused on giving Atlas stronger perception, reasoning, and task-learning capability.
NVIDIA is involved in supporting the broader physical AI and autonomy stack for Kia's future manufacturing strategy.
Hyundai Motor Group has set an annual robot factory goal of 30,000 robots by 2028.
Reuters reported Kia's investment plan totals $28 billion for the period from 2026 to 2029.
The company's key investment statistic is listed as KRW 49 trillion for the period from 2026 to 2030.

Atlas deployment focuses on narrow industrial use cases rather than broad human-like ambition, targeting tasks that are structured enough to automate but varied enough that fixed automation struggles.

Plant-level deployment plans raise practical questions regarding initial task selection, robot-to-supervisor ratios per shift, safety zones around forklifts and personnel, and cost recovery through reduced cycle-time loss and fewer ergonomic incidents.
source_title: Kia Extends Boston Dynamics Atlas Rollout to a Second Georgia Plant | Biped.News

facts: Governor Brian Kemp, in conjunction with the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), announced sustained momentum for business recruitment and expansions in Fiscal Year 2024.
Between July 1, 2023, and June 30, 2024, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 429 facility expansions and new locations.
These efforts resulted in more than $20.3 billion in investment and the commitment of 26,900 new private sector jobs statewide.
83% of expansions and new locations are going to communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region.
The rural Georgia focus represents more than 18,300 new jobs and $12.5 billion in investment.
Governor Brian Kemp stated the state is committed to continuing momentum by investing in key areas like education, workforce development, and infrastructure.
Metro Atlanta attracted nearly $7.8 billion in investments as a logistics and business hub.
Statewide, new locations account for $12.8 billion in investments.
Expansions are responsible for 9,800 new jobs statewide.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones highlighted the need to ensure businesses have a necessary workforce, citing the Technical College System of Georgia, University System of Georgia, College and Career Academies, and private-public partnerships building the workforce pipeline.

Speaker of the House Jon Burns noted the House worked alongside Governor Kemp and Senate colleagues on economic development success, cutting taxes and regulations, and investing in education, workforce development, and infrastructure.
During FY2024, Governor and First Lady Kemp led economic development missions to Korea and Switzerland focusing on strengthening relationships with Hyundai Motor Group and its supplier network.
In the prior year, they led similar missions to Israel, France, and Switzerland.
Georgia landed more than 8,100 new jobs and $5.9 billion in investments from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S. market this past fiscal year.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated Georgia's strong partnerships and diverse industry landscape are major advantages for businesses and people.
The State of Georgia celebrated more than 50 years of continuous representation in Europe and Japan in FY2024, remaining among top sources of foreign direct investment.
The coming fiscal year will mark 40 years of continuous representation in Korea, a partnership that earned GDEcD the James A. Van Fleet Award from the Korea Society.
All markets where Georgia maintains representation accounted for 29% of total new jobs and investments in FY2024.
GDEcD Commissioner Kristi Brigman noted Georgia's story from agribusiness and manufacturing to mobility and technology, celebrating a decade of excellence and project milestones.
Manufacturing, including aerospace, automotive, and e-mobility sectors, accounted for over 50% of expansions or new locations in FY2024.
Kia Georgia added the EV9 to its lineup in Troup County.
Toyota Industries Corporation established a campus in Jackson County.
StandardAero operates in Richmond County.
International life sciences company Gerresheimer announced a recent expansion in Fayette County.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America suppliers announced new locations in Bryan, Candler, Chatham, Coweta, Effingham, Harris, and Laurens County.
SOLARCYCLE, an innovator in solar panel recycling, announced plans for a new location in Polk County to support solar panel manufacturer Qcells’ operations.
Investments in food processing and agribusiness totaled more than $1.3 billion in FY2024.
Japanese probiotic beverage company Yakult located in Bartow County.
Korean bakery CJ Foodville located in Hall County.
Walmart will source milk from local dairy farmers at a future dairy processing facility in Lowndes County.
Technology and corporate hubs are creating at least 4,400 new jobs as a result of wins in FY2024.
Cargill Corporation is establishing a new office hub in the City of Atlanta.
U.S. Soccer’s future headquarters and national training center will be in Fayette County.
Georgia earned the rank of No. 1 state for business from Area Development magazine for 10 consecutive years.
Georgia earned the No. 1 spot in Site Selection magazine’s annual Prosperity Cup rankings for successful investment recruitment in 2023.
Georgia was awarded a Gold Shovel by Area Development for achieving significant success in attracting new business investment and job creation in 2023.
Business Facilities magazine ranked Georgia as the No. 1 state for EV investments.
Business Facilities magazine ranked Georgia as a top 10 state for food processing, cybersecurity, aerospace and defense, and AI growth hubs.

links.context: View further details of state-announced economic development projects

source_title: Georgia Job Creation & Momentum Remains Strong - Rome CEO

facts: Hyundai Motor Group has started construction of its new electric car plant in the US state of Georgia.
The Bryan County plant is scheduled to begin production in the first half of 2025.
The plant will have an annual capacity of 300,000 Hyundai, Kia and Genesis electric cars and batteries.
Hyundai announced the plant in May and originally planned to start construction in early 2023.
Construction was broken ground in October because of new rules for electric car subsidies in the USA.
The planned start of operations remains in the first half of 2025.
Hyundai plans to invest just over $5.5 billion.
The project will create 8,100 jobs at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA), often just called Metaplant.
Georgia governor Brian P. Kemp attended the groundbreaking ceremony in Savannah.
Hyundai CEO Euisun Chung attended the ceremony.

Hyundai CEO Euisun Chung stated: “Today, our EVs are recognized as best in class, and with this partnership, we are determined to be the global leader in electrification, safety, quality, and sustainability.”
Hyundai CEO Euisun Chung added: “With the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, we will continue to evolve beyond an automaker to the world’s leading mobility solutions provider.”
The South Korean government and some EU countries disagree with the new US subsidy for electric vehicles, specifically the US value-added requirements for eligibility.
US President Joe Biden expressed willingness in October to “continue discussions with South Korea on the recent US legislation”.
Hyundai currently builds its E-GMP models such as the Ioniq 5 or the Ioniq 6 only in South Korea.
Some of Hyundai’s upcoming electric models are particularly targeted at the US market, such as the Hyundai Ioniq 7 and Kia EV9 large E-GMP SUVs.
According to earlier reports, the Hyundai Ioniq 7 and Kia EV9 will initially be built at the new Georgia plant.
If these vehicles were not eligible for the $7,500 tax credit, it would be a major competitive disadvantage.
source_title: Hyundai launches construction of EV factory in the USA - electrive.com

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solutions EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solutions Joint Venture
facts: Hyundai is investing over $12 billion in a record-setting economic development deal with the state of Georgia for an electric vehicle battery plant.
State officials say the $12.6 billion project is the largest economic development deal in Georgia’s history.
The facility is a joint venture by Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solutions.
The construction site is located in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah, and is part of Hyundai’s massive 3,000-acre Metaplant campus.
South Korea’s President Lee Jae-myung promised at least $50 billion of investments during a recent meeting with President Trump.
The South Korean government recently agreed to invest hundreds of billions more in the U.S. in exchange for lower tariffs.
Hyundai also has a factory in Alabama and plans to invest $5 billion in a steel plant in Louisiana.
Federal agents arrested nearly 500 workers at the facility on Thursday, marking the largest-ever single-site immigration raid in U.S. history.
Approximately 475 people were detained during the raid, which began around 9:00 or 10:00 a.m. and lasted until as late as 6:30 p.m.
Some 300 of the 475 arrested workers are Korean nationals who were building the electric vehicle battery plant.
Detained workers also included individuals from Guatemala, Colombia, Chile, Mexico, Ecuador, and Venezuela.
Some U.S. citizens, mostly younger workers, were initially subjected to investigatory detention before being released.
None of the detained workers have been charged, and many reportedly hold valid U.S. work permits or pending asylum applications.

Over 500 federal, state, and local officials participated in the raid, including agents from ICE, Border Patrol, the Department of Labor’s Office of Inspector General, the FBI, the DEA, ATF, the IRS, and the Georgia State Patrol.

The majority of detained workers were taken to the Folkston ICE Processing Center, a GEO Group-run facility in south Georgia near the Florida line that has operated as an ICE detention center since approximately 2017.
DHS Secretary Tom Homan stated that companies hiring undocumented workers undercut competition paying U.S. citizen salaries and drive wages down.

South Korean officials negotiated the release of detained workers, with Chief of Staff Kang Hun-sik confirming that a chartered plane would depart to repatriate them once administrative procedures were completed.

The South Korean government will work with its Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy and related companies to review and improve residency status and visa systems for travelers on U.S.-related projects.

Meredyth Yoon, litigation director for Asian Americans Advancing Justice-Atlanta, reported that heavily armed federal agents threatened workers, used tear gas, blocked facility exits, took phones, and pressured people to sign papers.
Witnesses described hundreds of people being arrested, shackled at their waist and ankles, and loaded into buses.
No criminal arrests of plant owners or company officials involved in the raid have been reported so far.
The raid is viewed by advocates as a disincentive to companies wanting to invest in the U.S., running counter to Georgia Governor Kemp’s efforts to attract South Korean economic development.
Local organizations are providing coordinated support for detained individuals, including food, financial assistance, and legal help.
President Trump is quietly preparing to travel to South Korea next month (October) for the APEC summit, where he could meet with Chinese leaders.
source_title: ICE Conducts Largest-Ever Raid at Georgia Hyundai Plant: 475 Arrested, Mostly Korean | Democracy Now!

facts: Governor Brian Kemp, in conjunction with the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), announced sustained momentum for business recruitment and expansions in Fiscal Year 2024.

Between July 1, 2023, and June 30, 2024, GDEcD’s Global Commerce team supported 429 facility expansions and new locations resulting in more than $20.3 billion in investment and the commitment of 26,900 new private sector jobs statewide.
83% of expansions and new locations are directed to communities outside the 10-county Atlanta region, representing more than $12.5 billion in investment and over 18,300 new jobs.
Metro Atlanta attracted nearly $7.8 billion in investments as a logistics and business hub during FY2024.
Statewide, new locations accounted for $12.8 billion in investments while expansions were responsible for 9,800 new jobs.

Lt. Governor Burt Jones highlighted the Technical College System of Georgia, University System of Georgia, College and Career Academies, and private-public partnerships as key builders of Georgia's workforce pipeline for expanding businesses.

Governor and First Lady Kemp led economic development missions to Korea and Switzerland during FY2024, focusing on strengthening relationships with key investors like Hyundai Motor Group and its supplier network.
Georgia landed more than 8,100 new jobs and $5.9 billion in investments from international companies expanding or locating in the U.S. market during the past fiscal year.
Manufacturing, including the aerospace, automotive, and e-mobility sectors, accounted for over 50% of expansions or new locations in FY2024.
Kia Georgia added the EV9 to its lineup at its facility in Troup County.
Toyota Industries Corporation established a campus in Jackson County.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America suppliers announced new locations in Bryan, Candler, Chatham, Coweta, Effingham, Harris, and Laurens County.
SOLARCYCLE, an innovator in solar panel recycling, announced plans for a new location in Polk County to support solar panel manufacturer Qcells’ operations.
Business Facilities magazine ranked Georgia as the No. 1 state for EV investments.
GDEcD Commissioner Kristi Brigman noted that FY24 saw achievements across mobility and technology sectors, celebrating project milestones across the state.
source_title: Georgia Job Creation & Momentum Remains Strong - Valdosta CEO

facts: Hyundai has begun producing electric SUVs in Georgia less than two years after breaking ground on its sprawling, $7.6 billion manufacturing plant west of Savannah.
Hyundai's factory in Georgia held an employee-focused celebration Thursday as its first EV for commercial sale rolled off the assembly line.
Bianca Johnson, spokesperson for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, provided a statement to The Associated Press confirming the production milestone.
HMGMA started initial production of customer vehicles ahead of schedule after validating its production processes to ensure vehicles meet Hyundai Motor Group's high quality standards.
A grand opening celebration at the Georgia plant is expected in the first quarter of 2025.
South Korean automaker Hyundai and battery partner LG Energy Solution plan to employ 8,500 total workers at the Bryan County site once the plant is fully operational.
The Bryan County site is located about 50 miles (80 kilometers) west of Savannah.
Hyundai plans to produce up to 300,000 EVs per year in Georgia, as well as the batteries that power them.
The plant's vehicle production areas have been completed and are being staffed by more than 1,000 workers.
The plant's battery-making facilities remain under construction.
The first vehicles being produced at the Georgia site are 2025 models of Hyundai's Ioniq 5 electric SUVs.
American-made Ioniq 5 EVs from this plant will arrive at U.S. dealerships before the end of this year.
During the first half of 2024, the Ioniq 5 was America's second-best-selling electric vehicle not made by industry leader Tesla.
Hyundai broke ground on its Georgia plant in late October 2022.
The project is the largest economic development project the state has ever seen and came with a whopping $2.1 billion in tax breaks and other incentives from the state and local governments.
Hyundai rushed to start making EVs in Georgia within two years of groundbreaking, spurred by federal electric vehicle incentives that reward domestic production.

The Inflation Reduction Act, passed in 2022 with provisions intended to combat climate change, includes a tax credit that saves EV buyers up to $7,500, but only on cars made in North America with domestic batteries.
Hyundai executives complained the law was unfair, but Hyundai President and Global Chief Operating Officer Jose Munoz said it caused the automaker to push to open sooner in Georgia.
source_title: Hyundai has begun producing electric SUVs at its $7.6 billion plant in Georgia

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is in talks with Georgia officials to build a new electric vehicle (EV) plant in the U.S. state of Georgia.
A Kia Corp. plant is currently operating in Georgia, and Hyundai Motor Co. operates a separate facility in neighboring Alabama.
Both the existing Kia plant in Georgia and the Hyundai plant in Alabama currently produce internal combustion engine vehicles.
EV models such as the Ioniq 5 and EV6 are currently manufactured in Korea and exported to the United States.
The United States is the third-largest EV market in the world, following China and Europe.
A total of 500,000 EVs were sold in the United States in 2021.
Hyundai stated it is excited to announce a new U.S. EV plant plan soon but has no details to share at this stage.
Projected investment costs and expected job creation numbers for the proposed Georgia facility are not immediately known.
Once completed, the new Georgia plant is expected to produce fully electric SUVs (IONIQ 7 and EV9) aimed at the U.S. market.
Following a mid- to long-term U.S. investment plan announced in May 2021, Hyundai Motor Group chairman Chung Eui-sun formed a task force to select a site for a U.S. EV plant.
During a U.S. business trip last month, experts predicted that Hyundai chairman Chung Eui-sun would finalize the plant site selection.
Hyundai Motor Group previously announced it would invest US$300 million to build an EV production line at its Alabama plant for the EV Genesis GV70 and Santa Fe hybrid.
Hyundai was working to announce its U.S. EV manufacturing investment later that month to coincide with President Biden's planned visit to Seoul.
source_title: Hyundai Motor in Talks with Georgia to Build EV Plant in the U.S. - World-Energy

facts: The North American battery joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group and SK On has been officially named HSBMA (Hyundai SK Battery Manufacturing America).
The joint venture was announced in April 2023.
The project has reached over 90% completion.
The plant is scheduled to begin commercial operations this year.
The plant is located in Bartow County, Georgia.
The plant will have an annual production capacity of 35GWh.
The 35GWh capacity is equivalent to batteries for approximately 300,000 electric vehicles.
Hyundai Motor Group and SK On have jointly invested a total of $5 billion in the venture.
Each company holds a 50% stake in the venture.
source_title: The North American battery joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group a - Shanghai Metals Market (SMM)

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution EV Battery Plant
canonical_name: Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Joint Venture EV Battery Plant
facts: Construction on a $1.1 billion plant to make batteries for electric vehicles (EVs) started in Indonesia's Karawang New Industry City, near the capital Jakarta, on Wednesday.
Indonesian President Joko Widodo spoke at a news briefing after the ground-breaking ceremony.
President Joko Widodo stated the plant is the first EV battery facility in Southeast Asia.
The facility is a joint venture by Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution.
President Widodo said the factory is a manifestation of the government's seriousness to tap its rich nickel reserves.
Indonesia holds 24% of the world's total nickel reserves.
President Widodo stated Indonesia will become the main producer of nickel-based goods, adding that the factory will attract investment.
The Indonesian government fully supports the development of the battery and electric vehicle industry ecosystem.
The Indonesian government continues to roll out structural reforms to ensure ease in doing business.

Jong-hyun Kim, president and CEO of LG Energy Solution, said today marks a significant step in starting a new era of the EV battery industry in Indonesia with the establishment of its first battery cell manufacturing plant in the country.
Through the joint venture partnership, the companies stated they are now a step closer to establishing the world’s first EV comprehensive supply chain.
According to a joint statement by the two companies, plant construction will be completed by the first half of 2023.
Mass production of battery cells in the new facility is expected to commence in the first half of 2024.
When fully operational, the facility is expected to annually produce 10-gigawatt hours (GWh) of NCMA (nickel-cobalt-manganese-aluminum) lithium-ion battery cells to power more than 150,000 EVs.
The facility will be ready to increase its production capacity up to 30 GWh to meet future growth.
source_title: Work begins on EV battery plant in Indonesia

facts: A federal immigration raid occurred at a Hyundai mega plant in Georgia on Thursday.
The raid resulted in the arrests of about 475 suspects.
Most of the detained suspects were Korean nationals.
The raid took place about 20 miles outside of Savannah, Georgia.
The operation was part of a long-term investigation and one of many notable worksite operations amid the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown.
Hyundai officials stated they do not believe any of their direct employees were arrested but said they would comply with all laws and regulations.
LG Energy Solution, which also plays a role in the plant, stated it will work with authorities.
The EV manufacturing part of the facility is not believed to have been impacted by the raid.
The battery factory under construction was the focus of the multi-agency effort.
Former President Joe Biden previously touted the project as a win for American manufacturing.
Both Democratic and Republican politicians have hailed the project as a win for American manufacturing.
Scientist Matt Van Swol posted on X criticizing Democrats regarding the project's political reception.
Maik Peters, CEO of Pitco Engineering, posted that American companies with legal workers are cut out of mega projects because they are too expensive.
The South Korean government expressed concerns about the operation targeting Hyundai's Georgia facility.

Writer James Surowiecki criticized the Trump administration for arresting South Koreans temporarily in the U.S. to help Hyundai set up the factory, despite Lutnick and Trump bragging about getting South Korea to invest billions in the U.S.
Rep. Nikema Williams, D-Ga., described the raid as an "act of cruelty and injustice" by the Trump administration.
Rep. Williams reported that U.S. citizens and lawful permanent residents have also been wrongfully detained at the site.
Rep. Williams criticized the administration for instilling fear and chaos within marginalized communities and demanded they follow due process and constitutional rights.
Rep. Buddy Carter, R-Ga., issued a statement blaming Democrats for enabling illegal immigration issues in the U.S. for far too long.
Rep. Carter applauded the Trump Administration and Operation Take Back America for taking bold action to put American workers first and protect communities from illegal immigration at the facility.
Rep. Carter stated that no one can take jobs from hardworking Americans and give them to illegal immigrants under Donald Trump’s watch.
source_title: Georgia Hyundai plant raid sparks political debate after 475 detained | Fox News

facts: A federal agency plans to reassess its environmental permit for Hyundai's $7.6 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia.
The reassessment follows a conservation group complaint that regulators failed to properly examine the factory's potential impacts on the area's water supply.

The Army Corps of Engineers stated in a Friday letter that state and local economic development agencies applied for the project's 2022 permit without mentioning Hyundai's plan to withdraw up to 6.6 million gallons (25 million liters) per day from an underground aquifer.
This underground aquifer is a major regional source of drinking water.
Details about the plant's water needs emerged earlier this year as the Georgia Environmental Protection Division considered a proposal for four new wells to supply the auto factory.
As a result, the Army Corps will revisit its prior finding that the project would have "negligible impacts."
The Army Corps sent a similar letter to the Ogeechee Riverkeeper conservation group.
The conservation group gave notice in June that it planned to sue if the agency refused to revisit the permit issued for the Hyundai project in October 2022.
Ben Kirsch, legal director of the riverkeeper group, stated that the concentration of pumping will have local impacts on domestic and agricultural wells.
The conservation group is concerned about potential impacts on natural springs, wetlands, tributaries, and streams in the area.
Hyundai is pushing to start production before the end of the year at its 2,900-acre (1,170-hectare) plant in Bryan County west of Savannah.
The site will produce EVs and the batteries that power them.
The South Korean automaker plans to employ 8,000 workers at the plant.
This project is described as the largest economic development project Georgia has ever tackled.
The Army Corps ordered no delays or disruptions to construction at the plant site as a result of its permit reassessment.
Cheri Dragos-Pritchard, spokesperson for the Army Corps' Savannah District, stated via email that the permit is still valid and they have not requested that the permittee stop work.
It was unknown how long the additional review might take.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America stated it will assist as needed to ensure the Army Corps gets the information it needs.
The company stated in a Monday statement that it has worked tirelessly with relevant authorities to be good neighbors and ensure operations do not negatively impact the community's water resources.
Sara Lips, spokesperson for Georgia's Environmental Protection Division, said the extra federal scrutiny is "unlikely to impact or delay" a final decision by state regulators on permitting the wells.
The Army Corps permit obtained by state and local economic developers authorized the filling or dredging of 221 acres (89 hectares) of wetlands at the plant site.
This authorization occurred just a few months after Hyundai announced plans to build its EV factory in May 2022.
The Army Corps initially concluded the project would have "negligible impacts on municipal and private water supplies."
The agency's Friday letter stated it relied on information provided by economic developers for this conclusion.
Trip Tollison, president and CEO of the Savannah Area Economic Development Authority, stated they never purposefully withheld anything.
Tollison expected the updated information requested by the Army Corps to be submitted within 10 days.
He noted the federal agency would typically complete its review within a month.
Tollison expressed confidence that the reevaluation won't hold up the project.
Tollison stated, "There's enough water for everyone," and added, "We feel really good about where we are."
Georgia's environmental agency issued draft permits in July for the four wells to supply Hyundai.
The state is now evaluating public comments before reaching a final decision on the wells.
The wells would be drilled in neighboring Bulloch County.
Some farmers and rural residents in Bulloch County have expressed worry that the auto plant will siphon water away from their crops and homes.
State regulators concluded that water withdrawals by the Hyundai plant would lower aquifer levels up to 19 feet (5.8 meters) for private wells within 5 miles (8 kilometers).
Regulators stated most wells won't see impacts because they reach deeper into the ground.
The state agency also said nearby rivers and streams won't be affected because dense layers of rock seal off the aquifer from surface water.
Ben Kirsch with Ogeechee Riverkeeper hopes the Army Corps' second look will provide more details on how state regulators reached their conclusions.
Kirsch stated, "We definitely want to see the Corps independently evaluate this."
He added, "This should have been all considered before the wetlands were filled and buildings went up."
source_title: US agency to reexamine permit for Hyundai's $7.6 billion electric vehicle plant in Georgia | Georgia Public Broadcasting

facts: Georgia regulators fined Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America $30,000 for improper disposal of wastewater from its Bryan County electric vehicle manufacturing site.
The fine could have potentially surpassed $7 million under Georgia law.
Officials from the Georgia Environmental Protection Division and Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America reached a settlement.
On April 25, officials signed a consent agreement outlining the alleged violations and the agreed-upon fine.
The consent agreement notes that the Director determined an amicable disposition of the alleged violations is in the best interest of the citizens of the State of Georgia.
The consent agreement alleges two violations: Hyundai discharged industrial wastewater to a publicly owned treatment works without an industrial pretreatment permit for such discharge.
The second alleged violation is that Hyundai used a sewage holding tank at the facility for purposes other than receiving sanitary waste from construction trailers at a construction site.
The Current first reported on Hyundai’s wastewater troubles in March.
Hyundai’s initial wastewater disposal problems date to September when the EV plant began production.
The plant needed to dispose of industrial wastewater from processes including metal finishing and car washing.
Construction of the EV plant had outpaced the construction of a new $129 million North Bryan County wastewater treatment facility just across I-16.

Anticipating a short-term need for wastewater treatment until the Bryan facility came online, Hyundai applied for and received a permit from the city of Savannah to have its wastewater treated at the city’s Travis Field facility.
The pre-arranged stopgap treatment began in September but lasted only a few weeks before Hyundai’s wastewater exceeded limits on contaminants, including copper and zinc.
The city of Savannah stopped accepting Hyundai’s wastewater after it exceeded contaminant limits.
Savannah issues its own permits to companies seeking treatment of industrial wastewater.
The April 25 consent agreement does not address the violation of the permit Savannah issued to Hyundai.

Savannah took action regarding the permit violation, with city spokesman Joshua Peacock stating that the penalty for pretreatment permit violation was the cessation of flow on Sept. 26, 2024, and flow has not resumed.
With Travis Field unwilling to take the wastewater, Hyundai began a “pump and haul” operation that continued to the present.
The pump and haul operation involves holding wastewater onsite in sewage holding tanks and trucking it out to third-party treatment facilities, two of them publicly owned.
Treating industrial wastewater at a publicly owned wastewater treatment facility without first getting a pretreatment permit is not allowed.
Hyundai hauled its waste to the Richmond Hill facility late last year.
Hyundai also pumped waste to a tank on the Bryan County facility across I-16.
These alleged violations occurred for about 143 days from about October 1, 2024, until February 21, 2025, confirmed by EPD officials.
Georgia law provides for fines of up to $50,000 a day for each day that a violation occurs.
The maximum allowable fine for the alleged violation totals $7.15 million.
The use of sewage-holding tanks after the construction period ended is not allowed under Georgia law.
That practice appears to have begun at the Hyundai facility around Oct. 1, 2024.

The practice continues to the present, though the company is working with regulators to resolve this violation and follow conditions of the consent order to ensure safe operation of pumping and hauling the contents of the holding tanks.
It is unclear how many days Hyundai would have been subject to a fine under the sewage-holding tank alleged violation.
Savannah and Bryan County officials declined to comment on the fines placed on Hyundai.
The Ogeechee Riverkeeper discovered the wastewater issues as a result of Georgia Open Records Act requests.
The Ogeechee Riverkeeper said the alleged violations are serious.
The Ogeechee Riverkeeper wrote in a prepared statement that industrial waste being delivered to non-permitted treatment facilities is an imminent threat to water resources and human health.

The Ogeechee Riverkeeper expressed gladness that the Georgia EPD took enforcement actions against these violations and expects it will serve as a deterrent to industrial wastewater producers against any future violations.
The Ogeechee Riverkeeper hopes the facility has been able to correct whatever issues have been preventing it from meeting the City of Savannah’s pretreatment standards.

The Ogeechee Riverkeeper encouraged Bryan County to require the facility to meet pretreatment standards that protect the Ogeechee River before allowing any industrial wastewater to be discharged to the new North Bryan Water Reclamation Facility.
EPD has not yet signed off on a plan to return Hyundai to compliance with wastewater disposal.
Wastewater is currently being transported to off-site permitted facilities.
HMGMA submitted its Corrective Action Plan to Georgia EPD and is waiting for their review and approval, according to HMGMA spokeswoman Bianca Johnson.
Following EPD approval, HMGMA intends to return to the use of Travis Field until operations begin at the Bryan County Wastewater Reclamation Facility.
The new facility is still months away from being operational.
Bryan County spokesman Nicholas Beard wrote that the county is anticipating the Water Reclamation Facility being operational in Q3 of this year (August-October timeframe).
source_title: Hyundai avoids millions in fines over wastewater violations - The Current

facts: Hyundai Motor Group is headquartered in Seoul, Korea, employs 250,000 people worldwide, and sells vehicles in 193 countries through 5,000 dealerships and showrooms.

Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America LLC (HMGMA), its affiliate suppliers, and a battery joint venture will invest $5.545 billion to open Georgia’s first fully dedicated electric vehicle manufacturing facility and joint venture battery manufacturing facility.
The project will create 8,100 jobs with an average salary of $58,105 plus benefits at a state-of-the-art smart factory located at the Bryan County Megasite in Georgia’s coastal region.
Approximately another $1 billion is anticipated to be invested by non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project in other locations.
Construction on the new facility is expected to begin in early 2023, with full production expected in the first half of 2025 and an expected annual production of approximately 300,000 units.
Production plans at the new Georgia smart factory include a diverse range of innovative fully electric vehicles, with details on specific models to be shared at later dates.
The establishment of the JV battery manufacturing facility is intended to enhance the stabilization of the supply chain for EV battery components in the U.S. market.
Hyundai Motor Group’s corporate social responsibility programs focus on enriching lives, improving societal wellbeing, and environmental sustainability through time, talent, and resources.
Hyundai Hope programs are focused on improving health, safety, and supporting access to food and nutrition, while Genesis Gives supports STEAM education programs and youth sports.
Hyundai is currently coordinating with several local non-profit organizations that support community-based programming.

Decades of history exist between Hyundai Motor Group and the State of Georgia, including the Kia Georgia facility in West Point established in 2009 with a pledge to create 2,500 jobs and invest $1.2 billion.

The Kia West Point facility now operates four production lines for the Telluride, K5, Sorento, and Sportage models, covers 2,200 acres, employs approximately 2,700 workers, and has a capacity to produce 340,000 automobiles annually.
The Kia West Point plant and its suppliers have an estimated annual economic impact of $4 billion.
Hyundai Dymos Georgia, Glovis Georgia, Hyundai Transys, Mobis America, and other HMG-affiliated companies also operate across the state of Georgia.
HMGMA will receive a 26-year property tax abatement for its investment, with leasehold ad valorem property tax payments beginning in calendar year 2026 after initial construction is complete.

HMGMA’s estimated leasehold ad valorem property tax payments in 2026 are $12.696 million, which is more than 320 times the property taxes estimated to be currently generated on the property based on 2021 values of $38,748.29.
Over the 26-year abatement period, HMG is estimated to pay over $357 million in leasehold ad valorem property tax payments.
The leasehold ad valorem tax payments increase over time, with Year 12 payments estimated at over $15 million, Year 19 over $20 million, and Year 24 over $23 million.

Local communities will receive an approximate five-fold return on the Savannah JDA’s investment through leasehold ad valorem property tax payments alone, with the initial investment estimated to be recouped by Year 10.
In addition to leasehold ad valorem payments, local communities will benefit from increased sales tax revenues, new job-related benefits, and growth in housing, retail, and commercial sectors.

The Economic Development Agreement (EDA) is a binding contract between the State of Georgia, the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (Savannah JDA), and HMGMA to ensure all parties abide by agreed-upon terms.

Georgia’s Constitution requires a balanced budget every year and does not allow the state to give unrestricted cash directly to companies; instead, dollars are directed into project solutions to aid speed-to-market.

HMGMA commits to a minimum of $5.545 billion investment for the EV and battery manufacturing facilities and the creation of 8,100 full-time jobs by December 31, 2031, with continued maintenance of those jobs and investment through 2048.
Repayments to the State and Savannah JDA are due in any year if the investment and full-time jobs maintenance fall below 80% of the commitments.
The EDA requires adherence to environmental state and federal regulations, payment for certain overages on costs relating to changes in site plans, and annual compliance reports.
By December 31, 2031, HMGMA, affiliate suppliers, and the battery JV must collectively meet 80% of the $5.545 billion investment commitment and 8,100 job commitment, maintaining them through 2048.
If combined job and investment performance falls below 80%, a proportional repayment of total JDA property, state property, state land improvements, and estimated tax savings is due.
If jobs attainment falls to 20% or less in any given year during the measurement period, additional payments are due to the JDA and the state.

Statutory incentives available to HMGMA include tax credits for job creation, retraining, machinery, construction development, R&D, sales/use tax, and raw materials, which are non-discretionary and based on job creation.

The Mega Project Tax Credit allows companies to proactively claim credits based on a commitment to capital investment and jobs, requiring maintenance of those jobs for at least 5 years after reaching statutory thresholds or triggering repayment if failed.

Georgia and the Savannah JDA will partner with HMGMA to deliver a site ready for accelerated development, including a 2,967-acre site with all due diligence completed, clearing and grading, wetlands permitting and mitigation, and roadway/access route improvements.
A REBA grant of $50,000,000 will be spread over two annual installments of $25,000,000 each to assist with site development, building construction, or machinery and equipment purchases.

A separate REBA grant of approximately $112,608,688 will be provided to the Savannah JDA for additional land acquisition, wetlands mitigation costs, and site clearing and grading, reflecting price increases since the original offer.
When recruiting Kia in 2006, Georgia provided a $46,000,000 REBA Grant that adjusts to approximately $66.9 million with inflation, making the HMGMA grant higher in real terms.
The Georgia Department of Transportation (GDOT) will fund public road improvements at an estimated $200,000,000, including a new I-16 interchange and site access road.
source_title: Hyundai's $5.5B Georgia EV Plant Jobs | PDF | Taxes | Joint Venture

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) has accelerated its official start of production to the fourth quarter of 2024.
The grand opening celebration for HMGMA is targeted for the first quarter of 2025.
Production was previously announced to begin in the first quarter of 2025.
Georgia's General Assembly passed a Resolution commending Hyundai Motor Group and officially recognizing Feb. 26, 2024, as Hyundai Day at the State Capitol.
Hyundai and Genesis electric vehicles on display included the IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, GV60, and Electrified GV70.
The hydrogen-powered XCIENT Fuel Cell Truck will be deployed for logistics needs between HMGMA and its suppliers.

José Muñoz, president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company and president and CEO of Hyundai and Genesis Motor North America, stated that Hyundai's investments in Georgia will create tens of thousands of great-paying American jobs.
Muñoz added that these investments will bring tens of billions of dollars in economic impact to the state for decades to come.
Governor Brian Kemp highlighted Hyundai as an incredible partner helping recruit businesses, give back to communities, and work with colleges and universities to connect Georgians with opportunities.
Oscar Kwon, president and CEO of HMGMA, called the project the largest economic development project in Georgia's history.
Kwon credited the hard work of Meta Pros and continued support from state and local partners, including the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Joint Development Authority.
Lieutenant Governor Burt Jones and House Speaker Jon Burns attended the celebration alongside legislative leaders from both chambers.
Department of Economic Development Director Pat Wilson joined local elected and economic officials from the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority at the event.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $7.59 billion to develop HMGMA.
HMGMA will create 8,500 direct jobs.
Hyundai and SK On are building a $5 billion EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia.
The SK On joint venture plant is expected to employ 3,500 workers.
A recent Center for Automotive Research study projects Hyundai's investments in Georgia will create nearly 40,000 jobs.
The same study estimates $4.6 billion in individual earnings every year from Hyundai's Georgia investments.
HMGMA has developed educational partnerships with area colleges to create a pipeline for future employment.
The Electric Vehicle Professional Technical Certificate (EVP TCC) is being offered at four area technical colleges as part of this collaboration.
Students who successfully complete the EVP TCC program receive preferred status when applying for a position at HMGMA.
Hyundai is actively developing a hydrogen mobility value chain at HMGMA, as announced at CES 2024.
Through the Clean Logistics Project in Georgia, the Metaplant will utilize XCIENT Fuel Cell tractors to manage logistics for the plant and its suppliers.
The XCIENT is Hyundai's commercialized class 8 6×4 fuel cell electric model with more than 6.25 million accumulated miles globally.
Hyundai is developing hydrogen refueling infrastructure needed to support fuel cell activity at HMGMA.
This hydrogen collaboration is anticipated to stimulate economic growth and create job opportunities within the state.
HMGMA is Hyundai Motor Group's first dedicated Electric Vehicle mass-production plant.
The facility is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The plant will utilize a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem.
All production processes, including order collection, procurement, logistics, and production, will be optimized utilizing AI and data.
The manufacturing system will create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
Hyundai's U.S. operations, combined with those of its 835 independent dealers, contribute $20.1 billion annually and 190,000 jobs to the U.S. economy.
source_title: Hyundai Announces New Production Start Date at Metaplant America During Hyundai Day in Georgia - HMGMA

facts: The state of Georgia and local governments are giving $1.8 billion in tax breaks and other incentives to Hyundai Motor Group.
The incentives are provided in exchange for Hyundai building its first US plant dedicated to electric vehicles near Savannah.
The signed agreement was disclosed on Friday, July 22, 2022.
The project site covers 2,900 acres (1,170 hectares) in Bryan County, west of Savannah.
Hyundai will invest $5.5 billion in its Georgia plant as part of the agreement.
The agreement includes hiring 8,100 workers for the facility.
This deal is described as the state's greatest economic development deal in history.
The announcement comes only months after Georgia concluded another major deal with electric vehicle manufacturer Rivian to build a factory in the state.

Pat Wilson, the state's economic development commissioner, stated that these generational projects solidify Georgia's spot at the vanguard of the EV transition and ensure thousands of Georgians benefit from future jobs.
Hyundai executives and Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp celebrated the agreement with a champagne toast in May at the project site.
Hyundai intends to begin building the facility next year.
The plant is scheduled to produce up to 300,000 automobiles per year by 2025.
The new factory will also manufacture automobile batteries.
The deal is worth approximately $300 million more than the incentives offered to Rivian.
The incentive package costs nearly $228,000 for every job produced in Georgia and four counties in the Savannah area.
Hyundai's payroll at the new plant is estimated to exceed $4.7 billion over the next ten years.
Thousands of new employment opportunities are projected to be created by parts suppliers.
Greg LeRoy, executive director of Good Jobs First, noted that the $1.8 billion in incentives is the highest subsidy package a US state has ever committed for an automobile plant.
LeRoy described the subsidy as inherently super-risky because it bets a huge amount on one company and one facility.
Local governments are granting Hyundai more than $472 million in property tax breaks.
Hyundai will pay more than $357 million in lieu of taxes over a 26-year period beginning in 2023.
source_title: Georgia Grants Hyundai $1.8B Incentive

facts: Hyundai Motor missed out on United States tax incentives for its new $7.6-billion manufacturing plant -- Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America -- in Bryan County, Georgia.
The missed tax credit did not affect Hyundai Motor's investment in the plant.
Industry sources reported that Hyundai Motor was not included in the second round allocations of the Qualifying Advanced Energy Project Credit (48C).
The 48C program is an allocated tax credit funded by President Joe Biden’s Investing in America agenda through the Inflation Reduction Act.
The Biden administration has assigned a total of $10 billion to the 48C program.
Selected companies could receive up to a 30 percent tax credit for their investments on American soil.
A local report estimated that Hyundai Motor could have received up to 480 billion won ($344 million) in tax incentives.
Construction at the Georgia plant has proceeded smoothly, with early-stage production of electric vehicles beginning at HMGMA in October.
Hyundai Motor is expected to start full operation of the new plant in the first quarter of next year.
A Hyundai Motor official stated that while the company applied for the 48C program, it did not expect benefits because the incentives were intended for parts makers, not automakers.
The same official noted that the 48C program was not a factor in the decision to invest in HMGMA.
The US Department of Energy announced in April that 35 projects across 20 states were selected for $1.93 billion in the first round allocations of the 48C program.

The first-round 48C projects were categorized into four areas: clean energy and clean vehicle manufacturing, critical minerals and materials, grid components and modernization, and industrial decarbonization.
Mobis North America electrified Powertrain, a US subsidiary of Hyundai Motor Group’s auto parts maker Hyundai Mobis, was awarded $57.6 million in tax credits under the program.
Mobis is set to construct three different EV component plants that will provide parts for the production of Hyundai and Kia electric vehicles.
source_title: Hyundai Motor plays down missed IRA incentives for Georgia plant - The Korea Herald

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, LLC

Currently available positions include Admin Specialist (General Affairs).
Currently available positions include Compensation Specialist (HR).
Currently available positions include Cost Management Assistant Manager.
Currently available positions include Executive Assistant Specialist.
Currently available positions include General Accountant.
Currently available positions include General Purchasing Manager (Head of Section).
Currently available positions include Global Trade Manager.
Currently available positions include HR Recruitment Manager.
Currently available positions include Part Development Assistant Manager, with positions available in multiple departments.
Currently available positions include Procurement Management Assistant Manager.
Currently available positions include Recruitment Specialist (HR).
Currently available positions include Tax Manager.
Currently available positions include Treasury Manager.
Job seekers can find additional information about available positions and apply online.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group Announces Ongoing Job Recruitment • The Georgia Virtue

facts: Plans to deploy humanoid robots at its U.S. manufacturing plant in Georgia starting in 2028.
Aims to build a factory capable of manufacturing 30,000 robot units annually by 2028.
Robots will initially carry out parts sequencing tasks from 2028.
Applications will expand gradually as safety and quality benefits are validated.
By 2030, Atlas robots expected to move into component assembly.
Longer-term plan includes taking on tasks involving heavy loads, repetitive motions, and complex operations across production sites.
Robots designed to reduce physical strain on workers by taking on higher-risk and repetitive tasks.

At Hyundai Motor's affiliate Kia Corp, the labour union last year called to establish a body to address potential labour rights issues in preparation for the AI era due to worker concerns about expanding automation.
Hyundai Motor vice chair Jaehoon Chang stated at CES that the company understands concerns about job losses but people will be needed to maintain and train the robots, requiring additional personnel.

Company expects humanoid robots to become the largest segment of the physical artificial intelligence market, defined as AI systems embedded in hardware that collect real-world data and make autonomous decisions.
Atlas features human-scale hands with tactile sensing and can lift up to 50 kilograms (110 pounds).
Robot can operate autonomously and function in industrial environments ranging from minus 20 degrees Celsius to 40 degrees Celsius.
Hyundai is accelerating development through partnerships with global AI leaders, including collaborations with Nvidia and Google aimed at improving safety, efficiency, and real-world deployment.
Some automakers see an overlap between autonomous driving and robotics due to similar sensing, perception, and decision-making technologies underpinning embodied AI.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group plans to deploy humanoid robots at US factory from 2028

facts: The United Auto Workers (UAW) labor union condemned Hyundai for what it calls a "disgraceful record on worker safety" following an immigration raid at an electric car battery factory in Georgia.
Most of the 475 people detained on Sept. 4 under suspicion of being in the United States illegally were employees of subcontractors rather than direct LGES and Hyundai Motor Company employees.
At least 300 of the detained individuals reportedly hailed from South Korea.

The UAW alleged that for years, Hyundai, including its joint ventures and suppliers working in its plants, cut corners on industry-standard safety precautions, refused to respect workers’ right to a union, and relied on the exploitation of immigrant labor to build its factories and supply chains.
The UAW claimed that three workers at Hyundai died on the job in the last two years.
The UAW called on federal agencies including the Occupational Safety and Health Administration and National Labor Relations Board to increase workplace safety.
The UAW stated that the militarized federal crackdown on the detained workers further hurts safety at Hyundai, asserting that workers are not the problem but exploitative corporations are.
The UAW pledged to always stand with all workers, immigrant and native-born alike, against unsafe corporations and militarized attacks on workplaces.
Hyundai stated in a press release that the safety and well-being of everyone working at its sites, whether directly employed or working for suppliers, is a top priority.

Hyundai emphasized taking its responsibility as a corporate citizen seriously and noted that incidents like the raid highlight the importance of robust oversight throughout its entire supply chain and contractor network.
The detainees in Georgia are set to be released and sent back to their home country after South Korea and the U.S. reached an agreement.
source_title: UAW condemns Hyundai over worker safety after Georgia factory raid | Fox Business

facts: OSHA cited Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain Inc. – operating as Powertech America Inc. – for exposing employees to struck-by and fall hazards after a fatality at the company’s West Point, Georgia, facility.
The automobile transmission manufacturer faces $68,194 in penalties.
The employee suffered fatal injury when a transmission turnover machine activated while the employee performed maintenance.
OSHA cited the company for failing to train employees to control the release of hazardous energy and use energy isolation devices.
OSHA also cited the company for failing to provide lockout procedures to employees in their native language, provide fall protection and implement a hearing conservation program.
OSHA conducted the inspection in conjunction with the Agency’s Regional Emphasis Program for Safety Hazards in Auto Parts Industry.

OSHA Atlanta-West Area Director Jeffery Stawowy stated that failure to adequately control hazardous energy is a leading cause of serious injuries and fatalities in the auto parts industry, emphasizing that employers must provide workers with tools and information to ensure safety.

The company has 15 business days from receipt of the citations and proposed penalties to comply, request an informal conference with OSHA’s area director, or contest the findings before the independent Occupational Safety and Health Review Commission.
source_title: Georgia Transmissions Manufacturer Cited After Fatality - AllOnGeorgia

facts: Hyundai TRANSYS will invest more than $240 million in expanding its operations in Georgia.
The investment will create 678 full-time long-term positions at a new manufacturing facility in the West Point area.
The brand-new facility will be a 620,000-square-foot plant located near the company’s current location on the Kia campus.
Hyundai TRANSYS is the only automobile manufacturing company in the world that specializes in transmissions.
The new West Point plant will produce eight-speed transmissions for a global automotive leader.

Governor Brian P. Kemp announced the investment, citing Georgia's business climate, robust logistics network, and top-ranked workforce training programs as key factors attracting and retaining global companies.

Sangkil Jung, President of Hyundai TRANSYS Georgia Powertrain, stated that trust built through years of capital investment, employment, and training support, along with unmatched opportunities, made Georgia a natural choice for the expansion.
The company plans to create 165 supplementary construction handling and inspection jobs for the project.
This expansion will bring Hyundai TRANSYS’ total employment in Georgia to 1,741 jobs.

Mayor Steve Tramell of West Point expressed support for the long-term investment and new jobs, thanking the West Point Development Authority, State of Georgia, and Troup County for their contributions.
Patrick Crews, Chairman of Troup County, noted that the significant capital investment will strengthen the local economy and create economic opportunities for residents.

Senior Project Manager Amanda Fields represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce division on the project in partnership with the West Point Development Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson emphasized the state's commitment to supporting long-term growth for investing companies and thanked Governor Kemp and the General Assembly for their continued support of business recruitment efforts.
In 2020 alone, the State of Georgia facilitated more than $1 billion in investment and more than 2,000 jobs from South Korean companies.
Georgia is home to more than 200 automotive-related facilities and remains a national leader in advanced manufacturing.
Georgia has outpaced the United States in 10-year GDP growth in the manufacture of machinery, electrical equipment and components, and fabricated metals.
Individuals interested in career opportunities with Hyundai TRANSYS are encouraged to check the company’s website for updated information.

facts: Hyundai will purchase up to 147 MW of solar energy from Matrix Renewables' Stillhouse Solar Project.
Hyundai Motor Group announced plans to power its upcoming Georgia Metaplant with solar energy for EV production.
Hyundai signed a 15-year power purchase agreement (PPA) with Matrix Renewables to provide up to 147 MW of energy from the Stillhouse Solar Project.
The agreement highlights Hyundai's dedication to reducing its carbon footprint and adopting renewable energy solutions for EV production.
The Stillhouse Solar Project is expected to start commercial operations by October 2025.
Hyundai anticipates receiving around 378 GWh of solar electricity annually, which will reduce carbon emissions by 140,000 tons per year.
The solar energy will also power Hyundai's operations at three affiliated plants: Hyundai Mobis North America Electrified Powertrain, Hyundai Steel Georgia, and Hyundai Transys Georgia Powertrain.
By incorporating solar energy into its manufacturing process, Hyundai aims to influence more sustainable solutions across its supply chain.
The move sets Hyundai apart from competitors like Honda, which is developing an Ohio EV Hub, and Toyota, which announced a $1.4 billion investment for electric SUV production at its Indiana plant.
The Georgia Metaplant is Hyundai's first EV mass-production plant and will also produce EVs for its sister company, Kia.
Hyundai's success in U.S. EV sales with the IONIQ 5 is driving innovation in EV manufacturing processes and influencing the broader EV industry to adopt solar energy in production.
source_title: Hyundai Motor Group’s Georgia Plant To Use Solar Energy For EV Production | EV.com

facts: Hyundai Transys is implementing its self-developed AI system, “Transys Advanced Data Analytics (TADA),” across its production sites to boost quality competitiveness.

TADA encompasses developing AI systems for smart manufacturing technology, a big data platform for data-driven decision-making, and training programs to enhance employees’ digital transformation capabilities.

The “TADA Smart Solution” and “TADA Edge Solution” are self-developed programs utilizing AI deep learning technology to enhance production efficiency and defect inspection accuracy on the manufacturing floor.
TADA Smart Solution is a vision inspection system where deep learning AI detects defects during scanning, identifying small bubbles, damage, etc., in parts during manufacturing and assembly.
Trained with high-quality component X-ray images, the TADA Smart Solution improved inspection accuracy from 93% to 99.9%, detecting defects that human inspectors might miss.
As of July 2024, Hyundai Transys has implemented the TADA Smart Solution in 7 factories in Seosan, including Jigok and Seongyeon.
The TADA Smart Solution has been implemented in 54 processes at the Georgia Powertrain Plant in the United States.
Hyundai Transys has seen cost savings and productivity improvements by introducing a commercial deep learning program developed in-house.
TADA Edge Solution is a small AI inspection system designed to allow nonexperts to easily create and apply customized deep learning models on-site.
The TADA Edge Solution checks the number of bolts assembled, mounting status, and joint lifting inspection at four Hyundai Transys Seosan Plant locations.
Hyundai Transys supports partners with the TADA Edge Solution to help establish smart factories and enhance collaborative management.
The TADA Edge Solution can be installed at one-tenth the cost of traditional AI deep learning inspection equipment (vision systems).

The TADA Edge Solution does not require network construction work or specialized maintenance technicians, allowing small and medium-sized partners to enhance production efficiency and reduce defect rates effortlessly.
The TADA Edge Solution is currently installed and operational at a partner company responsible for manufacturing sheet parts.
Hyundai Transys has installed AI-based responsive CCTV in four areas of the Jigok Plant to monitor where logistics vehicles and forklifts frequently pass through.
These cameras sound an alarm to prevent accidents by alerting workers when vehicles approach.
Around 30 responsive CCTVs detect equipment damage and immediately send alerts and messages to facility managers in case of oil or wastewater leaks.
The Electrification R&D Division at Hyundai Transys has incorporated AI technology into the durability testing process for powertrain gear development.
Gears used in transmissions and reducers are crucial components that affect a vehicle’s safety, efficiency, and quietness.
A deep learning AI measurement machine can now analyze the damage range and complete the report within an hour for powertrain gear durability tests.
This task previously took researchers up to 24 hours to determine the damaged areas in gear durability tests.
The collected test data can assist in root cause analysis and the design process.
Kim Youngwook, director of Hyundai Transys ICT Group, stated that since 2022, they have been shifting employees’ work methods to be data-driven through TADA in-house training.
Kim Youngwook added that Hyundai Transys will continue to strive for digital innovation and improved work efficiency across all business areas, including production, development, and support.
source_title: News | HYUNDAI TRANSYS
