facts: Approximately 475 individuals were detained during a large-scale immigration enforcement operation at Hyundai's Metaplant in Bryan County, Georgia.
The detention operation occurred on Thursday, September 4.
Officials state the action represents one of the largest single-site operations in the 22-year history of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).

The coordinated effort involved ICE, Homeland Security Investigations, Border Patrol, FBI, Drug Enforcement Administration, Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives, IRS, and the Georgia State Patrol.
Authorities executed a federal search warrant as part of an ongoing criminal investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices and other federal crimes.
Construction on the site’s adjacent electric vehicle battery plant was temporarily halted during the operation.
Hyundai officials stated the company is cooperating fully with law enforcement and remains committed to following all labor and immigration regulations.
Normal operations at Hyundai’s electric vehicle manufacturing plant continued without interruption.
source_title: Nearly 500 Workers Detained by ICE at Hyundai's Metaplant in Georgia | ASSEMBLY

canonical_name: Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure Ecosystem in India
facts: Electric vehicles are in the limelight with more than a handful of startups getting ready to launch their EVs.
Startups like GoGreenBov, Tork, Ultraviolette, and Strom are ready to showcase/sell vehicles later this year (2019).
Early mover Ather Energy is already present on roads and expanding.
Revolt Intellicorp announced big investments to speed up release of electric bikes.
Yulu, Vogo, Bounce, and ONN Bikes operate in last-mile electric mobility solutions.
EVs primarily consist of bikes and three-wheelers growing in popularity.
Charging infrastructure supply has not kept pace with EV supply growth.
Worldwide EV charging options are fixed charging stations and swappable batteries.
Fixed charging systems use permanent docking systems set up in malls, homes, offices, or fast-charged depending on technology.
Batteries are increasingly lithium-ion rather than lead acid due to lead acid draining faster after eight months.
Saurabh Markandeya, Co-founder of Adarin (Bengaluru), states EVs running on Li-Ion batteries must operate in temperatures over 40 degrees Celsius, causing poor function in Indian summer cities.
Markandeya notes charging infrastructure lacks a consistent national standard.
Adarin manufactures motors, electric drivetrains, and batteries.
Fast charging infrastructure reduces battery life from five years to three years.
Adarin built fast charging solutions in India that do not reduce battery life.
Mahindra & Mahindra announced a partnership with SmartE to launch 100,000 electric three-wheelers by 2022.
Delhi-NCR-based SmartE already has charging infrastructure for 800 vehicles and plans expansion.
Mahindra & Mahindra is releasing the Treo range of electric two-wheelers.
Tata AutoComp Systems (subsidiary of Tata Group) signed an MoU with Australia-based Tritium to bring 50kW DC fast chargers to India for all EV types.
ABB, Sun Mobility, and Hero Future Energies are in advanced stages of setting up EV infrastructure.
Central government's FAME scheme allocated close to Rs 1,500 crore for 2019-20, Rs 5,000 crore for 2020-21, and Rs 3,500 crore for 2021-22.
Of the FAME allocation, Rs 1,000 crore is allocated for setting up EV charging stations in India.
FAME policy proposes establishing 2,700 charging stations along highways at intervals of about 25 km.
Close to 278,000 EVs are on India’s roads, mostly two-wheelers.
Private charging stations will be permitted at residences with DISCOMs facilitating them.
Setting up public charging stations (PCS) is a de-licensed activity open to individuals meeting government technical and performance standards.
DISCOM licensees must prioritize providing connectivity for PCS applications.
Charging stations may obtain electricity from any generation company via open-access protocols.
Guidelines provide minimum infrastructure requirements for charging stations for light and heavy vehicles.
Bengaluru has 32 charging stations built by Ather Energy alone.
Ather Energy plans to add more stations in Bengaluru this year and expand to four cities soon.
Ather Energy manufactures/sells electric scooters and makes its charging infrastructure available for other e-bikes and cars.
Tork is readying commercial launch later this year (2019) in Pune.
Tork's bike reaches 100 km per hour with a range of 100 kilometres on a single charge.
Kapil Shelke, Founder of Tork Motors, states the company will set up its own charging infrastructure starting in Pune.
Fixed charging infrastructure setup costs Rs 1 lakh to Rs 4 lakh for businesses, per draft policy specifications.
Swappable batteries remove battery cost from vehicle ownership, charging only for used range.
SUN Mobility (Chetan Maini-led, Bengaluru startup) is setting up its own charging infrastructure and plans 100 EV charging stations in the first phase this financial year.
SUN Mobility supports two-wheelers and three-wheelers with long-term plans for cars and buses.
SUN Mobility announced a partnership with SmartE to deploy swappable batteries at SmartE Park and Charge Hubs across Delhi-NCR.
SUN Mobility conducted trials in Gurugram last year and currently supports 500 electric three-wheelers in Phase 1.
Goldie Srivastava, Co-Founder and CEO of SmartE, states the partnership underscores commitment to easy, safe, affordable, eco-friendly last-mile commute.
Mumbai-based Magenta Power will use solar power to proliferate charging stations across India.
Magenta aims to set up over 450 charging stations by next year from current 32.
Magenta set up a solar power-based charging station for EVs on the Mumbai-Pune highway.
GoGreenBov is readying to sell 10,000 electric scooters soon.
Dhivik Ashok, Founder of GGBov, believes B2B industries should place EVs close to fixed infrastructure.
GGBov plans to set up fixed charging infrastructure at offices and later homes for efficiency and cost benefits.
GGBov has a manufacturing plant in Kolar with capacity to make 10,000 vehicles a year, expandable per FY 2020 growth.
India's EV charging infrastructure area currently has no subsidies available.
India must figure out what works for citizens and create impact beyond just launching new EV types.
source_title: I love my electric vehicle, but where do I charge it? This and other problems facing EV users in India | YourStory

facts: 1.3 million immigrants reside in Georgia, comprising 11.6% of the total population in the state.
49% of immigrants in Georgia are naturalized citizens.
653,000 U.S.-born Georgians live with at least one immigrant parent, accounting for 6.7% of the total U.S.-born resident population in the state.
Immigrant workers fill 15% of all jobs in Georgia and contribute 14.4% of the state’s economic output.
Manufacturing accounts for 17% of immigrant workers in Georgia.
Construction accounts for 27% of immigrant workers in Georgia.
Agriculture, forestry, fishing, and hunting account for 19% of immigrant workers in Georgia.
Other services account for 19% of immigrant workers in Georgia.
Professional and business services account for 17% of immigrant workers in Georgia.
Manicurists and pedicurists account for 78% of immigrant workers in Georgia.
Taxi drivers account for 43% of immigrant workers in Georgia.
Construction laborers account for 41% of immigrant workers in Georgia.
Carpenters account for 40% of immigrant workers in Georgia.
Software developers account for 40% of immigrant workers in Georgia.
108,000 immigrants in Georgia are entrepreneurs, representing 19.9% of the state’s total entrepreneurs.
Immigrant household income in Georgia totals $52.4 billion, which is funneled directly back into the Georgia economy through consumer spending.
Deporting Georgian workers would rob city and state coffers of nearly $929 million in tax revenue, making it more difficult to provide basic services to all residents.

Pursuing mass deportation would tank economic growth, lead to massive job losses, disrupt key industries, spike inflation, separate families, and create a culture of fear in workplaces and communities.
The fact sheet was developed with the Economic Policy Institute (EPI) and the Latino Community Fund (LCF Georgia).

EPI is a nonprofit, nonpartisan think tank working to counter rising inequality, low wages, weak benefits for working people, slower economic growth, unacceptable employment conditions, and a widening racial wage gap.

LCF Georgia is a 501(c)3 organization supporting Latino-serving organizations and families with advocacy, capacity building, program development, and collective investments to act as a catalyst for investment and positive narrative within the Latinx/Hispanic Community in Georgia.
source_title: What you need to know about the Georgia economy and immigrant workers - Georgia Budget and Policy Institute

facts: IMMI joined Blue Bird Corp. to celebrate the grand opening of its manufacturing facility in Macon, Georgia on Wednesday, October 8th, 2025.
IMMI’s new Macon plant spans 75,000 square feet and will support school bus manufacturer Blue Bird with industry-first safety upgrades for select model buses.
The facility will manufacture seats equipped with three-point seat belts as standard protection for all student passengers.
The facility will also produce steering wheel deployed airbags to safeguard school bus drivers.
IMMI announced the creation of 80 good-paying U.S. manufacturing jobs in middle Georgia.
Larry Gray, CEO of IMMI, announced the job creation and celebrated safety leadership alongside partner Blue Bird.

Norm Gould III, School Bus President & IMMI CFO, recognized Blue Bird’s commitment to advancing school bus safety by being the first manufacturer to make lap-shoulder belts standard and provide frontal airbags for drivers nationwide.
IMMI’s 4Front airbag safety system will be standard safety equipment on Blue Bird’s Vision-model school buses beginning in October 2025.
John Wyskiel, President and CEO of Blue Bird Corporation, stated that millions of children travel on school buses daily and called them the most precious cargo in the world.
Blue Bird has led in school bus safety for nearly 100 years and partnered with IMMI to bring industry-first standard student and driver safety systems to North American school districts.
Under the brand SafeGuard, IMMI’s Macon facility manufactures state-of-the-art school bus seats designed to protect millions of children daily.
Blue Bird was the first school bus manufacturer to announce in 2024 that lap-shoulder belts would be standard safety equipment on their school bus seats.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration and the National Transportation Safety Board have called for school bus manufacturers to provide lap-shoulder belts for over 25 million children who ride buses annually, which Blue Bird has answered.
IMMI expects to add more jobs to its Macon workforce as demand increases for SafeGuard’s lap-shoulder belt seats.
Illinois recently passed legislation mandating lap-shoulder belts on new school buses, joining eight other U.S. states in the requirement.
IMMI CEO Larry Gray expects other states to follow suit as they recognize that seat belts save lives and reduce injuries even on the safest vehicles on the road.
source_title: IMMI & Blue Bird Celebrate Opening of New Manufacturing Plant in Macon - Middle Georgia CEO

facts: The Ford plant will be located in Stanton, Haywood County, TN, approximately 50 minutes away from Memphis.
Discussions are beginning about a light rail connection from Memphis to Stanton to facilitate workforce transportation.
The author questions whether the proposed light rail would only serve as a temporary need before workers move closer to Stanton after sustaining higher incomes.
The Ford plant deployment is compared to Kia's plant in West Point, Troup County, GA, located near the larger population center of Columbus, Muscogee County, GA.
Kia opened its plant in West Point, GA in 2006 and employs approximately 2,700 workers.
The Ford plant will employ 6,000 workers, drawing from a larger population base in Shelby County than the Georgia region referenced for Kia.
A proportional similarity exists between projected occupational demands and regional populations in both the TN and GA regions based on their respective demographics.

BLS sourced data chart analysis compares Kia's economic impact on county total wage growth, mapping West Point’s Troup County to Haywood County, Columbus’ Muscogee County to Shelby County, and Georgia to Tennessee.
In 2006, when Kia production started, Troup County emerged from negative total wage growth when benchmarked against 2001 total wages.

In 2006, the population center of the region in Columbus GA’s Muscogee County experienced accelerated growth that briefly fell off at the beginning of the Great Recession alongside the State of Georgia and Troup County.

In 2009, as visibility began to clear from the Great Recession, Muscogee County’s percentage total wage growth exceeded the State of GA, while Troup County’s percentage total wage growth exceeded both the state and Muscogee County.

From 2012 to 2019, Troup County’s total wage growth sustained exceeding the percentage wage growth of Muscogee County, decelerating below the State of GA in 2015 but remaining competitive with the state through 2019.

Columbus GA’s Muscogee County and its population center were not competitive with GA statewide percentage total wage growth, raising questions about the future economic impact on Memphis and Shelby County from the Ford plant deployment in remote Haywood County.

The author questions whether a Memphis/Shelby County economic development plan exists to help ensure that Shelby County has competitive percentage total wage growth compared to the State of Tennessee.

The author concludes that a local economic development plan emphasizing Memphis and Shelby County small business and workforce development is needed instead of relying on the Ford plant in Haywood County.
source_title: FORD: Don’t Get The Memphis Benefit – Memphis Corporate Community Leadership Measured
