facts: Custom injection molder Thomson Plastics Inc. has reinvented itself through a comprehensive restructuring plan.

Five years ago, the company's operation reflected its largest customer's production cycle, molding parts for six to eight months and laying off most workers for four months until production restarted.
The restructuring plan is now paying off with year-round employment for processing staff.
The company has opened two new plants.
Thomson Plastics received supplier awards from customers.
The company acquired its first brand-new molding machines.
President and COO Rick Kibbey stated, "We are turning the corner, but this is a new Thomson Plastics," during an interview at the company's headquarters in Thomson, Ga.
Thomson Plastics was founded in 1973 in Mississippi as CAE Plastics to supply golf cart manufacturer Club Car LLC, located near Augusta, Ga.
In 1995, at the request of Club Car, CAE moved to Thomson, about 30 miles west of Augusta, and changed its name to Thomson Plastics.
Thomson Plastics has been owned by Altus Capital Partners since 2005.
Club Car tended to build golf carts from January to June, so Thomson Plastics previously followed that seasonal timeline.
Other lawn and garden customers followed a similar seasonal schedule.
The restructuring plan started with changing the seasonal nature of the business by finding additional year-round business.
Molding automotive parts became a key part of the strategy for Thomson Plastics, which previously had no automotive business.
Thomson Plastics now has 12 percent to 15 percent of its total business in the Tier 2 automotive sector.
President Rick Kibbey stated he would like to see the automotive business increase to about 25 percent because it is not seasonal.

Director of sales and program management Tom Talboys said, "We select our customers very carefully," noting they usually work with customers that have significant programs to launch and form a relationship before moving to the next.
Until this year, Thomson Plastics purchased used molding machines for all three of its plants.
Due to the restructuring program, the company purchased seven new presses with 220 tons of clamping force from Borche.
One new press was delivered to the plant in Thomson earlier this year, and two more were delivered in June.
Two new presses were delivered to each of the company's plants in Lexington, N.C., and Howell, Mich., in June.
Between September 2017 and September 2018, Thomson Plastics plans to launch 150 new molds for golf cart, recreational, and automotive products.
Three of the new Borche machines are earmarked for new projects.
President Rick Kibbey explained that price, reliability, and performance were key factors in choosing Borche presses over well-known names.
The company had a relationship with Nationwide UPM Machinery Sales in Troy, Mich., which represents Borche and offered a competitive price for well-equipped machines.
Kibbey visited three molders who use Borche presses and spoke to their maintenance people about performance before deciding to put them on the floor.
Regarding future press purchases, Kibbey noted they are looking at others as well, specifically mentioning Engel's economical Wintec model.
Kibbey commented that while high-priced green Engels look impressive, not every part needs that capability, and the Wintec looks promising.
Thomson Plastics performs sophisticated molding with machines it purchased and rebuilt, with an average machine age of about 15 years.
Nearly all 38 presses in the Thomson plant, which have clamping forces ranging from 165 tons to 3,150 tons, are equipped with Wittmann robots.
The headquarters' three new machines will sit side by side and be run by one operator, also equipped with Wittmann robots.
In January, Thomson Plastics volunteered to be a beta test site for RJG Inc.'s new CoPilot product, which is currently operating on six presses.

CoPilot functions as a notification system, communications tool, and coach that standardizes troubleshooting procedures, automatically performs process audits, logs process changes, and notifies operators if variables move outside set tolerances.
The CoPilot system is designed to be used by operators of any skill level.
Kibbey stated that when there is a problem, the processor does not have to twist dials because CoPilot tells them what to do, calling it a "road map."
Kibbey expects CoPilot will eventually be placed on every press companywide, noting the investment price versus losses from not having it makes it a simple decision.
Thomson Plastics has been using RJG for training for the past six years.
President Rick Kibbey emphasized that technical expertise is critical, stating, "Technical expertise makes you or breaks you."
All processing technicians and tool setters have gone through a knowledge assessment by RJG, which conducts training in the plant once a year.
Employees are sent to RJG training facilities in Georgia and North Carolina, and the company currently has four master molders certified by RJG.
In addition to CoPilot, Thomson Plastics uses an IQMS reporting system for tracking production and providing reports to support lean manufacturing efforts.
Kibbey stated the company does not have an allegiance to particular brands of auxiliary equipment because performance is equal across user-friendly options.
The Thomson plant staff is comfortable with Wittmann robots, while the other two plants use Sepro, Star, and some Wittmanns.
One collaborative robot from Universal Robotics is used on overmolding a steering wheel.

The collaborative robot process involves a 700-ton press making the first shot, the robot extracting the part, dropping it on a conveyor for water bath and air drying, then inserting it into a second press for overmolding.
The use of the collaborative robot enabled Thomson Plastics to reduce the number of operators for the manufacturing cell from three to one per shift.
The Thomson plant operates three shifts per day, five days a week.
Kibbey expects to use more collaborative robots in the future.
Another insert molding project involves a cover for a deep-cycle battery where a robot picks up battery terminal inserts and places them into the mold.
Automation of the battery cover process improved cycle time by 25 percent and provided more consistency compared to the original manual process.
Another automation example involves 6-volt battery cases that are molded, dropped onto a conveyor for gate seal-off testing, and then sorted.
The company has extensive experience with gas-assisted molding using the Cinpres gas-assist process.
Kibbey stated they have 25 to 30 different types of gas-assist parts being molded at the Thomson plant every day.
The company has taken over many gas-assist projects that were in trouble elsewhere.
Kibbey credits the company's success to staff's ability to use equipment to its full potential, with the company acting as its own integrator to put together work cells.
Kibbey views buying pieces and assembling work cells as an advantage because the workforce learns while doing it and owns the finished systems.
The team understands the capabilities of each press and does not try to mold parts the machine is not capable of running.
Club Car recently named Thomson Plastics its supplier of the year for 2017.
Kibbey said the supplier award is evidence that they have turned the corner.
source_title: Restructuring gives Thomson Plastics new lease on work | Plastics Machinery Manufacturing

facts: Engineering plastics provider Sabic recently reported test results underscoring the potential of thermoplastic-based thermal runaway barrier solutions to prevent fire spread in electric vehicle (EV) batteries.

Sabic’s sub-system level test series showed that a battery module box made from its Stamax 30YH570 long-glass-fiber polypropylene (PP) resin with an intercellular thickness as thin as 1 mm has potential to prevent thermal runaway propagation in 18650 cylindrical cells.

The Stamax grade provides the necessary thermal insulation and flame resistance to reduce the chances of cell-to-cell propagation in a thermal runaway scenario and mitigate risks of catastrophic safety incidents.
This material in combination with compressible foams has shown promising results in containing thermal runaway propagation in prismatic and pouch cells.
In 2023, the Stamax 30YH570 resin earned the UL Verified Mark from Underwriters Laboratories (UL) for effective flame-delay performance.
The UL assessment was based on UL 2596, Test Method for Thermal and Mechanical Performance of Battery Enclosure Materials, using the thermal runaway box method.

Thermal runaway propagation tests are performed using a cluster of 15 commercial 18650 and 11 21700 lithium-ion battery cells enclosed in a steel chamber, with and without injection-molded Stamax 30YH570 resin blocks serving as a thermal barrier.
Initiating cells were thermally abused by heating them through an electrical tape to begin thermal runaway during testing.

In tests without the thermal barrier, electric and chemical energies stored in the cells were discharged as high-temperature gas, open-flame, high-speed particles, and sudden pressure increases, causing immediate propagation to all neighboring cells and severe damage to the EV battery pack.

In tests using the thermal barrier made from Stamax 30YH570 resin, heat transfer and thermal propagation from thermal abuse of initiating cells were effectively suppressed, leaving all remaining cells safe and intact.

The flame-retardant material changes phase from solid to liquid and gas and removes heat via latent heat and intumescent capabilities during severe thermal events, acting as an effective thermal shield to mitigate heat transfer and fire spread.
At the recent NPE show, Sabic highlighted EV battery applications successfully employing its advanced thermoplastics.
One application is the battery module from the 2023 Hyundai Ioniq 6, which uses Sabic’s H1030 PP compound for module enclosures.
The H1030 material is a 30% glass-fiber-reinforced, non-halogenated, flame-retardant compound that meets China’s GB/T 41467.3-2015 fire safety regulation for EVs surviving five minutes at 1000°C.
Additional EV battery components displayed at the show included enclosures, end plates, and busbars, either in production or developed as prototypes.

Thermoplastics used for these applications offer benefits including weight and cost reduction, increased functional integration, and enhanced electrical and thermal insulation over conventional metals.

Fahad Al-Harthi, global director of automotive at Sabic, stated that their portfolio of flame-retardant materials and expertise in application design can help enable new thermal runaway barrier approaches to improve EV battery safety, efficiency, and performance.
source_title: Phase-change Thermoplastics Can Prevent EV Battery Thermal Runaway

facts: Manufactures and sells fluid storage, carrying, and delivery systems primarily for the light duty automotive market worldwide.

The FTDS segment offers fuel tank products, such as plastic fuel tanks for gasoline, diesel, and flex fuel vehicles.

The FTDS segment supplies pressurized fuel tank systems for hybrid vehicles.
The FTDS segment offers low emission vehicle fuel tanks, delivery and return components, and plastic fuel filler tubes.
The FTDS segment provides pump and module products, such as brushed and brushless pumps, modules, electronics controls, additive dosing systems, and sensors.

The State of Georgia is offering TI Automotive incentives including state job tax credits totaling $3,000 per employee per year during the facility’s first five years of operation.
TI Automotive can spread out the state income tax credits over a 10-year period.
New employees will be trained for many of the facility’s high-tech operations through the state’s Quick Start program at North Georgia Technical College.
The company expects to reach full production of fuel-system components for the two automakers in 2006.

facts: Governor Brian Kemp announced that Virginia Transformer will create 400 jobs in a new Georgia expansion.
Virginia Transformer Corp. will invest $40 million to expand its existing manufacturing facility near Rincon, creating over 400 new jobs in Effingham County.
The Rincon facility operates under the name Georgia Transformer.

Governor Brian Kemp stated: “Along with state and local leaders in the coastal region, we’re proud to celebrate this major expansion and Virginia Transformer’s decision to double down on Georgia,” and congratulated the company on the announcement.
The Georgia Transformer facility in Rincon specializes in manufacturing custom-designed large power transformers for utilities, data centers, and renewable energy sectors.
The 250,000-square-foot facility produces transformers ranging from 30 MegaVolt-Amperes (MVA) to 500 MVA at up to 525 kilovolts.
This expansion marks the latest in a series of investments at this facility since it became part of Virginia Transformer Corp. in 2015.
Speaker Jon Burns joined Governor Kemp and Southeast Georgia leaders to applaud the expansion, noting it arrives as the region recovers from mill closures and job losses.
Virginia Transformer is the largest U.S.-owned producer of power transformers in North America.
CEO Prabhat Jain stated the company has grown by focusing on delivering for customers and is known for engineering power transformers with the shortest lead times.
The Georgia Transformer production facility is located at 2789 GA-21 in Effingham County.
Construction on the expansion is expected to begin in January 2026.
Applications for positions in operations, maintenance, and assembly are currently being accepted.
Chairman Matt Saxon of the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority praised the investment as a powerful endorsement of the community’s skilled workforce and strategic location.
The project will retain more than 800 existing jobs while creating 400 new positions.
District 5 Effingham County Commissioner Phil Kieffer congratulated the company on the milestone and commended the ICDA for fostering a supportive business environment.
Senior Regional Project Manager Dorie Bacon represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project.
The project is supported in partnership with the Effingham County Industrial Development Authority, Georgia Power, and Georgia Quick Start.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that demand for utility and commercial scale is at an all-time high and attracting energy grid sectors ensures business growth in Georgia.

Virginia Transformer Corp. manufactures custom-designed power transformers for a diverse clientele across utilities, renewable energy, data centers, industrial, mining, oil and gas, commercial, institutional, specialty, and export markets.

facts: Target opened a new distribution center in Hampton, Georgia.
The new facility is Target’s fourth full-service “flow” center.
The facility helps replenish stores and ship products to customers with more speed and efficiency.
Target opened its first such facility in New Jersey in 2018.
Target now has seven supply chain facilities in Georgia.
The newest Hampton facility will support more than 400 jobs.
The new facility allows the company to improve its shipping efficiency in the Southeast.
Target CEO Brian Cornell told analysts during a Q4 2023 earnings call that the retailer planned to add 10 new supply chain facilities to its distribution network over the next 10 years.
Cornell said in a Q1 2024 earnings call that the company is continuing to introduce new sortation centers meant to enhance its last-mile delivery capabilities.
New facilities, such as the recently opened flow center in Hampton, can upgrade Target’s ability to offer additional delivery options.
Earlier this year, Target announced plans to open more than 300 new stores in addition to expanding its owned brands and membership rewards program.
Brian Cornell said in the Q4 2023 earnings call: “Without huge investments in stores, supply chain, and tech, there is no drive up or order pickup.”
As of February, Target had 58 total supply chain facilities with a combined footprint of 61.5 million square feet, according to a securities filing.
According to Gretchen McCarthy, Target’s chief supply chain and logistics officer, Georgia “has become an increasingly important location within Target’s supply chain network.”
McCarthy added that Target has “spent millions of dollars over the last several years expanding our presence” in Georgia.
The Peach State’s significance as a supply chain hub has been on the rise recently.
The Port of Savannah posted record growth in fiscal 2023.
The Georgia Ports Authority has continued to report increases in cargo volumes as companies like Hyundai invest in manufacturing plants in the state.
source_title: Target’s fourth ‘flow’ distribution center opens in Georgia | Supply Chain Dive

facts: Commissioner Pat Wilson accepted his current role at the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) in November 2016.
Wilson first joined the GDEcD in 2010 as Deputy Commissioner for Global Commerce.
A year after joining, he became Chief Operating Officer, managing daily operations and serving as the Department’s legislative liaison.
Wilson is a native of Franklin Springs in Franklin County and a graduate of the University of Georgia.
Former Governor Sonny Perdue suggested Wilson move into economic development after their conversation following Perdue's last legislative session.
Governor Perdue believed Wilson would excel at selling Georgia due to his years of lobbying for public policy in Washington and under the Gold Dome.
Governor Deal has been supportive of Wilson's career path and growing jobs in Georgia.
Governors and General Assemblies across party lines have a long history of being pro-business in Georgia.
Governor Deal and his team bolstered Georgia's pro-business reputation, leading to it being named the No. 1 state in the nation to do business for the last four years by Site Selection magazine.
Post-recession growth has spurred companies to focus on and invest funds into R&D and innovation.
This investment in R&D and innovation has spurred a rebirth of advanced manufacturing and advanced distribution jobs across Georgia.
Innovation-related jobs have become a major growth sector for the state.
The flooring industry has experienced a vibrant rebirth in northwest Georgia.
The high-tech automotive industry is present in northeast and west Georgia.
Innovations are occurring in food processing and distribution.
FinTech, cyber security, and advanced computing-related jobs are growing in the state.
These industry shifts are directly related to companies placing greater emphasis on innovation, competitiveness, and positioning for prosperity over the next 20 to 30 years.

Wilson hopes Georgia continues to diversify by leveraging assets such as Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, the Ports of Savannah and Brunswick, its interstate system, and unrivaled transportation network.
Wilson also hopes to leverage academic assets produced by technical colleges and universities to continue job growth across industries.
Wilson would love to see strengthening job growth in rural Georgia.
Governor Deal tasked the GDEcD with strategically and aggressively pursuing opportunities to grow jobs and investment specifically in rural Georgia.
All divisions of the department have been working to find growth opportunities in rural communities.
Consistency in job growth is the department's goal, and they are committed to pursuing that objective.
source_title: Q&A with January/February Cover Feature: GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson – Engineering Georgia Magazine

facts: Humiscope is an Australian owned and operated company that specialises in controlling indoor environments through energy-efficient solutions.
According to the latest statistics from the Australian Bureau of Statistics, only 14% of engineers in Australia are women.
In 2020, 22% of bachelor’s degree students in engineering were women, which suggests a significant drop-off in the number of women pursuing engineering careers after graduation.
Humiscope champions the importance of recognising women in the field of engineering and wants to showcase the three female engineers on its team.
Ladan Bage is a mechanical engineer with a Masters of Science in Mechanical Engineering who works on several large projects for Humiscope.

Ladan Bage offers practical mechanical designs while always considering a client’s requirements and specialises in drafting, documentation, mechanical design, reverse engineering, manufacturing, and its supervision.
Paula Andrea Gil is a drafts(wo)man with a Bachelor’s Degree in Architectural Drafting and Design and over six years’ hands-on experience.

Paula Andrea Gil works closely with Ladan Bage and is highly skilled in BIM modelling to create digital representations of the physical and functional characteristics of spaces Humiscope designs for clients.
Melissa Le Roux is the Lead Designer at Humiscope and has been with the company since it opened its doors in 2015.
Melissa Le Roux has led projects throughout Australia and its neighbouring islands with clients from a diverse range of industries.
Melissa Le Roux advocates for sharing knowledge to better inform clients, stating that educating clients on solutions and design rationale is key to delivering successful projects.
The Humiscope team is currently working on a significant project in lithium-ion battery production, a field relatively new within the Australian industrial sector.
Humiscope was contracted to provide a turnkey solution for a large lithium-ion manufacturing facility.
The contract covers conceptual designs and complete build of low dew point, ultra-low humidity, ultra-dry production rooms.
Ladan Bage has engineering experience in the R&D sector, manufacturing, HVAC and construction sites and is excited to learn within the emerging lithium-ion market.

Ladan Bage noted that designing dry rooms for lithium-ion battery production is a relatively new field where design can be challenging, requiring more research and optimisation to get the best results.
Paula Andrea Gil found projects involving low-dew point solutions enriching and enjoys seeing designs become reality through client consultation and 3D modelling.
Humiscope actively recruits and promotes women in engineering, with two thirds of its workforce being women across operational management, marketing, and administration.
Diverse teams are known to be more innovative and creative, which can lead to better outcomes for businesses and society as a whole.
source_title: Women breaking down barriers in engineering – Australian Manufacturing News

facts: On April 14, Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp declared a state of emergency stemming from ongoing disruptions to the state’s supply chain infrastructure.
The emergency measure eases regulations on the trucking industry and prevents price gouging on gasoline and diesel fuel.
The declaration aims to remedy supply chain disruptions stemming from the COVID-19 pandemic and Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.
UGA supply chain expert and Terry College of Business management lecturer Marty Parker explained that supply chain problems are bigger than one state or set of regulations.
Georgia's supply chain is difficult, impacted by labor shortages, truck shortages, the war in Ukraine, and recent COVID outbreaks in China.
The supply chain has not fully recovered from original shocks in 2020.

The crisis is described as a perfect storm involving rapid economic rebound post-pandemic, COVID lockdowns in China, the Ukraine war, booming U.S. economy, Great Resignation labor shortages, and truck shortages.
Bottlenecks include lag time between rising prices and decreasing demand for goods, port congestion, warehouse space shortage, and growth in e-commerce.
Supply chain issues originate with raw materials flowing through China, Brazil, and other countries facing manufacturing issues, followed by port congestion and trucker shortages.
The trucking industry faces labor shortages as drivers reach retirement and few young people enter the field.
Trucking is highly regulated regarding when drivers can sleep, eat, drive, and park, with electronic devices monitoring them at all times.
Companies are investing in better analytics and automation to improve truck driver culture, as discussed at the Trucking Profitability Strategies Conference.

Gov. Kemp's order removes some trucking restrictions but includes anti-price gouging provisions that Parker criticizes for creating shortages, comparing them to 1970s gasoline price caps and NYC rent controls.
Georgia ports increased the number of containers brought in by 50 percent between 2015 and 2021.
Ports expect to grow capacity now that dredging is complete.
There is a shortage of warehouse space, though more space will be built out over time.
Companies trying to build warehouse space face shortages of building materials and labor.
A large warehouse project in Savannah is on hold because the builder could not get roofing material despite having all other parts.
Farming faces a rough season due to record drought in the west, tractor shortages, fertilizer shortages from the Ukraine war, and shortages of other farming equipment.
Small companies making up the bulk of Georgia's economy are hurt by needing to pay upfront for imported goods well in advance of manufacturing or shipping, depleting working capital before sales.
Money is getting tighter as the Fed fights inflation.
Supply chain problems may improve due to a looming economic slowdown from higher interest rates slowing home building and large purchases.
The crisis is viewed as an anomaly in a generally healthy system caused by the pandemic and demand changes, but it is leading to a long-term shift.
Long-term shifts involve solving supply chain problems through better use of data, automation, and robotics for a more resilient supply chain with better information processing.
Conditions are expected to improve once COVID is controlled globally.
source_title: Q&A: Untangling Georgia’s supply chain emergency - Terry College of Business

entity_name: Toyota Industries Electric Systems North America, Inc. (TIESNA)

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced that Toyota Industries Electric Systems North America, Inc., (TIESNA) will create more than 250 new jobs while expanding the Toyota Industries Corporation campus in Pendergrass with a new manufacturing facility valued at $69 million in investment.
Japanese Consul General Mio Maeda and local representatives joined TIESNA for the groundbreaking ceremony this afternoon.

Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia has distinguished itself as a leader in the automotive industry and is the best state for business, pledging continued work with state and local partners to bring opportunity to communities across Georgia.

TIESNA is a subsidiary of Toyota Industries, which is headquartered in Japan and develops and manufactures automobile-related products including car air-conditioning compressors and car electronics components for worldwide carmakers.
The Toyota Industries Group has operated in Pendergrass since 2004, starting with TD Automotive Compressor Georgia, LLC (TACG).
TACG was followed by Toyota Industries Compressor Parts America, Co. (TICA) in 2012.
These existing companies currently support more than 400 jobs in Jackson County.
TIESNA’s new facility will be located at the 1700 Valentine Industrial Parkway campus in Pendergrass.
The new facility will produce DC-to-DC converters, which convert high voltage to low voltage to supply power to auxiliary equipment.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in 2025.

Jackson County Commission Chair Tom Crow welcomed TIESNA as the third Toyota Industries related company to Jackson County and praised TACG and TICA as outstanding corporate citizens, pledging community support for its success.

Griff Lynch, President and CEO of the Georgia Ports Authority, highlighted the expansion as part of Georgia’s growing automaking sector and noted that significant investments from Toyota Industries and other automotive suppliers are delivering career opportunities for Georgians.

Senior Regional Project Manager Kristen Miller represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with Jackson County Area Chamber of Commerce, Georgia Power, the Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Quick Start.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that Toyota Industries has been part of the fabric of Jackson County since 2004 and emphasized that maintaining 50 years of representation in Japan allows direct communication with corporate HQs including Toyota Industries’.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned itself as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to fiscal year 2021.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
source_title: GA: Toyota Industries Corporation Expands Georgia Presence, Creating 250 New Jobs | Trade and Industry Development

facts: Toyota's footprint in northeast Georgia is growing.
Toyota Industries Electric Systems North America (TIESNA), a subsidiary of Toyota that manufactures air conditioning compressors and other vehicle electronics, broke ground on a new facility.
The expansion adds 250 employees to the existing Jackson County campus.
County Commission Chair Tom Crow welcomed the third Toyota Industries related company to Jackson County and pledged support for its success.
TIESNA is making a $69 million investment in the new facility.
The facility will produce DC-to-DC converters.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin in 2025.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia has distinguished itself as a leader in the automotive industry and thanked Toyota for choosing to expand its footprint there.
TD Automotive Compressor Georgia moved to Pendergrass in 2004.
Toyota Industries Compressor Parts America, Co. (TICA) joined the Jackson County campus in 2012.
The TIESNA investment is part of a string of automotive manufacturing additions throughout Georgia.
Rivian Automotive recently secured property for its $5 billion EV plant in Morgan and Walton counties.
Hyundai is building a massive manufacturing facility near Savannah, scheduled to begin production in the coming months.
Hwashin Co. is committing $176 million for a plant in Dublin that will supply the Hyundai facility.
SK Battery America, which operates an EV battery plant in Jackson County, recently announced furloughs despite Georgia's growing automotive manufacturing industry.

facts: Trenton Pressing broke ground Thursday on a $10 million facility to expand its manufacturing.
The expansion will add 20 new jobs upon completion.
The facility will be 105,000 square-feet.

The expansion will help the company expand its assembly services and increase manufacturing capacity.
The expansion will allow the company to better service its growth and meet manufacturing needs.

facts: Valeo announced an investment of €200 million (Rs 2,147 crore) in India over the next three years.
The company aims to more than treble its sales to €700 million by 2028.
India currently contributes only 1% to Valeo’s global sales.
Valeo plans to grow its India business by tapping into the growing demand for feature-rich cars and electric vehicles.
Christophe Périllat, CEO of Valeo, stated that additional content and technology in cars sold in India is the reason for their determination to grow there.
Périllat noted that India is the fastest-growing automotive market in the world.
More than 60% of new orders in India are SUVs.
Valeo plans to triple its sales from €220 million to around €700 million by 2028.
The company is preparing for the next step of high growth beyond 2028, targeting 1 billion euros of sales.
Valeo currently supplies electric powertrains to Mahindra & Mahindra’s BE 6 and XEV 9e electric vehicles.
The auto parts supplier offers e-axles, battery cooling plates, chargers, inverters, sensors, lighting equipment, and ADAS (advanced driver assistance systems).
In India, Valeo will invest mainly in its electrification and ADAS divisions.
Nearly 4% of all new car sales in India are electric.
Périllat reaffirmed the importance of the Indian market, noting that technology content in Indian cars is growing.
Valeo is localizing the electric motor and power electronics in India.
The company is localizing battery cooling components in India.
For ADAS, Valeo is localizing sensors, cameras, and radars among others in India.
Valeo anticipates that by 2032, the share of EVs and hybrids will be 25% in India.
Valeo entered the Indian market in 1997.
The company operates six production sites across Chennai, Pune, Sanand, and Gurugram.
Valeo maintains global R&D centres in Chennai and Bengaluru.
Valeo employs over 7,500 people in India.
Over 50% of Valeo's Indian employees are engineers contributing to global research, development, and manufacturing activities.
Valeo has commissioned a new plant and dedicated e-axle production lines at its Pune facility.
The new Pune plant and e-axle lines were commissioned to support rising demand for Mahindra & Mahindra’s EVs.
source_title: Valeo to invest Rs 2,147 crore in India over the next three years - BusinessToday

canonical_name: Alvin W. Vogtle Electric Generating Plant
facts: Plant Vogtle is located in Waynesboro, Georgia, specifically in Burke County near Waynesboro along the Savannah River.
The facility features four reactors and is co-owned by Georgia Power, Oglethorpe Power Corporation, Municipal Electric Authority of Georgia, and Dalton Utilities.
It is one of Georgia Power's two nuclear facilities and one of three nuclear facilities in the Southern Company system.
Unit 1 began commercial operation in 1987.
Unit 2 began commercial operation in 1989.
Unit 3 entered commercial operation on July 31, 2023.
Unit 4 entered commercial operation on April 29, 2024.
Units 3 and 4 are the first new nuclear units to be constructed in the U.S. in the last 30 years.
The plant is now the largest generator of clean energy in the country.
Units 1 and 2 utilize Westinghouse 4-loop Pressurized Water Reactors.
Units 3 and 4 utilize Westinghouse AP1000 2-loop Pressurized Water Reactors.
The total generating capacity is approximately 4,800 megawatts.
Units 1 and 2 each generate around 1,150 MW of power.
Units 3 and 4 each generate around 1,117 MW of power.
Over 1,700 people oversee the plant's safe and reliable operations 24 hours a day, 365 days a year.
The workforce includes engineers, mechanics, control room operators, chemists, electricians, security officers, and others.
Plant Vogtle has been a certified wildlife habitat by the Wildlife Habitat Council since 1993.
The site is situated on approximately 3,000 acres, with hundreds of acres replanted with loblolly and native longleaf pines.
Management programs enhance habitats for species such as bluebirds, wood ducks, wild turkey, and the red-cockaded woodpecker.
The plant generates electricity through a fission process where the nucleus of an atom splits into smaller parts, causing a chain reaction that releases heat.
Water is pumped through the reactor core, heated by fission, circulated through steam generators, and returned to the reactor in a closed loop.
Cooler water in the steam generator meets hot tubes to turn to steam, which spins turbine blades connected to an electric generator.
The three water systems used to generate electricity are separate and do not mix with each other.
Nuclear power plants at this facility have minimal environmental impact and produce carbon-free energy around the clock.
The plant's ability to generate consistent power contributes to reducing greenhouse gas emissions and strengthening U.S. energy independence.
Safety systems include defense-in-depth designs with multiple passive and active systems to protect the reactor and surrounding public.
Passive safety barriers include fuel zirconium alloy cladding, reactor vessel thickness, and concrete containment.
Active safety systems ensure continuous core cooling and safe plant shutdown, including a reactor protection system, multiple core cooling systems, and containment isolation systems.
A person living within 50 miles of the plant receives an average of about 0.01 millirem of radiation per year.
The facility is named for Alvin W. Vogtle Jr., past Chairman of the Board of Southern Company, who was elected President in 1969, CEO in 1970, and Chairman in 1983.
Alvin W. Vogtle Jr. was a prisoner of war during World War II and escaped six times before reaching Switzerland.
source_title: Plant Vogtle: Alvin W. Vogtle Nuclear Power Plant in Georgia

facts: Each cooling tower stands 60 stories tall.
Plant Vogtle Units 3 & 4 have passed all their tests and safety inspections and can officially start generating energy for the state of Georgia.
Plant Vogtle is jointly owned by Georgia Power (45.7%), Oglethorpe Power Corporation (30%), Municipal Electric Authority of Georgia (22.7%) and Dalton Utilities (1.6%).
source_title: Plant Vogtle Units 3 & 4 Overview

facts: Claudine Rochette serves as VP of Strategy and Communication for Valeo’s Power Division.
Rochette joined Johnson Controls in 2003 as a systems integration engineer, surviving its division's acquisition by Valeo in 2005.
She has led hybrid platforms, 48V electronics, and full EV powertrain programmes from inside the industry.
Thermal management is identified as the defining frontier of EV performance rather than battery chemistry.
Electric cars lose winter range primarily because they lack a combustion engine’s waste heat.
Valeo’s integrated heat pumps recover over 25% of lost winter range by redistributing thermal energy already present in the vehicle.
Over 90% of EV motors today depend on rare earth magnets primarily sourced from China.
Valeo’s EESM technology and the Valeo-MAHLE iBEE system eliminate rare earth dependency for vehicles up to 350 kW.
OTA updates allow continuous motor efficiency improvement for 10 to 15 years after a vehicle leaves production.
Purchase price parity between EVs and petrol cars is now an active engineering target rather than a distant aspiration.
On first-generation electric cars, winter heating could decrease range by half, with a car rated for 400 to 500 kilometres delivering barely 200 on a January commute.
This range loss is caused by a thermal architecture designed around a heat source that no longer exists, not battery degradation.
Valeo formed its Power Division in 2024 by merging its Powertrain Systems and Thermal Systems business groups for engineering reasons.
The Power Division’s integrated system simultaneously handles electrical energy, mechanical energy, and thermal energy across the vehicle.

More than 90% of EV motors are permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSM) using heavy rare earths, creating structural supply chain vulnerabilities for European and American OEMs due to China's concentration of processing and production.
Rare earth extraction and processing generate significant CO2 emissions, resulting in a bad carbon footprint.
Valeo’s EESM (Externally Excited Synchronous Motor) generates its electromagnetic field using copper windings and an induced electrical current in the rotor instead of permanent magnets.
The EESM technology cuts the electric motor's carbon footprint by more than 40% compared to an equivalent permanent magnet motor.
Renault and BMW have used variants of EESM technology.
Supplying current to a high-speed spinning rotor traditionally uses brushes, which require roughly 5 centimetres of extra packaging space and wear out over the vehicle's lifetime.
Valeo and MAHLE jointly announced the iBEE (Inner Brushless Electrical Excitation) system in October 2024 to eliminate physical brushes.
The iBEE system uses inductive power transfer, the same physics as wireless phone charging, to supply current to the rotor without physical contact.
MAHLE contributes its Contactless Transmitter (MCT) technology for the brushless rotor, while Valeo provides the inverter, motor control algorithms, and high-efficiency power electronics.
The combined system targets peak power of 220 to 350 kW for premium segment vehicles.
This partnership extends a programme Valeo began in 2022 with an unnamed European OEM.
The first EESM validation program covered B and C segment vehicles, completed more than six months of testing, and exceeded initial targets on both power density and efficiency.
Valeo now operates software maintenance contracts with OEMs that extend active development work up to 10 to 15 years beyond a vehicle's Start of Production.
Approximately 25% of Tier 1 automotive software coding is now AI-assisted, a figure that stood at zero in 2016.
Valeo is developing a software layer called Valeo Predict for Range that draws on weather data, GPS routing, driving patterns, and vehicle sensor inputs to optimize energy management in real time.
EVs are more reliable than conventional cars due to simpler construction, architecture, and fewer actuators or sensors.
US-based taxi company Tesloop documented individual Tesla vehicles exceeding one million kilometres.
OEMs actively monitor battery state of health (SOH) through OTA telemetry to detect degradation before it affects the driver.
Renault managed early battery reliability by leasing the battery pack for the first-generation Zoe so it could be replaced if SOH declined below an acceptable threshold.
Battery pack costs have fallen sharply due to volume scaling and manufacturing efficiency, eliminating the need for a chemistry breakthrough to reach viability.
Similar cost reductions have flowed through Valeo’s own product lines, including inverters, onboard chargers, and motor systems.
The upfront purchase price for EVs currently remains 20 to 30% higher than a comparable combustion vehicle, but achieving parity is now an active commercial objective.
source_title: Valeo's Heat Pump Strategy for EV Winter Range

facts: Battery cooling systems optimize Li-ion batteries’ lifetime and durability.
These systems extend range and reliability of electric vehicles.
The cooling systems use either air or the A/C system’s refrigerant.
A chiller enables recovery of extra cooling in summer or extra heat from the battery pack in winter to be reused for cabin comfort.
source_title: Our battery cooling systems optimize Li-ion batteries | Valeo

facts: Valeo’s EV Tech Days took place from 23rd to the 27th September, 2024, in Hungary.
The event aimed to inform key customers and journalists from across Europe about the aftermarket division's role in electrification.
The program was made up of a plenary conference in Budapest, onsite workshops, and a visit to Valeo’s Veszprém Factory.
Valeo’s Veszprém Factory is a multi-products site that specializes in the production of high-voltage technologies and systems for well-known automotive car makers.
The EV Tech Days showcased Valeo’s approach to High Voltage Powertrain Electrification, Thermal Management, 48V Hybrid Technology, and EV Training with Valeo's Tech Academy.
By 2030, 93% of all new cars will include a form of electrification.
One-third of the European car parc will consist of electrified vehicles (either fully electric or hybrid) by 2030.

Electrification is emerging as the most viable, affordable, and energy-efficient solution to reducing the carbon footprint of the automotive industry, which currently stands at about 18% of global CO2 emissions.

Current and future car parcs include multiple levels of electrification: low-voltage systems like 48V mild hybrids (combining small electric motors with internal combustion engines for improved fuel efficiency), high-voltage systems like 400V and 800V for both full and plug-in hybrids, and fully electric vehicles.
Valeo’s strength lies in its ability to support all of these levels of electrification.
As a pioneer in electrification for over 20 years, Valeo has continually introduced innovative solutions for electrified vehicles.
In 2004, Valeo created the iStARS system, a belt starter/alternator that optimizes Stop-Start functionality.
In 2012, Valeo launched its 48V Belt Starter Generator (iBSG), providing mild-hybrid solutions.
In 2016, Valeo oversaw the creation of the Valeo Siemens eAutomotive JV.
In 2024, Valeo is set to produce the next generation of “rare-earth-free” electric motors with Renault.
As of today, one-third of the world’s vehicles are equipped with Valeo electrics, from alternators right through to high-voltage systems.
To meet the evolving needs of the industry, Valeo launched the Valeo Tech Academy, an innovative training program that offers both digital and in-person learning.
One in two mechanics is currently unable to maintain or repair electric vehicles.
Valeo’s goal is to help build the sustainable mobility of tomorrow.
source_title: Valeo EV Tech Days 2024 | Valeo Service

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and the Technical College System of Georgia (TCSG) announced the winners of the 2024 Governor’s Workforce Summit Awards.
The awards were presented during the annual two-day Governor’s Workforce Summit held at the Georgia World Congress Center in Atlanta.
The summit brings together more than 1,000 educators, business and industry leaders, government officials, and community leaders to highlight and strengthen workforce initiatives across Georgia.
Aurubis Richmond was named Business Partner of the Year.
Floyd County College and Career Academy was named College and Career Academy of the Year.
Jesse Price from Houston College and Career Academy and Northside High School won Work-Based Learning Student of the Year, with the award sponsored by the Georgia Foundation for Public Education.
Cullee Peavey Richardson from Ware County High School won Youth Apprenticeship Completer of the Year, with the award sponsored by the Georgia Foundation for Public Education.
Jayden Davis from Athens Technical College Academy won Dual Achievement Program Student of the Year, with the award sponsored by the TCSG Foundation.
Moatapari Agbere from Chattahoochee Valley College and Career Academy won Dual Enrollment Student of the Year, with the award sponsored by the TCSG Foundation.
Joshua Cain from Rockdale Career Academy won College and Career Academy Student of the Year, with the award sponsored by Georgia Power and The Munn Firm.

TCSG Commissioner Greg Dozier stated that the award winners demonstrate the power of strong partnerships between education and industry in creating opportunities for students and building a highly skilled workforce.
The Technical College System of Georgia consists of 22 colleges and serves as Georgia’s top resource for skilled workers.
TCSG offers world-class training in more than 600 associate degree, diploma, and certificate programs.
Students are trained on state-of-the-art equipment by instructors who are experts in their fields.
TCSG houses Georgia’s Office of Adult Education, which promotes and provides adult literacy and education programs, including the GED® testing program, throughout the state.
TCSG partners with companies through Quick Start, described as the nation’s top customized workforce training program.
Individual TCSG colleges work with local industry to provide workforce and training solutions.

links.context: Contact email for Mark D’Alessio, Executive Director of Communications

source_title: Gov. Kemp and TCSG Announce Winners of 2024 Governor’s Workforce Summit Awards – TCSG | Technical College System of Georgia

facts: French car parts maker Valeo posted slightly better-than-expected half-year core profit and reiterated its full-year guidance, citing a projected increase in global car output.

Valeo's half-year earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation fell nearly 8% year-on-year to 1.11 billion euros ($1.12 billion), but beat a company-supplied analyst consensus forecast of 1.07 billion euros.
CEO Christophe Perillat stated that the context has been difficult due to microchip shortages that have dogged the automotive sector, but said the chip situation is improving.
Additional pressures on auto parts manufacturers include rising costs in tight supply chains required to produce components.
Valeo forecast a 2022 core profit margin in a range of 11.8-12.3%, down from 13.4% the previous year.
IHS Markit forecast global production of light vehicles would hit 81 million in 2022, up from about 77 million in 2021.
Valeo reported being particularly pleased with its order intake, especially in vehicle electrification and advanced driving assistance systems.
The European Union is pushing ahead with plans for net-zero emissions by 2050, prompting automakers and suppliers to ramp up investments in electric vehicles.
Valeo signed a major contract with BMW to equip the German carmaker's upcoming EV platform with its advanced driving assistance systems (ADAS).
In February, Valeo announced plans jointly with Renault to develop a new-generation electric-motor vehicle in France.
Valeo unveiled its "Move Up" plan in February, which bets on the accelerated adoption of vehicle electrification and ADAS systems.
Order intake for Valeo in the first six months of 2022 was 13.1 billion euros, and 2.9 billion for Valeo Siemens eAutomotive, putting the group perfectly in line with the Move Up plan.
source_title: Valeo posts profit beat amid 'improving' chip shortage, EV adoption accelerates | Euronews

facts: Valeo recorded first-quarter sales of 5.3 billion euros.
Original equipment sales remained stable on a like-for-like basis (LFL).
Aftermarket sales increased by 3% on a like-for-like basis (LFL).
The company confirmed its 2025 objectives based on S&P Global Mobility assumptions, US tariffs, and related trade restrictions effective as of April 29, 2025.
Valeo maintained original equipment sales in Q1 despite delayed production start-ups in North America and a rapid change in the customer mix in China.

CEO Christophe Périllat stated that the company is accelerating restructuring plans to reduce administrative and selling costs by around 5% and investments in property, plant, and equipment as well as intangible assets by around 15% in the first half of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024.
The company is conducting an exhaustive review of its supply chain to neutralize the direct impact of tariffs and obtain compensation for 100% of remaining costs.
Valeo secured recent order intake from Volkswagen, demonstrating strong business momentum in ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems).
The company aims to increase content per vehicle through expertise in hardware and software for driving assistance technologies.
source_title: Press release – Q1 2025 Sales | Valeo

facts: Started in Monon, Indiana in 2003.
Has grown to be a premier manufacturer of dry and refrigerated semi-trailers for the North American market.
Operates factories in Monon, IN and Trenton, GA.
Takes great pride in offering superior corrosion protection, high strength and long-life specifications and advanced safety components as standard features on every trailer.
Is a wholly owned subsidiary of VGTH, Vanguard Global Trailer Holdings.
source_title: Vanguard National Trailer Corp. - About Us

facts: Optronics International announced the debut of what it says is the industry’s first stop, tail, turn lamps with integrated graphics.
The new lamps merge brand identities into the lighting design, juxtaposing a smooth GloLight lens appearance against a more traditional LED pattern.
GloLight technology allows Optronics to consolidate complex graphics into the functional operation of the lamps.
The lamp’s traditional LED-style lens pattern surrounds the logo graphic in the middle of the lamp.

The GloLight logo portion of the lamp illuminates when operating in its standard function as a tail lamp, and when the lamp’s turn signal or brake functions are in operation, all portions of the LED lamp brighten.
Brett Johnson, Optronics International president and CEO, stated this is an industry first and a novel use for their GloLight technology.
Johnson noted that logos and branding have been widely incorporated into conspicuity tape for decades, so the migration to lighting is a logical one.
Marketing professionals recognize that the 4-in. round and 6-in. oval lighting formats are among the most noticeable features on any vehicle.
Johnson said whether consciously aware or not, drivers constantly monitor commercial vehicle taillights, particularly at night.
Optronics employed its 3-D printing capabilities in preparing prototypes for manufacturers.
The company worked with a number of OEMs during lamp development, including Vanguard National Trailer Corporation and Miller Industries.
Charlie Mudd, president of Vanguard National Trailer Corporation, stated they were among the first trailer manufacturers to make Optronics’ GloLight part of their standard lighting package.

Will Miller, president and co-CEO of Miller Industries, stated that with the new custom logo stop, tail, turn lamps, Optronics presented an opportunity to further their brand’s recognition and strength.
The LED lamps offer users a broad level of creative design flexibility for branding because the GloLight technology can be used in both red and white or a combination of both.
Lens striations can be used to achieve unique and complementary visual effects.
source_title: Optronics introduces lamps with GloLight technology | Trucks, Parts, Service

facts: Orders for new trailers rose a slight 0.2% in July from a year earlier to 13,339, according to ACT Research.
Trailer orders increased year-over-year in 16 of the past 19 months but were down 3.2% to 128,144 through the first seven months of 2012.
The July order total was 2% below the 13,615 orders recorded for June.
Frank Maly, director of commercial vehicle transportation analysis at ACT, reported softness on the truck and trailer orders side over recent months due to marketplace uncertainty.
Fleets are exercising caution on investments due to a wait-and-see attitude regarding the economy, November elections, and fuel prices.

Chris Hammond, vice president of dealer sales at Great Dane Trailers in Savannah, Ga., noted that while the election is keeping some buyers on the sideline, there remains significant interest in upgrading fleets.

Jeff Bennett, vice president of engineering at Utility Trailer Manufacturing Co. in City of Industry, Calif., cited summer doldrums, slowing global and domestic economic activity, and rising fuel costs as factors indicating trailer demand will not grow substantially.
Refrigerated fleets are analyzing new reefer units ahead of new Environmental Protection Agency emissions standards taking effect on Jan. 1.
Hammond stated that flatbeds are picking up, vans are good, truckload reefers have slowed, and he remains optimistic about next year.
Mike Cobb, sales manager at Vanguard National Trailer Corp. in Monon, Ind., explained that potential buyers are waiting for 2013 vendor pricing from suspension and tire makers before placing orders.

David Giesen, vice president of sales and marketing at Stoughton Trailers LLC. in Stoughton, Wis., described the period as a usual summer slowdown but noted good quote and order activity relative to the season.
Maly observed that most trailer buyers are keeping existing orders on the books but are unwilling to significantly expand commitments into the future.
The July cancellation rate stood at 1.2% of manufacturers' backlogs, down from 1.8% in June.
A recent uptick in cancellations was driven by liquid and dry bulk trailers as the market adjusted to changes in energy exploration expenditures.
Manufacturers' backlogs stood at 92,214 at the end of July, down from 97,935 at the end of June.
Maly characterized the backlog decline as typical for summer and predicted order intake would kick up around the September and October timeframe.
Utility Trailer announced plans to open a 22,000-square-foot expansion at its Paragould, Ark., dry van plant in September.
Utility Trailer is constructing a 50,000-square-foot modification center for high option and complex refrigerated trailers at its Marion, Va., plant.
Bennett stated that expanding production facilities is necessary to meet goals, improve labor efficiency, and increase production even if trailer demand continues to slip.
Wabash National Corp. and Hyundai Translead declined to comment on market conditions.
source_title: July Trailer Orders Rise 0.2% as Fleets Remain Cautious - TT

facts: Vanguard National is building a new dry van trailer plant in Trenton, Georgia.
The company has been manufacturing van trailers in Monon, Indiana for its second decade of operations.
Management identified the Southeast United States as having zero dry-freight van manufacturing plants, creating a significant market opportunity.
Charlie Mudd, president of Vanguard National, stated that the Southeast was underserved due to the lack of dry van manufacturing plants.
Vanguard began searching for a Southeast location approximately three years prior to the article's publication.
The company evaluated current demand, future scalability, and overall trailer industry capacity when determining the appropriate plant size.
Trailer manufacturers set a production record in 2015, with the last time over 300,000 trailers were built in a year occurring in 1999.
Vanguard designed the new facility to produce 10,000 trailers per year to avoid diluting overall industry capacity.
The plant has been in operation since August and was designed exclusively for dry freight vans.
Vanguard acquired assets from HPA Monon, manufacturers of Monon Trailers, in 2003.
Mark Roush, Vanguard’s vice-president of engineering, noted the new plant consolidates operations under one roof, improving efficiency compared to the company's multi-building Monon campus.
The new plant features a U-shaped material flow with special carts carrying trailers through the assembly process.
The facility includes new automated equipment and 70 feet between support columns to accommodate potential 60-ft trailers.
Vanguard selected an industrial park site in Trenton, Georgia, located just off Interstate 59, 20 miles southwest of Chattanooga, Tennessee.
Charlie Mudd stated there was strong support from the state of Georgia, Dade County, and the city of Trenton for the project.
At full capacity, the plant will contribute 400 jobs to the local area.
Employment at the time of publication was 90 people, with plans to hire approximately 90 more.
Kevin Black serves as the plant manager for the new facility.
The company planned a gradual ramp-up to full production over a three-year period.
Vanguard broke ground on the project on September 3, 2015.
The 356,000-square-foot plant began manufacturing trailers less than a year after groundbreaking.
The project was completed on time and under a $36 million budget.
The estimated production capacity of 10,000 trailers aims to shrink customer backlogs that can stretch up to a year during periods of high trailer demand.
Vanguard designed and planned the entire project internally without external contractors.
Vanguard held an open house for dealers and suppliers on November 3 to celebrate the plant's opening.
source_title: Vanguard National seeks geographic leverage with new dry van plant | Trailer Body Builders

facts: Optronics International announced that it will be displaying stop, tail, turn lamps with integrated graphics.
The new lamps merge brand identities into the lighting design by juxtaposing a smooth GloLight lens appearance against a more traditional LED pattern.
Examples of the new 4” round and 6” oval stop, tail, turn lamps will be displayed publicly for the first time in booth 2547 at the North American Commercial Vehicle (NACV) Show 2019.
GloLight technology allows Optronics to consolidate even complex graphics into the functional operation of the lamps.
The new lamp’s traditional LED-style lens pattern surrounds the logo graphic in the middle of the lamp.
The GloLight logo portion of the lamp illuminates when operating in its standard function as a tail lamp.
When the lamp’s turn signal or brake functions are in operation, all portions of the LED lamp brighten.
Brett Johnson, president and CEO of Optronics International, stated that this is an industry first and a novel use for their GloLight technology.
Johnson noted that logos and branding have been widely incorporated into conspicuity tape for decades, so the migration to lighting is a logical one.
Marketing professionals recognize that the 4” round and 6” oval lighting formats are among the most noticeable features on any vehicle.
Johnson stated that whether consciously aware or not, drivers constantly monitor commercial vehicle tail lights, particularly at night.
Optronics employed its 3-D printing capabilities in preparing prototypes for the manufacturers.
The company worked with a number of OEMs during the development of the lamp, including Vanguard National Trailer Corporation and Miller Industries.
Charlie Mudd, president of Vanguard National Trailer Corporation, stated they were among the first trailer manufacturers to make Optronics’ GloLight part of their standard lighting package.
Mudd praised Optronics’ technical, creative, and pioneering spirit evident in the design of these new lamps.
The LED lamps offer users a broad level of creative design flexibility for company branding because the GloLight technology can be used in both red and white or a combination of both.
Lens striations can also be used to achieve unique and complementary visual effects.
Will Miller, president and Co-CEO of Miller Industries, said Optronics’ new custom logo stop, tail, turn lamps present an opportunity to further their brand’s recognition and strength.
source_title: Optronics introduces custom LED logo lamps with GloLight technology | Vehicle Service Pros

facts: Vanguard National Trailer Corporation is located at 8 Vanguard Drive, Trenton, GA 30752.
The company operates under NAICS code 336212 for Truck Trailer Manufacturing and Motor Vehicle Body and Trailer Manufacturing.
Records are current through July 17, 2023.
The establishment has had 2 OSHA inspections since August 20, 2018, resulting in 2 violations and $2,827 in penalties on record.
The most recent inspection took place on July 17, 2023, and has been closed.
Out of 2 violations documented at this establishment, all were classified as Serious, representing 100% of citations.
There are no willful or repeat violations in the inspection history for this establishment.
Every OSHA inspection on record at the location has been closed.

Common workplace hazards in this industry include welding fume exposure, cuts and lacerations from sheet metal, falls from working on large vehicle bodies, noise from metal fabrication, exposure to paints and adhesives, and ergonomic injuries from overhead and awkward-position work.

Employers in truck trailer manufacturing reported 4.9 work-related injuries and illnesses per 100 full-time workers in 2024 according to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses.
source_title: VANGUARD NATIONAL TRAILER CORPORATION in TRENTON, GA - OSHA Inspections: 2 Violations, $2,827 Penalties

facts: Georgia Governor Nathan Deal spoke with Denny Williams, COO of Vanguard, and Charles Mudd, President and CEO of Vanguard.
An announcement was made at a new Vanguard site in Trenton, Ga.
The semi-trailer manufacturing plant is bringing hundreds of jobs to Dade County.
The announcement event occurred on October 16, 2015.
source_title: Georgia Gov. Nathan Deal, left, speaks to Denny Williams COO of Vanguard, center, and Charles Mudd, President and CEO of Vanguard, right, after an announcement at the new Vanguard site in Trenton, Ga., that the semi-trailer manufacturing plant is bringing hundreds of jobs to Dade County. (Dan Henry/Chattanooga Times Free Press via AP Stock Photo - Alamy

facts: Truck fleets ordered 13,200 new commercial trailers during July.
July orders represented a 20% increase over July 2010 but marked the fourth straight month of declining figures.
Trailer manufacturers attributed the declines to a typical summer slowdown.
Two officials reported seeing a rebound in preliminary August orders.
Glenn Harney, chief sales officer of Hyundai Translead in San Diego, stated that their July orders mirrored ACT Research's data but noted an exceptionally strong August.
Harney noted that fleets are still replacing aging trailers and getting plans together for 2012.
Through July, there were 132,300 net trailer orders this year compared to 82,900 in the first seven months of 2010.
Monthly order totals remained above 20,000 through March, peaking at 28,705 before declining to 19,579 in April, 13,600 in May, and 13,428 in June.
Frank Maly, ACT’s director of commercial vehicle transportation and analysis, reported that net orders were up 60% year-over-year through July.
The existing trailer backlog was almost double that of a year ago according to ACT.
The 2011 build backlog stood at 93,600 at the end of July, up 97% from the July 2010 backlog of 47,400.
Charles Mudd, president of Vanguard National Trailer Corp. in Monon, Ind., stated there was no drop in activity in July and that they had started accepting orders for the first quarter of next year.
Vanguard’s factory was maintaining maximum capacity.
Mudd noted that many big fleets have not yet bought new trailers and does not detect stronger growth for 2012 than currently, but enough to support current manufacturing levels.

Craig Bennett, senior vice president of sales and marketing for Utility Trailer Manufacturing Co. in the City of Industry, Calif., said new orders slowed during July like a normal summer slowdown but remained fairly strong overall.
Utility’s backlog returned to 2011 beginning-of-the-year levels, averaging around three-and-a-half months.
ACT’s Maly explained that total new orders were exactly the same as June, with the difference coming from a 2% cancellation rate below normal.
Chris Hammond, vice president of dealer sales at Great Dane Trailers in Savannah, Ga., agreed with the summer lull assessment but expressed uncertainty about the second half of the year.
Hammond stated that trailer fleet averages are over eight years old, which will continue to drive the trailer business into 2012.
Dry-van sales led the early-year surge, but flatbed and reefer sales are now bursting at the seams according to Hammond.
Hammond noted economic uncertainty could impact the third and fourth quarters.
David Giesen, vice president of sales and marketing at Stoughton Trailers in Stoughton, Wis., reported customers are buying new trailers to replace old ones rather than expand fleets.
Giesen stated that more replacement is needed and growth will continue even if fleets do not expand.
June and July order levels were typical of summer slowdowns, with Stoughton busy building orders from earlier in the year.
Some potential customers were apprehensive about the slow economy, but August saw an uptick in the latter part of the month.
Hyundai’s Harney stated the company just started planning production expansion after being sold out for the year.
Hyundai’s trailer plant is located in Tijuana, Mexico.
In 2009, new trailer orders totaled 81,300, the lowest since 1975.
In 2010, orders quickened in the second half of the year, rising from about 11,000 in July to 21,314 in November.
source_title: July Trailer Orders at 13,200, Down for 4th Month in a Row - TT

facts: In 2004, Vernay moved its North American production operations to Georgia and closed its manufacturing plant at the corner of Dayton Street and East Enon Road.
Vernay relocated its production force, which once numbered about 200 employees, to two plants in Georgia and a small facility in South Carolina.
The company cited shifting markets and the demands of contamination cleanup in Yellow Springs as reasons for moving its production operations to Georgia.
source_title: Vernay to demolish building • The Yellow Springs News

facts: Vernay Laboratories will host a grand opening ceremony for its new Kennesaw facility on January 16, 2025.
The grand opening event will include a ribbon-cutting ceremony, facility tours, and presentations by Vernay’s leadership team.
Local officials, industry partners, and Vernay employees have been invited to attend the grand opening celebration.
The Kennesaw facility specializes in liquid silicone rubber (LSR) molding and micro-molding for medical and industrial markets.
The facility is currently equipped with 10 advanced production presses.
The facility currently utilizes only a quarter of its total capacity.
Vernay plans to expand the Kennesaw facility's press count to 45.
Each state-of-the-art production cell at the facility can produce 64 parts every 32 seconds.
The facility enables an annual production capacity of over 200 million micro-molded parts.
Andy Woodward, Vice President of Sales & Marketing, stated that the opening demonstrates Vernay’s dedication to advancing manufacturing technologies and meeting customer needs.
Woodward noted that the investment reinforces a focus on precision, efficiency, and delivering high-quality, custom-engineered solutions for highly regulated industries.
The facility allows Vernay to deliver fully functional prototypes quickly, moving ideas from concept to production faster than ever before.
Vernay Laboratories was founded in 1946 and is a technology leader in the design, development, and manufacture of customized flow-control components.
The company formulates its own compounds to address each specific application.
Vernay’s products play a vital role in millions of medical devices, automobiles, and consumer products.
Vernay is a privately held firm that produces more than 1 billion components per year globally.
The company draws from a library of more than 33,000 elastomeric materials and compound formulations.
Vernay operates six plants worldwide located in the U.S., the Netherlands, Italy, and China.

facts: Governor Andy Beshear congratulated Vista Metals Tuesday as Kentucky’s metals industry continues to grow at a rapid pace.
Vista Metals plans to locate a new specialty aluminum products operation in Bowling Green, creating over 50 full-time jobs behind an approximate $60 million investment.
Initial site preparation is already underway, and Vista Metals will commence with a phased build-out to support the growth in its core markets.
As part of this project, Vista Metals plans to acquire more than 100 acres and the construction of a 75,000-plus-square-foot facility.
Jobs created at the facility will include operations, planning, engineering and site management.
VMC Specialty Alloys LLC (dba Vista Metals Georgia) operates under a licensing agreement with Fontana, California-based Vista Metals Corporation.

Along with Vista Progressive Metals LLC (a producer of aluminum lithium products), the group of companies are market leaders in the manufacturing of specialty aluminum products supporting aerospace, commercial aircraft, defense, automotive and general industrial applications.
source_title: Vista Metals Bringing Jobs To Bowling Green

facts: Vista Metals Georgia has completed the company's seventh casthouse.
This completion is a significant milestone in Vista's ongoing $100 million long-range capital plan.
The expansion represents the second phase of the company’s plan.
The project includes the installation of melting furnaces, homogenizing ovens and support equipment at its Adairsville, Ga., facility.
Kelly Thomas is president of Vista Metals Georgia.
Vista’s investment in this expansion will further support growth in core markets including outer space, commercial aviation, defense and industrial applications.
The company is completing final equipment installation in a new world class finishing facility which is a critical part of this expansion phase.
Vista Metals previously announced an investment in a new location to serve the aerospace industry with specialty aluminum products.
The new facility is located in Bowling Green, Ky., and is part of Vista Metals’ long range investment plan.
This new Kentucky facility will expand upon Vista Metals’ existing capabilities in California and Georgia.
