facts: Adient plc is an American, Irish-domiciled company that operates as the largest manufacturer of automotive seating for customers worldwide and is based in Plymouth, Michigan, United States.
Adient was founded as a spin-off from Johnson Controls on October 31, 2016, establishing its legal domicile in Dublin, Ireland.
Johnson Controls had entered the automotive seating business in 1985 by acquiring Hoover Universal.

In September 2017, Adient acquired the Oak Park, Michigan-based automotive seat manufacturer Futuris from Clearlake Capital, which added 15 facilities in Asia and North America, including one facility based in Newark, California, and was anticipated to increase the company's revenue by $500 million annually.
In 2016, Adient announced plans to move its global operating headquarters to the Marquette Building in Detroit, but canceled those plans as of June 2018.

In January 2018, a joint venture was formed by Adient (50.01%) and Boeing (49.99%) to develop and manufacture airliner seats for new installations or retrofit, a $4.5 billion market in 2017 which was then forecast to grow to $6 billion by 2026, to be based in Kaiserslautern near Frankfurt and distributed by Boeing subsidiary Aviall, with its customer service center in Seattle.
In February 2020, Adient announced it agreed to sell its 30% stake in Yanfeng Global Automotive Interior Systems to Yanfeng Automotive Trim Systems for $379 million.
In March 2020, Adient entered into an agreement to sell its automotive fabrics manufacturing business to Sage Automotive Interiors for $175 million.
As of 2026, Adient employs more than 65,000 people across ~200 manufacturing/assembly plants in 29 countries.
Adient reported revenue of US$14.5 billion in 2025.
Jerome Dorlack serves as the CEO of Adient.

Adient operates manufacturing facilities in Morocco (Kénitra, Tiflet), Lesotho (Maseru), South Africa (Pretoria/Rosslyn, Silverton, Waltloo, Gqeberha/Korsten), Thailand (Chachoengsao, Laem Chabang), South Korea (Asan, Busan), Malaysia (Malacca), Indonesia (Purwakarta), India (Bengaluru, Chennai, Lucknow), China (Beijing, Changchun, Guangzhou, Hangzhou, Hefei), and the United States (Greenfield, Ohio).
Adient's ISIN is IE00BD845X29.

facts: Adient is a leader in automotive seating.
Adient presented its latest seating innovations at IAA 2023.
The current automotive business landscape is marked by shifting industry dynamics, showcasing a strong desire for mobility, with an emphasis on digitalization, cost, and sustainable products.
Adient’s overall approach focuses on responding to the need for more sustainable material use while taking advantage of the potential that sustainable practices hold for streamlining processes.
The Pure Essential seat is designed for overall cost and complexity reduction in manufacturing and is especially lightweight.

Environmentally-conscious practices such as material separation and recycling, and design for disassembly are embedded into the manufacturing process from the development stage for the Pure Essential seat.
The Pure Essential seat consists of two materials only: green steel and recyclable polyester (PET).
The Autonomous Elegance seat is specifically developed to meet new customer needs in terms of premium comfort and to fit Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS).
State-of-the-art findings on ergonomics and human body kinematics have been incorporated into the Autonomous Elegance seat following extensive occupant research.
The Autonomous Elegance seat is complemented by advanced comfort assets such as noise cancellation and advanced climate functions.
David Herberg serves as Vice President Engineering Adient EMEA.

David Herberg highlighted: “Our seat demonstrators provide solutions to our customers’ main concerns, and we are looking forward to continuing the strategic product dialogue with them, based on our new demonstrators.”
Most features of the Autonomous Elegance seat can already be offered for sourcing, such as the metal structure and seat kinematics (adjustment functions and mechanisms).
The Smart Efficiency seat has been updated considering optimized use of space as well as sustainability aspects.
The Smart Efficiency seat features a slimmer appearance than its predecessor without compromising on comfort.
The Smart Efficiency seat design helps save space and paves the way for new mobility concepts based on battery packaging in electric vehicles.

facts: Adient Automotive Seating announced that its latest foam plant in Alor Gajah, Melaka has begun exporting head restraint systems to Thailand and Indonesia.
The new RM10 million facility will serve both the local and export markets, and is fitted with state-of-the-art foam manufacturing technology.
The facility is equipped with a rainwater harvesting system and solar panels to supplement energy generation.
The plant features five-star power rated equipment that are low in carbon emissions, and the plant is completely lit via LED.

The company claims its manufacturing facility is the first in Malaysia to be equipped with the Hydra Manufacturing System, a smart technology that collects vast amounts of data from various equipment in real time.
Data from the system is accessible either onsite or remotely, allowing workers to quickly identify potential defects and their root causes.
Company managing director AJ Dabydin stated that people are at the centre of designing the automation despite the technology-driven approach.

Operators were reskilled and upskilled for effective man-machine interaction, and the plant layout was designed to allow for one-metre physical distancing and carefully planned logistic routes to keep workers safe.
Since 1983, Adient has been consistently investing to improve and expand the capability and capacity of its manufacturing operations in Malaysia.
With the new foam plant in Alor Gajah, Adient is elevating its Malaysian operations to become an automotive export hub for the Southeast Asia region.
Through this initiative, Adient will drive parts localisation, develop local suppliers, and create more job opportunities and export revenues for Malaysians and Malaysia.
source_title: Adient Malaysia announces new RM10 million facility in Melaka - to export headrests to Thailand, Indonesia - paultan.org

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp joined the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) in announcing the state has again made history by surpassing $60.2 billion in exports in calendar year 2025.
Georgia's export value saw a year-over-year increase of 12.7%, outperforming the national average of 5.6%.
Georgia exported goods to 213 unique countries and territories in 2025.
Georgia ranked No. 7 for dollar value of total trade among U.S. states.
Georgia ranked No. 9 in the country for dollar value of exports.
Georgia was last in the top ten exporting states in 2020.
Georgia companies increased exports over the past decade by 68.9% despite pandemic disruptions.
Governor Brian Kemp stated Georgia has broken export records for five consecutive years.
Georgia's coastal and inland ports are continuing to grow capacity.
The state is making key investments in other logistics infrastructure.

Motor vehicles were a top export category for Georgia.
Network communications equipment was a top export category for Georgia.

Georgia serves as a global gateway for aerospace, automotive, agriculture, and advanced manufacturing.
The state facilitated more than $210.7 billion in total trade in 2025.

The top five products by total trade were civilian aircraft and ancillary parts; computers, hardware, and storage units; motor vehicles; network communications equipment; and parts and accessories for motor vehicles.
GDEcD Deputy Commissioner of Trade Lizann Grupalo stated Georgia's exports outpaced imports in year-over-year growth combined with record-breaking totals.
The GDEcD Trade team provides hands-on support, customized consultations, and data-driven insights to large and small exporters.
Georgia maintains more than a dozen international representatives covering over 60 global markets.
Markets with full-time Georgia representation accounted for 79% of exports in 2025.
Markets with full-time Georgia representation accounted for 88% of bilateral trade in 2025.
Georgia is home to Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, described as the busiest and most efficient airport in the world.
The Port of Savannah is the largest single-terminal container facility and third-busiest container gateway in the U.S.
The Port of Brunswick is one of the nation's top gateways for Roll-on/Roll-off cargo.
Georgia offers a robust rail and highway infrastructure with more rail miles than any other state in the Southeast.
Expanded connectivity through Georgia's inland ports and intermodal yards connects directly to deepwater ports by rail.
GDEcD's annual International Trade Report is based on data from the U.S. Census Bureau and Trade Data Monitor.
Trade Data Monitor tracks merchandise trade using four-digit Harmonized System (HS) codes and origin of movement.
GDEcD's Trade team includes more than a dozen International Representatives located in strategic global markets to assist Georgia companies with expanding sales worldwide.

facts: Archer Aviation Inc. announced that it received the certificate of occupancy for its high-volume manufacturing facility, ARC, earlier this week.
ARC is connected to the Covington Municipal Airport in Georgia.
Archer is currently working to complete the tooling load-in for the initial manufacturing line at this facility.
Production is planned to begin in early 2025.
The goal is to ramp to a rate of two aircraft per month by the end of the year.
Archer built ARC in close partnership with Stellantis, and the two companies are continuing to progress their plans to mass-produce Archer’s Midnight aircraft.
Stellantis is contributing capital, advanced manufacturing technology and expertise and experienced personnel with the goal of scaling this facility to 650 aircraft annually by 2030.

Georgia Governor Brian Kemp commented that the announcement marks the next step in a journey reaffirming Georgia's commitment to working with innovative companies like Archer to create quality jobs and opportunities for years to come.

Congressman Mike Collins of Georgia, who sits on the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure and Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, stated that the completion of the facility highlights Georgia’s leadership in innovation, advanced manufacturing and job creation.

Adam Goldstein, Archer’s founder and CEO, noted that the completion is a testament to the industry shifting from R&D into commercialization and praised the team for getting construction done fast and on budget.

Arnaud Deboeuf, Stellantis Chief Manufacturing and Supply Chain Officer, commented that supporting Archer’s electric aircraft growth with deep manufacturing expertise is a great opportunity for both companies as they take clean mobility to the sky.
ARC will be where Archer plans to produce its all-electric vertical take-off and landing aircraft, Midnight.
The Midnight aircraft is designed to offer passengers a sustainable, low-noise and safe alternative to ground transportation in congested cities around the world.
source_title: Archer Aviation Completes Georgia Air Taxi Factory - Middle Georgia CEO

facts: eVTOLs are electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft that are battery-powered and electrically flown for short-distance urban air mobility spanning 20- to 50-mile urban routes.
eVTOLs produce zero emissions and are much quieter than helicopters.
eVTOLs combine vertical takeoff and landing capabilities with the fixed-wing efficiency of airplanes.
eVTOLs can maneuver through tight spaces in crowded cities and fly at speeds up to 200 miles per hour.
eVTOLs typically seat four passengers and a pilot, shrinking a 60-minute commute to the airport to under 10 minutes.
eVTOLs can be used to transport passengers, cargo, or medical products.
Archer Aviation Inc. is a pre-revenue leading player in the air taxi industry.
Archer's lead prototype is named Midnight and can fly four passengers and a pilot at speeds of up to 150 miles per hour.
Archer Aviation has partnerships with United Airlines Holdings Inc.
Archer plans to launch its air taxi routes in Los Angeles and New York.
United Airlines agreed to purchase up to 200 aircraft to transport passengers to and from airports.
Archer has a manufacturing partnership with auto giant Stellantis N.V.
Archer is building a high-volume production factory in Georgia and is awaiting final certification.
Archer is burning over $200 million in cash per quarter.
Joby Aviation Inc. acquired Uber Technologies Inc.’s Elevate division in 2020.
Joby strengthened its bond with Uber and positioned itself as a leading operator in urban air mobility.
Joby focused on developing its ElevateOS operating platform, which would be integrated with Uber’s mobility platform.
Uber invested up to $125 million in Joby.
Uber plans to offer Joby's air taxi rides through its platform.
Joby sealed a partnership with Toyota Motor Corporation to provide expertise in scaling production of its aircraft to over 500 per year by 2028.
Delta Air Lines Inc. is also a partner for Joby.
Both Archer and Joby are currently in pre-revenue and pre-commercial stages.
Both companies plan on launching commercial services by early 2026, though there is no guarantee.
Operations carrying passengers hinge on acquiring FAA certifications to begin operations.
Both companies are currently losing money and are expected to continue losing money even after commercial operations begin.
Both companies burn through hundreds of millions of dollars every quarter.
The financial losses increase the likelihood of additional capital raises, which can further dilute shareholders' interests.
source_title: Rise of the eVTOLs: Air Taxis are Coming to a City Near You

facts: Industry-first high-volume facility for manufacturing eVTOL aircraft is strategically located adjacent to the Covington Municipal Airport in Covington, GA.
Archer and Stellantis are set to join forces to begin manufacturing Archer’s Midnight eVTOL aircraft at the facility in 2024.
On February 28th, Archer closed on the local incentive transaction for the project that, along with a Georgia REBA grant, totals ~33% of the planned capital investment.
On March 2nd, Archer began construction on Phase 1 of the project -- build out of a ~350,000 square-foot facility capable of producing up to 650 aircraft per year.
The Covington facility will be capable of being expanded by an additional 800,000 square feet, which is estimated to support long-term production targets of up to 2,300 aircraft per year.
In January, Archer announced an expanded relationship with Stellantis under which the two companies will join forces to manufacture the company’s production aircraft, Midnight.

The partnership leverages Archer’s world-class team of eVTOL, electric powertrain and certification experts alongside Stellantis’s advanced manufacturing technology and expertise, experienced personnel, and capital.

The combination is intended to enable rapid scaling of aircraft production to meet Archer’s commercialization plans while allowing Archer to avoid hundreds of millions of dollars of spending during the manufacturing ramp up phase.
The goal is for Stellantis to mass produce Archer’s eVTOL aircraft as its exclusive contract manufacturer.
Adam Goldstein, Founder and CEO of Archer, stated that scale is key to unlocking the promise of the eVTOL industry and expressed excitement over support from the State of Georgia and Newton County.
A ceremonial event marking the milestone will be held in April in Covington, GA.
Archer Aviation Inc. designs and develops electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft for use in urban air mobility networks, with its team based in Santa Clara, CA.
source_title: Archer Aviation - Archer Closes Incentive Transaction; Begins Construction on First of its Kind High-Volume eVTOL Manufacturing Facility in Covington, GA

facts: Archer Aviation, Inc. is a California-based aerospace company developing electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft designed to serve as sustainable urban air mobility solutions.
The company was founded in 2018 by Adam Goldstein and Brett Adcock.
Archer focuses on the design, development and certification of zero-emissions air taxis aimed at reducing traffic congestion in densely populated metropolitan areas.
The company’s flagship prototypes are named “Maker” and “Midnight.”
The aircraft are engineered to deliver quiet, efficient short-haul flights with ranges of up to 100 miles per charge.
Archer is headquartered in Palo Alto, California.
Archer operates a manufacturing facility in nearby Santa Cruz County.
The company maintains research partnerships with automotive and energy companies, including a collaboration with Stellantis to integrate advanced battery systems.
Archer’s aircraft feature a modular battery architecture, distributed electric propulsion, and a composite fuselage to optimize performance and safety.
These technical innovations support a projected cruise speed of approximately 150 miles per hour and a low noise profile.
Archer has secured pre-orders and purchase agreements with major transportation and airline partners.
The company has a strategic partnership with United Airlines to provide regional air taxi services in select U.S. markets.

The company aims to launch commercial operations in key urban centers, leveraging its strong executive leadership under CEO Adam Goldstein and a team of experienced aerospace engineers and aviation specialists.
Archer targets airport-to-city and city-center-to-city-center routes to establish a scalable network addressing the growing demand for on-demand, point-to-point air travel.

As Archer advances through the certification process with the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), the company continues flight testing of its prototypes at its private test facility in southern California.
Archer’s development roadmap includes securing type certification for its production aircraft, ramping up manufacturing capacity, and expanding service offerings into new geographies.
source_title: Archer Aviation (ACHR) Stock Price, News & Analysis

facts: Stellantis invests an additional $55 million in Archer following the achievement of its transition flight test milestone last month.
This latest investment builds on $39 million worth of open market purchases of Archer stock that Stellantis completed in March of this year.
During 2023, Stellantis invested $110 million in Archer through a combination of open market stock purchases and investments under the companies’ strategic funding agreement.
Construction nears completion on Archer’s high-volume manufacturing facility in Covington, GA, which is on track to be completed later this year.
The first phase of the build out is a ~350,000 square foot facility on an ~100 acre site.
The facility is designed to support production of up to 650 aircraft annually.
Archer aims to establish a factory that can support its planned commercial ramp up by leveraging Stellantis as its contract manufacturer.
Stellantis has been a strategic partner to Archer since 2020 through various collaboration initiatives, and an investor since 2021.
Archer leverages Stellantis’ deep manufacturing, supply chain, and design expertise in connection with designing, developing, and commercializing its eVTOL aircraft.
Archer’s Midnight is a piloted, four-passenger aircraft designed to perform rapid back-to-back flights with minimal charge time between flights.

Archer aims to transform urban travel by replacing 60–90 minute car commutes with estimated 10–20 minute electric air taxi flights that are safe, sustainable, low noise, and cost-competitive with ground transportation.
Carlos Tavares serves as Stellantis CEO.
Adam Goldstein serves as Archer CEO.

facts: Archer Aviation is constructing a nearly 400,000 square foot high-volume manufacturing facility in Covington, Georgia.

The goal is to scale the ARC facility to produce 650 aircraft annually by 2030.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp commented on Archer's choice to build the new manufacturing facility in Newton County, highlighting the creation of quality jobs and opportunities for Georgians.

Congressman Mike Collins of Georgia, who sits on the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure and the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, highlighted Georgia’s leadership in innovation, advanced manufacturing, and job creation driven by the facility.
Adam Goldstein, Archer’s founder and CEO, stated that the completion of the facility marks a shift from R&D into commercialization, with production starting in early 2025.
Adam Goldstein noted that construction was completed fast and on budget.

Arnaud Deboeuf, Stellantis Chief Manufacturing and Supply Chain Officer, commented that supporting Archer’s electric aircraft growth leverages Stellantis's deep manufacturing expertise as a significant step toward taking clean mobility to the sky.

Archer has partnered with Southwest Airlines to make electric air travel a reality.

Archer Aviation launched in 2018.
Archer announced it has raised an additional $300 million, bringing total funding to $1 billion.
Archer's stock trades under the ticker ACHR on the New York Stock Exchange.
Archer's stock price increased by 161.69% over the last six months.
Archer conducts daily test flights at its location in California.
Archer has a partnership with the United Arab Emirates to launch commercial air taxis there within the next year.
Archer is working with United Airlines and Southwest Airlines in cities including San Francisco, Los Angeles, and New York City.
In October 2024, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) recognized eVTOLs as a new category of civil aircraft and issued guidance on operating them and pilot training.
Archer focuses on design and building a consumer experience for its aircraft.

facts: Pat Wilson serves as Commissioner of the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) and was named the 2023 Georgian of the Year.

Wilson previously served as director of government affairs for Gov. Sonny Perdue for three years starting in 2010, promoting the legislative agenda of the first Republican elected governor in Georgia since Reconstruction.
Wilson worked for Perdue in D.C. as federal affairs director and previously in the offices of U.S. Rep. Nathan Deal and the late Sen. Paul Coverdell.
Gov. Perdue encouraged Wilson to join GDEcD, telling him it would be great if he went to sell something he loves – the state of Georgia.
Wilson joined GDEcD as deputy commissioner for global commerce, later served as chief operating officer from 2011 to 2016, and was appointed commissioner by then-Gov. Nathan Deal.
Heidi Green, managing director of policy and government relations at Deloitte, was GDEcD commissioner in 2010 and noted Wilson's talent for building relationships and team culture.

Perdue, now chancellor of the University System of Georgia, credits Wilson with much of Georgia being named one of the best places to grow a business by communicating the governor and legislature's achievements.
During Wilson's six years leading GDEcD, Georgia has been named the No. 1 state to do business for nine consecutive years.
Georgia posted more than $21.2 billion in investments and 51,132 new jobs in FY2022.
Lee Thomas, deputy commissioner for film, music and digital entertainment at GDEcD (and Georgian of the Year in 2019), credits Wilson's institutional knowledge for helping the department.
Hyundai Motor Group’s new electric vehicle plant is located in Bryan County.
Rivian’s EV factory is located in Newton and Morgan counties.
Wilson grew up on his grandfather’s peach farm; his parents still live in Franklin County and his father serves on the City Council of Franklin Springs (population about 1,000).
In 2010, Wilson pitched a Caterpillar project to Gov. Perdue looking at Griffin for 300 jobs.
Kia operates a plant in West Point, Georgia, where the state continues to help with workforce needs as the facility expands.
The relationship leading to Hyundai's Georgia plant traces back to Kia’s first U.S. plant in West Point, announced in 2006.
SK Battery announced a production facility in Jackson County in 2018, giving Georgia a head start in the EV industry.
Gov. Brian Kemp took a 2020 trade trip to Stuttgart, Germany, where he met with executives from Mercedes-Benz and Porsche regarding their EV plans.
Both Mercedes-Benz and Porsche have their North American headquarters in Atlanta.
After the Stuttgart trip, Gov. Kemp told Wilson's team: “You guys really need to be focused on electrification.”
The site in Newton and Morgan counties was previously pitched to Toyota-Mazda and Tesla, but both companies chose other locations.
Georgia was initially in Rivian’s top three for its EV factory but was bumped down the list before being brought back onto the short list.
The Rivian deal was sealed during a meeting at the proposed site between Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe and Gov. Kemp.
Hyundai came to GDEcD in January and made its decision by April.

Hyundai Motor North America president José Muñoz wore a Hyundai backpack to identify himself during early project discussions, later telling Savannah Economic Development Authority president Trip Tollison he did it intentionally to spur the team.

Hyundai chair Chung Euisun (previously Kia’s president) recognized Georgia's Quick Start workforce education program, stating: “Governor, you don’t have to talk to me about workforce. We know Quick Start. We know the state of Georgia is going to live up to its promises in workforce solutions.”
GDEcD uses a team approach to recruiting new business, likened to a quarterback bringing in utilities, workforce solutions teams, and community leaders as part of the sales pitch.
Wilson balances his demanding travel schedule with attending his children’s activities, including baseball games, choral concerts, and symphonies.
Wilson attended the HOPE Scholarship program during his upbringing in Georgia.
Wilson thinks the COVID pandemic sped up industry timelines as companies came out of shutdown ready to invest and make up for lost time.
Wilson flew Delta Air Lines transatlantic flights during the pandemic to build relationships with international companies since they could not visit Georgia.

Wilson says: “On so many of these projects, you’re playing a long game. The company may choose to not do business with you today, but many times if they had a good experience in Georgia, things come back around.”
After hosting one prospect, a site consultant noted the team enjoyed dinner together regardless of outcome, highlighting Georgia's special collaborative recruiting approach.
source_title: 2023 Georgian of the Year: Successfully Selling the State - Georgia Trend Magazine

facts: Ohio has implemented a generous sales-tax exemption for companies operating data centers.
Investments announced by Amazon, Google, and Microsoft over the past two years could cause the state and localities to lose almost $1.6 billion in sales-tax revenue with only modest job creation.
Data centers demand enormous amounts of electricity due to artificial intelligence demands, which could increase electric rates for Ohioans.
Other states are questioning the wisdom of tax giveaways to wealthy companies for data center expansion.
Ohio’s sales-tax exemption was first created in 2013 and covers computer data center equipment as well as materials used in constructing the centers.
The Ohio Tax Credit Authority, a five-member board headed by the director of the state development department, approves initial exemptions.
To qualify for an exemption, a company must make at least $100 million in capital investment over three years and pay annual employee compensation of at least $1.5 million.
On Oct. 28, the Tax Credit Authority approved a 15-year sales tax exemption estimated to be worth $72.5 million for Microsoft covering three new data centers in Licking County.
Microsoft’s $1 billion investment is projected to create a minimum of just 20 new jobs.
Exemptions originally granted to Meta and Google required the creation of at least 50 and 30 jobs, respectively.
Each of those original exemptions was estimated to cost more than $1 million per job.
Ohio’s tax expenditure report pegged the total annual cost of this tax break at $127.4 million for the fiscal year.
As of late September, 13 agreements had been approved covering nearly $5.1 billion in investment but generating just 356 jobs and $31.6 million in annual payroll.
This results in a revenue loss for the state alone of $281.9 million.
Including local sales-tax revenue losses, total losses exceed $343 million, averaging nearly $1 million per job.
These estimates exclude recently announced centers like those by Amazon and Google.
Amazon’s latest $10 billion investment, announced December 16, could result in an exemption of more than half a billion dollars between now and 2030.

If Cologix receives a 75% sales-tax exemption on its planned $7 billion investment for a campus of eight data centers in Johnstown, it would cost the state more than $300 million in sales-tax revenue.
These exemptions reduce sales taxes flowing to counties and transit agencies.
Tax credit agreements require companies to annually report their sales-tax exemption amounts but some allow companies to reject public disclosure.
Policy Matters Ohio has requested copies of these reports from the state and companies without success.
Major data-center operators do not need new approval for tax breaks when expanding, as original approvals grant open-ended ability to avoid paying taxes if investment thresholds are met.

Based on existing and announced investments, Amazon will qualify for the exemption through 2055 under a 2014 agreement initially estimated to cost $77 million but potentially costing 20 times that amount.
Bo Williams, executive responsible for Microsoft’s data centers in North America, stated he could not think of a site selection decision decided on tax incentives.
American Electric Power is seeking guarantees from the Public Utilities Commission of Ohio that operators will cover the enormous capacity required for data center expansion.
Intervenors including Walmart and the Ohio Consumers’ Counsel (OCC) are concerned that data centers could boost electricity costs for businesses or individuals.
The OCC tried to prevent AES Energy (Dayton Power & Light) from shifting costs to consumers if investments to serve an Amazon data center in Fayette County do not pay off.
Massive subsidies to wealthy corporations could result in higher costs for Ohioans and Ohio businesses.
Electricity demand growth from data centers could threaten progress in cutting greenhouse gas emissions.
In Georgia, the legislature approved a two-year pause on new data center sales-tax exemptions while examining effects on the energy grid, but the measure was vetoed by the governor.

An analysis in Georgia found that the state’s sales tax exemption had a negative fiscal impact because increased tax collection from construction and operation was not high enough to offset forgone state tax revenue.
In late 2023, more than two dozen nonprofits, homeowners’ groups, and residents formed a Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition seeking regulatory and rate-making reforms.
A recently issued report by the Joint Legislative Audit and Review Commission in Virginia has raised the possibility of new regulation.
Legislators from Connecticut to South Carolina have been addressing the data center issue.
Many Ohio data centers also receive local subsidies through property tax abatements.
Ohio does not tax tangible property like data-center equipment, providing large invisible subsidies compared to states that do levy such taxes.
In Virginia, Loudoun County received more than $600 million in property taxes from data centers in 2023, mostly from taxes on equipment Ohio does not levy.
Amazon was receiving a Job Creation Tax Credit prior to its recent $10 billion announcement, allowing it to be credited with much of the state income tax paid by its data-center employees.
In Virginia, data centers and tenants reported saving $928.6 million in sales taxes from that state’s exemption in Fiscal Year 2023.
Recommendations include requiring all reports to be made public, including investment amounts, exemption values, job creation, payroll, and energy/water use.
Other recommendations include setting a cap for the total tax break allowed per job or each year, with Good Jobs First recommending a cap of $50,000 per job in 2016.

Additional recommendations include ending open-ended extension of the tax break without specific consideration by the Tax Credit Authority, requiring 60-day notice prior to exemption decisions, guaranteeing data center growth will not increase electric rates for other customers, and conducting regular independent audits of electricity use and grid effects.
source_title: Indefensible tax breaks for data centers will cost Ohio - Policy Matters Ohio

facts: German company Aurubis Richmond has filed a request with the Georgia Department of Environmental Protection (GA-EPD) for a permit that would allow the Aurubis to open a copper smelting plant in South Augusta.
Aurubis is asking Georgia’s Environmental Protection Division for permission to release toxins into Augusta’s air, including more than 30 dangerous chemicals.
The requested chemical releases include heavy metals, high amounts of particulate matter, arsenic, and o-xylene.
The proposed facility would be located on Valencia Way in South Augusta.
The site location is in a community that is over 80% African-American.
The South Augusta area already suffers from relic heavy metal toxicity, high particulate matter pollution, and numerous still-contaminated abandoned sites.
According to the EPA’s National Air Toxic Assessment, the Augusta area has a 95 to 99 percent cancer risk rate.
This cancer risk rate ranks Augusta as high as cities like Atlanta and New York.
Savannah Riverkeeper’s Research and Advocacy Unit identified federal Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) violations filed against Aurubis.
Those RCRA violations at Aurubis’ Buffalo, New York facility indicate that the company’s handling of toxic chemicals was unsafe, presenting a danger to the local community and employees.
The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) considers Aurubis a “significant” violator.
Augusta’s air quality is ranked 28th in the nation for year-round particulate pollution, which is worse than its previous ranking of 36th on the American Lung Association’s list.

Tonya Bonitatibus, executive director of Savannah Riverkeeper, stated that the community cannot afford more contamination and does not want known polluters like Aurubis as neighbors in exchange for a few jobs.
Tonya Bonitatibus also stated that the South Augusta community and Augusta’s future do not need known polluters as neighbors and deserve better.
source_title: Stop Aurubis - Savannah Riverkeeper

facts: Aurubis AG announced the official launch of production at its new Aurubis Richmond facility in Richmond County, Georgia on Sept. 24, 2025.
The facility is the first multimetal recycling plant in the United States.
Total investment for the facility is $800 million.
The plant will create more than 240 jobs in the Augusta area.
The site will process up to 180,000 tonnes of complex recycling material annually.
Additional expansion at the facility is planned for 2026.
The facility will recover copper, nickel, tin, and precious metals from complex recycling materials, including printed circuit boards and copper cables.
Recovered metals will help strengthen domestic supply for industries such as energy infrastructure, data centers, AI applications, and the defense sector.
Dr. Toralf Haag, CEO of Aurubis AG, stated that with Aurubis Richmond, the company is the frontrunner in multimetal recycling and processing in the U.S., a position it already holds in Europe.
Dr. Toralf Haag noted that strong availability of recycling material and favorable conditions in Georgia offer tremendous opportunities for Aurubis in the U.S. market.
Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp stated in a video message that Aurubis Richmond is now a significant employer for Georgia and plays a major role in the American multimetal supply chain.
Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp added that the facility stands as a testament to American manufacturing, independence, and resiliency.

U.S. Rep. Rick W. Allen (GA-12) stated that Aurubis Richmond is a strategic site that reinforces America’s ability to supply itself with metals essential to national security, infrastructure, and technology leadership.
U.S. Rep. Rick W. Allen noted that the facility exemplifies how international partnerships strengthen America’s economic independence and industrial foundation.
David Schultheis, President and Managing Director of Aurubis Richmond, stated that the facility is now an important player in the North American multimetal supply chain.

David Schultheis expressed pride in his team’s achievement in building the state-of-the-art facility and gratitude to partners in Georgia for enabling a smooth approval process and strong community support.
Aurubis AG is headquartered in Hamburg, Germany, and operates as a leading global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the world’s largest copper recyclers.

Aurubis AG produces and recycles copper, nickel, tin, precious metals, and other key industrial materials through an international network of production sites and service centers dedicated to sustainable resource management and supply chain resiliency.
source_title: Aurubis Richmond: First U.S. Multimetal Recycling Plant Starts Production - Expansion Solutions

facts: Aurubis's new multimetal recycling plant in the United States began production one year after its official ribbon-cutting ceremony.
The facility is located in Richmond County, Georgia.
The plant recovers strategic metals such as copper, nickel, tin and precious metals from complex recycling materials.
These recovered metals are central to the expansion of energy infrastructure, data centres, artificial intelligence applications, high-tech production and the defence industry.
Aurubis is investing around €740m in the US project.
The project will create more than 240 jobs.
CEO Toralf Haag stated that with Aurubis Richmond, the company is the frontrunner in multimetal recycling and processing in the US, a position it already holds in Europe.
Haag noted the US market offered "appealing prospects" due to high availability of recycling materials and good local conditions.
The facility is located in the City of Augusta, the seat of Richmond County.
The new recycling facility can currently process around 90,000 tonnes of complex recycling materials per year.
Processed materials include printed circuit boards, copper cables and other metal-bearing products.
Once the second construction phase is completed in 2026, capacity will double to 180,000 tonnes a year.
Aurubis Richmond is designed as a flexible "one-stop shop" and will be supplied with a wide range of metal-bearing recycling materials from US suppliers.
The US industry alone currently requires around 1.8 million tonnes of copper a year.
Copper demand in the US is expected to grow by almost 30 per cent over the next five years.
Around half of the metallic raw materials used by US manufacturers is imported.
Multimetal recycling could quickly close the gap in domestic metallic raw material supply for US manufacturers.
As the first and most technologically advanced secondary smelter, Aurubis Richmond strengthens the independence of US supply chains by processing complex recycling materials.
Numerous high-ranking political and industry figures attended the "first melt" celebration.
US Representative Rick W. Allen (R), who represents Georgia's twelfth district, described Aurubis Richmond as "strategic" for the region.
Rep. Allen stated the plant reinforces America’s ability to supply itself with metals that help strengthen national security, infrastructure and technology leadership.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp said in a video message that the plant is "a significant employer for Georgia and plays a major role in the American multimetal supply chain."
source_title: Aurubis celebrates first melt at Georgia multimetal recycling plant

facts: Aurubis AG broke ground on June 17, 2022, for the United States' first large secondary smelter for multimetal recycling.
Total investment in Augusta, Richmond County in Georgia is estimated at €300 million (approx. $320 million).
The plant is expected to be fully operational by mid-2024 (first half of 2024).
Once fully online, the facility will create over 120 new jobs directly.
Aurubis Richmond, USA will have the capacity to process about 90,000 tons of complex recycling materials annually.
Processed materials include printed circuit boards, copper cables, and other recycling materials containing metals such as copper, nickel, tin, and other industrial and precious metals.
Currently, much of these materials in the US are either shipped to Asia and Europe or trucked to local landfills.
The US currently produces about 6 million tons of recycling materials that contain valuable metals.

Roland Harings, CEO of Aurubis AG, stated the company has doubled down on commitments to use its technology and experience to become a forerunner for recycling valuable materials containing copper, nickel, tin, and other industrial and precious metals locally in the US.
Harings noted that current supply cannot meet demand for metals such as copper used in cars, mobile phones, batteries, cables, and computers, and that the US is lacking recycling capacities.
The technologically advanced facility in Augusta will be a game changer for the environment, manufacturers, and the larger transition to a renewable economy.
Copper is 100-percent recyclable without any loss in performance, but secondary smelter recycling at an industrial scale requires significant long-term investment.
Aurubis' facility will reduce waste and direct key metals to industries including renewables such as solar and wind, along with electric vehicle and battery manufacturers.
The state-of-the-art environmental technology meets or exceeds the highest State of Georgia and federal government standards.

Aurubis Richmond will have a very low impact on air quality in the region, with less than 0.2% share of dust emissions in Richmond county (estimation based on actual emissions at the Aurubis recycling site in Germany).
Governor Brian Kemp welcomed Aurubis as one of more than 500 German companies with operations in Georgia and congratulated state and local economic development teams.
Kemp stated this groundbreaking represents the largest single investment made by a German company in Georgia history, enabled by strong partnerships and world-class business assets.
The Georgia facility is expected to become a major player in the North American supply chain and a significant employer in greater Augusta, GA.
Aurubis AG is a leading global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide.
The company processes complex metal concentrates, scrap metals, organic and inorganic metal-bearing recycling materials, and industrial residues into metals of the highest quality.
Aurubis produces more than 1 million tons of copper cathodes annually, along with wire rod, continuous cast shapes, profiles, and flat rolled products made of copper and copper alloys.
The company also produces precious metals, selenium, lead, nickel, tin, zinc, sulfuric acid, and iron silicate.

Sustainability is a fundamental part of the Aurubis strategy, integrating sustainable conduct and business activities into corporate culture with a careful approach to natural resources and responsible social and ecological conduct.
Aurubis has about 7,200 employees, production sites in Europe and the US, and an extensive service and distribution system in Europe, Asia, and North America.
Aurubis shares are part of the Prime Standard Segment of the German Stock Exchange and listed in the MDAX, Global Challenges Index (GCX), and STOXX Europe 600.

links.context: Background information and groundbreaking ceremony impressions

source_title: Aurubis AG: Aurubis AG starts construction of a € 300 million (approx. $ 320 million) multimetal recycling plant in Augusta, Georgia | Corporate - EQS News

facts: Aurubis AG welcomed a visit from First Lady Jill Biden at its new site in Augusta, Georgia.
The company is investing around $700 million to build the first secondary smelter for complex recycling materials in North America.
Commissioning of the secondary smelter is planned for the second half of the coming year.
An expansion of the plant is already under construction.
The expansion will allow Aurubis Richmond to process a total of 180,000 tons of complex recycling materials per year as of 2026.
Complex recycling materials processed at the facility include printed circuit boards and insulated copper cable.
Augusta is one of five focus regions known as Workforce Hubs where skilled worker jobs will be created due to significant federal and private-sector investments.
The new recycling plant will create long-term, secure jobs for more than 200 people in an industry strategically important to the U.S.
The metals returned to circulation are essential to the mobility and energy transition and contribute to the electrical vehicle ecosystem.
This project aligns with Georgia’s Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance initiative.
Aurubis's Chief Operations Officer (COO) for Multimetal Recycling is Inge Hofkens.
The Workforce Hub initiative supports the company's commitment to training workers for challenging tasks in the secondary smelter.
Aurubis has pledged a community benefit agreement with technical colleges in Augusta and Aiken (South Carolina) as well as the Richmond County School System.
As part of this agreement, Aurubis will invest more than $200,000 in scholarships and job training programs for young workers over the next five years.
The company actively promotes women in the metal industry through its international Women4Metals initiative.
Georgia offers ideal conditions for tapping the growth potential of the U.S. recycling market due to its pro-business climate and excellent infrastructure.
Increasing awareness for sustainability and a significant jump in collection volumes generate roughly 6 million tons of recyclable, metal-bearing materials in the U.S. every year.
This high volume of recyclable materials is a main driver for Aurubis's investment decision.
The goal is to establish a reliable and sustainable supply chain by returning more recycling materials to local cycles.
Experts estimate that recycling volume in North America will increase by an average of 5 percent annually in the coming years.
Roland Harings serves as the chief executive officer of Aurubis.
Georgia's logistics and stable energy supply at competitive prices provide perfect conditions for profitable growth.
Support from Georgia state officials continues to present opportunities for contributing expertise and becoming a multi-metal recycling pioneer in the U.S.
The Inflation Reduction Act offers potential to accelerate Aurubis's future U.S. growth and investment, positioning the Southeast as a leading ecosystem for EV and multi-metal recycling.
Aurubis is in a strong financial position and ready to invest more in the U.S. in keeping with its 'Metals for Progress: Driving Sustainable Growth' strategy.
Aurubis's metals create the foundation for technologies of the future, including renewable energies, electric vehicles, digitalization, and urbanization.
Demand for metals will continue to rise as a result of these technological advancements.
For more than 150 years, Aurubis has produced copper and other metals crucial for modernization and ongoing development.
The company considers itself a real multimetal provider.
source_title: Aurubis to Invest $700 Million to Build Smelter in Augusta, Georgia | Mining Connection — The Link For All Your Mining Resources

facts: Aurubis AG and SMS group GmbH signed a supply contract for a second plant module at the new Aurubis Richmond recycling plant in Georgia as part of their strategic partnership.
The additional equipment will increase the capacity of the metal recycling plant in Richmond from an annual 90,000 tons to 180,000 tons of complex metal scrap materials.
Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated the expansion delivers on the company’s “Driving Sustainable Growth” strategy while keeping strategically important mineral raw materials in the resource cycle.
The plant will use top-blown rotary converter (TBRC) technology to process complex recycling materials and recover copper, nickel, tin, precious metals, and platinum group metals.
Recovered metals are crucial raw materials for future technologies including the energy transition, sustainable mobility, and digitalization.

SMS Executive Board member Michael Rzepczyk noted the partnership allows Aurubis to take advantage of the enormous potential of the US recycling market and assumes a leading role in multimetal recycling in the US.
The recycling technology features modularity, allowing additional components to be added to expand the plant flexibly to meet future requirements.
Aurubis is investing about € 640 million for both phases of the project to build the first secondary smelter specializing in multimetal recycling in the US.
Groundbreaking for the Aurubis Richmond plant took place in mid-2022, with construction currently running at full steam.
Stage one of the plant is scheduled to go online in the first half of 2024.
The TBRC equipment destined for the second stage is anticipated to start production in 2026.
Aurubis processes roughly 1 million tons of recycling material annually, and the Richmond plant will add another 180,000 tons to meet growth targets.
The Georgia investment will comply with the highest environmental standards in the US and create around 200 new jobs.

Aurubis AG is a leading global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide, processing complex metal concentrates, scrap metals, organic and inorganic metal-bearing recycling materials, and industrial residues.

Aurubis produces more than 1 million tons of copper cathodes annually, along with wire rod, continuous cast shapes, profiles, flat rolled products, precious metals, selenium, lead, nickel, tin, zinc, sulfuric acid, and iron silicate.
The company has around 6,900 employees and production sites in Europe and the USA.

SMS group GmbH is a Düsseldorf-based plant and machine manufacturer with worldwide sales of around 2.6 billion euros and 14,500 employees, providing future-oriented technologies for the metals industry.
source_title: Aurubis and SMS group forge ahead with the first multimetal recycling plant in the US - SMS group GmbH

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Aurubis will invest $340 million (€300 million) in a state-of-the-art recycling and secondary smelting facility for multi-metal recycling in Augusta.
The new facility will be the very first of its kind in the U.S.
The facility will create 125 jobs in the Augusta area.
Governor Kemp stated this marks the single-largest investment made by a German company in the State of Georgia.
Aurubis is one of the leading recyclers of copper, precious metals, and non-ferrous materials worldwide.
From the Augusta facility, the company plans to produce materials found in lithium-ion batteries and other electronics.
Roland Harings, CEO of Aurubis AG, said the investment sets a clear example of sustainable growth and positions the company as a forerunner for multi-metal recycling in the U.S.
Aurubis will build its facility on 150 acres at Augusta Corporate Park.
Augusta Corporate Park is a “Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified” site.
Operations at the new facility are expected to begin by the first half of 2024.
The company plans to begin hiring for positions as soon as summer 2022.

Steven Kendrick, Chairman of the Development Authority of Augusta, welcomed Aurubis as a new corporate tenant that will build a world-class advanced manufacturing facility and bring skilled jobs to Augusta.
Griff Lynch, Executive Director of the Georgia Ports Authority (GPA), stated that exports produced by the Aurubis recycling operation will strengthen the state’s global position via Savannah.

Fernanda Kirchner, Senior Project Manager for the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce division, represented GDEcD on this project in partnership with the Augusta Economic Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, and Georgia Power.
Sérgio Domingues, Managing Director for the State of Georgia’s Europe Office, played a supporting role in attracting Aurubis to Georgia.

Pat Wilson, GDEcD Commissioner, noted that Georgia is home to more than 500 German companies and highlighted the project's role in keeping Georgia in the driver’s seat of the green manufacturing future.
Augusta Corporate Park has completed due diligence and other specific certification requirements prior to visits by prospective businesses as part of the GRAD program.
The GRAD program, administered by GDEcD, is a proactive way for community planners and landowners to help catalyze economic growth and industrial development for their regions.
Thousands of Georgia jobs have been created on GRAD sites since the program’s inception in 2008.
Aurubis AG is a leading global provider of non-ferrous metals and one of the largest copper recyclers worldwide.
The company processes complex metal concentrates, scrap metals, organic and inorganic metal-bearing recycling materials, and industrial residues into metals of the highest quality.
Aurubis produces more than 1 million tons of copper cathodes annually.
From copper cathodes, Aurubis manufactures wire rod, continuous cast shapes, profiles, and flat rolled products made of copper and copper alloys.
Aurubis also produces precious metals, selenium, lead, nickel, tin, and zinc, along with sulfuric acid and iron silicate.
source_title: Aurubis to Open State-of-the-Art Recycling and Copper Smelting Facility in Augusta - Augusta Economic Development Authority

facts: Aurubis AG officially opened its $800 million smelting facility in Augusta, Georgia, a year ago.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp labeled the project “one of my favorite projects” for its strategic and economic value.
The company held a “first melt” ceremony on Wednesday with legislators, industry leaders, local officials, and German Ambassador to the United States Jens Hanefeld.
The plant processes scrap material ranging from copper cable to printed circuit boards using massive furnaces to produce blister copper and precious metals including gold, tin, zinc, and nickel.

Aurubis CEO Toralf Haag described the operation as very complex with high temperatures, significant material flow, and a complex off-gas and water cooling system that had to be synchronized over the last month.
The facility is called “the most technologically advanced multimetal recycling center” in the U.S.
Site preparation took more than two years, starting with a groundbreaking in 2023 and leading to the ribbon-cutting ceremony last year.
Aurubis has installed only half of its planned capacity in the first phase: 90,000 tons annually, with an expansion to 180,000 tons starting next year.
The project has already created all 240 promised jobs after the company announced a doubling of the project’s size in 2022.
It is unclear how tariffs on finished copper and other inputs might affect costs and profitability for Aurubis.
Aurubis will sell its output at the same international market price as mined copper, framing it as a win for the earth and U.S. interests.
Dr. Haag stated that while market dynamics are currently hard to predict, the U.S. is a huge and growing market where they concentrate production for local demand.
According to Aurubis, the U.S. imports half of the 1.8 million tons of copper it consumes annually, with total consumption expected to rise 30 percent over the next five years.
Dr. Haag added that U.S. demand is increasing mainly due to infrastructure, defense, AI, mobility, and transport spending, and global copper demand is expected to double by 2050.
Aurubis has secured long-term agreements with domestic scrap aggregators for input materials and has similar supply arrangements with metals “off-takers.”
The plant's circularity model turns waste into fuel for American factories and displaces imports, aligning with U.S. efforts to drive a manufacturing renaissance despite trade barriers.
At the September 2024 groundbreaking, Aurubis highlighted its role in driving U.S. resiliency, featuring a large American flag on stage.
Rep. Rick Allen (Republican, Georgia’s 12th district) stated that Aurubis exemplifies how international partnerships can solidify America’s industrial foundation and strengthen economic independence.
The Augusta plant is named for the county where it sits and is called Aurubis Richmond.
The facility aims to set a new standard for environmental responsibility by installing scrubbing and combustion technologies to remove pollutants from chimney emissions.
Wastewater at the plant is recovered and reused, and the company has committed to discharging no effluent into surrounding waterways.
Company officials noted that Aurubis's Hamburg facility is located in an urban area, contrasting with the Augusta site's environmental approach.
Dr. Haag reported that the plant faced few hurdles during startup, comparable to similar plants in Germany and elsewhere.
David Schultheis, president and managing director of Aurubis Richmond, thanked Georgia state leaders for strong support, smooth approval processes, and quick settlement enablement.
Georgia was named the top state for business by Area Development magazine for the 12th consecutive year on the same day the plant opened.
Aurubis operates other multimetal recycling sites in Spain, Belgium, and Germany, with smelters producing cathodes and rods in Bulgaria, Finland, Italy, and Germany.
The company employs 7,100 people globally.
source_title: Germany’s Largest Investor in Georgia Gets Smelting  - Global Atlanta

facts: Germany-based copper producer and recycler Aurubis AG has listed a doubling of capacity at its under-construction smelter in Richmond, Georgia, among its capital expenditure (capex) plans for the current fiscal year.
Aurubis announced three additional strategic capex projects with an investment volume of around 530 million euros ($563 million).

One of these new projects involves investing to expand the modular recycling system at the U.S. site in Georgia, which will double processing capacities at the location with a second recycling module.
Further investment adjustments for infrastructure requirements and inflation were approved for the ongoing Georgia smelter project.

Aurubis is implementing its “Driving Sustainable Growth” strategy, citing the United States plant Aurubis Richmond in Georgia alongside recycling projects ASPA and BOB at two Belgian locations as examples currently being implemented.
The company achieved operating earnings before taxes (EBT) of 532 million euros ($565 million) in its fiscal year 2021/22, which closed at the end of September.

For the upcoming 2022/2023 fiscal year, Aurubis forecasts profits in the 400 million euros ($425 million) to 500 million euros ($531 million) range, representing a decline in EBT of from 6 percent to 24 percent.
During the Oct. 1, 2021, to Sept. 30, 2022, fiscal year, the company benefited from significantly higher metal prices, especially for industrial metals such as copper, tin and nickel.
The former Metallo facilities now part of the Aurubis portfolio have increased its presence in the tin and nickel markets.
Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated that the company has the metallurgical expertise to offer process and recycling solutions to recover strategically necessary future metals for the circular economy.
Aurubis remains optimistic for the new 2022/23 financial year despite a general economic slowdown and higher burdens from rising energy prices.
source_title: Aurubis capex plans include added US capacity – Carolina Recycling Association

facts: Europe's biggest copper smelter Aurubis will invest around $700 million to build a smelter for complex recycling materials in North America.
The commissioning of the secondary smelter in Augusta, Georgia, is planned for the second half of 2024.
U.S. first lady Jill Biden toured the facility on Wednesday.
An expansion of the plant is already under way.
The expansion will allow the site to process 180,000 tons of complex recycling materials from 2026.
The complex recycling materials include printed circuit boards and insulated copper cable.
Aurubis is creating long-term, secure jobs for more than 200 people in an industry that is strategically important to the U.S., according to COO of multimetal recycling Inge Hofkens.
source_title: Aurubis to invest about $700 million to build smelter in US

facts: Multimetal producer and copper recycler Aurubis AG and Düsseldorf-based SMS group GmbH are partnering to accelerate the development of the recycling market for non-ferrous metals in the US.
The two companies signed a supply contract for a second plant module for the new Aurubis Richmond recycling plant in Georgia.
The additional equipment will increase the capacity of the metal recycling plant in Richmond from an annual 90,000 tons to 180,000 tons per year.
The metal recycling plant in Richmond is currently under construction.
Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated the expansion delivers on the company’s “Driving Sustainable Growth” strategy while keeping strategically important mineral raw materials in the resource cycle.
The SMS group recycling equipment will be installed in the second module of the new Aurubis plant in the US.

The core technology is the top-blown rotary converter (TBRC), which processes complex recycling materials to recover copper, nickel, tin, precious metals, and platinum group metals in downstream stages.
Recovered metals are crucial raw materials for technologies related to the energy transition, sustainable mobility, or digitalization.

Aurubis CEO Roland Harings said bundling SMS group’s plant know-how with Aurubis’ expertise creates an environment for highly efficient metal recovery and leverages the enormous potential of the US recycling market.
Michael Rzepczyk, a member of the SMS Executive Board, stated that doubling production volume at the new US site takes recycling to the next level in the US.
The recycling technologies at Aurubis Richmond feature modularity, allowing additional components to be added to expand the plant for future requirements.
Aurubis CEO Harings explained the modular system allows a prudent market strategy to respond flexibly to US market needs and benefit from constant market growth.
For about €640 million covering both phases, Aurubis is building the first secondary smelter specializing in multimetal recycling in the US.
Groundbreaking for the Aurubis Richmond plant took place in mid-2022.
Construction at the site is currently running at full steam.
Stage one of the plant is scheduled to go online in the first half of 2024.
The TBRC from the signed contract is destined for the second stage, which is anticipated to start production in 2026.
Aurubis processes roughly 1 million tons of recycling material annually on a global basis.
The Aurubis Richmond recycling plant will add another 180,000 tons of annual processing capacity.
The new plant will comply with the highest environmental standards in the US.
The investment will create around 200 new jobs.
source_title: Aurubis and SMS group: First multimetal recycling plant in the US

The additional equipment will increase the capacity of the metal recycling plant in Richmond from an annual 90,000 tons to 180,000 tons of complex metal scrap materials.

Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated the expansion delivers on the company’s “Driving Sustainable Growth” strategy while keeping strategically important and high-demand mineral raw materials in the resource cycle.
The SMS group recycling equipment is destined for the second module of the new US plant.

The core technology is the top-blown rotary converter (TBRC), used for processing complex recycling materials to recover copper, nickel, tin, precious metals, and platinum group metals in downstream stages.
Recovered metals are crucial raw materials for energy transition, sustainable mobility, and digitalization technologies.
SMS Executive Board member Michael Rzepczyk stated the partnership helps Aurubis assume a leading role in multimetal recycling in the US.
The planned doubling of production volume at the new US site takes recycling to the next level in the US.
Together, the companies are building a more sustainable future by closing value chains and maximizing the recovery of valuable metals from recycling materials.
The plant features modular technology that allows additional components to be added to expand capacity for future requirements.
Aurubis is securing its role as a forerunner in the US recycling market while ensuring capacity can be further expanded at any time.
The modular system enables a prudent market strategy to respond flexibly to US needs and benefit from constant US market growth.
Aurubis is investing about €640 million across both phases of the project to build the first secondary smelter specializing in multimetal recycling in the US.
Groundbreaking for the Aurubis Richmond plant took place in mid-2022.
Construction at the site is currently running at full steam.
Stage one of the plant is scheduled to go online in the first half of 2024.
The TBRC from the signed contract is destined for the second stage, which is anticipated to start production in 2026.
As a leader in multimetal recycling, Aurubis currently processes roughly 1 million tons of recycling material annually.
The Aurubis Richmond recycling plant will add another 180,000 tons of annual processing capacity.
The Georgia investment will contribute to meeting Aurubis Group growth targets.
The new plant will comply with the highest environmental standards in the US.
The new plant will create around 200 new jobs.
Aurubis CEO Roland Harings stated that economic growth and comprehensive corporate responsibility go hand in hand for the company.

facts: On November 8, 2023, First Lady Jill Biden visited Aurubis Richmond at its new site in Augusta (Richmond County), Georgia.
Aurubis is investing around US$ 700 million to build the first secondary smelter for complex recycling materials in North America.
Commissioning of the secondary smelter is planned for the second half of the coming year.
An expansion of the plant is already under construction.
The expansion will allow Aurubis Richmond to process a total of 180,000 tons of complex recycling materials per year as of 2026.
The facility will process complex recycling materials such as printed circuit boards and insulated copper cable.
The visit was part of the Biden-Harris Administration’s Investing in America agenda.

Augusta is designated as one of five focus regions, known as Workforce Hubs, where a large number of jobs for skilled workers are to be created due to significant federal and private-sector investments.
Aurubis is creating long-term, secure jobs for more than 200 people at the site.
The metals returned to circulation by the facility are essential to the mobility and energy transition and an important contribution to the electrical vehicle ecosystem.
The project aligns with Georgia’s Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance initiative.
Aurubis has pledged a community benefit agreement with the Technical Colleges in Augusta and Aiken (South Carolina) as well as the Richmond County School System.
As part of the community benefit agreement, Aurubis will invest more than US$ 200,000 in scholarships and job training programs for young workers over the next five years.
The company actively promotes women in the metal industry through its international Women4Metals initiative.
Increasing awareness for sustainability and a significant jump in collection volumes are generating roughly 6 million tons of recyclable, metal-bearing materials in the U.S. every year.
The growing supply of recyclable materials is a main driver for Aurubis's investment decision.
The goal is to establish a reliable and sustainable supply chain by returning more recycling materials to local cycles.
Experts estimate that the recycling volume in North America will increase by an average of 5% annually in the coming years.
Georgia offers ideal conditions for Aurubis due to its pro-business climate and excellent infrastructure, including logistics and a stable energy supply at competitive prices.
Support from Georgia State officials has enabled Aurubis to contribute expertise and become a multi-metal recycling pioneer in the U.S.
The Inflation Reduction Act offers great potential to accelerate Aurubis’s future U.S. growth and investment, helping position the Southeast as a leading ecosystem for EV and multi-metal recycling.
Aurubis is in a strong financial position and ready to invest more in the U.S. in keeping with its ‘Metals for Progress: Driving Sustainable Growth’ strategy.
In addition to strategic investments in its European sites, Aurubis recognizes great potential for further development in North America.

entity_name: Atlanta Solar Permit and Incentive Process
canonical_name: Atlanta Office of Buildings Solar Permitting and Georgia Power Interconnection Program
facts: Atlanta's Office of Buildings processes residential solar permits through a dedicated track with a specific submittal checklist.
An express permit option is available for qualifying smaller residential solar systems using an expedited PV inspection checklist.
Express solar permits must be submitted in person starting in February 2025 after Atlanta discontinued online express submission.
Georgia Power's BTM interconnection process is a critical step beyond city permitting for all solar installations.

Installers must submit a Facility Interconnection Application to Georgia Power including system design, total AC inverter capacity in kW, total panel nameplate capacity in kW DC, specification sheets, and a one-line diagram.
Georgia Power verifies grid compatibility and signs off before any system can be energized.
Only IEEE 1741SB and IEEE 1547-2018 compliant inverters are accepted for interconnection; older certifications are rejected.
Residential systems at or below 10kW peak generating capacity fall under Georgia Power's Renewable and Non-Renewable Tariff.
The RNR Tariff provides compensation based on the Renewable Cost Benefit Framework adjusted avoided cost, resulting in a buy-back rate lower than retail electricity rates.
Right-sizing solar systems to cover annual consumption maximizes value by offsetting retail rates rather than exporting at low buy-back rates.
Adding battery storage allows homeowners to capture solar production for self-consumption during peak rate hours, improving system economics.
Properties in designated historic or landmark districts require a Certificate of Appropriateness from the Urban Design Commission before a building permit can be issued.
The Urban Design Commission evaluates panel placement for visibility from public streets and compatibility with historic character.
Atlanta's historic neighborhoods including Inman Park, Grant Park, Virginia-Highland, Druid Hills, and West End have mature tree canopy that may impact shading analyses for solar viability.
Roof planes facing away from streets are generally more approvable than front-facing installations on historically significant streetscapes.
The Certificate of Appropriateness process adds 4–8 weeks to the permitting timeline for historic district properties.
The federal Investment Tax Credit under Section 48D/25D provides a 30% tax credit of total system cost for residential installations, available through at least 2032.
A $20,000 solar system qualifies for a $6,000 federal tax credit.
Georgia's statewide Home Energy Rebates administered by GEFA include electrification packages that combine solar with heat pump installations for qualifying households.
These rebates focus more on heating and cooling equipment but solar plus heat pump combinations may unlock larger package rebates based on income eligibility.

Georgia's Residential Solar Energy Credit previously provided a state income tax credit of 35% of costs up to $10,500 for several years but has undergone changes requiring confirmation with the Department of Revenue.
Atlanta does not offer utility fee exemptions for solar permits, unlike Sacramento which waives all building permit fees for solar with interconnection agreements.
Standard building and electrical trade permits in Atlanta cost approximately $300 to $500 total.
Atlanta has no mandatory solar requirement for new residential construction, and Georgia has not adopted a statewide solar mandate.
Solar installation remains a voluntary decision for Atlanta homeowners and ADU builders, contrasting with California cities that require solar on new detached ADUs under the 2025 Energy Code.
Standard residential solar permit review through the Office of Buildings takes 1–3 weeks.
Georgia Power's BTM interconnection application review typically takes 2–4 weeks.
The overall pre-installation wait for non-historic properties is usually 3–5 weeks, determined by the longer of the two parallel processes.
Total permitting and interconnection timeline for historic district properties ranges from 8–14 weeks from project initiation to Permission to Operate.
Non-historic properties typically complete the process in 4–7 weeks.
Atlanta has no tree-canopy height restrictions on solar mounting, unlike some other cities.

links.context: Atlanta city applications and checklists page

source_title: Complete Guide to Solar Panel Permits in Atlanta, GA (2026)

facts: A German company will build a $347 million (300 million euro) smelter in Georgia to recycle copper, hiring 125 workers.
Hamburg-based Aurubis AG announced the plans Wednesday, saying construction on the smelter in Augusta will start next year, with the plant beginning operations in 2024.
Aurubis said much copper is now being sent from the United States to Asia for recycling and it makes business sense to recycle material locally.

Aurubis CEO Roland Harings said in a statement, “The need for modern, sustainable, local processing capacities is very strong,” noting that current capacities are insufficient to cover high demand, so large volumes of valuable feed materials have to be exported.
Georgia Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson called it “one of the largest German investments in our state.”
Company spokesperson Meino Hauschildt said the plant will recycle circuit boards, copper cable and other materials including nickel, tin, zinc, gold, silver and platinum.

The company said it expects up to 6.6 million tons (6 million metric tons) of metal-bearing materials to be collected for recycling in the U.S. in coming years, with the amount of materials growing 5% annually.
Aurubis said the smelter supports environmental goals by reducing the need for new copper to be mined and will also allow the company to recover other materials that are combined with the copper.
Hauschildt said the smelter will be powered with electricity and natural gas, designed to “the latest environmental standards” with water reused, and will have “low” dust and air emissions.
The company said it expects the plant add about $93 million (80 million euros) to its profits before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization beginning in its 2025-2026 fiscal year.

Aurubis could claim various state tax breaks, including an income tax credit allowing it to annually deduct $4,000 per job from state income taxes, up to $2.5 million over five years, as long as workers make at least $28,000 a year.
The state will pay for job training, and company officials expect to receive property tax abatements and aid with infrastructure.
source_title: German company plans $347M smelter in Georgia, hiring 125 | AP News

facts: Aurubis, a global provider of non-ferrous metals and a copper recycler, has established a new US site called Aurubis Richmond in the state of Georgia.
The facility will produce key strategic metals, including copper, nickel, tin, and precious metals.
These metals are needed to expand data centres and AI applications, as well as for energy infrastructure, high-tech products, and the defence industry.
The American industry alone requires approximately 1.8 million tonnes of copper annually.
Experts predict demand for strategic metals like copper will increase by nearly 30% over the next five years.
Currently, the US imports around half of the copper it processes.
Aurubis Richmond is expected to reinforce the independence of American supply chains by processing complex recycling materials.
A 'First Melt' celebration was held with participants including Rick W. Allen (US representative for Georgia's 12th District) and Jens Hanefeld (German ambassador to the US).
Dr Toralf Haag, CEO of Aurubis AG, stated that Aurubis Richmond makes them the frontrunner in multimetal recycling and processing in the US, a position they already hold in Europe.
Dr Toralf Haag noted that high availability of recycling material and good local conditions make the American market appealing for future expansion.
Georgia Governor Brian P. Kemp stated in a video message that Aurubis Richmond is now a significant employer for Georgia and plays a major role in the American multimetal supply chain.
Governor Kemp added that the facility stands as a testament to American manufacturing, independence, and resiliency.
Aurubis is creating over 240 jobs in Georgia at the new facility.
The plant is expected to process up to 180,000 tonnes of complex recycling material annually.
The recycling materials will include printed circuit boards, copper cable, and other metal-bearing products.
The plant will recover critical metals from these materials to close the value chain on-site.

US Representative Rick W. Allen described Aurubis Richmond as a strategic site in Georgia’s 12th District that reinforces America’s ability to supply itself with metals for national security, infrastructure, and technology leadership.
Rep. Allen noted that Aurubis exemplifies how international partnerships can contribute to solidifying America’s industrial foundation and strengthening economic independence.
David Schultheis, president and managing director of Aurubis Richmond, stated the facility is now an important player in the North American multimetal supply chain.
Schultheis expressed pride in the team that developed and built the state-of-the-art recycling plant.
Schultheis thanked policymakers and governmental authorities in Georgia for strong support, smooth approval processes, and enabling quick settlement.
source_title: Aurubis Richmond to be the first US multimetal recycling plant producing strategic metals - Engineer Live

Aurubis Richmond operates as the first and most technologically advanced secondary smelter for multimetal recycling in the United States.

Dr. Toralf Haag serves as CEO of Aurubis AG.
