facts: An automotive parts manufacturer plans to spend $205 million on a new manufacturing facility.
Ecoplastic Corp., a South Korean company, said it would create 456 new jobs as part of the new facility near Statesboro in Bulloch County.
Ecoplastic manufactures plastic interior and exterior components and supplies Hyundai Motor Group and Kia.
A Georgia Department of Economic Development communications representative told The Center Square that the project is still active.
The designation allows state officials to decline to release details about tax incentives the state offered to entice a company to locate or expand in Georgia.
Georgia officials have targeted electric vehicle manufacturers and associated companies.
According to state officials, companies have announced more than 30 EV-related projects in Georgia since 2020.
Last May, Hyundai Motor Group announced it had selected Georgia for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America, its first dedicated EV plant in the U.S.
The company said it plans to invest more than $5.5 billion to build the facility.

Han Shang, president and CEO of Ecoplastic, stated that by increasing new jobs within the area and providing a stable supply of high quality products to their client HMGMA, they look forward to contributing to the economic development of the State of Georgia and Bulloch County.

Last month, the Michigan-based Center for Economic Accountability named Georgia’s decision to give $1.5 billion in incentives for a Rivian Automotive electric vehicle assembly plant 2022’s Worst Economic Development Deal of the Year.
State economic development officials have not responded to requests for comment on the recognition.
source_title: Automotive suppler to build $205M plant in Georgia, but incentives remain unknown | Georgia | thecentersquare.com

facts: An automobile parts supplier announced Thursday that it is joining a growing list of companies opening factories in Coastal Georgia to support Hyundai Motor Group’s future $5.54 billion electric vehicle plant.
Ecoplastic Corporation, a South Korean-based company that manufactures interior and exterior automotive parts, will build a $205 million plant in Bulloch County, according to a state news release.
The factory is expected to employ 456 workers.
The company has been a parts supplier for Hyundai and its subsidiary Kia for decades, supplying both with plastic bumpers, consoles, trims and molds.
Ecoplastic will build its new factory at 4822 U.S. 301 near Statesboro and expects to begin operations by October 2024.
source_title: Third Hyundai supplier announces factory near future Georgia EV plant

Nicolas Erdrich serves as the Chief Executive Officer.

facts: Erdrich Metal Forming Technology is headquartered in Germany and was founded 50 years ago producing stamping tools.
The company grew to mass-produce stamped and bent parts and developed considerable expertise in deep drawing, flow forming of thick sheets, and fineblanking.
Erdrich uses a stamping technology that combines fineblanking and forming in a single press.
The manufacturer expanded from a small craftsman’s shop to a global player with facilities in China, Germany, the Czech Republic, and the U.S.
Erdrich USA Inc. began production in Dublin, Ga., in 2013.
The Dublin facility stamps components for the automotive industry, ranging from large chassis parts to housings for electronic devices.
COO Alexander Kehrer stated that most cars on the streets are made with some of their parts.
The plant’s portfolio includes very slim aluminum covers for electronic boxes and robust, thick-gauge steel components for wheel axles.
The plant’s daily output is roughly 30,000 metal parts.
Most parts go to clients in the U.S. and Mexico, but also overseas.
Components must be engineered precisely to deliver safe and accurately finished products.
Coils weigh up to 10 tons each and require precise handling to ensure efficient processes.
The company relies on levelers to flatten advanced high-strength steel (AHSS) before stamping it.
Sheet thicknesses range from 0.06 to 0.31 inch (1.5 to 8 millimeters).
Erdrich revamped its coil feeding lines to boost efficiency due to a highly competitive environment and mounting cost pressures.

Previously, the company operated with coil lines from an overseas supplier, which caused operational vulnerabilities during increased customer demand due to time zone differences, time lags, language barriers, and additional travel costs for support.
COO Alexander Kehrer emphasized the need for on-site professional maintenance technicians for normal operational issues rather than just warranty issues.
In early 2017, Erdrich USA purchased an ARKU coil feeding line that includes a CoilMaster® 7050/17 precision leveler EcoPlan® and a zig-zag servo feeder.
Key factors for choosing the equipment were the manufacturer's nearby U.S. service office, strong connection to the service partner, user-friendly equipment, and state-of-the-art engineering.
Kehrer noted that calling the service partner results in a technician arriving the next day due to the stateside service center.
Good pricing was offered because service could be provided through the U.S. office.
A special zig-zag feeder stamps metal parts in a zig-zag pattern to keep material waste to a minimum, which Kehrer identified as key to enhancing production efficiency.
The coil feeder and leveler implementation was completed in May 2017.
Installation took only four weeks, allowing operations to resume earlier than planned and save valuable production time.
The line features ARKU’s EcoPlan energy-efficient drive, which delivers up to 30 percent more performance with the same electrical power requirements.
The line’s material transport system prevents rapid wear of the leveling rollers while also protecting the strip material.
The machine is equipped with process visualizations on the control panel to simplify control.
The software menu structure is similar to that used on personal computers, making it intuitive for use, especially during coil changes.
The user-friendliness of the system reduces training requirements for new employees.
source_title: New feeder improves stamper’s forming efficiency

canonical_name: New U.S. Factories Announced in November 2025
facts: U.S. manufacturing kept up its momentum in November 2025, with major announcements spanning pharmaceuticals, electronics, renewable energy, transportation and industrial equipment.
Collectively, the month’s announcements involve tens of billions in capital spending and thousands of new jobs across the country.
Toyota announced a $912 million investment to boost hybrid vehicle production across its U.S. plants in West Virginia, Kentucky, Mississippi, Tennessee and Missouri, adding 252 jobs.
Senior vice president Kevin Voelkel explained that the expansion aligns with Toyota’s philosophy of building where it sells.
First Solar inaugurated a 2.4 million square foot manufacturing facility in New Iberia Parish, Louisiana.
The $1.1 billion plant is equipped with artificial intelligence driven production systems and will produce Series 7 photovoltaic modules.
The First Solar project is expected to create more than 800 jobs.
CEO Mark Widmar called the project a commitment to U.S. energy leadership, affordable electricity, growth and prosperity.
Entegris opened its 135,000 square foot Manufacturing Center of Excellence in Colorado Springs, Colorado.
The facility expands production of filtration and purification products and semiconductor wafer carriers.
The Entegris project is supported by up to $100 million in government incentives.
Entegris president and CEO Dave Reeder stated the U.S. semiconductor industry's strength hinges on a reliable and advanced domestic supply chain of critical process chemistries and solutions.
MAHLE will invest $6.8 million to expand its Lockport, New York facility, relocating a research and testing center from Michigan.
The MAHLE project will add 11 jobs and retain 226.
The expansion will enhance thermal management R&D capabilities for automotive applications.
Vice president Stefan Land thanked officials for enabling growth and strengthening long term commitment to the region.
CEL Critical Power established its first U.S. manufacturing facility in James City County, Virginia, investing $5.225 million.
The plant will build generator enclosures and is expected to hire 250 people initially, rising to 500 by 2030.

Group managing director Niall McFadden noted Virginia consistently ranks among the top locations for business in the United States, offering a great supply chain ecosystem, excellent infrastructure and a large pool of technical talent.
Hyosung HICO will invest $157 million to expand its U.S. manufacturing headquarters in Memphis, Tennessee, increasing capacity by 50 percent and creating 240 new jobs.
President Jason E. Neal described the expansion as a shared achievement with the State of Tennessee and the partnership between their U.S. operations and South Korea headquarters.
Eaton is investing $6.8 million to expand its Middlesex, North Carolina facility by 40,000 square feet, adding 30 jobs.
Traci Melville, president of Eaton’s Fluid and Electrical Distribution Division, noted the expansion enhances their ability to serve customers with greater efficiency and responsiveness.
source_title: New U.S. Factories Announced in November 2025 - IndustrySelect®

facts: Commemorative ceremonies to name streets at the Milledgeville-Baldwin County Industrial Park will be held on Saturday, April 26, and Monday, April 28, 2025.
Earl Seagle Court and Henry Pope Way are the newly named streets honoring two individuals instrumental in shaping the area’s industrial landscape.
The newly named streets intersect at the park, symbolizing the lasting impact of Seagle and Pope’s shared commitment to economic development and community growth.
Mr. Earl Seagle is in his 90s and is being recognized for decades of dedicated public service following a distinguished career in the aerospace industry.
Seagle relocated to Baldwin County in the 1970s to work with Grumman after the company established a manufacturing facility in the area.
In retirement, Seagle served on both the Milledgeville-Baldwin County Development Authority Board of Directors and the Fall Line Regional Development Authority Board of Directors.
The dedication of Earl Seagle Court will take place at 11:30 a.m. on Saturday, April 26, 2025, at the Milledgeville-Baldwin County Industrial Park.
Henry Pope Jr. served for many years as a member and chairman of the Development Authority.
Under Pope’s leadership, the Authority completed successful spec building projects and helped recruit numerous new industries to Milledgeville and Baldwin County.
Pope was president of Exchange Bank in Milledgeville and built enduring relationships throughout the community.
One of the park’s previously unnamed streets, home to Fireline, Inc., will officially be named Henry Pope Way.
The dedication of Henry Pope Way will be held on Monday, April 28, 2025, at 2 p.m. at the Milledgeville-Baldwin County Industrial Park off GA Hwy 22.
In March 2024, the Milledgeville City Council unanimously passed resolutions to accept the conveyance of the two streets from the Development Authority.
Earl Seagle Court and Henry Pope Way will serve as lasting reminders of efforts to attract, retain, and grow business in the region.

The families of Henry Pope and Earl Seagle attended the Milledgeville City Council meeting in March 2024 when the council accepted the street conveyance from the Development Authority of the City of Milledgeville – Baldwin County.
source_title: News & Announcements | Baldwin County Development Authority

facts: Fouts Bros, Inc. hosted a "Home Day" open house event on Nov. 7 at its corporate headquarters on Roberson Mill Road in Milledgeville, Baldwin County, Georgia.
The company relocated its operations from Cobb County to Milledgeville in December 2019.
Fouts Brothers is a family-owned company dedicated to the manufacturing and upfitting of American work trucks for private and public sectors.
The company's products are sold through dealerships across the U.S., with its headquarters located in a 700,000-square-foot factory in Milledgeville.
Fouts Brothers operates a second facility in La Grange, Texas.

Attendees at the open house included Georgia’s District 8 Congressman Austin Scott, Georgia College & State University President Cathy Cox, Baldwin County Commissioner Henry Craig, Baldwin County Manager Carlos Tobar, and Baldwin County Fire Rescue Chief Victor Young.
The company currently employs 410 people organization-wide.
Sixty-three employees currently work across five dedicated production lines for building fire apparatus at the Milledgeville facility.
The company plans to expand its manufacturing capacity to eight production lines by April 1st, increasing the floor workforce to up to 85 employees.
It takes six weeks to build a single fire apparatus from start to finish.
Finished trucks undergo quality control testing conducted by a certified third party.
In addition to fire trucks, the company's manufacturing line includes bucket, crane, box, utility, tow, and flatbed trucks.
The founding brothers, C.J. Fouts and Jerry Fouts, started the company in 1952 with a Sinclair Service Station before operating car and truck dealerships across the Metro Atlanta area for 41 years.
Commercial vehicle body manufacturing began through their dealerships, eventually expanding to include fire trucks based on C.J. Fouts' hobby.
The open house event was held in connection with the company's membership in the Fire Apparatus Manufacturers Association (FAMA).
Presentations at the event highlighted federal and state-level funding available for fire departments, emergency service agencies, and first responder training facilities.
Federal grants are available to assist fire departments, as explained by CEO Scott Edens during the event.
The company participates in annual Capitol Hill advocacy efforts known as "Hill Day" in Washington D.C. through its membership in FAMA.
CEO Scott Edens stated that the event showcased the company's progress over seven decades as a family-owned business.
Scott Edens serves as President & CEO of Fouts Brothers, while his wife Heather Edens serves as executive vice president.
John Slawson serves as the Executive Vice President of Sales & Business Development for the company.
Cody Jacobs serves as the Director of Operations and oversees the manufacturing line tours.
source_title: Fouts Bros showcases its impact in community, economy | The Baldwin Bulletin

facts: Rivian secured a $6.6 billion loan from the Biden administration to fund construction of its Georgia factory.
The electric startup had to pause its initial plans to bring the plant online this year.
The approved loan will be a key part of Rivian's plans to expand its product lineup beyond the R1T and R1S models.
Vivek Ramaswamy, co-lead of the advisory committee for Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency, criticized the loan approval on Twitter/X.
Ramaswamy claimed the $6.6 billion loan implies a cost of $880k per job based on a projected 7,500 jobs at the Georgia plant.
Ramaswamy characterized the loan as a "political shot across the bow" targeting Elon Musk and Tesla.
The article clarifies that the federal government is not spending $880,000 per job because Rivian must repay the loan with interest.
A second factory will allow Rivian to build several new, more affordable models and significantly increase overall sales.
Rivian is projected to sell about 50,000 vehicles this year.
Tesla remains the most valuable automaker in the world despite being described as "hilariously overvalued" and not needing government assistance.
source_title: Vivek Ramaswamy Is Mad About Rivian's Georgia Factory Because He Doesn't Know How Loans Work

facts: Fox Factory released its 2022 global sustainability report detailing strategy and achievements.
The company introduced 2030 aspirations for positive impacts across its operations and communities.

CEO Mike Dennison stated the company formally embarked on its sustainability journey in 2021 and aims for win-win visions benefiting business, stakeholders, people, and the environment while increasing performance.
Fox Factory awarded over $1.2 million USD in grants to 85 nonprofit organizations focused on sustainable adventure through its Trail Trust community outreach initiative.
The company conducted a comprehensive climate risk and opportunity assessment aligned with the Task Force on Climate-Related Financial Disclosures framework.
Fox Factory became the official suspension partner of Extreme E, described as the world’s most sustainable extreme racing series.
The company achieved a 69% engagement score on its global employee survey, representing a 5% increase from 2021.
Fox Factory launched the Women’s Interactive Network Employee Resource Group with over 75 members.
The company provided over $180,000 USD in donations and corporate matches to 550 nonprofits through its employee giving program.
Fox Factory rolled out a Supplier Code of Conduct to all Tier 1 suppliers outlining expectations for responsible and ethical practices.
The company increased pre-production testing volume by 40% for the Specialty Sports Group and by 100% for the Powered Vehicles Group to ensure optimal product quality.
Fox Factory achieved ISO 14001 certification at its largest manufacturing facility in Gainesville, Georgia, demonstrating a commitment to responsible environmental management in operations.
The company began developing a new Enterprise Risk Management framework and Compliance Management System.
Fox Factory achieved a 100% completion rate for Code of Ethics training by Director-level and above employees.
The company developed a comprehensive Information Technology Policy to reinforce defenses against cyber threats and uphold data security standards.
For 2030, Fox Factory aims to consistently achieve top quartile employee engagement scores and attain increased representation internally and externally.
By 2030, the company plans to introduce cost-effective sustainable thinking into 100% of new development projects as well as packaging material usage and design.

Fox Factory’s 2030 social impact goals include globally granting $10 million USD, expanding access to 100,000 underrepresented adventurers through Trail Trust, and achieving 35% employee giving participation in its global giving program.
The company targets a 25% reduction in operational emissions (Scope 1 & 2) from its 2022 baseline by 2030.
Fox Factory aims to ensure that at least 90% of its Tier 1 supply chain partners have signed its Supplier Code of Conduct by 2030.
source_title: Fox Factory Releases 2022 Sustainability Report, Detailing 2030 Aspirations - Motor Sports NewsWire

facts: Fox Factory Holding Corp. is headquartered in Braselton, GA.
The company engages in designing, engineering, manufacturing, and marketing performance ride dynamics products.

Product categories include bicycles, side-by-sides, on-road vehicles with off-road capabilities, off-road vehicles and trucks, all-terrain vehicles, snowmobiles, specialty vehicles and applications, and motorcycles.
The company operates through geographic segments including North America, Asia, Europe, and Rest of the World.
Fox Factory Holding Corp. was founded on December 28, 2007.
In its earnings report released on 06/30/2021, the company reported quarterly revenue of $328.16 million and net profit of $52.72 million.
The company's market capitalization is $6.02 billion.
Berenberg Bank analyst Rudy Yang maintains a Hold rating on Fox Factory Holding (NASDAQ:FOXF) with a price target of $162, approximately 13.36% above the present share price of $142.91.
Analyst consensus for Fox Factory Holding shares is a Hold rating with an average price target of $164, ranging from a high of $170 to a low of $160.
Yang expects Fox Factory Holding to post earnings per share (EPS) of $1.05 for the third quarter of 2021.
According to TipRanks.com, analyst Rudy Yang is ranked with 1 star on a 0-5 stars scale, with an average return of -2.8% and a 25.00% success rate.
source_title: Berenberg Bank Stick to Their Hold Rating for Fox Factory Holding By Investing.com

facts: Fox Factory reported a company record of $1,602.5 million in sales for 2022, up 23.4% from the prior year.
Sales in Fox's powered vehicle business increased by 28% in 2022.
Sales in Fox's specialty sports business, which comprises its bicycle brands, increased by 17.6% for the year.
Fox Factory's bike-related brands include Fox, Easton, RaceFace, and Marzocchi.
In the fourth quarter of 2022, company-wide sales rose 19.4%, while specialty sports business sales declined 1.9% due to a return to seasonality after pandemic-driven growth.
Fox Factory cited continued improvement at its Gainesville, Georgia facility in 2022, which the company stated provides a solid foundation for further improvements in 2023.
CEO Mike Dennison attributed the company's strong performance to leveraging a diverse portfolio to combat supply chain and labor constraints, as well as inflation threats.
Fox Factory announced the acquisition of Custom Wheel House, a California-based manufacturer of wheels for cars, trucks, and off-road vehicles, for $132 million.
For the full year 2022, Fox Factory's gross margin was 33.2%, representing a 10 basis point decrease from 2021.
Fox Factory's net income for fiscal year 2022 was $205.3 million, compared to $163.8 million in the prior fiscal year.
Earnings per diluted share for fiscal year 2022 were $4.84, up from $3.87 in fiscal year 2021.
For the first quarter of fiscal 2023, Fox Factory expects sales between $380 million and $400 million, with non-GAAP adjusted earnings per diluted share between $1.10 and $1.30.
For fiscal year 2023, Fox Factory projects sales between $1.67 billion and $1.7 billion, with non-GAAP adjusted earnings per diluted share between $5.15 and $5.45.
Fox Factory's shares trade on Nasdaq under the symbol FOXF.
source_title: Fox Factory notches sales of $1.6 billion in 2022, up 23% | Bicycle Retailer and Industry News

facts: Fox Factory reports its net sales were up 4.8% in the third quarter, largely thanks to the company's powered-vehicle businesses.
The Speciality Sports Group (SSG), which includes bicycle-related businesses and ball-sports brands, had an 11.2% decrease in revenues in the quarter.
The company lowered its earnings forecast for the fourth quarter and full-year.

SSG underperformed expectations as OEMs, distributors and retail partners actively managed toward leaner inventories ahead of year-end, which impacted third quarter results and is reflected in the updated full-year outlook.
The SSG division includes Fox's bicycle business through the Fox, Marzocchi, Ride Concepts, RaceFace, Easton cycling and Lizardskins brands.
SSG also includes Fox's Marucci business, which sells baseball and softball gear.
In the third quarter, SSG sales were $133 million, down 13% from $150 million in the quarter last year.
Year-to-date, SSG sales were $391 million, up from $387 million in the same period last year.
Fox recorded a net loss of $662,000 in the third quarter and $258 million in the first nine months.
CEO Mike Dennison stated that during the quarter, the company further addressed tariff headwinds and made necessary investments in innovation and products.
These investments created near-term margin pressure that wasn't in initial expectations, but are believed to be cementing the company's competitive position.
The company is updating its near-term outlook to reflect these dynamics and remains focused on executing operational excellence, product innovation, and strengthening its balance sheet.
Fox updated its expectations for the fourth quarter, now expecting net sales of $340 million to $370 million and adjusted earnings per diluted share of $0.05 to $0.25.
For fiscal year 2025, Fox now expects net sales of $1.445 billion to $1.475 billion and adjusted earnings per diluted share in the range of $0.92 to $1.12.
Previously, Fox had forecast full-year net sales of $1.45 billion to $1.51 billion and adjusted earnings per diluted share of $1.60 to $2.00.
The financial report is filed from Duluth, Ga.
source_title: Fox Factory's specialty sports group 'underperforms' in Q3 | Bicycle Retailer and Industry News

facts: Freudenberg-NOK is a Plymouth, MI-based manufacturer of seals and gaskets, custom rubber products, PTFE components and other products for the automotive industry.
The company operates 17 U.S. manufacturing facilities as part of a global network of 47 plants operated by Germany-based parent, the Freudenberg and NOK group.
Freudenberg-NOK's 17 U.S. facilities generated $1 billion in sales in 2003 and contributed to global sales of $7.5 billion.
The company underwent a company-wide shift toward lean systems in the 1990s, reaching an industry milestone of 17,000 completed kaizens by 2002 as chairman and CEO Joseph Day retired.
Kaizen initiatives helped boost productivity, reduce defects, and slash work-in-process inventory by 80%.

Freudenberg-NOK's Cleveland, GA plant makes valve-stem seals, radial shaft seals and crank shaft seals used in automobile engines, small-equipment engines, outboard motors and similar powered equipment.
The Cleveland facility is a 15-year-old, 125,000-sq.-ft. site located 65 miles north of Atlanta.
The Cleveland plant won the 2001 Shingo Prize for Excellence in Manufacturing and is certified in ISO 14000, 9001, and QS 9000.
The plant will turn out approximately 200 million oil and valve-stem seals in 2004 in its role as a company "lead" center specializing in specific product types for customers.
The Cleveland facility is vertically integrated from "cradle to grave," making its own synthetic rubber on-site to quickly adapt to design changes and experiments.

Gary VanWambeke, general manager of Freudenberg-NOK's radial shaft seal division (with the company since 1997), oversees major kaizens that are typically five-day events using a cross-functional team, with 12 to 30 conducted per year.
The plant also conducts truncated or "minor" kaizens designed for daily problem-solving by small work groups, performing over 300 of these per year.

The Cleveland plant employs 420 workers and utilizes a multi-faceted improvement program created in 1992 called Growtth (Get Rid of Waste Through Team Harmony) that combines kaizen activity with Six Sigma and lean principles based on the Toyota Production System.

Worker certification at the Cleveland plant progresses from running a single cell devoted to one seal product, to cross-cell training, to certification in lean systems (green- and black-belt levels), and Six Sigma (introduced at Cleveland in 2000, also with green- and black-belt levels).
To date, the Cleveland plant has produced nine black belts (four lean, five Six Sigma) and 52 green belts (12 lean, 40 Six Sigma).

Employees must demonstrate a basic understanding and support of Six Sigma and lean to be promoted from a set-up to a lead position, shown through certification progress, number of minor kaizens completed, and participation in major events.
Emily Bailey, the plant's materials manager, is a Six Sigma black belt pursuing her lean-systems black belt.
Donna Unger, VanWambeke's executive assistant, holds a lean-systems and Six-Sigma green belt and has participated in Growtth activity, kaizens, and a Six Sigma project.
The plant dedicates cells to a single customer's orders, typically a major automaker, ensuring that customer always has a direct contact at Freudenberg-NOK familiar with their seal usage.
Ford Motor Co. recently sent a letter congratulating the Cleveland plant for shipping zero defectives last year.
Defects at the plant dropped from 650 parts per million in 1997 to 9 ppm by 2003.
Sales per associate at the Cleveland facility jumped 57% during the same period defects were reduced.
The plant's safety improved as OSHA reportables dropped 40% and lost work days dropped 80%.
The plant's total 2003 savings from lean and Six Sigma activities was $1.2 million.

VanWambeke is also in charge of five other facilities (one each in Brazil and Mexico and three in New Hampshire) and plans to drive standardization across the division so perfected processes spread to others.

The company's philosophy avoids adding brick and mortar; recent kaizens freed up seven presses worth of equipment that are being sent to open floor space in Mexico and Brazil to give greater delivery flexibility for customers operating in both countries.
Workers receive cash incentives for their participation in successful improvement projects.

A suggestion system called the "Make My Job Easier" box evolved into an implementation system where Growtth managers help workers form small work groups to fix problems, eventually leading to a natural trickle of ideas as employees learned to self-facilitate.

Green- and black-belt certification requires significant time spent outside the plant for extensive reading and completing projects at other Freudenberg-NOK facilities, with workers making their own arrangements alongside daily responsibilities.

Doug Hints, the plant's valve-stem seal operations manager, notes that green- and black-belt applicants must become their own lean systems managers due to the self-directed nature of the certification.
Management uses a unique hierarchy with two plant managers and single supervisors for all three shifts to ensure accountability across shifts.

Business-cell leaders are empowered to adjust their schedules, typically coming in early to catch the end of third shift at 7 a.m., staying through first shift, and catching the beginning of second shift.
Cleveland won the 2001 Shingo award in the Large Business category after pursuing it with help from previous Shingo winners within the company and receiving an audit on its first application.
The plant is placing new emphasis on improving its 5S strategy and implementing a behavior-based safety program to further reduce OSHA reportables.
The company encourages suppliers to adopt lean principles, sending experts from the Cleveland plant to supplier facilities to help them start the process.

Operations manager Doug Hints believes the auto industry will become more global but also more centralized with fewer suppliers and higher requirements, stating that suppliers not heavily into lean will not survive.
President & CEO Dr. Mohsen Sohi, a Six-Sigma black belt, continues building on lean and Six Sigma principles and requires all plant managers to be similarly certified.
source_title: Best Practices: Freudenberg-NOK/Cleveland, GA: Continuous Kaizens | Manufacturing.net

facts: Freudenberg has been active in the US market for more than 175 years.

The company supplies batteries for heavy-duty applications, gaskets and seals, vibration control technologies, filters, medical components, confectionary applications, technical textiles, release agents, lubricants, expansion joints, and household products to US customers.

Freudenberg’s AI Innovation Center is based in the United States, where a team of data scientists and engineers use data-driven methods to improve material and product development through enhanced integration of artificial intelligence.
The company employs more than 8,500 people across the US, helping power wind turbines, improve mobility and transportation, and develop life-saving medical devices.
Freudenberg Performance Materials LP operates a facility at 660 Allied Industrial Blvd, Macon, GA 31206 USA.

The company offers US employees benefits including international opportunities, diversity & inclusion initiatives, sustainability programs, safe work environments, personal development trainings, and flexible work models.

facts: Sigma Freudenberg NOK (SFN), a collaborative venture between India, Germany, and Japan specializing in the production of oil seals and sealing products for domestic and global markets, is poised to make significant advancements in its Indian operations.
On February 4, SFN will unveil a cutting-edge manufacturing facility in Basma, near Rajpura in Punjab.
The new facility represents an investment of Rs 185 crore.
The facility spans 40 acres and boasts 250,000 square feet of enclosed space.
This marks SFN's second plant in Basma.
The inauguration ceremony will be graced by Punjab Chief Minister Prakash Singh Badal.

facts: FNST is a global producer of elastomeric seals and custom molded products for industries including the automotive, aerospace, energy and construction fields.

Walker stated that scientists must affect positive change in the environment by creating industrial solutions that factor in megatrends like fewer natural resources, dwindling landfill space and global warming.

Walker emphasized that industrial strategy must be innovative enough to solve customer issues using new materials, fewer resources, cleaner processes and innovative technologies that improve fuel economy and lower emissions.
FNST has long been at the forefront of material research and development efforts to address changes in the polymer and rubber industries, which are heavily dependent on petrochemicals and oil.
Walker noted that sustainable practices, when implemented correctly, should yield savings for companies and customers.

Walker suggested that trade associations and other organizations should encourage manufacturers to pursue sustainable products and processes by adopting new business scorecards that give credit to their efforts.

Walker will focus his presentation on how FNST applies sustainability throughout its global operations, from research and development efforts to manufacturing production and supply chain relations.
The company’s successful migration to single cavity molding is producing better product consistency while using less energy in its manufacturing plants.

facts: GEDA held its Annual Awards luncheon on a Monday to recognize top deals of the year and excellence by individuals.
Awards presented included Deals of the Year, Volunteer of the Year, Rip Wiley Award for Professional Excellence, and Committee Chair and Member of the Year.
Chris Womack, Chairman, President & CEO of Georgia Power, spoke and conferred the awards.
Over 200 economic developers from around the state attended the ceremony in-person at the Georgia Tech Hotel at Tech Square in Midtown Atlanta.

The GEDA Deal of the Year Awards recognize outstanding achievement in the location or expansion of new businesses in Georgia that meet community economic development goals and involve significant community program support, such as financial or permitting assistance, workforce development, and state financial support.
Winners were selected in three community categories: Small, Mid-Size, and Large.
The Small category Deal of the Year winner was Bacon County Development Authority – La Regina Atlantica.
The Mid-Size category Deal of the Year winner was Camden County Joint Development Authority – Plug Power.
The Large category Deal of the Year winner was Dalton-Whitfield County Joint Development Authority – GEDIA Georgia.
Volunteer of the Year recipient is Alan Verner, Chairman of the Joint Development Authority of Jasper, Morgan, Newton & Walton Counties.
Tim Evans, Vice President – Economic Development of the Greater Hall County Chamber of Commerce, was named the 2021 recipient of the Rip Wiley Award for Professional Excellence.

The Rip Wiley Award is named after H. W. “Rip” Wiley, who worked for the Georgia Department of Industry, Trade and Tourism (now the Department of Economic Development) as Director of the Industry Division when he passed in October 1978.
The award was established upon his death in 1978 to be presented to the person in economic development who best represents his spirit of excellence.
Jason Dunn, GEDA Chair of the Professional Development Committee and Executive Director of the Fitzgerald-Ben Hill County Development Authority, received the Committee Chair of the Year award.

Rachel Barnwell, Manager of Economic Development Programs for the Development Authority of Bulloch County, was recognized as Member of the Year for her efforts on the 2021 Annual Conference Committee.

GEDA is a nonprofit association of professionals, volunteers and supporters that provides networking and professional development opportunities for its members and supports public policies that promote quality job creation and sustainable economic development throughout the state.
source_title: GEDA Recognizes Top Deals & Excellence By Individuals | AM 1180 Radio

entity_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia Battery Plant Joint Venture
canonical_name: Hyundai-LG Energy Solution Georgia EV Battery Plant Joint Venture
facts: Federal authorities launched an immigration raid on a Georgia construction site for a Hyundai electric vehicle battery plant.
The raid led to the arrests of nearly 500 workers, about 300 of whom were South Korean nationals.
Seoul is working with U.S. law enforcement to return those detained workers home.
Hyundai stated it is "committed to full compliance with all laws and regulations" regarding the raid.
Hyundai noted that "none of those detained is directly employed by Hyundai Motor Company."
In the United States, Hyundai is the nation's fourth bestseller of new vehicles, behind GM, Toyota and Ford.
Hyundai stated it will invest in American manufacturing and create thousands of jobs in full accordance with U.S. law.
Hyundai declined to say specifically if it would alter its plans because of last week’s raid.
LG Energy Solution, which will operate the factory in a joint venture with Hyundai, has reportedly decided to delay the start of production until the first half of 2026.
South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun told lawmakers that if the factory completion is delayed, the United States will suffer significant losses.
Trump dismissed concerns about the raid straining relations with South Korea, stating Sunday: "No, we have a great relationship with South Korea."

The South Korean government has been working with the Trump administration to finalize a framework trade agreement under which South Korea would buy $100 billion of U.S. energy products and invest $350 billion in the United States.
Trump said those $350 billion investment funds would be his to use any way he sees fit.
Days after the raid, Trump admonished foreign companies on Truth Social, encouraging them to legally bring smart people with great technical talent to build World Class products.

Other projects touted by Trump that could require visas for similar workers include semiconductor factories, high-powered AI data centers, pharmaceutical plants, energy infrastructure, and aircraft assembly lines.

An ICE spokesperson told NBC News the investigation was focused on ensuring accountability for law violators, protecting jobs for Georgians, ensuring a level playing field for compliant businesses, safeguarding economic integrity, and protecting workers from exploitation.
Trump’s border czar, Tom Homan, told CNN the administration plans "a lot more worksite operations."
Homan cited his personal experience hiring a roofing company, noting he had to call five different companies before finding one that guaranteed a legal workforce.
The Labor Department reported a slowdown in jobs growth on Friday, indicating a cooling broader economy.
The administration touted a decline in foreign-born workers despite the jobs report.

Farms across California, meatpacking plants in Nebraska, and businesses in other states received ICE/Border Patrol visits in May and June, leading to protests and complaints from industries losing reliable workers.
Farmers were already under pressure from Trump’s tariff policy, which hurt demand for American products overseas.
Trump posted in June that aggressive immigration policy is taking good, long-time workers away from farmers and the hotel/leisure business, making jobs almost impossible to replace.
Trump stated: "We must protect our Farmers, but get the CRIMINALS OUT OF THE USA. Changes are coming!"
ICE initially paused visits to farms, hotels, restaurants, fisheries, and other workplaces, but DHS walked that back after about a week and visits resumed.
Trump conceded the administration will have to grant visas to overseas workers, especially those who could train U.S. workers in specialty industries like establishing battery factories.
Trump stated: "We’re going to have to train people. And the way you train people is [to] bring people in that know what they’re doing and let them stay for a little while and help."
Trump added: "So, I’m going to look at that. It’s a very interesting situation that took place in Georgia."

Trump has pointed to the Hyundai factory and related U.S. investments as evidence that his administration’s economic agenda is working, even though such facilities take years to begin producing tangible output.
Wells Fargo economists wrote Friday that stricter immigration enforcement has likely reduced new entrants to the workforce, following a jobs report showing just 22,000 jobs added in August.
The August jobs report included revisions that showed a labor market contraction in June.
EY’s chief economist concurred, noting that "tighter immigration policies are increasingly restraining labor supply."
source_title: Trump trade, immigration agendas collide in Hyundai raid

entity_name: Hyundai Motor Group Georgia EV and Battery Manufacturing Facility
facts: Hyundai Motor Group (HMG) will open a state-of-the-art U.S. smart factory at the Bryan County Megasite.
Non-affiliated Hyundai Motor Group suppliers will invest approximately another $1 billion in the project, delivering approximately 8,100 new jobs to Georgia’s coastal region.
Since 2020 alone, Georgia has announced more than 20 EV-related projects that will invest more than $13.54 billion and create nearly 18,000 jobs in the state.
In choosing Georgia, Hyundai Motor Group cited speed-to-market, workforce, and the state’s ability to meet the company’s carbon neutrality standards.
Georgia is home to an existing network of Hyundai subsidiaries and suppliers.

At a signing ceremony on May 20, 2022, Governor Brian P. Kemp and Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung announced that Hyundai Motor Group will open its first fully dedicated electric vehicle (EV) and battery manufacturing facility in the State of Georgia.
Hyundai Motor Group will invest $5.54 billion in opening a state-of-the-art U.S. smart factory at the Bryan County Megasite.

Governor Kemp was joined by Jay Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, and José Muñoz, President and COO of Hyundai Motor Company, along with Hyundai Motor Group executives and state and local economic development leaders at the premier 2,923-acre site.
The megasite was purchased jointly by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority (JDA) and the State less than a year ago.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that the announcement is now the largest economic development project in Georgia’s history.
Hyundai Motor Group Executive Chair Euisun Chung stated the company is proud to share its plan to open its first dedicated full EV and battery manufacturing facilities in the U.S.
Hyundai Motor Group expects to begin construction on the new facility in January 2023.
Full production is expected in the first half of 2025, with an annual capacity of 300,000 units.
Wages throughout the project will be well-paying advanced manufacturing positions competitive in the local market.
Jaehoon (Jay) Chang, President and CEO of Hyundai Motor Company, stated that America embraces change and drives innovation, making it the location for their first dedicated EV plant in the U.S.

Developed by the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA over the last several years in partnership with the state, the Bryan County Megasite offers speed-to-market requirements that reduce barriers to speedy operations.
Industrial utilities are adjacent to the site, and extensive due diligence reports have been completed.

President and COO of Hyundai Motor Company José Muñoz stated they are thrilled to partner with Governor Kemp and the Savannah Joint Economic Development Authority to bring a flexible EV smart factory to Bryan County.
Headquartered in Seoul, Korea, Hyundai Motor Group employs 250,000 people worldwide.
The Group’s vehicles are sold in 193 countries through 5,000 dealerships and showrooms.
At the new Georgia highly connected, automated, and flexible EV smart factory, Hyundai Motor Group plans to produce a diverse range of innovative full electric vehicles.
Details of production models will be shared at later dates.
Through the battery manufacturing facility, the Group aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV battery and other EV components in the U.S. market.
When it nears its planned 2025 start of operations, Hyundai Motor Group will seek local vendors and suppliers.

Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor Joint Development Authority Board of Directors Chairman Carter Infinger stated the jobs being created and investment made by Hyundai Motor Group will reverberate for years and generations to come.
Bryan County is the fastest-growing county in the state.
The strategic purchase of the Bryan County Megasite in 2021 was the largest in state history.
The site is adjacent to Interstate 16 with immediate access from I-95 and I-16 to 250 major metro areas.

The site is less than 30 miles from the Port of Savannah, the single-largest and fastest-growing container terminal in the U.S., with two Class I rail facilities on-site provided by Georgia Central Railway.

Georgia Department of Transportation Commissioner Russell McMurry stated Hyundai Motor Group’s new facility location provides stellar access to global and domestic markets for both assembly components and finished vehicle marketing.

Building on the state’s existing automotive assets and developing workforce, Georgia is consistently welcoming increased investment in the electric mobility ecosystem and supply chain across the state.
Georgia is the Southeastern leader for EV registrations per 1,000 registered automobiles and offers more EV charging outlets per capita than anywhere else in the Southeast.

Georgia’s Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance is a public-private-nonprofit initiative focused on growing the electric mobility ecosystem in the state and strengthening Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) was represented by Project Manager Taylor Walden in partnership with the Department’s Georgia-based Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim, as well as the Savannah Harbor-Interstate 16 Corridor JDA, which includes Bryan County, Bulloch County, Chatham County, and Effingham County; the Georgia Department of Transportation; the Georgia Ports Authority; Georgia Quick Start; and Georgia Power.
Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated under Governor Kemp’s leadership they have been focused on putting Georgia at the forefront of electric mobility.

At the new facility, Hyundai Motor Group plans to implement many of its advanced intelligent manufacturing technologies that are currently under test at the Group’s innovation hub, including intelligent A.I.-equipped manufacturing and data-driven processes.
The plant will help support Hyundai Motor Group’s efforts to achieve its carbon neutrality target through the active use of renewable energy for the facility.
Hyundai Motor Group is a global enterprise that has created a value chain based on mobility, steel, and construction, as well as logistics, finance, IT, and service.
With about 250,000 employees worldwide, the Group’s mobility brands include Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp announced that Hyundai Industrial Co., Ltd. will create 100 new jobs and invest more than $24 million in a new manufacturing facility near Newnan in Coweta County.
Partners like Kia and Hyundai are attracting a large network of suppliers, creating jobs and opportunity for hardworking Georgians across the entire state.
Suppliers have now committed to bringing over 5,300 jobs to Georgia, mostly to communities outside of large urban areas, since last year’s Hyundai Metaplant announcement.
Hyundai Industrial was established in 1978 and is headquartered in Ulsan, Korea.
The Hyundai and Kia supplier specializes in manufacturing car seats, arm rests, and head rests.

Mr. Woosuck Kim, Team Leader at HDI America Inc., stated that Hyundai Industrial has more than four decades of experience providing comfortable and safe experiences for drivers and passengers with some of the world's biggest automotive brands.

Hyundai Industrial is excited to be part of the e-mobility transition happening in Georgia and looks forward to working with Coweta County to create mutually beneficial growth for the community and future employees.
Hyundai Industrial has purchased an existing building at 83 Amlajack Way in Coweta County.
The building will be retrofitted to produce arm and head rests for OEMs, including Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America.
Hyundai Industrial will focus on filling manufacturing production roles.
Jobs will be posted online when open.

Coweta County Board of Commissioners Chairman John Reidelbach said Hyundai joins their thriving manufacturing industries and will have close proximity to I-85, making transport to nearby automobile plants effortless.
We are excited for the opportunities Hyundai will bring our citizens as workforce opportunities continue to increase within the community.

Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development’s (GDEcD) Global Commerce team on this competitive project in partnership with the Coweta County Development Authority and Georgia EMC.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson noted that with Kia in West Georgia and Hyundai on the eastern coast, suppliers like Hyundai Industrial can locate almost anywhere in the state and still be center of at least two major manufacturers.
With a variety of options available, companies are able to select a community that’s the right fit for them, and vice versa.

This relationship between Coweta County and Hyundai Industrial is another classic example of Georgia’s partnership approach to economic development, matching a community with an existing asset and plan to a company whose goals aligned.

For over a century, Georgia has fostered healthy industry practices, encouraged collaboration and innovation, and positioned the state as a leader in developing and harnessing emerging technologies for the evolving automotive and mobility industry.
As the electric vehicle market continues to grow, Georgia has pursued the entire supply chain, creating more than $25.1 billion in investments and 29,000 jobs since 2020.
In fiscal year 2023, job creation in the automotive industry increased by 324 percent when compared to fiscal year 2021.
Suppliers attracted by Georgia’s mobility original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) resulted in over $2 billion in investment across the state in FY23.
Hyundai Industrial aims to manufacture automotive car seats that not only provide comfort but also play a critical role in safety.

Hyundai Industrial creates safer and more comfortable experiences through developing cutting-edge products based on talent and advanced technology while also striving for mutually beneficial growth for its employees and the community.

facts: Hyundai Motor America announced Dec. 11 the pricing and equipment details for the all-new 2024 Hyundai Kona Electric SUV.
The 2024 Kona Electric comes in three price trims and an estimated EPA range of more than 250 miles.
This second-generation model adds improved cabin and cargo space, up to 261 miles of EPA-estimated range, and faster charging speeds with an upgraded 11-kW Level 2 onboard charger.
The SE trim features a 99-kW (133-HP) Electric Motor with an MSRP of $32,675.
The SEL trim features a 150-kW (201-HP) Electric Motor with an MSRP of $36,675.
The Limited trim features a 150-kW (201-HP) Electric Motor with an MSRP of $41,045.
The 2024 Kona Electric comes standard with Hyundai SmartSense advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS).

Standard ADAS features include forward collision-avoidance assist with junction-turning and direct-oncoming (FCA), blind-spot collision warning (BCW), lane keeping assist (LKA), rear cross-traffic collision-avoidance assist (RCCA), driver attention warning (DAW), safe exit warning (SEW), and rear occupant alert (ROA).
The vehicle includes a haptic-feedback steering wheel that alerts drivers of possible dangers, as well as second-row seat belt reminders.
The Kona Electric comes standard with a 12.3-inch digital instrument cluster integrated with Hyundai’s next-generation 12.3-inch touchscreen infotainment system.
The infotainment system delivers a new graphic user interface, faster processing speeds, customizable layouts, and EV-specific menus for charging preferences and finding charging stations.
Advanced connectivity features include standard Wireless Android Auto and Apple CarPlay, over-the-air (OTA) software updates, and Wi-Fi hotspots.
The new i-PEDAL feature enables a one-pedal driving mode that allows drivers to accelerate, decelerate, and stop using only the accelerator when desired.
The Smart Regenerative System adjusts regenerative braking levels based on real-time data from forward traffic flow to optimize efficiency.
Electric features include standard LED projector headlights, LED daytime running lamps and taillights, and an available “Seamless Horizon” LED DRL array.
The Kona Electric has grown nearly six inches in length, the second row has gained three inches in legroom, and the cargo area has grown by 33%.
Standard wheels on this SUV are 17-inch aero alloys which can help to improve aerodynamic efficiency.
The 2024 Kona Electric features Bluelink+, a connected car services program that Hyundai intends to include for all 2024 model vehicles.

Bluelink+ gives original owners of all 2024 model-year Hyundai vehicles complimentary access to all services included in the previous Bluelink connected care, remote and guidance subscription packages (4G LTE network and technology dependent).
The Kona Electric features an expansion of the recently launched Hyundai Pay platform to enable in-vehicle payments.
In addition to parking, Kona Electric owners can pay for charging at select locations through Hyundai Pay.
source_title: Hyundai Announces 2024 Kona Electric Pricing | Automotive Fleet

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is located in Georgia and is described as America's largest car manufacturing plant.
Nearly all operations at HMGMA are powered by AI, including drones that scan stacked pallets for inventory.
Robotic arms execute programmed arcs, while cameras direct sanding tools to correct paint blemishes.
Uncrewed taxis transport vehicles and parts to their next manufacturing destination within the facility.
A four-legged Boston Dynamics AI dog named Spot walks through the facility scanning components to verify they are snapped into place.
The HMGMA facility spans roughly 278 football fields and carries a nearly $7.6 billion price tag according to Hyundai.
HMGMA celebrated its grand opening in March.
Unlike most US automakers that retrofit old plants, Hyundai built HMGMA from the ground up with AI, Nvidia chips, and robotics at its core.
Each vehicle produced at the plant runs through at least 23 AI-based or robotic systems before finalization.
Industry analysts state Hyundai's AI-first approach helps navigate policy changes and customer trends impacting profitability.
The technology improves quality control and reduces manufacturing costs beyond traditional manufacturing capabilities.
Erin Keating, executive analyst at Cox Automotive, noted Hyundai's integration of humanoid robots sets a new benchmark for smart manufacturing.
Keating added that the AI integration potentially offers cost savings and faster responses to industrial disruptions.
The facility features an integrated digital command hub acting as a real-time digital twin computer simulation of the physical plant.

Miles Johnson, Hyundai's communications representative for Ioniq vehicles and tech, explained sensors collect production floor data for an AI algorithm to create virtual replicas of each manufacturing step.
Johnson stated the AI predicts optimized outcomes, identifies root causes of production issues, searches trained past data for likely causes, and suggests corrective action plans.
Keating noted the AI detects defects in real time, analyzes root causes, and suggests corrective actions to significantly reduce rework, scrap, and warranty claims.
The plant currently builds Hyundai's Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 electric SUVs using these technical processes.
Kia production at HMGMA is scheduled to begin in 2026, with Genesis vehicles planned for an unspecified future date.
Hyundai stated AI and robotics will assist humans who remain responsible for on-the-ground robotics maintenance, data analysis, and parts installation.
Hundreds of current roles focus on programming the factory's 850 robots, troubleshooting AI, or interpreting production data instead of repetitive physical tasks.
Hyundai aims to hire 8,500 employees at HMGMA by 2031 and calls it the largest single business investment in Georgia's history.
In March, Hyundai reported having already hired 1,000 full-time staff members at the plant.
Hyundai built a nearby Hyundai Mobility Training Center of Georgia to train college-level talent for the facility.
The training program is coordinated with three local technical colleges and is expected to open this year.
Both the Ioniq 5 and Ioniq 9 were positioned to benefit from President Joe Biden's Inflation Reduction Act.
The vehicles are eligible for $7,500 tax credits for EVs assembled in American plants.
Hyundai broke ground on HMGMA in October 2022, after which American EV uptake underperformed analyst predictions.
Federal EV subsidies are set to end in September, creating pushback against EV adoption.
Hyundai is betting its EVs, including the three-row Ioniq 9 launched in May 2025, will attract buyers without tax incentives.
Analysts note Hyundai's AI-powered manufacturing can still succeed despite current reliance on EV sales and pressure from cheap Chinese vehicles and supply chain volatility.
Morningstar analyst David Whiston stated companies adopting AI for manufacturing and logistics are better positioned to manage costs and respond to disruptions.

Keating emphasized that leveraging AI for smart factories, autonomous logistics, and predictive analytics helps automakers scale production efficiently and meet regulatory and consumer demands faster.
Keating concluded that AI and robotics are becoming non-negotiable for competitiveness in the automotive industry.
source_title: How AI Boosts Quality Control at Hyundai's Georgia Plant - Business Insider

facts: The raid on Hyundai’s Savannah-area electric vehicle plant was part of a lengthy investigation into the unlawful hiring of illegal aliens at the industrial site, federal officials said Friday.
Georgia officials had promised that the industrial site would create thousands of jobs for locals.
Operation Low Voltage resulted in more than 475 people detained — more than 300 of whom were Koreans — for alleged immigration violations.
Federal agents executed a criminal search warrant to gather evidence at the battery plant jointly owned by Hyundai Motor Group and LG, two of South Korea’s largest companies.

According to the search warrant, federal agents were seeking evidence including employment records from the lithium battery factory that could reveal illegal immigration and hiring practices at the HL-GA Battery Company LLC and five other companies identified by federal officials as subcontractors.
Federal officials had identified four people with Hispanic names as part of the basis for the warrant signed by a magistrate judge in the Southern District of Georgia.

Steven Schrank, the special agent in charge of Homeland Security Investigations for Georgia and Alabama, said the raid at the Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America was the largest single site enforcement operation ever carried out by the Department of Homeland Security’s enforcement division.

Schrank told reporters that while some of the detained workers illegally crossed the U.S. border, others had entered the country legally but had expired visas or had entered on a visa waiver that prohibited them from working at the $7.6 billion site where Hyundai Motor Group and an interconnected group of suppliers are building vehicles.

Hyundai broke ground at the site two years ago after Georgia promised billions of dollars in tax credits and tax abatements if the world’s largest carmaker met its goals of creating more than 8,000 jobs at an average salary of approximately $58,000.
As of March, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America reported that it had 1,200 people currently employed, not counting Koreans, and was operating one shift at the plant.
At that time, the battery facility was not operational and remains under construction.
No criminal charges had been filed Friday.
Federal officials said the majority of workers detained this week have been sent to Folkston, the Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency’s detention center in Charlton County.
The governor’s office responded to the raid by saying that state agencies remain committed to the rule of law.
Spokesman Carter Chapman stated: “All companies operating within the state must follow the laws of Georgia and our nation.”
There was no immediate comment from Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr.
The South Korean government expressed “concern and regret” over the operation.

South Korean Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lee Jaewoong said in a televised statement from Seoul: “The business activities of our investors and the rights of our nationals must not be unjustly infringed in the process of U.S. law enforcement.”
President Trump said about the people arrested: “We had as I understand it a lot of illegal aliens,” adding, “Some not the best of people. But we had a lot of illegal aliens working there.”

South Korean officials told The Wall Street Journal that many of the Koreans detained worked for LG Energy were at the metaplant on a business trip and had visas that allowed them to train employees in Georgia.

In addition to Koreans, foreign nationals from Guatemala, Colombia, Chile, Venezuela and Mexico had been detained, according to Migrant Equity Southeast, a Coastal Georgia migrant workers’ organization.
Thursday’s operation was more complex than other ICE enforcement actions that have occurred since President Donald Trump took office earlier this year.

The raid involved 10 federal agencies, including the Federal Bureau of Investigation and the Internal Revenue Service’s criminal investigation unit, according to Margaret Heap, the U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of Georgia.
Steven Schrank said: “This was not a immigration operation where agents went into the premises, rounded up folks, and put them on buses.”

Schrank added: “This has been a multi-month criminal investigation where we have developed evidence and conducted interviews, gathered documents and presented that evidence to the court in order to obtain a judicial search warrant.”

The Current has previously reported on a pattern of workplace deaths and injuries affecting both American and foreign workers at the metaplant, including the death of a Korean technical worker in March.
There is also a reported pattern by key Hyundai suppliers of not reporting those incidents to the federal agency responsible for workplace safety.

HMGMA, the legal entity that negotiated nearly $2 billion in state tax abatements and credits with Georgia, has previously said that it was not responsible for the actions of its supplier companies on the site.

In response to the ongoing federal investigation, Hyundai Motor Group said Friday that its North America Chief Manufacturing Officer Chris Susock would assume governance of the entire EV site in Bryan County.
Hyundai Motor Group stated: “We will conduct an investigation to ensure all suppliers and their subcontractors comply with all laws and regulations.”

The company added: “We are reviewing our processes to ensure that all parties working on our projects maintain the same high standards of legal compliance that we demand of ourselves. This includes thorough vetting of employment practices by contractors and subcontractors.”
source_title: Federal operation at Savannah-area Hyundai plant nets 475 detentions • Georgia Recorder

facts: Hyundai CEO José Muñoz sought to distance his company from an Immigration and Customs Enforcement raid earlier this month at the company’s battery factory in Georgia.
The immigration raid resulted in the arrest of hundreds of South Korean workers.
Hyundai CEO José Muñoz clarified that the immigration raid had targeted a facility operated by LG Energy Solutions.
The factory is operated by LG Energy Solutions, and most of the detained workers were employed by suppliers for that company, not Hyundai.
Muñoz made these remarks during a briefing with reporters on Thursday after the automaker’s CEO Investor Day event in New York City.
Muñoz stated he could not believe what he saw because he would have known beforehand if it involved his facility.
Upon reviewing details, Muñoz realized the raid targeted LG’s battery plant, not Hyundai’s facility.
Muñoz noted that all media photos of the raid showed Hyundai’s Metaplant facility rather than the neighboring LG factory.
The plant’s construction was delayed two to three months due to labor shortages.
The September 4th raid resulted in the arrest of 475 workers, most of whom were from South Korea.
The battery plant is jointly operated by Hyundai and LG Energy Solutions.
President Donald Trump announced he will allow foreign companies to temporarily bring in “people of expertise” to train American workers.
Critics warn that the raid may have chilled foreign investment in the US, particularly targeting automakers working on EVs.
Labor experts warn that the ICE raid in Georgia could chill foreign investment in the US and target particular industries, especially automakers working on EVs.
Muñoz defended the foreign workers who were in Georgia to help build the factory, arguing that the expertise to build these specialized factories does not exist in the US.
Muñoz called for the creation of a new type of visa for highly skilled workers who help develop enormous projects to ensure future raids do not happen.
Muñoz stated that both the South Korean and US governments are working actively to ensure situations like this do not happen again.

entity_name: Kia Georgia Inc. and Hyundai Mobis Workers' Wage Lawsuit Settlement
canonical_name: Kia Georgia Inc. / Hyundai Mobis Wage Suit Settlement
facts: A joint motion filed Thursday by former production line employees, Kia Georgia Inc., Hyundai Mobis, staffing agencies Allswell and SPJ Connect, and SPJ Connect CEO Youngjin Lee asked a federal judge for preliminary approval of a settlement ending the workers' lawsuit.
The lawsuit included Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) and racketeering claims.
Subject to court approval, the defendants reached a nonreversionary settlement of $11.5 million for approximately 614 class members.
The $11.5 million settlement total includes $3.45 million in attorney fees and expenses.
FLSA settlement collective members, including named plaintiffs, will receive an average of $1,672.61, totaling $999,870.00.
The $999,870.00 payment is a pro-rated amount based on the number of workweeks each collective member worked for the defendants.
The parties described the proposed settlement as fair and reasonable, resulting from lengthy settlement negotiations.

Separately, the workers and third defendant staffing agency Job Knowledge filed a motion Friday seeking approval for a separate settlement providing $7,500 to named plaintiff Aaron Hernán Pérez Salazar and $5,000 to each remaining plaintiff with claims against Job Knowledge, totaling $27,500.
The parties argued the separate Job Knowledge settlement is reasonable, provides substantial recovery, and would reduce litigation complexity, expense, and duration.
The original suit was initially filed in August 2022 with only one named plaintiff: engineer and electromagnetics technician Jorge Oswaldo Aquino Martinez.

Aquino Martinez alleged that Allswell and SPJ Connect helped him secure a nonimmigrant TN visa, which is reserved for Canadians and Mexicans working in the U.S. under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement.

He alleged he came to the U.S. based on promises to work at Hyundai as an engineer but was assigned manual labor installing car dashboards and paid "substantially less per hour" than his white, American co-workers.
The latest version of the complaint alleges violations including an FLSA collective action claim and federal and state Racketeering Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act (RICO) claims.
Representatives for the parties did not respond immediately to requests for comment.

The workers are represented by Daniel Werner and James Radford of Radford Scott LLP, Rachel B. Benjamin and Brian J. Sutherland of Beal Sutherland Berlin & Brown LLC, Christopher B. Hall of Hall & Lampros LLP, and Julia Solórzano and Abigail R. Kerfoot of Centro De Los Derechos Del Migrante Inc.
Hyundai Mobis is represented by Jon M. Gumbel, Ingu Hwang, Michael L. Lucas, and Allison Hawkins of Burr & Forman LLP.
Kia Georgia Inc. is represented by Sarah M. Phaff and Frank B. Shuster of Constangy Brooks Smith & Prophete LLP.
Allswell and SPJ Connect are represented by John S. Gibbs II, Charles E. Peeler, and Emily E. Schifter of Troutman Pepper Locke LLP.
Job Knowledge is represented by Charles K. McKnight Jr. and Nicholas F. McDaniel of Taylor Duma LLP.
The case is Martinez et al. v. Mobis Alabama LLC et al., case number 3:22-cv-00145, in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia.
source_title: Ex-Kia, Hyundai Workers Score $11.5M Deal In Wage Suit - Law360

EHS Specialist duties include communicating HR policies, conducting exit interviews, collecting and analyzing employee data, organizing employee files, adhering to regulatory and safety standards, and representing the company at job fairs and college campuses.

The EHS Specialist role requires regular talking and hearing, frequently sitting, standing, squatting, bending, walking, using hands to type and handle objects, reaching items at chest level or above shoulder, lifting up to 50 lbs, and working extended hours as needed.
The EHS Specialist position may require domestic and/or international travel.
Employees must wear PPE as required in each area and generally work in indoor office and warehouse environments.

Position requirements for the EHS Specialist include proven work experience, demonstrated knowledge of employee law, outstanding interpersonal skills, exceptional written and verbal communication, excellent organizational skills, strong problem-solving capabilities, and relevant training or certifications.
Education requirement for the EHS Specialist is a Bachelor's degree in Human Resources or relevant amount of experience.
Software skills required include proficiency in Microsoft Office, experience with ADP and SAP preferred, database maintenance skills, and understanding of HRIS systems.
Benefits offered include 401(k), health insurance, life insurance, supplemental insurance, dental insurance, vision insurance, and paid time off.
Hyundai Transys Georgia Seating System is an Equal Opportunity Employer that prohibits discrimination and harassment of any kind.

The company is committed to achieving a diverse workforce through application of equal opportunity, non-discrimination, and anti-harassment policies across all aspects of employment including recruitment, hiring, promotions, transfers, discipline, terminations, wage and salary administration, benefits, and training.

All employment decisions are based on business needs, job requirements, and individual qualifications without regard to race, color, religion, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, family or parental status, national origin, veteran status, disability, or other protected statuses.

facts: Inalfa opened its initial 300-job operation in Cherokee County during a surge in the Southeast automotive industry.
The company announced a 100,000-square-foot expansion to its Acworth location.
The expansion adds 100 more jobs and space for additional high-volume production lines.
The new building is utilized to encapsulate glass panels via injection molding and steel reinforcement bonding.
Inalfa has a large customer base in the Southeast.
Quick Start has been Inalfa’s training partner since the beginning of its operations.
Quick Start produced company-specific training videos on topics such as glass and steel reinforcement bonding processes, glass encapsulation and Inalfa’s TP10-BMW F15 Line for the BMW X5.
The TP10-BMW F15 Line is described as the longest and most complex production line.
Everyone on the production floor went through Quick Start’s pre-hire assessment process.
Michael Adoyo, Inalfa’s production trainer of operations, stated that “[The pre-hire] gives them an awareness of what it’s like and prepares them mentally before they actually hit the floor.”
Inalfa is headquartered in Venray, The Netherlands.
Inalfa's vision is to become the first-choice OEM partner in roof systems and integrated technologies in the changing automotive industry landscape — a partner that is trusted to deliver.
Chattahoochee Technical College is listed as a contact on the page.

facts: JCB began construction on a new $500 million factory in San Antonio, Texas, marking the biggest investment in the company's history.
An official groundbreaking ceremony was held on June 4, 2024, where The Hon. Alice Bamford turned the first shovelful of dirt.
The new facility will occupy a 720,000 sq. ft. (67,000m²) building on a 400-acre site in Bexar County.
Construction is underway to create 1,500 new jobs over five years.
The factory will produce Loadall telescopic handlers and aerial access equipment, with production scheduled to start in 2026.
The facility will have the capacity to expand and manufacture other products in the future.
It will be JCB's second largest plant globally, rivaled only by its world headquarters in Rocester, Staffordshire, England.
Chairman Lord Bamford stated that construction equipment manufacturers sell more than 300,000 machines annually in North America, making it the single largest market worldwide.
Governor Greg Abbott noted that Texas was named The Best State for Business for a record-breaking 20th year and welcomed JCB as a new business to the state.
JCB CEO Graeme Macdonald highlighted the vast opportunity for growth in North America and the significance of this record investment.

Richard Fox-Marrs, President & CEO of JCB North America, explained that Texas is the largest consumer of construction equipment in the USA and San Antonio was chosen for its central location, supply chain proximity, and labor force.
The Loadall telescopic handler is JCB's biggest selling product in North America and represents a single largest market for aerial access equipment worldwide.
JCB sold its first machine in North America in 1964 and opened its first US manufacturing plant in Savannah, Georgia, in 2001.
The existing Savannah, Georgia plant currently employs 1,000 people.
JCB North America currently employs over 1,000 people and operates out of its headquarters in Savannah, Georgia.
The company employs 19,000 people globally and operates 22 factories worldwide, including 11 in the UK, seven in India, and others in Brazil and China.
JCB North America has regional offices in Dallas, Texas; Santa Monica, California; and Miami, Florida.

JCB manufactures a range of more than 300 products for customers in 150 countries, including backhoe loaders, tracked and wheeled excavators, wheel loaders, compact excavators, skid steer loaders, compact track loaders, Access aerial work platforms, rough terrain forklifts, and Fastrac tractors.
The new San Antonio facility will manufacture machines specifically for customers in North America.
JCB will mark its 80th anniversary in 2025.
source_title: JCB BREAKS GROUND AT NEW $500 MILLION NORTH AMERICAN FACTORY

facts: Textron Inc. announced its intent to separate its Industrial segment from its core aerospace and defense businesses to enhance strategic focus and drive long-term stakeholder value.
Textron intends to explore multiple paths for the separation, including a sale of the Industrial businesses or a tax-free separation into a standalone, publicly traded company.
The planned separation will result in New Textron becoming a pure-play aerospace and defense company aligned with Textron Aviation, Bell, and Textron Systems.
Lisa M. Atherton serves as Chief Executive Officer of Textron.
Scott C. Donnelly serves as Textron’s Executive Chairman.
Following the separation, New Textron expects over $12 billion in 2026 revenues and $19 billion in backlog.

New Textron will be anchored by general aviation under the Cessna and Beechcraft brands, military and commercial rotorcraft under the Bell brand, and Textron Systems’ aerospace and defense products and services.
The Industrial segment has over $3 billion in expected 2026 revenues.
The Industrial segment is composed of Kautex, a leader in plastic fuel systems, battery enclosures, and clear-vision systems for the automotive industry.

The Industrial segment also includes Textron Specialized Vehicles, a manufacturer of specialized vehicles and equipment with globally recognized brands including E-Z-GO, PACE Technologies, Jacobsen, and TUG Technologies.
Textron is targeting completion of the separation within 12 to 18 months, subject to customary conditions including regulatory approvals and final board approval.
Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC is serving as financial advisor to Textron for the transaction.
Latham & Watkins LLP is serving as legal advisor to Textron for the transaction.
source_title: Document

The State Route (SR) 400 Express Lanes Project was named the Proximo North American Transport Deal of the Year 2025, recognizing its innovative financing, collaborative approach, and Public-Private Partnership (P3).
Work along the State Route (SR) 316 corridor on Bundle 1 continues to move forward, with crews making steady progress on roadway, bridge, and interchange improvements throughout the project area.
The State Transportation Board held its monthly meetings on June 17 and 18 at the Georgia DOT headquarters in Atlanta.
Georgia DOT awarded a total of six projects valued at approximately $14.3 million at the monthly State Transportation Board meeting held in May.
Contracts were awarded to the lowest qualified bidders on May 29, 2026.
Georgia DOT has proposed amendments to the Automated School Zone Device rules.
