facts: ICE agents detained 450 illegal migrants at a construction site in Georgia on September 4.
The detained group included hundreds of fly-in Koreans who likely misused B-1/B-2 business tourist visas to take jobs from American construction workers.
More than 30o Koreans were arrested in the sweep, Korean news sites said.
A giant Korean-owned battery factory is being built at the construction site in Ellabell, Georgia.
The battery factory in Ellabell, Georgia, is being built for two Korean companies, Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution Ltd.
More than 1,400 people are working at the site.
South Korea’s government filed a sharp complaint regarding the detention sweep because of the ongoing battery factory construction.

South Korean ministry spokesperson Lee Jae-woong stated that the economic activities of companies investing in the U.S. and the rights and interests of their nationals must not be unfairly violated, and conveyed concern through the U.S. Embassy in Seoul.
Immigration expert Jay Palmer labeled Hyundai a "repeat offender" for using staffing companies to legally shield executives from immigration liability while flying migrants to job sites.
Palmer noted that other companies, including Mercedes, Kia, and Tesla, also use staffing agencies and master service agreements to avoid responsibility for workers' immigration status.
Palmer explained the economic motive behind hiring foreign workers at $5, $6, $7, $8 an hour without insurance instead of American workers at $20, $25, $30 an hour.
The corporate outsourcing strategy clashes with President Donald Trump’s push to hire blue-collar Americans for skilled trades.
Palmer warned that the Georgia raid will cost Hyundai a lot of money via construction delays.
In 2020, Fox News reported on the widespread use of B-1/B-2 visas by Korean workers at another Georgia battery factory for SK Battery America.
David Cagle with Georgia Local Union 72 complained that Georgians were told nothing was available when applying for construction jobs to build the SKI battery plant.
Cagle stated that about 500 people out of work could have performed generic construction tasks like electrical work, ductwork, pipework, pipe fitting, and pipe welding at the plant.
The recent detentions were made by ICE, with support from the FBI, ATF, and the Georgia State Patrol.
In July, South Korea pledged $350 billion in U.S. investment to lower President Donald Trump’s threatened tariffs on its products, which he ended up setting at 15%.
In March, Hyundai said it would invest $21 billion in U.S. onshoring from 2025 to 2028, a number it said last month had increased to $26 billion.
Hyundai’s investment initiatives include a new $5.8 billion steel plant in Louisiana, expanded U.S. auto production capacity, and a state-of-the-art robotics facility.
These Hyundai initiatives are expected to create about 25,000 new direct jobs in the U.S. over the next four years.
Breitbart News has closely covered the illegal use of B-1 visas for foreign labor inflows into American trucking, technology, and construction industries.
Foreign governments quietly back this labor travel activity to extract U.S. dollars in taxes and remittances from workers who enter legally but work illegally.
Trump’s deputies have begun efforts to curb the misuse of B-1 visas, which are intended for tourists and visiting business executives.
source_title: Reports: ICE Detains 300 Korean Migrants at Georgia Construction Site

entity_name: Circular City Forum / Atlanta Sustainability Startups
canonical_name: Circular City Forum on Sustainability & Clean Energy
facts: The Circular City forum, sponsored by Atlanta International School, was held at its new Sandy Springs campus along the Chattahoochee River to spotlight innovators creating green technology models for trade, investment, or educational exchange.

Joulea provides an operating system that helps building managers uncover energy leakages and direct investments to fix them, using drones and AI to crunch usage data and make real-time recommendations.
Ramtin Motahar of Joulea noted that data centers already consume 4 percent of U.S. electricity, with the Department of Energy projecting this proportion to rise to 12 percent by 2028.
Motahar stated that buildings consume about 40 percent of energy across residential and commercial sectors, making them the largest consumption of energy across all industries.
Joulea is currently being entertained for pilots in places like Canada and Europe to help portfolio managers cut consumption and reduce costs.

Emrgy is an 11-year-old Atlanta startup that assembles hydroelectric turbines into modules and deploys them in irrigation canals, securing long-term contracts and power-purchase agreements with utilities.
Emrgy CEO Emily Morris explained that the company makes more economic value by selling power on 30-year contracts than by selling hardware one time.
Emrgy's technology aids in water conservation by modernizing outdated infrastructure, addressing water waste spent on cooling massive server farms for data centers.

Morris noted that half of the water diverted from places like the Chattahoochee to deliver to battery factories or automotive and food/bev sectors is lost along the way through seepage or cracks in 100-year-old water infrastructure.
XiQ co-founder and CEO Karlos Walkes introduced a patented FleetQi device that replaces keyed ignitions in golf carts, enabling zero-emissions vehicle tracking and data collection.
XiQ's carbon-reduction strategy bets that more capable zero-emissions vehicles will encourage consumers to forego gas-guzzling cars or get rid of them altogether.
The company is eyeing a United Arab Emirates distributor to capitalize on rapid development with little zoning restriction in the Middle East.

Michelin vice president Olivier Brauen stated that ninety percent of a tire's environmental impact comes from its time installed on the car, making efficiency improvements more critical than production or recycling efforts.
The France-based tire giant operates a factory in South Carolina and boasts 30,000 employees around the U.S.
Michelin has invested some $400 million in metro Atlanta and instituted net-zero goals while buying a subsidiary to recover key materials from recycled tires.
Brauen noted that disparate environmental regulations across 50 states create considerable balkanization that is a real problem for scaling circularity in the U.S.
SK Battery America operates a Commerce, Ga.-based factory reporting to South Korea's SK On and produces electric vehicle batteries for automakers.

SK Battery America government affairs and communications director Christian Bokich stated the company is recycling scrap metal shavings into its supply chain while shifting battery chemistry away from nickel cadmium toward lithium iron phosphate to reduce strategic exposure and environmental risk.
SK has landed deals that will see its Commerce plant pivot to making battery energy storage systems to offset declining electric vehicle demand.
Bokich emphasized the growing need for designing batteries with recycling in mind and transitioning original materials sourcing to countries that are not controversial.

Emrgy's CEO Morris highlighted that all clean energy subsidies for hydro are preserved through 2033 under the current administration, positioning the company well despite potential future policy swings.
Panelists noted that corporate sustainability efforts are being buoyed by consumer preferences and the demands of a younger workforce bringing environmental idealism into their companies.
source_title: No Quick Fixes for Atlanta Sustainability Startups, Mobility Giants Tackling Systemic Global Problems  - Global Atlanta

facts: Metro Atlanta is charging ahead as a leader in clean energy innovation.
Georgia is rising as a top state for e-mobility, battery tech, sustainable fuels, and solar.
Billions have been invested in green infrastructure in the region.
The Metro Atlanta area boasts over 800 cleantech firms.
Atlanta blends sustainability with opportunity—backed by world-class talent and a pro-business climate that is advancing multiple facets of the cleantech industry.
source_title: Cleantech - Metro Atlanta Chamber

entity_name: LG Energy Solution and SK Innovation
canonical_name: LG Energy Solution and SK Innovation
facts: Two big South Korean electric vehicle battery makers (LG Energy Solution and SK Innovation) settled a long-running trade dispute on Sunday, allowing one company to move ahead with plans to manufacture batteries in Georgia.
President Joe Biden called the settlement "a win for American workers and the American auto industry."

The agreement ended the need for Biden to intervene in a case closely watched for its implications on his clean-energy agenda, which includes a sharp increase in the number of electric vehicles as part of his plan to address climate change.
Biden had until Sunday night to make a decision following a February ruling by a trade commission.
SK will provide LG Energy with a total of $1.8 billion and an undisclosed royalty.
The companies agreed to withdraw all pending trade disputes in the United States and South Korea and not assert new claims for 10 years.

Jun Kim, CEO and president of SK, and Jong Hyun Kim, CEO and president of LG Energy, issued a joint statement saying they decided to settle and compete amicably for the future of the U.S. and South Korean electric vehicle battery industries.

The companies pledged to work together to strengthen the EV battery supply chain in the U.S. and support the Biden administration’s efforts to advance clean energy policies, including electric vehicles.
The U.S. International Trade Commission decided in February that SK stole 22 trade secrets from LG Energy.
The commission ruled that SK should be barred from importing, making or selling batteries in the United States for 10 years.
The decision could have left Ford and Volkswagen scrambling for batteries as they both roll out additional electric vehicle models, a priority for the companies and the Biden administration.
SK has contracts to make batteries for an electric Ford F-150 pickup truck and an electric Volkswagen SUV.
The commission said SK could supply batteries to Ford Motor Co. for four years and to Volkswagen AG for two years.
The decision had jeopardized a $2.6 billion battery factory that SK is building in Commerce, Georgia.

Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff of Georgia, who at Biden’s request had jump-started negotiations between the two companies, said the settlement "has saved the battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, ensuring thousands of jobs, billions in future investment, and that Georgia will be a leader in electric vehicle battery production for years to come."
U.S. Trade Representative Katherine Tai said the deal "builds confidence" in the reliability and responsibility of the two companies as suppliers to the U.S. auto industry.

Tai said the agreement puts the U.S. "in a stronger position to drive innovation and ... clean energy technology while also respecting the rights of technology innovators at the heart of trade and manufacturing policy."
Sen. Raphael Warnock, D-Ga., said the best way to protect workers in Commerce and the jobs Georgians were promised is for the companies involved to negotiate a settlement in good faith.
Warnock said he raised the battery issue with Biden during the president’s March 19 visit to Atlanta.
Biden said building electric vehicles and the batteries needed for them is an important part of his $2.3 trillion infrastructure plan.

Biden stated, "We need a strong, diversified and resilient U.S.-based electric vehicle battery supply chain, so we can supply the growing global demand for these vehicles and components — creating good-paying jobs here at home, and laying the groundwork for the jobs of tomorrow."
Biden added that today's settlement is a positive step in that direction.
Gov. Brian Kemp, R-Ga., called the settlement "fantastic news for northeast Georgia and our state’s growing electric vehicle industry."

Ford said in a statement that the deal "allows us to focus on delivering a range of Ford world-class battery electric vehicles for our retail and fleet customers, while also supporting American workers, the economy and our shared goal of protecting the planet."
source_title: Biden sees 'win’ for US in electric vehicle battery deal | FOX 5 Atlanta

entity_name: SK Innovation Georgia EV Battery Plant
facts: A U.S. International Trade Commission ruling threatens to shut down a giant electric vehicle battery factory set to employ upward of 2,600 workers in the suburbs north of Athens, Georgia.
Georgia’s Republican lawmakers have emerged as the project’s most vocal defenders.
President Biden has just 19 days left to veto trade authorities’ ruling before it takes effect.
The situation is enormously important for jobs in Georgia and the administration’s plans to electrify the nation’s automobiles over the next decade.
Last month, the ITC ruled in favor of South Korean battery giant LG Chem, finding rival SK Innovation guilty in a trade secrets case.
The ITC ruling bans SK Innovation from importing what it needs to run its planned Georgia factory for the next decade.
There has been no sign from the White House that it will block the decision.
SK Innovation has hired consultants to draft a plan to shut down its $2.6 billion plant in Georgia.
The potential shutdown threatens one of the largest economic development projects in Georgia’s history.
Pat Wilson, the GOP commissioner of Georgia’s Department of Economic Development, stated: “There’s nothing partisan about the jobs of the future.”
Georgia’s Republican leaders have been very public in their call for Biden to act to save the Commerce, Georgia, plant.
None of the six Democrats representing Georgia in the House of Representatives responded to requests for comment.
The Georgia Democratic Party did not respond to requests for comment.
Five emails to Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff’s office went unanswered.
Former gubernatorial candidate Stacey Abrams declined an interview request on the topic.

Sen. Raphael Warnock (D-Ga.) declined an interview but provided a link to a March 4 confirmation hearing video where he pressed Polly Trottenberg, Biden’s pick for the No. 2 job at the Department of Transportation, on whether the agency would provide an analysis of the ITC ruling’s effect on U.S. battery supplies.
During the hearing, Warnock said: “It’s not a good position for U.S. automakers if they’re only relying on almost one cell supplier.”

Warnock stated during the hearing (which took place a month after the ruling): “An adverse ruling by the International Trade Commission threatens seriously the future of that project, whether it’ll happen at all,” and noted it would be “one of the largest economic investments in Georgia’s history and a severe punch in the gut, if you will, for folks who are counting on those jobs, not to mention President Biden’s own goals.”
Warnock faces reelection in two years amid mounting pressure from the right in Georgia.

Conservative Atlanta-based pundit Erick Erickson called the ITC’s decision “a bad day for all of us here in Georgia” and compared the potential job loss to construction workers laid off after Biden blocked the Keystone XL oil pipeline.

Erickson wrote: “You would think Biden would want to salvage this because Georgia is a state where Raphael Warnock and Jon Ossoff have gotten elected,” adding that if they lack political clout to reverse the decision, it will “help contribute to the loss of over 2,000 jobs in Georgia” going into 2022.

Carol Browner, former Clinton-era EPA administrator, served as an expert witness for SK Innovation and testified before the ITC that eliminating its Georgia plant put the U.S. electric vehicle supply chain at risk.

Former Obama-era deputy attorney general Sally Yates told The New York Times’ Dealbook that the case could “seriously undermine critical progress on the climate crisis” and risked “killing jobs and setting back the Georgia economy.”

Civil rights leader and former UN ambassador Andrew Young warned in an opinion piece that the ITC ruling “imperils thousands of direct and indirect Georgia jobs and postpones the future of the new era auto industry” over what he called a technicality.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp (R) repeatedly urged Biden to “support thousands of hardworking Georgians and their communities.”

On March 12, Gov. Kemp sent a letter to Biden asking him to “exercise the authority granted to you under law to disapprove the ITC ruling on grounds that it is contrary to the public interest and will seriously jeopardize your administration’s environmental and economic goals.”
Kemp wrote: “Simply put: the livelihoods of thousands of Georgians are now in your hands,” and “This critical opportunity requires us to work together for the good of my state and our country.”
GOP Rep. Buddy Carter hoped Biden was helping broker an agreement between LG Chem and SK Innovation to allow manufacturing plans to proceed.

Carter said: “We don’t want to lose those jobs in Georgia and we don’t want to lose those jobs in the electric vehicle industry,” and expressed hope that the president has staff engaged in trying to broker a deal.
The White House’s U.S. Trade Representative stated it was “currently reviewing the policy implications of the ITC determination.”
Spokesman Adam Hodge said the review will be completed within the 60-day deadline outlined by federal law.
The dispute started in 2019 when LG’s battery subsidiary, LG Energy Solutions, lodged a complaint with the ITC.
The underlying dispute began in 2017 when SK Innovation poached engineers in Seoul, prompting LG to file lawsuits accusing them of violating noncompete contracts.
Around the time SK Innovation announced plans to open its massive Georgia factory to supply Ford Motor Company and Volkswagen with batteries, LG turned to the ITC.
LG filed a separate lawsuit in Delaware federal courts for monetary damages for alleged intellectual property theft, pending the conclusion of the ITC case.
Going to the ITC is considered “the nuclear option” because guilty verdicts typically carry trade bans that restrict imports.
SK Innovation denied stealing trade secrets but on Feb. 10, the ITC found it guilty of destroying documents allegedly detailing what it learned from LG hires.
SK Innovation argued that South Korean laws do not require companies to retain the same documents as U.S. statutes.
The commission ruled unanimously to ban SK Innovation from importing raw materials for the next 10 years, which LG said it would take to develop batteries independently.
The ITC granted a carve-out allowing SK Innovation to build batteries for the next four years for Ford’s electric F-150 pickup truck and two years for Volkswagen’s Crossover series.
The commission determined this timeframe should give automakers enough time to find alternative sources or for SK Innovation to reach a settlement with LG.
U.S. manufacturing capacity for electric vehicle batteries is currently nascent and fragile.
Tesla is the largest battery maker in the U.S., but its Nevada Gigafactory only produces packs for its own vehicles.
LG operates projects across the Midwest that, when completed, will produce roughly 30 gigawatt-hours per year to provide batteries to General Motors and other automakers.
SK Innovation’s Georgia plant would add another 22 gigawatt-hours of annual capacity.
The rest of the battery market is made up of much smaller companies.

LG sent a letter on March 10 to Warnock vowing to “do whatever we can to help the people and workers of Georgia,” hinting it was prepared to make investments in the state to offset the closure of SK Innovation’s factory.
David Callahan, a lawyer for LG, stated the company is preparing to invest $4.5 billion in the U.S., resulting in at least two more factories, one of which could be in Georgia.
SK Innovation stated that LG is “using the ITC to try to muscle SK out of the US EV battery market, period.”
A SK Innovation spokesperson said: “SK has always been open to good-faith settlement talks with LG,” but criticized LG as “inflexible and unreasonable” and noted it “doesn’t want competition.”
James Frith, an analyst at BloombergNEF, warned that allowing LG to dominate the battery market would pose risks to producing EVs at the speed and affordability the Biden administration wants.

Frith stated: “Competition within the battery industry has been one of the key factors that helped to drive down cell and pack prices over the past decade,” and noted it’s not a good position for U.S. automakers to rely on almost one cell supplier that could set prices as it liked.
Other manufacturers that could fill SK Innovation’s void include Japan’s Panasonic Corporation, South Korea’s Samsung, and China’s CATL or BYD.
Callahan denied LG was seeking a monopoly and accused SK Innovation of bluffing about its willingness to shutter the plant to avoid a costly settlement.

Callahan said: “If SK Innovation intends to be in this hugely growing market in the long haul, which they have said they are, it would be suicide to leave Ford and Volkswagen in the lurch,” and called the threat to walk away an “idle threat.”
Callahan added: “They’re threatening Georgia. If I were Georgia, I would be mad. I understand for political reasons not everyone is going to acknowledge that.”
SK Innovation already started hiring hundreds of workers, and it could take years for another company to replace the plant or increase supply to levels needed to electrify the U.S. auto fleet.

Pat Wilson stated: “We need batteries in order to keep up with the demand, this was not only an investment into Georgia but an investment into electric vehicles in the United States and a diversification of the supply chain outside of Asia,” adding, “We saw this as a win-win for the state and the country.”
source_title: The Georgia GOP Is Begging Biden To Act On Battery Plant, While Dems Stay Silent | HuffPost Latest News

facts: SK Battery’s north Georgia plant remains on schedule despite recent setbacks including a pending dispute that could eventually hamper the growth of the massive electric-vehicle battery complex.
The subsidiary of Korea-based SK Innovation has already hired 200 people at the first of two planned plants in Commerce, Ga.
SK has committed to investing a total of $2.6 billion at the site.
SK plans to hire more than 2,600 workers by 2024.
The first plant is 90 percent complete and is set to begin pilot production during the second half of 2021.
The company plans to issue $986 million in “green bonds” to pay for the construction of the second facility.

SK is the subject of a complaint at the U.S. International Trade Commission by Korean rival LG Chem, which alleges that SK’s battery cells and components are made using trade secrets gained unlawfully from former LG Chem employees.
The ITC made an initial determination in LG’s favor last year and was slated to issue a final ruling in October.
The decision was delayed until Feb. 10 without explanation.

A negative ruling could result in SK being banned from importing its batteries, as well as the cells and components used in making them, potentially stalling EV projects at U.S. customers like Fordand Volkswagen.
SK remains confident that regardless of the dispute’s outcome, its plans in the U.S. were on solid footing, noting that an array of suppliers have already begun to set up shop near the new facility.

An SK spokesperson stated that SK Innovation remains confident it will prevail in its ITC case and continues moving forward to make batteries and create jobs for the Georgia economy, with no changes to that commitment.
Immigration issues have dogged the plant over the last year.
Allegations surfaced in 2020 that the company’s subcontractors were bringing in Korean workers with fraudulent employment letters during the construction phase, potentially displacing local workers.
Thirteen Korean citizens working at the plant were taken into custody in September.
Four months prior, U.S. Customs and Border Protection blocked 33 Koreans destined to work at the plant from entry at the Atlanta airport.
SK pledged cooperation with authorities regarding the immigration allegations.
SK said it required all subcontractors on the site to comply with U.S. immigration laws.
More than 1,000 Americans were employed in the building of the first facility.
SK later installed a checkpoint where immigration documents were checked daily upon entry into the site.

Economic development leaders have praised SK, which received a $300.3 million incentives package from the state, as being at the vanguard of a new electric-vehicle industry cluster adding even more heft to its already strong auto industry.
SK Battery says it is also creating employment opportunities upstream, with suppliers Enchem, EcoPro BM, Dongwon Tech and TopTech all setting up shop in the state.
source_title: SK Battery Plant Moving Ahead on Schedule as Ruling Looms in Trade Secrets Case - Global Atlanta

facts: SK On Co., a major South Korean battery manufacturer, has laid off nearly 1,000 workers at its U.S. battery plant in Georgia amid slowing electric vehicle demand.
SK Battery America Inc., SK On's U.S. unit, dismissed exactly 968 employees at its battery manufacturing facility in Commerce, Georgia.
The layoffs were reported via a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice posted on a Georgia state website.
The workforce reduction accounts for approximately 37 percent of the plant's total workforce of around 2,500 employees.
The company stated the reduction is part of restructuring efforts prompted by slowing EV sales and changing market conditions.
The decision was made to adjust operations while maintaining the company's commitment to the state of Georgia and efforts to build a strong U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
The Georgia plant has been supplying EV batteries to automakers including Germany's Volkswagen and South Korea's Hyundai Motor.
The plant also supplied batteries for Ford Motor's F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck, though profitability was affected after Ford canceled production plans for the model.
SK On is currently building a second battery plant in Georgia to supply batteries to Hyundai Motor.
Production at the second Georgia plant is scheduled to begin in the first half of this year (2026).
Another SK On plant in Tennessee is expected to begin production in 2028.
source_title: SK On lays off nearly 1,000 workers at Georgia battery plant amid EV slowdown | Yonhap News Agency

facts: Commerce, Georgia, where the plant is located, is an hour and a half drive from Atlanta and has a population of 7,000.
A road name sign "SK Boulevard" welcomes visitors to the area housing SK Battery America (SKBA).
SKBA's Georgia plant serves as SKON's electric vehicle battery production base.
Georgia's 1st and 2nd plants employ 3,000 workers, which is about half of Commerce City's population.
The plants are locally called "human resources black holes" and operate 24/7 with lights not extinguished.
SKBA operates as a security facility that strictly controls outsiders' access as an intensive force of advanced battery technology.
Workers wear dustproof suits, masks, and gloves to prevent even the slightest dust contamination during operations.
Countless machines and equipment operate nonstop to manufacture key battery materials including anode and cathode materials.
A dedicated machine performs "stacking," a key process of making battery cells.
Real-time production yields and targets are updated in minutes and seconds on large monitors installed for each line.
Monitor screens show yield data at levels where almost no defective products could be found, such as 98 percent and 99 percent.
SKBA's Georgia 1 and 2 plants have 12 lines in full operation.
Nine of the operating lines are dedicated to Hyundai Motor Group.
Hyundai Motor Group's North American electric vehicle production plant Metaplant is in full operation starting this year.
SKBA is supplying most of the electric vehicle batteries required for Metaplant.
Yield is the most important factor to produce high-quality batteries while meeting tight delivery dates.
SKBA's Georgia plant is raising its overall average yield to around 95% through a continuous yield maximization strategy.
Achieving results relied on local personnel hired in the United States rather than domestic personnel with excellent battery technology understanding or production know-how.
Choi Yoon-sang, head of SKBA, stated that recruiting more than 3,000 unskilled local workers in Georgia and training them as skilled workers was extremely difficult.
It was not easy for middle-aged workers in the United States, who have mainly been engaged in the service industry, to transition to manufacturing.
All local residents who applied to SKBA previously worked in concentration industries such as chicken farms or at outlets and restaurants.
SKBA was the first to introduce a two-week "boot camp" training system.
Basic safety education and basic manufacturing training are systematically conducted and repeated before entering the production line.
Many applicants left without enduring the first month due to high initial dropout rates, prompting additional hiring.
SKBA introduced a strikeout system that allows people to leave the company three times for being late to inject Korean-style production DNA.
Adapting American workers to accept working weekends if necessary without being late was a significant cultural challenge.
Securing adequate manpower was critical because failing to complete roles would affect the entire line and make it impossible to increase yield above the target.
Production equipment and process technology made to fit Korean body types have been modified to suit Americans.
The eye level of the machine screen has been raised, and the size of the button to be pressed when working has been replaced much larger.
Non-technical improvements that minimize human errors such as operator manipulation mistakes are equally important for maximizing yield alongside machine upgrades.
Typhoons have hit every year due to abnormal climates, making it difficult for factories to control humidity.
Battery quality can be shaken even if the humidity increases by only 1-2%.
Flexibility to respond appropriately through comprehensive judgment is essential when external variables cannot be eliminated.
Efforts to improve quality led to favorable reviews from local automakers and positive perceptions in the region, resulting in a virtuous cycle structure.
Companies that have looked at SK-on's battery production system are positively considering new contracts by sending confidence in SK-on.
The full operation of the plant could lead to a long-term, not temporary or one-time operation.
Choi Yoon-sang stated that battery production has tripled compared to last year and efforts have paid off.
SKBA is trying to deliver good news and achieve more production efficiency in the second half of the year.
SKBA is working on more than 100 production improvement projects to further boost yields in the second half of this year.
A "language service team" will be organized to solve language problems to facilitate communication between engineers and workers and to harmonize culturally.
The good performance of SK-on North American production base is expected to be an opportunity for SK Group to rebound in its performance.
SK Group aims to seize opportunities for growth as a future food, not a concern, by deriving tangible indicators and numbers in the battery business sector after previously lacking clear results.
source_title: Commerce, an hour and a half drive from Atlanta, Georgia, United States. When you enter a small city.. - MK

facts: SK On, a major South Korean battery manufacturer, has laid off nearly 1,000 workers at its US battery plant in Georgia amid slowing electric vehicle (EV) demand.

SK Battery America, SK On's US unit, dismissed 968 employees at its battery manufacturing facility in Commerce, Georgia, according to a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) notice posted on a Georgia state website.
The layoffs account for about 37 per cent of the plant's total workforce of around 2,500 employees.
The company stated the workforce reduction was part of restructuring efforts prompted by slowing EV sales and changing market conditions.
SK On made the decision to adjust operations while maintaining its commitment to the state of Georgia and efforts to build a strong U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
The Georgia plant has been supplying EV batteries to automakers including Germany's Volkswagen and South Korea's Hyundai Motor.
The facility also supplied batteries for Ford Motor's F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck, though profitability was affected after Ford canceled production plans for the model.
SK On is currently building a second battery plant in Georgia to supply batteries to Hyundai Motor, with production scheduled to begin in the first half of this year.
Another SK On plant in Tennessee is expected to begin production in 2028.
In October last year, Governor Brian Kemp of Georgia met with the head of SK On to discuss ways to enhance cooperation and strengthen business ties.
Kemp and his delegation visited SK On's headquarters in central Seoul earlier in the day and had talks with Lee Seok-hee, chief executive officer (CEO) of the Korean battery manufacturer.
Governor Kemp and Lee Seok-hee previously met in June 2024 during Kemp's visit to Seoul.
SK On operates a 22 gigawatt-hour battery plant in Commerce, Georgia, through its U.S. unit, SK Battery America.
SK On is building another facility jointly with Hyundai Motor Group in Bartow County.
source_title: SK On lays off nearly 1,000 workers at US battery plant amid EV slowdown - www.lokmattimes.com

entity_name: SK Innovation / SK Battery America Georgia EV Battery Plant
facts: SK Innovation's plan to expand its EV battery business in the US hit a snag after a U.S. lawmaker called for an investigation into its affiliate SK Battery America for allegedly illegally employing Korean workers at its Georgia factory construction site.

On Aug. 20, Georgia Congressman Doug Collins sent a letter to U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) requesting an investigation into whether foreign nationals were illegally employed to build the battery factory.
Collins cited information that CBP officers caught 33 Korean nationals at Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport attempting to enter the U.S. without proper work authorizations in May.
Collins stated that CBP determined the May incident was not isolated and was part of a larger scheme to illegally bring foreign workers into the United States.
Collins said he was contacted by a constituent who observed additional Korean nationals illegally working at the Georgia facility on multiple occasions.
According to the constituent, the 33 Korean nationals intercepted in May represent only a small fraction of the workforce systemically and illegally brought to the U.S. to displace American workers.
Collins stated that if true, these actions hurt eager American workers, are illegal, and must be stopped.
SK Innovation, Korea's third-largest EV battery maker, broke ground for the battery factory in Commerce, Jackson County, Georgia, in March 2019 after securing 1.2 square kilometers of land.
The plant is scheduled to begin mass production of EV battery cells in 2022.
SK Battery America promised that the new factory would create about 2,600 jobs once completed.

Georgia Local Union 72, whose members include plumbers, pipefitters, and HVACR technicians, said the company is taking away about 500 jobs from them by providing construction positions to Korean workers.
David Cagle, a member of Georgia Local Union 72, stated that Georgians have gotten the shaft over the SKI battery plant.
Cagle reported that when his union members applied for construction jobs to build the plant, they were told nothing was available.
Cagle said about 500 people are currently out of work who could take these jobs and make a living, but instead Koreans are making a living.
SK Innovation stated it hired Korean workers only to build the factory because they are specialized in constructing battery factories.
SK Innovation confirmed that once construction is completed, the Georgia plant will be operated by American employees.
SK Innovation reiterated that the new Georgia factory will provide about 2,600 new jobs in the Jackson County area.
source_title: SK's plan to expand EV battery business in US hits snag - The Korea Times

facts: The State of Georgia maintains full-time representation in a dozen international markets.

The Georgia Department of Economic Development and its economic development partners capitalized on the 2024 SelectUSA Investment Summit to demonstrate why Georgia stands out to international companies seeking to locate or expand in the U.S.
Georgia has emerged as a leader in the e-mobility sector, with more than $27.3 billion in investments since 2018, creating over 32,200 new jobs.
Major Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) making significant investments include Kia, Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA), Rivian, and Archer Aviation.
Battery manufacturers at the forefront of battery innovation include SK Battery America and LG Energy Solution.
Recyclers and fuel cell manufacturers advancing recycling and hydrogen fuel technologies include Aurubis AG, Ascend Elements, and Plug Power.
Georgia’s traditional automotive sector exports reached $4.1 billion in 2023.
The Georgia Ports Authority’s Brunswick facility is the second-busiest automotive Roll-on/Roll-off port in the U.S.

The state offers an affordable cost of doing business, available sites, and a robust workforce pipeline from institutions like the University System of Georgia and the Technical College System of Georgia.
The Georgia Center of Innovation supports e-mobility, aerospace, and defense sectors with industry-specific expertise and strategic partnerships.
For over a decade, Georgia has established itself as a prime location for global business growth, offering diverse opportunities across key industries.
Georgia ensures a steady, skilled workforce pipeline through its 22 technical colleges across the state.

facts: Companies looking to lower emissions and reduce carbon footprint for themselves and their customers have invested over $11.5 billion in Georgia since 2018.

The state’s competitive advantages, combined with its responsiveness to rapidly evolving business needs, have attracted a range of manufacturers, recyclers, and innovators who are leading the way to a greener, cleaner, more sustainable future.
Ascend Elements, formerly known as Battery Resourcers, invested $43 million to open a commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling plant in Georgia.
The new facility will extract critically scarce materials like lithium, cobalt, and nickel from batteries.
The facility will become the largest of its kind in North America when fully operational later this year (2022).
Roger Lin, Ascend Elements’ Vice President of Marketing and Government Relations, selected Georgia primarily because of the growing electric vehicle and battery ecosystem in the state.
Proximity to partners and customers is essential in the sustainable battery materials industry, according to Lin.
The Southeast is a mecca for the electric vehicle and battery industry, and Georgia provides proximity to factories like SK Battery America.
Locating in Georgia minimizes transportation costs, offers proximity to top talent via nearby universities, and provides access to renewable energy.
Korea’s SK Battery America is investing nearly $2.6 billion in two facilities near Atlanta.
SK Battery America will supply batteries to Ford and Volkswagen as well as other electric vehicle makers.
SK Battery America made its first announcement regarding the Georgia investment in 2018.
Plug Power recently announced a new $84 million, zero-emission hydrogen fuel production facility near I-95 and Georgia’s deepwater ports.
Plug Power provides fuel cell systems for e-mobility that deliver efficiency, fast fueling, and low cost to customers.
Georgia Governor Brian Kemp launched the Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance.

The Electric Mobility and Innovation Alliance is a statewide initiative between government, industries, electric utilities, nonprofits, and other stakeholders focused on growing the electric mobility ecosystem in the state.
The initiative aims to strengthen Georgia’s position in electrification-related manufacturing and innovation.
In 2021, EV manufacturer Rivian announced an investment of $5 billion to build a carbon-conscious facility.
Rivian's facility will make vertically connected electric platforms for trucks, sport/utility vehicles, and delivery vans.
Hyundai Motor Group will open its first fully dedicated EV and battery manufacturing facility in the State of Georgia.
Hyundai announced a $5.54 billion investment with operations slated to begin in the first half of 2025.
Many other Georgia companies are already making parts for EVs.
Companies from Korea, Germany, Turkey, and the Netherlands have announced plans to build EV-related facilities in the state.
Site Selection Magazine ranked Georgia No. 3 for sustainability in the South Atlantic region.
The Atlanta metro area ranked in the Top 10 nationally for sustainability.
Since 2009, Georgia has increased net electricity generation from renewable sources by more than 93%.
Georgia’s electricity mix currently consists of nearly 12% renewables.

GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that renewable energy capacity installation in Georgia has been driven organically by companies and consumers, accelerated through economic attractiveness and removing barriers rather than mandate.

Smart policies have allowed Georgia to promote renewable power development and offer a comprehensive portfolio of clean energy options, giving the state a competitive edge in attracting leading businesses.
Solar PV is Georgia’s fastest-growing source of renewable energy.
NanoPV will manufacture solar panels and equipment in south Georgia’s Sumter County.
Qcells is investing more than $320 million in its solar panel assembly plant in northern Georgia.
Qcells' plant is the largest solar panel assembly plant in the Western hemisphere.
Recent sustainability-focused announcements in Georgia include German copper recycler Aurubis.
Electronic waste recycler Igneo has announced plans for a facility in Georgia.
Danimer Scientific, a manufacturer and developer of biodegradable materials, announced a presence in Georgia.
Recycled furniture maker SoPoly announced plans for a facility in Georgia.
In Atlanta, EF Block turns everyday recycled Styrofoam into earth-friendly, stronger, sustainable walls that replace traditional building materials.
EF Block launched a city-wide Styrofoam recycling initiative with support from GDEcD’s Center of Innovation and the City of Atlanta.
NANTRenewables is opening a plant on Georgia’s coast to manufacture biodegradable products like plates, straws, cutlery, trays, and cups.
NANTRenewables provides sustainable products to companies within the food, agriculture, and pharmaceutical industries.

Harold Meherg, a NANT Renewables senior executive, selected SeaPoint Industrial Terminal Complex in Savannah for shared sustainability values and development-ready infrastructure including roads, rail access, sea access, and nearby utility connections.
Companies leading mobility research & development, battery manufacturing and recycling, and electric vehicle production all call Georgia home.
source_title: Georgia Emerges as Hub for Sustainability - Savannah CEO

facts: Stryten Energy is a U.S. energy storage solutions manufacturer based in Alpharetta, Georgia.
The company produces nearly 15 gigawatt-hours of energy storage annually through domestic manufacturing facilities.
It develops solutions for transportation, essential power, and motive power applications.
Stryten Energy is committed to environmental sustainability, achieving a 99% recycling rate for lead batteries.
The company uses up to 90% recycled materials in its production.
Its product portfolio spans advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium battery technologies for industries from automotive to military.

facts: The Georgia Institute of Technology and Stryten Energy, a Georgia-headquartered energy storage solutions provider, have announced the successful installation of Stryten Energy’s Lead Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) at the Carbon Neutral Energy Solutions Laboratory (CNES).
The installation aims to create a living-learning lab on campus that supports research and real-world applications of medium-duration energy storage solutions.
Lead BESS was selected for this initial installation due to its cost-effectiveness, high discharge rates, and recyclability.
Extensive research demonstrates the reliable performance of lead BESS, according to a media release.

The CNES building is located in the North Avenue Research Area of the Georgia Tech campus and houses the Strategic Energy Institute (SEI), an interdisciplinary research institute focused on energy research, along with multiple research groups dedicated to renewable energy and energy infrastructure-related topics.
The BESS integrates renewable energy sources into the existing power mix, providing stable and dependable backup power and reducing grid dependency during peak hours.

With additional components and software, the system is capable of bi-directional charging, allowing current to flow into the battery for charging and out of the battery to power the grid or microgrid.

Christine Conwell, SEI’s interim executive director, stated that Georgia Tech’s strategic plan envisions the campus as a dynamic laboratory and experimental test bed where sustainable practices are seamlessly integrated into operations.

Through enduring partnerships with organizations like Stryten, Georgia Tech is creating mini ecosystems that yield valuable situational data to help chart a path for innovative energy research well beyond the campus.

Scott Childers, vice president of essential power at Stryten Energy, noted that while large utility-scale implementations were clearly the focus when solar and other renewables hit the market years ago, behind-the-meter applications are now getting their day in the sun.
Stryten Energy is particularly excited about deploying this unit in commercial and industrial microgrids and pairing it with EV charging stations to help the U.S. achieve its energy goals.
Stryten Energy aims to demonstrate the advantages of lead BESS from cost savings, technology, environmental, and safety perspectives.
source_title: Georgia Tech, Stryten install lead BESS for advanced research

facts: Georgia Institute of Technology collaborated with Stryten Energy LLC to successfully complete the installation of a Lead Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) at the university’s Carbon Neutral Energy Solutions Laboratory (CNES).

The CNES facility is located within the North Avenue Research Area of the Georgia Tech campus and houses the Strategic Energy Institute (SEI) along with research groups focused on renewable energy and energy infrastructure.

The installation serves as a foundational step toward establishing a living-learning laboratory on campus to support academic research and real-world exploration of medium-duration energy storage technologies.
The decision to use a lead battery system was driven by its affordability, high power discharge capability, proven recyclability, and extensive performance data.

Christine Conwell, SEI’s interim executive director, stated that Georgia Tech’s strategic plan envisions the campus as a dynamic laboratory and experimental test bed where sustainable practices are integrated into operations, creating mini ecosystems with partners like Stryten to yield situational data for innovative energy research.

Scott Childers, vice president of essential power at Stryten Energy, noted that while large utility-scale implementations were previously the focus for solar and renewables, this BESS highlights behind-the-meter applications getting their moment.
Childers stated excitement about deploying the unit in commercial and industrial microgrids and paired with EV charging stations to help the U.S. achieve its energy goals.
Childers praised Georgia Tech as a tremendous partner and highlighted the advantages of lead BESS regarding cost savings, technology, environmental impact, and safety perspectives.
The newly installed BESS integrates renewable energy sources with the CNES lab's existing power infrastructure to offer reliable backup power and reduce grid dependence during high-demand periods.

The system is equipped with smart software and hardware that support bi-directional energy flow, allowing it to store energy during off-peak periods and discharge it back to the grid or a microgrid when needed.

Richard Simmons, Director of Research and Studies at the Strategic Energy Institute, emphasized the Stryten system as a key component in Georgia Tech’s broader Distributed Energy Resources (DER) research efforts.
The lab can now manage the battery’s charge and discharge cycles in conjunction with an existing solar photovoltaic array and a recently added electric vehicle (EV) charging test bed.
This setup enables researchers to explore methods for time-shifting solar energy to meet late afternoon building demands or store power for overnight vehicle charging.

Simmons expressed hope that the lead BESS will serve as one of several living lab battery pilots at Georgia Tech, alongside regional partners exploring R&D and testing projects involving flow batteries for longer-duration storage.
Researchers are also exploring lithium-ion BESS that may integrate second-life EV battery modules to improve grid resilience and drive advancements in sustainable energy research.
The use of DERs like this BESS is a growing area of study as institutions and industries seek to enhance grid reliability, manage energy costs, and support the clean energy transition.
This installation marks an important step in advancing Georgia Tech’s mission to contribute practical solutions and research to the evolving field of sustainable energy.
source_title: Georgia Tech And Stryten Energy Install Lead Battery Energy Storage System For Advanced Research - SolarQuarter

facts: Stryten Energy LLC is a U.S.-based energy storage solutions provider headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia.
Stryten Energy was awarded part of a five-year contract by the Department of Defense for submarine valve regulated lead acid (SVRLA) batteries.
Mike Judd serves as Chief Executive Officer and President of Stryten Energy.
Mike Judd stated that Stryten is pleased to continue supporting the U.S. Navy mission and appreciates the opportunity to supply domestically manufactured battery solutions and components.
Stryten’s E-Series SVRLA AGM battery technology is cost-effective, virtually maintenance-free, and meets the U.S. Navy’s requirements.
Stryten Energy will deliver the batteries and components to the U.S. Navy starting in the fall of 2023 through the summer of 2028.
Stryten Energy provides energy storage solutions across the Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military, and Government sectors.
Stryten’s stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium, and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers, and energy performance management software.
Stryten Energy powers applications ranging from submarines and subcompacts to microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains, and trucks.
source_title: View Press Releases | Battery Power Online

facts: Effective Friday, January 31, 2025, Largo Clean Energy Corp. and Stryten Critical E-Storage, LLC finalized the formation of Storion Energy LLC.

Storion is a joint venture focused on removing the barriers to entry for battery manufacturers to domestically sourced, price-competitive components for the flow battery industry, including vanadium electrolyte.

The partnership leverages access to Largo’s high-quality vanadium supply, Largo Physical Vanadium Corp.’s innovative electrolyte leasing model and Stryten’s U.S.-based manufacturing expertise to enhance grid stability and energy security while enabling the broader adoption of vanadium flow battery solutions.

Francesco D’Alessio, President of LCE, stated that the closing of this transaction represents a transformational step for Largo as they advance their investment strategy of integrating into the fast-growing vanadium flow battery market.

Storion Energy now provides a direct channel for Largo’s high-quality vanadium products into the long duration energy storage sector, reinforcing their position as a leading and reliable supplier of vanadium.

Through Storion, they are establishing a fully integrated vanadium electrolyte supply chain in North America, addressing a key challenge for vanadium flow battery adoption while unlocking new growth opportunities for Largo.
Stryten has paid US$1 million in cash directly to Largo and will contribute US$6 million over time to fund Storion’s operations.
LCE and Stryten each hold a 50% equity interest in Storion, with customary pre-emption rights and anti-dilution protections.
Storion’s board composition will be generally proportional to ownership, with Stryten holding one additional seat.
Largo has amended its Safekeeping and Supply Agreements with LPV and assigned its Safekeeping Agreement to Storion.
Largo and Storion have entered into a separate supply agreement, granting Storion a right of first offer for vanadium products.
Required third party consents, which were closing conditions, were received.

Storion Energy intends to bring energy resilience and security to the U.S. by removing the barrier to entry for battery manufacturers to domestically sourced, price-competitive electrolyte used in vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFB) for long-duration energy storage (LDES).

Storion is a joint venture between a Stryten Energy affiliate and Largo Clean Energy Corp., a subsidiary of Largo Inc., one of the world’s largest and highest quality vanadium suppliers, that will support scalable domestic electrolyte production to establish a fully integrated vertical supply chain for utility-scale VRFB LDES solutions.
VRFB technology is a safe and reliable option for Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) that need to provide energy storage of four or more hours.
Storion has locations in Alpharetta, Georgia and Wilmington, Massachusetts.

Stryten Energy helps solve the world’s most pressing energy challenges with a broad range of energy storage solutions across the Essential Power, Motive Power, Transportation, Military and Government sectors.
Headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, Stryten Energy partners with some of the world’s most recognized companies to meet the growing demand for reliable and sustainable energy storage capacity.
Stryten Energy powers everything from submarines to subcompacts, microgrids, warehouses, distribution centers, cars, trains and trucks.

Its stored energy technologies include advanced lead, lithium and vanadium redox flow batteries, intelligent chargers and energy performance management software that keep people on the move and supply chains running.

Largo Physical Vanadium (LPV) aims to provide a secure, convenient and exchange-traded investment alternative for investors interested in having direct exposure to physical vanadium, a metal essential to achieving a greener world in key industries such as steel, aerospace and energy storage.
Vanadium is non-degrading and fully recyclable when used as electrolyte in vanadium flow batteries and offers carbon reducing attributes when used in steel alloying applications.

LPV's strategy is not only to achieve appreciation through the acquisition of vanadium, but to own and actively supply vanadium to end users of vanadium flow batteries to advance to integration of renewable energy in long duration storage.
Largo is a globally recognized supplier of high-quality vanadium and ilmenite products, sourced from its world-class Maracás Menchen Mine in Brazil.
As one of the world’s largest primary vanadium producers, Largo produces critical materials that empower global industries including steel, aerospace, defense, chemical, and energy storage sectors.

Largo is also strategically invested in the clean energy storage sector through its 50% ownership of Storion Energy, a joint venture with Stryten Energy focused on scalable domestic electrolyte production for utility-scale vanadium flow battery long-duration energy storage solutions in the U.S.
source_title: Largo and Stryten Energy Finalize Formation of Storion Energy to Strengthen U.S. Energy Resilience and Address Long Duration Energy Storage Needs - Junior Mining Network

facts: Stryten Energy LLC is a US-based energy storage solutions provider.
Installed its advanced vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) at Snapping Shoals EMC.
Snapping Shoals EMC is a utility provider for some of the fastest-growing areas in the nation.
Partnership with Snapping Shoals will focus on demonstrating the VRFB’s energy storage capabilities.
Partnership will evaluate additional use cases for long-duration energy storage technology.
Project represents the first VRFB energy storage system manufactured and installed in the state of Georgia.
Shaun Mock serves as President and CEO of Snapping Shoals EMC.
Snapping Shoals EMC serves over 100,000 residential and business customers.
Mike Judd serves as CEO and President of Stryten Energy.
VRFB offers industry-leading power density with a versatile, modular platform independently scalable in power and capacity.
VRFB features an unlimited cycle life with proper maintenance.
VRFB system can operate for more than 20 years without the electrolyte losing energy storage capacity.
VRFB is suited for medium to long-duration energy storage applications requiring six or more hours.
VRFB helps ensure grid stability and facilitates increased utilization of renewables for businesses and consumers across the US.
Demonstration project aims to prove VRFB capabilities in utility use cases including energy cost control, peak shaving, and avoiding curtailment.
source_title: Stryten Energy and Snapping Shoals EMC install Georgia’s first vanadium redox flow battery system | Energy Global

facts: Stryten Energy LLC offers a comprehensive portfolio of motive power solutions, including batteries, application management and chargers for industrial operations and material handling fleets.
On April 14, 2026, in Atlanta, Ga., Stryten Energy announced expanded capabilities of its M-Series X-7 charger, highlighting major updates that expand compatibility, power and flexibility.
On April 13, 2026, in Atlanta, Ga., Stryten Energy launched the U.S.-manufactured M-Series Li610 lithium-ion battery, which features a longer battery cycle life for material handling operations.
On April 9, 2026, in Atlanta, Ga., Stryten Energy introduced a new Material Handling Battery Finder tool to quickly match batteries to trucks, showcasing it at MODEX 2026 in Booth #B9325.

On March 26, 2026, in Atlanta, Ga., Stryten Energy announced it will showcase latest advancements to its inCOMMAND™ Energy Performance Management Platform at MODEX 2026, featuring enhanced customization, faster performance and improved mobile usability.
On August 25, 2025, in Alpharetta, Ga., Stryten Energy unveiled major performance and usability enhancements to its inCOMMAND™ IoT Platform to enable more efficient fleet management.

Stryten Energy will provide premium lead battery and charger solutions to KION’s North America dealer network, available through KION North America’s extensive network of 200 sales and service locations for nationwide access and support.
On June 17, 2025, in Alpharetta, Ga., Stryten Energy announced that its M-Series Li600 material handling batteries are now certified and available from Hyster-Yale.

Lithium batteries developed in the 1970s and commercialized in the 1990s are characterized by higher energy density, higher energy efficiency, a longer cycle life, and a longer calendar life compared to earlier technologies.

On December 19, 2024, in Alpharetta, Georgia, Stryten Energy debuted a trailblazing mobile microgrid solution at CES 2025, featuring a cutting-edge hybrid diesel-electric vehicle that demonstrates a resilient energy ecosystem managing energy sources, storage and usage.
Stryten Energy received two top marketing awards with creative partner Stakeholder Marketing and Media, including a Platinum Award for its “Legends of the Loading Dock” video series in 2024.

On March 27, 2024, in Alpharetta, Ga., Stryten Energy received Mitsubishi Logisnext Americas “Vendor of the Year” Award, with representatives Matt Gould and Ian Dickie (Stryten Energy) and Peyton Maziarz (Stryten Energy) attending alongside John Lamancusa, Craig Sundstrom, and David Unger (Logisnext).
Stryten Energy launched a new line of lithium batteries at MODEX 2024 in Atlanta, introducing the M-Series Li600, a new line of Class I, II and III lithium batteries.
Motive power batteries represent about a third of the total cost of a forklift, making proper maintenance critical for fleet operations.
Stryten Energy provides 24/7 Motive Power Services to help minimize downtime and maximize motive power fleet performance.

The material handling industry is undergoing one of its fastest technology transitions in decades as fleets modernize, reshaping how operations think about power, infrastructure and long-term strategy.
Stryten Energy's Dan Autey explores four forklift battery trends to watch in 2024.
Vertical integration allows domestic battery manufacturing partners to control every stage of production, ensuring quality and consistency.

Georgia Tech’s leading researchers work across the energy value chain in basic and applied science in EVs, photovoltaics, hydrogen, carbon capture, industrial decarbonization, grid security and resilience, and related social sciences.
U.S. News & World Report ranked Georgia Tech as the No. 1 public university and No. 3 overall in energy and fuels research.

Stryten Energy is backed by more than a century of expertise in delivering top-performing energy solutions.

facts: Lithium-ion battery recycler and raw materials provider SungEel Recycling Park Georgia will locate its first US recycling facility in Georgia.
A subsidiary of the Korea-based global industry leader SungEel HiTech Co., Ltd., the company will invest more than $37 million in the new facility and create 104 jobs in Stephens County.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that Georgia announced record-breaking numbers for fiscal year 2022.
Governor Brian Kemp noted that companies like SungEel are evidence that the rapidly growing electric mobility ecosystem continues to generate new jobs for hardworking Georgians across the state.
Governor Brian Kemp highlighted that Korea has been a key partner in Georgia’s growing sustainable technology industry.
Governor Brian Kemp expressed excitement that SungEel chose the Peach State for their first U.S. facility.
With more than 10 years of patented technology, SungEel specializes in complete recycling technology using a 100 percent full circulatory system.
The company's system recycles end-of-life batteries and battery manufacturing scrap.
The recycling process has a metal recovery rate of more than 95 percent.
Recovered metals from the process include nickel, cobalt, and lithium.

Suk Jae Yim, Representative of SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, stated that SungEel HiTech’s entry into Georgia is the last piece of the puzzle to build a sustainable ecosystem of Georgia’s electric vehicle supply chain.

Suk Jae Yim stated that SungEel Recycling Park Georgia will conduct its full responsibility to build a U.S. eco-friendly industrial ecosystem in line with the expectations of the State of Georgia and Stephens County.
SungEel Recycling Park Georgia’s new facility will be located at the Hayestone Brady Business Park, a Georgia Ready for Accelerated Development (GRAD) Certified site, in Toccoa.
The GRAD Program is a proactive effort by Georgia’s economic development community to develop a portfolio of available sites ready for fast-track development.
Operations are expected to begin in early 2024.
Over the next few years, the company will be hiring for technicians, operators, administrative, and sales roles.
Brian Akin, Chairman of the Stephens County Development Authority, stated it has been a pleasure working with SungEel HiTech over the past eight months.
Brian Akin noted that investments in infrastructure, workforce development, and education were significant factors in the selection process.
Brian Akin expressed excitement for Toccoa-Stephens County and called the project a solid boost for the local job market.

Director of Korean Investment Yoonie Kim represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) on this competitive project in partnership with the Stephens County Development Authority and Georgia EMC.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that SungEel is a company at the cutting edge of sustainable technology.
Pat Wilson stated that SungEel’s proximity to other members of Georgia’s electric mobility ecosystem will lead to amazing success.
Pat Wilson emphasized creating jobs of the future and protecting opportunities by preparing key industries like the auto industry for the next technological revolution.
Pat Wilson expressed gratitude to partners in Stephens County for their investment in speed to market solutions like site preparation.

Georgia’s prime location, extensive infrastructure, skilled workforce, and business-friendly climate has made it an attractive location for a diverse array of rapidly developing industries focused on creating a sustainable future.
Georgia is cultivating a vertically integrated supply chain that will help companies increase efficiencies by reducing the reliance on imported materials.
EV-related projects have contributed more than $13 billion in investments and more than 18,100 new jobs to Georgia since 2020.

SungEel HiTech Co., Ltd. is headquartered in Korea and has grown into a global leader in lithium ion battery recycling and raw material provider under the vision of Finite Resources to Infinite Resources.
SungEel HiTech currently operates facilities in South Korea, China, Malaysia, India, and Hungary.
New facilities are underway in Poland and Germany.

The company’s objectives are to further improve the environment and to create value to customers, stockholders, and communities through innovation in recycling technology, material processing, and products.

facts: SungEel Recycling Park Georgia, LLC is a Korean-based lithium battery recycler that has purchased property in the Hayestone Brady Industrial Park to locate a new recycling facility.
The company will pay ad-valorem taxes on the property this year, marking the first time the county has collected taxes on the property for many years.
Stephens County Development Authority (SCDA) does not pay taxes on property it owns, but SungEel paid full price for its property and will pay taxes this year.
The company will receive a tax abatement in the coming years as part of an incentive package completed and recorded with the SCDA.

SCDA President and CEO Brittany Ivey explained that local incentives are necessary for the state to offer incentives, and without them, Stephens County would not have been in the running for the development.

The tax abatement follows a declining schedule: 0 percent in year one of operation, 20 percent in year two, adding another 20 percent each year until year six, when the company will pay 100 percent of taxes due.
The tax abatement incentive is legally tied through the MOU to financial investment levels and employment figures.
The city has agreed to waive tap fees for city utilities in exchange for the company obtaining utilities through the city where possible.
An MOU between SungEel and the State of Georgia was signed on April 17 of this year.
The state MOU stipulates a REBA Grant in the amount of $700,000 will be awarded to SungEel through the Stephens County Development Authority.
REBA Grant funding comes from the Georgia Department of Community Affairs and is used to help close deals when companies consider Georgia versus other locations or countries.
REBA funds may finance fixed-asset needs including infrastructure, real estate acquisition, construction, or machinery and equipment.
WNEG News will publish a multi-part series on facility safety questions posed to SungEel officials earlier in the month and their responses.
source_title: SCDA releases MOU with SungEel outlining incentives - WNEG

facts: TCI Powder Coatings is located in Ellaville, Georgia.

The strategic alliance between TCI Powder Coatings and BASF was announced on June 17, 2016.
The agreement allows TCI to sell the BASF CathoGuard® electrocoat technology to specified market segments.
The electrocoat technology is suitable for use on parts that are difficult to paint efficiently, including those with multiple angles and crevices.
The technology offers coverage and corrosion edge protection.
The company states the technology lowers total material costs by nearly 20 percent.
TCI market segments with access to this technology through the new alliance include off-road and heavy equipment, general finishing, office furniture, and appliance.
The electrocoat technology will also be available to custom coaters that supply major subassemblies and modular components for the automotive market.

facts: TCI Powder Coatings is a subsidiary of RPM International Inc. (NYSE: RPM).
Thomas G. Whalen was appointed as Director of Marketing and Segment Strategy at TCI Powder Coatings.
The appointment was announced from Ellaville, GA on August 21, 2015.
Whalen brings over 25 years of experience in the industrial coatings market to his new role.
For the two years prior to this appointment, Whalen served as Global Business Director, Consumer Electronics, and Performance Glass Coatings at PPG Industries.
Before joining PPG, Whalen was Vice President of Liquid and Mirror Coatings Units at Spraylat, which was acquired by PPG.
Prior to Spraylat, Whalen worked at Valspar with increasing management experience, including serving as Global Segment Director.
Whalen began his coatings career in 1986 at Moline Paint in sales and later held senior positions including International Business Director.
Jack Bostock, TCI Vice President Sales and Marketing, stated the company is thrilled to have Whalen join their team.
Whalen will help lead TCI in organizing around global segmentation and identifying strategic segments to differentiate TCI from other powder coating companies.
