facts: RPM stands for Reaching Potential Through Manufacturing and is a public-private partnership between Textron Specialized Vehicles and the Richmond County School System based in Augusta, Ga.
The program combines an instructional day with a $10 an hour four-hour shift in manufacturing at a facility that is half schoolhouse and half manufacturing plant.
The facility includes classrooms and educational space to allow students to make progress toward their high school degree while earning valuable paid work experience.
Students produce components and subassemblies for products manufactured by Textron, which is Richmond County’s largest industrial employer with a workforce exceeding 1,700 in Augusta.
The company manufactures E-Z-GO golf cars and Cushman utility vehicles at its Augusta facilities.

Students apply for admission to RPM and are selected for participation by Richmond County School System officials based on factors including lack of progress toward a high school diploma, absenteeism, and personal situations such as poverty, homelessness, or being teen parents.
More than 300 students have graduated from the RPM program.
More than 100 graduates have gone on to full-time employment at Textron Specialized Vehicles.
About one-third of RPM graduates go on to work for Textron.
Derek Wynns, director of GSE operations for Textron Specialized Vehicles, oversaw the RPM program until a recent internal job change within the company.
Textron and the Richmond County School System announced the creation of the RPM program in January 2016.
Jason Moore was hired as the RPM principal and given seven months to get the program up and running before school started in August.

The RPM model was inspired by Southwire’s "12 For Life" program, which Southwire (a leading manufacturer of wire and cable based in Carrollton, Ga.) started in 2007 in collaboration with Carroll County schools.
Southwire's initiative helped raise the total district graduation rate in Carroll County to over 91% and now also incorporates schools in Florence, Ala.
RPM debuted in the summer of 2016 with teachers in place and an initial cohort of 75 students who started running machines on day two.
Parts built by RPM students are incorporated into E-Z-GO and Cushman vehicles that are used all over the world.
Adrianne Bogans, Ph.D., serves as the RPM assistant principal.

Janiya McKinnie dropped out of T.W. Josey High School in Augusta during her junior year due to the COVID-19 pandemic but was enrolled in RPM by her mother and later helped turn tassels for the class that pushed RPM above 300 graduates.
Phillip Bowman, director of assembly operations at Textron Specialized Vehicles, oversees assembly operations and highlights the program's impact on student growth and accountability.
source_title: Driven to Excel: Textron’s high school outreach initiative - GCMOnline.com

facts: Jason Alford, vice president of Integrated Supply Chain for Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc. in Augusta, is one of four Georgians to receive the 2017 Faces of Manufacturing Award.
The Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership (GaMEP) named Jason Alford a 2017 Faces of Manufacturing Award recipient.
Alford has been with Textron for 14 years and accepted his award on Oct. 10 at a special ceremony at the company’s Augusta facility.
Jason Alford stated he credited hundreds of talented, dedicated TSV employees who build vehicles and equipment to serve customers around the world.

GaMEP is a federally funded economic development program of the Georgia Institute of Technology’s Enterprise Innovation Institute that works with manufacturers across the state to help them remain viable and economically competitive.
More than 10,000 manufacturing companies operate in Georgia.
These companies employ more than 365,000 people and produce a total manufacturing output of $53 billion per year.
The Faces campaign showcases the sector’s importance to Georgia’s economy.

Alford and the other three award recipients were chosen through a public voting contest consisting of 10 finalists selected from an initial pool of nominations, with more than 4,500 votes cast resulting in Alford emerging as one of the top four.

Kevin Holleran, president and CEO of Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., stated that Jason’s keen intellect, analytical mindset and tireless work ethic are matched only by his devotion to the company and employees.
Textron Specialized Vehicles is a Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT) company that designs and manufactures vehicles and equipment under several well-known brands.

TSV products include E-Z-GO golf cars, Arctic Cat snowmobiles, Cushman commercial utility vehicles, Textron Off Road side-by-sides and ATVs, Jacobsen professional turf-care equipment, and Dixie Chopper zero-turn mowers.
The company manufactures ground support equipment for the aviation industry under its Textron GSE umbrella.
TSV employs about 1,400 people in Georgia and more than 4,200 worldwide.
Alford began at Textron as director of financial planning and analysis before discovering a natural talent for manufacturing integration.
Alford spearheaded Textron Specialized Vehicles’ partnership with the Richmond County School System to create the Reaching Potential Through Manufacturing (RPM) program.

The RPM program, now in its second year, provides high school students at risk of dropping out with an alternative path to graduation while earning manufacturing work experience building components for TSV products.
Alford drew on his own lessons learned from persevering during recovery from a motorcycle accident that left him wheelchair-bound as inspiration for the RPM program.

GaMEP Director Karen J. Fite stated that Jason’s commitment and success at Textron, along with his dedication to inspiring the industry’s next generation workforce, reflect the caliber of people in Georgia manufacturing.
The Faces of Manufacturing in Georgia campaign is an initiative of the Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership that honors people who work in or are affected by manufacturing in Georgia.
GaMEP has been serving Georgia manufacturers since 1960 and offers a solution-based approach through coaching and education designed to increase top line growth and reduce bottom line cost.
Textron Specialized Vehicles markets products under the E-Z-GO®, Cushman®, Arctic Cat®, Textron Off Road™, Jacobsen®, Dixie Chopper®, Ransomes®, TUG™, Douglas™, Premier™ and Safeaero™ brands.
TSV vehicles are found in environments ranging from golf courses to factories, airports to planned communities, and theme parks to hunting preserves.
Textron Inc. is a multi-industry company that leverages its global network of aircraft, defense, industrial and finance businesses to provide customers with innovative solutions and services.
source_title: Textron Specialized Vehicles executive receives Georgia Manufacturing Extension Partnership’s 2017 Faces of Manufacturing Award - Enterprise Innovation Institute

facts: Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., a Textron Inc. company, announces the launch of the Cushman Hauler XL utility vehicle.
The Cushman Hauler XL features a 68.5-inch-long aluminum flat bed and a 1,600-lb payload capacity.
The vehicle provides truck-inspired design, functional dash, purpose-built accessories, and an automatic parking brake.

Adam Harris, Vice President and General Manager of Cushman and E-Z-GO at Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., states the Hauler XL is an industry leader in bed size, vehicle rating, and bed load capacity.
The Hauler XL comes in two powertrain options: a fully electric ELiTE series model powered by Samsung SDI lithium-battery technology, or a gas-powered model with a 13.5-hp gas engine.
The ELiTE series is the result of years of collaboration between Textron Specialized Vehicles and Samsung SDI to create a zero-maintenance battery for golf cars and utility vehicles.
The Hauler XL ELiTE provides customers with reduced maintenance, a five-year battery warranty, an automatic parking brake, and street-legal capability in most jurisdictions.
The street-legal Hauler XL ELiTE is designed to drive on roads with up to 35 mph speed limits at up to 25 mph by meeting the federally required FMVSS 500 and voluntary SAE J2358 standards.
Cushman vehicles are designed and manufactured in Augusta, Ga., by the Textron Specialized Vehicles business of Textron Inc.

Since 1901, the diverse Cushman product line-up has included utility, personnel transport, burden carriers, tow tractors, food and beverage, and turf maintenance vehicles for commercial, industrial, and golf applications.
source_title: Textron Inc.  - Introducing the Cushman Hauler XL Long-Bed Utility Vehicle

facts: Textron Specialized Vehicles plans to recruit more than 75 people to work in Augusta, Georgia.

The recruitment event will be held on Thursday, Feb. 9, from 10 a.m. to 6 p.m.
The recruitment location is the GDOL Augusta Career Center located at 601 Greene St. in Augusta.

Textron Specialized Vehicles will recruit and conduct on-site interviews for assemblers, painter-production workers, powder painter technicians, press brake operators, sequence schedulers, team leaders, warehousing material handlers, welders, along with computer numerical control fabricators or laser operators.
All applicants must be at least 18 years old to qualify for the positions.
Experience requirements depend on the position.
source_title: Ga. Dept. of Labor to hold recruitment for Textron Specialized Vehicles in Augusta

facts: Textron Inc. announced its intent to separate its Industrial segment from its core aerospace and defense businesses to enhance strategic focus and drive long-term stakeholder value.
The separation will result in New Textron becoming a pure-play aerospace and defense company aligned with Textron Aviation, Bell, and Textron Systems.
Textron intends to explore multiple paths for the separation, including a sale of the Industrial businesses or a tax-free separation into a standalone publicly traded company.
Lisa M. Atherton serves as Chief Executive Officer of Textron.
The planned separation creates greater clarity and focus for both New Textron and Industrial, which operate in distinct markets with unique business opportunities and investment requirements.

As separate companies, each is expected to benefit from enhanced agility and focus, tailored capital allocation strategies, increased strategic flexibility, compelling investment profiles, and distinct management teams.
Scott C. Donnelly serves as Textron's Executive Chairman.
The Board of Directors concluded that pursuing the separation is the right approach to sharpen strategic focus and support long-term value creation for shareholders.
New Textron expects over $12 billion in expected 2026 revenues and $19 billion in backlog.
New Textron will be a premier pure-play aerospace & defense company with world-class engineering capabilities, a long track record of innovation, and proven manufacturing and support operations.

New Textron's core franchises include general aviation under the Cessna and Beechcraft brands, military and commercial rotorcraft under the Bell brand, and Textron Systems' differentiated suite of aerospace and defense products and services.

New Textron expects a successful separation to increase its revenue growth profile and operating margins while maintaining a strong balance sheet and consistent capital allocation priorities, including investments in research and development and capital expenditures.
Industrial is expected to have over $3 billion in expected 2026 revenues.
Industrial is composed of Kautex and Textron Specialized Vehicles.
Kautex is a leader in plastic fuel systems, battery enclosures, and clear-vision systems for the automotive industry.
Textron Specialized Vehicles is a manufacturer of specialized vehicles and equipment with globally recognized brands such as E-Z-GO, PACE Technologies, Jacobsen, and TUG Technologies.
Atherton stated confidence that the separation will enable Industrial to build on its strong foundation and deliver enhanced value for employees, customers, and shareholders.
The Company is targeting completion of the separation within 12 to 18 months, subject to customary conditions including regulatory approvals and final Board approval.
There can be no assurance regarding the ultimate timing or structure of the proposed separation or that a transaction will be completed.

Textron will continue to operate its Industrial businesses in alignment with its current strategy during the separation pursuit, including continued investments in growth, margin improvement, and innovation.
Textron announced its first-quarter results in a separate press release issued today.
Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC is serving as financial advisor to Textron.
Latham & Watkins LLP is serving as legal advisor to Textron.
The press release contains forward-looking statements regarding the separation, expected revenues, timing, benefits, and performance, which involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties.

Factors that could cause actual results to differ materially include board approval, regulatory approvals (including IRS rulings), closing conditions, execution risks, costs or disruptions from the separation, and potential delays in receiving consents or approvals.
source_title: Textron Announces Intent to Separate its Industrial Segment, Enhancing Strategic Focus as a Pure-Play Aerospace and Defense Company | INN

facts: After an 18-month process to evaluate expansion opportunities, Textron Specialized Vehicles announces it is purchasing 235 acres in Augusta, Ga.
The new site is located a half mile away from the company's current operations and near a facility currently owned by Procter and Gamble.
The purchased facility is about 600,000 square feet.
Textron Specialized Vehicles plans to add 400 jobs at the new location within the next 5 years.
The expansion investment is estimated at $40 million.

Textron Specialized Vehicles manufactures commercial, recreational, off-road utility and light transportation vehicles for golf courses, consumers, aviation, government and industrial users under the E-Z-GO, Cushman, Bad Boy Buggies and TUG brands.
Textron President Kevin Holleran stated that the expansion "gives us room to not only move into today, but it will allow us to grow into in the future, it's more than we need today."
source_title: Textron Specialized Vehicles Expanding Operations

facts: E-Z-GO vehicles are designed and manufactured by Textron Specialized Vehicles Inc., a Textron Inc. (NYSE: TXT) company.
Michael R. Parkhurst serves as Vice President, Golf for Textron Specialized Vehicles.
Woods, Mickelson, Manning and Brady will each ride in a tailored E-Z-GO Freedom® RXV® ELiTE™ car while playing the 18-hole course at Medalist Golf Club.
John Collins serves as TSV Vice President, Personal Transportation Vehicles.

The ELiTE lithium vehicles used by the players in “The Match” use Samsung SDI lithium battery technology, which is environmentally friendly, maintenance-free and comes with an 8-year warranty for personal use.

E-Z-GO’s new Dealer to Driveway program provides the ability to customize a ride, find the nearest dealer and apply for financing from the comfort and safety of home. When the vehicle is built, it will be delivered to the new owner’s driveway.
Products sold under the E-Z-GO brand include RXV® and TXT® fleet golf cars, Freedom® RXV, Freedom TXT and Valor personal golf cars, and E-Z-GO Express™ personal utility vehicles.

Known for innovation in electric-vehicle technology, E-Z-GO’s newest offerings include the ELiTE series of lithium-ion powered golf cars and PTVs, and the company’s exclusive 72-volt AC electric powertrain found in its latest Express series vehicles.
Founded in 1954 in Augusta, Ga., E-Z-GO became part of Textron Inc. in 1960, and today operates as part of the company’s Textron Specialized Vehicles division.

facts: Augusta, Georgia-based Textron Specialized Vehicles is recalling certain Personal Transportation Vehicles (PTVs) citing fall and injury risks.
The recall involves about 280 units of 2022 E-Z-GO Freedom RXV ELiTE, Freedom RXV Gas, and Freedom RXV 48V personal transportation vehicles with a rear-facing seat.
Recalled units have serial numbers within the range of 5633701 to 5651753.
These vehicles are commonly referred to as golf carts or golf cars and are used primarily to transport people.
The recalled vehicles were manufactured in the United States.
Units were sold at E-Z-GO dealerships nationwide and online through E-Z-GO's Dealer to Driveway program from August 2021 through December 2021.
Retail prices for the recalled vehicles ranged between $9,900 and $17,450.
The defect involves a rear seat structure that can fail, resulting in the rear seat tilting unexpectedly and posing fall and injury hazards to riders.
Textron Specialized Vehicles has not received any reports of incidents or injuries related to the recalled vehicles to date.
Consumers are asked to stop using the rear-facing seat on their recalled vehicle and contact Textron for a free repair.
The recall was announced by the U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC).
source_title: Textron Specialized Vehicles Recalls Personal Transportation Vehicles

facts: Standardized Structure Design includes energy storage batteries, power conversion systems (PCS), photovoltaic modules, and charging modules in a compact and highly efficient cabinet.
Flexible Expansion is designed to support off-grid switching and photovoltaic energy charging, making it ideal for use in commercial buildings, residential communities, and microgrids.
Intelligent Management provides real-time remote monitoring and management with cloud-based control for operational status, energy usage, and fault detection to ensure optimized energy consumption.

Space-Efficient Design features integrated air conditioning units and a compact layout that reduce space usage, improve structural integrity, and offer an aesthetically pleasing solution for energy storage.
High Efficiency allows the system to support photovoltaic and energy storage in combination with charging solutions, providing a flexible and scalable approach to renewable energy storage.
The ELECOD Outdoor Cabinet ESS for PV Storage & Charging offers an integrated and scalable energy storage solution designed for photovoltaic energy generation and charging applications.
The system is highly suitable for use in microgrids, remote areas, industrial parks, EV charging stations, and residential buildings.
It integrates advanced energy storage management, photovoltaic charging, and real-time monitoring capabilities in one unit.
System flexibility ensures customization to meet various application needs, ranging from small-scale residential use to large commercial and industrial energy storage systems.
Integrated Design combines energy storage, photovoltaic charging, and power conversion systems in a single, compact unit.
Real-Time Monitoring and Control supports remote operation and real-time monitoring to allow users to optimize energy management and ensure smooth operation.
High-Performance Cooling is equipped with intelligent air-cooling technology to prevent overheating and ensure long-term performance under high-demand conditions.
Flexible Charging Solutions support various types of battery storage and can be easily expanded with additional modules as energy needs grow.
Durability and Protection features an IP55 rating, built to endure harsh environments and ensure reliable performance for years.
Certifications include IEC 61000, CE Certified, RoHS Compliant, and IP55 Protection Rating.
Shenzhen Elecod Electric Co., Ltd. is a leading Chinese manufacturer specializing in high-performance energy storage systems and power conversion solutions.

facts: Anovion Technologies broke ground on a new manufacturing facility in Georgia.
The project will create more than 400 jobs and over $800 million in investment in Decatur County.
Georgia Gov. Brian P. Kemp and First Lady Marty Kemp joined state and local leaders to celebrate the groundbreaking.
The facility will supply premium synthetic graphite anode materials for lithium-ion batteries.

Governor Brian Kemp stated the event is the culmination of months of hard work by the local community and sustained talks and efforts at the state level which is fast becoming the e-mobility capital of the nation.
Since Governor Kemp took office in 2018, EV-related projects have committed over $24 billion in investment and more than 31,000 jobs to communities across Georgia.

These projects are the result of local leaders, from development authorities to elected officials, and state officials from the Department of Economic Development, General Assembly, and Governor’s Office working with businesses to build lasting relationships.
The State of Georgia has been recognized as the No. 1 state for business for nine consecutive years.

Georgia’s top-ranked workforce training program, QuickStart, and the hard work of the Department of Economic Development are consistently cited as leading causes of the explosive growth Georgia has enjoyed.
Anovion’s new facility will be 1,500,000 square feet in size.
The facility is located at Downrange Industrial Park in Bainbridge.
The facility will use proprietary furnace technologies and will be the first of its size and scale in North America.
The synthetic graphite produced will be used in batteries that power EVs, electric storage systems, electronics, military applications, and other products.
Operations are expected to begin in late 2025.

With major EV-related projects now announced, under construction, or operational in all four corners of Georgia (southwest, southeast, northeast, northwest), the state remains well-positioned to set itself apart as the e-mobility capital of the nation.

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp and the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD) announced that Georgia earned the No. 1 spot in Site Selection’s annual Prosperity Cup rankings for successful investment recruitment in 2023.
The Prosperity Cup criteria include total values of new and expanded facilities, business-climate attractiveness, and workforce readiness.
In 2023, Georgia attracted 318 new and expanded facilities to secure the No. 1 ranking.
An expanded scope for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) is included in the rankings as the largest project in state history.
HMGMA announced an additional 400 new jobs and $2 billion in investment in 2023.
HMGMA’s total investment is now $7.59 billion and will net 8,500 new jobs for Georgians.
Anovion Technologies selected southwest Georgia in May for an $800 million, 400-job synthetic graphite manufacturing facility.
Anovion Technologies broke ground on its synthetic graphite facility in June of last year (2023).
Meissner Corporation plans to create more than 1,700 new jobs at a new campus in northeast Georgia.
Meissner Corporation’s expansion will more than double the company’s manufacturing footprint in the U.S.

Site Selection’s Mark Arend stated that the Prosperity Cup recognizes state-level economic development competitiveness and that Georgia’s consistent delivery of location attributes demonstrates it competes to win.
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America held its groundbreaking in October 2022.
In 2023, more than 10 Hyundai and automotive suppliers reached middle Georgia and beyond following the HMGMA groundbreaking.
These Hyundai and automotive suppliers will ultimately create over 4,300 new jobs and bring $1.2 billion in combined investment, with current numbers reported as even higher.
Kia’s facility in West Point announced an expansion during 2023.
Toyota’s campus in Pendergrass announced an expansion during 2023.
The combined Kia and Toyota expansions created a total of 440 new jobs and $286 million in investment.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson highlighted the collaborative nature of Georgia’s economic development team, including sister state agencies, utility partners, and local economic developers.
Governor Brian Kemp stated that decades of partnerships, community-to-state groundwork, and consistent pro-business policies have led to positive economic results for Georgia families.

Governor Brian Kemp noted that companies choosing Georgia gain access to global markets through logistics assets like Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport and some of the fastest-growing ports in the nation.

facts: Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp escalated his attack on President Joe Biden's electric vehicle policy at a groundbreaking for a company that received more than $100 million to refine graphite for electric batteries from the infrastructure law Biden signed.

According to advance remarks of his speech at Anovion Technologies, Kemp stated: "Georgia's electric mobility boom is taking place because our state is second to none for companies looking to invest, relocate, expand, and innovate – not because the federal government continues to put their thumb on the scale, favoring a few companies over the industry as a whole."
The factory groundbreaking event was described as unusually partisan.
Anovion's investment is $800 million.
Anovion promises 400 new jobs in rural Bainbridge, in Georgia's far southwest corner.

Georgia has been a top beneficiary of a nationwide electric vehicle investment boom, with more than 40 electric vehicle-related projects since 2020 pledging $22.7 billion of investment and 28,400 jobs in the state.
Kemp stated: "When President Biden and others falsely try to take credit for Georgia's success, don't forget that next year is an election year."
The remarks were timed knowing Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff would likely share his stage at the groundbreaking.
Ossoff has been Georgia's most high-profile supporter of Biden's electric vehicle policies.
Kemp and Ossoff could be rivals for the Senate seat in 2026.
Ossoff argued that Georgia's investment boom wouldn't happen without Democratic policies.
Ossoff told The Associated Press before the event: "It is bizarre to attend a groundbreaking and launch a political attack on the very policy that made the groundbreaking possible."
Ossoff characterized Kemp's remarks as "a panicked political tantrum over the success of federal manufacturing policies in his own state."
Kemp has always opposed the Inflation Reduction Act, which is pumping billions into electric vehicle subsidies.
Kemp disagreed with the IRA's domestic content standards, which are meant to increase America's clean-energy manufacturing capacity.
The domestic content standards make tax incentives on electric vehicles available only when the vehicle, the battery, and key raw materials in the battery are all made in the United States.
Hyundai Motor Group is building a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia, near Savannah.
Hyundai has said the tax credits are unfair because its electric vehicles aren't currently eligible.
Kemp referenced Hyundai's criticism in his speech Tuesday, saying "that approach simply doesn't work."
Fellow Democratic senator Raphael Warnock proposed making the tax credits more flexible.
Ossoff emphasized that benefits will be available to Hyundai once the Ellabell plant starts production.
Kemp noted that Rivian Automotive announced a $5 billion plant east of Atlanta in December 2021, while Hyundai announced in May 2022, both before Biden signed the Inflation Reduction Act.
Kemp said he wants to make Georgia the "e-mobility capital of the nation" as his second-term legacy.
Ossoff claimed credit for Biden and Democrats, including for solar panel plant expansions in northwest Georgia.
Hyundai and LG Energy Solution announced a $4.3 billion electric battery plant in May at Hyundai's new complex.
Ossoff assertively trumpeted the news while Kemp was in Israel, a move that chafed some Kemp administration officials.
Kemp attributed Anovion's choice of location to state and local officials, saying "they don't posture or showboat, and they don't try to steal credit."
Many Republicans oppose electric vehicles.
Weeks after the Hyundai battery announcement, former President Donald Trump told the Georgia Republican convention that he would abolish Biden's electric vehicle policies on his first day in office.
Kemp did not attend that convention out of dissatisfaction with state party leadership.

Kemp has tried to persuade Republicans to break off their love affair with Trump while opposing a Democratic president whose administration lavished electric vehicle makers with billions in incentives.

Kemp stated: "Unlike top-down systems like China's, and those advocated by some on the federal level, we aren't dictating how this growth happens. We aren't picking winners and losers. We're letting the market drive this innovation and expansion."
Anovion is a Chicago-based firm.
Anovion's Georgia factory will make synthetic graphite, a key ingredient for lithium batteries.
The factory benefits from content standards that are boosting domestic graphite demand.
Anovion won $117 million in federal financing to build and improve factories.
Anovion may be able to claim federal tax credits of 10% on the costs of producing graphite as well as 30% on its factory investment, both part of the Inflation Reduction Act.
Ossoff stated: "Manufacturing is coming back to America and it's coming to Georgia, as we intended when we passed these infrastructure and manufacturing policies."
source_title: Georgia Governor Attacks Biden's EV Policy at Federally Backed Battery Material Maker | Manufacturing Business Technology

facts: Ascend Elements plans to increase U.S. production of lithium carbonate by 60% with the start of a new lithium recovery line in 2025.
The company will begin producing >99% pure lithium carbonate (Li2CO3) recovered from used lithium-ion batteries at its facility in Covington, Ga. in 2025.
Ascend Elements plans to produce up to 3,000 metric tons of domestic Li2CO3 per year.
Currently, recycled Li2CO3 is not produced at a commercial scale anywhere in the United States.
The only other domestic source of Li2CO3 is from a mining operation in Nevada.
Eric Gratz, co-founder and CTO of Ascend Elements, stated this is the first new, domestic source of lithium carbonate in the 21st Century.
Gratz noted that this new domestic supply of a critical battery material will help U.S. industries meet growing demand while avoiding the possibility of tariffs on imported materials.
Li2CO3 is used to make advanced batteries for grid-scale energy storage applications as well as electric vehicles, boats and aircraft.
Ascend Elements’ innovative lithium recovery process produces a remarkably low level of carbon dioxide emissions compared to conventional methods like mining and brine extraction.
The company’s lithium extraction process produces just 2.27 kg of CO2 emissions per 1 kg of Li2CO3 produced.
Spodumene mining produces approximately 16.7 kg of CO2 emissions per 1 kg of Li2CO3.
Chilean brine extraction produces approximately 3.6 kg of CO2 emissions per 1 kg of Li2CO3.
These preliminary emission comparisons are from a life cycle assessment (LCA) not yet critically reviewed.

Gratz stated that the current process of lithium extraction from used lithium-ion batteries is about 86% less carbon-intensive compared to spodumene mining and 37% less carbon-intensive compared to Chilean brine extraction.
The Ascend Elements facility in Covington is one of North America’s largest lithium-ion battery recycling facilities.
The plant has been in operation since August 2022.
The Covington plant has the capacity to recycle up to 30,000 metric tons of lithium-ion battery materials per year.
This recycling capacity equates to approximately 70,000 EV battery packs annually.

Ascend Elements is a North America- and Europe-based battery materials leader that converts spent lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap into premium precursor cathode material (pCAM) and battery-grade lithium carbonate through its patented Hydro-to-Cathode® process.
source_title: Ascend Elements | Ascend Elements to Increase U.S. Production of Lithium Carbonate By 60% With Start of New Lithium Recovery Line in 2025

facts: Ascend Elements is a Massachusetts-based company with a billion-dollar plan for battery recycling using black mass (recyclable material from gigafactory scrap and end-of-life lithium-ion batteries).
The company broke ground for a greenfield site in October 2022 in Christian County, Southwestern Kentucky.
Roger Lin serves as vice president of global marketing and government relations for Ascend Elements and sits on the board of directors at the U.S. Energy Storage Association.
Lin estimates that 6% to 10% of a lithium-ion battery factory's output is scrap.
Any battery factory that creates scrap or requires cathode active materials (CAM) is considered a potential customer for Ascend Elements.
The company worked with 50 different sites throughout the Southeast during its site selection process.
A key requirement was proximity to Ascend Elements' "Base 1" facility in Covington, Georgia.
Base 1 recently began operations and shreds 30,000 tons of batteries a year to create black mass.
Site selection criteria included clear height, power, zoning, and access to labor.
The company preferred a greenfield site because its factory model is unique in the country.
The new Hopkinsville facility is dubbed "Apex 1".
Community factors influencing the site selection included technical requirements, timing for availability, workforce access, and the welcoming attitude of local residents.

Assistance during site selection came from the office of Gov. Andy Beshear, the Kentucky Cabinet for Economic Development, the Hopkinsville mayor's office, Christian County officials, and the South Western Kentucky Economic Development Council.

In early February 2023, 12 county economic development organizations in Western Kentucky formed "Kentucky Cornerstone" to market the region near EV battery production centers in Kentucky and Tennessee.
The Kentucky Cornerstone initiative started in late 2021 after Ford projects were announced.
Partnered communities are located along the I-69 and I-24 corridors connecting BlueOval City in Tennessee and BlueOval SK Battery Park in Kentucky.
Apex 1 is located on over 140 acres in Commerce Industrial Park II.
The facility spans 500,000 square feet and is scheduled to begin operations in late 2023.
With potential expansion phases, the facility will employ up to 400 people in roles including engineers, chemists, warehouse associates, and manufacturing operators.
Michael O’Kronley serves as CEO of Ascend Elements.
The Hopkinsville facility will produce sustainable active battery material (both CAM and engineered precursor/pCAM) for approximately 250,000 EVs per year.
Ascend Elements operates facilities in Westborough, Massachusetts; Michigan; Georgia; and Kentucky.
The company's intellectual property was developed more than a decade ago near Worcester Polytechnic Institute in Massachusetts.
An IDTechEx analysis released in February 2023 projects that current plans for new cell production capacity will reach 3 TWh by 2030.
U.S. battery manufacturing capacity is expected to grow to 18% of global capacity by 2027, up from approximately 7% in 2021.

Plants within a close travel radius of the Hopkinsville site include SK On (Kentucky and Georgia), Freyr (Georgia), Envision AESC (Kentucky), LG partnered with GM (Tennessee), and Rivian (whose planned $5 billion plant is near Ascend Elements' Georgia facility).
A division of SK was among several investors that helped Ascend Elements raise $300 million in equity and debt financing.
As of November 2022, approximately 44 companies in Canada and the United States and 47 companies in Europe recycled or planned to recycle lithium batteries.
Since 2020, Kentucky has seen roughly $10 billion in EV-related investments and more than 9,000 full-time jobs announced by sector companies.

Martinrea Hopkinsville LLC, a Canada-based Tier 1 automotive supplier, recently announced a $31 million, 33-job expansion in Christian County for lightweight structural steel products for ICE and BEV vehicles.
Ford and SK On updated their $5.8 billion, 1,500-acre BlueOval SK Battery Park project in Glendale, Kentucky.
BlueOval SK Battery Park is slated to start production in 2025 and will create 5,000 new jobs in Kentucky.
Two battery manufacturing facilities at the site will collectively produce more than 80 gigawatt-hours (GWh) annually.
Ford targets an annual run rate of 2 million electric vehicles globally by the end of 2026.
Jee Dong-seob serves as president and CEO of SK On.
Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear called BlueOval SK the largest economic development project in state history and part of Ford's biggest investment ever.
Lisa Drake serves as vice president of Ford EV Industrialization.
The complex will train 5,000 new workers at the Elizabethtown Community and Technical College (ECTC) BlueOval SK Training Center located on site.

The training center spans 42,000 square feet, is the only co-branded learning facility in the Kentucky Community College System, and represents a $25 million investment by the Commonwealth of Kentucky.
Lisa Slaven serves as Hardin County Schools Transition Readiness Director.
Japan-based Envision AESC announced a $2 billion investment in April 2022 to build a gigafactory in the Kentucky Transpark in Bowling Green, Warren County.
The 30-GWh plant will create 2,000 skilled jobs and produce battery cells and modules for multiple global automotive manufacturers.
Jeff Deaton serves as Envision AESC U.S. Managing Director.
Todd Alcott is the mayor of Bowling Green.
New-generation battery cells produced at the facility will have 30% more energy density, reduced charging time, increased range and efficiency, and power up to 300,000 vehicles annually by 2027.
Envision AESC will receive up to $116.8 million from state incentive programs and up to $5 million in grant-in-aid for skills training.
The gigafactory will be powered by 100% renewable energy from onsite generation and purchased locally from the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA).
Adam Murray serves as a target market specialist with TVA Economic Development.
TVA's seven-state territory has welcomed $17.2 billion in EV-related capital investment creating 17,500 jobs.
LOTTE Chemical announced a joint venture with LOTTE Aluminium in August 2022 to build a facility in Elizabethtown producing 36,000 tons of cathode foil annually.
Cathode foil is a core material for EV batteries, and demand is anticipated to increase by an average of 32% annually by 2030.
The Hardin County operation sits on 40 acres in the T.J. Patterson Industrial Park and will be the company's first U.S. aluminum foil facility, expected to begin operation in 2025.
The site received $500,000 in state funding in 2020 through Kentucky's pilot Product Development Initiative for infrastructure improvements.
JV partners are subsidiaries of South Korea's LOTTE Group.
South Korea-based Advanced Nano Products (ANP) announced a $49.6 million investment creating 93 jobs in Hardin County to supply battery producers including BlueOval SK Battery Park.
Dr. Changwoo Park serves as CEO of ANP.

Firestone Airide, a Bridgestone Americas subsidiary, moved forward with a $50 million, 250-job expansion at its Williamsburg plant in Whitley County for automotive air springs supporting the EV market.
In January 2022, Gov. Beshear announced Piston Automotive LLC would increase its Jefferson County expansion scope to create 117 full-time jobs with a $26.3 million-plus investment.
Piston is a subsidiary of Michigan-based Piston Group LLC, led by former NBA player Vinnie Johnson.
Toyota Motor Manufacturing Kentucky in Georgetown announced additional investment and jobs to support EV production.
Rebecca Goodman serves as Kentucky Energy and Environment Cabinet Secretary.
Kentucky received federal approval in September 2022 to develop a nearly $70 million electric vehicle charging network.

Kentucky's Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Deployment Plan was developed by the Kentucky Transportation Cabinet (KYTC), Energy and Environment Cabinet (EEC), Public Service Commission, and Federal Highway Administration.
The approval secured federal NEVI formula program funds, bringing total available funding to $86.9 million over five years with matching funds.
Initial NEVI funding will build direct current fast-charging (DCFC) stations capable of fully charging a battery in 30 minutes or less at interchanges and parkways.
In July 2022, the FHWA approved Kentucky's plan for Alternative Fuel Corridors (AFCs).
All of Kentucky's 11 Interstates and eight parkways are designated as EV AFCs.
Fast-charging stations must be located no more than 50 miles apart along AFCs, within one mile of the corridor, have four ports at 150-kilowatts each (600-kilowatt total), and cannot be proprietary.
The goal is to have a statewide EV charger network by 2025.
Jim Gray serves as KYTC Secretary.
source_title: Kentucky’s EV Ecosystem Begins to Take Shape – Site Selection Magazine

facts: Ascend Elements secured $300 million in equity and debt financing.
The funding includes $200 million in Series C equity investments from an international group of strategic and financial investors.
The funding round was led by Fifth Wall Climate.
SK ecoplant, the environmental unit of South Korean conglomerate SK Group, joined the funding round.
New investors include Oman Investment Authority, Lithium Americas Corporation, GLy Capital Management’s New Mobility Fund, Mirae Asset Capital & LS and Shinhan GIB.

Previous investors participated in the latest round, including Hitachi Ventures, Jaguar Land Rover’s InMotion Ventures, TDK Ventures, Orbia Ventures, At One Ventures, TRUMPF Venture, and Doral Energy-Tech Ventures.
The funding is in addition to two recently awarded grants totaling $480 million from the Department of Energy.
The funding will accelerate commercialization of Ascend Elements’ Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis process.
The proprietary process establishes a closed-loop, EV battery materials supply chain in North America.
Ascend Elements plans to invest up to $1 billion to build a sustainable lithium-ion battery materials facility in Hopkinsville, Ky.
The Kentucky facility, known as “Apex 1”, will produce enough lithium-ion battery pCAM and sustainable CAM to equip up to 250,000 electric vehicles per year.
Ascend Elements is already generating revenue at its “Base 1” facility in Georgia.
The Georgia facility processes end-of-life batteries and manufacturing scrap.
The Georgia facility will have 30,000 metric tons of EV battery recycling capacity by the end of 2022.
The Georgia facility will ultimately feed the Apex 1 facility in Kentucky.
Ascend Elements expects to recycle more than 150,000 metric tons of lithium-ion batteries per year globally by 2026.
The company transforms recycled batteries into high-performance, customized EV battery cathode active material precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).
The produced pCAM and CAM meet or exceed performance requirements of top-tier battery manufacturers.

CEO Mike O’Kronley stated: “Our investment partners understand the urgent need to produce sustainable, lithium-ion battery materials. It’s not enough to simply recycle lithium-ion batteries and recover metals for the global commodity markets. Our patented, closed-loop process goes beyond simple battery recycling. Instead, we produce sustainable, high-performance cathode active materials that can go directly back into new EV batteries.”

Fifth Wall Partner Peter Gajdoš noted that securing recyclability of critical metals is essential to achieve energy independence from fossil fuels and supports creating sustainable batteries for automotive and stationary storage.

SK ecoplant CEO Park Kyung-il stated the investment continues their strategic partnership and lays the foundation for SK ecoplant as the first shareholder to dominate the global battery recycling industry.

Oman Investment Authority Director Ibrahim Al Eisri stated the investment aligns with OIA’s strategy and Vision 2040 priorities to develop and transform the lithium-ion battery recycling industry globally.
Ascend Elements is based in Westborough, Mass., and is a leading provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery materials solutions.

The company's proprietary technology produces new CAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods, resulting in reduced cost, improved performance, and lowered GHG emissions.
source_title: Ascend Elements | Ascend Elements Secures $300 Million in Funding

facts: US-based Ascend Elements has raised funding totalling $300 million.
The company began building its factory to produce sustainable battery materials for electric vehicles in southwestern Kentucky a few days ago.
The funding raised includes $200 million in investments as part of the company’s Series C financing round led by Fifth Wall Climate.
SK Ecoplant, a unit of the South Korean conglomerate SK Group, joined the round as a new investor and has reportedly invested $50 million alone.
Previous investors including Hitachi and Jaguar Land Rover also participated in the latest financing round.
Ascend Elements recently received two grants from the US Department of Energy totalling $480 million.
Just over $310 million of the DOE grants is earmarked for the construction of the Kentucky factory.
The Hopkinsville plant, construction of which has now begun, will use the company’s “hydro-to-cathode” technology.

The technology processes black matter from recycled electric vehicle batteries directly into new cathode materials without the usual intermediate step of hydrometallurgically splitting the black matter into individual materials.
Ascend Elements CEO Mike O’Kronley stated that their patented, closed-loop process produces sustainable, high-performance cathode active materials that can go directly back into new EV batteries.
The company has already started generating revenue by processing end-of-life batteries and manufacturing scrap at its “Base 1” facility in Georgia.
The Georgia facility is meant to have 30,000 metric tons of EV battery recycling capacity by the end of 2022.
The Georgia facility should eventually feed the Apex 1 facility in Kentucky.
The company aims to recycle more than 150,000 metric tons of lithium-ion batteries per year globally by 2026.

Fifth Wall Partner and Co-Lead of the Climate Investment team Peter Gajdoš stated that securing the recyclability of critical, valuable metals in batteries is an essential avenue to achieve energy independence from fossil fuels.
source_title: Ascend Elements raises $300 million - electrive.com

facts: Based in Westborough, Mass., USA, Ascend Elements is a leading provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery material solutions.
The company recycles used lithium-ion batteries and Gigafactory manufacturing scrap.
Ascend Elements manufactures sustainable cathode precursor (pCAM) and cathode active material (CAM) made from recycled lithium-ion batteries.
Its Hydro-to-Cathode™ direct precursor synthesis technology was developed at Worcester Polytechnic Institute.
The Hydro-to-Cathode™ process recovers up to 98% of the critical battery materials in spent Li-ion batteries and scrap.
The process produces new cathode material at a lower cost and with up to 90% lower greenhouse gas emissions than similar cathode made with newly mined metals.
The technology can customize and engineer cathode active material composition, particle size and distribution, crystallinity, morphology, and porosity to specific customer requirements.
Products include sustainable pCAM and CAM made to customer specifications.
High-Nickel Cathode products include NMC 811, NMC 622, NMC 532, and NCMA.
Raw materials produced include recycled lithium carbonate, recycled nickel sulfate, and black mass.
Recent federal legislation – the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and the Inflation Reduction Act – are further increasing demand for recycled battery materials.
Tax credits detailed in the Inflation Reduction Act require use of domestic battery materials and recycled content in new EV batteries.
Founded in 2015, Ascend Elements recently completed its Series C funding round of $300 million (equity and debt).
In October 2022, Ascend Elements received two grants from the U.S. Department of Energy totaling $480 million.
The growth of the lithium-ion battery recycling industry is tied to the growth of the electric vehicle market and grid-scale energy storage market.
Every new lithium-ion battery manufacturing facility will need a battery recycling facility of similar size.

The technology plays an important role in the United States’ domestic battery manufacturing and recycling infrastructure plans, as described in the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and Pres. Biden’s recent invocation of the Defense Production Act (DPA).
Ascend Elements received $480 million from the U.S. Department of Energy to commercialize the Hydro-to-Cathode technology at a one-of-a-kind manufacturing facility in Hopkinsville, Ky.
Construction is ongoing for Apex 1, an Ascend Elements battery recycling and sustainable cathode manufacturing facility in Hopkinsville, KY.
Ascend Elements will celebrate the grand opening of its Base 1 battery recycling facility in Covington, Georgia, USA in 2023.
The company is exploring opportunities to expand into Europe and may announce new projects in 2023.
Co-founders of Ascend Elements are commercializing technology that transforms used lithium-ion batteries and manufacturing scrap into sustainable cathode active material for EV batteries.
Independent study by the United States Battery Consortium (USABC) found Ascend Elements' sustainable cathode active material performs as well or better than materials made from newly mined metals.
The patented Hydro-to-Cathode™ technology can produce battery-ready cathode active materials with up to 93% lower greenhouse gas emissions compared to traditional recycling methods.
The technology enables recycling of any type of lithium-ion battery, including cell phone, laptop, and EV batteries, regardless of chemistry.
In addition to lithium, nickel, cobalt and graphite, the recycling processes recover plastics, aluminum and copper.
The company's goal is to recover every last bit of recyclable material and keep 100% of batteries out of landfills.
source_title: INTERVIEW WITH ASCEND ELEMENTS – FineEngineering Magazine

facts: Ascend Elements is a Westborough, MA-based engineered materials and lithium-ion battery recycling company.
The company raised $300M in equity and debt financing.
The financing includes $200m in Series C equity investments.
The funding round was led by Fifth Wall Climate.
SK ecoplant joined the funding round.

Other participants in the funding round include Oman Investment Authority, Lithium Americas Corporation, GLy Capital Management New Mobility Fund, Mirae Asset Capital & LS, Shinhan GIB, Hitachi Ventures, InMotion Ventures, TDK Ventures, Orbia Ventures, At One Ventures, TRUMPF Venture, and Doral Energy-Tech Ventures.
The funding is in addition to two recently awarded grants totaling $480 million from the Department of Energy.
Ascend Elements intends to use the funds to accelerate commercialization of its proprietary Hydro-to-Cathode direct precursor synthesis process.
The company is led by CEO Mike O’Kronley.
Ascend Elements is a provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery materials solutions.
The company operates from EV battery recycling to commercial-scale production of lithium-ion battery precursor (pCAM) and cathode active materials (CAM).
Its proprietary Hydro-to-Cathode direct precursor synthesis technology produces new CAM from spent lithium-ion cells in a different way.
This technology results in reduced cost, improved performance, and lowered GHG emissions.
The company recently disclosed plans to invest up to $1 billion to build a sustainable lithium-ion battery materials facility in Hopkinsville, Ky.
The manufacturing facility is known as “Apex 1”.
Apex 1 will produce enough lithium-ion battery pCAM and sustainable CAM to equip up to 250,000 electric vehicles per year.
source_title: Ascend Elements Raises $300M in Funding

facts: US battery-materials company Ascend Elements is teaming up with South Korean firms SK Ecoplant and its subsidiary TES to construct a cutting-edge battery recycling plant in Hopkinsville, Kentucky.
The project has a budget of $65 million.
Construction is scheduled to commence in November and anticipated for completion by January 2025.
The recycling plant will possess the capacity to process up to 12,000 tons of black mass extracted from used EV batteries.
Ascend Elements will utilize these recycled materials to bolster its battery production efforts.
The Hopkinsville region is quickly emerging as a hub for advanced battery technologies.
Ascend Elements is also in the process of constructing a battery materials facility, aptly named Apex 1, within the same area.
Apex 1 is slated to become operational next year and is projected to produce cathode material and precursors for an impressive 750,000 electric vehicle batteries annually.
The projected annual output of 750,000 EV batteries represents a threefold increase compared to the company’s 2022 estimates.
SK Ecoplant holds a majority stake of 64 percent in the new battery recycling plant.
Ascend Elements will possess a significant 25 percent share, while TES will account for the remaining eleven percent.
Mike O’Kronley, CEO of Ascend Elements, stated that this venture marks the beginning of an entirely new industry in the United States.
Mike O’Kronley noted that for every new EV battery gigafactory built, a new battery recycling facility will be needed to process manufacturing scrap and end-of-life batteries.
Mike O’Kronley described the project as a capital-intensive endeavor where joint ventures between strategically aligned partners are an ideal way to fund new infrastructure projects.
In March 2023, Ascend Elements marked a significant milestone by commissioning its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling plant in the US state of Georgia, named ‘Base 1.’
‘Base 1’ is regarded as North America’s largest recycling plant for electric vehicle batteries.
‘Base 1’ has the capacity to process up to 30,000 tonnes of batteries annually, equivalent to approximately 70,000 e-vehicle batteries.
source_title: Ascend Elements, SK Ecoplant, and TES Collaborate to Build $65 Million Battery Recycling Plant in Kentucky

facts: United States market size in 2025 (Value) is USD 295.9 million.
United States market size in 2026 (Value) is USD 335.0 million.
United States forecast in 2036 (Value) is USD 1,157.5 million.
United States CAGR from 2026 to 2036 is 13.2%.
Key sub-regions and industrial corridors for the United States include Nevada, Georgia, Ohio, Tennessee, South Carolina, and Michigan.
United States demand is expected to rise at a 13.2% CAGR as gigafactory scrap and recycled lithium carbonate projects create direct bulk acid needs.
United States buyers are scaling reagent programs around domestic critical-mineral security and specialist recycling campuses.
Bulk acid supply and documented lithium recovery shape supplier selection in the United States.
Recyclers in the United States are expected to prioritize contracts that combine delivery reliability and application support.
Battery material producers will value reagent partners able to support quality records for recovered lithium carbonate and precursor inputs.
The United States separates itself through large gigafactory scrap streams and critical-mineral security programs linked to battery recycling.
Redwood Materials leads the United States Battery Leaching Reagents Market, supported by Ascend Elements and Aqua Metals across recycling and hydrometallurgy programs.
Adoption in the United States is driven by plants seeking reliable acids and reducing agents for manufacturing scrap and domestic lithium recovery.
Redwood Materials holds a high leach chemistry depth, strong black mass feedstock access, strong battery-grade recovery link, strong channel reach, and a North America geographic footprint.

Ascend Elements holds a high leach chemistry depth, very strong black mass feedstock access, very strong battery-grade recovery link, moderate channel reach, and a North America and Europe geographic footprint.

facts: The battery plant just north of Commerce, Ga. looms over Interstate 85 with sheer gray walls many stories high and a vast parking lot extending almost half a mile.
Online employees complain about having to trek long distances to reach their posts at the Commerce facility.
On a Friday afternoon in late March, dozens of workers lined up at the guardhouse to start shifts building batteries for vehicles like the Ford F-150 Lightning and the Volkswagen ID.4.
The factory opened in early 2022.
The plant employs over 2,600 people, which is about a third of Commerce's population.
The battery plant is operated and owned by the U.S. wing of the South Korean company SK Group.
The facility is part of a bonanza of electric vehicle investments transforming rural Georgia into a bustling "battery belt."
Gov. Brian Kemp (R) aims to make Georgia the "electric mobility capital of America."
Kemp and other Republicans do little to put more EVs on state roads or acknowledge the climate reasons behind the switch.
Many major projects announced since President Biden signed the Inflation Reduction Act are landing in congressional districts represented by Republicans.
The Inflation Reduction Act includes numerous clean energy incentives.
Georgia is one of the biggest winners in the quest for clean energy jobs.
Since 2020, Georgia has attracted 35 electric-vehicle-related projects and an estimated 27,400 jobs.
With $15.27 billion pledged so far, Georgia leads the country in new clean energy investments.
Battery and vehicle manufacturers, battery recycling plants, and auto parts suppliers have flocked to Southern states chasing expanded federal tax credits and generous state incentives.
About an hour and a half southwest of Commerce, excavators are tearing up ground for Rivian's 20-million-square-foot, $5 billion factory.
Rivian's factory reportedly will include a driving test track and an "adventure trail."
Just 30 miles outside Savannah, Korean automaker Hyundai is building a "metaplant" that promises to produce 300,000 electric vehicles every year.
Georgia Republican lawmakers haven't fully embraced electric vehicles; last month the state Senate passed a tax on public EV charging.
About 90 minutes south of the SK factory, three workers heaved silver-plated lithium-ion battery modules onto a conveyor belt at Ascend Elements.
The modules came from car companies and battery manufacturers around Georgia.
A machine with long, rotating metal teeth crushes and tears the modules into small shreds of plastic, metal, and "black mass."
Black mass consists of dustings of lithium, manganese, and other valuable rare earth elements that could be recycled into new batteries.
The Ascend Elements battery recycling facility is newly opened and the largest of its kind in North America.
At full capacity, the facility will be able to recycle 70,000 electric vehicle batteries per year.
The process can recover up to 98 percent of the critical metals used in those batteries, including nickel, cobalt, manganese, and lithium.
Recovered materials can be made into new battery cathodes by Ascend or sold back to battery manufacturers.
Ascend came to Georgia largely because SK needed a recycler to process its battery scrap into new materials.
Ascend executives also made a strategic decision to follow the growth of the battery belt and a jackpot of state incentives.
"We wanted to geographically align ourselves with battery manufacturing," said Mike O'Cronley, Ascend's CEO.
Companies like Toyota, Ford, and BMW have descended on South Carolina, Tennessee, and Kentucky investing billions in battery plants and EV manufacturing facilities.
In Georgia, the Department of Economic Development helps companies find a site for their plants and guides them through the permitting process.
Big players in Georgia can receive tax abatements and employee training.
Rivian's factory near Rutledge, Ga. received an estimated $1.5 billion in tax abatements, funding for the factory, and employee training.
The SK factory in Commerce pulled in around $300 million from state and county governments.

Pat Wilson, commissioner of the Georgia economic development agency, said the state wants to be ready for any opportunity for job development with free land, ready-to-go permitting, and workforce training.
Georgia Quick Start, a program affiliated with the Technical College System of Georgia, can rapidly spin up a training program to provide workers for newly opened factories.

"While they are building their plant, we will in parallel find and train employees here in Georgia. So on day one, when they turn on their facility, they have employees," Wilson said. "That is our secret sauce."
Quick Start has sent representatives to Korea to learn from Hyundai's training program.
Almost all Southern states, including Georgia, Kentucky, Alabama, and Tennessee, are "right to work" states where employees can't be required to join a union as a condition of employment.
Right-to-work states have gotten the lion's share of clean energy and EV money, including four of the six that have received the most new investments: Georgia, Tennessee, South Carolina, and Texas.
Michigan recently repealed its right-to-work law.
A study by researchers at Georgia Tech examining almost 20,000 collective bargaining agreements found that right-to-work laws are associated with lower wages among unionized employees.
"The right-to-work aspect is compelling to a certain number of CEOs," said Mike Carr, partner at the consultancy Boundary Stone.
With a tight labor market, Carr noted that competition for talent in Georgia will be pretty fierce.
Even as Georgia becomes a hub for EV manufacturing, its lawmakers have been hesitant to fully embrace electric cars or mention how going electric could help curb global warming.
In March, Georgia legislators approved a plan to tax electricity delivered by public chargers at the rate of 2.84 cents per kilowatt-hour.
The tax is meant to compensate for lost revenue from the gas tax, but environmental and climate groups say it amounts to double-charging Georgians who drive electric cars.
EV drivers in Georgia already pay a $211 registration fee to compensate for lost gas taxes, one of the highest such fees in the nation.
Jennette Gayer, state director for Environment Georgia, said she worries the tax will dissuade people from switching to electric vehicles.
"This extra tax is kind of galling because it's like, 'Wait, then what is this registration fee that we're paying?'" Gayer said.
Georgia ranks 10th in the country with 34,000 fully electric vehicles registered, according to the Department of Energy.
Gayer says Georgia's high EV ranking is largely because the state once had a $5,000 tax credit for electric cars that was discontinued in 2015.
Other Southern states in the battery belt have almost no electric vehicles and similarly unfriendly policies.
South Carolina has only around 7,000 EVs registered.
Alabama and Kentucky have less than 5,000 EVs registered.
South Carolina has been ranked one of the top 10 least EV-friendly states due to its paucity of charging stations.
Tennessee will soon impose the highest registration fee for electric cars in the nation.
Georgia is among a group of states that outlaw direct sales of vehicles, stymying companies like Tesla and Rivian that don't sell through dealerships.
Tesla has received a carve-out to allow the company to operate five direct-to-consumer stores in the Atlanta area.
Rivian will not be able to sell its vehicles in Georgia despite locating its second factory there.

"If the products of the manufacturers you're attracting can't be readily sold or purchased in that state - well, you've got a challenge," said Stan Cross, electric transportation policy director at the Southern Alliance for Clean Energy.
Gov. Kemp champions the shift to e-mobility but stops short of mentioning climate change or pushing residents to buy EVs, arguing Georgia is simply allowing the free market to operate.
"He's not going to force anyone to get an EV," said Garrison Douglas, the governor's press secretary. "He's not looking to force the market in any one direction or another."
State Sen. Steve Gooch (R), majority leader and co-sponsor of recent EV legislation, said he continues to be unsure whether electric cars are better for the environment than gas-powered ones.
"I think the jury's still out on that," Gooch said.
Gooch helped oversee a comprehensive report on electrifying Georgia's transportation system that stretches to 513 pages.
The 513-page report mentions "climate change" only once.
Democratic U.S. senators Jon Ossoff and Raphael G. Warnock point to the Inflation Reduction Act as the reason for the boom in battery manufacturing.
The Inflation Reduction Act includes a generous tax credit for the production of battery components in the United States and a consumer tax credit for electric vehicles assembled in the country.
According to Mike Carr, "It's hard to see even a third of these investments happening" without the IRA.

State Republicans point to the generous incentives offered by the state itself as the driver for attracting top-level investments from folks like Rivian and Hyundai prior to the Inflation Reduction Act.
Republican voters are more likely to live in areas where jobs are closely tied to carbon-emitting industries like auto manufacturing or coal-fired power generation.
Democratic voters are more likely to live on the coasts and work at a desk in "knowledge-based" jobs.
"If you get a growing number of people employed in the clean energy sector, that could increase support for climate policy in red and purple states," Oatley said.
Former GOP senator David Perdue ran for governor against Kemp in 2022 and was a vocal critic of the Rivian plant, calling the carmaker a "woke California company."
Perdue's anti-EV message failed to gain much support and he was soundly defeated in the primary.
"If Georgians start to think of themselves as the heart of clean energy manufacturing - I don't know how you run against that and win," Carr said.
source_title: The unlikely center of America's EV battery revolution

facts: Joint venture AE Elemental will own and operate an advanced Li-ion EV battery recycling facility in Zawiercie, Poland.
Another AE Elemental facility is planned for central Germany.
Elemental Strategic Metals of Zawiercie, Poland, and Ascend Elements of Westborough, Mass., US, announced the formation of the JV on April 9, 2024.
The JV partners will jointly own (50/50) and operate the newly constructed facility in Zawiercie, Poland.

The Zawiercie facility will disassemble, discharge, and shred EV batteries to produce black mass for new engineered EV battery materials, including cathode active material (CAM) and cathode precursor (pCAM).
The Zawiercie facility has a capacity to recycle up to 12,000 metric tons of batteries per year, or approximately 28,000 EV batteries annually.
JV partners agreed to jointly invest in large-scale lithium extraction from black mass.
Lithium extraction capabilities processing up to 20 000 metric tons of black mass per year will begin construction in Fall 2024.
The lithium extraction facility is scheduled to be operational in 2026.

Companies plan to begin building a new EV battery recycling facility in central Germany with a capacity to recycle up to 25,000 metric tons of batteries per year, or approximately 58,000 EVs annually.
Pawel Jarski, CEO of Elemental Holding, stated the partnership is a transformative step towards becoming a key player in the battery materials industry.

Jarski noted that combining Ascend Elements’ cutting-edge technology with their recycling expertise, large collection network, recycling platform, and global footprint puts AE Elemental in a pole position in this evolving market.
Mike O’Kronley, CEO of Ascend Elements, stated the JV represents their first commercial-scale battery recycling facility in Europe.
O’Kronley noted expanding into Europe will allow them to better service customers locally and help the industry comply with new EU rules requiring recycled material in new batteries.

Elemental Strategic Metals is a member of Elemental Group and operates a large, state-of-the-art multi-metal extraction facility in Poland focused on recovery of battery metals and platinum group metals.

Elemental Group is a global leader in urban mining, extracting strategic metals from spent automotive catalysts (SACs), waste electrical and electronic equipment, printed circuit boards (PCBs), used Li-Ion batteries, and non-ferrous scrap metal.
Elemental recovers strategic metals such as platinum group metals, copper, and other base and precious metals in an environmentally friendly way.
Elemental currently has subsidiaries in 35 countries across Europe, Asia, and North America.
Ascend Elements is based in Westborough, Mass., and is a leading provider of sustainable, closed-loop battery material solutions.
Ascend Elements uses Hydro-to-Cathode® direct precursor synthesis technology to produce new pCAM and CAM from spent lithium-ion cells more efficiently than traditional methods.
This technology results in improved economics and lowered GHG emissions for the lithium-ion battery industry.
source_title: Ascend Elements | Ascend Elements and Elemental Strategic Metals Establish AE Elemental, an Electric Vehicle Battery Recycling JV in Poland

facts: Aspen Aerogels Inc priced its upsized public offering of 25.3 million shares at $9.50 per share.
The company granted underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 3.8 million shares.
Gross proceeds from the offering are expected to be approximately $240 million, previously proposed as $200 million.
Aspen Aerogels intends to use net proceeds to partially fund the construction of Phase I of its second aerogel manufacturing facility in Statesboro, Georgia.
The equity offering is expected to close on December 2, 2022.
Aspen Aerogels entered into a definitive loan agreement with General Motors Holdings LLC for a secured lending commitment of $100 million in connection with construction of the Statesboro facility.
General Motors Holdings LLC is an affiliate of General Motors Company.
source_title: Aspen Aerogels Arranges Funds For Construction Of Statesboro Facility Via Equity Offering, Loan

facts: Aspen Aerogels will invest in excess of $300 million to build a new factory in Bulloch County, Georgia.
The new factory will produce aerogel thermal insulating material used in electric vehicle batteries.
Aspen Aerogels' investment coincides with the construction of a Hyundai Motors electric vehicle and battery manufacturing plant in nearby Bryan County, Georgia.
Once operational, the Aspen Aerogels plant is expected to create approximately 250 jobs for the region.
MJW has been contracted by Turner Construction to design, fabricate, and install plant-wide fire protection and fire suppression systems for the facility.

MJW's team initially served in a design-assist role alongside Turner Construction and Engineer of Record Thompson Consultants Inc. to develop a cost-effective design and construction approach for the facility's protection systems.

The installation sequence began with approximately 1,200 linear feet of fused 10-inch HDPE high-pressure discharge piping running from a fire pump in the main utility building to several system risers inside the manufacturing facility.
The facility will be protected by multiple wet and dry automatic sprinkler systems, several foam/water sprinkler systems, and equipment-specific high-pressure CO2 fire suppression systems.

MJW's current work represents an early phase of the project, with potential to expand the partnership with Turner Construction and Aspen Aerogels on future manufacturing building additions and expansions at the site.
source_title: Aspen Aerogels Engages MJW for Fire Protection and Suppression Systems at New Bulloch County, Georgia Plant

facts: The consensus analyst price target for Aspen Aerogels has recently been revised downward from $9.58 to $8.75.

Analyst sentiment weighs both the company's growth opportunities in the electric vehicle supply chain and heightened risks tied to macroeconomic uncertainties and the removal of key industry incentives.
Barclays downgraded Aspen Aerogels to Underweight from Equal Weight and reduced its price target to $6 from $7.
Barclays cited the removal of the $7,500 electric vehicle tax credit as a factor impacting near-term performance.
The removal of the $7,500 EV tax credit is expected to reduce General Motors' EV production plans.
Reduced GM EV production plans will directly impact Aspen Aerogels' Thermal Barrier business volumes and margins.
Aspen Aerogels relies heavily on General Motors as a customer.
Aspen Aerogels' gross margins are sensitive to production volumes.
Aspen Aerogels revised its full-year 2025 earnings guidance, now projecting revenue between $270 million and $280 million.
The revised 2025 revenue forecast is a decrease from the prior forecast of $297 million to $317 million.
Aspen Aerogels anticipates a higher net loss and loss per share for 2025 compared to earlier predictions.
For the fourth quarter of 2025, Aspen Aerogels provided revenue guidance of $40 million to $50 million.
Impairment guidance for property, plant, and equipment in 2025 was updated, with expected impairment at $287.6 million.
The expected 2025 impairment is a slight increase from the previous estimate of $287.57 million.
The discount rate for Aspen Aerogels edged down slightly from 7.91% to 7.80%.
Revenue growth projections for Aspen Aerogels have risen sharply, moving from 8.92% to 18.14%.
Net profit margin estimates for Aspen Aerogels have increased from 12.26% to 14.76%.
The future P/E ratio for Aspen Aerogels has declined significantly from 15.88x to 10.60x.
Cost reductions and US manufacturing are strengthening Aspen Aerogels' path to profitability, even with softer revenue trends.
Aspen Aerogels is positioned in the booming EV thermal barrier market.
Key risks for Aspen Aerogels include heavy reliance on certain customers and market uncertainty that could impact earnings and valuation going forward.
