facts: South Korean parts manufacturer Seohan Auto Georgia Corp. is investing $72 million in coastal Georgia.
Seohan Auto Georgia Corp. is the first supplier of the Hyundai Meta Plant to land in Liberty County.
The Hyundai Meta Plant represents a $5.54 billion investment.
The Meta Plant will be Hyundai's first purpose-built electric-vehicle factory in the United States.
The Meta Plant broke ground last October.
Since breaking ground, the Meta Plant has attracted seven suppliers to the area.
These seven suppliers have pledged about $2 billion of additional investment.
These seven suppliers have pledged to create 4,500 jobs.
Seohan operates as a tier-one supplier.
Seohan's products include actuators, pumps, halfshafts, crankshafts, and other parts that feed directly into Hyundai and other brands' manufacturing processes.
The parent Seohan Group posted sales of 2.8 trillion won ($2.1 billion) in 2022.
The Seohan Group also manufactures bearings for wind turbines.
The Seohan Group is headquartered in Seoul and manages 16 domestic companies in Korea.
One of Seohan's seven international affiliates is located in Auburn, Alabama.
Seohan USA established its Auburn facility in 2008 to serve Hyundai's first U.S. plant.
A second Seohan USA location was added in 2014.
Both Alabama facilities received a $28.5 million expansion in 2019.
The latest expansion at the Alabama facilities, focused on EVs, added $13.5 million in investment.
The latest EV-focused expansion added 12 jobs last year.
The new Georgia plant will employ 180 workers.
The Georgia plant will focus on manufacturing electric-vehicle parts including halfshafts, axles, and brake systems.
The company's website refers to Georgia as the "new hub of future car production."
Jung Kee Koo is the CEO of Seohan Auto Georgia.
Jung Kee Koo stated that sustained EV market growth suggests accelerated changes to the automotive market are inevitable.
Jung Kee Koo believes Georgia will be the center of the EV industry and a new frontier for Seohan's future.
The quote was issued in a news release by Gov. Brian Kemp’s office.
Seohan's chosen site is located within the Tradeport East Business Center.
Liberty County is the first county to secure supplier investment outside of Bryan, Bulloch, Chatham, and Effingham counties, which formed a joint development authority for the Hyundai plant.
source_title: Another Hyundai Supplier Lands in Coastal Georgia, Invests $72M - Global Atlanta

facts: A South Korean company will build a $72 million factory to make half shafts, axles and brake systems for a Hyundai Motor Group auto assembly plant in coastal Georgia.
Seohan Group announced Tuesday that it would build the plant near Midway, south of Savannah, with plans to hire at least 180 new employees.

Seohan Auto Georgia is the seventh major supplier to locate in the region after Hyundai said in May it would build a $5.5 billion plant to assemble electric vehicles and batteries in Ellabell, Georgia.
The site could grow to 8,100 employees and is slated to begin producing vehicles in 2025.
Seohan and six other suppliers have since pledged to invest nearly $2 billion and hire more than 4,600 people.
Seoul-based Seohan already supplies a Hyundai assembly plant in Montgomery, Alabama and a Kia plant in West Point, Georgia.
Seohan makes axles and driveshafts at two plants in Auburn, Alabama, that opened in 2008 and 2014.
The company said last year that it would invest $13.5 million in the plants, which jointly employ more than 200 people, to make parts for electric vehicles.
But the company said Tuesday that it needs additional capacity to make parts as automakers convert to electric propulsion.
Seohan Auto Georgia Corp. CEO Jung Kee Koo said in a statement, “Sustained growth of the EV market over the past few years suggests that accelerated changes to the automotive market is inevitable.”
CEO Jung Kee Koo added, “We believe Georgia will be the center of the EV industry, and will be a new frontier for Seohan’s future with limitless opportunities and potential.”
The plant will be located in a business park off Interstate 95 near Midway, in Liberty County.
The company plans to begin production in late 2024.
Seohan also could qualify for $2.7 million in state income tax credits, at $3,000 per job over five years, as long as workers make at least $31,300 a year.
The state will also pay to train workers.
source_title: Hyundai supplier Seohan sets $72M, 180-job Georgia plant – WSB-TV Channel 2 - Atlanta

facts: Seohan Auto Georgia held a grand opening ceremony for its new manufacturing facility in Midway, Liberty County, at the Tradeport East Business Center.
The new manufacturing facility will produce H/shafts, axles, and brake systems for Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs).
Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America is an OEM customer for the facility's products.
The project will create at least 180 new jobs in Liberty County.
The project represents an investment of more than $72 million in Liberty County.

Georgia Department of Economic Development Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that community investment in the Tradeport East Business Center allowed Seohan Auto Georgia to meet a challenging timeline to get up and running.
State Rep. Al Williams, chairman of the Liberty County Development Authority board, said the process to locate the facility began in November 2022 after other locations were considered.
Williams credited the Liberty County Development Authority staff, board members, community partners, and state partners for successfully winning the project for Liberty County.
Seohan Auto Georgia Corporation is a new subsidiary of Korea Movenex Co., Ltd.
Korea Movenex Co., Ltd was established in 1974 and employs more than 2,800 employees globally.
Korea Movenex possesses advanced technology and experience in the automotive parts industry.
source_title: Seohan Auto Georgia holds grand opening - Coastal Courier

facts: Seohan Auto Georgia announced a USD 72 million plant.
The new manufacturing facility will produce H-shafts, axles, and brake systems for OEMs.
The plant is intended to supply Hyundai Metaplant.
source_title: Seohan Auto Georgia announces USD 72 million plant to supply Hyundai Metaplant  - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: The Hyundai Metaplant is projected to bring thousands of jobs to the Liberty County area.
Governor Brian Kemp announced the most recent development in Liberty County.
Seohan Auto Georgia is a supplier for Hyundai.
Seohan Auto Georgia is expected to bring 180 new jobs to Liberty County.
Many counties surrounding the Hyundai Metaplant have experienced development since the plant was announced.
Seohan Auto is the first manufacturing plant to come to Liberty County as a result of the Metaplant's announcement.
Seohan Auto is expected to invest more than $72 million in its Georgia facility.
Liberty County Commission Chairman Donald Lovette stated that the facility is a great enhancement for competitive jobs in the area.
Donald Lovette noted that many residents currently commute to other jobs in and around the Coastal Empire and will now be able to work locally.
The Seohan Auto Georgia facility will be located in Liberty County's industrial park at Tradeport East, just off exit 76 on I-95.
Seohan Auto Georgia is a tier one manufacturer for the Hyundai Metaplant, producing parts needed early in the production process.
Donald Lovette stated that being a tier one provider puts the plant's construction on the fast track.
Donald Lovette also serves on the Liberty County Development Authority.
Donald Lovette stated that being a tier one provider puts Liberty County on the cutting edge of supplying parts for the Hyundai megaplant.
Donald Lovette said the county's location made it the ideal spot for the development.
Donald Lovette cited Liberty County's status as a coastal county and I-95 running through it as reasons the location is a perfect fit for manufacturers.
The facility's location is in close proximity to the Georgia Ports Authority.
Seohan Auto is expected to begin production on its automobile parts next year.
source_title: ‘It’s a great enhancement:’ Seohan Auto Georgia facility coming to Liberty County

facts: According to a report in the Washington Post, Audi is considering building a factory in the United States to produce electric vehicles.
In just the past three years, several automakers and battery manufacturers have announced plans to spend billions on new facilities in the Southern Auto Corridor.
Alabama’s four major assembly plants continue to grow.
Mazda Toyota is adding jobs in Huntsville.
Hyundai (Montgomery) and Mercedes-Benz (Tuscaloosa County) continue to expand their electric vehicle lineups.
Honda plans updates on several models at its plant in Lincoln.
Korean automaker Hyundai rolled out its first electric vehicle at its large plant in Montgomery, Ala.
The Genesis Electrified GV70 SUV was the first model to be produced.
The first EV comes 10 months after Hyundai announced that it would build the Electrified GV70 SUV and a hybrid version of the Santa Fe at the Montgomery plant.
Hyundai invested $300 million and added 200 jobs to install the EV line at the plant.
Samkee Corp., a Tier 1 supplier to Hyundai’s plant in Montgomery, will open a $128 million factory in Tuskegee, its first U.S. operation.
The project will create 170 new jobs.
Electric vehicle plants like Rivian and Hyundai in Georgia make up seven of the 10 largest economic development projects as of April.
The Hyundai project, worth billions, is one of the largest economic development projects announced in the South.
South Korean automaker Hyundai has added to its massive portfolio in Bryan County, Ga.
In a joint venture with LG Energy Solution Ltd., the two companies will build a $4.3 billion electric vehicle battery plant near Savannah in Bryan County.
The facility will become the second battery plant Hyundai is developing in the state.
A $50 million lithium-ion battery recycling facility is now open in Covington, a critical piece of Georgia’s growing electric vehicle supply chain.
Massachusetts-based Ascend Elements currently employs 100 workers at the facility, which is near Rivian’s planned $5 billion electric vehicle plant in Social Circle, Ga.
The battery recycler plans to increase its headcount to 185 by 2024, and called the project the largest of its kind in North America.
In the spring quarter, Georgia announced Sewon America would invest more than $300 million in a new manufacturing facility in Rincon in Effingham County, Ga.
The deal will generate more than 700 jobs.
A steady stream of automotive parts suppliers has announced plans to serve the gigantic Hyundai battery and vehicle plant in Bryan County, Ga.
South Korea-based PHA announced in March it will invest $67 million in a new facility near the Hyundai plant.
The deal will create over 400 jobs.

Gov. Brian Kemp announced that Seoyon E-HWA, a global auto parts supplier that manufactures both interior and exterior components, will create 740 new direct and indirect jobs and invest almost $76 million in a new manufacturing facility in Chatham County, Ga.
Microvast Advanced Membrane is investing $504 million in a project in Hopkinsville, Ky., to build the world’s first mass production facility for its cutting-edge polyaramid separator technology.
The polyaramid technology is used in firefighting garments and insulating papers.
The facility will help integrate the product into EV battery designs, including Microvast’s cells as well as those of other third-party battery manufacturers.
The project will create 562 full-time jobs.

A new study conducted by the Kentucky League of Cities and Murray State University shows the potential economic impact of the BlueOval SK Battery Park on local governments in Hardin County and the surrounding region.
Once at full employment in 2025, the Ford facility is estimated to have a yearly value-added impact of nearly $800 million.
Toyota Boshoku America Inc. (TBA) will invest more than $225 million to construct a new facility in Hopkinsville and create 157 quality jobs for Kentuckians, according to the Lane Report.
The new facility will include a 365,400-square-foot building pad on 49 acres in Christian County.
The plant will be the first TBA location globally to be considered a “Smart Plant.”

The facility will employ innovative, cutting-edge technologies such as advanced robotics, planning and automation to streamline processes, reduce waste and enhance productivity to maximize efficiency and operations.
Rivian is investing $10 million in Bullitt County for a planned remanufacturing facility in Shepherdsville that will create 218 high-paying jobs.
The state of Mississippi recently announced it was allocating $5.1 million to Madison County to build an additional access point to the industrial site near the Nissan factory.
Toyota is adding another $2.1 billion in investment to its vehicle battery plant under construction near Greensboro.

According to The Business Journals, the expansion of the Toyota Battery Manufacturing North Carolina plant under construction near Liberty in northern Randolph County brings total investment there to nearly $6 billion.
After announcements by Toyota and others, most of the existing megasites in North Carolina are now occupied.
The North Carolina legislature has passed funding to replenish the state’s lineup of sites of 1,000 acres or more.
Charlotte-based Albemarle is investing at least $1.3 billion and creating more than 300 new jobs to construct a new “Mega-Flex” lithium hydroxide processing facility in Chester County, S.C.
The facility will support the surging demand for electric vehicles and other energy storage applications that use lithium-ion batteries.
Situated on nearly 800 acres near Richburg, Albemarle’s new “Mega-Flex” conversion facility will support the fast-growing global electric vehicle and energy storage markets.
The term “Mega-Flex” refers to the facility’s ability to process diverse lithium feedstock, including lithium from recycled batteries.

The new facility is expected to annually produce approximately 50,000 metric tons of battery-grade lithium hydroxide from multiple sources, with the potential to reach up to 100,000 metric tons, according to the South Carolina Department of Commerce.
Cirba Solutions, a comprehensive battery management and materials company, announced plans in the spring to build its newest state-of-the-art, flagship operations in Richland County, S.C.
The integrated lithium-ion battery materials campus will encompass an initial investment of over $300 million and will create more than 300 new jobs.

The company plans to build an approximately 400,000-square-foot, world-class lithium-ion battery recycling and materials campus that will focus on processing end-of-life hybrid and EV batteries and gigafactory scrap to extract critical materials such as nickel, cobalt and lithium.
Tin Thanh Group Americas, a tire manufacturer, announced plans to establish its first United States operations in Allendale County.
The company’s $68 million investment will create 1,031 new jobs.

Supplying energy from reusable sources, Tin Thanh Group Americas will provide operations in recycled energy, closed industry-agriculture, recycled waste and tire leasing while serving the environment, energy, high-tech agriculture and technology markets.

Gov. Henry McMaster, the South Carolina Department of Commerce and the South Carolina Ports Authority announced that the state’s 2022 export sales totaled $31.5 billion, up 6 percent over 2021, according to data from the U.S. Department of Commerce.
The state remains the nation’s top exporter of completed passenger motor vehicles and tires.
BorgWarner is planning a $42.7 million expansion in Seneca, S.C., as part of its “Charging Forward” initiative to upgrade its facility and integrate new EV battery systems, creating 122 new jobs.
In the spring quarter, Tennessee Governor Bill Lee and Ford Motor Company marked significant progress on construction at the historic BlueOval City complex in West Tennessee.
The project is the single largest investment in Tennessee history.

In 2021, Ford Motor Company and SK On committed a $5.6 billion investment to build a 3,600-acre mega-campus called BlueOval City at the Megasite of West Tennessee, where the production of Ford’s second generation electric trucks will begin in 2025.
The project is expected to create 30,000 jobs in West Tennessee, anchored by Memphis, including auto parts suppliers and other support projects.
The construction is expected to create 6,000 jobs.

The Tennessee Department of Economic and Community Development reported in the spring quarter that Ford’s suppliers for its electric vehicle facility in Stanton, Tenn., (located just outside of Memphis) are considering sites in more places in the Volunteer State than West Tennessee.
Supplier activity is hot on the site of the 11-million-square-foot plant shared by Ford and battery maker SK.
Gov. Bill Lee announced that 6K Energy will invest $166 million in a full-scale PlusCAM™ battery material manufacturing plant in Jackson, Tenn.
With its initial investment, 6K Energy plans on expanding to $250 million in future phases.
The company will also be using its $50 million U.S. Department of Energy grant for the factory.
Microvast, a maker of batteries for EVs, is investing $150 million to expand its facility in Clarksville, Tenn., which will double the plant’s capacity.
Houston-based Microvast is one of several automotive companies pouring new investment into Greater Nashville and hitching the largest piece of Tennessee’s manufacturing economy to electric vehicles.
So far, Microvast has committed to close to 290 jobs at its first Clarksville factory and another 600 to 700 at a potential second factory nearby, according to the Nashville Business Journal.
Microvast reported 35 percent growth in revenue from 2021 to 2022.
Its backlog of orders more than tripled to $410.5 million.
VW announced in the spring quarter it is adding 500 workers at its plant in Chattanooga.
That puts the German automaker’s employee headcount at 5,500 and solidifies it as Hamilton County’s largest private employer.
Nokian Tyres is expanding its workforce in Dayton, Tenn., generating 125 new jobs.
Tesla’s East Austin gigafactory is now making 5,000 vehicles in a week.
That is more than double the 1,600-a-week milestone the factory was able to roll out a year earlier.
Austin-based Tesla broke ground on a new $375 million lithium refinery in rural Neuces County near Corpus Christi in the spring quarter.
The facility will produce lithium for batteries made in Austin at the company’s major electric vehicle plant.

German auto parts manufacturer STS Group AG will establish its North American headquarters in Salem, Va., moving into an existing facility near Roanoke instead of its originally announced greenfield project in Wythe County, Va.
The investment of $39 million will create 120 jobs.
source_title: Southern Auto Corridor - Spring 2023 | Southern Business and Development

facts: Auto parts manufacturer and Hyundai supplier broke ground on a $76 million facility in Chatham County on Friday.
Seoyon E-HWA broke ground at the Savannah Chatham Manufacturing Center.
The new facility will create 740 new direct and indirect jobs.
Officials expect production at the facility to start in October 2024.

Governor Brian Kemp said in a Tweet Monday morning: "We're excited for this new chapter for Seoyon E-HWA and the Savannah-Chatham community as Georgia continues to see more jobs and investment come to all parts of our state."
More than two-thirds, or around 500, of the announced jobs will be permanent roles in human resources, development, quality and production personnel.
source_title: Auto parts manufacturer breaks ground on $76 million facility in Chatham County

facts: Seoyon E-Hwa is listed on the Korea Composite Stock Price Index (KOSPI).
Secured a lucrative contract from Tesla for Cybertruck parts supply.
Order value expected to be around 300 billion South Korean won ($227 million USD).
Contract involves the supply of A, B, and C pillar parts for Tesla’s upcoming Cybertruck.
Report attributed to Maekyung (via @Tslachan).
Seoyon E-Hwa inked a three-year deal to supply these integral structural components.
Pillars connect the body and roof of a vehicle and are named A, B, and C depending on their positioning from front to back.
Company secured orders for all three types of pillars, with the parts contract extending until 2028.
Rented a factory in Monterrey, Mexico with a 13 billion won budget ($9.8 million USD) to manufacture Cybertruck pillar components.
Set to send workers to Mexico next month to start production of the parts.
Primarily a vendor for Hyundai Motor Company and specializes in interior and exterior automobile materials.
Generates over 90% of its sales from Hyundai Motor and Kia.
Recorded consolidated sales of KRW 2,845.3 billion in the first quarter.
Recorded an operating profit of KRW 150.6 billion in the first quarter.
First quarter consolidated sales marked a year-on-year increase of 30.5%.
First quarter operating profit marked a year-on-year increase of 128.4%.
Contract follows Seoyon E-Hwa’s earlier expansion into the American market.
Established a corporation in Alabama in 2005.
Established a corporation in Georgia in 2010.
Invested 37.9 billion Won to expand a new plant in Savannah, Georgia.
Savannah plant expansion aims to supply parts to Hyundai Motor Group’s exclusive EV factories and broaden its North American business.
Anticipated to invest up to 100 million dollars in the future to further penetrate the US market.
Seeking additional orders following the Tesla Cybertruck pillar parts contract.
Inclusion of floor parts is expected to significantly boost sales despite the relatively low unit price of the filler supply.
A company official stated, “It is still in progress internally,” and added that a confidentiality agreement prevents sharing further details about the order.
source_title: Tesla Partners with Seoyon E-Hwa for Cybertruck Parts Supply: Report | TeslaNorth.com

facts: The Tucson sold in the U.S. is manufactured in Alabama.
Hybrid and plug-in hybrid versions of the Tucson will be available.
Both electrified variants pair a 180-hp, turbocharged 1.6-liter engine with an electric motor and a six-speed automatic transmission with all-wheel drive.
The standard hybrid uses a 59-hp electric motor, while the plug-in hybrid upsizes to a 90-hp motor.
The standard hybrid produces 226 combined horsepower, while the PHEV generates 261 combined horsepower.
Hyundai promises an all-electric range of up to 32 miles for the PHEV.
The PHEV will qualify for a federal tax credit of $6,587 based on battery capacity.
The 2022 Tucson is expected to go on sale in June.
Pricing starts at $26,135 for the base SE FWD model and ranges up to $37,285 for the Limited AWD.
Hybrid model pricing starts at $30,235 and goes up to $38,535 for the Limited trim.
Based on worldwide sales, the Tucson is Hyundai's best-selling vehicle.
source_title: 2022 Hyundai Tucson Evolves in Style

facts: SRG Global Inc.'s Ripley, TN molding, coating, and assembly facility has been recognized by Ford Motor Co. with its Q1 quality award.

The Warren, MI headquartered automotive supplier was created in May 2008 when Guardian Automotive Inc., a subsidiary of Guardian Industries Corp., acquired Siegel-Robert Automotive from Siegel-Robert Inc.
A company spokesperson told PlasticsToday that SR and Guardian have both been working with Ford for 'decades,' with the new company, SRG, sourcing parts to Ford since its inception.
SRG Global is one of the world's largest manufacturers of chrome-plated plastic parts for the automotive industry.
SRG has eight facilities in North America, including the recognized Ripley operation, as well as sites in Spain, Poland, and China.
The Ripley site, including manufacturing and warehouse space, covers 344,000 sq ft.
The Ripley facility utilizes molding machines from 500 to 3000 tons.
In terms of decoration technology, the Ripley facility features painting with manual and robotic booths, monocoat 2K painting, lacquer, plating, and a bright chrome-only line.
The Ripley facility employs 300-plus people.
The Ripley facility is registered ISO 9001 and TS 16949 & ISO 14001 Certified Environmental Management.
Other SRG facilities that have achieved Ford Q1 designation include Evansville, IN; Morehead, KY; Valencia, Spain; Suzhou, China, and Covington, GA.

The Q1 award requires facilities to achieve 'excellence' in several areas, according to Ford, including capable systems, ongoing performance, site action plan, customer endorsements, and continuous improvement.
To achieve Q1 status, suppliers must maintain an overall score of at least 600 points.
The individual areas tracked for the Q1 award range from materials management assessment to PPM performance to customer satisfaction.
Duane Williams serves as a STA engineer at Ford.
Keith Taylor is the plant manager at SRG Ripley, TN.
Greg Lobo works in Ford supplier technical assistance.
source_title: SRG’s Tennessee molding/decoration facility honored by Ford

facts: SRG Global Inc. has broken ground on an injection molding, top coat, chrome-plating, and assembly plant in Irapuato, Mexico.
The company estimates that the initial phase of the project will cost between $25 and $35 million.
The facility is expected to be completed by the first quarter of 2012.
At this point in time, the facility's final size and machine count are to be determined.
The build-out for the new facility is expected to last one year.
The company expects to install injection molding machines ranging from 250 to 3000 tons, but that could be subject to change.
SRG currently supplies "many" of the OEMs in Mexico, shipping product from its sites in Kentucky, Indiana, Georgia, Tennessee, and Missouri.
The Irapuato site is located east of Guadalajara and northwest of Mexico City in the Mexican state of Guanajuato.
The site will serve a variety of automotive and non-automotive OEMs and customers.
Initial plans call for staffing up with 220 direct employees.
There is potential to bring on an additional 220 indirect employees.
Construction of the Mexico facility is part of SRG Global's business strategy to maintain local and regional production capabilities.
SRG Global's other recent investments include its Advanced Development Center in Taylor, MI, which opened in 2010.
SRG Global also opened a production facility in Boleslawiec, Poland, in 2009.
Within the automotive sector, SRG supplies front and rear end components including grilles, emblems/nameplates, liftgates, deck lids, and spoilers.
SRG supplies body side parts such as rocker panels, body side moldings, fuel filler caps, wheel flares, door handles, and wheel covers/caps.
SRG supplies a variety of interior components including pillars, window surround moldings, cowls, door belt moldings, parts for door panels, center consoles, instrument panels, and steering wheels.
The current company was formed by the former Guardian's acquisition of Siegel-Robert Automotive in 2008.
SRG Global was created in 2009 by combining the two companies' trim operations, with headquarters in Warren, MI.

In a 2008 presentation, Bill Conwell, VP of planning and strategic initiatives at Tier One supplier Lear, noted that there were 13 OEM plants in Mexico spread among 10 states, including Baja, Sonora, Coahuila, Aguascalientes, Jalisco, Guanajuato, Puebla, Morelos, and San Luis Potosi.
Conwell stated that OEMs operating in Mexico at that time included GM, Ford, Chrysler, Toyota, Nissan, Honda, and Volkswagen.
By 2011, Conwell forecast there would be 12 OEMs in the country spread among 18 plants, with an influx of Chinese carmakers including Geely, Chery, and ZhongXing, in addition to Fiat.
source_title: SRG to open new Mexican molding site

facts: TDK manufactures aluminum electrolytic capacitors used in frequency converters, wind power converters, solar inverters, professional power supplies and UPS, medical applications, professional photoflash units, and a wide range of automotive applications.
The capacitance of TDK's aluminum electrolytic capacitors is defined by the surface area of the aluminum anode foil.
Special etching processes are used to maximize the anode foil surface area.
These etching processes give the capacitors the highest volumetric capacitance of all capacitor technologies.
The resulting design provides an attractive cost per capacitance ratio.
TDK aluminum electrolytic capacitors feature outstanding ripple current capabilities.
TDK aluminum electrolytic capacitors offer very long-term reliability.
source_title: Home | TDK Electronics - TDK Europe

facts: Governor Brian P. Kemp, in conjunction with the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDEcD), announced that the state has been recognized as the No. 1 state for business by Area Development magazine for the 12th year in a row.
The 2025 Top States for Doing Business rankings from Area Development are determined by scores in 15 categories, based on input from approximately 50 leading site consultants nationwide.
Georgia ranked in the top five in 10 categories and claimed the overall No. 1 ranking.
The Top States for Doing Business Survey is the 16th in the annual series.

Building on this recognition, Georgia continues investing in future opportunities through the Rural Site Development Initiative, which helps local communities prepare development-ready industrial sites.
GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson stated that success in economic development takes years of planning, investment, and strong partnerships to achieve and sustain success.

Through support from the Georgia General Assembly, local leadership, and collaborations with sister state agencies, Georgia has been able to strengthen communities and prepare sites for future growth.
GDEcD highlighted combining these efforts with world-class workforce programs and unmatched global connectivity to ensure companies have what they need to thrive.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones noted that the announcement reflects partnership between Governor Kemp’s Office, the legislature, state agencies, and local leaders and economic developers across the state.
Lt. Governor Burt Jones praised Georgia's workforce for continuing to excel and serve as an example of why Georgia is the best state to live, work, and raise a family.

Speaker of the House Jon Burns stated that Georgia's designation as the No. 1 state for business for 12 consecutive years is the direct result of decades of strong leadership, forward-thinking policies, and strategic investments.
Speaker of the House Jon Burns noted that this accomplishment represents historic job creation and economic growth in every corner of the state.
The Georgia House remains committed to building on this record of success and ensuring that Georgia’s economy and communities continue to thrive for generations to come.
The article was published courtesy of Georgia CEO on September 25, 2025.
source_title: Georgia Named #1 for Business for 12th Straight Year - The ATL Airport Chamber

facts: TE Connectivity reported a surge in new orders from clients such as Google, Facebook, and global carmakers.
Net sales for the three months ended Dec. 31 hit $3.5 billion, up 14 percent from a year earlier.
More than half the growth came from new sales by established TE Connectivity businesses, with the rest from newly acquired units.
The company expects sales will total around $3.6 billion in the quarter ending March 31, up 12 percent over last year.
TE Connectivity reported a quarterly loss totaling $40 million, or 11 cents a share, due to a tax restatement that wiped out fourth-quarter net income.
The company was forced to declare past losses it had hoped to use for future tax relief as a result of last year's U.S. income tax overhaul.

TE Connectivity has been reviewing facilities for closure and sale, including its former AMP/Tyco Electronics office building in Harrisburg which is for sale because the company moved offices to Middletown.

CEO Terrence Curtin told investors that because the company's legal headquarters is in Switzerland, it will not benefit from lower U.S. corporate rates and tax legislation will have little impact on investment decisions.
A majority of TE Connectivity's customers and factories are located outside the United States.
The company boosted sales by concentrating on factory automation sensors at a time when manufacturers are rapidly replacing human workers with robots.
New orders for the quarter totaled around $3.9 billion, including plans for undersea cables linking Europe, the U.S., and South America.
In the automotive sector, TE Connectivity's transport margins increased strongly and sales grew several times faster than world auto production.

Demand for TE Connectivity auto electronics connectors, sensors, and assemblies is decoupled from global production and rising even where car and truck sales are not going up, noted Bank of America analyst Wamsi Mohan.
Electric vehicles are still a small market today compared with internal-combustion cars and trucks, noted Morgan Stanley analyst Craig Hettenbach.
The decline in diesel-vehicle sales following revelations that Volkswagen lied about engine pollution boosted demand for electric-car parts faster than expected, especially in Europe.
TE Connectivity now supplies around $62 worth of components for every electrical vehicle made.
CEO Terrence Curtin said the company has developed programs to sell up to $500 worth of sensors and assemblies per vehicle, whether it's a wireless or a connected solution.
Chinese vehicle-electronics parts demand has been very strong.
The move by U.S. companies to expand data centers supporting remote cloud-computing and wireless applications has also juiced sales.
TE Connectivity's SubCom business recently announced orders to run private cables under the Atlantic to Denmark and under the Pacific to Chile for Google and its partners.
Manufacturers of medical devices, appliances, vehicles, and data services are rapidly automating their processes regardless of unit sales, driving demand for TE Connectivity's components.
CEO Terrence Curtin concluded that the company is benefiting from cloud computing and miniaturization trends, with heavier take-up rates as volumes strengthen.
source_title: TE Connectivity boosts sales despite tax hit (Update)

facts: Governor Brian Kemp has championed policies supporting charging stations and battery recycling as part of his clean-energy economy agenda.
Hours before his second-term inauguration, Korean conglomerate Hanwha announced plans for a massive solar-panel facility in Georgia.
The Hanwha announcement is the latest in a multibillion-dollar series of economic development trophies Governor Kemp has claimed in the energy sector.
These investments include immense EV supply chain investments from companies like Hyundai, Rivian, and SK Battery.
In his Thursday inaugural address, Kemp vowed that by the end of his new term, Georgia would be “the electric mobility capital of America.”
Kemp plans to attend the World Economic Forum in Switzerland to promote Georgia's economic trajectory and electric-mobility prowess globally.
Kemp states his agenda is focused on fulfilling a promise to create good-paying jobs for the state.
He claims there are “a lot of conservatives that are driving electric vehicles.”
He points to Ford’s F-150 Lightning electric pickup, predicting: “You’re gonna have a lot of Republicans driving that truck.”
Kemp is critical of California's plan to end sales of gas-powered cars by 2035.

Republican governors are deploying billions of dollars in state incentives to lure green-manufacturing business, and getting help from tax benefits and made-in-America rules enacted by President Biden and his party in the Inflation Reduction Act.
Kemp criticizes federal incentives for “manipulating the market” and compares Biden’s manufacturing agenda unfavorably to Donald Trump’s use of tariffs.
When challenged on his own subsidies, Kemp argues Georgia offers development deals within a balanced state budget, whereas Biden finances his by “running up the country's debt and deficits.”
Kemp campaigned for reelection using a message of fighting crime, cutting taxes, and growing the economy with help from the electric car.
He recently addressed a conference in Sea Island, Georgia, hosted by Heritage Action, arguing that Republicans “have to be for something.”
Indiana Governor Eric Holcomb attended the COP 27 summit in Egypt to compete for green auto jobs.
Ohio Governor Mike DeWine cheered Honda’s announcement of a $3.5 billion battery plant.
Tennessee Governor Bill Lee unveiled plans for a cathode manufacturing facility backed by $3.2 billion from LG Chem.
Kemp stated his intention is to serve his full second term as governor.
source_title: Brian Kemp and the Electric Car: A Love Story - White House Wire

facts: SK Battery Incorporated laid off 1,000 workers at their $2.6 billion battery plant in Commerce, Georgia.
The layoffs followed a vote by Donald Trump and Congressional Republicans to end electric vehicle (EV) tax incentives.
According to Climate Power’s Energy Crisis Snapshot, 2,534 clean energy jobs have been lost or delayed in Georgia on Trump’s watch.
China signed an EV trade deal with Canada to fill the gap left by U.S. policy changes.
The article claims surging gas prices are due to Trump’s war in Iran.

Republican lawmakers sided with Trump to kill the American EV industry, a move claimed to increase gas prices by at least 25 cents per gallon and preserve Big Oil’s monopoly on roadway transportation.
Donald Trump told Reuters he does not have any concern about skyrocketing gas prices, despite them rising at the fastest pace since 2005.
Climate Power Senior Advisor Jesse Lee stated the layoffs represent a direct Trump betrayal and that 1,000 workers in Georgia got pink slips because Trump wants to kill American EV manufacturing.
Jesse Lee added that every Republican lawmaker in Georgia knew working families would be crushed but cowered before Trump.
Jesse Lee stated that with gas prices spiking, Trump is stripping Americans of consumer choice because his oil donors wanted to kill the competition.
Jesse Lee noted that China is gobbling up all the EV and clean energy jobs that Trump is destroying, while American people face layoffs and skyrocketing energy costs.
Our Next Energy laid off 29 employees at their Detroit plant.
An electric vehicle battery plant in Kentucky closed its doors in December 2025, killing 1,600 workers.
A battery plant canceled a project in Missouri, citing regulatory changes, killing 1,000 jobs.
In just two months, seven clean energy projects were closed in Michigan.
General Motors laid off 1,200 workers at its electric vehicle plant in Detroit.
Hundreds of additional permanent and temporary layoffs occurred at battery plants in Ohio and Tennessee.
In October, Fox 2 Detroit reported that over 100 employees at Dana Incorporated, an electric vehicle battery component manufacturer in Auburn Hills, Michigan, were laid off.
General Motors canceled a $55 million factory that would have created 300 jobs, citing decisions of the DOE.
Fortescue blamed U.S. policy settings and the elimination of critical tax credits in Trump and Republicans’ budget bill for canceling their $210 million Detroit EV battery factory.

Trump’s federal energy policies contributed to battery startup Natron Energy shutting down and canceling its planned $1.4 billion factory in Eastern North Carolina, which would have created 1,000 jobs.
source_title: BREAKING: 1,000 Workers Laid Off at Georgia Battery Manufacturing Plant Thanks to Trump’s War on Clean Energy | Climate Power

facts: Hyundai has 5 new models coming in 2026.
Hyundai continues its record sales pace from 2025 with more highlight numbers in 2026.
The brand plans to add a host of fresh models to its current lineup rolling into 2027.
Some upcoming vehicles are totally new, while others are refreshed or updated versions of existing Hyundai family members.
One upcoming vehicle gets a whole new powertrain option that could appeal to EV fence-sitters.
The Hyundai Elantra is due for a refresh with expected tweaks to the nose and tail along with an updated interior layout.
Powertrain options for the standard Elantra variant are expected to remain the same.
There could potentially be a new Elantra N engine on the horizon.
The Hyundai Ioniq 3 is a compact electric hatchback capable of more than 300 miles of range and decent power.
The Hyundai Ioniq 3 is not currently planned for sale in the United States.
Hyundai paused production of its Kona EV for 2026 because the 2025 supply remained higher than expected.
A fully updated Hyundai Kona is expected for the 2027 model year with a tougher stance design direction.
The 2027 Kona could arrive with a choice between a turbocharged 1.6-liter hybrid model and an updated EV version.
Both updated Kona powertrains will feature Hyundai's updated cabin technology to complement a more Crater-concept-inspired exterior design.
Hyundai has been spending big bucks investing in its Georgia Metaplant.
The automaker has long spoken of bringing an extended-range EV to market, and it appears the time is upon us.
The 2027 Hyundai Santa Fe is likely to offer an EREV powertrain option.
An EREV drives as an electric vehicle with electric motors and a battery pack, but also includes an onboard combustion engine that serves as a generator for the battery pack.
An EREV Santa Fe should deliver a driving range of around 560 miles.
The Hyundai Tucson has remained a smart option for SUV shoppers for two decades now.
Hyundai's compact SUV should adopt a boxier exterior aesthetic while retaining the latest familial design trademarks, such as lighting elements.
It is safe to assume Hyundai will continue to offer its hybrid and plug-in hybrid options for the 2027 Tucson.
There is talk of a potential Tucson N performance version arriving down the road, which could feature a 295-horsepower hybrid setup and all-wheel drive.
The next-gen Tucson might ditch its combustion-only version, potentially leading to a significant jump in the starting price for the 2027 Tucson.
Two of Hyundai's most intriguing upcoming models are an SUV and a pickup truck based on the Boulder concept.
These two models ride on the same platform.
They likely will not arrive as 2027 model-year vehicles, but could land somewhere between 2028 and 2030.
Hyundai is bolstering an already strong lineup with a range of sedan and crossover options featuring multiple powertrain options at different price points.
Hyundai's sales records could continue to pile up.
source_title: New Hyundai Models 2026

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) officially named the 2025 Hyundai IONIQ 5 as the first production vehicle manufactured at its new Metaplant in north Bryan County during a presentation at Savannah’s AutoNation Hyundai dealership.
The 2025 all-electric SUV has yet to be officially released for North American markets.
HMGMA is readying to begin production of the IONIQ 5 in the fourth quarter of this year (2024).
The new Ellabell plant will produce all IONIQ 5 trim lines except the IONIQ 5 N.
The Ellabell plant will eventually become the sole facility building IONIQ 5 models for the U.S. market.
Joe LaMuralglia, head of communications at HMGMA, stated that the Hyundai IONIQ 5 is one of the most award-winning and popular EVs on sale today.
The Hyundai IONIQ 5 has received numerous accolades from MotorTrend, Car and Driver, and U.S. News and World Report.
Joe LaMuralglia described the production milestone as a monumental occasion for HMGMA and expressed pride in partnering with the local community for the future of mobility.
81% of employees hired so far at the Metaplant are native to southeast Georgia, living within 60 miles of the north Bryan facility.
Hyundai Motor Group is investing $7.59 billion to develop HMGMA.
The development of HMGMA will create 8,500 direct jobs.
Hyundai and battery supplier SK On are currently building a $5 billion EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia.
The SK On battery cell plant will employ 3,500 workers.
Additional vehicles to be produced at the Metaplant will be announced in the future according to official sources.
The Metaplant will produce Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia vehicles.
source_title: Hyundai announces first vehicle produced at Metaplant - Bryan County News

facts: Volkswagen, Ford and GM are making nickel batteries.
Nickel batteries are currently about twice as expensive as iron LFP batteries.
LFP battery volume will be about 80% of the total battery market in 2024-2025.
Nickel prices are increasing and LFP battery mass production will drive down their prices faster.
Iron LFP batteries could become three times less expensive than nickel batteries.
EV Companies without Iron LFP batteries will only have the supplies to compete for 20% of the EV market in 2015.
If Tesla retains 60% of the nickel battery EV market, then legacy auto combined will have a maximum of 10% of the EV market in 2025.
It will take a minimum of 2-3 years and probably longer to launch new iron LFP EV models and to build and adapt factories.
EV Companies with 1-2% market share will have massive economies of scale disadvantages.
They will also be using more expensive batteries with further cost disadvantages.
Ford’s EV plans are for 240 GWh of annual global EV battery capacity by 2030 which would be enough for 2 million F150 Lightning.

Ford and SK On have to actually execute to reach that level of battery production and Ford will have to make the factories to build the EVs and Ford will have to be able to successfully sell those EVs.
Ford will get about 80% (up to 170-185 GWh) from SK On.
SK On’s second plant in Georgia, US: 11 GWh
Ford and SK Innovation’s SK On joint venture (BlueOvalSK): 129 GWh
Stanton, west Tennessee – Blue Oval City (43 GWh) – 2025
Glendale, central Kentucky – BlueOvalSK Battery Park (2x 43 GWh) – 2025
New JV in Turkey: 30-45 GWh
Total: 170-185 GWh or 70-77% out of 240 GWh total
Ford will be struggling to reach the 120 GWh/year battery level in 2026 to have the batteries for 1 million EVs per year.
GM is developing their own Ultium battery line.
Ultium batteries are using nickel chemistry.
The main selling point of the Ultium batteries is that they use 70% less cobalt than other nickel batteries.
Nickel prices have gone up a lot with the war in Ukraine.
It is not just the cobalt making nickel chemistry batteries more expensive.
GM’s third battery plant for its Ultium Cells LLC joint venture with LG Energy Solution will be located in Lansing, Michigan.
Site preparations for the $2.4 billion plant are scheduled to begin this summer, while cell production is scheduled to begin in 2024.

GM expects these projects to give it North American manufacturing capacity for 1 million EVs annually, including 600,000 electric trucks once both the Orion facility and the Detroit-area “Factory Zero” are fully ramped up.
Tesla produced 936000 EVs in 2021 and had manufacturing capacity over 1.3 million cars at the end of 2021.
Tesla’s production in December 2021, was an annualized runrate of 1.3 million cars.
VW, GM and Ford have to have fantastic execution in order to be only four years behind Tesla.
Toyota is partnering with BYD to make an iron LFP blade battery version of the Toyota Corolla.
Partnering with Chinese carmakers who have a lot of iron LFP battery supply and experience is a reasonable path to making a competitive EV.
There is no guarantee that such joint venture cars will be profitable or successful.
Profits would be halved for Toyota.
source_title: EV Companies Not Using Iron LFP Batteries Are Doomed | NextBigFuture.com

facts: Battery manufacturing infrastructure in the United States has undergone dramatic transformation since 2024, with production capabilities expanding from minimal grid storage capacity to comprehensive domestic supply sufficiency.
2024 baseline shows 70 GWh annual production capacity across limited facility networks.
2025 expansion reaches 145 GWh capacity through foreign investment integration.
2026 projections indicate 280 GWh capacity representing 300% growth over two years.
2027 targets project 421.5 GWh capacity with mature supply chain networks.
The Centre on Global Energy Policy documented cost reduction mechanisms driving this growth, noting production cost decreases of up to 30 percent through federal incentive structures.
Current supplier networks include 10 module assembly operations and projected expansion to 11 suppliers by 2027.
Great Lakes Corridor operations in Michigan and Ohio have $15+ billion investment focused on LFP battery cells.
Southeast Hub plants in Georgia and Tennessee have $20+ billion investment focused on module assembly.
Southwest Expansion facilities in Texas and Arizona have $8+ billion investment focused on system integration.

Lithium iron phosphate (LFP) chemistry dominates stationary storage applications due to enhanced safety profiles and extended operational lifespans compared to traditional lithium-ion formulations used in transportation applications.

The Inflation Reduction Act created competitive positioning for domestic manufacturing through multiple incentive mechanisms including manufacturing tax credits, local content requirements, supply chain incentives, and system integration benefits.
Manufacturing tax credits provide direct production cost reduction averaging 30%.
Investment data from S&P Global indicates the United States imported over $100 billion in batteries and components since 2021, with approximately 50% sourced from Chinese suppliers.
Korean manufacturing companies committed approximately $20 billion toward U.S. battery manufacturing expansion between 2025 and 2029.
Benchmark Minerals projects Korean producers will contribute over 40% of domestic capacity growth during this period.
Chinese suppliers control approximately 85% of graphite processing, 60% of lithium processing, and 75% of cathode material production.
LG Energy Solution invested $1.4 billion to establish dedicated lithium iron phosphate production lines with 16.5 GWh current capacity at its Holland, Michigan operation.
LG Energy Solution projects capacity expansion to 50 GWh by end-2026, representing 200% growth within 18 months.
Samsung SDI and SK Innovation complement LG's expansion through coordinated facility development across multiple states with combined Korean investment exceeding $20 billion.
Tennessee, Georgia, and Michigan emerged as preferred locations for Korean companies due to existing automotive manufacturing expertise and logistical infrastructure.
Tesla Energy operates the largest single-site battery manufacturing facility in California with 40 GWh annual Megapack production capacity.

Stryten Energy pursues multi-chemistry diversification across lead-acid, lithium-ion, and vanadium redox flow battery technologies and operates 11 existing facilities with planned 10 GW capacity addition across military, grid storage, and transportation market segments.
International Energy Agency analysis identifies production capacity and technical expertise concentration in Asian markets as primary supply security risks.
Active materials are primarily supplied by China at a 75% dependency level.
Precursor chemicals are primarily supplied by China at an 80% dependency level.
Processing equipment is primarily supplied by China and Korea at a 70% dependency level.
Quality control systems are primarily supplied by Korea and Japan at a 60% dependency level.
Training programmes require 6-12 months for technician certification in electrochemical processing operations.
Energy storage demand growth is 21% annually, driven primarily by renewable energy integration requirements and data centre backup power needs.
Production capacity growth exceeds 50% annually, creating potential oversupply conditions.
Domestic production capacity is projected at 280 GWh annually in 2026.
Projected U.S. demand in 2026 is 200 GWh combined grid and transportation.
Export potential in 2026 is 40 GWh available for international markets.
Market concentration involves 10-12 major suppliers competing for market share.
Data centre expansion represents the fastest-growing battery storage market segment as artificial intelligence and cloud computing requirements drive backup power needs.

Major facility investments include Toyota-Panasonic in Liberty, NC ($14 billion, 75 GWh, 2025-2027), LG Energy Solution in Holland, MI ($1.4 billion, 50 GWh, 2025-2026), AESC in Tennessee ($2.8 billion, 30 GWh, 2026-2027), and Samsung SDI in multiple states ($5 billion, 45 GWh, 2025-2028).
Financial constraints and market uncertainty resulted in approximately $8 billion in cancelled investments, including KorePower Arizona ($1.2 billion) and Freyr Georgia ($2.6 billion) projects.
Project cancellations concentrated amongst smaller companies lacking established customer relationships or proven manufacturing expertise.

Solid-state battery development threatens current lithium-ion manufacturing investments through superior energy density and safety characteristics, with commercial deployment delayed until 2030 or later.
Alternative chemistry development includes sodium-ion and iron-air storage systems targeting different market segments than traditional lithium-ion applications.
Potential industry consolidation by 2027-2028 reflects manufacturing capacity exceeding demand absorption rates.
Market share concentration amongst 3-5 major producers appears likely as competitive pressures eliminate marginal operations.
North American integration through Canadian and Mexican partnerships creates natural export opportunities for excess production capacity.

The U.S. battery manufacturing expansion represents a fundamental restructuring of global energy storage supply chains, transforming America from import-dependent to potentially export-capable within five years.
source_title: US Battery Manufacturing Expansion Surges Ahead

facts: On June 5, 2023, UL Solutions officially began construction of its North America Advanced Battery Laboratory in Auburn Hills, Michigan.
The facility is located near the center of the United States automotive industry.

The laboratory will help enable electric vehicle and industrial battery original equipment manufacturers and their suppliers with shorter development cycles, faster time-to-market, and the ability to be more competitive in the global marketplace.

Groundbreaking attendees included U.S. Sen. Debbie Stabenow (D-MI), U.S. Representative Haley Stevens (D-MI), Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer, Quentin Messer, Jr. (chief executive and economic competitiveness officer – chair, Michigan Strategic Fund/Michigan Economic Development Corporation), David Coulter (county executive, Oakland County), and Kevin McDaniel (mayor of the City of Auburn Hills).

Jennifer Scanlon, president and CEO of UL Solutions Inc., stated the company is thrilled to be part of the region’s innovation ecosystem and sit side-by-side with world’s top OEMs, electric vehicle and industrial battery product manufacturers, and high-tech companies focusing on the future of mobility and electrification.
Sen. Stabenow stated Michigan workers are the best in the world and the investment will create jobs while helping solidify Michigan’s position at the forefront of advanced manufacturing and mobility.
Rep. Stevens noted that once completed, the facility will be one of the most extensive battery testing and engineering laboratories in North America and expressed pride that it chose Michigan.
Gov. Whitmer stated UL Solutions’ investment proves their work to grow Michigan’s economy, invest in people, and build the future of cars, chips, and batteries is paying off.
Gov. Whitmer added the laboratory will test electric vehicle batteries being built in Michigan so they can safely go into Michigan-made cars and trucks.
Gov. Whitmer stated Michigan won this investment over several other states because of its strong workforce and leading battery and automotive manufacturers.

Mayor McDaniel stated the City of Auburn Hills, in coordination with the State of Michigan, has made new mobility, specifically the automotive and EV battery sectors, a priority driver for the local economy.

Supply chain issues, energy independence, sustainability goals, and local innovation and manufacturing velocity are key factors driving an influx of public and private investment to establish the foundation of a new domestic supply chain for EV and industrial batteries.
The U.S. government reported that EV suppliers and OEMs have announced $36 billion USD of investment to meet growing demand.
The laboratory will house dedicated UL Solutions experts and equipment capable of providing thermal fire propagation, electrical, mechanical abuse, and environmental testing.
Testing will be based on various UL and International Electrotechnical Commission standards, United Nations goals and initiatives, and Society of Automotive Engineers and OEM specifications.
UL Solutions plans to open its North America Advanced Battery Laboratory in mid-2024.
When completed, the facility will be one of North America’s most extensive battery testing and engineering laboratories.
UL Solutions is a global leader in applied safety science that delivers testing, inspection, certification services, software products, and advisory offerings across more than 100 countries.

Groundbreaking attendees also included Kathy Seegebrecht (regional vice president, Americas), Jeff Smidt (senior vice president, Industrial Testing, Inspection and Certification), Milan Dotlich (vice president and general manager, Energy and Industrial Automation), and Mary Joyce (vice president and general manager, New Mobility).
source_title: UL Solutions Breaks Ground at North America Advanced Battery Laboratory in Auburn Hills, Michigan | UL Solutions

facts: Georgia Power is boring holes thousands of feet into the Earth’s crust at a trio of spots in rural Georgia.
The company is testing whether rocks as much as 2 miles down are suitable for storing carbon dioxide produced by its oil- and gas-fired power plants.
The testing evaluates sites for geologic carbon sequestration, a technique that captures CO2, compresses it into a liquid-like state, and injects it into porous rock.
Georgia Power and its parent company Southern Company state that the testing is preliminary.
The federal government is pumping millions of dollars into advancing carbon storage technology.
New, more lucrative tax credits are available for companies that pursue carbon storage.
New emissions limits for power plants could force many utilities to capture and store CO2 to remain in operation.
source_title: Georgia Power is drilling holes more than a mile underground. Here’s why - Southern Business & Development

facts: Kia Georgia’s West Point-built Telluride took over Times Square’s New Year’s Eve countdown.
The vehicle spotlighted local workers and a father-son duo during the 2025 Ball Drop.
source_title: Kia Georgia highlighted at Times Square Ball Drop

facts: Auto OEMs need an undisrupted flow of material and components to their assembly lines to meet growing automobile demand.
Geopolitical factors pose risks to automotive supply chains, ranging from the Russia-Ukraine war to strikes in West Bengal or riots in South Africa.
The EV industry faces several battery recycling supply chain risks classified into three main themes: demand planning, geopolitical risk, and underutilised capacity.

By 2030, roughly 1.2 million end-of-life EV batteries will need to be recycled per year, with the number potentially rising to 14 million per year by 2040, according to the International Council on Clean Transportation.
NITI Aayog’s 2022 report estimated the cumulative potential of lithium-ion batteries in India during 2022-30 will be around 600 GWh across all segments in the base case.
Of that potential, 128 GWh will be available for recycling by 2030, with 46 per cent (59 GWh) estimated to come from EVs alone.
Planning for this demand without locking in capital in underutilised assets is identified as the biggest challenge.

Recent supply chain disruptions from the COVID-19 pandemic and Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, combined with rapidly growing demand in the Global South, have dramatically increased material and energy costs.
The average price of lithium was nearly four times higher in 2022 than in 2019, while cobalt and nickel prices doubled.
Battery metal price hikes in early 2022 led to battery prices increasing nearly 10 per cent globally relative to 2021 after years of continuous decline.
During 2019-20, the supply chain became a bottleneck due to COVID-19, prompting private and public entities to focus on local manufacturing and self-reliance.
Cell and battery manufacturers currently struggle to ensure steady daily raw material supply through legally binding contracts due to highly uncertain geopolitical factors.

A growing trend encourages global integration of localised supply chains, which may enable Just In Time (JIT) inventories in the future, though current evidence is limited to a vast diaspora of investors in the lithium supply chain.
More than 200 businesses globally now have a combined capacity to recycle more than 1 million metric tonnes of end-of-life batteries per year, according to Circular Energy Storage (CES).
Automotive OEMs are building their own EV battery recycling facilities because most materials in EV batteries are expensive but recyclable.
Mercedes-Benz inaugurated its battery recycling facility in Germany in October 2024.
Most existing recycling facilities represent locked-up capital that is far from a profitable rate of utilisation.
Hans Eric Melin, founder and managing director of CES, stated: “On a global basis, we have much more capacity than we need,” as quoted in c&en.
Capacity planning remains a significant bottleneck due to uncertainty in building robust supply chains.
At least 2GWh of recycling capacity has come up in India, with 58.88 GWh of waste EV battery volumes expected by 2030.
Current input volumes do not match the scale of CAPEX invested in recycling infrastructure.
Currently, around half of recycling plants’ feedstock material consists of production scrap from battery factories, including electrode offcuts and faulty cells.
Over the next decade, end-of-life batteries will come to dominate feedstock as battery production becomes more efficient and EOL battery availability grows.
Underutilisation of capacity is expected to occur in India similar to China, with many Indian recyclers currently operating with underutilised capacities.
source_title: India's EV Transition: Addressing Battery Recycling Supply Chain Risks

entity_name: US Government EV Battery Recycling & Critical Minerals Initiative
canonical_name: US Government EV Battery Recycling & Critical Minerals Initiative
facts: The US government announced in early May 2022 that it will provide more than $3bn to boost domestic production of electric vehicle (EV) batteries.
The proposal was announced as the US car industry pivots to meet President Joe Biden’s goal for half of vehicle sales in the country to be electric by 2030.
Experts warn of a gap between future production and demand for the critical minerals needed for EV batteries.
Scaling up mining in the short term is difficult, as on average it takes 16.5 years to take a new mine from planning to production.
Most minerals needed for EVs are produced in a handful of countries, making the supply chain vulnerable to price volatility and disruptions.
The Biden administration announced a separate $60m programme for battery recycling in early May 2022.
By reusing materials from end-of-life batteries, a secondary domestic supply chain independent of the mining supply chain could be created.
US-based analysts Allied Market Research valued the global EV battery recycling market at around $139m in 2017 and expected it to grow to $2.27bn by 2025.
The need for independence and security of supply became more urgent since Russia invaded Ukraine, causing halts to gas deliveries to Poland, Bulgaria, and Finland.
A month before announcing the $3bn EV battery boost, Biden invoked the Defense Production Act to increase the mining of EV battery minerals.
The Defense Production Act gives the government more access to funding to support mining, processing and recycling of critical minerals.

According to the US Geological Survey, the country had a net import reliance of 100% for graphite and manganese in 2021, followed by 75% for cobalt, almost 50% for nickel and more than 25% for lithium.

Shirley Meng, professor of molecular engineering at the University of Chicago and chief scientist at Argonne National Laboratory, stated that government funding can help bring more companies in North America to responsibly manufacture and recycle batteries.
Shirley Meng emphasized that batteries should be made next to where consumers are and reused/recycled, noting how they are made and used makes a fundamental difference to their carbon footprint.

US Secretary of Energy Jennifer Granholm stated in a 2 May press release that positioning the United States front and centre in meeting growing demand for advanced batteries boosts competitiveness and electrifies the transportation system.

Jennifer Granholm added that President Biden’s historic investment in battery production and recycling will give the domestic supply chain the jolt it needs to become more secure and less reliant on other nations.
The International Energy Agency (IEA) reported that clean energy technologies are becoming the fastest-growing segment of demand for critical minerals.

The IEA estimates that in a world on track to meet Paris Agreement goals, demand for copper and rare earth elements will rise by more than 40% in the next two decades, more than 60% for nickel and cobalt, and almost 90% for lithium.
Without a steady supply of critical minerals, the energy transition is at risk of slowing down.

IEA executive director Fatih Birol stated in May 2021 that data shows a looming mismatch between strengthened climate ambitions and availability of essential critical minerals, warning that unaddressed vulnerabilities could make global progress toward a clean energy future slower and more costly.
Recycling is well established globally for some metals like lead and iron, but not yet for many critical minerals due to the young EV market and current low prices of key minerals.
Policymakers play an important role in encouraging investment in EV battery recycling now.
The IEA stated that policies can play a pivotal role in preparing for rapid growth of waste volumes by incentivising recycling for products reaching the end of their operating lives.

Increasing collection and sorting rates is a crucial starting point to scale up recycling, with government policies facilitating waste collection to ensure a sufficiently large waste stream to justify infrastructure investment.
Governments can fund research into new recycling technologies and provide incentives for manufacturers to recycle.
The European Commission recognizes the need for improved collection and recovery of valuable materials from batteries in its European Green Deal Circular Economy Action Plan.
Securing access to secondary raw materials through recycling is a priority action for the European Battery Alliance.
The US has allocated funds for recycling but has yet to target domestic recycling of lithium-ion batteries in regulation.
Kunal Phalpher, chief strategy officer at lithium-ion recycling company Li-Cycle, stated that domestic manufacturing and recycling of lithium-ion batteries is critical to accelerating EV adoption.
Li-Cycle was founded in 2016 in Canada and has expanded its recycling hubs to the US in recent years.
Kunal Phalpher noted that continued commitment from the Biden administration to support EV battery manufacturing and recycling can help fuel growth and support sustainable electrification.
With an average EV battery life of between one and two decades, there are not yet large numbers of lithium-ion batteries available for recycling.
Large volumes of EV batteries will become ready for recycling from 2030 due to booming EV sales.
One study indicates that the number of EVs sold in 2019 alone could result in 500,000 tonnes of battery waste when they reach their end of life.
Scrap from battery manufacturing can already be recycled before end-of-life batteries become available.
Manufacturing scrap is collected from a manufacturer, recycled, and battery-grade minerals are returned for use in new EV batteries.

Kunal Phalpher explained that recycling scrap generated during battery production is an important component of the lithium-ion battery recycling industry and will ramp up as EV production keeps pace with demand.
Almost all of the valuable material contained within manufacturing scrap and end-of-life batteries can be used to make new EV batteries.
The US Department of Energy (DOE) reported that less than 5% of lithium-ion batteries were collected and recycled in 2019.
The DOE estimates that recycled material could provide one-third of United States cathode material needs for lithium-ion batteries by 2030.

The low recycling rate is explained by the limited supply chain for collecting, sorting, storing and transporting spent lithium-ion batteries, as well as the cost of recycling compared with the value of end-of-life batteries.
Recycling company Redwood Materials was founded by JB Straubel, the former chief technology officer of Tesla.
Redwood Materials states that batteries need an end-of-life solution to make EVs fully sustainable and affordable.

Redwood Materials explained in an email to Energy Monitor that this means not just collecting and recycling batteries but also fully refining recovered metals and turning them back into precision battery materials to reuse.
Recycling companies are teaming up with car and battery manufacturers.
Redwood Materials is working with Ford.
Panasonic will use recycled material from Redwood to make new batteries for Tesla.
Glencore and Britishvolt teamed up to build a recycling plant for lithium-ion batteries in the UK.
General Motors and LG partnered with Li-Cycle to process and return their scrap material.
Li-ion batteries are more difficult to recycle than average batteries partly due to their design.

Shirley Meng noted that traditional recycling methods designed for lead-acid batteries are not optimal for lithium-ion batteries, which operate near 4V using non-aqueous electrolytes, are often hermetically sealed, contain flammable materials, and require recycling of light and reactive lithium metal.
Recyclers initially used high-temperature smelting to melt material, but it takes a lot of energy and does not recover all valuable materials.
Li-Cycle uses a wet-chemistry process that first processes batteries by 'submerged shredding' to create a ‘black mass’ containing critical minerals.
In another facility, battery-grade minerals are subsequently extracted from the black mass using Li-Cycle's method.
This wet-chemistry method reduces the risk of batteries catching fire and recovers more valuable materials than smelting.
Partnerships between car/battery manufacturers and recyclers can help improve EV battery recycling and move towards a circular economy.
Rethinking how to design for recycling, such as making it easier to take batteries apart, would improve the sustainability of EVs beyond current priorities of durability and range.
Li-Cycle and multinational Umicore state that technological advances mean around 95% of materials in an EV battery can already be recovered.
Unlike plastic, there is no limit to how many times critical minerals in Li-ion batteries can be recycled without a loss of quality.
Caroline Jacobs, media relations manager at Umicore, stated that indefinite recycling avoids unnecessary pressure on natural resources and mining.
EV battery recycling could help solve mineral deficits long term and contribute to the overall sustainability of EVs.
Getting to a circular economy for batteries requires investments and continued evolution of battery and recycling technologies.
Government policies and funding are essential to establish an EV battery recycling supply chain now.
source_title: EV battery recycling: As EV sales rise, recyclers are getting ready

facts: Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA), in cooperation with Glovis America, has announced it has deployed Hyundai Xcient hydrogen fuel-cell electric trucks for clean logistics operations.
In the first stage, 21 Xcient trucks will be in operation.
These hydrogen heavy-duty trucks will transport vehicle parts from HMGMA suppliers across the USA to the Megasite in Bryan County, Georgia, on a daily basis.
During the initial operations, the trucks will transport parts between suppliers and the on-site consolidation center.
Later logistics operations will expand to a broader network of suppliers.
The 21 trucks will represent more than one-third of the Glovis America truck fleet at HMGMA.

Oscar Kwon, HMGMA CEO, said, “Our Hyundai Xcient hydrogen fuel-cell trucks that support the Metaplant release zero emissions and offer a cleaner alternative to the traditional freight logistics methods used at other manufacturing facilities.”
At this year’s CES, Hyundai announced HTWO, its new hydrogen value chain business brand.
HTWO encompasses the group’s businesses and affiliates, enabling each stage of the entire hydrogen value chain, from production and storage to transportation and use.
HTWO Logistics, a joint venture between Hyundai Motor Company and Glovis America, will also install a mobile hydrogen refueling station at the Megasite for efficient refueling.
Hyundai will also establish a hydrogen production and refueling station that will be announced later.
source_title: Hyundai deploys Xcient hydrogen trucks for clean logistics | Automotive Powertrain Technology International

entity_name: US Battery-Building Announcements (BMW, Envision AESC, Hyundai)
canonical_name: US Battery-Building Announcements (BMW, Envision AESC, Hyundai)
facts: The US battery-building spree continues as BMW and Hyundai each made major announcements this month.
BMW Group announced a $700 million investment to build a new high-voltage battery assembly facility in Woodruff, SC.
The new facility is located close to the company’s Spartanburg automobile manufacturing facility.
Plant Spartanburg will receive an influx of $1 billion to make it EV production-ready.
The new Woodruff facility will encompass over 1 million sq. ft.
The facility will produce next generation batteries for fully electric vehicles.
Around 300 new jobs will be created on site at the Woodruff facility.
Plant Spartanburg currently produces lithium-ion battery modules for the BMW X3 xDrive30e and BMW X5 xDrive45e plug-in hybrid electric vehicles.
The Woodruff facility will leverage the experience and expertise developed in Plant Spartanburg’s existing operations.
BMW concurrently announced an agreement with battery-maker Envision AESC to build a new battery cell factory in South Carolina to supply Plant Spartanburg.
Envision will produce newly developed round lithium-ion battery cells specifically designed for the sixth generation of BMW eDrive technology.
The new cells will be used in next generation electric vehicles.
The annual capacity of the Envision battery cell factory will be up to 30 GWh.
The new battery format is said to increase energy density by more than 20%.
The new battery format is said to improve charging speed by up to 30%.
The new battery format is said to enhance range by up to 30%.
CO2 emissions from cell production will be reduced by up to 60% through the partial use of secondary lithium, cobalt, and nickel material.
CO2 emissions from cell production will also be reduced by up to 60% through the use of renewable energy for production.
On October 25, Hyundai Motor Group officially broke ground on a new manufacturing plant, ‘Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America’ (HMGMA), in Bryan County, GA.
The $5.54 billion investment by Hyundai and its affiliate suppliers was announced five months prior to the groundbreaking.
HMGMA plans include producing a new battery manufacturing facility alongside facilities for making a range of Hyundai, Genesis, and Kia EVs.
HMGMA will create more than 8,100 jobs over the next few years.
The company aims to establish a stable supply chain for EV battery and other EV components in the U.S. market through HMGMA.
HMGMA’s new smart factory is expected to begin commercial production in the first half of 2025.
Approximately $1 billion is anticipated to be invested by non-affiliated HMGMA suppliers in connection with the project in other locations.
The new plant will feature a highly connected, automated, and flexible manufacturing system that organically connects all elements of the EV ecosystem.
All production processes—including order collection, procurement, logistics, and production—will be optimized utilizing AI and data.
The manufacturing system will help create a human-centered work environment with robots assisting human workers.
The Georgia facility will primarily rely on renewable energy sources to power operations.
The plant will use emission-reduction technologies to meet the RE100 requirements.
source_title: US Battery-Building: Updates from BMW, Envision, Hyundai

entity_name: Georgia Environmental Protection Division (EPD) Air Permit Fees
canonical_name: Georgia Environmental Protection Division (EPD) Air Permit Fee Structure
facts: Effective July 1, 2025, the Georgia Environmental Protection Division (EPD) will implement significant increases in both air permit application fees and annual operating permit emissions fees.
The fee adjustments are applicable to the 2024 calendar year emissions data.
EPD states these adjustments address funding shortfalls resulting from the retirement of several large facilities.
The adjustments aim to ensure the continued effectiveness of the state’s air quality programs.
The revised fee structure aims to cover reasonable costs associated with reviewing and acting upon permit applications, implementing and enforcing permit terms, and other related activities.
Increased annual air permit fees for calendar year 2024 emissions are due September 2, 2025.
Flat annual permit fees represent a 30% increase from the previous year.
Per-ton-based annual permit fees represent an approximate 20% increase from the previous year.
The updated NSPS Fee is $2,470.
The updated Synthetic Minor Source Fees is $2,730.
The updated Title V Sources – Maintenance Fee is $1,300.
The updated Title V Source – Minimum Fee is $5,850.
The updated Title V Sources per ton of emissions (PM, VOC, NOX, and SO2) for coal-fired electric generating units is $45.37.
The updated Title V Sources per ton of emissions (PM, VOC, NOX, and SO2) for all other sources is $43.13.
Permit application fees represent a 50% increase over the existing rates.
The Generic (Minor or Synthetic Minor) Permit application fee is $0.
The Title V Renewal application fee is $0.
The Off-Permit Change Request application fee is $0.
The No Permit Required Exemption application fee is $0.
The Name / Ownership Change application fee is $750.
The Minor Source Permit or Amendment application fee is $2,000.
The Permit-by-Rule application fee is $2,000.
The Synthetic Minor Source Permit or Amendment application fee is $3,000.
The Title V Minor Modification with Construction application fee is $6,000.
