The Title V Minor Modification without Construction application fee is $6,000.
The Title V Significant Modification with Construction application fee is $6,000.
The Title V Significant Modification without Construction application fee is $6,000.
The Title V 503(b)(10) Permit Amendment application fee is $6,000.
The Major Source Permit not PSD or 112(g) application fee is $6,000.
The PSD Permit application fee is $22,500.
The 112(g) Permit application fee is $22,500.
EPD increased the expedited permit application fees earlier this year.
Standard air permit application fees are increasing effective July 1, 2025, to support the costs associated with reviewing and processing permit applications.
Application fees are assessed per application and are separate from annual emissions fees.

Permittees subject to Title V (Part 70) permitting must calculate their annual fees based on the total tons of regulated air pollutants emitted, using the current per-ton fee rate set annually by EPD.
Facilities must use the most recent emissions data and multiply the total eligible emissions by the published fee rate.
Synthetic minor sources pay a flat annual fee.
Title V sources also pay a maintenance fee in addition to their emissions-based fee.
Certain NSPS sources pay a flat fee, in addition to any other fee.
Reviewing fee calculations annually helps avoid discrepancies and potential enforcement actions when submitting emissions inventories or permit renewal documents.
The cost of an expedited application for a synthetic minor permit amendment in July 2025 is $13,000, up from $7,000 a year ago.
Applicants should not submit payment until prompted by EPD staff after application submission.
Payments can be made via check or credit card through the Georgia Environmental Connection Online (GECO) system.
Trinity Consultants offers assistance with upcoming permit applications and guidance in navigating these fee increases through its Atlanta, Georgia office.
Contact for assistance is Katie Brubaker, Atlanta Office Manager.
source_title: Big Increase in Georgia EPD's Air Permit Fees - Trinity Consultants

facts: American company Ascend Elements has secured a major order worth USD 5 billion in the US to provide cathode precursor materials (pCAM) for battery manufacturing.
Beginning in Q4 2024, Ascend Elements will initially ship $1 billion worth of pCAM under the contract.
The client for the multi-year contract was not named by the company.
After the first delivery, the customer has an option to renew and expand the contract worth $5 billion.
Ascend Elements is building a factory in Kentucky to manufacture standard battery products.
The Kentucky facility is expected to produce enough cathode material (CAM) and precursors for up to 750,000 electric vehicle batteries per year after the expected end of 2023.
This annual production capacity is three times more than what the company had previously reported.
The company had previously only talked about producing CAM and pCAMs for 250,000 electric vehicles.
Groundbreaking for the Kentucky facility was laid in October 2022.
The US Department of Energy awarded Ascend Elements two grants totaling $480 million to accelerate the Kentucky facility.

Mike O’Cronley, CEO of Ascend Elements, stated that almost 100 percent of the world’s pCAM is produced in Asia and emphasized the need to make critical battery products in North America to secure the supply chain, reduce carbon emissions, and ensure energy independence.
In March 2023, the company opened its first commercial-scale lithium-ion battery recycling facility in Georgia, USA.
The Georgia facility, named Base 1, is described by Ascend Elements as North America’s largest recycling facility for electric vehicle batteries.
The Georgia facility processes 30,000 tons of batteries every year.
The annual processing capacity of the Georgia facility corresponds to about 70,000 electric vehicle batteries.
source_title: Announces major order from Ascend Elements USA

canonical_name: National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Formula Program
facts: On May 22, Southern Alliance for Clean Energy joined a coalition of environmental, consumer, and community organizations to challenge the Trump administration’s unlawful and indefinite freeze of billions in federal funding for the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) Formula Program.
In 2022, Congress allocated $5 billion for the program through the bipartisan Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA).
The program was designed to build electric vehicle charging stations every 50 miles on major corridors across all 50 states.
In February, the Federal Highway Administration (FHWA), acting on behalf of the Trump administration, halted the funds from flowing with the issuance of a memorandum.
The Southeast’s portion of the frozen funding totals over $679 million.

The NEVI program was designed to increase access to high-speed charging infrastructure along the interstate system and build confidence among American drivers that electric vehicles can function reliably for long-distance travel.
Currently, the Southeast lacks access to charging stations, with only 0.40 charging stations per 1,000 people.
This lack of accessible charging keeps consumers from having the confidence to purchase and drive electric vehicles.
Southeast states have significantly underinvested in charging station deployment as a region, so formula funds would guarantee investment and increased access for EV drivers.

The Southeast has long stretches of highway in rural areas currently without access to highway charging stations, and NEVI would increase investments and economic opportunities to these rural areas similar to the Rural Electrification Administration's impact on electrical power.
Many electric vehicles that the NEVI network would support are built in the Southeast, which is home to 31% of the national total labor force that manufactures EVs.

The region relies heavily on tourists and snowbirds who require access to fast charging while driving long distances, and the funding would provide up to 80% of the funding to install charging to meet this need.

Funds need to be restored so infrastructure that supports healthier, lower-cost transportation is available to every American, especially Southerners in rural communities without access to reliable high-speed charging.

Stephen Smith, Executive Director of the Southern Alliance for Clean Energy, stated that suspending the NEVI Program puts hard-won progress at risk and leaves Southern communities without the resources to lead in the transition to clean energy.
The Government Accountability Office (GAO) found that the February Federal Highway Administration memo directing the freeze violates the Impoundment Control Act.
The White House subsequently ordered the Department of Transportation to ignore the GAO ruling.

On June 25, a federal judge in Washington issued a preliminary ruling ordering the Trump administration to restore NEVI Formula Program funding, unlocking about $1 billion in federal funds while the case moves forward.
The court order did not take effect until July 2 to provide the federal government an opportunity to appeal, which they did not.
The ruling restores funding for 14 of the 17 Plaintiff States.
Minnesota, Vermont, and the District of Columbia were excluded from relief because the judge found they did not provide sufficient evidence on how the freeze harmed them.
While the ruling is not a final decision, it provides forward momentum for states getting EV charging projects moving forward.
SACE and allies are still awaiting a ruling on the motion to intervene and will continue seeking nationwide restoration of NEVI funds.

Electrify the South is a Southern Alliance for Clean Energy program that leverages research, advocacy, and outreach to promote renewable energy and accelerate the equitable transition to electric transportation throughout the Southeast.

links.context: Learn more about Electrify the South program

source_title: The NEVI Funding Saga: The Need, the Freeze, and the Challenge - Southern Alliance for Clean Energy (SACE)

facts: President Trump has frozen a $5 billion federal program aimed at installing new public EV fast charging stations in all 50 states, Washington DC, and Puerto Rico.
The NEVI EV Charging Station Program is funded with a total of $7.5 billion carved out of the 2021 Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
As of July 2024, 39 states have released solicitations for their NEVI programs and eight states have opened their first NEVI-funded stations (61 ports in total).
Additional stations are in the pipeline with more than 2,500 additional ports having been awarded or conditionally awarded by the states.
The midstream halt of NEVI funds jeopardizes millions in state and private investments.
IONNA is an EV charging joint venture combining BMW, General Motors, Honda, Hyundai, Kia, Mercedes-Benz, Stellantis, and Toyota.
On February 4, IONNA announced that it has contracted for more than 100 EV charging station sites across the US.
IONNA aims to bring 1,000 more charging bays online before the end of this year.
IONNA plans to build 30,000 charging bays over the next 5 years, representing new jobs across the nation and cutting-edge technological innovation.
The IONNA 'Rechargery' model features a lounge-style experience for drivers waiting to charge.
Rechargeries will offer seamless charging across all automakers, including reservations, route optimization, and in-car payments.
Rechargeries will feature an AI-assisted, automated grab-and-go payment system for snacks and refreshments.
The first IONNA Rechargery is installed at a site in Garner, North Carolina.
Electrify America remains active in the EV charging market alongside new players.
The New York Green Bank announced its first-ever EV charging station transaction, enabling Revel to install 267 charging ports spread among five public locations by 2027.
Illinois utility ComEd announced a $100 million rebate program for home EV chargers, business and commercial fleet rebates, and upgrades for non-residential ratepayers.
Ford is extending its 'Ford Power Promise' complimentary home charger and standard installation through March 31, 2025.
Ford announced the extension on January 3 following record electric vehicle year-over-year sales in 2024.
On February 12, Texas-based CLEAResult announced the launch of its new 'ChooseEV' assessment tool to help utilities and fleet owners transition to EVs.

CLEAResult launched in 2003 and bills itself as North America’s largest energy efficiency, energy transition and energy sustainability services provider, with 2,700 employees stationed in 60 US and Canadian cities.
Divakar Jandhyala serves as CLEAResult's Chief Product & Technology Officer.
source_title: New EV Charging Schemes Thwart Trump NEVI Squeeze

facts: Morgan County is denying the electric automaker $1.5 billion in bonds.
One of Georgia’s biggest economic development projects in history could be at risk, thanks to a Thursday ruling by a Morgan County judge.

Ocmulgee Superior Courts Chief Judge Brenda Holbert Trammell ruled the local development authority trying to lure a Rivian electric vehicle automotive plant to Morgan County has failed to establish the bonds at the center of the project are reasonable.
Those bonds are part of the $1.5 billion incentive package offered to the company.
Trammell’s ruling is likely to be appealed.
In exchange for those bonds, Rivian is promising to bring more than 7,000 jobs to the area as part of the $5 billion plant.

The Irvine, California-based electric vehicle manufacturer announced last year it would build the facility on a 2,000-acre site in Morgan and Walton counties about 45 miles east of Atlanta along Interstate 20.
It plans to produce up to 400,000 vehicles a year there and said it would bring 7,500 new jobs to Georgia.
Thanks to the ruling, that plan is now in jeopardy.

Attorney John Christy, who represents residents who contested the approval of the bond money, stated: “The court ruled that this project is just not going to be entitled to subsidization from the taxpayers of the three towns which is what the pilot program was all about.”
Christy argued the property tax breaks offered to Rivian take away from important potential benefits for the residents of the community where the plant is located.

Christy added: “All they have to do is just go ahead and build the plant that they want to with their own money, with their loan from a bank or whatever and pay their taxes, but they wanted to have this pilot program which was a very big subsidy by the taxpayers of these three counties.”
JDA and Georgia Department of Economic Development released a joint statement in response to the ruling.

In the joint statement, the JDA and Georgia Department of Economic Development expressed disappointment and disagreement with Judge Trammell’s decision, calling the project transformational for the community, state, and nation.
The joint statement noted they are currently assessing all legal options and intend to work with Rivian to move this project forward and see it through to completion.
Rivian also has a plant in Normal, Illinois.
Rivian said it hoped to break ground this past summer and begin production in 2024.
source_title: Huge Georgia Rivian project threatened by judge’s ruling

canonical_name: Rivian Georgia Electric Vehicle Assembly Plant
facts: A factory to produce electric trucks is slated to open in Georgia.
Estimates say it will create 400,000 emissions-free trucks per year.
The company, called Rivian, has invested $5 billion in the project.
It hopes to help speed the transition to clean energy and create new jobs.
Some residents have raised concerns about the project.
The company plans to sell its vehicles directly to customers in Georgia.
This could lead to a change in state laws on sales.
Rivian is currently planning two consumer models: a pickup and an SUV.
Both of these vehicles will cost approximately $70,000.
The pickup has already been named the 2022 truck of the year by Motor Trend.
It’s also been endorsed by companies like Amazon, which has purchased 100,000 of its trucks.
Georgia state transportation officials expect truck traffic to double by 2040.
They believe that a truck-only highway and lanes would help alleviate traffic congestion.
The state transportation department has backed the project and is eager to see it succeed.
Rivian, Georgia, a proposed manufacturing facility, is the focus of fierce opposition in the surrounding communities.
Located 45 miles east of downtown Atlanta, the plant is on a 2,000-acre site.
Local residents have expressed fears about light pollution and groundwater contamination.
The company has agreed to limit the impact of the plant on the surrounding area.
Rivian’s plans to build a second manufacturing facility in Georgia are ambitious.
The new facility will be nearly 20 million square feet, spread over 2,000 acres, and will be five times the size of its current facility in Normal, Illinois.
During the initial construction phase, the company will still employ over 5,000 people at its current manufacturing facility.
Ultimately, the plant will produce around 400,000 vehicles a year.
To complete its expansion, the company will need to obtain environmental permits.
This process will involve several public comment sessions and committees.
Rivian’s plans to build an electric vehicle assembly plant in Morgan County, Georgia, will eventually result in the creation of thousands of jobs in the surrounding area.
The factory is slated to open its doors in early 2024.
The company has been working behind the scenes with local officials and elected officials to ensure that local residents have their say in the project.
There are still many questions about the project’s impact on the surrounding communities.
The Rivian Electric Vehicle Assembly Plant is a massive 20 million square foot development on 2,000 acres east of Atlanta.
That’s almost three times as big as Disneyland and four times larger than the Vatican City.
The company plans to start construction in 2022 and expects to be in production by 2024.
Ultimately, the plant will employ more than 7,500 people.
There were several factors considered in choosing the Georgia site, including logistics, environmental impact, renewable energy, and the quality of talent in the area.
Georgia’s large workforce is highly educated, diverse, and interested in technology.
According to Rivian’s website, this environment is ideal for a manufacturing facility.
Additionally, the state is well placed as a logistics and distribution hub.
The company says that its Georgia location allows it to scale quickly while maintaining high standards in its products and facilities.
If this is the case, Georgia is an excellent location for their company.
The Rivian plant in Georgia will have a huge impact on the surrounding communities.
Several residents of the neighborhood have begun organizing.
Clint Powell, an electrical contractor, is among them.
Rivian has not disclosed its exact impact on the area’s residents.
Rivian Automotive has received $1.5 billion in incentives from Georgia to build its second manufacturing plant there.
The plant will be used to build the company’s second platform of inexpensive EVs.
The company currently has one plant in Illinois.
This new one will begin production in 2024.
When it’s ready, Rivian plans to hire about 800 people in this new facility.
Rivian has faced opposition from the local community and is attempting to address the concerns of those who oppose its location.
They say they are committed to protect the environment and water while partnering with the community.
Some residents and the city are concerned that the plant will bring too much noise and too many people to the area.
Rivian opponents have cited various concerns about the plant, including concerns about groundwater contamination and light pollution.
They are also worried about the negative impact the plant will have on the area’s farms and wildlife.
They have also said they oppose the lucrative public incentives offered to the company.
They worry that the massive factory will alter the bucolic character of the region and spur further development.
Several politicians, including Gov. Brian Kemp, have been critical of the plant.
Despite local government’s efforts to slow the project, some residents of the affected communities continue to protest the project.
They say the proposed manufacturing plant isn't compatible with the ecosystem, and they fear it will negatively affect the environment.
The project’s opponents have also rallied at town halls and board meetings.
Some have created Facebook groups to express their views.
One of them is called "Our Communities Oppose the Rivian Plant," and has more than 2,000 members.
Rivian opponents claim that the project will disrupt local well-water supplies, cause light pollution, and disrupt farmland and wildlife habitat.
Local opponents point out that unemployment in Morgan County is about two percent, and that the plant should create jobs in areas where they are needed.
Although the plant is based in California, local opponents say they don't care about the company’s national reputation.
They are pleased with Georgia Gov. Sonny Perdue’s backing of the project.
A Georgia state senator has said he is open to the Rivian plant’s proposal to build a battery factory in Rutledge.
During a debate Sunday, former U.S. Sen. David Perdue, a Republican, said Georgia should not give taxpayer-funded incentives to Rivian, which is owned by the billionaire George Soros.
Kemp’s stance on the plant has not changed.
In his speech Sunday, Perdue invoked George Soros, a prominent Democratic donor who owns more than $2 billion of Rivian stock.
Soros has been the target of conservatives and has been accused of supporting a Democratic candidate.
The Georgia legislature has not acted on the bill yet, but Rivian is pushing lawmakers to amend SB 398 and allow it to sell electric vehicles.
The company is currently working to develop a 2,000-acre site in the state.
Eventually, the company hopes to make 100,000 electric delivery vans for Amazon.
Before it can begin manufacturing, it will need an exemption from the state law to sell electric vehicles.
Rivian is a new electric car maker based just outside Detroit.
Its all-electric SUVs and trucks are poised to change the automotive world.
A truck model debuted earlier this year, and an SUV version will be released later.
Both vehicles will be built for rugged terrain and offer semi-autonomous driving abilities.
Rivian cars are based on the same skateboard platform architecture.
They have four electric motors per wheel and do not use conventional transmissions or differentials.
They also have adjustable air suspension systems and big towing ratings.
They are available in either front-drive or all-wheel-drive versions.
Rivian was founded in 2009 by RJ Scaringe, an MIT mechanical engineering graduate.
He originally had plans to develop a battery-powered sports car, but faced difficulties.
He eventually switched to developing pickup trucks and luxury SUVs.

Rivian has recently announced plans to build another manufacturing plant in Georgia. It will span more than two million square feet and nearly 2,000 acres, nearly five times the size of the company’s existing Normal, Illinois factory.
Rivian is an early mover in the field of commercial electric vehicles.
It has received backing from Ford, which invested $500 million in the company in 2009.
Ford still holds a 9.7% stake in the company.
source_title: How Truck Became Georgia? [Answered 2026] | Prettymotors

facts: The plant will be Rivian's second production facility in the U.S., joining its existing one in Normal, IL.
EV maker Rivian gained approval to start construction on a new manufacturing plant outside of Atlanta, GA.
Rivian was granted final approval for the project by the Georgia Department of Economic Development (GDECD) and the Joint Development Authority (JDA) of Jasper, Morgan, Newton and Walton counties.
The GDECD and JDA announced the final approval on Nov. 9 via WSB-TV.
The approval gives Rivian long-term rights to construct an automotive plant in the Stanton Springs industrial area east of Atlanta.
Plans for the facility were first set in motion in 2021.
The JDA and GDECD stated that they are closing and issuing bonds for the Rivian project.
Renting the site to Rivian is considered the next step in delivering a generational opportunity for Georgians in Jasper, Morgan, Newton and Walton counties.
The factory is expected to manufacture Rivian’s R2 next-generation platform for future vehicles.
The site is located near the I-20 corridor to Atlanta’s east, near the historic Walton County community of Social Circle and touching on Morgan County.
The facility will join Rivian's first factory in Normal, IL, which produces the R1T pickup and the R1S SUV.
Rivian gained approval for its Economic Development Agreement (EDA) on the factory earlier this year.
The new approval allows the automaker to officially begin construction.
Shortly after Rivian’s initial commitment from Georgia for the Stanton Springs North site in 2021, the state approved a $43 million EV battery recycling plant nearby.
The Rivian location includes just under 2,000 acres at a site previously called the East Atlanta Megasite.
The facility is expected to bring as many as 7,500 jobs to the area.
Rivian will invest $5 billion into building the facility.
Rivian is targeting 2026 for initial production.
The company eventually expects to reach an annual volume production of around 400,000 vehicles.

In addition to building the manufacturing facility, Rivian is expected to accelerate charging station deployment in the area in nearby shopping centers, state parks, and near its Ponce City Market showroom.
The company’s production facility has forced Georgia lawmakers to consider direct sales laws in the state.
Georgia currently bans the sales of automobiles that aren’t done through a dealership.
Tesla is exempt from the direct sales ban, though it is limited to five statewide sales sites as of last year.
Rivian is introducing an apprenticeship program in which the company will select local students for a 24-month training to support the Stanton Springs North facility.
The Georgia HOPE Grants will provide tuition for the apprenticeship program.
Rivian plans to fund additional expenses for the apprenticeship, including textbooks and other learning materials.
source_title: Rivian Can Begin Construction on $5B Manufacturing Plant in Georgia - Autobody News

canonical_name: Rivian Electric Vehicle Assembly Plant (Stanton Springs North)
facts: Rivian resumed site-preparation work at its Stanton Springs North site in Social Circle, Ga., in August 2025.
Site preparation activities include installing utilities and carrying out grading and other preparatory work.
The company hosted a community event on Sept. 14, 2025.
Rivian plans an invitation-only ceremonial groundbreaking on Sept. 16, 2025.
Current on-site activity is focused on site prep and utility installation rather than full structural work.
Vertical construction, defined as the main structural build including foundations and building shells, is slated to begin in 2026.
The restart of work follows a pause in construction that occurred in March 2024.
Local officials and company representatives will attend the Sept. 16 ceremony to highlight expected economic and job benefits for the region.
The Georgia factory project was first announced in December 2021 when Rivian unveiled plans for a $5 billion electric vehicle assembly plant at Stanton Springs North.
On Jan. 15, 2025, the U.S. Department of Energy announced a $6.57 billion loan package to Rivian to support construction of an EV manufacturing facility in Georgia.
Rivian confirmed on Jan. 16, 2025 that it had closed a loan agreement with the DOE for up to $6.6 billion.
The loan funds will help finance the Stanton Springs plant and expand Rivian's U.S. production capacity.
Approximately $5.98 billion of the loan covers principal interest, with an additional $592 million covering capitalized interest.
This funding aligns with the federal government’s strategic push to strengthen the domestic supply chain for clean energy manufacturing and electric vehicles.
Rivian is among automakers benefiting from the DOE’s Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing loan program.
Previous recipients of the DOE loan program include Ford Motor Company ($5.9 billion in 2009), Nissan North America ($1.45 billion in 2010), and Tesla Motors ($465 million in 2010).
Rivian announced on December 19, 2023, that it selected a construction company to build its planned manufacturing factory in Georgia.
Clayco, a Chicago-based company, was selected to build the facility.
The factory will be approximately 16 million square feet and sit on an expansive 1,800-acre site spanning Morgan and Walton counties in Georgia.
The project is expected to create approximately 7,500 direct jobs once manufacturing begins.
Rivian is headquartered in California and currently operates a factory in Illinois that transforms a former Mitsubishi facility to manufacture its R1T truck and R1S SUV.
The new custom-built Georgia facility will focus on producing Rivian’s upcoming vehicle lineup, including the R2 crossover model.
In November 2024, the U.S. Department of Energy issued a conditional commitment for a multi-billion-dollar loan to support the project.
On Sept. 14, 2025, Rivian hosted a public community celebration at the site that offered vehicle demos and family activities.
The Sept. 16, 2025 ceremonial groundbreaking included state and local officials and signaled the formal restart of the project.
Vertical construction of the plant’s main structures is scheduled to begin in early 2026 (Q1 2026), covering foundations, building shells, and major structures.
Rivian targets a production start at the Georgia facility around 2028, with commissioning and ramp-up expected around that time.
The fully built plant will have a capacity of up to 400,000 vehicles per year.
source_title: Rivian Restarts Site-Prep at Georgia Plant; Vertical Construction Set for 2026

facts: A leading tier 1 automotive supplier recently placed a large order for eight (8) custom designed walk-in ovens for aluminum aging of automotive parts with furnace manufacturer DELTA H.
The ovens will be qualified at class 2 (+/-10°F).
The ovens feature all necessary performance requirements for compliance to CQI-9 and AMS2750E specifications.
DELTA H collaborated with the customer for 6 months on the design and approval phase.
All 8 ovens will be commissioned in late summer 2020.
source_title: Leading Automotive Tier 1 Supplier Orders Eight Custom Furnaces - Page 54 of 127 - Heat Treat Today

facts: SK Battery America is a subsidiary of South Korea-based SK On.
SK Battery America is laying off workers from its production facility in Commerce, Georgia.
The layoffs coincide with slowing electric vehicle battery demand and drying up federal incentives.
The lithium-ion battery producer filed a Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification on Friday.
The notification informed Georgia of job cuts affecting 958 people.
Affected workers will receive pay and benefits for the next 60 days.
A spokesperson confirmed the severance details to ESG Dive sister publication Manufacturing Dive via email.
Friday, March 6 was the last day of work for the affected employees.
The Commerce site previously employed 2,566 workers.
The layoffs reduced the facility's headcount to 1,600.
Joe Guy Collier serves as SK Battery America’s spokesperson.
Collier stated the company will work with affected workers during the transition period.
SK Battery America remains committed to Georgia and building a robust U.S. supply chain for advanced battery manufacturing.
The company is pursuing future customers in the Battery Energy Storage System arena.
The EV battery industry is currently navigating demand challenges.
Many automakers and battery companies are making cutbacks due to slower sales and a pullback of federal incentives under the current administration, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas.
Companies are shifting focus to cater to growing data center demand for backup power and energy storage systems.
In December, Ford Motor Co., a customer of SK Battery America, announced it was shifting electrification plans to include hybrid vehicles and smaller, more affordable EVs.
Ford is also expanding its energy storage business.
The automaker expects to record a $19.5 billion write-down due to the strategic shift.
Ford dissolved its BlueOval SK battery joint venture with SK On as it shifts away from EVs.
Ford canceled the electric F-150 Lightning due to sluggish sales.
In 2023, SK Battery America announced plans to expand its Commerce site workforce by several hundred employees to meet rising EV demand.
The planned expansion exceeded the company's initial goal of 2,600 employees.
The Occupational Safety and Health Administration later fined the battery maker twice for exposing workers to respiratory hazards.
Law firm Strauss Borrelli announced an investigation into SK Battery for allegedly failing to notify the 958 employees per the WARN Act.
The WARN Act mandates that employers notify workers in writing at least 60 days before a plant closure or job cuts take effect.
Collier stated that SK Battery America’s reduction complies with WARN guidelines.
source_title: Nearly 1,000 workers laid off at Georgia EV battery plant | ESG Dive

facts: The domestic EV market and the battery industry serving it are in steep decline.
SK Battery America cut 958 jobs at its Georgia plant amid an EV sales slowdown.
The South Korean battery manufacturer reduced its workforce at its Commerce, Georgia, facility by about 37% as EV demand weakens and policy changes reshape the market.
Author Ashby Lincoln explained how shifting market and political winds resulted in the malinvestment.
SK Battery America stated it is still committed to long-term battery investments for the U.S. with business from Volkswagen and Hyundai models.
Volkswagen has pumped the brakes on major US factory plans.
Hyundai and Kia have cut and delayed EV models.
In all, 18 EV carmakers have announced cutbacks or other retreats.
SK Battery America’s website currently claims it has solidified supply agreements with top-tier global automotive leaders at the forefront of the electric vehicle revolution.

The company’s website states its commitment to expanding the electric vehicle battery industry extends to collaboration across diverse sectors in the U.S. and maintains enduring partnerships with prominent companies rooted in advanced technology and steadfast reliability.
The mega-agreement with Ford is kaput.
The advertised workforce of 2,600 on the company’s website is incorrect.
The company’s planned new battery plant in Tennessee is at risk.
A recent study by the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas warned that projects under construction reflect a significant amount of potential capacity, especially when considering those focused on EVs.
The fate of these plants hangs in the balance, whether they will be canceled or postponed indefinitely in 2026 due to the changing dynamics of the U.S. battery market.
source_title: Leading EV Battery Company Joins the Bust - Master Resource

facts: A year after breaking ground, Hyundai Motor Group has hired about 290 people at its Metaplant near Savannah.
The company announced the hiring progress as it released photos and renderings of the factory being built along Interstate 16.
Hired employees are referred to as “Meta Pros” in company parlance and are direct employees of Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA).
These hires represent the first fruits of a promised 8,500 jobs on the site.
The site will include a joint-venture battery facility with LG that accounts for the bulk of the financial investment.
The employee tally does not include the 2,000 construction workers laboring to build the facility in Bryan County.
Hyundai says supplier jobs will boost its total employment impact to 14,476 jobs.
The company is racing to meet demand for made-in-America vehicles that qualify for the $7,500 tax credit outlined in the Inflation Reduction Act.
A U.S. manufacturing base will provide regulatory certainty under the Biden administration.
The Korean auto maker could face a changing market and new political risks when it comes online by early 2025.
EV sales globally have cooled since the company announced its $5.54 billion investment in May 2022.
Hyundai describes the new plant on its careers website as an idyllic place for employees who want to forge the future of car manufacturing.
The company is hiring as construction continues.

The plant will feature a highly-connected, automated and flexible manufacturing system where all processor production — order collection, procurement, logistics and production — will be optimized utilizing AI and data.
Hyundai eschewed terms like “factory” in favor of “Metaplant,” according to the Atlanta Journal-Constitution.
The Atlanta Journal-Constitution compared the Georgia plant with a much smaller Singapore factory sharing some of the same features.
The company latched on to “Meta” because in science it can mean transformation or transcending, aiming to transform what an automotive plant is and transcend employee perceptions.
The factory design includes parallel lights along the front of the building to allow interstate travelers to see vehicles moving from the paint area to general assembly by conveyor.
An exhibition hall designed in a square pays homage to Savannah’s historic squares, complete with a courtyard and water features.
The production floor features open layouts, indoor landscaping and respite zones with skylights designed to enhance collaboration.
A solar parking lot with 1,878 spaces will produce 5.2 megawatts of energy, helping the plant reach its goal of being 100 percent powered by renewable energy at the start of mass production.
The site includes a 41-acre ecological park with trails, tracks and sports fields.
Construction progress shows that 27,045 tons of steel have been used in competing 81 percent of the facility’s framing.
Oscar Kwon, CEO of HMGMA, stated they are extremely pleased with the design of their state-of-the-art smart plant and are eager to see the finished product.
The 290-employee figure breaks down to 182 salaried and 108 hourly workers.
So far, 53 Korean expatriates are on site to aid with the construction process and commissioning aspects of the plant.

links.url: Read the full news release here

source_title: A Year After Groundbreaking, Hyundai Ramps Up Hiring at Electric Vehicle Factory - Global Atlanta

facts: Field Service Engineering is moving its corporate headquarters from Oakland County, Mich., to Hart County, Ga., creating 80 new full-time positions in an expanded facility.

GDEcD Commissioner Chris Cummiskey stated that Georgia’s central location in the middle of the Southeast will make it very easy for FSE to travel from Hart County to anywhere in the southeastern U.S. to reach its customers.
Field Service Engineering is a leader in global field mitigation and full service quality assurance.
The company will invest $5 million in expanding its operations in Hart County.
Plans include building a new 150,000-square-foot facility in Gateway Industrial Park at exit 177 on Interstate 85.
FSE is adding 50 more positions to its current 30-person workforce in Hart County.
The total number of employees will reach 80 when the company reaches full operation in 2015.

FSE President and CEO Mark G. Belding stated that with support from Hart County and Georgia state officials, the company has established its presence in the Southeast and expects to continue growing in various sectors, creating larger demand for workforce expansion.
FSE will expand its quality assurance services to the region’s robust automotive industry.
The company also supports non-automotive manufacturers, as well as companies in the logistics and medical fields.
Hart County Board of Commission Chairman Bill Myers expressed pleasure at seeing Field Service Engineering aggressively grow its business and workforce in the area.

Hart County Industrial Building Authority Chairman Douglas Cleveland noted the expansion represents a significant commitment by FSE and expressed excitement about the career opportunities created for local citizens.
Field Service Engineering was founded in 2005 and focuses on providing global field mitigation solutions.
The company has evolved into a multifaceted organization focusing on quality improvement services with an overall objective of achieving total customer satisfaction.

Services provided include product containment and inspection, CS2, End of Line (EOL) containment, line visualization, evaluation of plant quality and maintenance systems, transportation of hazardous or fuel-exposed products, and reduction of Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ).

links.context: Georgia Department of Economic Development website

source_title: GA: Field Service Engineering Relocates, Creates 80 jobs in Hart County | Trade and Industry Development

facts: Hyundai Motor Group deploys XCIENT hydrogen fuel cell trucks for HMGMA clean logistics.
In cooperation with Glovis America, HMGMA announced the deployment of Hyundai XCIENT hydrogen fuel cell trucks.
HMGMA announced that its second production vehicle will be the all-electric 2026 IONIQ 9 three-row SUV.
Hyundai announced the release of the refreshed 2025 IONIQ 5, including a rugged new IONIQ 5 XRT variant.
HMGMA officially announced that the first production vehicle to be produced at its new Metaplant will be the 2025 Hyundai IONIQ 5.
Hyundai Motor Company shared its hydrogen vision and introduced its U.S. clean logistics business powered by the Class 8 XCIENT.
Hyundai contributed to local Savannah nonprofit organizations to support safety, education, and reforestation in the community.
Hyundai Motor Group signed a 15-year power purchase agreement (PPA) with Matrix Renewables.
Hyundai Motor America and HMGMA joined the 200th Anniversary of the Saint Patrick’s Day Parade in Savannah, Georgia.
Governor Brian P. Kemp, First Lady Marty A. Kemp, along with the president and global COO of Hyundai Motor Company, participated in HMGMA events.
HMGMA announced the design of a Bryan County water tank created by Savannah College of Art and Design (SCAD) students during a SCADpro design challenge.
Hyundai announced a new wage structure for its production team members at Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama.
HMGMA celebrated the one-year anniversary of its groundbreaking in Bryan County by officially releasing the latest design renderings.
source_title: Press Releases Archives - Page 2 of 4 - HMGMA

facts: Kia America announced immediate assistance programs to help in recovery efforts following Hurricane Helene in the Southeastern U.S.

Eligible Kia owners in Florida, Georgia, South Carolina, and North Carolina will receive $500 in support for the purchase or lease of select new Kia vehicles between October 4, 2024, and January 2, 2025.
Customers must have an insurance claim for loss of a vehicle due to Hurricane Helene to be eligible for the payment assistance program.
The $500 payment assistance can be combined with cash incentives from Kia America, Inc., and finance or lease offers from Kia Finance America (KFA).
The offer is valid on new 2024 or 2025 Kia vehicles, excluding the 2024 K5, 2024 Sorento Hybrid, and 2024 Sorento PHEV.
Customers must take delivery from retailer stock by January 2, 2025.
Current customers affected by the disaster can contact Kia Finance America to discuss financial hardship support options.
Kia America partnered with Kia Georgia, Inc., to donate $250,000 to the American Red Cross for disaster relief efforts throughout the region.
SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon serves as President and CEO of Kia North America and Kia America.
Alicia Doherty serves as Regional Executive for the Red Cross of Georgia.
Hurricane Helene made landfall as a Category 4 hurricane on Sept. 26, causing catastrophic damage throughout Georgia, Florida, South Carolina, North Carolina, and Tennessee.
The donation extends Kia’s “Accelerate the Good” charitable program, which has provided more than $30 million to those in need since 2019.
Kia America is headquartered in Irvine, California.
Kia is recognized as one of the TIME World’s Most Sustainable Companies of 2024.
Kia serves as the “Official Automotive Partner” of the NBA and WNBA.
Kia offers gasoline, hybrid, plug-in hybrid, and electric vehicles sold through a network of over 775 dealers in the U.S.
Certain 2025 EV9 all-electric three-row SUV, Sportage (excludes HEV/PHEV), Sorento (excludes HEV/PHEV), and Telluride are assembled in the United States from U.S. and globally sourced parts.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is the first manufacturing site in North America for Kia Corporation based in Seoul, Korea.
Kia Georgia has an annual production capacity of 350,000 units.
Kia Georgia is located on 2,200 acres in West Point, Ga.
Kia Georgia began mass production on Nov. 16, 2009.
Kia Georgia manufactures the Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV (excludes HEV and PHEV models), Sportage SUV (excludes HEV and PHEV models), and the all-electric EV9 SUV.

“Kia is committed to supporting our customers as well as the local communities, our friends and neighbors throughout the Southeast affected by Hurricane Helene, and we are grateful to have tremendous partners like the American Red Cross who are ready to quickly assist communities and provide a comforting hand in times of need,” said SeungKyu (Sean) Yoon.

“Our team is so appreciative for Kia’s generous contribution, which allows the Red Cross and its partners to provide support to those with the greatest need following Hurricane Helene,” said Alicia Doherty.
source_title: KIA AMERICA ANNOUNCES CUSTOMER ASSISTANCE PROGRAM AND DONATES $250,000 TO THE AMERICAN RED CROSS FOR THOSE IMPACTED BY HURRICANE HELENE - Kia Georgia

facts: An electric vehicle (EV) is a vehicle propelled mostly by electric power, encompassing road vehicles, rail, boats, aircraft, and spacecraft.
EVs originated in the late 19th century but were hindered by limited range until lithium battery advances revived interest in the late 20th century.
Manufacturers initially switched to hybrids with internal combustion engines and electric motor supplements before plug-in hybrids saw mass production in the late 2000s.
Battery electric cars became practical for consumers in the 2010s.
Technological progress in EV batteries, traction motors, and automotive electronics made EVs more feasible and cost-efficient than conventional ICE vehicles in the 21st century.
Government incentives are available in many areas to promote EV adoption to reduce tailpipe emissions and fossil fuel dependency.
As of 2026, China's electric vehicle industry produces more than the rest of the world combined.
In January 1990, General Motors President introduced the "Impact" EV concept at the Los Angeles Auto Show.
The California Air Resources Board mandated major automaker EV sales starting in phases from 1998.
From 1996 to 1998, GM produced 1,117 EV1s, with 800 available through three-year leases.
Chrysler, Ford, GM, Honda, and Toyota produced limited EVs for California drivers during this period.
GM discontinued the EV1 in 2003 after lease expiration, citing federal court challenges to zero-emission mandates, spare parts regulations, and industry media campaigns.
A 2005-2006 film titled "Who Killed the Electric Car?" explored roles of automakers, oil industries, the U.S. government, and the public in limiting EV deployment.
Ford released Ecostar delivery vans, while Honda, Nissan, and Toyota repossessed and crushed most of their leased EVs; Toyota later sold 200 RAV4 EVs at over $40,000 each after protests.
Citroën stopped production of the Berlingo Electrique in September 2005, and Zenn produced vehicles from 2006 to 2009.
Renewed interest in EVs emerged in the late 20th/early 21st century due to petroleum infrastructure impacts and peak oil fears.
EVs consume electricity generated from fossil fuels, nuclear power, renewables like solar and wind, or combinations thereof.
Recent advancements in battery technology and charging infrastructure have addressed earlier adoption barriers.
EV carbon footprints vary based on electricity generation fuel and technology.
Electricity can be stored via batteries, flywheels, or supercapacitors.
Regenerative braking recovers kinetic energy typically lost as heat during friction braking, restoring it to the onboard battery.
Over 20% of new cars sold in 2024 were electric-only, while only 2% of trucks were.
China accounted for more than 70% of global EV production and 67% of global sales in 2024.
EVs are categorized by fuel source: combustion-only (ICE), micro hybrid, mild hybrid, plug-in hybrid (PHEV), all-electric (BEV/AEV), and fuel cell (FCV/FCEV).
EVs are much more efficient than ICE vehicles with few direct emissions but rely on grid electricity from non-fossil and fossil plants.
Some areas allow individuals to request utilities provide renewable energy for EV charging, increasing energy resilience.
EVs connect to generator plants via direct electric grids (trains/trams), online power strips via electromagnetic induction, or onboard generators (diesel, fuel cell, nuclear, solar).
Hybrid EVs can combine onboard rechargeable storage with direct land-based grid connections for highway recharging or fueled propulsion sources.
Full electric vehicles store power externally before disconnecting, using chemical batteries (typically lithium-ion), kinetic flywheels, or static double-layer capacitors.
Electro-chemical battery conversions are reversible, storing energy chemically and improving efficiency by avoiding intermediate mechanical steps.
Battery, traction motor, and controller designs vary by vehicle size and application, from shopping carts to industrial forklifts.
EV batteries typically use lithium-ion technology due to higher energy density, longer lifespan, and higher power density compared to other practical batteries.
Most BEVs include a separate 12-volt auxiliary battery powered by the traction battery via a DC/DC converter for accessories and controls.
In 2024, Hyundai and Kia recalls cited Integrated Charging Control Unit (ICCU) failures that could stop 12-volt charging and cause drive power loss.

Lithium-ion batteries were first commercially released in 1991 and dominate EVs due to safety, durability, thermal breakdown, environmental impact, and cost factors requiring safe temperature/voltage operation.
Operating a subset of battery cells at a time and switching them is a technique to increase lifespan and reduce costs/environmental impact.
Nickel-metal hydride batteries were previously used by GM but are outdated due to heat self-discharge, Chevron patent restrictions, and high costs.
Lithium-ion battery prices declined dramatically over the past decade, reducing EV prices, though critical mineral lithium prices increased from 2021 to late 2022.
Electric motor power is measured in kilowatts (kW) and can deliver maximum torque over a wide RPM range, often exceeding comparable ICE performance.
Charging efficiency varies by charger type, with energy lost during electrical-to-mechanical conversion.
DC electricity is typically fed into a DC/AC inverter to power 3-phase AC motors, though DC or universal motors are used for trains, forklifts, and some cars.
Tesla vehicles use induction motors, while Nissan Leaf and Chevrolet Bolt use permanent magnet machines.
Electric motors achieve approximately 90% energy conversion efficiency over full speed/power ranges and can be finely controlled with high stationary-to-moving torque without gearboxes.
Linear motors drive maglev trains via magnetic levitation, eliminating rolling resistance and mechanical wear but restricting operations to high-speed point-to-point services.
Regenerative braking in electric traction transforms train motion into electrical power fed back into lines, advantageous for mountainous and start-and-stop city use.
EVs provide quiet operation with less noise/vibration than ICE vehicles, prompting many countries to mandate slow-speed warning sounds for pedestrian safety.
Pure-electric vehicles are powered exclusively by electric motors from batteries, solar panels, or fuel cells.
Hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) combine conventional ICE with one or more electric engines, using energy recovery technologies like motor-generators and regenerative braking.
Some HEVs use an internal combustion engine to directly drive a generator for battery recharging or motor power, known as a range extender.
Many HEVs reduce idle emissions via start-stop systems that shut down the combustion engine at idle and restart it when needed.
Hybrid-electric systems produce less tailpipe emissions than comparable petrol vehicles due to smaller engine displacement and lower fuel consumption.

Parallel hybrids mechanically couple engines and motors to wheels, series hybrids use only electric motors (often called EREVs/REEVs), and series-parallel hybrids allow engine-only, motor-only, or combined operation.
Plug-in electric vehicles (PEVs) recharge from external sources like wall sockets, with stored electricity driving wheels; PEVs include BEVs, PHEVs, and conversions.
Range-extended electric vehicles (REEVs) use an electric motor and plug-in battery, with an auxiliary combustion engine only supplementing charging.
On-road EVs include cars, trolleybuses, buses, trucks, bicycles, motorcycles, scooters, neighborhood vehicles, golf carts, milk floats, and forklifts; off-road includes ATVs and tractors.
Electric trucks transport cargo or perform utilitarian work, using lead-acid batteries historically but now lighter, energy-dense chemistries for broader roles.
Electric trucks reduce noise/pollution and have dramatically lower ownership/operating costs due to high efficiency, low component counts, no idle fuel burning, and silent acceleration.
Long-distance freight truck electrification is hindered by battery weight reducing payload and frequent recharging reducing delivery time, while short-haul urban delivery has electrified rapidly.
Railborne EVs use fixed overhead lines or third rails for power, enabling electric locomotives, multiple units, trams, light rail, and rapid transit common in Europe and Asia.
Electric trains have high power-to-weight ratios, allowing TGVs to operate at 320km/h (200mph)+ and locomotives to exceed diesel power output, with regenerative braking returning power to the grid.
Maglev trains are nearly always EVs using magnetic levitation for near-zero rolling resistance.
Battery electric passenger trains operate on non-electrified rail lines.
Fuel-cell electric trains are gaining popularity in Europe, with Germany ordering Alstom Coradia iLINT sets since 2018 and France planning orders.
The UK, Netherlands, Denmark, Norway, Italy, Canada, and Mexico show interest in hydrogen trains; SNCF plans to replace remaining diesel-electric trains with hydrogen trains by 2035.
Alstom announced in 2018 plans to retrofit British Rail Class 321 trainsets with fuel cells.
Electric boats saw renewed interest due to quiet/renewable marine transport potential, with solar cells giving motorboats infinite range and electric motors replacing diesel in sailboats.
Fully electric tugboats operate in Auckland (June 2022), Vancouver (October 2023), and San Diego.
Electric aircraft include piloted and unpiloted aerial vehicles, with power sources for spacecraft including batteries, solar panels, and nuclear power.
Spacecraft propulsion methods include arcjet rockets, electrostatic ion thrusters, Hall-effect thrusters, and Field Emission Electric Propulsion.

Electric rovers are the only option for outer space/exoplanetary exploration due to lack of oxygen, with Apollo missions (1971-1972) using silver-oxide battery-powered Lunar Roving Vehicles traveling up to 35.7 km.
Solar-powered uncrewed rovers have explored the Moon and Mars.
Charging stations (EVSE) supply power for recharging plug-in EV batteries, with AC and DC types being main categories.
EV batteries require DC charging, so most vehicles have onboard AC-to-DC converters; DC chargers integrate this converter to bypass vehicle constraints.
Battery swapping mechanically replaces batteries at stations in minutes as an alternative to socket recharging.
Metal-air fuel cells like zinc-air batteries may require mechanical refueling by replacing anodes/electrolytes instead of electrical recharging.
GM is adding V2H bidirectional charging to 2024-2026 models (Silverado, Blazer EV) to send battery power to homes during grid outages.
EVs release no tailpipe pollutants, reduce respiratory illnesses like asthma, and could prevent hundreds of thousands of early deaths annually from air pollution.
EVs significantly reduce urban noise pollution, improving quality of life.
In most cases, EV lifecycle carbon emissions are lower than conventional vehicles, though cost-responsive charging strategies can increase embedded carbon emissions.

Urban EVs almost always pollute less than ICE vehicles, but grid electricity sources (especially coal) and battery manufacturing/recycling create negative environmental impacts including toxic metals.
ICE vehicles use more raw materials over their lifetime than EVs; estimates suggest over a fifth of lithium and about 65% of cobalt for electric cars will come from recycled sources by 2035.
Switching privately owned car fleets from ICE to EVs does not free up road space for active travel or public transport.
source_title: Electric vehicle - Wikipedia
